topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Satellite Orbit and Orbital Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 1 — Satellite Orbit and Orbital Equations

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> A **satellite orbit** is the gravitationally curved trajectory of an artificial or natural body around a much larger central mass (the Earth, in our case), whose motion is governed by **Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation** and constrained by **Kepler's Three Laws of Planetary Motion**. The **orbital equations** are the mathematical relations (position, velocity, and period as functions of time) that completely describe this motion.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stone on a String"

Imagine tying a small stone to a piece of string and swinging it in a circle above your head. The string pulls the stone inward (toward your hand), constantly changing its direction. Now let go — and the stone flies off tangentially. 

In a satellite orbit:
- The **Earth's gravity** plays the role of the **string** — it constantly *pulls* the satellite inward.
- The satellite's **forward (tangential) velocity** plays the role of the **circular motion** — it tries to make the satellite fly off in a straight line.
- The balance between these two forces keeps the satellite perpetually *falling toward Earth* but *missing it*, producing a stable curved path — the **orbit**.

If the stone is swung *slowly*, it spirals inward; if *very fast*, it escapes. A satellite is like a stone thrown at *just the right speed* — neither falling back nor escaping.

### 1.2 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Earth's Standard Gravitational Parameter:**
> $$\mu \;=\; GM_E \;=\; 3.986 \times 10^{14} \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$$
> 
> where $G = 6.674 \times 10^{-11}\;\text{N·m}^2/\text{kg}^2$ is the universal gravitational constant and $M_E = 5.972 \times 10^{24}\;\text{kg}$ is Earth's mass.
> 
> **Mean Earth Radius:** $R_E = 6371\;\text{km}$
> 
> **Geostationary Orbit Radius:** $r_{GEO} = 42{,}164\;\text{km}$ (from Earth's centre)

### 1.3 Kepler's Three Laws — The Foundation of Orbital Mechanics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Johannes Kepler (1571–1630)** formulated three empirical laws by analysing Tycho Brahe's planetary observations. They form the **axiomatic basis** of all satellite orbit analysis.

| Law | Name | Statement (in KTU terminology) |
|---|---|---|
| **1st Law** | Law of Ellipses | The orbit of a satellite is an **ellipse** with the Earth's centre at **one focus**. |
| **2nd Law** | Law of Equal Areas | The line joining the satellite and the Earth's centre sweeps out **equal areas in equal times** ($\frac{dA}{dt} = \text{constant}$). |
| **3rd Law** | Law of Harmonies | The square of the orbital period is proportional to the cube of the **semi-major axis**: $T^2 \propto a^3$. |

### 1.4 Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Two bodies of masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ separated by a distance $r$ attract each other with a force:
> $$F \;=\; \frac{G\,m_1\,m_2}{r^2}$$
> 
> The associated **gravitational potential energy** is:
> $$U \;=\; -\frac{G\,m_1\,m_2}{r}$$

This single law, combined with Newton's second law ($F = ma$), is sufficient to derive *all* the orbital equations that follow.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Classical Orbital Elements (COOTWES)

To fully specify a satellite's orbit, **six parameters** are required. KTU examiners **frequently** ask for this list.

| # | Element | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Semi-major axis | $a$ | Half the longest diameter of the elliptical orbit |
| 2 | Eccentricity | $e$ | Shape measure; $e = 0$ circle, $0 < e < 1$ ellipse |
| 3 | Inclination | $i$ | Angle between orbit plane and Earth's equatorial plane |
| 4 | Right Ascension of Ascending Node (RAAN) | $\Omega$ | Angle from vernal equinox to ascending node (in equatorial plane) |
| 5 | Argument of Perigee | $\omega$ | Angle from ascending node to perigee (in orbit plane) |
| 6 | True Anomaly | $\nu$ | Angle from perigee to current satellite position |

> [!NOTE]
> Of these six, $a$ and $e$ define the **shape** of the orbit, $i$ and $\Omega$ define the **orientation of the orbit plane** in space, $\omega$ rotates the ellipse within that plane, and $\nu$ locates the **satellite's instantaneous position** along the orbit.

## 2.2 Geometric Relations for an Elliptical Orbit

Let $a$ = semi-major axis, $b$ = semi-minor axis, $e$ = eccentricity, $c$ = focal distance.

$$
c \;=\; a\,e
$$

$$
b \;=\; a\sqrt{1 - e^2}
$$

$$
r(\nu) \;=\; \frac{a\,(1 - e^2)}{1 + e\cos\nu} \quad \text{(Polar equation of the ellipse)}
$$

- **Perigee distance** (closest approach): $r_{per} = a(1 - e)$
- **Apogee distance** (farthest point): $r_{apo} = a(1 + e)$

## 2.3 Kepler's Third Law — Explicit Form

For any body orbiting Earth:

$$
T^2 \;=\; \frac{4\pi^2}{\mu}\,a^3
$$

$$
T \;=\; 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{a^3}{\mu}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Killer shortcut for KTU exams:** For a *geostationary* orbit ($T = 24$ hr = 86,400 s), this directly gives $a_{GEO} = 42{,}164$ km, hence the *orbital height* above Earth's surface is $h_{GEO} = a_{GEO} - R_E \approx 35{,}786$ km.

## 2.4 Vis-Viva Equation (The Most Important Equation in Orbital Mechanics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vis-Viva (Latin: "living force") equation** relates the satellite's speed $v$ to its current distance $r$ from Earth's centre:
> 
> $$v^2 \;=\; \mu\left(\frac{2}{r} - \frac{1}{a}\right)$$
> 
> This is **conservation of energy** in disguise. The satellite moves *fastest at perigee* and *slowest at apogee*.

## 2.5 Velocities at Perigee and Apogee

Using Vis-Viva:

$$
v_{per} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\mu\,(1 + e)}{a\,(1 - e)}}
$$

$$
v_{apo} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\mu\,(1 - e)}{a\,(1 + e)}}
$$

## 2.6 Special Velocities

| Velocity | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Circular orbital velocity** at radius $r$ | $v_c = \sqrt{\mu/r}$ | Speed for a perfectly circular orbit |
| **Escape velocity** at radius $r$ | $v_{esc} = \sqrt{2\mu/r}$ | Minimum speed to escape Earth's gravity ($v_{esc} = \sqrt{2}\,v_c$) |
| **Hohmann transfer** $\Delta v$ at perigee | $\Delta v_1 = v_{per}^{transfer} - v_{per}^{initial}$ | Required boost to change orbit |

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — KTU board exams routinely test 2–3 of these per question paper.**

| S.No. | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mu = GM_E = 3.986 \times 10^{14}\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$ | Gravitational parameter (Earth) |
| 2 | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{a^3/\mu}$ | Orbital period |
| 3 | $v^2 = \mu(2/r - 1/a)$ | Vis-Viva |
| 4 | $r_{per} = a(1-e)$, $r_{apo} = a(1+e)$ | Perigee/Apogee radii |
| 5 | $r(\nu) = a(1-e^2)/(1+e\cos\nu)$ | Radius at true anomaly $\nu$ |
| 6 | $v_{per} = \sqrt{\mu(1+e)/[a(1-e)]}$ | Velocity at perigee |
| 7 | $v_{apo} = \sqrt{\mu(1-e)/[a(1+e)]}$ | Velocity at apogee |
| 8 | $v_c = \sqrt{\mu/r}$ | Circular velocity |
| 9 | $v_{esc} = \sqrt{2\mu/r}$ | Escape velocity |
| 10 | $E = -\mu/(2a)$ | Total orbital energy (per unit mass) |
| 11 | $\frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\mu\,a(1-e^2)}$ | Areal velocity (Kepler's 2nd law) |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations are not academic exercises — they directly determine:

1. **Launch vehicle trajectory design** (ISRO's PSLV, GSLV).
2. **Station-keeping fuel budgets** for GEO communication satellites (INSAT, GSAT series).
3. **Hohmann transfer orbit** sizing for moving a satellite from LEO to GEO.
4. **Orbital lifetime prediction** for low-altitude Earth observation satellites.
5. **Inter-satellite link geometry** in constellations (e.g., OneWeb, Starlink).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Kepler's Third Law from Newton's Laws

We want to show that for a circular orbit (special case of ellipse), $T^2 \propto a^3$.

**Step 1 — Equate gravitational force to centripetal force** (Newton's 2nd law for circular motion):

For a satellite of mass $m$ at distance $r$ from Earth's centre, the only force is gravity:

$$
F_g \;=\; \frac{G M_E m}{r^2}
$$

This provides the centripetal force:

$$
F_c \;=\; \frac{m v^2}{r}
$$

Setting $F_g = F_c$:

$$
\frac{G M_E m}{r^2} \;=\; \frac{m v^2}{r}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the orbital speed:**

Cancel one $r$ and the mass $m$:

$$
v^2 \;=\; \frac{G M_E}{r} \;=\; \frac{\mu}{r}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the period:**

The satellite traverses a circumference $2\pi r$ at speed $v$:

$$
T \;=\; \frac{\text{distance}}{\text{speed}} \;=\; \frac{2\pi r}{v}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $v$ from Step 2:**

$$
T \;=\; \frac{2\pi r}{\sqrt{\mu/r}} \;=\; 2\pi r \sqrt{\frac{r}{\mu}} \;=\; 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{r^3}{\mu}}
$$

**Step 5 — Square both sides:**

$$
T^2 \;=\; \frac{4\pi^2}{\mu}\,r^3
$$

For a circular orbit, $r = a$ (semi-major axis), so:

$$
\boxed{\,T^2 \;=\; \frac{4\pi^2}{\mu}\,a^3\,}
$$

This is **Kepler's Third Law**, derived purely from Newton's laws. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Vis-Viva Equation (Conservation of Energy)

**Step 1 — Write total energy per unit mass:**

Total mechanical energy (per unit satellite mass) = Kinetic + Potential:

$$
E \;=\; \frac{1}{2}v^2 \;-\; \frac{\mu}{r}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate energy at perigee using the known perigee speed:**

At perigee, $r = r_{per} = a(1-e)$ and $v = v_{per}$:

$$
E_{per} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}v_{per}^2 \;-\; \frac{\mu}{a(1-e)}
$$

**Step 3 — Use angular momentum conservation to find $v_{per}$:**

For a central force, angular momentum $L = m r v_\perp$ is conserved. At perigee, velocity is purely tangential, so $v_\perp = v$:

$$
L \;=\; m\,r_{per}\,v_{per} \;=\; m\,a(1-e)\,v_{per}
$$

**Step 4 — Use Kepler's 2nd law to express $L$:**

The areal velocity is constant: $\frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{L}{2m}$. For the whole orbit, $\frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{\text{ellipse area}}{T} = \frac{\pi a b}{T}$. Equating and using $b = a\sqrt{1-e^2}$:

$$
\frac{L}{2m} \;=\; \frac{\pi a^2 \sqrt{1-e^2}}{T}
$$

Using $T^2 = 4\pi^2 a^3/\mu$ (from Kepler's 3rd), substitute and simplify:

$$
L \;=\; m\sqrt{\mu\,a(1-e^2)}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $v_{per}$:**

From $L = m\,a(1-e)\,v_{per}$:

$$
v_{per} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{\mu\,a(1-e^2)}}{a(1-e)} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\mu(1+e)}{a(1-e)}}
$$

**Step 6 — Plug back into energy equation:**

$$
E \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\mu(1+e)}{a(1-e)}\right) - \frac{\mu}{a(1-e)}
$$

Factor out $\frac{\mu}{2a(1-e)}$:

$$
E \;=\; \frac{\mu}{2a(1-e)}\left[(1+e) - 2\right] \;=\; \frac{\mu}{2a(1-e)}(e - 1) \;=\; -\frac{\mu}{2a}
$$

**Step 7 — Equate energies at general point and perigee:**

Since total energy is constant:

$$
\frac{1}{2}v^2 - \frac{\mu}{r} \;=\; -\frac{\mu}{2a}
$$

Rearrange:

$$
\boxed{\,v^2 \;=\; \mu\left(\frac{2}{r} - \frac{1}{a}\right)\,}
$$

This is the **Vis-Viva Equation**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Radius as a Function of True Anomaly

**Step 1 — Start from Newton's 2nd law in polar form:**

For a central force $F(r) = -\mu/r^2$, the equation of motion in polar coordinates is:

$$
\frac{d^2 u}{d\theta^2} + u \;=\; \frac{\mu}{L^2}
$$

where $u = 1/r$ and $\theta = \nu$ (true anomaly).

**Step 2 — Solve this linear ODE:**

The general solution is:

$$
u(\nu) \;=\; \frac{\mu}{L^2}\left[1 + e\cos(\nu - \nu_0)\right]
$$

By choosing the reference axis such that perigee is at $\nu = 0$, we get $\nu_0 = 0$:

$$
u(\nu) \;=\; \frac{\mu}{L^2}(1 + e\cos\nu)
$$

**Step 3 — Express $L^2$ in terms of $a$ and $e$:**

From previous results, $L^2 = \mu\,a(1-e^2)$. Substituting:

$$
\frac{1}{r} \;=\; \frac{\mu}{\mu\,a(1-e^2)}(1 + e\cos\nu) \;=\; \frac{1 + e\cos\nu}{a(1-e^2)}
$$

**Step 4 — Invert to get $r(\nu)$:**

$$
\boxed{\,r(\nu) \;=\; \frac{a(1-e^2)}{1 + e\cos\nu}\,}
$$

This is the **polar equation of the satellite's orbit**, valid for $0 \le e < 1$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem:** A satellite orbits Earth in an elliptical orbit with perigee altitude $h_{per} = 600$ km and apogee altitude $h_{apo} = 1200$ km. Determine: (a) semi-major axis, (b) eccentricity, (c) orbital period, (d) velocity at perigee and apogee, (e) total energy per unit mass.

**Given:**
- $h_{per} = 600$ km $\Rightarrow r_{per} = R_E + h_{per} = 6371 + 600 = 6971$ km $= 6.971 \times 10^6$ m
- $h_{apo} = 1200$ km $\Rightarrow r_{apo} = R_E + h_{apo} = 6371 + 1200 = 7571$ km $= 7.571 \times 10^6$ m
- $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14}\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$

### Part (a) — Semi-major axis:

$$
a \;=\; \frac{r_{per} + r_{apo}}{2} \;=\; \frac{6.971 \times 10^6 + 7.571 \times 10^6}{2} \;=\; 7.271 \times 10^6\;\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,a \;=\; 7271\;\text{km}\,}
$$

### Part (b) — Eccentricity:

Using $r_{per} = a(1-e)$:

$$
e \;=\; 1 - \frac{r_{per}}{a} \;=\; 1 - \frac{6.971}{7.271} \;=\; 1 - 0.9586 \;=\; 0.0414
$$

$$
\boxed{\,e \;\approx\; 0.0414\,}
$$

### Part (c) — Orbital period:

$$
T \;=\; 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{a^3}{\mu}} \;=\; 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{(7.271 \times 10^6)^3}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}}
$$

Compute numerator: $(7.271 \times 10^6)^3 = 3.844 \times 10^{20}\;\text{m}^3$

$$
\frac{a^3}{\mu} \;=\; \frac{3.844 \times 10^{20}}{3.986 \times 10^{14}} \;=\; 9.644 \times 10^5\;\text{s}^2
$$

$$
\sqrt{9.644 \times 10^5} \;=\; 982.0\;\text{s}
$$

$$
T \;=\; 2\pi \times 982.0 \;=\; 6171\;\text{s} \;\approx\; 102.85\;\text{minutes}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T \;\approx\; 102.85\;\text{minutes}\,}
$$

### Part (d) — Velocities at perigee and apogee:

**Perigee velocity:**

$$
v_{per} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\mu(1+e)}{a(1-e)}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 1.0414}{7.271 \times 10^6 \times 0.9586}}
$$

Numerator: $3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 1.0414 = 4.151 \times 10^{14}$

Denominator: $7.271 \times 10^6 \times 0.9586 = 6.971 \times 10^6$

$$
v_{per} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{4.151 \times 10^{14}}{6.971 \times 10^6}} \;=\; \sqrt{5.955 \times 10^7} \;=\; 7717\;\text{m/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,v_{per} \;\approx\; 7.72\;\text{km/s}\,}
$$

**Apogee velocity:**

$$
v_{apo} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\mu(1-e)}{a(1+e)}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 0.9586}{7.271 \times 10^6 \times 1.0414}}
$$

Numerator: $3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 0.9586 = 3.821 \times 10^{14}$

Denominator: $7.271 \times 10^6 \times 1.0414 = 7.572 \times 10^6$

$$
v_{apo} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{3.821 \times 10^{14}}{7.572 \times 10^6}} \;=\; \sqrt{5.046 \times 10^7} \;=\; 7103\;\text{m/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,v_{apo} \;\approx\; 7.10\;\text{km/s}\,}
$$

**Check via conservation of angular momentum:** $r_{per} v_{per} = r_{apo} v_{apo}$?

$6.971 \times 10^6 \times 7717 = 5.379 \times 10^{10}$ ✓

$7.571 \times 10^6 \times 7103 = 5.378 \times 10^{10}$ ✓ (matches to rounding)

### Part (e) — Total energy per unit mass:

$$
E \;=\; -\frac{\mu}{2a} \;=\; -\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14}}{2 \times 7.271 \times 10^6} \;=\; -2.741 \times 10^7\;\text{J/kg}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,E \;\approx\; -27.41\;\text{MJ/kg}\,}
$$

The negative sign confirms a **bound elliptical orbit** (satellite cannot escape).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Elliptical Orbit Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Earth - Focus F1] --- B[Perigee Point P]
    B --- C[Apogee Point A]
    A --- D[Empty Focus F2]
    C --- E[Semi-major axis a]
    B --- F[Semi-minor axis b]
    G[True Anomaly nu] --> A
    G --> B
    H[Perigee radius r_per = a1-e] --> A
    H --> B
    I[Apogee radius r_apo = a1+e] --> A
    I --> C

    classDef pointClass fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef focusClass fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef radiusClass fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef angleClass fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,D focusClass
    class B,C pointClass
    class E,F,H,I radiusClass
    class G angleClass
```

**Caption:** Geometry of an elliptical satellite orbit. Earth's centre occupies focus $F_1$; the other focus $F_2$ is empty. The satellite is fastest at **Perigee** ($P$) and slowest at **Apogee** ($A$).

## 4.2 Orbital Mechanics — Energy & Velocity Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Satellite in Orbit] --> B[Total Energy per unit mass E = -mu over 2a]
    A --> C[Velocity from Vis-Viva v squared = mu times 2 over r minus 1 over a]
    A --> D[Period from Kepler T = 2 pi sqrt a cubed over mu]
    A --> E[Areal Velocity dA by dt = 1 over 2 sqrt mu a 1 minus e squared]

    B --> F[Bound Orbit: E less than 0, e less than 1]
    B --> G[Parabolic Escape: E equals 0, e equals 1]
    B --> H[Hyperbolic Escape: E greater than 0, e greater than 1]

    C --> I[At Perigee: v_per = sqrt mu 1+e over a 1-e]
    C --> J[At Apogee: v_apo = sqrt mu 1-e over a 1+e]
    C --> K[For Circular Orbit: v_c = sqrt mu over r]

    D --> L[For Geostationary: T equals 24 hr, a equals 42164 km]
    D --> M[For LEO at 500 km: T approximately 95 min]

    classDef inputClass fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef resultClass fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef subClass fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A inputClass
    class B,C,D,E resultClass
    class F,G,H,I,J,K,L,M subClass
```

**Caption:** Functional architecture of orbital mechanics — the four governing relations (energy, velocity, period, areal velocity) that completely describe satellite motion.

## 4.3 Orbital Element Classification (COOTWES)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Six Classical Orbital Elements] --> B[Shape Parameters]
    A --> C[Plane Orientation]
    A --> D[Position in Orbit]

    B --> B1[Semi-major axis a]
    B --> B2[Eccentricity e]

    C --> C1[Inclination i]
    C --> C2[Right Ascension of Ascending Node RAAN Omega]

    D --> D1[Argument of Perigee omega]
    D --> D2[True Anomaly nu]

    B1 --> P1[a determines orbit size and energy]
    B2 --> P2[e equals 0 circle, 0 less than e less than 1 ellipse, e equals 1 parabola]

    C1 --> P3[i equals 0 equatorial, i equals 90 polar, i equals 0 to 180 inclined]
    C2 --> P4[Omega measured eastward from vernal equinox to ascending node]

    D1 --> P5[omega locates perigee in orbit plane]
    D2 --> P6[nu is time varying, locates satellite on orbit]

    classDef rootClass fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef groupClass fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef elemClass fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef propClass fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF

    class A rootClass
    class B,C,D groupClass
    class B1,B2,C1,C2,D1,D2 elemClass
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P6 propClass
```

**Caption:** COOTWES — the six classical orbital elements grouped by function: shape, plane orientation, and instantaneous position.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]** *[KTU University Exam - June 2024]*
> 
> **Q1.** State Kepler's three laws of planetary motion.

**Model Answer:**

> **Kepler's First Law (Law of Ellipses):** The orbit of every planet (or satellite) is an ellipse with the Sun (or Earth's centre) located at one of the two foci.
> 
> **Kepler's Second Law (Law of Equal Areas):** The line joining a satellite to the Earth's centre sweeps out equal areas in equal intervals of time. Mathematically, $\frac{dA}{dt} = \text{constant} = \frac{L}{2m}$.
> 
> **Kepler's Third Law (Law of Harmonies):** The square of the orbital period $T$ is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis $a$ of the orbit: $T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{\mu}a^3$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]** *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]*
> 
> **Q2.** What is the Vis-Viva equation? State its significance.

**Model Answer:**

> The Vis-Viva equation relates the orbital speed $v$ of a satellite to its instantaneous radial distance $r$ from the central body and the semi-major axis $a$ of its orbit:
> 
> $$v^2 \;=\; \mu\left(\frac{2}{r} - \frac{1}{a}\right)$$
> 
> **Significance:**
> 1. It expresses conservation of total orbital energy at any point in the orbit.
> 2. It allows computation of satellite velocity at *any* position once $a$, $r$, and $\mu$ are known.
> 3. It is the foundation for **Hohmann transfer** calculations and orbit determination in mission planning.
> 4. It is the only equation needed (along with Kepler's 3rd) for the majority of practical satellite orbit calculations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing perigee/apogee radii with altitudes.** Remember: $r_{per} = R_E + h_{per}$ (radius from Earth's **centre**, not surface).
> 2. **Wrong sign in Vis-Viva.** Forgetting that $-\frac{1}{a}$ is *subtracted* in the formula.
> 3. **Mixing up $a$ and $r$ in Kepler's 3rd Law.** $a$ is the **semi-major axis** of the ellipse; for circular orbits only is $a = r$.
> 4. **Units mismatch.** $\mu$ is in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$, distances in **metres** (not km), period in **seconds** (not hours). Always convert first.
> 5. **Forgetting the eccentricity factor** in $v_{per}$ and $v_{apo}$ — students often write $v_c = \sqrt{\mu/r}$ for elliptical cases, losing 2–3 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A]** *[KTU University Exam - June 2024]*
> 
> **Q.A.** (a) Derive Kepler's Third Law of planetary motion using Newton's Law of Gravitation. (7 marks)
> 
> (b) A satellite is in an elliptical orbit around Earth with perigee altitude of 500 km and apogee altitude of 1500 km. Calculate: (i) semi-major axis, (ii) eccentricity, (iii) orbital period, (iv) velocity at perigee. (7 marks)

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation of Kepler's Third Law:**

**Step 1:** The gravitational force between Earth (mass $M$) and satellite (mass $m$) at distance $r$:

$$
F_g \;=\; \frac{GMm}{r^2}
$$

**Step 2:** This force provides the centripetal force required for circular motion:

$$
\frac{GMm}{r^2} \;=\; \frac{mv^2}{r}
$$

**Step 3:** Solving for $v$:

$$
v \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{GM}{r}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r}}
$$

**Step 4:** The orbital period is the circumference divided by speed:

$$
T \;=\; \frac{2\pi r}{v} \;=\; 2\pi r \sqrt{\frac{r}{\mu}} \;=\; 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{r^3}{\mu}}
$$

**Step 5:** Squaring both sides, and noting that for a circular orbit $r = a$:

$$
\boxed{\,T^2 \;=\; \frac{4\pi^2}{\mu}a^3\,}
$$

This is Kepler's Third Law. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b) — Numerical computation:**

**Given:** $h_{per} = 500$ km, $h_{apo} = 1500$ km, $R_E = 6371$ km, $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14}\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$.

**Converting altitudes to radii:**

$$
r_{per} \;=\; 6371 + 500 \;=\; 6871\;\text{km} \;=\; 6.871 \times 10^6\;\text{m}
$$

$$
r_{apo} \;=\; 6371 + 1500 \;=\; 7871\;\text{km} \;=\; 7.871 \times 10^6\;\text{m}
$$

**(i) Semi-major axis:**

$$
a \;=\; \frac{r_{per} + r_{apo}}{2} \;=\; \frac{6.871 + 7.871}{2} \times 10^6 \;=\; 7.371 \times 10^6\;\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,a \;=\; 7371\;\text{km}\,}
$$

**(ii) Eccentricity:**

$$
e \;=\; \frac{r_{apo} - r_{per}}{r_{apo} + r_{per}} \;=\; \frac{7.871 - 6.871}{7.871 + 6.871} \;=\; \frac{1.000}{14.742} \;=\; 0.0678
$$

$$
\boxed{\,e \;\approx\; 0.0678\,}
$$

**(iii) Orbital period:**

$$
T \;=\; 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{a^3}{\mu}} \;=\; 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{(7.371 \times 10^6)^3}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}}
$$

$a^3 = 4.003 \times 10^{20}\;\text{m}^3$

$$
\frac{a^3}{\mu} \;=\; \frac{4.003 \times 10^{20}}{3.986 \times 10^{14}} \;=\; 1.0043 \times 10^6\;\text{s}^2
$$

$$
\sqrt{1.0043 \times 10^6} \;=\; 1002.1\;\text{s}
$$

$$
T \;=\; 2\pi \times 1002.1 \;=\; 6296\;\text{s} \;\approx\; 104.9\;\text{min}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T \;\approx\; 104.9\;\text{minutes}\,}
$$

**(iv) Velocity at perigee:**

$$
v_{per} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\mu(1+e)}{a(1-e)}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 1.0678}{7.371 \times 10^6 \times 0.9322}}
$$

Numerator: $4.256 \times 10^{14}$; Denominator: $6.871 \times 10^6$

$$
v_{per} \;=\; \sqrt{6.194 \times 10^7} \;=\; 7870\;\text{m/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,v_{per} \;\approx\; 7.87\;\text{km/s}\,}
$$

**(Total: 14 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question B]** *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]*
> 
> **Q.B.** (a) Define the Vis-Viva equation. Starting from conservation of total mechanical energy, derive the Vis-Viva equation for an elliptical orbit. (7 marks)
> 
> (b) A satellite in a circular orbit at 800 km altitude experiences a collision that puts it into an elliptical orbit with the same perigee altitude but apogee altitude of 2000 km. Compute: (i) the velocity in the original circular orbit, (ii) the new semi-major axis and eccentricity, (iii) the velocity at apogee of the new orbit, (iv) the new orbital period. (7 marks)

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation of Vis-Viva Equation:**

**Step 1:** The total mechanical energy per unit mass of a satellite at distance $r$ with speed $v$ is:

$$
E \;=\; \frac{1}{2}v^2 - \frac{\mu}{r}
$$

**Step 2:** Since the gravitational force is **conservative**, $E$ is constant throughout the orbit.

**Step 3:** Evaluate $E$ at the perigee ($r = r_p = a(1-e)$):

$$
E_p \;=\; \frac{1}{2}v_p^2 - \frac{\mu}{a(1-e)}
$$

**Step 4:** The perigee velocity is found from angular momentum conservation, $L = m r_p v_p$, with $L^2 = \mu m^2 a(1-e^2)$:

$$
v_p^2 \;=\; \frac{\mu(1+e)}{a(1-e)}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back:

$$
E_p \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\mu(1+e)}{a(1-e)} - \frac{\mu}{a(1-e)} \;=\; \frac{\mu}{2a(1-e)}\big[(1+e) - 2\big]
$$

$$
E_p \;=\; \frac{\mu(e-1)}{2a(1-e)} \;=\; -\frac{\mu}{2a}
$$

**Step 6:** Equating $E = E_p$ at a generic point:

$$
\frac{1}{2}v^2 - \frac{\mu}{r} \;=\; -\frac{\mu}{2a}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,v^2 \;=\; \mu\left(\frac{2}{r} - \frac{1}{a}\right)\,}
$$

This is the Vis-Viva equation. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b) — Numerical computation:**

**Given:** Original circular orbit at $h_0 = 800$ km; new orbit has $h_{per} = 800$ km, $h_{apo} = 2000$ km. $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$, $R_E = 6371$ km.

**Radii:**

$$
r_{per} \;=\; 6371 + 800 \;=\; 7171\;\text{km} \;=\; 7.171 \times 10^6\;\text{m}
$$

$$
r_{apo} \;=\; 6371 + 2000 \;=\; 8371\;\text{km} \;=\; 8.371 \times 10^6\;\text{m}
$$

**(i) Velocity in original circular orbit:**

$$
v_c \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r_{per}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14}}{7.171 \times 10^6}} \;=\; \sqrt{5.559 \times 10^7} \;=\; 7456\;\text{m/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,v_c \;\approx\; 7.46\;\text{km/s}\,}
$$

**(ii) New semi-major axis and eccentricity:**

$$
a \;=\; \frac{r_{per} + r_{apo}}{2} \;=\; \frac{7.171 + 8.371}{2} \times 10^6 \;=\; 7.771 \times 10^6\;\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,a \;=\; 7771\;\text{km}\,}
$$

$$
e \;=\; \frac{r_{apo} - r_{per}}{r_{apo} + r_{per}} \;=\; \frac{8.371 - 7.171}{8.371 + 7.171} \;=\; \frac{1.200}{15.542} \;=\; 0.0772
$$

$$
\boxed{\,e \;\approx\; 0.0772\,}
$$

**(iii) Velocity at apogee of new orbit:**

$$
v_{apo} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\mu(1-e)}{a(1+e)}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 0.9228}{7.771 \times 10^6 \times 1.0772}}
$$

Numerator: $3.679 \times 10^{14}$; Denominator: $8.371 \times 10^6$

$$
v_{apo} \;=\; \sqrt{4.395 \times 10^7} \;=\; 6629\;\text{m/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,v_{apo} \;\approx\; 6.63\;\text{km/s}\,}
$$

**(iv) New orbital period:**

$$
T \;=\; 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{a^3}{\mu}} \;=\; 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{(7.771 \times 10^6)^3}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}}
$$

$a^3 = 4.693 \times 10^{20}\;\text{m}^3$

$$
\frac{a^3}{\mu} \;=\; \frac{4.693 \times 10^{20}}{3.986 \times 10^{14}} \;=\; 1.1774 \times 10^6\;\text{s}^2
$$

$$
T \;=\; 2\pi \times 1085.1 \;=\; 6819\;\text{s} \;\approx\; 113.6\;\text{min}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T \;\approx\; 113.6\;\text{minutes}\,}
$$

**(Total: 14 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Know for KTU 2024 Exam:**

### ⚡ Core Laws
- [ ] **Kepler's 1st Law:** Elliptical orbit, Earth at one focus.
- [ ] **Kepler's 2nd Law:** Equal areas in equal times ($dA/dt = \text{const}$).
- [ ] **Kepler's 3rd Law:** $T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{\mu}a^3$ — bind this to memory.
- [ ] **Newton's Gravitation Law:** $F = \frac{GM_Em}{r^2}$ — basis of all derivations.

### ⚡ Critical Constants
- [ ] $\mu = GM_E = 3.986 \times 10^{14}\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$ (don't use $3.98$ or $3.96$).
- [ ] $R_E = 6371$ km (use this exact value unless problem states otherwise).
- [ ] $T_{GEO} = 86164$ s ≈ 24 hr (sidereal day, not solar day of 86400 s).
- [ ] $a_{GEO} = 42164$ km; $h_{GEO} = 35786$ km.

### ⚡ Six Orbital Elements
- [ ] $a$ (size), $e$ (shape), $i$ (inclination), $\Omega$ (RAAN), $\omega$ (argument of perigee), $\nu$ (true anomaly).

### ⚡ Master Formulas
- [ ] Vis-Viva: $v^2 = \mu(2/r - 1/a)$ — *the most-tested equation*.
- [ ] Polar orbit: $r(\nu) = a(1-e^2)/(1+e\cos\nu)$.
- [ ] Perigee/apogee radii: $r_{per} = a(1-e)$, $r_{apo} = a(1+e)$.
- [ ] Perigee/apogee velocities: $v_{per/apo} = \sqrt{\mu(1\pm e)/[a(1\mp e)]}$.
- [ ] Total energy: $E = -\mu/(2a)$ — *negative* always for bound orbits.
- [ ] Escape velocity: $v_{esc} = \sqrt{2}\,v_c$ — *exactly* $\sqrt{2}$ times circular velocity.

### ⚡ Common Mistake Traps
- [ ] Altitude vs. radius: $r = R_E + h$ (always).
- [ ] Circular velocity uses $r$, but elliptical Vis-Viva uses $r$ *and* $a$.
- [ ] For a circular orbit, $a = r$, so Vis-Viva reduces to $v^2 = \mu/r$. ✓
- [ ] Unit conversions: **m** for distance, **s** for time, **m/s** for velocity.
- [ ] Geostationary orbit requires $T = 24$ hr *sidereal* AND $i = 0$ AND $e = 0$ (all three!).

### ⚡ Engineering Relevance
- [ ] Hohmann transfer = 2-impulse ellipse between two circular orbits.
- [ ] Station-keeping fuel budget depends directly on $v_{per}$ and $v_{apo}$ variations.
- [ ] LEO altitude (~500–1500 km) → $T \approx 90$–110 min.
- [ ] MEO (~20000 km) used by GPS ($T \approx 12$ hr).
- [ ] GEO (~36000 km) used by communication satellites ($T = 24$ hr).

> **End of Module 1 — Satellite Orbit and Orbital Equations**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kepler's Laws of Planetary Motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Kepler's Laws of Planetary Motion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Kepler's Laws

In the year **1609–1619**, German mathematician and astronomer **Johannes Kepler** formulated three revolutionary laws of planetary motion based on the meticulous observational data of his mentor, **Tycho Brahe**. These laws stripped away the ancient Greek notion of perfectly circular celestial orbits and established the **elliptical geometry** that truly governs all gravitational motion — from planets around the Sun to artificial satellites around the Earth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (OEECT832 – Module 1):**
> Kepler's Laws form the **mathematical foundation** for understanding satellite orbital mechanics. Every orbital parameter used in satellite communication — apogee, perigee, orbital period, mean motion, and orbital velocity — is a direct descendant of Kepler's original work.

For the KTU 2024 scheme, students must understand that **all three laws are universally applicable** to:
- Natural satellites (Moon around Earth)
- Artificial satellites (INSAT, GSAT, IRNSS around Earth)
- Any two-body system bound by an inverse-square gravitational force

---

## 1.2 Formal Statement of Kepler's Three Laws

> [!NOTE]
> **Kepler's First Law (Law of Ellipses, 1609):**
> *All planets move in elliptical orbits with the Sun located at one of the two foci of the ellipse.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Kepler's Second Law (Law of Equal Areas, 1609):**
> *The line joining a planet to the Sun (the radius vector) sweeps out equal areas in equal intervals of time.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Kepler's Third Law (Law of Harmonies, 1619):**
> *The square of the orbital period ($T^2$) of a planet is directly proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis ($a^3$) of its elliptical orbit.*

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are spinning a ball attached to a string around your hand in a **circular path**. If you pull the string tighter at one moment and loosen it at another, the ball traces an **ellipse** — and your hand sits at one focus. This is exactly how a satellite moves around the Earth!

| Celestial Body | Central Focus | Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| Earth orbiting the Sun | Sun at one focus | Sling/skateboard track |
| Moon orbiting Earth | Earth at one focus | Spinning on a stretched string |
| Communication satellite (LEO/GEO) | Earth's center at one focus | A ball on a stretched rubber band |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Picture a **comet** like Halley's Comet. It zooms close to the Sun (perihelion) at breakneck speed, then drifts slowly far away (aphelion). Kepler's Second Law says it sweeps **equal areas in equal times** — meaning it must move *faster* when close to the Sun to compensate for the smaller triangle it draws. The same applies to a LEO satellite moving fast at perigee and slow at apogee.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KTU 2024)

- **Universal Gravitational Constant:** $G = 6.674 \times 10^{-11} \ \text{N·m}^2/\text{kg}^2$
- **Mass of Earth:** $M_E = 5.972 \times 10^{24} \ \text{kg}$
- **Earth's Standard Gravitational Parameter:** $\mu = GM_E = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$
- **Mean Radius of Earth:** $R_E = 6371 \ \text{km}$ (approximately)
- **Eccentricity of a circular orbit:** $e = 0$
- **Eccentricity of Earth's actual orbit:** $e \approx 0.0167$ (nearly circular)
- **Eccentricity of a highly elliptical Molniya orbit:** $e \approx 0.74$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Kepler's First Law — The Law of Ellipses

### 2.1.1 Geometric Foundation of the Ellipse

An **ellipse** is the locus of points such that the **sum of the distances** to two fixed points (the **foci**, $F_1$ and $F_2$) is a constant equal to **$2a$** (the major axis length).

For a satellite orbiting Earth (treating Earth as a point mass at one focus $F_1$):

$$r_1 + r_2 = 2a$$

where:
- $r_1$ = distance from the satellite to focus $F_1$ (true focus)
- $r_2$ = distance from the satellite to focus $F_2$ (empty focus)
- $a$ = semi-major axis

### 2.1.2 Orbital Terminology (KTU Must-Know)

| Term | Symbol | Definition | Significance in Satellite Communication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Semi-major axis | $a$ | Half of the longest diameter | Determines orbital energy & period |
| Semi-minor axis | $b$ | Half of the shortest diameter | Determines orbit "roundness" |
| Eccentricity | $e$ | $e = c/a = \sqrt{1 - b^2/a^2}$ | $e = 0 \Rightarrow$ circle; $e \to 1 \Rightarrow$ parabola |
| Perigee | $r_p$ | Closest point to Earth | $r_p = a(1 - e)$ — for Earth orbits |
| Apogee | $r_a$ | Farthest point from Earth | $r_a = a(1 + e)$ — for Earth orbits |
| True anomaly | $\nu$ | Angle from perigee to satellite position | Locates satellite in orbit |
| Mean motion | $n$ | Average angular velocity | $n = 2\pi/T$ |

### 2.1.3 The Polar Equation of an Orbit (Kepler's Equation in Polar Form)

The **radius vector** $r$ (distance from Earth's center to the satellite) as a function of the **true anomaly** $\nu$ is given by:

$$r = \frac{a(1 - e^2)}{1 + e\cos\nu}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most important equation in orbital mechanics** for KTU 2024. When $\nu = 0$ (satellite at perigee), $r$ is minimum. When $\nu = \pi$ (satellite at apogee), $r$ is maximum.

---

## 2.2 Kepler's Second Law — The Law of Equal Areas

### 2.2.1 Mathematical Statement

The **areal velocity** $\frac{dA}{dt}$ of the radius vector is **constant** throughout the orbit:

$$\frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{1}{2} r^2 \frac{d\nu}{dt} = \text{constant} = \frac{L}{2m}$$

where:
- $L$ = angular momentum of the satellite (constant for a central force)
- $m$ = mass of the satellite
- $r$ = instantaneous orbital radius
- $\frac{d\nu}{dt}$ = instantaneous angular rate (swept angular velocity)

### 2.2.2 Engineering Consequence

A satellite moves **faster at perigee** (low altitude) and **slower at apogee** (high altitude). For a GEO station-keeping satellite in a near-circular orbit ($e \approx 0$), the velocity variation is small. For a **Molniya orbit** (highly elliptical, $e \approx 0.74$), velocity varies dramatically.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter in satellite communication?** For elliptical orbits, the satellite is "visible" to a ground station for long durations near apogee (slow movement) — this is why **Molniya and Tundra orbits** are used by Russia for high-latitude communication coverage. The satellite "dwells" at apogee.

---

## 2.3 Kepler's Third Law — The Law of Harmonies

### 2.3.1 The Universal Form (with Gravitational Constant)

For two bodies of masses $M$ (central) and $m$ (orbiting), the orbital period $T$ and semi-major axis $a$ are related by:

$$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{G(M+m)} a^3 \approx \frac{4\pi^2}{\mu} a^3 \quad (\text{since } m \ll M)$$

For Earth-orbiting satellites, $M = M_E$ and $\mu = GM_E = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$:

$$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 a^3}{\mu}$$

### 2.3.2 The Mean Motion

The **mean motion** $n$ (in rad/s) is the constant average angular velocity:

$$n = \frac{2\pi}{T} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{a^3}}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD** — The table below contains every equation KTU expects you to know for Module 1 problems.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r = \dfrac{a(1-e^2)}{1+e\cos\nu}$ | Polar (Keplerian) orbit equation | $r$ in m, $a$ in m, $\nu$ in rad |
| 2 | $r_p = a(1-e)$ | Perigee radius (Earth orbit) | Distance from Earth's center |
| 3 | $r_a = a(1+e)$ | Apogee radius (Earth orbit) | Distance from Earth's center |
| 4 | $e = \dfrac{r_a - r_p}{r_a + r_p}$ | Eccentricity from perigee/apogee | Dimensionless, $0 \le e < 1$ |
| 5 | $a = \dfrac{r_a + r_p}{2}$ | Semi-major axis | Geometric mean axis |
| 6 | $T^2 = \dfrac{4\pi^2 a^3}{\mu}$ | Kepler's Third Law | $\mu = GM_E$ for Earth |
| 7 | $n = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{a^3}}$ | Mean motion | rad/s |
| 8 | $V_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{r_p}(1+e)}$ | Velocity at perigee | m/s |
| 9 | $V_a = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{r_a}(1-e)}$ | Velocity at apogee | m/s |
| 10 | $\dfrac{dA}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\mu a(1-e^2)}$ | Constant areal velocity | m²/s |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Kepler's Laws |
|---|---|
| **Geostationary (GEO) Satellite Design** | Kepler's Third Law sets the exact altitude ($a \approx 42164$ km) for a 24-hour period. |
| **LEO Constellations (Starlink, OneWeb)** | Period $T \approx 95$ min for 550 km altitude — derived from $T^2 \propto a^3$. |
| **MOLNIYA / TUNDRA Orbits** | High $e$ chosen so apogee dwells over high latitudes (Kepler's 2nd Law). |
| **GPS / IRNSS / NAVIC** | Precise orbital period calculations for atomic-clock synchronization. |
| **Orbit Determination (Ground Tracking)** | Two observations of position + time → solve for $a, e, T$ using Kepler's equations. |
| **Interplanetary Missions (ISRO, NASA)** | Hohmann transfer orbits between planets use Keplerian ellipses. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Kepler's Third Law from Newton's Law of Gravitation

This derivation is **the single most important derivation for KTU 2024 Module 1** and is frequently asked as a 14-mark question.

### Step 1: Start with Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation

The gravitational force between Earth (mass $M_E$) and satellite (mass $m$) at distance $r$:

$$F_g = \frac{G M_E m}{r^2}$$

### Step 2: Recognize that for a circular orbit, Gravity provides Centripetal Force

For circular motion, the required centripetal force is:

$$F_c = \frac{m v^2}{r} = m \omega^2 r = m \left(\frac{2\pi}{T}\right)^2 r$$

### Step 3: Equate the Two Forces (Circular Orbit Special Case)

$$\frac{m v^2}{r} = \frac{G M_E m}{r^2}$$

This gives us the **orbital velocity** $v$ for a circular orbit:

$$v^2 = \frac{G M_E}{r} = \frac{\mu}{r}$$

### Step 4: Express Velocity in Terms of Period

The satellite travels one circumference $2\pi r$ in time $T$:

$$v = \frac{2\pi r}{T}$$

### Step 5: Substitute and Square

Substituting $v$ into the orbital velocity equation:

$$\left(\frac{2\pi r}{T}\right)^2 = \frac{\mu}{r}$$

$$\frac{4\pi^2 r^2}{T^2} = \frac{\mu}{r}$$

### Step 6: Rearrange to Isolate $T^2$

Cross-multiplying:

$$4\pi^2 r^2 \cdot r = \mu \cdot T^2$$

$$4\pi^2 r^3 = \mu T^2$$

### Step 7: Final Form of Kepler's Third Law

$$\boxed{T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 r^3}{\mu} = \frac{4\pi^2 a^3}{GM_E}}$$

For an **elliptical orbit**, this generalizes by replacing $r$ with the semi-major axis $a$ (this is the *vis-viva* extension), giving the full Keplerian form.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Orbital Velocity using Vis-Viva Equation

The **vis-viva equation** is the energy-conservation statement that yields satellite speed at any point in the orbit.

### Step 1: Total Energy of the Satellite

For an elliptical orbit, total specific mechanical energy $\mathcal{E}$ is:

$$\mathcal{E} = \frac{v^2}{2} - \frac{\mu}{r} = -\frac{\mu}{2a}$$

This is a constant of motion (independent of position $r$).

### Step 2: Solve for $v^2$

$$v^2 = \frac{2\mu}{r} - \frac{\mu}{a}$$

### Step 3: Express in Standard Form (Vis-Viva Equation)

$$\boxed{v^2 = \mu \left(\frac{2}{r} - \frac{1}{a}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Vis-Viva** is Latin for *"living force"* — so named because it represents the orbital "energy of motion." It is universally valid for any point $r$ in any Keplerian orbit.

### Step 4: Special Cases for KTU Board Exams

**Case 1 — Perigee velocity** (substitute $r = r_p = a(1-e)$):

$$V_p^2 = \mu \left(\frac{2}{a(1-e)} - \frac{1}{a}\right) = \frac{\mu}{a} \cdot \frac{1 + e}{1 - e}$$

$$V_p = \sqrt{\frac{\mu(1+e)}{a(1-e)}}$$

**Case 2 — Apogee velocity** (substitute $r = r_a = a(1+e)$):

$$V_a^2 = \mu \left(\frac{2}{a(1+e)} - \frac{1}{a}\right) = \frac{\mu}{a} \cdot \frac{1 - e}{1 + e}$$

$$V_a = \sqrt{\frac{\mu(1-e)}{a(1+e)}}$$

**Case 3 — Circular orbit** ($e = 0$, $r = a$):

$$v_c = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{a}} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Constant Areal Velocity (Kepler's Second Law)

### Step 1: In a small time $dt$, the satellite sweeps a triangular area

The infinitesimal area swept is:

$$dA = \frac{1}{2} r \cdot (r \, d\nu) = \frac{1}{2} r^2 \, d\nu$$

### Step 2: Divide by $dt$

$$\frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{1}{2} r^2 \frac{d\nu}{dt}$$

### Step 3: Angular Momentum Conservation

For a central force, angular momentum $L = m r^2 \frac{d\nu}{dt}$ is constant. So:

$$\frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{L}{2m} = \text{constant}$$

### Step 4: Express in terms of orbital elements

Using $L = m \sqrt{\mu a (1-e^2)}$:

$$\frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\mu a (1-e^2)}$$

> [!TIP]
> This means the **total orbit area** $\pi a b = \pi a^2 \sqrt{1-e^2}$ is swept in period $T$:

$$\frac{\pi a^2 \sqrt{1-e^2}}{T} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\mu a (1-e^2)}$$

This rearranges back to **$T^2 = 4\pi^2 a^3 / \mu$** — confirming the consistency of all three laws!

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style Problem)

> **Problem:** A satellite is in an elliptical orbit around Earth with perigee altitude $h_p = 600$ km and apogee altitude $h_a = 3600$ km. Calculate:
> (a) Semi-major axis $a$
> (b) Eccentricity $e$
> (c) Orbital period $T$
> (d) Velocity at perigee and apogee

**Given:**
- $h_p = 600$ km $\Rightarrow r_p = R_E + h_p = 6371 + 600 = 6971$ km $= 6.971 \times 10^6$ m
- $h_a = 3600$ km $\Rightarrow r_a = R_E + h_a = 6371 + 3600 = 9971$ km $= 9.971 \times 10^6$ m
- $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$

### Solution (Step-by-Step)

**(a) Semi-major axis:**

$$a = \frac{r_p + r_a}{2} = \frac{6.971 \times 10^6 + 9.971 \times 10^6}{2}$$

$$a = \frac{16.942 \times 10^6}{2} = 8.471 \times 10^6 \ \text{m} = 8471 \ \text{km}$$

**(b) Eccentricity:**

$$e = \frac{r_a - r_p}{r_a + r_p} = \frac{9.971 - 6.971}{9.971 + 6.971} = \frac{3.000}{16.942} = 0.1771$$

**(c) Orbital period:**

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{a^3}{\mu}}$$

$$a^3 = (8.471 \times 10^6)^3 = 6.080 \times 10^{20} \ \text{m}^3$$

$$\frac{a^3}{\mu} = \frac{6.080 \times 10^{20}}{3.986 \times 10^{14}} = 1.5255 \times 10^6 \ \text{s}^2$$

$$\sqrt{\frac{a^3}{\mu}} = 1235.1 \ \text{s}$$

$$T = 2\pi \times 1235.1 = 7761 \ \text{s} \approx 129.4 \ \text{minutes}$$

**(d) Velocities:**

$$V_p = \sqrt{\frac{\mu(1+e)}{a(1-e)}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 1.1771}{8.471 \times 10^6 \times 0.8229}}$$

$$V_p = \sqrt{\frac{4.692 \times 10^{14}}{6.971 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{6.730 \times 10^7} \approx 8204 \ \text{m/s}$$

$$V_a = \sqrt{\frac{\mu(1-e)}{a(1+e)}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 0.8229}{8.471 \times 10^6 \times 1.1771}}$$

$$V_a = \sqrt{\frac{3.280 \times 10^{14}}{9.971 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{3.290 \times 10^7} \approx 5736 \ \text{m/s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification (Kepler's 2nd Law check):** $V_p \cdot r_p = 8204 \times 6.971 \times 10^6 = 5.719 \times 10^{10}$
> $V_a \cdot r_a = 5736 \times 9.971 \times 10^6 = 5.720 \times 10^{10}$ ✓ (Angular momentum is conserved!)

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State Kepler's three laws of planetary motion.**

**Model Answer:**

**Kepler's First Law (Law of Ellipses):** All planets move in elliptical orbits with the Sun at one focus of the ellipse. Mathematically, $r = \dfrac{a(1-e^2)}{1 + e\cos\nu}$.

**Kepler's Second Law (Law of Equal Areas):** The radius vector joining a planet to the Sun sweeps out equal areas in equal intervals of time, i.e., $\dfrac{dA}{dt} = \text{constant}$.

**Kepler's Third Law (Law of Harmonies):** The square of the orbital period is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis: $T^2 \propto a^3$, or $T^2 = \dfrac{4\pi^2 a^3}{\mu}$ for Earth-orbiting satellites.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**A satellite is at a perigee distance of $r_p = 7000$ km from Earth's centre. If the orbit has eccentricity $e = 0.2$, find the apogee distance and semi-major axis.**

**Model Answer:**

Given: $r_p = 7000$ km, $e = 0.2$

Apogee distance:

$$r_a = \frac{r_p (1+e)}{1-e} = \frac{7000 \times 1.2}{0.8} = 10500 \ \text{km}$$

Semi-major axis:

$$a = \frac{r_a + r_p}{2} = \frac{10500 + 7000}{2} = 8750 \ \text{km}$$

**Answer:** $r_a = 10500$ km, $a = 8750$ km.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — WITH INTERNAL CHOICE

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** State and explain Kepler's three laws of planetary motion. Derive Kepler's third law from Newton's law of gravitation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A communication satellite is in an elliptical orbit with perigee altitude $h_p = 1000$ km and apogee altitude $h_a = 5000$ km above Earth's surface. Calculate:
- (i) Semi-major axis
- (ii) Eccentricity
- (iii) Orbital period
- (iv) Perigee and apogee velocities
Take $R_E = 6371$ km and $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Statement and Derivation:**

- **First Law:** Elliptical orbits, Sun at one focus (already discussed in Section 1.2).
- **Second Law:** Equal areas in equal time (already discussed in Section 1.2).
- **Third Law:** $T^2 = \dfrac{4\pi^2 a^3}{\mu}$.

**Derivation** (full step-by-step as in Section 3.1):

Step 1: Equate gravitational force to centripetal force for a circular orbit: $\dfrac{GM_E m}{r^2} = \dfrac{mv^2}{r}$

Step 2: This gives $v^2 = \dfrac{\mu}{r}$ (for $\mu = GM_E$).

Step 3: Velocity in terms of period: $v = \dfrac{2\pi r}{T}$

Step 4: Substituting: $\dfrac{4\pi^2 r^2}{T^2} = \dfrac{\mu}{r}$

Step 5: Rearranging: $T^2 r = \dfrac{4\pi^2 r^3}{\mu}$

Step 6: Generalizing to ellipse: replace $r$ with $a$:

$$\boxed{T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 a^3}{\mu}}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- $h_p = 1000$ km $\Rightarrow r_p = 6371 + 1000 = 7371$ km $= 7.371 \times 10^6$ m
- $h_a = 5000$ km $\Rightarrow r_a = 6371 + 5000 = 11371$ km $= 1.1371 \times 10^7$ m
- $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$

**(i) Semi-major axis:**

$$a = \frac{r_p + r_a}{2} = \frac{7.371 \times 10^6 + 1.1371 \times 10^7}{2}$$

$$a = \frac{1.8742 \times 10^7}{2} = 9.371 \times 10^6 \ \text{m} = 9371 \ \text{km}$$

**(ii) Eccentricity:**

$$e = \frac{r_a - r_p}{r_a + r_p} = \frac{1.1371 \times 10^7 - 7.371 \times 10^6}{1.1371 \times 10^7 + 7.371 \times 10^6}$$

$$e = \frac{4.000 \times 10^6}{1.8742 \times 10^7} = 0.2134$$

**(iii) Orbital period:**

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{a^3}{\mu}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{(9.371 \times 10^6)^3}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}}$$

$$a^3 = 8.231 \times 10^{20} \ \text{m}^3$$

$$\frac{a^3}{\mu} = \frac{8.231 \times 10^{20}}{3.986 \times 10^{14}} = 2.065 \times 10^6 \ \text{s}^2$$

$$T = 2\pi \times 1437.0 = 9030.6 \ \text{s} \approx 150.5 \ \text{min}$$

**(iv) Velocities:**

$$V_p = \sqrt{\frac{\mu(1+e)}{a(1-e)}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 1.2134}{9.371 \times 10^6 \times 0.7866}}$$

$$V_p = \sqrt{\frac{4.836 \times 10^{14}}{7.371 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{6.561 \times 10^7} \approx 8100 \ \text{m/s}$$

$$V_a = \sqrt{\frac{\mu(1-e)}{a(1+e)}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 0.7866}{9.371 \times 10^6 \times 1.2134}}$$

$$V_a = \sqrt{\frac{3.135 \times 10^{14}}{1.137 \times 10^7}} = \sqrt{2.757 \times 10^7} \approx 5251 \ \text{m/s}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain Kepler's first and second laws. Discuss how Kepler's second law leads to the conservation of angular momentum. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a geostationary satellite, calculate:
- (i) The orbital radius
- (ii) The orbital velocity
- (iii) The altitude above Earth's surface
- (iv) The escape velocity from this orbit

Given: $T = 24$ hours, $R_E = 6371$ km, $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Kepler's First and Second Laws with Diagrams and Angular Momentum:**

- **First Law:** The orbit of a planet (or satellite) is an ellipse with the central body at one focus. (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram.)
- **Second Law:** Equal areas in equal times. (Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram.)

**Connection to Angular Momentum Conservation:**

For a satellite at position $r$ moving with tangential velocity $v_\theta$, the angular momentum is:

$$L = m r v_\theta = m r^2 \frac{d\nu}{dt}$$

The areal velocity is:

$$\frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{1}{2} r^2 \frac{d\nu}{dt} = \frac{L}{2m}$$

Since gravity is a **central force** (always directed along the line joining the two bodies), it exerts **no torque** on the satellite:

$$\tau = \frac{dL}{dt} = 0 \implies L = \text{constant}$$

Therefore, $\dfrac{dA}{dt} = \text{constant}$, which **is** Kepler's Second Law. QED.

**(b) Geostationary Satellite Calculations:**

Given: $T = 24 \ \text{h} = 86400 \ \text{s}$, $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$

**(i) Orbital radius (from Kepler's Third Law):**

$$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 a^3}{\mu} \implies a^3 = \frac{\mu T^2}{4\pi^2}$$

$$a^3 = \frac{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times (86400)^2}{4\pi^2}$$

$$a^3 = \frac{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 7.46496 \times 10^9}{39.478}$$

$$a^3 = \frac{2.9755 \times 10^{24}}{39.478} = 7.537 \times 10^{22} \ \text{m}^3$$

$$a = (7.537 \times 10^{22})^{1/3} = 4.2164 \times 10^7 \ \text{m} \approx 42164 \ \text{km}$$

**(ii) Orbital velocity:**

$$v = \frac{2\pi a}{T} = \frac{2\pi \times 4.2164 \times 10^7}{86400}$$

$$v = \frac{2.649 \times 10^8}{86400} \approx 3066 \ \text{m/s}$$

**(iii) Altitude above Earth's surface:**

$$h = a - R_E = 42164 - 6371 = 35793 \ \text{km}$$

**(iv) Escape velocity from this orbit:**

The escape velocity from distance $r$ is:

$$v_{esc} = \sqrt{\frac{2\mu}{r}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 3.986 \times 10^{14}}{4.2164 \times 10^7}}$$

$$v_{esc} = \sqrt{1.8906 \times 10^7} \approx 4348 \ \text{m/s}$$

Note: $v_{esc} = \sqrt{2} \cdot v_{orbit} = 1.414 \times 3066 \approx 4335$ m/s ✓ (matches within rounding)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Unit Mistakes:** Mixing km with m in the same equation. Always convert ALL distances to metres before plugging into $\mu$ (which is in m³/s²).
> 2. **Forgetting to add $R_E$:** When given *altitude* (e.g., $h_p = 600$ km), you MUST add $R_E = 6371$ km to get the *orbital radius* $r_p$. KTU examiners deduct **2 full marks** for this.
> 3. **Mean Motion Confusion:** Mean motion $n$ is in **radians/second**, not degrees or revolutions. Convert carefully.
> 4. **Circular Orbit Assumption Trap:** If $e = 0$ is not explicitly given, do NOT assume a circular orbit.
> 5. **Period vs Frequency:** $T$ is in seconds, $f = 1/T$ is in Hz. The question may ask for orbital frequency — read carefully.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Module 1**

### Three Laws (One-Liners)
- **Law 1:** Ellipse with central body at one focus.
- **Law 2:** Equal areas in equal times → angular momentum conservation.
- **Law 3:** $T^2 \propto a^3$ → period depends only on $a$.

### Must-Memorize Numbers
- $\mu_E = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$
- $R_E = 6371$ km
- GEO altitude = 35,786 km ($a = 42{,}164$ km)
- GEO period = 23h 56m 4s (sidereal day)
- LEO period ≈ 90–100 minutes
- ISS altitude ≈ 400 km

### Must-Memorize Formulas
- $r = \dfrac{a(1-e^2)}{1+e\cos\nu}$
- $r_p = a(1-e)$, $r_a = a(1+e)$
- $T = 2\pi\sqrt{a^3/\mu}$
- $v = \sqrt{\mu(2/r - 1/a)}$ (vis-viva)
- $V_p = \sqrt{\mu(1+e)/[a(1-e)]}$
- $V_a = \sqrt{\mu(1-e)/[a(1+e)]}$

### Conversion Traps
- 1 hour = 3600 s; 1 day = 86,400 s
- Altitude vs. orbital radius: Add $R_E$ to altitude to get radius.

### Conceptual Links
- Central force $\Rightarrow$ No torque $\Rightarrow$ Angular momentum conserved $\Rightarrow$ Kepler's 2nd law
- Energy equation (vis-viva) is the most general single-point velocity formula
- Kepler's 3rd law assumes $m \ll M$ (satellite mass negligible)
- For interplanetary transfers (Hohmann), use Keplerian ellipses between two circular orbits

> **End of Module 1 — Kepler's Laws of Planetary Motion Notes**
> **KTU 2024 Scheme | OEECT832 — Satellite and Radar Communication**

topics.title: Locating Satellite in the Orbit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Locating Satellite in the Orbit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Locating a satellite in its orbit** is the process of uniquely specifying the position and orientation of a satellite's orbital path with respect to a defined Earth-centered reference frame at a given epoch. In astrodynamics, this is accomplished using **six classical Keplerian orbital elements** (also called the *osculating elements*), which together form a complete, unambiguous mathematical descriptor of the satellite's trajectory, its size, shape, and spatial orientation in inertial space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The position of any satellite in space is fully described by a set of **six independent parameters** (Keplerian elements), three of which define the *size and shape* of the orbit, while the other three define its *orientation in space and instantaneous position of the satellite within the orbit*.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hanging Lamp Post"

Imagine a giant **Christmas ornament** (the satellite) hanging from a fixed point (Earth's center) by a rigid but invisible string of length $r$. Now tilt this ornament as it swings. To fully describe where it is, you need to tell someone:

1. **How long is the string?** → Defines the size of orbit (semi-major axis $a$)
2. **How oval is the loop?** → Defines the shape (eccentricity $e$)
3. **How much is the swing plane tilted relative to your room floor?** → Inclination $i$
4. **Which corner of the room does the swing start from?** → Right Ascension of Ascending Node $\Omega$
5. **Where on the loop is the lowest point of the swing?** → Argument of Perigee $\omega$
6. **Where exactly along the loop is the ornament right now?** → True anomaly $\nu$ (or Mean anomaly $M$)

These six numbers, taken together, locate the satellite **uniquely and exactly** in the vastness of space — just like a house address, postal code, and GPS coordinates identify your home uniquely.

### 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants Used

The following constants govern all Earth-orbiting satellite mechanics:

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Geocentric Gravitational Constant | $\mu = GM$ | $3.986 \times 10^{14}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$ |
| Mean Earth Radius | $R_E$ | $6.378 \times 10^{6}$ | m |
| Sidereal Day | $T_{sid}$ | $86164.0905$ | s |
| Earth's Rotation Rate | $\omega_E$ | $7.2921 \times 10^{-5}$ | rad/s |
| Astronomical Unit | AU | $1.496 \times 10^{11}$ | m |
| Standard Gravitational Accel. | $g_0$ | $9.80665$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Earth's equatorial bulge (oblateness factor $J_2 \approx 1.08263 \times 10^{-3}$)** is the dominant perturbation that makes the actual orbit deviate from a perfect Keplerian ellipse — but for initial "locating" we begin with the ideal two-body model.

### 1.4 Coordinate Reference Systems Used

- **ECI (Earth-Centered Inertial) Frame**: Fixed with respect to distant stars; used for defining orbital elements.
- **ECEF (Earth-Centered Earth-Fixed) Frame**: Rotates with the Earth; used for ground station pointing.
- **Topocentric Frame**: Local horizon system at the ground station (azimuth–elevation coordinates).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Classical Keplerian Orbital Elements

The **mathematical DNA** of an orbiting satellite consists of:

**A. Size & Shape Elements (3):**
1. **Semi-major axis ($a$)** — Half the longest diameter of the ellipse, in metres or km.
2. **Eccentricity ($e$)** — A dimensionless number $0 \le e < 1$ for bound orbits. $e=0$ → circle, $e\to1$ → parabola.
3. **Mean anomaly ($M$)** (or true anomaly $\nu$ / time of perigee passage $t_p$) — Positions the satellite along the orbit at a given epoch.

**B. Orientation Elements (3):**
4. **Inclination ($i$)** — Tilt of the orbital plane w.r.t. Earth's equator, $0° \le i \le 180°$.
5. **Right Ascension of Ascending Node ($\Omega$)** — Where the orbit crosses the equator going north, measured from the vernal equinox direction, $0° \le \Omega < 360°$.
6. **Argument of Perigee ($\omega$)** — Angle from ascending node to perigee measured in the orbital plane, $0° \le \omega < 360°$.

### 2.2 The Three Kepler's Laws (Foundational Pillars)

**Law 1 (Ellipses):** Every satellite orbit is a conic section with Earth's center at one focus.
$$\frac{a(1-e^2)}{r} = 1 + e\cos\nu$$

**Law 2 (Equal Areas):** The line joining the satellite and Earth sweeps equal areas in equal times — this is the principle of **conservation of angular momentum**.
$$\frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{h}{2} = \text{constant}$$

**Law 3 (Harmonic Law):** The square of the orbital period is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis.
$$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{\mu}a^3$$

### 2.3 Logical Flow of Locating a Satellite

1. **Define reference frame** (ECI by default).
2. **Set epoch $t_0$** — the moment at which all elements are tabulated.
3. **Propagate time** — using mean motion $n$, compute $M(t) = n(t - t_p)$.
4. **Solve Kepler's equation** $M = E - e\sin E$ iteratively for the eccentric anomaly $E$.
5. **Convert $E$ to true anomaly** $\nu$ via $\tan(\nu/2) = \sqrt{\frac{1+e}{1-e}}\tan(E/2)$.
6. **Compute radius** $r = a(1 - e\cos E)$.
7. **Rotate from orbital plane to inertial frame** using $i$, $\Omega$, $\omega$.
8. **Convert to ECEF** by accounting for Earth's rotation since the epoch.
9. **Apply topocentric transformation** to obtain look angles (azimuth, elevation, range) for a ground station.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{a^3}{\mu}}$ | Kepler's Third Law — Orbital Period | s |
| 2 | $n = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{a^3}}$ | Mean Motion (average angular speed) | rad/s |
| 3 | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{r}}$ | Instantaneous Orbital Speed (vis-viva) | m/s |
| 4 | $v_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{a}} = n \cdot a$ | Circular Orbital Speed | m/s |
| 5 | $r = \dfrac{a(1-e^2)}{1+e\cos\nu}$ | Radius Vector in Orbit | m |
| 6 | $r = a(1 - e\cos E)$ | Radius via Eccentric Anomaly | m |
| 7 | $h = \sqrt{\mu a (1-e^2)}$ | Specific Angular Momentum | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 8 | $M = n(t - t_p)$ | Mean Anomaly at time $t$ | rad |
| 9 | $M = E - e\sin E$ | Kepler's Equation | rad |
| 10 | $\tan\dfrac{\nu}{2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1+e}{1-e}}\tan\dfrac{E}{2}$ | Eccentric → True Anomaly | rad |
| 11 | $\sin\nu = \dfrac{\sqrt{1-e^2}\sin E}{1-e\cos E}$ | Alternate $\nu$ from $E$ | rad |
| 12 | $R_{asc} = R_E + h_{alt}$ | Geocentric radius of satellite | m |
| 13 | $d = \sqrt{R_E^2 + R_{asc}^2 - 2R_E R_{asc}\cos\psi}$ | Slant Range to satellite | m |
| 14 | $\cos\psi = \sin\phi_s \sin\phi_e + \cos\phi_s \cos\phi_e \cos(\lambda_s - \lambda_e)$ | Central angle (ground to sat) | rad |
| 15 | $E_{lat} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\tan\phi_s}{1 - \frac{R_E}{R_{asc}}^2}\right)$ | Sub-satellite point latitude | rad |
| 16 | $v_{esc} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\mu}{r}}$ | Escape Velocity | m/s |
| 17 | $h_p = a(1-e)$, $h_a = a(1+e)$ | Perigee / Apogee altitude | m |
| 18 | $\dot{\Omega} = -\dfrac{3}{2}J_2 R_E^2 n \dfrac{\cos i}{p^2}$ | Nodal regression (J2) | rad/s |
| 19 | $\dot{\omega} = \dfrac{3}{4}J_2 R_E^2 n \dfrac{5\cos^2 i - 1}{p^2}$ | Apsidal rotation (J2) | rad/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Utility:** The look-angle equations (slant range $d$, elevation $El$, azimuth $Az$) are used in real-time by ground-station antenna controllers (e.g., at ISRO's ISTRAC, NASA's Deep Space Network) to physically point a dish at the satellite as it transits the sky.

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Mission planning**: Allocating frequency, time, and orbital "slots" (GEO slot at $i=0$, $e=0$, $a=42164$ km).
- **Collision avoidance (Conjunction Screening)**: NORAD, CSpOC and SpaceX use TLE (Two-Line Element) data — which is just a numerical encoding of these six elements — to predict satellite positions.
- **Launch window calculation**: Determining when a launch must occur so the satellite reaches its target $\Omega$ and $\omega$.
- **Ground station handovers & tracking**: Antenna tracking algorithms (e.g., SGP4) iterate these formulas at ~Hz rates.
- **GNSS** (GPS, GLONASS, IRNSS, Galileo) directly transmits the orbital elements of each satellite in the navigation message, allowing your phone to compute its own 3-D position.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Kepler's Third Law (Orbital Period vs Semi-Major Axis)

**Statement:** $T^2 = \dfrac{4\pi^2 a^3}{\mu}$

**Step 1 — Newton's Law of Gravitation (inverse-square central force):**
The gravitational pull on a satellite of mass $m$ at distance $r$ is:
$$F_g = \frac{G M_E m}{r^2} = \frac{\mu m}{r^2}$$

**Step 2 — For a circular orbit, equate gravity to centripetal force:**
$$\frac{m v_c^2}{r} = \frac{\mu m}{r^2}$$
Dividing both sides by $m/r$:
$$v_c^2 = \frac{\mu}{r}$$

**Step 3 — Express velocity in terms of period and radius:**
A satellite traveling one circumference $2\pi r$ in time $T$ has:
$$v_c = \frac{2\pi r}{T} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad v_c^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 r^2}{T^2}$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two expressions of $v_c^2$ and cancel $r$:**
$$\frac{4\pi^2 r^2}{T^2} = \frac{\mu}{r}$$
$$4\pi^2 r^3 = \mu T^2$$
$$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 r^3}{\mu}$$

**Step 5 — Generalise from circle to ellipse using semi-major axis $a$:** For any conic orbit (Kepler's 1st law + Newton's extension), $r$ is replaced by $a$ because the period depends only on $a$, not on $e$:
$$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 a^3}{\mu} \qquad \text{Q.E.D.}$$

**Numerical verification (GEO satellite):**
$a = 42164\,\text{km} = 4.2164 \times 10^7$ m
$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{(4.2164 \times 10^7)^3}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}}$
$T \approx 86164$ s $\approx 23$ h $56$ min $4$ s (one sidereal day) ✓

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Vis-Viva Equation (Instantaneous Speed)

**Statement:** $v^2 = \mu\left(\dfrac{2}{r} - \dfrac{1}{a}\right)$

**Step 1 — Total orbital energy** is the sum of kinetic and potential energy (per unit mass):
$$\mathcal{E} = \frac{v^2}{2} - \frac{\mu}{r}$$

**Step 2 — By conservation of total energy along the orbit, $\mathcal{E}$ is constant and equals the specific orbital energy** which is uniquely determined by $a$:
$$\mathcal{E} = -\frac{\mu}{2a}$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $\mathcal{E}$:**
$$\frac{v^2}{2} - \frac{\mu}{r} = -\frac{\mu}{2a}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $v^2$:**
$$\frac{v^2}{2} = \frac{\mu}{r} - \frac{\mu}{2a} = \mu\left(\frac{1}{r} - \frac{1}{2a}\right) = \mu\left(\frac{2a - r}{2ar}\right)$$
$$v^2 = \mu\left(\frac{2}{r} - \frac{1}{a}\right) \qquad \text{Q.E.D.}$$

**Step 5 — Special cases:**
- Circular orbit ($r = a$): $v_c^2 = \mu/a$ ✓
- At perigee ($r = a(1-e)$): $v_p^2 = \mu(1+e)/[a(1-e)]$
- At apogee ($r = a(1+e)$): $v_a^2 = \mu(1-e)/[a(1+e)]$ → satellite moves slowest at apogee.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Locating Satellite in Inertial Coordinates from Six Elements

**Given:** $a$, $e$, $i$, $\Omega$, $\omega$, $\nu$ at epoch $t_0$.

**Step 1 — In the orbital (perifocal) frame $P$**, with $x$-axis toward perigee, the satellite's position vector is:
$$\vec{r}_{\text{orb}} = \begin{bmatrix} r\cos\nu \\ r\sin\nu \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
where $r = \dfrac{a(1-e^2)}{1+e\cos\nu}$.

**Step 2 — Rotate about the orbital $z$-axis (perifocal $z$) by argument of perigee $\omega$** to put the line of nodes along the new $x'$-axis. The rotation matrix is:
$$R_z(\omega) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\omega & -\sin\omega & 0 \\ \sin\omega & \cos\omega & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$\vec{r}_1 = R_z(\omega)\,\vec{r}_{\text{orb}}$$

**Step 3 — Rotate about the (new) $x$-axis by inclination $i$** to bring the orbital plane from the equatorial plane to its tilted orientation:
$$R_x(i) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos i & -\sin i \\ 0 & \sin i & \cos i \end{bmatrix}$$
$$\vec{r}_2 = R_x(i)\,\vec{r}_1$$

**Step 4 — Rotate about the (final) $z$-axis by $\Omega$** to align the ascending node with the vernal equinox direction:
$$R_z(\Omega) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\Omega & -\sin\Omega & 0 \\ \sin\Omega & \cos\Omega & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Combined transformation (ECI coordinates):**
$$\vec{r}_{\text{ECI}} = R_z(\Omega)\,R_x(i)\,R_z(\omega)\,\vec{r}_{\text{orb}}$$

The full matrix is:
$$\vec{r}_{\text{ECI}} = r \begin{bmatrix} c\Omega\,c\omega - s\Omega\,s\omega\,c i \\ s\Omega\,c\omega + c\Omega\,s\omega\,c i \\ s\omega\,s i \end{bmatrix} \begin{aligned}+\text{similar terms with }\nu \end{aligned}$$

(where $c\theta = \cos\theta$, $s\theta = \sin\theta$).

**Step 6 — Convert ECI → ECEF** by rotating about the Earth-fixed $z$-axis by the Greenwich Sidereal Time $\theta_G(t)$:
$$\vec{r}_{\text{ECEF}} = R_z(-\theta_G)\,\vec{r}_{\text{ECI}}$$

**Step 7 — Convert ECEF → Topocentric** at a ground station with geodetic latitude $\phi$, longitude $\lambda$, height $H$:
Apply the geodetic-to-ECEF rotation, then subtract the station's ECEF position vector to obtain the local $(\text{North}, \text{East}, \text{Up})$ coordinates, and finally compute:
$$\text{Elevation } El = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{z_{\text{topo}}}{\vert \vec{r}_{\text{topo}} \vert}\right) \qquad
\text{Azimuth } Az = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y_{\text{topo}}}{x_{\text{topo}}}\right)$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Slant Range $d$ to Satellite

**Geometry:** Ground station at geodetic position $(\phi_e, \lambda_e)$ observes satellite at sub-satellite point $(\phi_s, \lambda_s)$ with orbital radius $r$. The central angle $\psi$ between them is:
$$\cos\psi = \sin\phi_e \sin\phi_s + \cos\phi_e \cos\phi_s \cos(\lambda_s - \lambda_e)$$

Applying the law of cosines in the Earth-centered triangle:
$$d^2 = R_E^2 + r^2 - 2 R_E r \cos\psi$$
$$\boxed{\,d = \sqrt{R_E^2 + r^2 - 2 R_E r \cos\psi}\,}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Which Element Tells You What?

| Question you want answered | Keplerian Element used | Symbol |
|---------------------------|------------------------|--------|
| How big is the orbit? | Semi-major axis | $a$ |
| How stretched is the orbit? | Eccentricity | $e$ |
| How tilted is the orbit? | Inclination | $i$ |
| Where does it cross the equator going north? | Right ascension of ascending node | $\Omega$ |
| Where is the closest approach? | Argument of perigee | $\omega$ |
| Where is the satellite *right now*? | True anomaly / Mean anomaly | $\nu$, $M$ |
| How fast does it orbit? | Mean motion $n = \sqrt{\mu/a^3}$ | derived |
| How long is one revolution? | Period $T = 2\pi/n$ | derived |
| What is the ground velocity? | $v_g = r\dot{\lambda}\cos\phi$ | derived |

```mermaid

    A1 --> A2[Propagate to Current Time t]
    A2 --> A3[Compute Mean Anomaly M = n t-tp]
    A3 --> A4[Iteratively Solve Kepler Equation: M = E - e sin E]

    A5 --> A6[Compute Radius r = a 1-e cos E]

    A10 --> A11[Position in ECEF Frame]
    A11 --> A12[Subtract Ground Station Vector]
    A12 --> A13[Topocentric Position North East Up]
    A13 --> A14[Compute Azimuth Az and Elevation El and Slant Range d]
    A14 --> A15[Output: Pointing Angles for Tracking Antenna]
```

A clear diagram is worth 2–3 easy marks in the ESE.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper | Module 1]**

**Q1. List the six classical Keplerian elements used to locate a satellite in its orbit. Differentiate between mean anomaly and true anomaly.**

**Model Answer:**

The six Keplerian elements that uniquely locate a satellite are:
1. **Semi-major axis ($a$)** — defines the size of the orbit.
2. **Eccentricity ($e$)** — defines the shape.
3. **Inclination ($i$)** — defines tilt w.r.t. Earth's equator.
4. **Right Ascension of Ascending Node ($\Omega$)** — orientation of the orbit's line of nodes.
5. **Argument of Perigee ($\omega$)** — orientation of the ellipse within the orbital plane.
6. **True Anomaly ($\nu$)** at epoch — instantaneous position of the satellite.

**Mean Anomaly ($M$)** is a fictitious, uniformly increasing angle: $M = n(t - t_p)$, where $n$ is the mean motion. It is *linear* in time but has **no direct geometric meaning** in the orbit.

**True Anomaly ($\nu$)** is the actual angle measured at the Earth's centre between the perigee direction and the satellite's current position vector. It is *geometrically meaningful* but **non-uniform** in time (the satellite sweeps equal areas in equal times, so it moves faster near perigee than near apogee).

The two are linked through **Kepler's equation** $M = E - e\sin E$ and the relation $\tan(\nu/2) = \sqrt{(1+e)/(1-e)}\tan(E/2)$.

**[3 marks — KTU pattern: 1 mark list, 2 marks differentiation]**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper | Module 1]**

**Q2. Define (i) Apogee, (ii) Perigee, and (iii) Line of Nodes for a satellite orbit. Why is the line of nodes significant for locating a satellite?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Apogee:** The point on the orbit *farthest* from Earth's centre. Distance $r_a = a(1+e)$.
- **Perigee:** The point on the orbit *closest* to Earth's centre. Distance $r_p = a(1-e)$.
- **Line of Nodes:** The straight line formed by the intersection of the satellite's orbital plane with the Earth's equatorial plane.

The **line of nodes is significant** because it provides the reference direction inside the orbital plane from which the **argument of perigee ($\omega$)** is measured, and the **right ascension of the ascending node ($\Omega$)** is measured from the vernal equinox to this line in the equatorial plane. It is, therefore, the geometric bridge connecting the orbital plane orientation to the Earth's equatorial reference, making unambiguous satellite location possible.

**[3 marks — KTU pattern: 1.5 marks definitions, 1.5 marks significance]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper | Module 1, 14-Mark Internal Choice]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**Q.A (a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the six classical Keplerian orbital elements used to locate a satellite in its orbit. State the range of values each element can take for a bound Earth orbit. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The orbit of a satellite around the Earth is a Keplerian ellipse with Earth's centre at one focus. To uniquely describe the satellite's position in space, six parameters — called the *Keplerian orbital elements* — are required. They fall into two groups:

**Group 1 — Elements describing the size and shape of the orbit:**

1. **Semi-major axis ($a$):** Half the longest diameter of the ellipse.
   - For bound Earth orbits: $a > R_E$ (≈ 6378 km). Typical range: 6500 km (LEO) to ~42164 km (GEO).
   - **Unit:** km or m.

2. **Eccentricity ($e$):** Measure of how stretched the ellipse is.
   - Range for closed (elliptical) orbit: $0 \le e < 1$.
   - $e = 0$ → perfect circle, $e \to 1$ → very elongated.

3. **Mean Anomaly ($M$):** Angle that would be swept by the satellite if it moved uniformly with the mean motion $n = \sqrt{\mu/a^3}$. 
   - Range: $0 \le M < 2\pi$ rad. Used instead of true anomaly for time-keeping.

**Group 2 — Elements describing the orientation in inertial space and position within the orbit:**

4. **Inclination ($i$):** Tilt of the orbital plane with respect to Earth's equatorial plane, measured at the ascending node.
   - Range: $0° \le i \le 180°$.
   - $i < 90°$ → prograde (eastward) orbit, $i > 90°$ → retrograde, $i = 90°$ → polar, $i = 0°$ → equatorial.

5. **Right Ascension of Ascending Node ($\Omega$):** Angle in the equatorial plane measured eastward from the vernal equinox (♈) direction to the ascending node.
   - Range: $0° \le \Omega < 360°$.

6. **Argument of Perigee ($\omega$):** Angle measured in the orbital plane from the ascending node to the perigee, in the direction of satellite motion.
   - Range: $0° \le \omega < 360°$.

**Sub-satellite point, look angles, and Earth-fixed coordinates are then derived** through a sequence of rotations $R_z(-\Omega) \to R_x(-i) \to R_z(-\omega)$ from the perifocal frame, plus accounting for Earth rotation by the Greenwich Sidereal Time.

**[7 marks — KTU distribution: 4 marks explanation + 3 marks diagram/values]**

---

**Q.A (b)** A low-Earth-orbit satellite is at a height of 600 km above Earth's surface. Compute (i) the orbital period, (ii) the orbital speed, and (iii) the mean motion. Take $R_E = 6378$ km and $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^5$ km³/s². **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$h_{alt} = 600$ km, $R_E = 6378$ km, $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s² (or $3.986 \times 10^{5}$ km³/s²)

**Step 1 — Compute semi-major axis (circular orbit ⇒ $a = R_E + h_{alt}$):**
$$a = 6378 + 600 = 6978 \text{ km} = 6.978 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$$

**(i) Orbital Period $T$:**
Using Kepler's third law:
$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{a^3}{\mu}}$$

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{(6.978 \times 10^6)^3}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}}$$

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{(6.978)^3 \times 10^{18}}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}}$$

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{339.74 \times 10^{18}}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}}$$

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{8.524 \times 10^{5}}$$

$$T = 2\pi \times 923.3 \approx 5802 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 96.7 \text{ minutes}}$$

**(ii) Orbital Speed (vis-viva, $r = a$ for circular orbit):**
$$v = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{a}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14}}{6.978 \times 10^6}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{5.713 \times 10^{7}} \approx 7.558 \times 10^{3} \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 7.56 \text{ km/s}}$$

**(iii) Mean Motion $n$:**
$$n = \frac{2\pi}{T} = \frac{2\pi}{5802} \approx 1.083 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad/s}$$

Or equivalently:
$$n = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{a^3}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14}}{(6.978 \times 10^6)^3}} = 1.083 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\boxed{n \approx 1.083 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad/s} \approx 14.18 \text{ rev/day}}$$

**[7 marks — KTU distribution: 2 marks formula, 3 marks substitution, 2 marks final answer with units]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

**Q.B (a)** Derive the vis-viva equation for a satellite in an elliptic orbit starting from the principle of conservation of energy. Hence, find the velocity of a satellite at apogee and perigee. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Starting point — Conservation of total specific energy:**

The total specific mechanical energy of a satellite of mass $m$ in a gravitational field is the sum of kinetic energy per unit mass and gravitational potential energy per unit mass. For an inverse-square central force field generated by a mass $M$ (Earth), the potential is $-\mu/r$ where $\mu = GM$. Therefore:
$$\mathcal{E} = \frac{v^2}{2} - \frac{\mu}{r}$$

Because the only force acting is conservative (gravity), the total specific energy is **conserved** at every point of the orbit:
$$\mathcal{E} = \text{constant (along the orbit)}$$

**The constant value depends only on the semi-major axis $a$:**

For a Keplerian orbit, the energy can be shown (from the two-body problem or from the elliptical orbit integration) to equal:
$$\mathcal{E} = -\frac{\mu}{2a}$$

This is the standard result from Newton's two-body problem; it can be derived by evaluating the energy at perigee and apogee where velocity is purely tangential.

**Equate the two expressions and solve for $v$:**

$$\frac{v^2}{2} - \frac{\mu}{r} = -\frac{\mu}{2a}$$

$$\frac{v^2}{2} = \frac{\mu}{r} - \frac{\mu}{2a} = \mu\left(\frac{2a - r}{2ar}\right)$$

$$\boxed{v^2 = \mu\left(\frac{2}{r} - \frac{1}{a}\right)}$$

This is the **vis-viva** (Latin: "from living force") equation.

**Velocity at perigee** ($r_p = a(1-e)$):
$$v_p^2 = \mu\left(\frac{2}{a(1-e)} - \frac{1}{a}\right) = \frac{\mu}{a}\left(\frac{2 - (1-e)}{1-e}\right) = \frac{\mu(1+e)}{a(1-e)}$$
$$\boxed{v_p = \sqrt{\frac{\mu(1+e)}{a(1-e)}}}$$

**Velocity at apogee** ($r_a = a(1+e)$):
$$v_a^2 = \mu\left(\frac{2}{a(1+e)} - \frac{1}{a}\right) = \frac{\mu}{a}\left(\frac{2 - (1+e)}{1+e}\right) = \frac{\mu(1-e)}{a(1+e)}$$
$$\boxed{v_a = \sqrt{\frac{\mu(1-e)}{a(1+e)}}}$$

**Ratio check:** $v_p / v_a = (1+e)/(1-e)$ — confirms the satellite moves faster at perigee, consistent with Kepler's 2nd law.

**[7 marks — KTU distribution: 3 marks derivation of vis-viva, 2 marks apogee/perigee velocities, 2 marks final boxed results]**

---

**Q.B (b)** A geostationary satellite is at an altitude of 35786 km. A ground station is located at $(8.5°\text{N}, 76.9°\text{E})$ (Thiruvananthapuram). At a particular instant, the sub-satellite point is at $(0°\text{N}, 75°\text{E})$. Compute (i) the central angle $\psi$ between the station and sub-satellite point, (ii) the slant range $d$ between station and satellite, and (iii) the elevation angle $El$ as seen from the station. Take $R_E = 6378$ km. **(7 marks)**
