**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $r = R_E + h_{alt} = 6378 + 35786 = 42164$ km
- $\phi_e = 8.5°$ (station latitude), $\lambda_e = 76.9°$ (station longitude)
- $\phi_s = 0°$ (sub-satellite latitude), $\lambda_s = 75°$ (sub-satellite longitude)
- $R_E = 6378$ km

**(i) Central angle $\psi$:**

Using the spherical cosine law:
$$\cos\psi = \sin\phi_e \sin\phi_s + \cos\phi_e \cos\phi_s \cos(\lambda_s - \lambda_e)$$

$\lambda_s - \lambda_e = 75° - 76.9° = -1.9°$, so $\cos(-1.9°) \approx 0.99945$.

$$\cos\psi = \sin(8.5°)\sin(0°) + \cos(8.5°)\cos(0°)\cos(-1.9°)$$

$$\cos\psi = 0 + (0.9890)(1)(0.99945) = 0.98845$$

$$\psi = \cos^{-1}(0.98845) = 8.557° = 0.1494 \text{ rad}$$

**(ii) Slant range $d$:**

By the law of cosines:
$$d = \sqrt{R_E^2 + r^2 - 2 R_E r \cos\psi}$$

$$d = \sqrt{(6378)^2 + (42164)^2 - 2(6378)(42164)(0.98845)}$$

Computing each term:
- $R_E^2 = 4.0678 \times 10^7$ km²
- $r^2 = 1.7778 \times 10^{9}$ km²
- $2 R_E r = 2 \times 6378 \times 42164 = 5.3787 \times 10^8$ km²
- $2 R_E r \cos\psi = 5.3787 \times 10^8 \times 0.98845 = 5.3164 \times 10^8$ km²

$$d = \sqrt{4.0678 \times 10^7 + 1.7778 \times 10^9 - 5.3164 \times 10^8}$$

$$d = \sqrt{(0.040678 + 1.7778 - 0.53164) \times 10^9}$$

$$d = \sqrt{1.2869 \times 10^9} \approx 35876 \text{ km}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 35876 \text{ km}}$$

**(iii) Elevation angle $El$:**

Using the sine rule in the Earth-centred triangle:
$$\sin(El) = \frac{\cos\psi - \frac{R_E}{r}}{\sin(\psi) \cdot 1}$$

Wait — apply the elevation formula correctly. The angle at the station between the local horizontal and the line to the satellite:
$$\sin(El) = \frac{r \cos\psi - R_E}{d}$$

$$\sin(El) = \frac{42164 \times 0.98845 - 6378}{35876}$$

$$\sin(El) = \frac{41678 - 6378}{35876} = \frac{35300}{35876} = 0.9840$$

$$El = \sin^{-1}(0.9840) \approx 79.7°$$

$$\boxed{El \approx 79.7°}$$

This high elevation is expected for a station just $1.9°$ of longitude away from the sub-satellite point of a GEO bird.

**[7 marks — KTU distribution: 2 marks each for the three sub-parts]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Mixing up $\omega$ (argument of perigee) and $\Omega$ (RAAN).** $\Omega$ is measured in the *equatorial* plane, $\omega$ is measured in the *orbital* plane. Students often draw them as the same angle. → **Penalty: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $M$ to $E$ before computing true anomaly.** Mean anomaly has no direct geometric meaning — always go through Kepler's equation $M = E - e\sin E$. → **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Units mismatch in Kepler's third law.** Always use SI (m, s) or consistently km³/s² and km; mixing the two causes an answer off by a factor of $\sqrt{10^3}$ or similar. → **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Writing vis-viva as $v = \sqrt{\mu/r}$** for an *elliptical* orbit. That formula is only valid for a **circular** orbit. For elliptical orbits the full vis-viva $v^2 = \mu(2/r - 1/a)$ must be used. → **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 5. **Sign errors in longitude differences** when computing the central angle $\psi$. Always use $\lambda_s - \lambda_e$ (sub-satellite minus ground) consistently.
> 6. **In a 14-mark question, never skipping the diagram.** A clearly labelled orbit diagram showing $i$, $\Omega$, $\omega$, $\nu$ earns at least 1–2 free marks. KTU examiners explicitly reward well-labelled diagrams.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Six Keplerian elements** $(a, e, i, \Omega, \omega, \nu)$ uniquely locate a satellite in its orbit at a given epoch.
- **Three (a, e, i) define shape/size/orientation tilt; three ($\Omega, \omega, \nu$) define the absolute orientation and instantaneous position.**
- **Kepler's 3rd law** $T^2 = 4\pi^2 a^3/\mu$ gives the period — depends only on $a$, not on $e$.
- **Vis-viva equation** $v^2 = \mu(2/r - 1/a)$ gives the instantaneous speed; for circular orbits this reduces to $v = \sqrt{\mu/r}$.
- **Kepler's equation** $M = E - e\sin E$ is transcendental; solved iteratively (Newton–Raphson) for $E$, then converted to $\nu$.
- **Mean motion** $n = \sqrt{\mu/a^3}$ rad/s; the satellite sweeps this angle "on average" per second.
- **GEO orbit**: $a = 42164$ km, $T = 86164$ s (sidereal day), $i = 0°$, $e = 0°$ — appears stationary from Earth.
- **LEO orbit**: $a \approx 6500$–$7500$ km, $T \approx 90$–120 minutes, high $v \approx 7.8$ km/s.
- **Look angles** (Az, El) are obtained by transforming ECI → ECEF → topocentric via the three rotations $R_z(-\theta_G), R_x(-\phi), R_z(\lambda)$.
- **Slant range** $d = \sqrt{R_E^2 + r^2 - 2R_E r \cos\psi}$ — used for link-budget timing and signal-strength calculation.
- **TLE (Two-Line Element) sets** distributed by NORAD/Celestrak are practical real-world encodings of the six elements, propagated using the SGP4 model.
- **J2 perturbation** is the largest non-Keplerian effect; it causes the orbital plane to regress for $i < 90°$ (rate $\dot\Omega$) and perigee to rotate (rate $\dot\omega$) — must be considered for long-term mission planning.
- **Exam mnemonic to remember all six elements in order**: *"A-E-I-O-M-V"* → "**A**lways **E**valuate **I**nertial **O**rbits **M**echanically **V**isually." Or: $a$ (size), $e$ (shape), $i$ (tilt), $\Omega$ (heading), $\omega$ (perigee position), $\nu$ (instantaneous spot).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Locating Satellite with Respect to Earth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Locating Satellite with Respect to Earth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Locating a satellite with respect to the Earth** is the process of uniquely determining the position and orientation of an artificial satellite in three-dimensional space relative to a chosen Earth-fixed or Earth-centered reference frame. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is achieved by defining a satellite's **state vector** (position + velocity) using a set of six classical **orbital elements (Keplerian elements)**, and subsequently computing **look angles** (azimuth and elevation) and **slant range** from a ground-based Earth station to the satellite.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The student must master the geometry of the Earth–satellite link, the orbital parameters that fully specify a satellite's position, and the trigonometric transformation that converts orbital position into measurable ground-station pointing angles.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Lighthouse and the Compass

Imagine you are standing on a beach at night watching a lighthouse rotating in the distance.

- The **lighthouse's orbit** (its predictable circular path) is like a satellite's orbit around the Earth.
- The **beam of light** always points in a fixed direction in space, but **you** are rotating with the Earth. The angle at which you see the beam from your beach chair is constantly changing.
- To tell someone else "where the lighthouse is from your perspective," you don't say "it is 17,000 miles above the equator" — you say, "**look 215° clockwise from North, and tilt your head 42° above the horizon**." That pair of numbers (azimuth + elevation) is exactly how we *locate a satellite with respect to the Earth*.

In short: orbital elements tell us **where the satellite is in space**, and look angles tell us **where the satellite is in the sky from a particular point on Earth**.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Board Examinations

> [!NOTE]
> This topic is the **mathematical backbone** of Module 1. It appears in **Part A (3 marks)** as a definition question and in **Part B (14 marks)** as a full derivation problem (look angle geometry, slant range, and orbital element problems). Expect at least **one compulsory 14-mark derivation** in the ESE.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Mean radius of the Earth:** $R_E = 6378.14 \text{ km}$ (≈ **6,371 km** for spherical approximation)
- **Geostationary orbital radius:** $r_{GEO} = 42{,}164 \text{ km}$ from Earth's center
- **Geostationary altitude:** $h_{GEO} = 35{,}786 \text{ km}$ above mean sea level
- **Universal gravitational constant:** $G = 6.674 \times 10^{-11} \text{ N·m²/kg²}$
- **Earth's gravitational parameter:** $\mu = GM_E = 3.986 \times 10^{5} \text{ km}^3/\text{s}^2$
- **Earth's rotation rate:** $\omega_E = 7.2921 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad/s}$
- **Sidereal day:** $T_{sidereal} = 86{,}164.09 \text{ seconds} \approx 23\text{h}\,56\text{m}\,4\text{s}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reference Frames Used to Locate a Satellite

A satellite's position is meaningless until we declare **from where** and **against what** we are measuring. Three reference frames dominate:

### 2.1.1 Geocentric Celestial Reference Frame (GCRF / ECI)
- **Origin:** Earth's center of mass.
- **Axes:** Fixed with respect to distant stars (non-rotating with Earth).
- **Use:** Best suited for describing **orbital mechanics** because Newton's laws apply cleanly in an inertial frame.

### 2.1.2 Earth-Centered Earth-Fixed Frame (ECEF / ITRF)
- **Origin:** Earth's center of mass.
- **Axes:** Rotate with the Earth. The +Z axis pierces the **North Pole**; the +X axis lies in the equatorial plane at the **Greenwich Meridian**.
- **Use:** Best suited for **ground station look angles**, because the observer's latitude/longitude are defined in this frame.

### 2.1.3 Topocentric (Observer-Centric) Frame
- **Origin:** The Earth station antenna.
- **Axes:** Local **North**, **East**, and **Up (Zenith)**.
- **Use:** The natural frame for an antenna — its motors move in **azimuth (Az)** and **elevation (El)**.

## 2.2 The Six Classical Orbital Elements (Keplerian Elements)

Any unperturbed orbit is fully specified by **six scalar quantities**. These are the *DNA* of an orbit.

| # | Element | Symbol | What it Specifies |
|---|---------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | Semi-major axis | $a$ | The **size** of the orbit (half the longest diameter) |
| 2 | Eccentricity | $e$ | The **shape** of the orbit ($e=0$ circle, $0<e<1$ ellipse) |
| 3 | Inclination | $i$ | The **tilt** of the orbital plane relative to Earth's equator |
| 4 | Right Ascension of Ascending Node | $\Omega$ (RAAN) | Where the orbit **crosses the equator going north** (in inertial frame) |
| 5 | Argument of Perigee | $\omega$ | Where **perigee** lies within the orbital plane |
| 6 | True Anomaly | $\nu$ (or $\theta$) | The satellite's **current position** along the orbit, measured from perigee |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - A **Geostationary Orbit (GEO)** has: $e = 0$, $i = 0°$, $\omega$ = undefined, $\Omega$ = undefined, $a = 42{,}164 \text{ km}$, and the satellite sits at $\nu$ such that it remains over a fixed longitude.
> - A **circular orbit** ($e=0$) makes $\omega$ and $\nu$ ambiguous; $\omega$ is replaced by the **longitude of the satellite**.

## 2.3 Geometry of an Elliptical Orbit

For an elliptical orbit with semi-major axis $a$ and eccentricity $e$:

- **Perigee radius:** $r_p = a(1-e)$
- **Apogee radius:** $r_a = a(1+e)$
- **Distance to focus (Earth's center) at any true anomaly $\nu$:** $r = \dfrac{a(1-e^2)}{1 + e\cos\nu}$

The denominator $(1 + e\cos\nu)$ is the *fingerprint* of conic-section orbits.

## 2.4 Look Angles — The Ground Station Perspective

Given a satellite's orbital position and an Earth station at known latitude $\phi$ and longitude $\theta_E$, we compute:

- **Azimuth ($Az$):** Compass bearing measured clockwise from True North, in the observer's local horizontal plane.
- **Elevation ($El$):** Angle above the local horizon, in the observer's local vertical plane.
- **Slant Range ($d$):** Straight-line distance from the station to the satellite.

### 2.4.1 The Central Angle (Nadir Angle from Earth Station)

The **central angle $\gamma$** is the angle at the Earth's center, subtended between the Earth station and the satellite's sub-point (nadir point). It is the *bridge* between orbital geometry and ground geometry.

$$\gamma = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\cos\phi\,\cos(\theta_S - \theta_E)\,\cos i + \sin\phi\,\sin i\right)$$

where:
- $\phi$ = latitude of Earth station
- $\theta_E$ = longitude of Earth station
- $\theta_S$ = subsatellite longitude (geostationary) **or** subsatellite point on equator for inclined orbits
- $i$ = orbit inclination

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{r}}$ | Circular orbital speed at radius $r$ |
| 2 | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{a^3}{\mu}}$ | **Kepler's Third Law** — orbital period |
| 3 | $r = \dfrac{a(1-e^2)}{1 + e\cos\nu}$ | Radius (focus-to-satellite) at true anomaly $\nu$ |
| 4 | $r_p = a(1-e)$, $\quad r_a = a(1+e)$ | Perigee & apogee radii |
| 5 | $\gamma = \cos^{-1}(\cos\phi\cos(\theta_S - \theta_E)\cos i + \sin\phi\sin i)$ | Central (geocentric) angle |
| 6 | $d = \sqrt{R_E^2 + r^2 - 2R_E\, r \cos\gamma}$ | **Slant range** (law of cosines) |
| 7 | $El = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{r\cos\gamma - R_E}{r\sin\gamma}\right)$ | Elevation angle |
| 8 | $\cos(Az) = \dfrac{\sin(\theta_S - \theta_E)}{\sin\gamma}\,\cos i$ | Azimuth (azimuth-from-North) — see derivation |
| 9 | $r_{GEO} = \left(\dfrac{\mu\, T_{sidereal}^2}{4\pi^2}\right)^{1/3} = 42{,}164 \text{ km}$ | Geostationary orbit radius |
| 10 | $h_{GEO} = r_{GEO} - R_E \approx 35{,}786 \text{ km}$ | Geostationary altitude |
| 11 | $v_{GEO} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{r_{GEO}}} \approx 3.07 \text{ km/s}$ | Geostationary orbital speed |
| 12 | $E - e\sin E = M$ | **Kepler's Equation** (E = eccentric anomaly, M = mean anomaly) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Every GPS chip, every satellite TV dish, every Inmarsat maritime terminal, and every NORAD space-track catalog entry relies on these equations. They are also the foundation of modern **collision-avoidance maneuvers** in Low Earth Orbit (LEO) mega-constellations like Starlink and OneWeb.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Kepler's Third Law from Newton's Law of Gravitation

**Starting point:** For a satellite of mass $m$ in a circular orbit of radius $r$, gravitational attraction provides centripetal force.

$$
F_{grav} = F_{centripetal}
$$

$$
\frac{G M_E m}{r^2} = \frac{m v^2}{r}
$$

**Step 1 — Cancel $m$ and multiply by $r$:**

$$
\frac{G M_E}{r} = v^2
$$

**Step 2 — Express $v$ in terms of orbital period $T$ (one full circumference per period):**

$$
v = \frac{2\pi r}{T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 r^2}{T^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and rearrange:**

$$
\frac{G M_E}{r} = \frac{4\pi^2 r^2}{T^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply both sides by $T^2$:**

$$
G M_E \, T^2 = 4\pi^2 r^3
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $T$ (recognize $\mu = G M_E$):**

$$
T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{r^3}{\mu}}
$$

**Step 6 — Generalize to elliptical orbit ($r \to a$, the semi-major axis):**

$$
T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{a^3}{\mu}}
$$

> This is **Kepler's Third Law of Planetary Motion** expressed in modern notation. For a **geostationary** satellite, we set $T = T_{sidereal} = 86{,}164.09$ s and solve backward for $a$, which gives $a = 42{,}164$ km.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Slant Range from the Law of Cosines

**Geometric Setup:** The Earth station, the Earth's center, and the satellite form a triangle with sides:
- $R_E$ (Earth radius) from center to station
- $r$ (orbital radius) from center to satellite
- $d$ (slant range) from station to satellite
- The angle at the Earth's center between these two radii is the **central angle $\gamma$**.

Applying the **law of cosines** to this triangle:

$$
d^2 = R_E^2 + r^2 - 2 R_E r \cos\gamma
$$

$$
d = \sqrt{R_E^2 + r^2 - 2 R_E r \cos\gamma}
$$

For a **geostationary** satellite with $r = 42{,}164$ km and minimum $\gamma = 0$ (satellite directly overhead), $d_{\min} = r - R_E = 35{,}786$ km.

For maximum range (satellite on the horizon, $\gamma = \arccos(R_E/r) \approx 81.3°$):

$$
d_{\max} \approx 41{,}680 \text{ km}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Elevation Angle from Local Geometry

The elevation angle $El$ is measured at the Earth station above the local horizon. Looking at the same triangle, the angle at the Earth station between the **local vertical** (radius $R_E$) and the **slant range** $d$ is $(90° - El)$.

**Step 1 — Apply the law of sines to find the angle at the Earth station:**

$$
\frac{\sin(90° + El)}{r} = \frac{\sin\gamma}{d}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify $\sin(90° + El) = \cos(El)$:**

$$
\frac{\cos(El)}{r} = \frac{\sin\gamma}{d}
$$

**Step 3 — Express $d$ in terms of $\gamma$ (from law of cosines, isolate the sin term):**

Using the identity $r^2 = R_E^2 + d^2 - 2 R_E d \cos(90° + El)$ after appropriate rearrangement, we can solve for $El$ directly:

$$
\sin(El) = \frac{r \cos\gamma - R_E}{d}
$$

**Step 4 — Hence:**

$$
El = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{r \cos\gamma - R_E}{d}\right)
$$

which is algebraically equivalent (and KTU-preferred) to the tangent form:

$$
El = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{r \cos\gamma - R_E}{r \sin\gamma}\right)
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Azimuth Angle Using Spherical Trigonometry

The azimuth is the compass bearing from True North. On a unit sphere centered at the Earth, the Earth station, North Pole, and subsatellite point form a **spherical triangle**. Applying the **spherical law of cosines** to the sides and angles of this triangle, and then taking the ratio of two sines, yields:

$$
\cos(Az) = \frac{\sin(\theta_S - \theta_E) \cos i}{\sin\gamma}
$$

**Resolution of ambiguity (north or south of the east-west line):**

$$
\sin(Az) = \frac{\cos i \sin(\theta_S - \theta_E) \cos\phi - \sin i \cos\phi}{\sin\gamma}
$$

In practice, the formula used in most KTU textbooks is the clean form:

$$
\tan(Az) = \frac{\sin(\theta_S - \theta_E)}{\tan i \cos\phi - \sin\phi \cos(\theta_S - \theta_E)}
$$

with $Az$ measured from True North (clockwise).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples

### Example 1: Slant Range for Geostationary Satellite
**Given:** $R_E = 6378$ km, $h = 35{,}786$ km (so $r = 42{,}164$ km), Earth station at the equator with $\gamma = 20°$.

**Solution:**

$$
d = \sqrt{6378^2 + 42164^2 - 2 \times 6378 \times 42164 \times \cos(20°)}
$$

$$
d = \sqrt{40{,}678{,}884 + 1{,}777{,}802{,}896 - 2 \times 6378 \times 42164 \times 0.9397}
$$

$$
d = \sqrt{1{,}818{,}481{,}780 - 505{,}499{,}640} = \sqrt{1{,}312{,}982{,}140}
$$

$$
\boxed{d \approx 36{,}235 \text{ km}}
$$

### Example 2: Geostationary Orbit Radius
**Given:** $T = 86{,}164$ s, $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^5 \text{ km}^3/\text{s}^2$.

**Solution:**

$$
a = \left(\frac{\mu T^2}{4\pi^2}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{3.986 \times 10^5 \times (86{,}164)^2}{4 \pi^2}\right)^{1/3}
$$

$$
a = \left(\frac{3.986 \times 10^5 \times 7.4242 \times 10^9}{39.478}\right)^{1/3} = \left(7.496 \times 10^{13}\right)^{1/3}
$$

$$
\boxed{a \approx 42{,}164 \text{ km}}
$$

### Example 3: Period of LEO at 500 km Altitude
**Given:** $r = 500 + 6378 = 6878$ km.

**Solution:**

$$
T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{6878^3}{3.986 \times 10^5}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{3.254 \times 10^{11}}{3.986 \times 10^5}}
$$

$$
T = 2\pi \sqrt{8.164 \times 10^5} = 2\pi \times 903.5
$$

$$
\boxed{T \approx 5677 \text{ s} \approx 94.6 \text{ minutes}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reference Frame Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **three-tier reference frame hierarchy** used to locate a satellite — from the inertial frame (orbital mechanics) down to the observer's local antenna pointing frame.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["GCRF / ECI Inertial Frame\n Origin: Earth Center\n Axes: Fixed to Stars\n Used for: Orbital Mechanics"]
    B["ECEF Earth Fixed Frame\n Origin: Earth Center\n Axes: Rotate with Earth\n Used for: Ground Station Position"]
    C["Topocentric Local Frame\n Origin: Earth Station Antenna\n Axes: North East Up\n Used for: Azimuth and Elevation"]
    D["Six Classical Orbital Elements\n a semi major axis\ne eccentricity\ni inclination\nOmega RAAN\nomega argument of perigee\nnu true anomaly"]
    E["Satellite Position Vector\n in Inertial Coordinates"]
    F["Rotation Matrix R t\n ECI to ECEF\n Depends on GMST and t"]
    G["Satellite Position Vector\n in Earth Fixed Coordinates"]
    H["Sub Satellite Point\n Latitude and Longitude"]
    I["Central Angle Gamma\n Geocentric Angle"]
    J["Azimuth and Elevation\n Slant Range d"]
    A --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    B --> H
    H --> I
    B --> I
    I --> C
    C --> J
    B --> J
```

## 4.2 Geometry of the Earth–Station–Satellite Triangle

The figure below shows the **law-of-cosines triangle** that underlies every slant-range and look-angle calculation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Earth Center O"]
    P2["Earth Station ES\n Latitude phi\n Longitude thetaE"]
    P3["Satellite SAT\n Orbital Radius r\n Subsatellite Point S"]
    P4["Slant Range d\n Straight Line ES to SAT"]
    P5["Central Angle gamma\n Angle at Earth Center\n Between ES and S"]
    P6["Local Vertical\n Radius R_E from O to ES"]
    P7["Orbital Radius r\n From O to SAT"]
    P1 --- P2
    P1 --- P3
    P2 --- P3
    P1 -.-> P5
    P2 -.-> P6
    P1 -.-> P7
    P2 -.-> P4
    P3 -.-> P4
    P5 --- P3
    P5 --- P2
```

**Read this diagram as follows:**
- Triangle with vertices at $O$ (Earth's center), $ES$ (Earth station), and $SAT$ (satellite).
- Side $O \to ES = R_E$.
- Side $O \to SAT = r$.
- Side $ES \to SAT = d$ (slant range — what we want).
- Angle at $O$ is the **central angle $\gamma$**.

## 4.3 Orbital Element Geometry

The figure below shows how the **six classical orbital elements** locate a satellite within its orbit relative to Earth.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EQUATOR["EQUATORIAL PLANE OF EARTH"]
        X1["Vernal Equinox\n Reference Direction"]
        X2["Ascending Node\n Where Orbit Crosses Equator"]
        X3["RAAN Omega\n Angle from X to Ascending Node"]
    end
    subgraph ORBITPLANE["ORBITAL PLANE"]
        Y1["Perigee P\n Closest Point to Earth"]
        Y2["Argument of Perigee omega\n Angle from Ascending Node to Perigee"]
        Y3["True Anomaly nu\n Angle from Perigee to Satellite"]
        Y4["Satellite Position\n Current Location"]
        Y5["Apogee A\n Farthest Point from Earth"]
    end
    X3 -.-> ORBITPLANE
    Y1 --- Y3
    Y2 --- Y3
    Y3 --> Y4
    Y1 --- Y5
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **vernal equinox** (the direction of the Sun at the March equinox) is the universal reference direction in inertial space.
- **RAAN ($\Omega$)** is the angle measured eastward, in the equatorial plane, from the vernal equinox to the ascending node.
- The **orbital plane** is tilted by the inclination $i$ relative to the equator.
- Within the orbital plane, $\omega$ locates perigee and $\nu$ locates the satellite.

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — End-to-End Satellite Location Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1\n Define Reference Time Epoch t0\n and Satellite State at t0"]
    S2["Step 2\n Propagate Orbit Forward\n Using Keplers Equation\n E minus e sin E equals M"]
    S3["Step 3\n Compute Inertial Position Vector\n r and v in ECI Coordinates"]
    S4["Step 4\n Apply Rotation R t\n Convert ECI to ECEF\n Using GMST at time t"]
    S5["Step 5\n Subtract Earth Station Position\n Compute Range Vector rho"]
    S6["Step 6\n Transform to Topocentric Frame\n North East Up Coordinates"]
    S7["Step 7\n Compute Azimuth\n Az = atan2 East North"]
    S8["Step 8\n Compute Elevation\n El = asin Up divided by magnitude"]
    S9["Step 9\n Compute Slant Range\n d = magnitude of rho"]
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7
    S6 --> S8
    S6 --> S9
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **exact pipeline** executed millions of times per second by every TT&C (Telemetry, Tracking, and Command) ground station in the world. KTU expects you to be able to **describe and draw** this pipeline in a 7-mark sub-question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the following orbital elements: (i) Semi-major axis, (ii) Inclination, (iii) Right Ascension of Ascending Node (RAAN).**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Semi-major axis ($a$):** It is one-half of the longest diameter of the elliptical orbit. For a circular orbit, $a$ equals the orbital radius. It determines the **size** of the orbit and, through Kepler's third law, the **orbital period**.

**(ii) Inclination ($i$):** It is the angle between the **orbital plane** and the **Earth's equatorial plane**, measured at the ascending node. Values: $i = 0°$ ⇒ equatorial orbit; $i = 90°$ ⇒ polar orbit; $i = 63.4°$ ⇒ Molniya (frozen) orbit.

**(iii) Right Ascension of Ascending Node ($\Omega$, RAAN):** It is the angle, measured **eastward in the equatorial plane** from the **vernal equinox** (First Point of Aries) to the **ascending node** (the point where the satellite crosses the equator moving northward). It orients the orbit in inertial space.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is meant by "look angle" in satellite communication? List the two look angles and define them.**

**Model Answer:**

A **look angle** is the pair of angular coordinates that points a ground-station antenna directly at a satellite in the sky. The two look angles are:

1. **Azimuth ($Az$):** The compass bearing of the satellite measured **clockwise from True North**, in the observer's local horizontal plane. Range: $0°$ to $360°$.

2. **Elevation ($El$):** The angle of the satellite **above the local horizon** of the observer. Range: $0°$ (horizon) to $90°$ (zenith). A negative elevation means the satellite is **below the horizon** and is therefore not visible.

These angles, together with the **slant range $d$**, completely describe the geometric relationship between a ground station and a satellite.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**(a)** Derive an expression for the **slant range** of a satellite from an Earth station, starting from the law of cosines. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A geostationary satellite is located at $90°$ E longitude. An Earth station is at $(0°, 75°)$ — i.e., on the equator at $75°$ E. Compute the **central angle** and the **slant range**. Use $R_E = 6378$ km and $r = 42{,}164$ km. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a) — Slant Range Derivation

**Geometric Setup:** The Earth station (ES), the Earth's center (O), and the satellite (S) form a triangle. The sides are:
- $OE = R_E$ (Earth radius)
- $OS = r$ (orbital radius from Earth's center)
- $ES = d$ (slant range — unknown)
- The angle at $O$ between $OE$ and $OS$ is the **central angle $\gamma$**.

**Apply the Law of Cosines:**

$$
ES^2 = OE^2 + OS^2 - 2 \cdot OE \cdot OS \cdot \cos(\angle EOS)
$$

**Substitute symbols:**

$$
d^2 = R_E^2 + r^2 - 2 R_E r \cos\gamma
$$

**Take the square root:**

$$
\boxed{d = \sqrt{R_E^2 + r^2 - 2 R_E r \cos\gamma}}
$$

**Special cases:**
- When $\gamma = 0$ (satellite directly overhead): $d = r - R_E$ (minimum range).
- When $\gamma = \arccos(R_E/r) \approx 81.3°$ (satellite at the horizon): $d = \sqrt{r^2 - R_E^2}$ (maximum range).

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:**
- Satellite longitude $\theta_S = 90°$ E
- Earth station longitude $\theta_E = 75°$ E
- $\phi = 0°$ (equator)
- $i = 0°$ (geostationary)
- $R_E = 6378$ km, $r = 42{,}164$ km

**Step 1 — Central angle formula for geostationary ($i = 0°$):**

For an equatorial station ($\phi = 0°$) and geostationary satellite ($i = 0°$):

$$
\gamma = \cos^{-1}(\cos\phi \cos(\theta_S - \theta_E) \cos i + \sin\phi \sin i)
$$

$$
\gamma = \cos^{-1}(\cos 0° \cdot \cos(15°) \cdot \cos 0° + \sin 0° \cdot \sin 0°)
$$

$$
\gamma = \cos^{-1}(1 \cdot 0.9659 \cdot 1 + 0)
$$

$$
\boxed{\gamma = 15°}
$$

**Step 2 — Slant range:**

$$
d = \sqrt{R_E^2 + r^2 - 2 R_E r \cos\gamma}
$$

$$
d = \sqrt{6378^2 + 42164^2 - 2 \times 6378 \times 42164 \times \cos(15°)}
$$

$$
\cos(15°) = 0.9659
$$

$$
d = \sqrt{40{,}678{,}884 + 1{,}777{,}802{,}896 - 2 \times 6378 \times 42164 \times 0.9659}
$$

$$
d = \sqrt{1{,}818{,}481{,}780 - 519{,}756{,}953} = \sqrt{1{,}298{,}724{,}827}
$$

$$
\boxed{d \approx 36{,}037 \text{ km}}
$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**(a)** Explain the **six classical orbital elements** used to fully describe the position of a satellite in its orbit, with the help of a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A LEO satellite is at altitude $h = 500$ km. Compute its **orbital period** and **orbital speed** using Kepler's laws. Take $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^5 \text{ km}^3/\text{s}^2$ and $R_E = 6378$ km. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a) — Six Orbital Elements

The position and orientation of any unperturbed satellite orbit is uniquely specified by **six scalar parameters** known as the **Keplerian (classical orbital) elements**:

1. **Semi-major axis ($a$):** Defines the **size** of the orbit. For a circular orbit, $a = r$.

2. **Eccentricity ($e$):** Defines the **shape**. $e = 0$ (circle); $0 < e < 1$ (ellipse); $e = 1$ (parabola); $e > 1$ (hyperbola — not a closed orbit).

3. **Inclination ($i$):** The angle between the **orbital plane** and the **Earth's equatorial plane**, measured at the ascending node. Range: $0°$ to $180°$.

4. **Right Ascension of Ascending Node ($\Omega$):** The angle in the equatorial plane, measured **eastward** from the **vernal equinox** to the **ascending node**. Range: $0°$ to $360°$. It is measured in the **inertial (ECI)** frame.

5. **Argument of Perigee ($\omega$):** The angle, measured in the **orbital plane**, from the **ascending node** to the **perigee** (closest point to Earth). Range: $0°$ to $360°$.

6. **True Anomaly ($\nu$):** The angle, measured in the **orbital plane**, from the **perigee** to the **satellite's current position**. Range: $0°$ to $360°$.

> For a **circular equatorial orbit** ($e=0$, $i=0°$), $\omega$ and $\nu$ are ambiguous and are replaced by a single parameter, the **longitude of the satellite**.

**Diagrammatic representation:** (Refer to the orbital element geometry diagram in Section 4.3 of these notes.)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (b) — LEO Period and Speed

**Given:**
- Altitude $h = 500$ km
- $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^5 \text{ km}^3/\text{s}^2$
- $R_E = 6378$ km

**Step 1 — Orbital radius:**

$$
r = R_E + h = 6378 + 500 = 6878 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 2 — Orbital period (Kepler's Third Law, circular orbit so $a = r$):**

$$
T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{r^3}{\mu}}
$$

$$
T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{(6878)^3}{3.986 \times 10^5}}
$$

$$
(6878)^3 = 6.878^3 \times 10^9 = 325.4 \times 10^9 = 3.254 \times 10^{11} \text{ km}^3
$$

$$
T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{3.254 \times 10^{11}}{3.986 \times 10^5}} = 2\pi \sqrt{8.164 \times 10^5}
$$

$$
\sqrt{8.164 \times 10^5} = 903.5 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{T \approx 5677 \text{ s} \approx 94.6 \text{ minutes}}
$$

**Step 3 — Orbital speed (circular orbit formula):**

$$
v = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^5}{6878}}
$$

$$
v = \sqrt{57.95} = 7.612 \text{ km/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{v \approx 7.61 \text{ km/s}}
$$

**Physical insight:** At ~7.6 km/s, the LEO satellite completes ~15 orbits per day, which is why LEO constellations like Starlink require hundreds to thousands of satellites for continuous global coverage.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert** latitude/longitude **differences into radians** when using trigonometric functions. Always state units clearly.
> 2. **Sign confusion in azimuth**: In the Northern Hemisphere, geostationary satellites to the **east** have $Az$ between $90°$ and $180°$; to the **west**, between $180°$ and $270°$. Mixing these is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing $r$ with $h$**: Many students use altitude $h$ directly in the slant-range formula. Always convert to **orbital radius** $r = R_E + h$ **first**.
> 4. **Forgetting inclination** in the central angle formula: For GEO ($i = 0°$), the formula reduces to $\gamma = \theta_S - \theta_E$, but if you are dealing with an inclined orbit, the full formula must be used.
> 5. **Unit mixing**: $\mu$ in km³/s² requires distances in **km** and times in **seconds**. Do not mix km with miles or seconds with minutes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A satellite is **located in space** using the **six classical orbital elements**: $a, e, i, \Omega, \omega, \nu$.
- A satellite is **located from a ground station** using **look angles** $Az$ and $El$, plus **slant range** $d$.
- The **bridge** between orbital geometry and ground geometry is the **central angle $\gamma$**.
- **Kepler's Third Law:** $T = 2\pi\sqrt{a^3 / \mu}$ — directly links orbital size to period.
- **Geostationary** orbit: $a = 42{,}164$ km, $h = 35{,}786$ km, $T = 1$ sidereal day, $i = 0°$, $e = 0$.
- **Slant range** formula: $d = \sqrt{R_E^2 + r^2 - 2R_E r \cos\gamma}$ — derived from the law of cosines.
- **Elevation** is measured above the **local horizon**; **azimuth** is measured **clockwise from True North**.
- For a **geostationary** satellite over an **equatorial** station: $\gamma = \vert \theta_S - \theta_E \vert$ (simplification).
- **Reference frames**: ECI for orbital mechanics, ECEF for ground positions, topocentric for antenna pointing.
- The **vernal equinox** (First Point of Aries) is the universal reference direction for measuring RAAN.
- **True anomaly** is measured from perigee; **argument of perigee** is measured from the ascending node; **RAAN** is measured from the vernal equinox.
- **Kepler's Equation** $E - e\sin E = M$ is the tool to convert mean anomaly $M$ to eccentric anomaly $E$ for elliptical orbits.
- Always use $R_E = 6378$ km, $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^5 \text{ km}^3/\text{s}^2$, $T_{sidereal} \approx 86{,}164$ s unless otherwise specified in the question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Look Angle Calculation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Look Angle Calculation

# Module 1 — Look Angle Calculation (Look Angles to a Geostationary Satellite)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Look Angles** are the precise pointing coordinates (azimuth and elevation) of the antenna of an **Earth Station (ES)** required to correctly align itself toward a **Geostationary Satellite (GEO)** orbiting in the equatorial plane at a nominal altitude of **35,786 km** above the Earth's mean sea level.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus for *OEECT832 — Satellite and Radar Communication*, the "look angle calculation" sub-topic falls under **Module 1: Fundamentals of Satellite Communication**. It is mathematically treated as a pure **3-D spherical-geometry** problem whose inputs are the *position vectors* of two points on/above an idealised spherical Earth, and whose outputs are the **Elevation (E)** and **Azimuth (A)** measured at the Earth Station.

### 1.1 The Two Output Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Azimuth Angle | $A$ | The horizontal angle measured **clockwise from True North** to the projection of the satellite direction onto the local horizontal plane of the Earth Station (range $0^{\circ}$ to $360^{\circ}$). |
| Elevation Angle | $E$ | The vertical angle measured **upward from the local horizontal plane** of the Earth Station to the satellite (range $0^{\circ}$ to $90^{\circ}$). |
| Slant Range | $d$ | The straight-line distance from the Earth Station antenna feed to the satellite, **measured along the line of sight (LOS)**. |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "Stargazing with a flashlight."**
> Imagine you are standing somewhere in India at night and you want to point a laser beam at a *fixed, very high-altitude drone* hovering at 36 000 km over the equator. To aim correctly you must answer two questions:
>
> **(a) Which direction on the compass must I face?** → This is **Azimuth** (horizontal angle).
> **(b) How high above the horizon must I tilt my beam?** → This is **Elevation** (vertical angle).
>
> Your *latitude* tells you how far north/south you are, and the *longitude gap* between you and the drone tells you how far east/west the drone appears. The geometry of the Earth–Drone–You triangle fixes the aim. That is exactly what a satellite dish installer computes before bolting the dish to the wall.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Constants (used throughout KTU questions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Mean radius of the Earth:** $R_e = 6{,}378 \text{ km}$ (≈ 6 371 km; KTU accepts 6 378 km).
> - **Geostationary orbital radius:** $R_s = 42{,}164 \text{ km}$ (equatorial radius of GEO orbit).
> - **Geostationary altitude above mean sea level:** $h = 35{,}786 \text{ km}$.
> - **Latitude range:** $-90^{\circ} \leq \lambda_e \leq +90^{\circ}$ (North positive).
> - **Longitude range:** $-180^{\circ} \leq \phi \leq +180^{\circ}$ (East positive).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Geometry of Look-Angle Determination

The geometry is built from three points in 3-D space:

1. **Earth's Centre (O)** — origin.
2. **Earth Station (E)** — located on the Earth's surface at latitude $\lambda_e$ and longitude $\phi_e$.
3. **Satellite (S)** — located on the **equatorial plane** at radius $R_s$ and longitude $\phi_{ss}$ (the *sub-satellite point* lies on the equator directly below S).

A triangle $\triangle OES$ is formed. The side $OE = R_e$, the side $OS = R_s$, and the included angle at O is the **Earth-central angle $\beta$**, which is the angular separation between the Earth Station and the sub-satellite point as seen from the Earth's centre.

The look-angle problem proceeds in the following **logic pipeline**:

- **Step 1 — Longitude difference:**
  Compute the East-West angular separation between the Earth Station and the sub-satellite point.

- **Step 2 — Earth-central angle $\beta$:**
  Apply the spherical-cosine (or the equatorial-plane projection) relation between the Earth Station's latitude and the longitude gap.

- **Step 3 — Slant range $d$:**
  Apply the **Law of Cosines** in $\triangle OES$ to obtain the straight-line ES-to-satellite distance.

- **Step 4 — Elevation angle $E$:**
  Resolve the line of sight into its vertical component relative to the local horizon at the Earth Station.

- **Step 5 — Azimuth angle $A$:**
  Resolve the horizontal component into its North–East projection to obtain the compass bearing.

### 2.2 Why Each Step Exists (Engineering Rationale)

| Step | Mathematical Reason | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|
| $\phi$ | The Earth is round; east-west separation is an angle at the equator. | Determines whether the satellite is **east or west** of the Earth Station — controls the sign convention of $A$. |
| $\beta$ | $\triangle OES$ has two known sides ($R_e$, $R_s$) and we need the included angle to compute the third side. | Defines the geometric chord length to the satellite. |
| $d$ | Law of Cosines $\to$ LOS distance. | Sets the **free-space path loss** and round-trip time delay ($\approx 240$ ms one-way). |
| $E$ | Decompose LOS into vertical + horizontal local components. | Determines whether the satellite is **visible** (needs $E > 0^{\circ}$; terrain, buildings, foliage obscure). |
| $A$ | Project the horizontal component onto True-North. | Directs the **mechanical azimuth drive** of the antenna mount. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Inputs:** $\lambda_e$ (ES latitude), $\phi_e$ (ES longitude), $\phi_{ss}$ (satellite longitude).

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Longitude gap | $\phi \;=\; \phi_e - \phi_{ss}$ | degrees, signed (East +ve) |
| 2 | Earth-central angle | $\beta \;=\; \cos^{-1}\!\bigl(\cos\lambda_e \cdot \cos\phi\bigr)$ | degrees ($0^{\circ}\le\beta\le 180^{\circ}$) |
| 3 | Slant range | $d \;=\; \sqrt{R_e^{2} + R_s^{2} - 2\,R_e R_s \cos\beta}$ | km |
| 4 | Elevation | $E \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\Bigl(\dfrac{\cos\beta - R_e/R_s}{\sin\beta}\Bigr)$ | degrees |
| 5a | Azimuth (satellite **west** of ES, i.e. $\phi<0$) | $A \;=\; 180^{\circ} + \tan^{-1}\!\Bigl(\dfrac{\tan\phi}{\sin\lambda_e}\Bigr)$ | degrees, $0^{\circ}$–$360^{\circ}$ |
| 5b | Azimuth (satellite **east** of ES, i.e. $\phi>0$) | $A \;=\; 180^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}\!\Bigl(\dfrac{\tan\phi}{\sin\lambda_e}\Bigr)$ | degrees, $0^{\circ}$–$360^{\circ}$ |
| 6 | Visibility half-cone (max $\beta$) | $\beta_{max} = \cos^{-1}\!\bigl(R_e/R_s\bigr) \approx 81.3^{\circ}$ | degrees |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign convention reminder:** A negative $\phi$ (i.e., $\phi_e < \phi_{ss}$) means the satellite is **east** of the Earth Station; a positive $\phi$ means the satellite is **west**. Always reconcile with the rule "5a ↔ west, 5b ↔ east" before writing $A$.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

Look-angle computation is the **first step** in every satellite-link budget (KTD module on link design). It feeds:
- **Antenna mount design** (az-el vs polar mounts),
- **Free-space path loss**: $\text{FSPL} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})$ dB,
- **Propagation delay** (≈ $d/c$),
- **Depolarisation & Faraday rotation corrections** (because $E$ sets the path through the ionosphere),
- **Rain-fade margin** (slant path through rain is longer when $E$ is small).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Earth-Central Angle $\beta$

Consider a Cartesian frame whose $z$-axis is the Earth's spin axis. The unit vector to the Earth Station is

$$
\hat{e}_{ES} \;=\; \bigl(\cos\lambda_e \cos\phi_e,\;\cos\lambda_e \sin\phi_e,\;\sin\lambda_e\bigr)
$$

The unit vector to the sub-satellite point (lying on the equator, longitude $\phi_{ss}$) is

$$
\hat{e}_{SS} \;=\; \bigl(\cos\phi_{ss},\;\sin\phi_{ss},\;0\bigr)
$$

The Earth-central angle $\beta$ is the angle between these two unit vectors:

$$
\cos\beta \;=\; \hat{e}_{ES}\cdot\hat{e}_{SS} \;=\; \cos\lambda_e\cos\phi_e\cos\phi_{ss} + \cos\lambda_e\sin\phi_e\sin\phi_{ss}
$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\cos A\cos B + \sin A\sin B = \cos(A-B)$:

$$
\cos\beta \;=\; \cos\lambda_e\,\cos(\phi_e - \phi_{ss}) \;=\; \cos\lambda_e\,\cos\phi
$$

where $\phi = \phi_e - \phi_{ss}$. Inverting the cosine:

$$
\beta \;=\; \cos^{-1}\!\bigl(\cos\lambda_e \cdot \cos\phi\bigr)
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Slant Range $d$

In $\triangle OES$, the two sides adjacent to the included angle $\beta$ are $OE = R_e$ and $OS = R_s$. The side opposite the angle $\beta$ is $ES = d$. Applying the **Law of Cosines**:

$$
d^{2} \;=\; R_e^{2} + R_s^{2} - 2\,R_e\,R_s\,\cos\beta
$$

Taking the positive square root (distance is non-negative):

$$
d \;=\; \sqrt{R_e^{2} + R_s^{2} - 2\,R_e\,R_s\,\cos\beta}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Elevation Angle $E$

Drop a perpendicular from the satellite $S$ onto the local horizontal plane at the Earth Station $E$. Let the foot of the perpendicular be $H$. Then in the vertical plane containing $O$, $E$ and $S$:

- The horizontal projection of $ES$ has length $d\cos E$.
- The vertical projection of $ES$ has length $d\sin E$.

Apply the Law of Cosines in $\triangle OES$ but for the projection on the radial line $OE$ extended:

$$
R_s \cos(\beta + 90^{\circ}) \;=\; R_e \cos 90^{\circ} + d\sin E
$$

A more elegant route: use the formula for the angle at $E$ in the triangle $OES$. The interior angle at $E$ between $EO$ and $ES$ equals $(90^{\circ} + E)$, because $EO$ points vertically downward and $ES$ makes elevation $E$ above horizontal. Applying the Law of Sines:

$$
\frac{\sin\beta}{d} \;=\; \frac{\sin(90^{\circ}+E)}{R_s} \;=\; \frac{\cos E}{R_s}
$$

Combining with $d = \sqrt{R_e^{2}+R_s^{2}-2R_eR_s\cos\beta}$, and solving for $E$:

$$
\tan E \;=\; \frac{\cos\beta - R_e/R_s}{\sin\beta}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;E \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\Bigl(\frac{\cos\beta - R_e/R_s}{\sin\beta}\Bigr)\;}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation of the Azimuth Angle $A$

Project everything onto the **local horizontal plane** of the Earth Station. In that plane, three directions are relevant:

- **True North**,
- **The East-West line**,
- **The projection of the line-of-sight $ES$** onto the horizontal plane.

In the right-triangle on the local horizontal plane, with hypotenuse = $d\cos E$:

- **North-component** of the horizontal projection: $d\cos E \cdot \cos A$,
- **East-component**: $d\cos E \cdot \sin A$.

Equating these to the projections obtained from the spherical geometry (East-component scales with $\sin\phi\cos\lambda_e$; North-component scales with $\cos\phi\sin\lambda_e - \sin\lambda_e\cos\phi$, etc.), one obtains after trigonometric simplification:

$$
\tan A \;=\; \frac{\tan\phi}{\sin\lambda_e}
$$

Combining with the rule that $A$ is always measured clockwise from North and must lie in $[0^{\circ}, 360^{\circ}]$ gives the two-case formula:

$$
A \;=\;
\begin{cases}
180^{\circ} + \tan^{-1}\!\bigl(\tan\phi/\sin\lambda_e\bigr), & \text{satellite WEST of ES } (\phi > 0)\\[4pt]
180^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}\!\bigl(\tan\phi/\sin\lambda_e\bigr), & \text{satellite EAST of ES } (\phi < 0)
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Some textbooks define $\phi = \phi_{ss}-\phi_e$ (opposite sign). Then the west/east labels swap. **Always state your sign convention first** in a KTU answer — this is one of the highest-frequency marking deductions.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** An Earth Station at latitude $\lambda_e = 30^{\circ}\text{N}$, longitude $\phi_e = 90^{\circ}\text{W}$ communicates with a geostationary satellite at longitude $\phi_{ss} = 100^{\circ}\text{W}$. Determine the Azimuth, Elevation and Slant Range. Take $R_e = 6{,}378$ km and $R_s = 42{,}164$ km.

**Step 1 — Longitude gap:**

$$
\phi \;=\; \phi_e - \phi_{ss} \;=\; 90^{\circ} - 100^{\circ} \;=\; -10^{\circ}
$$

Negative ⇒ the satellite lies **east** of the Earth Station (closer to prime meridian by 10°? No — *west* by 10°; the satellite longitude 100°W is further west than 90°W. The convention here is that $\phi_e - \phi_{ss} = -10^{\circ}$ means ES is *east* of the satellite). We adopt the rule **5b (east-ES)** below.

**Step 2 — Earth-central angle:**

$$
\cos\beta \;=\; \cos(30^{\circ})\cdot\cos(-10^{\circ}) \;=\; 0.8660 \times 0.9848 \;=\; 0.8528
$$

$$
\beta \;=\; \cos^{-1}(0.8528) \;=\; 31.55^{\circ}
$$

**Step 3 — Slant range:**

$$
d^{2} \;=\; (6378)^{2} + (42164)^{2} - 2(6378)(42164)\cos(31.55^{\circ})
$$

$$
d^{2} \;=\; 40.679\times 10^{6} + 1777.80\times 10^{6} - 2(6378)(42164)(0.8528)
$$

$$
d^{2} \;=\; 1818.48\times 10^{6} - 458.65\times 10^{6} \;=\; 1359.83\times 10^{6}\ \text{km}^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;d \;\approx\; 36{,}872 \text{ km}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Elevation angle:**

$$
\frac{R_e}{R_s} \;=\; \frac{6378}{42164} \;=\; 0.1513
$$

$$
\tan E \;=\; \frac{\cos(31.55^{\circ}) - 0.1513}{\sin(31.55^{\circ})} \;=\; \frac{0.8528 - 0.1513}{0.5232} \;=\; \frac{0.7015}{0.5232} \;=\; 1.3408
$$

$$
\boxed{\;E \;\approx\; 53.28^{\circ}\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Azimuth angle:**

Since the satellite longitude $100^{\circ}\text{W}$ is west of the ES longitude $90^{\circ}\text{W}$, the satellite is **west** of the Earth Station, so we use **Rule 5a**:

$$
\tan^{-1}\!\Bigl(\frac{\tan\phi}{\sin\lambda_e}\Bigr) \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\Bigl(\frac{\tan(-10^{\circ})}{\sin(30^{\circ})}\Bigr) \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\Bigl(\frac{-0.1763}{0.5}\Bigr) \;=\; \tan^{-1}(-0.3526) \;=\; -19.42^{\circ}
$$

$$
A \;=\; 180^{\circ} + (-19.42^{\circ}) \;=\; 160.58^{\circ}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;A \;\approx\; 160.6^{\circ}\ \text{(measured clockwise from True North)}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** The answer $A \approx 160.6^{\circ}$ is *south of east*, which is exactly what one expects when looking from latitude $30^{\circ}\text{N}$ at a satellite whose sub-point is $10^{\circ}$ to the west on the equator. A south-of-east bearing combined with elevation $\approx 53^{\circ}$ is geometrically consistent.

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Formula When?

| Given | Use this formula | Watch out for |
|---|---|---|
| Both $\lambda_e$ and $\phi_{ss}$ known, looking for $E$ | First compute $\phi$, then $\beta$, then $E$ formula | Don't use degrees/radians mixed. |
| $\phi$ very small ($\approx 0$, satellite overhead in longitude) | $\beta \approx \lambda_e$; $E$ is high ($\approx 90^{\circ}-\lambda_e$) | Don't divide by zero in $\sin\beta$. |
| ES on the equator ($\lambda_e = 0$) | $A$ formula becomes indeterminate; physically the satellite is due East or West. | Use $\lim_{\lambda_e\to 0} A = 90^{\circ}$ or $270^{\circ}$. |
| ES at the pole ($\lambda_e = \pm 90^{\circ}$) | Satellite is always on the horizon if $\phi \ne 0$; $\beta = 90^{\circ}$. | $A$ formula blows up; the azimuth is the direction of the satellite's longitude. |
| Slant range required for non-GEO satellite | Replace $R_s$ with the *actual orbital radius* $R_s = R_e + h$. | KTU problems mostly use GEO ($h = 35{,}786$ km). |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Geometry of the Earth-Station / Sub-Satellite / Satellite Triangle (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The physical picture is the spherical triangle $OES$, but rendered as a clear block flow it becomes a **computational pipeline**:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: ES Latitude lambda_e] --> C[Compute longitude gap phi]
    B[Input: ES Longitude phi_e] --> C
    D[Input: Satellite Longitude phi_ss] --> C
    C --> E[Compute Earth-central angle beta via spherical cosine]
    E --> F[Compute Slant Range d using Law of Cosines]
    F --> G[Compute Elevation Angle E using tan inverse ratio]
    F --> H[Compute Azimuth Angle A with west or east rule]
    E --> G
    E --> H
    G --> I[Output: Look Angles and Slant Range]
    H --> I
    F --> I
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture of a Real Earth-Station Antenna Pointing System

The mathematical look angles drive the **mechanical servo** of the antenna mount:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC[Source Inputs]
        X1[ES Latitude lambda_e]
        X2[ES Longitude phi_e]
        X3[Satellite Longitude phi_ss]
    end

    subgraph CALC[Look-Angle Computation Block]
        Y1[Coordinate Conversion]
        Y2[Spherical Geometry Solver]
        Y3[Look-Angle Calculator]
    end

    subgraph CTRL[Antenna Control Unit]
        Z1[Azimuth Servo Drive]
        Z2[Elevation Servo Drive]
    end

    subgraph ANT[Antenna Assembly]
        W1[Azimuth Rotary Joint]
        W2[Elevation Tilt Mechanism]
        W3[Reflector and Feed Horn]
    end

    X1 --> Y1
    X2 --> Y1
    X3 --> Y1
    Y1 --> Y2
    Y2 --> Y3
    Y3 --> Z1
    Y3 --> Z2
    Z1 --> W1
    Z2 --> W2
    W1 --> W3
    W2 --> W3
```

### 4.3 Sign-Convention Decision Tree (Azimuth)

This block flow is the single most-marked decision in a KTU answer; commit it to memory:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Compute phi = phi_e minus phi_ss] --> Q{phi positive or negative?}
    Q -- phi greater than 0 --> W[Satellite is WEST of ES]
    Q -- phi less than 0 --> E[Satellite is EAST of ES]
    Q -- phi equals 0 --> N[Satellite is directly overhead in longitude - rare]
    W --> AW[Apply Azimuth Rule 5a: A = 180 deg plus tan inverse of tan phi over sin lambda_e]
    E --> AE[Apply Azimuth Rule 5b: A = 180 deg minus tan inverse of tan phi over sin lambda_e]
    N --> AN[Satellite is due South if lambda_e positive; A = 180 deg]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Every block above maps a **mathematical step** to a **physical hardware stage**. In a KTU 14-mark question, a clearly-labelled block diagram is worth 2–3 easy marks; examiners explicitly reward block-flow sketches even when the algebraic work is partial.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define the terms **Azimuth angle** and **Elevation angle** as used in the alignment of an earth-station antenna toward a geostationary satellite.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Azimuth angle (A)** is the horizontal angle measured *clockwise from True North* to the projection of the satellite line-of-sight onto the local horizontal plane of the Earth Station. Its range is $0^{\circ}$ to $360^{\circ}$ **(1 mark)**. The **Elevation angle (E)** is the vertical angle measured *upward from the local horizontal plane* of the Earth Station to the satellite line-of-sight. Its range is $0^{\circ}$ to $90^{\circ}$ **(1 mark)**. Together, $(A, E)$ constitute the **look angles** that the antenna mount must track; for a geostationary satellite these are constants in time **(1 mark)**.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Why is the **maximum Earth-central angle** subtended at the Earth's centre by an earth station to a geostationary satellite limited to approximately $81.3^{\circ}$? What is its physical significance?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
At the extreme, the elevation angle $E \to 0^{\circ}$ (satellite on the horizon). Setting $E = 0$ in the elevation formula gives:

$$
\cos\beta = R_e / R_s
$$

With $R_e = 6{,}378$ km and $R_s = 42{,}164$ km:

$$
\beta_{max} = \cos^{-1}(0.1513) \approx 81.3^{\circ}
$$

**(1 mark)** for the formula. The **physical significance** is that an Earth Station cannot communicate with a geostationary satellite whose sub-satellite point lies more than $81.3^{\circ}$ away in Earth-central angle — beyond this the satellite is geometrically below the local horizon **(1 mark)**. The **cone of visibility** of a GEO satellite therefore covers only ~$42\%$ of the Earth's surface; polar regions are unreachable by GEO constellations, motivating **highly-inclined elliptical orbits (HEO)** like Molniya or **LEO/MEO constellations** like Iridium **(1 mark)**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **Earth-central angle $\beta$** between an Earth Station and the sub-satellite point of a geostationary satellite. State clearly the meaning of every symbol used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An Earth Station is located at latitude $\lambda_e = 20^{\circ}\text{N}$ and longitude $\phi_e = 75^{\circ}\text{E}$. A geostationary satellite is parked at longitude $\phi_{ss} = 83^{\circ}\text{E}$. Compute the **slant range** $d$, the **elevation angle** $E$, and the **azimuth angle** $A$ of the antenna. Take $R_e = 6{,}378$ km and $R_s = 42{,}164$ km. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Solution to (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Let $\lambda_e$ be the latitude of the Earth Station, $\phi_e$ its longitude and $\phi_{ss}$ the longitude of the sub-satellite point on the equator. Place the origin at the Earth's centre $O$, with $z$-axis along the spin axis.

**(1 mark)** Unit vector from $O$ to the Earth Station $E$:

$$
\hat{e}_{ES} = (\cos\lambda_e\cos\phi_e,\;\cos\lambda_e\sin\phi_e,\;\sin\lambda_e)
$$

**(1 mark)** Unit vector from $O$ to the sub-satellite point $S'$ (which lies on the equator):

$$
\hat{e}_{SS} = (\cos\phi_{ss},\;\sin\phi_{ss},\;0)
$$

**(1 mark)** The dot product gives the cosine of the included angle:

$$
\cos\beta = \hat{e}_{ES}\cdot\hat{e}_{SS} = \cos\lambda_e\cos\phi_e\cos\phi_{ss} + \cos\lambda_e\sin\phi_e\sin\phi_{ss}
$$

**(1 mark)** Applying the identity $\cos A\cos B + \sin A\sin B = \cos(A-B)$:

$$
\cos\beta = \cos\lambda_e\cos(\phi_e - \phi_{ss})
$$

**(1 mark)** Defining the longitude gap $\phi = \phi_e - \phi_{ss}$:

$$
\cos\beta = \cos\lambda_e\cos\phi
$$

**(1 mark)** Final expression (boxed, since this is the KTU expected answer format):

$$
\boxed{\;\beta = \cos^{-1}(\cos\lambda_e \cdot \cos\phi)\;}
$$

**(1 mark)** Symbol table:

- $\lambda_e$ — Earth Station latitude (positive North).
- $\phi_e$ — Earth Station longitude (positive East).
- $\phi_{ss}$ — Sub-satellite longitude (positive East).
- $\phi = \phi_e - \phi_{ss}$ — East-West angular gap (signed).
- $\beta$ — Earth-central angle subtended at $O$ between $E$ and $S'$.

---

**Solution to (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Longitude gap (1 mark):**

$$
\phi = \phi_e - \phi_{ss} = 75^{\circ} - 83^{\circ} = -8^{\circ}
$$

Since $\phi < 0$, the satellite is **east** of the Earth Station.

**Step 2 — Earth-central angle (1 mark):**

$$
\cos\beta = \cos(20^{\circ})\cos(-8^{\circ}) = 0.9397 \times 0.9903 = 0.9305
$$

$$
\beta = \cos^{-1}(0.9305) = 21.55^{\circ}
$$

**Step 3 — Slant range (2 marks):**

$$
d = \sqrt{(6378)^{2} + (42164)^{2} - 2(6378)(42164)\cos(21.55^{\circ})}
$$

$$
d = \sqrt{40.679\times 10^{6} + 1777.80\times 10^{6} - 2(6378)(42164)(0.9305)}
$$

$$
d = \sqrt{1818.48\times 10^{6} - 500.32\times 10^{6}} = \sqrt{1318.16\times 10^{6}} \approx 36{,}306 \text{ km}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;d \approx 36{,}306 \text{ km}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Elevation angle (2 marks):**

$$
\tan E = \frac{\cos(21.55^{\circ}) - (6378/42164)}{\sin(21.55^{\circ})} = \frac{0.9305 - 0.1513}{0.3674} = \frac{0.7792}{0.3674} = 2.121
$$

$$
\boxed{\;E = \tan^{-1}(2.121) \approx 64.74^{\circ}\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Azimuth angle (1 mark):**

Since the satellite is **east** of the Earth Station ($\phi<0$), use Rule 5b:

$$
A = 180^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}\!\Bigl(\frac{\tan(-8^{\circ})}{\sin(20^{\circ})}\Bigr) = 180^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}\!\Bigl(\frac{-0.1405}{0.3420}\Bigr) = 180^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}(-0.411)
$$

$$
A = 180^{\circ} - (-22.34^{\circ}) = 202.34^{\circ}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;A \approx 202.3^{\circ}\ \text{(i.e., south-southwest bearing)}\;}
$$

**Geometric sanity:** South of West bearing with elevation ~$65^{\circ}$ is consistent with viewing a satellite whose sub-point is $8^{\circ}$ east of the Earth Station from latitude $20^{\circ}\text{N}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled geometric diagram (Earth–Satellite–Earth Station triangle), derive the expressions for the **elevation angle $E$** and the **slant range $d$** of an Earth Station antenna pointing at a geostationary satellite. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An Earth Station at latitude $\lambda_e = 10^{\circ}\text{S}$ and longitude $\phi_e = 40^{\circ}\text{E}$ is to communicate with a geostationary satellite at $\phi_{ss} = 60^{\circ}\text{E}$. Determine the **azimuth angle**, **elevation angle** and **slant range** of the antenna pointing direction. Given $R_e = 6{,}378$ km and $R_s = 42{,}164$ km. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Solution to (a) — Derivations (7 marks):**

Consider $\triangle OES$ where $O$ is the Earth's centre, $E$ the Earth Station and $S$ the satellite. $OE = R_e$, $OS = R_s$ and the angle at $O$ is $\beta$.

**(1 mark)** Apply the Law of Cosines in $\triangle OES$:

$$
d^{2} = R_e^{2} + R_s^{2} - 2 R_e R_s \cos\beta \quad\Longrightarrow\quad d = \sqrt{R_e^{2} + R_s^{2} - 2 R_e R_s \cos\beta}
$$

**(2 marks)** Now resolve the line-of-sight $ES$ at the Earth Station. The interior angle of $\triangle OES$ at $E$ is $(90^{\circ} + E)$, because $EO$ points vertically downward while $ES$ makes an elevation $E$ above horizontal. Apply the Law of Sines:

$$
\frac{\sin\beta}{d} = \frac{\sin(90^{\circ}+E)}{R_s} = \frac{\cos E}{R_s}
$$

**(1 mark)** Cross-multiplying:

$$
\cos E = \frac{R_s \sin\beta}{d}
$$

**(1 mark)** Apply the Law of Sines again, but to express $\cos E$ purely in $\beta$:

Using $d = R_e/\sin(90^{\circ}+E)$ … a cleaner route is to project $OS$ onto the line $OE$ extended. From the cosine rule on $\triangle OES$ viewed along $OE$:

$$
R_s\cos\beta = R_e + d\sin E
$$

**(1 mark)** Solving for $\tan E$:

$$
d\sin E = R_s\cos\beta - R_e,\qquad d\cos E = R_s\sin\beta
$$

$$
\tan E = \frac{R_s\cos\beta - R_e}{R_s\sin\beta} = \frac{\cos\beta - R_e/R_s}{\sin\beta}
$$

**(1 mark)** Final boxed formula:

$$
\boxed{\;E = \tan^{-1}\!\Bigl(\frac{\cos\beta - R_e/R_s}{\sin\beta}\Bigr)\;}
$$

---

**Solution to (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Longitude gap (1 mark):**

$$
\phi = \phi_e - \phi_{ss} = 40^{\circ} - 60^{\circ} = -20^{\circ}
$$

Negative ⇒ satellite is **east** of the Earth Station.

**Step 2 — Earth-central angle (1 mark):**

$$
\cos\beta = \cos(-10^{\circ})\cos(-20^{\circ}) = 0.9848 \times 0.9397 = 0.9254
$$

$$
\beta = \cos^{-1}(0.9254) = 22.32^{\circ}
$$

**Step 3 — Slant range (2 marks):**

$$
d = \sqrt{(6378)^{2} + (42164)^{2} - 2(6378)(42164)\cos(22.32^{\circ})}
$$

$$
d = \sqrt{1818.48\times 10^{6} - 500.97\times 10^{6}} = \sqrt{1317.51\times 10^{6}} \approx 36{,}298 \text{ km}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;d \approx 36{,}298 \text{ km}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Elevation angle (2 marks):**

$$
\tan E = \frac{\cos(22.32^{\circ}) - 0.1513}{\sin(22.32^{\circ})} = \frac{0.9254 - 0.1513}{0.3796} = \frac{0.7741}{0.3796} = 2.039
$$

$$
\boxed{\;E \approx 63.87^{\circ}\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Azimuth angle (1 mark):**

Satellite east ⇒ Rule 5b:

$$
A = 180^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}\!\Bigl(\frac{\tan(-20^{\circ})}{\sin(-10^{\circ})}\Bigr)
$$

$$
= 180^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}\!\Bigl(\frac{-0.3640}{-0.1736}\Bigr) = 180^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}(2.097)
$$

$$
= 180^{\circ} - 64.49^{\circ} = 115.51^{\circ}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;A \approx 115.5^{\circ}\ \text{(south-east bearing)}\;}
$$

**Geometric sanity:** Elevation $64^{\circ}$ with a south-east bearing for a station in the southern hemisphere looking at a satellite $20^{\circ}$ to the east — consistent.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on Look-Angle Calculation (top 6 pitfalls):**
>
> 1. **Sign of $\phi$:** Writing $\phi = \phi_e - \phi_{ss}$ vs $\phi_{ss} - \phi_e$ without declaring the convention. *(−2 marks lost in part (a) and cascade error into $A$.)*
> 2. **Wrong azimuth rule:** Applying the "west" rule to an "east" satellite or vice-versa. Always draw a quick compass sketch before substituting.
> 3. **Radian / degree mode:** Forgetting the calculator is in radian mode when using $\cos^{-1}$ or $\tan^{-1}$. *A single factor of $57.3$ ruins all three outputs.*
> 4. **Latitude sign for southern hemisphere:** $\lambda_e = -10^{\circ}$ for southern latitudes — but $\sin\lambda_e$ is negative; the formula still works as long as the convention is consistent.
> 5. **Geometric sanity skipped:** Reporting $E < 0^{\circ}$ or $E > 90^{\circ}$ without flagging it. KTU examiners reward a one-line "sanity check" comment.
> 6. **Missing symbol table:** In a 7-mark derivation sub-part, a clear boxed final formula plus a symbol legend is mandatory; partial credit is severely reduced without it.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Look Angles** = $(A, E)$, the two angles an Earth Station antenna must point at to "see" a GEO satellite.
- [x] **Inputs:** Earth Station latitude $\lambda_e$, longitude $\phi_e$, and satellite longitude $\phi_{ss}$.
- [x] **Longitude gap:** $\phi = \phi_e - \phi_{ss}$ (signed).
- [x] **Earth-central angle:** $\beta = \cos^{-1}(\cos\lambda_e \cdot \cos\phi)$ — comes from the spherical dot-product of the two unit vectors.
- [x] **Slant range:** $d = \sqrt{R_e^{2}+R_s^{2}-2R_eR_s\cos\beta}$ — Law of Cosines in $\triangle OES$.
- [x] **Elevation:** $E = \tan^{-1}\!\bigl[(\cos\beta - R_e/R_s)/\sin\beta\bigr]$ — vertical tilt above local horizon.
- [x] **Azimuth (west):** $A = 180^{\circ} + \tan^{-1}\!\bigl(\tan\phi/\sin\lambda_e\bigr)$ when satellite is west.
- [x] **Azimuth (east):** $A = 180^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}\!\bigl(\tan\phi/\sin\lambda_e\bigr)$ when satellite is east.
- [x] **Constants:** $R_e = 6{,}378$ km, $R_s = 42{,}164$ km, $h = 35{,}786$ km.
- [x] **Visibility limit:** $\beta_{max} \approx 81.3^{\circ}$, so a GEO satellite cannot serve latitudes above $\approx 81.3^{\circ}$.
- [x] **Always box** the final $A$, $E$ and $d$ in a KTU answer.
- [x] **Always include a one-line geometric sanity check** — e.g., "for $\lambda_e = 20^{\circ}\text{N}$ and satellite east, an elevation of $65^{\circ}$ and bearing $\approx 202^{\circ}$ are consistent."
- [x] **Slant range feeds** free-space path loss and one-way propagation delay ($d/c$); remember for link-budget questions.
- [x] **Azimuth formula breaks at the poles** ($\sin\lambda_e \to 0$); expect a separate argument in any *exceptional geometry* sub-question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coverage Angle and Slant Range
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Coverage Angle and Slant Range

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Coverage Angle (η)

**Coverage Angle** is the angle measured at the **satellite**, subtended between the satellite's **nadir direction** (the line joining the satellite to the Earth's center, extended) and the **line-of-sight** from the satellite to the edge of the **Earth's footprint** (i.e., the earth station at the limit of visibility).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** The coverage angle of a satellite is the angle at the satellite between the local vertical (nadir line) and the line joining the satellite to an earth station located at the edge of the coverage region on Earth's surface.

Mathematically:

$$\eta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{R}{R+h}\cos E\right)$$

where:
- $R$ = Radius of the Earth ≈ **6371 km** (or **6400 km** in some texts)
- $h$ = Orbital altitude of the satellite above Earth's surface
- $E$ = Elevation angle at the earth station

---

### 1.2 Slant Range (d)

**Slant Range** is the **line-of-sight distance** between the earth station antenna and the satellite antenna. It is the actual path length traversed by the electromagnetic signal in free space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Slant range is NOT the orbital altitude.** It is always **greater than or equal to** the altitude $h$, and reaches its maximum value when the elevation angle $E$ is at its minimum.

For minimum elevation $E_{\min}$ (typically **5°** to **10°** in KTU problems), the slant range is **maximum** ($d_{\max}$).

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Lighthouse and the Beach":**
> Imagine standing on a beach at night. A lighthouse is mounted high on a cliff (analogous to a satellite). The beam of light from the lighthouse hits the beach at an angle — this angle from the sand (horizontal) is the **elevation angle** $E$. The straight-line distance from your eyes to the lighthouse bulb is the **slant range** $d$. The angle at the lighthouse between the vertical (the pole) and the line to you is the **coverage angle** $\eta$. As you walk further away, $E$ decreases, $d$ increases, and $\eta$ increases — but only up to a point where the Earth's curvature hides the lighthouse (the **horizon**).
>
> For satellites, the same geometry holds, but the "cliff" is **35,786 km high** for a geostationary orbit, and the "beach" curves significantly over such distances.

---

### 1.4 KTU 2024 Scheme Relevance

| Parameter | Typical KTU Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Minimum Elevation $E_{\min}$ | **5°** | Prevents excessive path loss and terrestrial interference |
| Earth's Radius $R$ | **6371 km** | Standard IUGG value |
| GEO Altitude $h$ | **35,786 km** | Geostationary orbit |
| Maximum Coverage $\eta_{\max}$ | ~ **8.66°** (GEO, 5° elev) | Defines satellite's visible Earth footprint |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A higher altitude $h$ produces a **larger coverage angle** and hence a **larger footprint on Earth**, allowing the satellite to serve a greater geographical area. This is why LEO satellites need constellations — their individual coverage is small.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Earth–Station–Satellite Geometry

Consider three points forming a triangle:
- **O** = Center of the Earth
- **E** = Earth Station
- **S** = Satellite

The triangle **OES** has sides:
- $OE = R$ (Earth's radius)
- $OS = R + h$ (distance from Earth's center to satellite)
- $ES = d$ (slant range)

The interior angles are:
- At **O** = Central angle **γ** (determines the size of the footprint on Earth's surface)
- At **E** = $90° + E$ (since the local horizontal at E is perpendicular to the radius OE)
- At **S** = Coverage angle **η** (measured from nadir to the earth station direction)

The **angle sum** condition gives the fundamental geometric identity:

$$\gamma + \eta + (90° + E) = 180°$$

which simplifies to:

$$\boxed{\gamma + \eta + E = 90°}$$

This single identity is the **master equation** for all KTU numerical problems on this topic.

---

### 2.2 Why These Angles Matter (The "Why")

- **Elevation Angle (E)**: Determines the **path length through the atmosphere**. A small $E$ means a long atmospheric path → more rain attenuation, more noise pickup, and possible obstruction by terrain/horizon.
- **Coverage Angle (η)**: Determines the **angular "reach" of the satellite's antenna beam** over the Earth's surface. The larger the $\eta$, the larger the geographic area visible to the satellite.
- **Central Angle (γ)**: Determines the **arc length on Earth's surface** from the sub-satellite point to the edge of the footprint. The footprint radius on Earth is approximately $R \cdot \gamma$ (in radians).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` for absolute value to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Coverage Angle | $\eta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{R \cos E}{R+h}\right)$ | degrees or radians |
| 2 | Slant Range (Law of Cosines) | $d = \sqrt{R^{2} + (R+h)^{2} - 2R(R+h)\cos\gamma}$ | km |
| 3 | Slant Range (Law of Sines form) | $d = \dfrac{(R+h)\sin\gamma}{\cos E}$ | km |
| 4 | Central Angle | $\gamma = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{R\cos E}{R+h}\right)$ | from cos(γ) = sin(E+η) |
| 5 | Footprint Radius (on Earth) | $r_{\text{foot}} = R \cdot \gamma_{\max}$ | km (γ in radians) |
| 6 | Angle Sum Identity | $\gamma + \eta + E = 90°$ | universal |
| 7 | Maximum Coverage | $\eta_{\max} = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{R\cos E_{\min}}{R+h}\right)$ | at $E = E_{\min}$ |
| 8 | Maximum Slant Range | $d_{\max} = \dfrac{(R+h)\sin\gamma_{\max}}{\cos E_{\min}}$ | km |
| 9 | Maximum Central Angle | $\gamma_{\max} = 90° - E_{\min} - \eta_{\max}$ | degrees |
| 10 | Path Loss (related) | $L_p = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})$ | dB (for reference) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Earth's Radius Convention:** KTU problems may use $R = 6400$ km (older Indian textbooks) or $R = 6371$ km (modern/WGS-84). **Always check** which value the problem statement specifies.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Satellite Footprint Planning**: Coverage angle determines the **antenna beamwidth** required to illuminate the desired service area. A wide-beam satellite antenna is needed when $\eta_{\max}$ is large.
- **Link Budget Design**: Slant range directly enters the **free-space path loss** equation. A GEO satellite at $E = 5°$ has $d \approx 41{,}124$ km — the path loss is enormous (~ 200 dB at C-band).
- **Rain Margin Design**: Lower elevation angles → longer atmospheric path → requires higher **rain fade margin** in the link budget.
- **Frequency Coordination**: Minimum elevation angle of **5°** is a coordination standard to limit interference between geostationary satellites.
- **Constellation Design**: LEO satellites (e.g., Starlink at $h \approx 550$ km) have small $\eta_{\max}$ (~ 4°–8°), so many satellites are needed for continuous global coverage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Coverage Angle $\eta$

**Starting Geometry:** Triangle $OES$ as defined in §2.1.

**Step 1 — Identify the angle at E:**
The radius $OE$ is perpendicular to the local horizontal at the earth station. The elevation angle $E$ is measured from the horizontal *upward* to the line $ES$. Therefore, the interior angle of the triangle at vertex $E$ (i.e., $\angle OES$) is the sum of the right angle (radius to horizontal) and the elevation angle:

$$\angle OES = 90° + E$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Law of Sines to triangle OES:**

$$\frac{OE}{\sin(\angle OSE)} = \frac{OS}{\sin(\angle OES)} = \frac{ES}{\sin(\angle EOS)}$$

Substituting the sides and angles:

$$\frac{R}{\sin\eta} = \frac{R+h}{\sin(90° + E)} = \frac{d}{\sin\gamma}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the middle ratio:**

$$\sin(90° + E) = \cos E$$

So the first two ratios become:

$$\frac{R}{\sin\eta} = \frac{R+h}{\cos E}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\eta$:**

$$\sin\eta = \frac{R \cos E}{R+h}$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{R \cos E}{R+h}\right)\;}$$

**Why this works:** As $E \to 90°$ (satellite overhead), $\cos E \to 0$, so $\eta \to 0$ (satellite sees the earth station directly below). As $E \to 0°$ (station at horizon), $\cos E \to 1$, so $\eta$ is maximum.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Slant Range $d$

**Method A — From the Law of Cosines:**

**Step 1 — Write the Law of Cosines for side $ES = d$ opposite angle $\gamma$ at $O$:**

$$d^{2} = OE^{2} + OS^{2} - 2 \cdot OE \cdot OS \cdot \cos(\angle EOS)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known quantities:**

$$d^{2} = R^{2} + (R+h)^{2} - 2R(R+h)\cos\gamma$$

**Step 3 — Take the square root:**

$$\boxed{\;d = \sqrt{R^{2} + (R+h)^{2} - 2R(R+h)\cos\gamma}\;}$$

---

**Method B — From the Law of Sines (more useful for numerical computation):**

**Step 1 — From the third ratio in §3.1 Step 2:**

$$\frac{d}{\sin\gamma} = \frac{R+h}{\cos E}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $d$:**

$$\boxed{\;d = \frac{(R+h)\sin\gamma}{\cos E}\;}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Central Angle $\gamma$ in terms of $E$

**Step 1 — Use the angle sum identity:**

$$\gamma + \eta + E = 90° \;\Rightarrow\; \gamma = 90° - E - \eta$$

**Step 2 — Apply the cosine of both sides:**

$$\cos\gamma = \cos(90° - E - \eta) = \sin(E + \eta)$$

**Step 3 — Expand $\sin(E + \eta)$ using angle addition:**

$$\cos\gamma = \sin E \cos\eta + \cos E \sin\eta$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\sin\eta = R\cos E/(R+h)$ from §3.1:**

$$\cos\gamma = \sin E \cos\eta + \cos E \cdot \frac{R\cos E}{R+h}$$

This is the most general form. For KTU numerical problems, it is easier to compute $\eta$ first and then find $\gamma = 90° - E - \eta$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Problem)

> **Problem:** A geostationary satellite is at an altitude of **35,786 km** above Earth's surface. The minimum elevation angle at the earth station is **5°**. Take Earth's radius $R = 6371$ km. Calculate:
> (a) The coverage angle $\eta_{\max}$
> (b) The maximum slant range $d_{\max}$
> (c) The central angle $\gamma_{\max}$ and the approximate radius of the footprint on Earth.

**Given:** $R = 6371$ km, $h = 35{,}786$ km, $E_{\min} = 5°$.

**Step 1 — Compute $(R + h)$:**

$$R + h = 6371 + 35786 = 42157 \text{ km}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\cos E_{\min}$:**

$$\cos 5° = 0.99619$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\sin\eta_{\max}$:**

$$\sin\eta_{\max} = \frac{R \cos E_{\min}}{R+h} = \frac{6371 \times 0.99619}{42157} = \frac{6346.61}{42157} = 0.15054$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\eta_{\max}$:**

$$\eta_{\max} = \sin^{-1}(0.15054) = 8.66°$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\gamma_{\max}$ from the angle sum identity:**

$$\gamma_{\max} = 90° - E_{\min} - \eta_{\max} = 90° - 5° - 8.66° = 76.34°$$

**Step 6 — Compute maximum slant range using Method B:**

$$d_{\max} = \frac{(R+h)\sin\gamma_{\max}}{\cos E_{\min}} = \frac{42157 \times \sin 76.34°}{0.99619}$$

Compute $\sin 76.34° = 0.97165$:

$$d_{\max} = \frac{42157 \times 0.97165}{0.99619} = \frac{40962.7}{0.99619} = 41120.3 \text{ km}$$

**Step 7 — Verification using Law of Cosines (Method A):**

$$d^{2} = R^{2} + (R+h)^{2} - 2R(R+h)\cos\gamma_{\max}$$

$$d^{2} = (6371)^{2} + (42157)^{2} - 2 \times 6371 \times 42157 \times \cos 76.34°$$

Compute each term:
- $R^{2} = 40{,}589{,}641$
- $(R+h)^{2} = 1{,}777{,}214{,}249$
- $2R(R+h) = 2 \times 6371 \times 42157 = 537{,}130{,}094$
- $\cos 76.34° = 0.23620$

$$d^{2} = 40{,}589{,}641 + 1{,}777{,}214{,}249 - 537{,}130{,}094 \times 0.23620$$

$$d^{2} = 1{,}817{,}803{,}890 - 126{,}870{,}128 = 1{,}690{,}933{,}762$$

$$d = \sqrt{1{,}690{,}933{,}762} \approx 41{,}121 \text{ km} \; \checkmark$$

Both methods agree.

**Step 8 — Footprint radius on Earth:**

Convert $\gamma_{\max}$ to radians:

$$\gamma_{\max} = 76.34° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.3324 \text{ rad}$$

$$r_{\text{foot}} = R \cdot \gamma_{\max} = 6371 \times 1.3324 = 8488 \text{ km}$$

**Final Answer:** $\eta_{\max} = 8.66°$, $d_{\max} \approx 41{,}121$ km, $\gamma_{\max} = 76.34°$, footprint radius $\approx 8488$ km.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Symbol | Function of $E$ | Behaviour at $E = 90°$ (zenith) | Behaviour at $E = E_{\min}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Slant Range | $d$ | Decreasing | $d = h$ (minimum) | $d = d_{\max}$ (maximum) |
| Coverage Angle | $\eta$ | Decreasing | $\eta = 0°$ | $\eta = \eta_{\max}$ |
| Central Angle | $\gamma$ | Decreasing | $\gamma = 0°$ | $\gamma = \gamma_{\max}$ |
| Path Length (atmospheric) | $\propto 1/\sin E$ | Increasing | Minimum (1) | Maximum ($1/\sin 5° \approx 11.5$) |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick:** Whenever a problem gives $E_{\min}$ and asks for "maximum" anything, you are evaluating at $E = E_{\min}$. The word **"maximum"** in KTU problems on this topic is almost always the value corresponding to the **edge of the coverage zone**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Geometry of the Earth–Station–Satellite System

The following block diagram captures the **functional relationships** between the three angles, the slant range, and the orbital altitude. It is suitable for **derivation mapping** and is reproducible in any Mermaid renderer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Orbital Inputs: R, h, Emin"] --> B["Compute R_plus_h = R + h"]
    B --> C["Compute sin_eta = R cos Emin / R_plus_h"]
    C --> D["Coverage Angle eta_max = arcsin sin_eta"]
    D --> E["Central Angle gamma_max = 90 - Emin - eta_max"]
    E --> F["Slant Range d_max = R_plus_h sin gamma_max / cos Emin"]
    E --> G["Footprint Radius r_foot = R gamma_max in radians"]
    F --> H["Final Output Block: d_max, eta_max, gamma_max"]
    G --> H
    D --> H
    A --> I["Limiting Cases Check"]
    I --> J["E = 90 deg: d=h, eta=0, gamma=0"]
    I --> K["E = 0 deg horizon: eta_max approx 90 deg - horizon offset"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Solving a KTU Numerical

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Read R, h, Emin from problem"] --> P2["Compute R+h"]
    P2 --> P3["Compute sin_eta using R cos Emin / R+h"]
    P3 --> P4["eta = arcsin of step 3"]
    P4 --> P5["gamma = 90 - E - eta"]
    P5 --> P6["d = R+h sin gamma / cos E"]
    P6 --> P7["Verify using law of cosines"]
    P7 --> P8["Report final values with units"]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Description (for sketch in answer book)

> [!NOTE]
