# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Define parking orbit. Why is it used in satellite launching missions?**

**Model Answer:**
A *parking orbit* is a temporary low-altitude circular orbit (typically LEO) into which a launch vehicle first places its payload after upper stage cutoff. It is used because it allows **orbital parameter verification** (apogee, perigee, inclination) via ground tracking before committing the spacecraft to the more energetic **Hohmann transfer** or **geostationary transfer orbit (GTO)**. If the parking orbit parameters are off-nominal, mission controllers can **correct them with small trim burns** before the critical apogee kick motor (AKM) firing — saving mission fuel and ensuring precise final-orbit insertion. (3 marks: 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for justification.)

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]
**State Kepler's three laws of planetary motion and write the mathematical form of the third law.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **First Law (Law of Ellipses):** Every planet/satellite moves in an elliptical orbit with the central body (Earth) at one of the two foci.
2. **Second Law (Law of Equal Areas):** The radius vector from the central body to the satellite sweeps out equal areas in equal time intervals. (Implies the satellite moves faster at perigee and slower at apogee.)
3. **Third Law (Harmonic Law):** The square of the orbital period is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis.

$$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{\mu} \, a^3$$

where *T* = orbital period (s), *a* = semi-major axis (m), *μ* = 3.986 × 10¹⁴ m³/s² for Earth. (3 marks: 1 each for the three laws, with the formula carrying 1 mark.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a) A communication satellite is to be transferred from a low Earth orbit (LEO) of radius 7,000 km to a geostationary orbit (GEO) of radius 42,164 km using a Hohmann transfer. Using μ = 3.986 × 10¹⁴ m³/s², calculate:**
**(i) The velocity in the initial circular LEO.**
**(ii) The velocity at the perigee of the transfer ellipse.**
**(iii) The total Δv required.**
**(7 marks)**

**(b) Explain with a neat sketch the concept of geostationary orbit. Mention any three of its applications. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Step 1: Given data.**
$r_1 = 7{,}000 \text{ km} = 7.0 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$
$r_2 = 42{,}164 \text{ km} = 4.2164 \times 10^7 \text{ m}$
$\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}^2$

**Step 2: Velocity in initial circular LEO (i).**

$$v_1 = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r_1}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14}}{7.0 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{5.694 \times 10^7} \approx 7.546 \text{ km/s}$$

**Step 3: Semi-major axis of transfer ellipse.**

$$a_t = \frac{r_1 + r_2}{2} = \frac{7.0 \times 10^6 + 4.2164 \times 10^7}{2} = 2.4582 \times 10^7 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Velocity at perigee of transfer ellipse using vis-viva (ii).**

$$v_p = \sqrt{\mu \left( \frac{2}{r_1} - \frac{1}{a_t} \right)}$$

$$v_p = \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^{14} \left( \frac{2}{7.0 \times 10^6} - \frac{1}{2.4582 \times 10^7} \right)}$$

$$v_p = \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^{14} \left( 2.857 \times 10^{-7} - 4.068 \times 10^{-8} \right)}$$

$$v_p = \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 2.450 \times 10^{-7}} = \sqrt{9.766 \times 10^7} \approx 9.883 \text{ km/s}$$

**Step 5: First Δv (perigee burn).**

$$\Delta v_1 = v_p - v_1 = 9.883 - 7.546 = 2.337 \text{ km/s}$$

**Step 6: Velocity at apogee of transfer ellipse.**

$$v_a = \sqrt{\mu \left( \frac{2}{r_2} - \frac{1}{a_t} \right)} = \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times \left( \frac{2}{4.2164 \times 10^7} - \frac{1}{2.4582 \times 10^7} \right)}$$

$$v_a = \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times (4.7435 \times 10^{-8} - 4.068 \times 10^{-8})}$$

$$v_a = \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 6.755 \times 10^{-9}} = \sqrt{2.693 \times 10^6} \approx 1.641 \text{ km/s}$$

**Step 7: Velocity in final circular GEO.**

$$v_2 = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r_2}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14}}{4.2164 \times 10^7}} = \sqrt{9.453 \times 10^6} \approx 3.075 \text{ km/s}$$

**Step 8: Second Δv (apogee burn, iii).**

$$\Delta v_2 = v_2 - v_a = 3.075 - 1.641 = 1.434 \text{ km/s}$$

**Step 9: Total Δv.**

$$\Delta v_{total} = \Delta v_1 + \Delta v_2 = 2.337 + 1.434 \approx \mathbf{3.771 \text{ km/s}}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

A **geostationary orbit** is a unique circular orbit in the Earth's **equatorial plane** at an altitude of approximately **35,786 km** above mean sea level, with an **orbital period equal to Earth's sidereal rotation period (23 h 56 min 4 s)**. A satellite in this orbit appears *stationary* to a ground observer and hence requires **no tracking antennas** on the ground.

*(Sketch: Equatorial plane showing Earth, satellite at 35,786 km altitude, vector arrows for orbital velocity and Earth's rotation, both in the same direction — west to east.)*

**Three Applications:**
1. **Communication satellites** (e.g., INSAT, GSAT, Intelsat) for television broadcasting, telephony, and data relay.
2. **Weather and meteorological satellites** (e.g., INSAT-3D, GOES series) for continuous surveillance of one hemisphere.
3. **Navigation augmentation satellites** and **data relay satellites** (e.g., TDRSS) for global coverage with only three satellites.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern, Alternative Choice]

**(a) Derive the expression for the velocity of a satellite in a circular orbit around Earth. A satellite is to be placed in a circular orbit at an altitude of 1,500 km above the Earth's surface. Find the orbital velocity and the time period of revolution. Take μ = 3.986 × 10¹⁴ m³/s² and R_E = 6,371 km. (7 marks)**

**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the various subsystems of a satellite. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Step 1: Equate gravitational force to centripetal force.**

$$\frac{GMm}{r^2} = \frac{mv^2}{r} \implies \frac{\mu}{r^2} = \frac{v^2}{r} \implies v = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r}}$$

**Step 2: Calculate orbital radius.**

$r = R_E + h = 6{,}371 + 1{,}500 = 7{,}871 \text{ km} = 7.871 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$

**Step 3: Calculate orbital velocity.**

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14}}{7.871 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{5.064 \times 10^7} \approx 7.117 \text{ km/s}$$

**Step 4: Calculate time period using Kepler's third law.**

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{r^3}{\mu}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{(7.871 \times 10^6)^3}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}}$$

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{4.876 \times 10^{20}}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}} = 2\pi \sqrt{1.2234 \times 10^6}$$

$$T = 2\pi \times 1106.1 \approx 6{,}950 \text{ s} \approx 1.93 \text{ hours}$$

**Result:** Orbital velocity ≈ **7.12 km/s**; Time period ≈ **1.93 hours (116 minutes)**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

A communication satellite consists of two major sections: the **spacecraft bus (platform)** and the **payload**. The main subsystems are:

*(Block diagram representation:)*

| Subsystem | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Structure Subsystem** | Provides mechanical integrity; houses all components. |
| **Power Subsystem** | Solar arrays + batteries; regulates and distributes DC power. |
| **TTC (Telemetry, Tracking & Command)** | Communicates with ground station; receives commands, transmits health data. |
| **Attitude & Orbit Control (AOCS)** | Stabilizes satellite orientation using sensors (star trackers, sun sensors) and actuators (reaction wheels, thrusters). |
| **Thermal Control** | Maintains operating temperature using radiators, heaters, MLI blankets. |
| **Propulsion** | Apogee kick motor + reaction control thrusters for station keeping. |
| **Payload** | Transponders, antennas — the *business end* of the communication satellite. |
| **Command & Data Handling** | On-board computer that manages housekeeping and payload operations. |

The payload antennas (horn, parabolic, phased array) are mounted on the **Earth-facing panel**, while the **solar arrays** and **Earth sensors** point outward. The AKM is located at the base of the spacecraft.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to add R_E:** Many students compute vis-viva with altitude *h* instead of radius *r = R_E + h*. This single mistake causes a **2–3 mark deduction** and a cascade of wrong answers.
> 2. **Wrong units for μ:** μ must be in **m³/s²** (not km³/s²) when *r* is in meters. Mixing units is the #1 numerical error.
> 3. **Δv direction confusion:** Δv is *always* a **scalar magnitude of velocity change** (difference in speeds), not a vector — do not use Pythagoras unless the burn is non-tangential.
> 4. **Hohmann vs Direct Ascent:** A direct ascent to GEO from the surface is *not* Hohmann. Hohmann requires the **parking orbit first**.
> 5. **Inclination constraint:** A launch site at latitude *L* **cannot directly launch into an orbit with inclination less than L**. This rule frequently appears in 3-mark questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> ✅ **Satellite Launching — Quick-Recall Cheat Sheet**

- **Foundational Equation:** $v_{orb} = \sqrt{\mu/r}$ for circular orbit; $\mu_{Earth} = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}^2$.
- **Always use r = R_E + h** (radius from center of Earth, not altitude).
- **Vis-Viva Equation:** $v^2 = \mu(2/r - 1/a)$ — valid for *all* conic orbits.
- **Escape Velocity:** $v_{esc} = \sqrt{2}\,v_{orb}$ at the same radius.
- **Kepler's Third Law:** $T^2 = (4\pi^2/\mu)\,a^3$ — relates period and orbit size.
- **Hohmann Transfer:** *Two-burn*, *coplanar*, *most fuel-efficient* between two circular orbits.
- **Hohmann Transfer Time:** $T_t = \pi\sqrt{(r_1+r_2)^3/(8\mu)}$.
- **LEO altitude:** 160 – 2,000 km; speed ~ 7.8 km/s; period ~ 90 minutes.
- **GEO altitude:** 35,786 km; speed ~ 3.07 km/s; period 23 h 56 min 4 s.
- **Inclination rule:** $i_{min}$ = launch site latitude.
- **Apogee kick motor (AKM):** performs the critical circularization burn at GEO apogee.
- **Δv budget is everything** in mission design — every kg of propellant counts.
- **Total energy of orbit:** $E = -\mu/(2a)$ — negative for closed (bound) orbits.
- **Parking orbit is for safety** — to verify and correct before final burns.
- **GTO = Geostationary Transfer Orbit** — elliptical with apogee at GEO altitude, perigee at LEO.

> 🎯 **Final KTU Tip:** In numerical answers, always **box the final result**, **show the unit conversion step explicitly** (e.g., km → m), and **quote the value of μ and R_E at the start of the solution**. This earns *method marks* even if your final number has an arithmetic slip.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Orbital Effects in Communication Subsystem Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Orbital Effects in Communication Subsystem Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Orbital Effects in Communication Subsystem Performance** refer to the set of geometric, kinematic, and time-varying phenomena arising from the relative motion between a satellite in orbit and an Earth-based (or airborne/maritime) communication terminal, which directly influence the design, pointing, signal strength, latency, and link budget of the satellite communication subsystem.

In the strict KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, the major orbital effects include:

1. **Look Angle Geometry** — Elevation ($\theta_{el}$) and Azimuth ($\theta_{az}$) determination for Earth Station antenna pointing.
2. **Slant Range Variation** — Continuous change in the line-of-sight distance between Earth station and satellite.
3. **Propagation Delay** — Time taken by the EM wave to travel up-link and down-link.
4. **Coverage Footprint** — The ground region visible to the satellite, governed by the **beam coverage angle** ($\theta_{cov}$).
5. **Path Loss & Free-Space Attenuation** — Signal-level degradation with slant range and frequency.
6. **Orbital Eclipses** — Sun outages (Sun transit) and Earth eclipses that affect payload thermal/noise performance.
7. **Doppler Frequency Shift** — Variation in received frequency due to relative motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 1, the focus is on the *deterministic* orbital geometry effects. Atmospheric effects (rain fade, scintillation) are covered under later modules. The two highest-weightage sub-topics for board exams are **(a) Look Angle Calculation** and **(b) Slant Range / Coverage Angle derivation.**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a huge open field at night, and a drone carrying a flashlight is flying overhead on a circular path at a fixed height of 1 km.

- **You are the Earth Station.** The drone is the **satellite**.
- To see the flashlight beam, you must **tilt your head upward** by a specific angle — that tilt is the **elevation angle** ($\theta_{el}$).
- You must also **turn your body left or right** to face the drone — that rotation is the **azimuth angle** ($\theta_{az}$).
- The straight-line distance from your eyes to the drone is the **slant range** ($R$).
- The lit patch of ground the drone can illuminate is the **coverage footprint**.
- As the drone flies in a circle, the distance, tilt angle, and brightness of the light **keep changing** — these are the **orbital effects** on the communication link.

This is exactly how a real satellite appears to "move" across the sky to a ground observer, even when the satellite is in a nominally stationary (Geostationary) orbit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values** (used throughout KTU problems):
> - Mean Earth Radius: $R_e = 6371$ **km** (often rounded to **6378.14 km** at the equator)
> - Geostationary Orbit Radius: $r = 42164$ **km** (from Earth's center)
> - Geostationary Altitude: $h = 35786$ **km** above mean sea level
> - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Orbital Geometry — Step-by-Step Logic

For a Geostationary Earth Orbit (GEO) satellite, the satellite appears *almost* stationary in the sky but is not perfectly so. The communication subsystem must account for the following:

### Step 1 — Define Earth Station Position
The Earth station is located at some latitude $L$ and longitude difference $\lambda$ from the satellite's nadir (sub-satellite point). These two parameters — $L$ (latitude) and $\lambda$ (longitude difference) — uniquely fix the satellite's apparent position.

### Step 2 — Central Angle ($d$) Computation
The central angle $\angle EOS$ (Earth center to Earth station to Sub-satellite point) is computed using the spherical law of cosines:

$$d = \cos^{-1}(\cos L \cdot \cos \lambda)$$

This angle defines the arc on Earth's surface between the Earth station and the sub-satellite point.

### Step 3 — Elevation Angle ($\theta_{el}$)
The elevation angle is measured **above the local horizon** of the Earth station:

$$\theta_{el} = \tan^{-1}\left[\frac{\cos d - \dfrac{R_e}{R_e + h}}{\sin d}\right]$$

- If $\theta_{el} < 0°$, the satellite is **below the horizon** (not visible).
- For practical KTU problems, Earth stations are placed at $\theta_{el} \geq 5°$ to avoid ground clutter and atmospheric multipath.

### Step 4 — Azimuth Angle ($\theta_{az}$)
The azimuth is the compass bearing from the Earth station to the satellite, measured clockwise from **True North**:

$$\theta_{az} = 180° + \tan^{-1}\left[\frac{\tan \lambda}{\sin L}\right]$$

(Adjusted by quadrant correction for the Northern/Southern hemisphere.)

### Step 5 — Slant Range ($R_{sl}$)
Using the law of cosines in triangle $E$-$O$-$S$:

$$R_{sl} = \sqrt{(R_e + h)^2 + R_e^2 - 2 R_e (R_e + h) \cos d}$$

### Step 6 — Path Loss ($L_p$)
Free-space path loss is the dominant loss in satellite links:

$$L_p = \left(\frac{4 \pi R_{sl} f}{c}\right)^2 \quad \text{(dimensionless ratio)}$$

In decibels:

$$L_p \text{ [dB]} = 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(R_{sl} \text{ in km}) + 20 \log_{10}(f \text{ in MHz})$$

### Step 7 — Propagation Delay ($t_p$)
$$t_p = \frac{R_{sl}}{c}$$

For a GEO satellite, one-way delay ≈ **240 ms**; round-trip ≈ **480 ms**; double-hop (GEO-GEO) ≈ **500–600 ms**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used daily by satellite operators (ISRO, INTELSAT, SES) to plan **TT&C (Telemetry, Tracking & Command)** station locations, calculate **link budgets** in $\text{dBW}$, and design **hand-off strategies** for LEO/MEO constellations like Starlink and OneWeb.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Central Angle | $d = \cos^{-1}(\cos L \cos \lambda)$ | Degrees or radians |
| 2 | Elevation Angle | $\theta_{el} = \tan^{-1}\left[\dfrac{\cos d - R_e/(R_e+h)}{\sin d}\right]$ | Degrees |
| 3 | Azimuth Angle | $\theta_{az} = 180° + \tan^{-1}\left[\dfrac{\tan \lambda}{\sin L}\right]$ | Degrees (compass bearing) |
| 4 | Slant Range | $R_{sl} = \sqrt{(R_e+h)^2 + R_e^2 - 2R_e(R_e+h)\cos d}$ | km |
| 5 | Free-Space Path Loss (linear) | $L_p = (4 \pi R_{sl} f / c)^2$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Path Loss in dB | $L_p = 32.44 + 20\log R_{sl} + 20\log f$ | dB (R in km, f in MHz) |
| 7 | One-way Delay | $t_p = R_{sl} / c$ | seconds |
| 8 | Round-trip Delay (GEO) | $\approx 2 \cdot R_{sl} / c$ | ≈ 0.48 s |
| 9 | Coverage Half-Angle | $\theta_{cov} = \sin^{-1}\left[\dfrac{R_e}{R_e + h} \cdot \cos\theta_{el,\min}\right] - \theta_{el,\min}$ | Degrees |
| 10 | Footprint Radius (on ground) | $R_{fp} = R_e \cdot \theta_{cov}$ | km |
| 11 | Doppler Shift | $\Delta f = f \cdot v_{rel} / c$ | Hz |
| 12 | Eclipse Duration (worst case) | $T_e \le 72$ minutes per day | Minutes (around equinox) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When $\lambda$ and $L$ are both in degrees, **convert to radians** before applying trigonometric functions in calculator-based numerical problems, or be sure your calculator is in degree mode (it is by default in most KTU exams).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Elevation Angle Formula

**Starting Figure (conceptual):** Consider the Earth as a sphere of radius $R_e$, with center $O$. The Earth station is at point $E$ on the surface. The satellite is at point $S$, located at distance $R_e + h$ from $O$. The line $OE$ is the local vertical at the Earth station. The line $ES$ is the slant range. The angle $\angle OES$ is the co-elevation $(90° - \theta_{el})$.

**Step 1:** From the spherical law of cosines applied to triangle $O$-$E$-$S$ (with the arc $ES$ on Earth's surface subtending central angle $d$):

$$\cos d = \cos L \cdot \cos \lambda$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **law of cosines** to the *planar* triangle $O$-$E$-$S$:

$$(R_e + h)^2 = R_e^2 + R_{sl}^2 - 2 R_e R_{sl} \cos(90° + \theta_{el})$$

Note: The angle at $E$ between $OE$ (pointing toward Earth's center) and $ES$ (pointing to satellite) is $90° + \theta_{el}$ because $\theta_{el}$ is measured above the local horizon (perpendicular to $OE$).

Since $\cos(90° + \theta_{el}) = -\sin \theta_{el}$:

$$(R_e + h)^2 = R_e^2 + R_{sl}^2 + 2 R_e R_{sl} \sin \theta_{el}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $\sin \theta_{el}$:

$$\sin \theta_{el} = \frac{(R_e + h)^2 - R_e^2 - R_{sl}^2}{2 R_e R_{sl}}$$

**Step 4:** Use the law of cosines a second time, this time expressing $R_{sl}$ in terms of $d$:

$$R_{sl}^2 = R_e^2 + (R_e + h)^2 - 2 R_e (R_e + h) \cos d$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the identity $\cos d = \cos L \cos \lambda$ and simplify. After several lines of algebra (standard in textbooks), we obtain the **standard textbook form**:

$$\theta_{el} = \tan^{-1}\left[\frac{\cos d - \dfrac{R_e}{R_e+h}}{\sin d}\right]$$

**Step 6:** The alternative, more elegant form (often used in KTU numericals) is:

$$\theta_{el} = \cos^{-1}\left[\frac{R_e \sin d}{R_{sl}}\right] - 90°$$

These two forms are **mathematically equivalent** and may be used interchangeably.

## 3.2 Derivation of Slant Range from First Principles

**Given:** Earth radius $R_e$, orbit altitude $h$, and central angle $d$.

**Step 1:** In triangle $O$-$E$-$S$, by the law of cosines:

$$R_{sl}^2 = R_e^2 + (R_e + h)^2 - 2 R_e (R_e + h) \cos d$$

**Step 2:** Expand $(R_e + h)^2 = R_e^2 + 2R_e h + h^2$:

$$R_{sl}^2 = R_e^2 + R_e^2 + 2R_e h + h^2 - 2 R_e (R_e + h) \cos d$$

$$R_{sl}^2 = 2R_e^2 + 2R_e h + h^2 - 2R_e (R_e + h) \cos d$$

**Step 3:** Factor:

$$R_{sl}^2 = h^2 + 2R_e(R_e + h)(1 - \cos d)$$

**Step 4:** This is the **most compact form** for KTU numericals. The maximum slant range occurs when $d = 180°$ (satellite at the antipode, $\cos d = -1$):

$$R_{sl,\max} = R_e + h + R_e = 2R_e + h$$

**Step 5:** The minimum slant range occurs when $d = 0°$ (satellite directly overhead, $\cos d = 1$):

$$R_{sl,\min} = h$$

For GEO: $R_{sl,\min} = 35786$ km, $R_{sl,\max} \approx 41142$ km.

## 3.3 Derivation of Coverage Angle (Footprint Geometry)

**Step 1:** Consider an Earth station at the *edge* of the coverage footprint. The ray from the satellite to this station makes a half-angle $\theta_{cov}/2$ with the line from satellite to Earth's center.

**Step 2:** From the satellite's frame, the angle between the local vertical at the Earth station and the line-of-sight to satellite is the co-elevation $(90° - \theta_{el,\min})$.

**Step 3:** Apply the law of sines in triangle $O$-$E$-$S$:

$$\frac{\sin(\theta_{el,\min})}{R_e + h} = \frac{\sin(\angle ESO)}{R_e}$$

**Step 4:** Since $\angle EOS = d$ and the angles of the triangle sum to $180°$:

$$\angle ESO = 180° - d - (90° + \theta_{el,\min}) = 90° - d - \theta_{el,\min}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting and solving for the coverage half-angle:

$$\frac{\theta_{cov}}{2} = \sin^{-1}\left[\frac{R_e}{R_e + h} \cos \theta_{el,\min}\right] - \theta_{el,\min}$$

**Step 6:** Total beam coverage angle:

$$\theta_{cov} = 2 \left\{\sin^{-1}\left[\frac{R_e}{R_e + h} \cos \theta_{el,\min}\right] - \theta_{el,\min}\right\}$$

**Step 7:** Footprint radius on Earth's surface:

$$R_{fp} = R_e \cdot \theta_{cov} \quad \text{(with } \theta_{cov} \text{ in radians)}$$

**Numerical Illustration (KTU-style):** For a GEO satellite with $\theta_{el,\min} = 5°$:

$$\frac{R_e}{R_e+h} = \frac{6371}{42164} = 0.1510$$

$$\frac{\theta_{cov}}{2} = \sin^{-1}(0.1510 \times \cos 5°) - 5° = \sin^{-1}(0.1505) - 5°$$

$$\frac{\theta_{cov}}{2} = 8.65° - 5° = 3.65°$$

$$\theta_{cov} = 7.30° \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_{fp} = 6371 \times (7.30 \times \pi/180) \approx 811 \text{ km}$$

But this is the **half-beam**; the full visible Earth disc from GEO is $\theta_{cov} = 17.4°$ (footprint radius ≈ 4250 km), covering roughly **42% of Earth's surface**.

## 3.4 Propagation Delay Numerical (GEO)

$$t_p = \frac{R_{sl}}{c} = \frac{42164 \times 10^3}{3 \times 10^8} = 0.1405 \text{ s} \approx 240 \text{ ms (max)}$$

Round trip: $\approx 480$ ms. This is the famous **"satellite delay"** that makes GEO voice calls feel sluggish and is the reason **LEO constellations (Starlink)** target altitudes of 500–1200 km for sub-50 ms latency.

## 3.5 Eclipse & Sun Transit Outage (Qualitative)

- **Solar Eclipse:** During equinoxes, GEO satellite enters Earth's shadow for up to **72 minutes/day**. Solar panels fail → onboard batteries drain → thermal gradients distort antenna gain.
- **Sun Transit Outage:** For a few days around equinox, the Sun passes directly behind the satellite (as seen from Earth station). Solar noise temperature ($\sim 10,000$ K) overwhelms the receiver for **5–10 minutes/day** for ~7 days.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Look-Angle Geometry (Earth Station Frame)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Earth Center O"] --> B["Earth Station E on surface"]
    A --> C["Satellite S at altitude h"]
    B --> D["Local Horizon at Earth Station"]
    B --> E["Local Vertical OE"]
    C --> F["Sub-satellite point on Equator"]
    B --> G["Slant Range Line ES (Line of Sight)"]
    D --> H["Elevation Angle theta_el"]
    G --> H
    B --> I["Azimuth Angle theta_az measured from North"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The slant range $ES$ crosses the local horizon plane.
- The angle between $ES$ and the local horizon is $\theta_{el}$ (elevation).
- The compass rotation from True North to the projection of $ES$ on the ground is $\theta_{az}$.

## 4.2 Coverage Footprint Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Geostationary Satellite S"] --> B["Beam Cone with Half-Angle theta_cov/2"]
    B --> C["Footprint Disc on Earth Surface"]
    C --> D["Inner Zone: Elevation 90 to 75 degrees"]
    C --> E["Middle Zone: Elevation 75 to 30 degrees"]
    C --> F["Outer Zone: Elevation 30 to theta_el_min degrees"]
    F --> G["Edge of Coverage - EOC"]
    A --> H["Earth Center O"]
    H --> I["Earth Radius Re = 6371 km"]
    A --> J["Orbit Radius Re + h = 42164 km"]
```

## 4.3 Eclipse & Sun-Transit Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Orbit Geometry"] --> B["Equinox Period"]
    B --> C["Sun behind Satellite relative to Earth Station"]
    C --> D["Sun Transit Outage Window approx 5 to 10 min per day"]
    B --> E["Earth Shadow on Satellite"]
    E --> F["Solar Panels OFF"]
    F --> G["Battery Discharge for up to 72 min per day"]
    D --> H["Receiver Saturated by Solar Noise Temperature approx 10000 K"]
    G --> I["Thermal Distortion of Antenna Pattern"]
    H --> J["Link Degradation"]
    I --> J
```

## 4.4 Slant-Range vs. Elevation (Functional Relationship)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Elevation theta_el = 90 deg"] --> B["Slant Range = h = 35786 km (minimum)"]
    C["Elevation theta_el = 0 deg (horizon)"] --> D["Slant Range = 41142 km (maximum)"]
    E["Elevation theta_el = theta_el_min approx 5 deg"] --> F["Slant Range approx 40500 km"]
    G["Elevation theta_el = 45 deg"] --> H["Slant Range approx 37715 km"]
    I["Higher elevation equals lower path loss"] --> J["Better C/N and lower delay"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term *look angle* in satellite communication and list its two components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The term **look angle** refers to the pair of angles that an Earth station antenna must be set to in order to point directly at a satellite in orbit. The two components are:
> 1. **Elevation angle ($\theta_{el}$)** — the angle measured above the local horizontal plane of the Earth station.
> 2. **Azimuth angle ($\theta_{az}$)** — the compass bearing measured clockwise from True North to the projection of the slant range on the ground plane.
> Both are essential for accurate antenna tracking, especially for non-GEO satellites.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by *slant range* in a satellite link? Mention the maximum and minimum slant range for a GEO satellite.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **slant range** is the straight-line distance from the Earth station antenna to the satellite along the line-of-sight (LOS).
> - **Minimum slant range** = $h = 35786$ km (when satellite is directly overhead, $d = 0°$).
> - **Maximum slant range** $\approx 41142$ km (when the satellite is at the edge of visibility, $\theta_{el} \to 0°$).
> It determines the **free-space path loss** and the **propagation delay** of the link.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Note:** Per KTU ESE 2024 pattern, students answer either **Question A** or **Question B**.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` — **(14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **elevation angle** of an Earth station antenna pointing at a geostationary satellite. Clearly state all assumptions and define all variables. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An Earth station is located at latitude $L = 13°$ N and longitude difference $\lambda = 45°$ E from the sub-satellite point of a GEO satellite. Compute the **elevation** and **azimuth angles** of the antenna. Take $R_e = 6371$ km, $h = 35786$ km. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

1. **Assumptions:** Spherical Earth of radius $R_e$, circular orbit of radius $R_e + h$, and clear line of sight (no atmospheric refraction correction).
2. **Geometry:** Earth station $E$ on surface, satellite $S$ at distance $R_e + h$ from center $O$, sub-satellite point on the equator.
3. **Central angle** from spherical law of cosines:

$$d = \cos^{-1}(\cos L \cos \lambda)$$

4. **Apply law of cosines** in triangle $O$-$E$-$S$:

$$(R_e+h)^2 = R_e^2 + R_{sl}^2 - 2 R_e R_{sl}\cos(90°+\theta_{el})$$

5. Using $\cos(90°+\theta_{el}) = -\sin\theta_{el}$ and solving for $\theta_{el}$:

$$\theta_{el} = \tan^{-1}\left[\frac{\cos d - \dfrac{R_e}{R_e+h}}{\sin d}\right]$$

6. **Azimuth:**

$$\theta_{az} = 180° + \tan^{-1}\left[\frac{\tan \lambda}{\sin L}\right]$$

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $L = 13°$, $\lambda = 45°$.

**Step 1:** Compute $\cos L \cos \lambda$:

$$\cos 13° = 0.9744, \quad \cos 45° = 0.7071$$

$$\cos L \cos \lambda = 0.9744 \times 0.7071 = 0.6891$$

**Step 2:** Central angle:

$$d = \cos^{-1}(0.6891) = 46.43°$$

**Step 3:** Elevation:

$$\cos d - \frac{R_e}{R_e+h} = 0.6891 - \frac{6371}{42164} = 0.6891 - 0.1511 = 0.5380$$

$$\sin d = \sin 46.43° = 0.7245$$

$$\theta_{el} = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.5380}{0.7245}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.7426) = \boxed{36.6°}$$

**Step 4:** Azimuth:

$$\tan 45° = 1.0, \quad \sin 13° = 0.2250$$

$$\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1.0}{0.2250}\right) = \tan^{-1}(4.444) = 77.31°$$

$$\theta_{az} = 180° + 77.31° = \boxed{257.31°} \text{ (WSW direction)}$$

**Step 5 (Optional sanity check):** Slant range:

$$R_{sl} = \sqrt{42164^2 + 6371^2 - 2(6371)(42164)\cos 46.43°} \approx 37700 \text{ km}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — **(14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain **orbital eclipse** and **sun transit outage** in a GEO satellite. Discuss their impact on communication subsystem performance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a GEO satellite, the minimum elevation angle at an Earth station is limited to $5°$. Calculate the **(i)** coverage half-angle, **(ii)** footprint radius, and **(iii)** one-way propagation delay at this minimum elevation. Take $R_e = 6371$ km, $h = 35786$ km, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Eclipse & Sun Transit Explanation (7 marks):**

1. **Orbital Eclipse:** During the **equinoxes** (March 21 and September 23), the Sun, Earth, and GEO satellite are aligned such that the satellite passes through Earth's shadow. The eclipse lasts up to **72 minutes per day** around local midnight.
2. **Impact:** Solar panels cannot generate power → onboard **batteries discharge**, payload electronics experience **thermal cycling** that alters gain and noise figure, and the link is susceptible to **TWTA (Travelling Wave Tube Amplifier) linearity drift**.
3. **Sun Transit Outage:** For about 7 days around the equinoxes, the Sun, satellite, and Earth station are perfectly aligned. The Sun's disk acts as a powerful radio noise source.
4. **Impact:** Receiver **saturates** due to solar noise temperature ($\sim 10,000$ K), causing **C/N degradation** of 20–30 dB for 5–10 minutes per day. Communication link may be **completely lost** during the worst minutes.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $\theta_{el,\min} = 5°$, $R_e = 6371$ km, $h = 35786$ km.

**(i) Coverage half-angle:**

$$\frac{\theta_{cov}}{2} = \sin^{-1}\left[\frac{R_e}{R_e+h} \cos\theta_{el,\min}\right] - \theta_{el,\min}$$

$$\frac{R_e}{R_e+h} = \frac{6371}{42164} = 0.1511$$

$$0.1511 \times \cos 5° = 0.1511 \times 0.9962 = 0.1505$$

$$\sin^{-1}(0.1505) = 8.65°$$

$$\frac{\theta_{cov}}{2} = 8.65° - 5° = 3.65°$$

$$\theta_{cov}/2 = \boxed{3.65°}$$

**(ii) Footprint radius on Earth:**

The central angle subtended by the footprint at Earth's center is $\beta$ where:

$$\cos\beta = \frac{R_e}{R_e+h} \cdot \cos\theta_{el,\min} \cdot \cos(0) + \sin(0)\cdot\sin\theta_{el,\min}$$

Wait — using the standard textbook result, the central angle subtended is $\beta$:

For the edge of coverage, the central angle is:

$$\beta = \cos^{-1}\left[\frac{R_e}{R_e+h} \cos\theta_{el,\min}\right] - \theta_{el,\min} \quad \text{(in radians, the relationship is direct)}$$

$$R_{fp} = R_e \cdot \theta_{cov}/2 = 6371 \times (3.65° \times \pi/180)$$

$$R_{fp} = 6371 \times 0.0637 = \boxed{405.7 \text{ km}}$$

(Note: This is the **footprint radius from the sub-satellite point** corresponding to the minimum elevation contour.)

**(iii) Propagation delay:**

First find slant range at $\theta_{el} = 5°$:

$$R_{sl} = \sqrt{(R_e+h)^2 + R_e^2 - 2R_e(R_e+h)\cos d}$$

where $d$ is found from the geometry. Using the relationship:

$$R_{sl} = \frac{(R_e+h)\sin d}{\cos\theta_{el,\min}}$$

Alternatively, use direct calculation:

$$\cos d = \frac{R_e}{R_e+h}\sin\theta_{el,\min} + \cos\theta_{el,\min}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{R_e}{R_e+h}\right)^2}$$

This yields $d \approx 76.2°$, and $R_{sl} \approx 40,515$ km.

$$t_p = \frac{R_{sl}}{c} = \frac{40515 \times 10^3}{3 \times 10^8} = \boxed{0.1351 \text{ s} = 135.1 \text{ ms}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** $R_e$ and $h$ must both be in the same unit (km). Mixing km with m in path-loss equations is the #1 error.
> 2. **Degree vs. Radian Mode:** Ensure the calculator is in **degree mode** unless explicitly computing in radians. Inverse trig functions are extremely sensitive to this.
> 3. **Azimuth Formula Sign:** For Southern hemisphere latitudes ($L < 0°$) and for satellites east of the Earth station ($\lambda < 0°$), the formula requires a **quadrant correction**. Read the problem carefully.
> 4. **Path-Loss Formula:** The constant 32.44 holds only when $R$ is in **km** and $f$ in **MHz**. If $f$ is in **GHz**, use $L_p = 92.44 + 20\log R + 20\log f$.
> 5. **Forgetting the 180° offset** in azimuth — students often report azimuths like 77° instead of 257° and lose a mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Look angles** = Elevation + Azimuth; both needed to point an Earth station antenna.
- [x] **Central angle** $d$ depends only on Earth station latitude $L$ and longitude difference $\lambda$.
- [x] **Elevation** $\theta_{el}$ decreases as Earth station moves away from sub-satellite point; reaches $0°$ at the edge of GEO visibility.
- [x] **Azimuth** is always measured clockwise from True North.
- [x] **Slant range** $R_{sl}$ varies from $h$ (overhead) to $\sim 41142$ km (horizon) for GEO.
- [x] **Free-space path loss** $L_p$ increases with $f^2$ and $R_{sl}^2$ — higher frequency = more loss.
- [x] **Propagation delay** for GEO one-way is ~240 ms; round-trip ~480 ms; this is the *famous* satellite phone delay.
- [x] **Coverage footprint** = cone of visibility; radius $\sim 4250$ km for full GEO disc, $\sim 405$ km for $\theta_{el} \geq 5°$.
- [x] **Solar eclipse** affects satellite for up to 72 min/day during equinox; **sun transit outage** affects Earth station for 5–10 min/day.
- [x] **Standard KTU constants:** $R_e = 6371$ km, $h = 35786$ km, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $f$ in MHz for the 32.44 formula.
- [x] **Higher elevation = better C/N, lower path loss, lower delay** → always prefer high-elevation satellites in link budget.
- [x] **Remember to convert** $d$ in degrees when computing footprint radius in km.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Satellite Subsystems – Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1)**
> A **Satellite Subsystem** is an independent, functionally dedicated assembly of hardware, software, and supporting mechanisms within a spacecraft that collectively performs a specific mission-critical operation, such as power generation, attitude control, thermal regulation, telemetry, propulsion, or payload data handling. The integrated set of all these subsystems is what keeps the satellite alive, oriented, communicating, and operationally useful throughout its design lifetime in orbit.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, a communication satellite is treated as an engineered system built from **multiple coordinated subsystems**, each with a sharply defined responsibility. They are not just "parts" — they are *interdependent modules* that exchange data and power through the spacecraft's central data bus and electrical harness.

## Conceptual Analogy — The "Flying Hospital" Analogy

Imagine a satellite as a **fully automated flying hospital** that must survive alone in a hostile environment (space):

- The **Power Subsystem** is the *generator + fuel cells* — without electricity, nothing works.
- The **Thermal Subsystem** is the *HVAC system* — it keeps the "rooms" at the right temperature so patients (electronics) don't overheat or freeze.
- The **Attitude and Orbit Control Subsystem (AOCS)** is the *navigation + steering* — it keeps the hospital facing the right way and on the right path.
- The **Propulsion Subsystem** is the *engine room* — used for orbit corrections and de-orbit.
- The **TT&C Subsystem** is the *radio operator* — sends health reports to Earth and receives doctor’s orders.
- The **Communication Payload** is the *actual medical equipment* — the reason the hospital exists.
- The **Mechanical/Structural Subsystem** is the *building frame* — holds everything together against vibration and launch stress.

Without **any one** of these, the satellite cannot function for long. This is exactly what KTU examiners test — the **inter-dependence** of subsystems.

## List of Major Satellite Subsystems (KTU Module 1 Focus)

| # | Subsystem | KTU-Standard One-Line Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mechanical / Structural** | Provides rigidity, strength, and a frame to mount all components |
| 2 | **Power** | Generates, stores, and regulates electrical energy |
| 3 | **Thermal Control** | Maintains operating temperature range for all units |
| 4 | **Attitude & Orbit Control (AOCS)** | Stabilizes and points the satellite correctly |
| 5 | **Propulsion** | Provides thrust for orbit corrections and station-keeping |
| 6 | **Telemetry, Tracking & Command (TT&C)** | Two-way communication with ground for health & control |
| 7 | **Communication Payload (Transponder)** | The actual mission — relays user signals |
| 8 | **Antenna** | Transmits and receives RF signals |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Distinction to Remember**
> The **Payload** (communication transponder) is the *mission-specific* subsystem. All others are called the **"Bus"** or **"Platform"** of the satellite. Board questions often test: *"Differentiate between payload and bus subsystems."*

## Physical Constants and Standard Metrics to Memorize

- **GEO orbital altitude:** $\mathbf{35{,}786 \text{ km}}$ above Earth’s equator
- **LEO typical altitude range:** $\mathbf{160 \text{ km} - 2000 \text{ km}}$
- **MEO typical altitude range:** $\mathbf{2000 \text{ km} - 35{,}786 \text{ km}}$
- **Solar flux constant (AM0):** $\mathbf{1361 \text{ W/m}^2}$
- **GEO orbital period:** $\mathbf{23 \text{ h } 56 \text{ min}}$ (sidereal day)
- **LEO orbital period:** $\mathbf{\approx 90 \text{ minutes}}$
- **Standard bus voltage on modern GEO satellites:** $\mathbf{28 \text{ V DC}}$ or $\mathbf{42 \text{ V DC}}$ or $\mathbf{100 \text{ V DC}}$
- **Design lifetime (modern GEO comm. satellite):** $\mathbf{15 \text{ years}}$
- **Safe operating temperature range of electronics:** $\mathbf{-40^\circ C \text{ to } +85^\circ C}$ (typical commercial-grade)

## Why Subsystem Design Matters (KTU Examiner Perspective)

Every subsystem design choice is governed by four universal constraints that examiners love to ask:

1. **Mass** — every kg launched costs money (typically **\$10,000–\$25,000 per kg** to LEO)
2. **Power** — limited by solar array size and battery capacity
3. **Thermal** — space is a vacuum; only radiation governs heat loss
4. **Reliability** — no repairs possible after launch; components are *space-qualified*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Point**
> A satellite is designed with **redundancy** (cold-redundant or hot-redundant units) for every critical subsystem because failure in space is **irreversible**. This is a guaranteed 2- to 3-mark question in Part A.

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanical / Structural Subsystem

The structural subsystem is the **skeleton** of the satellite. It must survive the violent vibration and acoustic noise of launch, the vacuum of space, and the thermal stresses of alternating sunlight/shadow.

**Primary functions:**
- Hold all subsystems in correct geometric alignment
- Provide mechanical interface with the launch vehicle
- Maintain alignment of antennas and sensors (pointing accuracy)
- Protect against micro-meteoroid and orbital debris (MMOD) impacts

**Standard construction materials:**

- **Aluminum alloys (Al 6061, Al 7075):** Light, strong, easy to machine → main bus structure
- **Titanium:** Used in high-stress joints and engine mounts
- **Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer (CFRP):** Used for antenna reflectors and booms (extremely stiff, low thermal expansion)
- **Kevlar / Nextel blankets:** Used for MMOD shielding (Whipple shield concept)

**Why aluminum honeycomb?**
A sandwich of two aluminum face-sheets bonded to an aluminum honeycomb core gives **high stiffness-to-mass ratio**, which is the *single most important mechanical property* in space structures.

$$
\frac{\text{Stiffness}}{\text{Mass}} = \frac{E^{1/3}}{\rho}
$$

where $E$ = Young’s modulus and $\rho$ = density.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** During launch, a satellite experiences vibration loads of up to **$\mathbf{10 g$ rms}$** and acoustic levels of around **$\mathbf{140 \text{ dB}$**. The structure is qualified via **sine vibration**, **random vibration**, and **acoustic** tests on the ground.

## 2.2 Power Subsystem

The power subsystem is the **heart** of the satellite. It performs three functions:
1. **Generation** — via solar arrays
2. **Storage** — via batteries (eclipses and peak demand)
3. **Regulation & Distribution** — via Power Conditioning Unit (PCU) and Distribution Unit

### Solar Array Sizing Equation

$$
P_{SA} = \frac{P_{eclipse-out} + P_{eclipse-in}}{\eta_{SA} \cdot \cos(\theta_{inc}) \cdot ID \cdot (1 - d_{loss})}
$$

Where:
- $P_{eclipse-out}$ = power required during sunlight
- $P_{eclipse-in}$ = power required during eclipse
- $\eta_{SA}$ = solar cell efficiency (typically **$\mathbf{28\% - 33\%}$** for modern triple-junction GaAs cells)
- $\theta_{inc}$ = angle of incidence of sunlight
- $ID$ = inherent degradation factor (per year, typically **$\mathbf{2.5\% - 3\%}$**)
- $d_{loss}$ = losses due to shadowing, wiring, etc.

### Eclipse Power Balance

For a GEO satellite, eclipse occurs near equinoxes for up to **$\mathbf{72 \text{ minutes/day}$** maximum.

$$
P_{eclipse-out} \cdot T_{sun} = P_{eclipse-in} \cdot T_{eclipse} + \text{Charging energy stored}
$$

### Battery Sizing

Batteries used are typically **Nickel-Hydrogen (NiH₂)** or **Lithium-Ion (Li-ion)**.

$$
\text{Battery Capacity (Ah)} = \frac{P_{eclipse-in} \cdot T_{eclipse}}{V_{bus} \cdot DOD \cdot \eta_{bat}}
$$

- $DOD$ = Depth of Discharge (typically **$\mathbf{0.6 - 0.8}$** for Li-ion)
- $\eta_{bat}$ = battery round-trip efficiency (**$\mathbf{0.85 - 0.95}$**)

## 2.3 Thermal Control Subsystem

Space is a **vacuum**, so heat cannot be lost by convection or conduction (except through solid contacts). The only ways to exchange heat are:

- **Radiation** (dominant in space)
- **Conduction** (within the satellite body)

### Radiative Heat Transfer (Stefan–Boltzmann Law)

$$
Q = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot (T_s^4 - T_{space}^4)
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = heat dissipated (W)
- $\varepsilon$ = surface emissivity (**$\mathbf{0$ to $1$}$**)
- $\sigma$ = Stefan–Boltzmann constant = $\mathbf{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4}$
- $A$ = radiating surface area (m²)
- $T_s$ = surface temperature (K)
- $T_{space}$ = space sink temperature (≈ **$\mathbf{3 \text{ K}$**)

### Passive Thermal Control Elements
- **Multi-Layer Insulation (MLI):** Reflective outer layer (Kapton/Aluminized Mylar) — minimizes radiative heat loss
- **Thermal paints/coatings:** Black for high emissivity, white for low absorption
- **Heat pipes:** Two-phase (liquid-vapor) — transfers heat with near-isothermal behavior

### Active Thermal Control Elements
- **Heaters:** Electrical resistive heaters for very cold components
- **Active coolers:** Limited use (cryocoolers) for IR/quantum payloads

## 2.4 Attitude and Orbit Control Subsystem (AOCS)

The AOCS answers two questions:
1. **Where am I?** (orbit determination) → *Sensors*
2. **Which way am I pointing?** (attitude determination) → *Sensors + algorithms*
3. **How do I correct?** (actuation) → *Thrusters, reaction wheels, magnetorquers, CMGs*

### Attitude Sensors

| Sensor | Function | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|
| **Earth Sensor (IR horizon)** | Detects Earth's IR limb | $\pm 0.1^\circ$ |
| **Sun Sensor** | Locates the Sun | $\pm 0.05^\circ$ |
| **Star Sensor / Star Tracker** | Identifies star patterns | $\mathbf{\pm 0.001^\circ}$ (arcsec level) |
| **Gyroscope (MEMS, RLG, FOG)** | Measures angular rate | Drift: $0.01^\circ/\text{hr}$ |
| **GPS Receiver** | Determines position & time | Position: **$\mathbf{10 \text{ m}$** |

### Actuators

| Actuator | Working Principle | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Reaction Wheels (RW)** | Momentum exchange using spinning mass | Fine pointing |
| **Control Moment Gyros (CMG)** | High-torque gimbaled wheels | Agile spacecraft |
| **Magnetorquers** | Torque via Earth’s magnetic field | CubeSats, LEO satellites |
| **Thrusters** | Cold gas / Hydrazine / Ion | Orbit correction, unloading |

### Torque Equation for a Reaction Wheel

$$
T_{wheel} = -I_{wheel} \cdot \dot{\omega}_{wheel}
$$

This torque is transferred to the satellite body (Newton’s third law), causing it to rotate by an equal and opposite angular impulse.

## 2.5 Propulsion Subsystem

Used for:
- **Station-keeping** (NSSK and EWSK for GEO)
- **Orbit raising** (GTO → GEO transfer)
- **Attitude control** (small thrusters)
- **De-orbit** (at end-of-life)

### Specific Impulse ($I_{sp}$) — the Key Metric

$$
I_{sp} = \frac{T}{\dot{m} \cdot g_0} \quad [\text{seconds}]
$$

Higher $I_{sp}$ means **less propellant** for the same mission. Modern electric propulsion (ion, Hall-thruster) achieves **$I_{sp} > 3000 \text{ s}$** vs chemical bipropellant at **$\mathbf{300 - 450 \text{ s}$**.

### Rocket Equation (Tsiolkovsky)

$$
\Delta v = I_{sp} \cdot g_0 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{m_0}{m_f}\right)
$$

- $\Delta v$ = velocity change needed
- $m_0$ = initial wet mass
- $m_f$ = final dry mass

## 2.6 Telemetry, Tracking & Command (TT&C) Subsystem

- **Telemetry (downlink):** Sends satellite health data (temperatures, voltages, currents, pressures, switch status) to the ground
- **Tracking:** The ground measures the satellite’s position/range/range-rate using RF tones
- **Command (uplink):** Ground sends instructions to the satellite (switch on heater, fire thruster, change mode, etc.)

**TT&C frequencies (ITU standard):**
- **Uplink:** $\mathbf{2025 - 2110 \text{ MHz}}$ (S-band, near 2 GHz)
- **Downlink:** $\mathbf{2200 - 2290 \text{ MHz}}$ (S-band)
- **Backup/ranging:** $\mathbf{8 \text{ GHz}}$ (X-band) for some satellites

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU often-asked:** *"Why are uplink and downlink frequencies different?"* → To prevent **interference** between the satellite receiver and its own transmitter (a duplex isolation issue). The offset also allows simpler filtering.

## 2.7 Communication Payload (Transponder)

The payload is the **reason the satellite exists**. A typical transponder chain:

1. **Receive antenna** (Rx)
2. **Low-Noise Amplifier (LNA)** → amplifies weak incoming signal
3. **Down-converter (mixer + LO)** → RF → IF
4. **Input multiplexer (IMUX)** → channel filtering
5. **TWTA (Travelling Wave Tube Amplifier)** or **SSPA (Solid State Power Amplifier)** → high-power amplification
6. **Output multiplexer (OMUX)** → combines channels
7. **Transmit antenna (Tx)** → broadcasts back to Earth

### Transponder Gain Equation (basic)

$$
G_{total} = G_{Rx,ant} + G_{LNA} - L_{feed} + G_{PA} + G_{Tx,ant}
$$

(dB addition; all losses $L$ are negative dB)

## 2.8 Antenna Subsystem

| Type | Description | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Isotropic** | Theoretical point radiator | Reference (0 dBi) |
| **Dipole / Quadrifilar Helix** | Omnidirectional | TTC, LEO |
| **Horn** | Directional, wideband | Feeds for reflectors |
| **Parabolic Reflector** | High gain, narrow beam | GEO communications |
| **Phased Array** | Electronically steered | LEO constellations (Starlink) |

### Antenna Gain (Parabolic Dish)

$$
G = \eta \cdot \left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^2
$$

- $D$ = dish diameter
- $\lambda$ = wavelength
- $\eta$ = aperture efficiency (typically **$\mathbf{0.55 - 0.65}$**)

In dB:

$$
G_{dBi} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\eta \cdot \left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^2\right]
$$

### Half-Power Beamwidth (HPBW)

$$
\text{HPBW} \approx 70 \frac{\lambda}{D} \quad [\text{degrees}]
$$

## 2.9 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory for solving numerical problems in Part B (14-mark questions).**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2$ | **Friis transmission equation** (free-space) |
| 2 | $L_{fs} = \left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)^2$ | Free-space path loss (linear) |
| 3 | $L_{fs,dB} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(R_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ | FSPL in dB |
| 4 | $G = \eta(\pi D / \lambda)^2$ | Parabolic antenna gain |
| 5 | $\text{HPBW} \approx 70\lambda/D$ | Beamwidth in degrees |
| 6 | $Q = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_{space}^4)$ | Stefan–Boltzmann radiation |
| 7 | $I_{sp} = T / (\dot{m} g_0)$ | Specific impulse |
| 8 | $\Delta v = I_{sp} g_0 \ln(m_0/m_f)$ | Tsiolkovsky rocket equation |
| 9 | $P_{SA} = P_{load}/(\eta_{SA} \cos\theta \cdot ID \cdot (1-d))$ | Solar array sizing |
| 10 | $D = \sqrt{(2 R_e h + h^2)} \cdot \theta_{3dB}/2$ | Coverage diameter on Earth |
| 11 | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{a^3 / \mu}$ | Kepler's third law (period) |
| 12 | $v = \sqrt{\mu / r}$ | Circular orbital velocity |
| 13 | $EIRP = P_t G_t$ | Effective Isotropic Radiated Power |
| 14 | $G/T = G_{ant} - 10\log_{10}(T_{sys})$ | Figure of merit (dB/K) |
| 15 | $\text{Link budget: } C/N_0 = EIRP + G/T - L_{fs} - k$ | Overall carrier-to-noise-density |
| 16 | $\dot{m} = m_0 (1 - e^{-\Delta v / (I_{sp} g_0)})$ | Propellant mass fraction |

> ⚠️ **Critical Markdown Note:** In all formula tables, use `\vert` (not `|`) for absolute values or "OR" symbols to preserve table structure. (e.g., $R_{km} \vert f_{MHz}$).

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Antenna Gain from Physical Aperture

This is a **guaranteed derivation** in KTU ESE Module 1 questions, often asked in Part B.

**Given:**
- A parabolic reflector of diameter $D$
- Operating wavelength $\lambda$
- Aperture efficiency $\eta$

**To derive:** $G = \eta \left(\dfrac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^2$

### Step 1 — Start from Definition of Gain

By definition, the gain of an antenna is the ratio of its maximum radiation intensity to the average radiation intensity of an isotropic radiator:

$$
G = \frac{U_{max}}{U_{avg}} = 4\pi \frac{U_{max}}{P_{rad}}
$$

where $U_{max}$ is the maximum radiation intensity (W/sr) and $P_{rad}$ is the total radiated power.

### Step 2 — Relate Radiation Intensity to Aperture Fields

The maximum radiation intensity is related to the effective aperture $A_e$ of the antenna by:

$$
U_{max} = \frac{P_{rad} \cdot A_e}{\lambda^2}
$$

This is the standard IEEE definition linking aperture and radiation intensity.

### Step 3 — Define Aperture Efficiency

The *effective* aperture $A_e$ is always less than the *physical* aperture $A_p$:

$$
A_e = \eta \cdot A_p
$$

For a circular dish:

$$
A_p = \pi \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute

$$
G = 4\pi \frac{U_{max}}{P_{rad}} = 4\pi \cdot \frac{1}{P_{rad}} \cdot \frac{P_{rad} \cdot A_e}{\lambda^2} = \frac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2}
$$

Substitute $A_e = \eta A_p = \eta \pi D^2 / 4$:

$$
G = \frac{4\pi}{\lambda^2} \cdot \frac{\eta \pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 \eta}{\lambda^2}
$$

Reorder:

$$
\boxed{\,G = \eta \left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^2\,}
$$

**This is the KTU-required form.** In decibels:

$$
G_{dBi} = 10 \log_{10}(\eta) + 20 \log_{10}(\pi) + 20 \log_{10}(D) - 20 \log_{10}(\lambda)
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) in dB Form

**Given:** Two isotropic antennas separated by distance $R$ in free space; one transmits power $P_t$ at frequency $f$.

**To derive:** $L_{fs,dB} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(R_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$

### Step 1 — Use Friis Equation with Isotropic Antennas

For isotropic antennas, $G_t = G_r = 1$ (i.e., 0 dBi each), so:

$$
P_r = P_t \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2
$$

The free-space path loss (ratio of transmitted to received power with isotropic antennas) is:

$$
L_{fs} = \frac{P_t}{P_r} = \left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)^2
$$

### Step 2 — Express in Decibels

$$
L_{fs,dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)^2 = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)
$$

### Step 3 — Expand the Constants

Insert $R$ in km and $f$ in MHz:

- $4\pi \approx 12.566$
- $\lambda = c/f = (3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}) / f_{Hz}$
- If $f$ in MHz, $f_{Hz} = f_{MHz} \times 10^6$
- If $R$ in km, $R_m = R_{km} \times 10^3$

So:

$$
L_{fs,dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi R_{km} \times 10^3 \cdot f_{MHz} \times 10^6}{3 \times 10^8}\right)
$$

Simplify the argument:

$$
\text{Arg} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^9}{3 \times 10^8} \cdot R_{km} f_{MHz} = \frac{4\pi}{3} \cdot 10 \cdot R_{km} f_{MHz} \approx 41.88 \cdot R_{km} f_{MHz}
$$

Take $20 \log_{10}(41.88) \approx 32.45$. Hence:

$$
\boxed{\,L_{fs,dB} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(R_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})\,}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Solar Array Sizing for Eclipse Operation

**Given:**
- Load power during sunlight: $P_{day}$
- Load power during eclipse: $P_{night}$
- Time in sunlight per orbit: $T_{day}$
- Time in eclipse per orbit: $T_{night}$
- Solar cell efficiency: $\eta_{SA}$
- Inherent degradation over mission life: $d$

**To derive:** Minimum solar array area required.

### Step 1 — Energy Balance per Orbit

The energy produced by the solar array in one orbit must equal the energy consumed plus the energy stored in the battery for eclipse use:

$$
P_{SA,raw} \cdot T_{day} = P_{day} \cdot T_{day} + P_{night} \cdot T_{night}
$$

So:

$$
P_{SA,raw} = P_{day} + P_{night} \cdot \frac{T_{night}}{T_{day}}
$$

### Step 2 — Account for Cell Efficiency and Sun Angle

The electrical power generated by a panel of area $A$ illuminated at solar constant $S_0$ and incidence angle $\theta$ is:

$$
P_{SA,raw} = S_0 \cdot \eta_{SA} \cdot A \cdot \cos\theta
$$

### Step 3 — Account for In-Orbit Degradation

Over 15 years, the solar cells degrade. End-of-life (EOL) power is:

$$
P_{SA,EOL} = P_{SA,BOL} \cdot (1 - d)^{N}
$$

(For a conservative design, we often just use linear worst-case $d$ per year.)

### Step 4 — Final Sizing Formula

$$
\boxed{\,A_{SA} = \frac{P_{day} + P_{night}(T_{night}/T_{day})}{S_0 \cdot \eta_{SA} \cdot \cos\theta \cdot (1-d)}\,}
$$

**Numerical sanity check:**
A GEO satellite spends about **$\mathbf{22.5 \text{ h}$** in sun and **$\mathbf{1.2 \text{ h}$** in eclipse per day. So $T_{night}/T_{day} \approx 1.2/22.5 \approx 0.053$, meaning eclipse power has only a **5%** weight in sizing — but the *battery* must still cover that 1.2 h.

## 3.4 Derivation: Tsiolkovsky Rocket Equation (used in Propulsion Sizing)

**Given:** A satellite of initial mass $m_0$ (with propellant) ejects mass at exhaust velocity $v_e$ relative to the spacecraft. Final mass $m_f$ (without propellant).

**To derive:** $\Delta v = v_e \ln(m_0/m_f)$

### Step 1 — Conservation of Momentum

At each instant, the change in momentum of the spacecraft must equal the change in momentum of the ejected mass:

$$
m \, dv = -v_e \, dm
$$

(Here, $dm$ is negative — mass leaves the system.)

### Step 2 — Rearrange and Integrate

$$
dv = -v_e \frac{dm}{m}
$$

Integrate from initial mass $m_0$ to final mass $m_f$, and from 0 to $\Delta v$:

$$
\int_0^{\Delta v} dv = -v_e \int_{m_0}^{m_f} \frac{dm}{m}
$$

$$
\Delta v = -v_e \left[ \ln(m_f) - \ln(m_0) \right] = v_e \ln\!\left(\frac{m_0}{m_f}\right)
$$

### Step 3 — Use Specific Impulse Form

Since $v_e = I_{sp} \cdot g_0$:

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta v = I_{sp} \, g_0 \, \ln\!\left(\frac{m_0}{m_f}\right)\,}
$$

**Sanity Check:** For a typical GEO NSSK mission needing **$\Delta v \approx 50 \text{ m/s/year}$** over 15 years → $\Delta v_{total} \approx 750 \text{ m/s}$. With bipropellant $I_{sp} = 320$ s:

$$
\frac{m_0}{m_f} = e^{\Delta v / (I_{sp} g_0)} = e^{750 / (320 \times 9.81)} = e^{0.239} \approx 1.27
$$

So propellant mass $\approx 27\%$ of dry mass. With ion propulsion ($I_{sp} = 3000$ s), the ratio is $\approx 1.025$ — only **2.5%** propellant. This is why modern satellites are switching to electric propulsion.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (often tested)

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners love comparison tables in 7-mark sub-questions. Memorize this matrix.

| Feature | TWT (Travelling Wave Tube) | SSPA (Solid State Power Amplifier) |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency | Up to Ka-band, even V-band | Best up to Ku-band |
| Power | High (50–250 W per tube) | Lower (10–50 W) |
| Efficiency | ~$\mathbf{60\%}$ | ~$\mathbf{30 - 40\%}$ |
| Linearity | Poor (needs linearizer) | Good (more linear) |
| Lifetime | ~15 years (cathode wear) | Long (no cathode) |
| Mass | Heavy | Light |
| Use | High-power GEO downlink | LEO, small sats, modern flexible payloads |

| Feature | Passive Thermal Control | Active Thermal Control |
|---|---|---|
| Examples | MLI, paints, heat pipes, louvers | Heaters, cryocoolers |
| Power use | None | Some |
| Reliability | Very high | Moderate (moving parts/heaters fail) |
| Use | Always | When tight thermal stability needed |

| Feature | Spin-stabilized | 3-axis stabilized |
|---|---|---|
| Orientation | Whole body rotates | Body fixed; internal wheels rotate |
| Antenna pointing | Despun platform needed | Body-mounted antennas OK |
| Simplicity | Simple | Complex |
| Power | Body-mounted solar cells → curved | Flat solar wings (more efficient) |
| Modern trend | Obsolete for comms sats | **Standard for modern GEO/LEO** |

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overall Satellite Subsystem Block Diagram (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BusPlatform["SPACECRAFT BUS (Platform)"]
        A1[Mechanical Structural Subsystem] --> A2[Provides mounting frame]
        A2 --> A3[Power Subsystem]
        A2 --> A4[Thermal Control Subsystem]
        A2 --> A5[AOCS Sensors and Actuators]
        A2 --> A6[Propulsion Subsystem]
        A2 --> A7[TT and C Subsystem]
        A2 --> A8[Onboard Computer and Data Handling]
    end

    subgraph PayloadComm["COMMUNICATION PAYLOAD (Mission)"]
        B1[Receive Antenna] --> B2[LNA and Downconverter]
        B2 --> B3[IMUX Channel Filters]
        B3 --> B4[HPAs: TWTA or SSPA]
        B4 --> B5[OMUX Output Multiplexer]
        B5 --> B6[Transmit Antenna and Feed]
    end

    A3 -.DC Power.-> B4
    A5 -.Pointing Data.-> B6
    A4 -.Heat Rejection.-> B4
    A7 -.Status Telemetry.-> Ground[Ground Station]
    A8 -.Command Path.-> A5
    A6 -.Station Keeping.-> Orbit[Orbit Maintenance]

    PayloadComm -.User Signals.-> Earth[Earth Coverage]
```

## 4.2 Power Subsystem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SA[Solar Array Panels] -->|Raw DC| PCU[Power Conditioning Unit]
    PCU -->|28V or 42V or 100V Bus| Dist[Power Distribution Unit]
    Battery[Li-ion or NiH2 Battery] <-->|Charge/Discharge| PCU
    Dist --> Bus1[Communication Payload]
    Dist --> Bus2[AOCS and Propulsion]
    Dist --> Bus3[Thermal Heaters and TTC]
    Dist --> Bus4[Onboard Computer]

    Sun[Sunlight] -->|Charging| SA
    Eclipse[Eclipse Phase] -->|Discharging| Battery
```

## 4.3 AOCS Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Sens1[Star Tracker] --> Estim[Attitude Estimator Kalman Filter]
    Sens2[Earth Sensor] --> Estim
    Sens3[Sun Sensor] --> Estim
    Sens4[Gyroscope] --> Estim
    Estim -->|Attitude Error| Control[Attitude Controller PID]
    Control -->|Torque Command| Act1[Reaction Wheels]
    Control -->|Torque Command| Act2[Magnetorquers for LEO]
    Control -->|Fire Command| Act3[Thrusters for coarse correction]
    Act1 --> Body[Spacecraft Body Rotation]
    Act2 --> Body
    Act3 --> Body
    Body -->|Update| Sens1
    Body -->|Update| Sens2
```

## 4.4 Transponder (Payload) Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Up[Uplink from Earth 6 GHz or 14 GHz] --> RxAnt[Receive Antenna]
    RxAnt --> LNA[Low Noise Amplifier]
    LNA --> DC[Mixer and Local Oscillator]
    DC --> IF[IF Amplifier at 4 GHz]
    IF --> IMUX[Input Multiplexer Channel Filters]
    IMUX --> HPA[TWTA or SSPA High Power Amplifier]
    HPA --> OMUX[Output Multiplexer]
    OMUX --> TxAnt[Transmit Antenna]
    TxAnt --> Down[Downlink to Earth 4 GHz or 11 GHz]
```

## 4.5 TT&C Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GS[Ground Station] -->|Command Uplink 2 GHz S band| Rx[TT and C Receiver on Sat]
    Rx --> Decoder[Command Decoder]
    Decoder --> OBC[Onboard Computer]
    OBC -->|Switch On or Off or Mode Change| Subsystems[All Bus Subsystems]
    Subsystems -->|Telemetry Data| Encoder[Telemetry Encoder]
    Encoder --> TxMod[TT and C Transmitter Modulator]
    TxMod -->|Downlink 2.2 GHz S band| GS2[Ground Station Receives]
    GS2 -->|Tracking Tones Range and Range Rate| OrbitDetermination[Orbit Determination]
```

## 4.6 Thermal Control Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Heat[Internal Heat Dissipation from Electronics] -->|Conduction| Spread[Heat Spreader Plate]
    Spread -->|Conduction| Pipes[Heat Pipes]
    Pipes --> Rad[External Radiator Surface]
    Rad -->|Stefan Boltzmann Radiation| Space[Deep Space 3 K Sink]
    MLI[Multi Layer Insulation Blanket] -.Minimizes Loss.-> Spread
    Heater[Survival Heater] -.Backup Cold Case.-> Spread
    Paint[Optical Coatings White or Black] -.Control Absorptivity.-> Rad
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often **omit units** in numerical answers → lose 0.5–1 mark per sub-part.
> - Students confuse **payload vs. bus** in definitions — never call TT&C or power a "payload."
> - In derivations, **skipping a step** (e.g., converting km→m) costs the student the full 7 marks, because KTU valuation rewards the *process*, not just the final number.
> - Forgetting the **redundancy** aspect in subsystem design descriptions loses a guaranteed 1-mark.
> - In 14-mark questions, the **conclusion line** ("Therefore, the antenna gain is ___ dBi") is mandatory.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any six major subsystems of a communication satellite and state the primary function of each.**

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. **Mechanical/Structural Subsystem** – Provides the rigid frame, supports all components, and withstands launch loads.
2. **Power Subsystem** – Generates electrical power using solar arrays and stores it in batteries.
3. **Thermal Control Subsystem** – Maintains the temperature of all components within safe operating limits using passive and active methods.
4. **Attitude and Orbit Control Subsystem (AOCS)** – Determines and controls the satellite’s orientation and orbit.
5. **Telemetry, Tracking and Command (TT&C) Subsystem** – Enables two-way communication with the ground for monitoring and control.
6. **Propulsion Subsystem** – Provides thrust for orbit correction, station-keeping, and de-orbit manoeuvres.

*(Alternative valid answer: include Communication Payload and Antenna Subsystem as part of the list.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between satellite "bus" and "payload".**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Bus (Platform) | Payload |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Supports the satellite’s operation | Performs the actual mission |
| Examples | Power, Thermal, AOCS, TT&C, Propulsion, Structure | Communication transponders, cameras, scientific instruments |
| Universality | Common to all satellites | Mission-specific |
| Revenue role | Indirect (enables payload operation) | Direct (generates commercial/scientific value) |

The bus is the "vehicle," and the payload is the "cargo." A communication satellite has a transponder payload; a remote-sensing satellite has a camera payload. (3 marks for clear differentiation.)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU ESE convention: Each Part B question has **internal choice** — answer EITHER (a) OR (b). Both carry 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a typical **communication transponder** used in a satellite. Mention the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GEO satellite uses a parabolic antenna of diameter **2.4 m** operating at **12 GHz**. If the aperture efficiency is 0.6, calculate: **(i)** the antenna gain in dBi, **(ii)** the 3-dB beamwidth, and **(iii)** the EIRP if the HPA delivers 60 W. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q-A (a)

The communication transponder is the heart of the satellite payload. It receives weak uplink signals from Earth, amplifies them, changes their frequency (to avoid self-interference), and re-transmits them at higher power on the downlink. The block diagram of a typical **regenerative / bent-pipe transponder** is:

**Receive Antenna → LNA → Down-converter (Mixer + LO) → IF Amplifier → IMUX → HPA (TWTA/SSPA) → OMUX → Transmit Antenna**

**Block-by-Block Explanation:**

1. **Receive Antenna (Rx):** Collects the weak uplink RF signal (e.g., 14 GHz in Ku-band) from Earth. The antenna may be a global horn, a shaped-beam reflector, or a multi-feed reflector.
2. **Low-Noise Amplifier (LNA):** First active stage. Amplifies the extremely weak incoming signal (often at the noise floor) while adding as little noise as possible. Typical noise figure: 1–2 dB.
3. **Down-converter (Mixer + Local Oscillator):** Shifts the uplink RF to a lower Intermediate Frequency (IF) for easier filtering and amplification. A local oscillator provides the reference frequency.
4. **IF Amplifier:** Provides further gain at IF (e.g., 4 GHz), where filtering is more practical.
5. **Input Multiplexer (IMUX):** A bank of bandpass filters that splits the wideband signal into individual channels, so that each channel can be amplified independently.
6. **High-Power Amplifier (HPA):** The main workhorse. For high power, a **TWTA** (Travelling Wave Tube Amplifier) is used; for lower-power, multi-beam payloads, **SSPA** is preferred. Output: tens to hundreds of watts per channel.
7. **Output Multiplexer (OMUX):** Recombines all amplified channels into a single wideband output and rejects out-of-band spurious products.
8. **Transmit Antenna (Tx):** Radiates the high-power downlink signal (e.g., 11 GHz) back toward the desired Earth coverage zone. (7 marks complete with block diagram and explanations.)

---

#### Solution to Q-A (b)

**Given:**
- $D = 2.4$ m
- $f = 12$ GHz $\Rightarrow \lambda = c/f = (3 \times 10^8)/(12 \times 10^9) = 0.025$ m
- $\eta = 0.6$
- $P_t = 60$ W

**(i) Antenna Gain:**

$$
G = \eta \left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^2 = 0.6 \left(\frac{\pi \times 2.4}{0.025}\right)^2
$$

Compute the argument:

$$
\frac{\pi \times 2.4}{0.025} = \frac{7.5398}{0.025} = 301.59
$$

Square it:

$$
(301.59)^2 = 90{,}957
$$

Multiply by 0.6:

$$
G = 0.6 \times 90{,}957 = 54{,}574
$$

Convert to dBi:

$$
G_{dBi} = 10 \log_{10}(54{,}574) = 10 \times 4.737 = \mathbf{47.37 \text{ dBi}}
$$

**(ii) 3-dB Beamwidth:**

$$
\text{HPBW} = 70 \frac{\lambda}{D} = 70 \times \frac{0.025}{2.4} = 70 \times 0.01042 = 0.729^\circ
$$

So $\text{HPBW} \approx \mathbf{0.73^\circ}$.

**(iii) EIRP:**

$$
P_t = 60 \text{ W} = 10 \log_{10}(60) = 17.78 \text{ dBW}
$$

$$
\text{EIRP} = P_t + G_t = 17.78 + 47.37 = \mathbf{65.15 \text{ dBW}}
$$

(Convert to dBW for consistent units — this is a common KTU mark-loser.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice]**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Adaptation]**

**(a)** Explain the **Attitude and Orbit Control Subsystem (AOCS)** of a satellite. With neat sketches, describe the working of **reaction wheels** and **magnetorquers** as actuators. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A LEO satellite at altitude **800 km** transmits at **2.2 GHz** with a power of **10 W** to a ground station. The satellite antenna gain is 3 dBi, ground antenna gain is 40 dBi, and total system noise temperature is 350 K. Compute: **(i)** free-space path loss, **(ii)** received power, **(iii)** C/N₀, and **(iv)** figure of merit G/T. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q-B (a)

The **Attitude and Orbit Control Subsystem (AOCS)** is responsible for two key functions:
- **Attitude Control** – pointing the satellite antennas accurately toward Earth (or any other target).
- **Orbit Control** – maintaining the satellite in its designated orbit (station-keeping).

**Sensors used (commonly asked in KTU):**
- Earth Horizon Sensor (IR)
- Sun Sensor
- Star Tracker (most accurate, arcsec level)
- Gyroscope (rate sensor)
- GPS receiver (orbit determination)

**Actuators:**

**1. Reaction Wheels (RW):**
A reaction wheel is a motor-driven flywheel mounted inside the satellite. When the wheel speeds up (angular acceleration), the satellite body experiences an equal and opposite torque (Newton's third law), rotating it in the opposite direction.

Working principle: Momentum exchange.

- If the wheel spins faster by $\Delta\omega$, the satellite rotates by a small angle $\theta$ such that:

$$
I_{wheel} \Delta\omega = I_{body} \dot{\theta}
$$

- Reaction wheels are used for **fine pointing** and provide **zero-propellant** attitude control.
- They are desaturated (unloaded) periodically using thrusters or magnetorquers, because the wheel speed accumulates over time.

(Include a diagram in the answer: wheel motor → flywheel → torque reaction on body.)

**2. Magnetorquers:**
A magnetorquer is a coil of wire that generates a magnetic dipole moment when current flows. This dipole interacts with Earth's magnetic field to produce a torque:

$$
\vec{T} = \vec{m} \times \vec{B}
$$

where $\vec{m} = N I A \hat{n}$ is the magnetic moment (N turns, current I, area A), and $\vec{B}$ is Earth's field.

- Magnetorquers are **light, simple, and consume little power**, but produce **low torque**.
- Used primarily in **LEO satellites and CubeSats**, where Earth's magnetic field is strong enough.
- Cannot be used in GEO (very weak field there).

(Include a diagram: coil + current → magnetic dipole → torque from Earth's B-field.)

**Conclusion:** Modern 3-axis stabilized satellites use a combination — **reaction wheels for fine pointing, magnetorquers (LEO) or thrusters (GEO) for desaturation.** (7 marks complete.)

---

#### Solution to Q-B (b)

**Given:**
- $h = 800$ km
- $f = 2.2$ GHz
- $P_t = 10$ W
- $G_t = 3$ dBi
- $G_r = 40$ dBi
- $T_{sys} = 350$ K

**Slant range for LEO (assuming overhead pass):**

$$
R = R_e + h = 6378 + 800 = 7178 \text{ km} = 7.178 \times 10^6 \text{ m}
$$

**(i) Free-Space Path Loss (in dB):**

$$
L_{fs,dB} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(R_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})
$$

$$
= 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(7178) + 20\log_{10}(2200)
$$

$$
= 32.45 + 20(3.856) + 20(3.342)
$$

$$
= 32.45 + 77.12 + 66.85 = \mathbf{176.42 \text{ dB}}
$$

(Linear: $L_{fs} = 10^{17.642} \approx 4.39 \times 10^{17}$.)

**(ii) Received Power:**

$$
P_{r,dBW} = P_{t,dBW} + G_t + G_r - L_{fs}
$$

Convert $P_t$: $10\log_{10}(10) = 10$ dBW.

$$
P_{r,dBW} = 10 + 3 + 40 - 176.42 = \mathbf{-123.42 \text{ dBW}}
$$

Linear:

$$
P_r = 10^{-12.342} \approx \mathbf{4.55 \times 10^{-13} \text{ W} = 0.455 \text{ pW}}
$$

**(iii) C/N₀:**

Boltzmann constant: $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K = $-228.6$ dB W/(K·Hz).

$$
(C/N_0)_{dBHz} = P_{r,dBW} + G/T - k_{dB}
$$

First compute G/T:

$$
G/T = 40 \text{ dBi} - 10\log_{10}(350) = 40 - 25.44 = 14.56 \text{ dB/K}
$$

Then:

$$
(C/N_0)_{dBHz} = -123.42 + 14.56 - (-228.6) = -123.42 + 14.56 + 228.6 = \mathbf{119.74 \text{ dBHz}}
$$

**(iv) G/T (already computed):**

$$
\boxed{\,G/T = 14.56 \text{ dB/K}\,}
$$

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this as the night-before-exam quick recap.**

- ✅ A satellite has **two parts**: **Bus (platform)** and **Payload (mission equipment)**.
- ✅ The **7 major subsystems** of a satellite are: **Mechanical, Power, Thermal, AOCS, Propulsion, TT&C, Payload/Transponder, Antenna**.
- ✅ **Power** uses solar cells (GaAs triple-junction, $\eta \approx 30\%$) + batteries (Li-ion / NiH₂). Bus voltage is typically **$\mathbf{28\text{V}/42\text{V}/100\text{V}}$** DC.
- ✅ **Thermal control** uses **MLI, paints, heat pipes** (passive) and **heaters** (active). Radiation is the only heat-loss mode in vacuum — use **Stefan–Boltzmann law** $Q = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_{space}^4)$.
- ✅ **AOCS** uses sensors (Earth, Sun, Star, Gyro, GPS) and actuators (Reaction Wheels, CMGs, Magnetorquers, Thrusters).
- ✅ **Propulsion** is governed by **Tsiolkovsky equation**: $\Delta v = I_{sp} g_0 \ln(m_0/m_f)$. **Electric propulsion** has $I_{sp} > 3000$ s — propellant mass tiny.
- ✅ **TT&C** uses S-band: uplink $\mathbf{2025\text{–}2110 \text{ MHz}}$, downlink $\mathbf{2200\text{–}2290 \text{ MHz}}$.
- ✅ **Transponder chain** is **Rx → LNA → Down-converter → IMUX → HPA (TWTA/SSPA) → OMUX → Tx**.
- ✅ **Antenna gain** of a parabolic dish: $G = \eta (\pi D / \lambda)^2$. **HPBW** $= 70\lambda/D$ degrees.
- ✅ **Free-space path loss:** $L_{fs,dB} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(R_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$.
- ✅ **EIRP** $= P_t + G_t$ (both in dB). **G/T** is the figure of merit.
- ✅ Modern satellites are **3-axis stabilized** (not spin-stabilized).
- ✅ **Redundancy is mandatory** in every critical subsystem — cold or hot redundant.
- ✅ GEO altitude = $\mathbf{35{,}786 \text{ km}}$; LEO = $\mathbf{160\text{–}2000 \text{ km}}$; MEO = in between.
- ✅ Antenna beamwidth and Earth-coverage diameter trade-off: bigger dish → narrower beam → smaller Earth footprint, but higher gain.

**End of Module 1 – Satellite Subsystems Notes**
