3. **Power Conditioning** — regulation, filtering, and maximum power point tracking (MPPT) by the PCU.
4. **Power Distribution & Protection** — supply to all loads via fused, switched, and monitored bus lines with fault isolation. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**What is the solar constant? Why is its value at Earth's orbit important for satellite design?**

**Model Answer:**
The **solar constant** is the rate at which solar electromagnetic energy is received per unit area at a distance of **1 AU** from the Sun, outside Earth's atmosphere. Its standard value is:

$$
G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

It is important because:
- It defines the **maximum incident power** available to a satellite's solar array.
- It is the basis for sizing the array: $P_{gen} = G_{sc} \cdot A \cdot \eta \cdot \cos\theta$.
- Variations over a solar cycle (~11 years) are small (~±1 %) and accounted for in EOL margins. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the operation of the **Electrical Power Subsystem** of a geostationary communication satellite. Explain the role of the **battery** during eclipse and the role of the **shunt regulator** during sunlight.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A GEO satellite has a payload load of **3 kW** during eclipse. The bus voltage is **50 V**, the eclipse duration is **72 minutes**, the battery is Li-ion with **DoD = 20 %** and **round-trip efficiency = 90 %**. Compute the **battery capacity in Ah**. If each Li-ion cell is **3.6 V, 60 Ah**, find the **number of cells** in series and parallel.

#### Model Solution for (a)

The EPS of a GEO satellite consists of four interconnected blocks: (refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4)

**Operation in Sunlight (Eclipse-free period):**
1. Solar arrays (GaAs multi-junction, η ≈ 20–25 %) generate DC current proportional to incident solar flux.
2. The **shunt regulator** (or sequential switching shunt) dumps the **excess array current** into dummy load resistors to maintain the bus voltage at the regulated value (e.g., 50 V). This is the dominant regulation technique in GEO satellites because the array is intrinsically over-sized.
3. Simultaneously, the **battery charge regulator (BCR)** charges the battery to full SoC.
4. The PCDU distributes regulated 28 V/12 V/5 V rails to payload, TTC, AOCS, and heaters.

**Operation in Eclipse:**
1. Solar array output = 0 (Sun blocked by Earth).
2. The **battery discharge regulator (BDR)** switches ON and feeds the bus at 50 V.
3. The shunt regulator is inactive; instead, the BCR acts as a boost converter.
4. The bus voltage is held within ±2 % by the BDR feedback loop.
5. Housekeeping telemetry continuously reports bus V, I, battery SoC, and array temperature.

**Key point:** The battery is sized to deliver the *full eclipse load* with at most 20 % DoD for Li-ion (to extend cycle life beyond 2000 cycles). *(7 marks: 3 for diagram, 2 for sunlit operation, 2 for eclipse operation)*

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Energy required from battery during eclipse:**

$$
E_{req} = P_L \times T_e = 3000 \text{ W} \times 1.2 \text{ hr} = 3600 \text{ Wh}
$$

**Step 2 — Account for η_round-trip:**

$$
E_{store} = \frac{3600}{0.90} = 4000 \text{ Wh}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply DoD:**

$$
E_{rated} = \frac{4000}{0.20} = 20{,}000 \text{ Wh}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Ah:**

$$
C_{Ah} = \frac{20{,}000}{50} = 400 \text{ Ah}
$$

**Step 5 — Cell configuration:**
- Cells in series: $N_s = 50 / 3.6 = 13.89 \Rightarrow 14$ cells
- Strings in parallel: $N_p = 400 / 60 = 6.67 \Rightarrow 7$ strings (round up for margin)
- **Total cells: $14 \times 7 = 98$** *(7 marks: 1 each step + 2 for cell configuration)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **photovoltaic effect**. Draw the I-V characteristic of a solar cell and mark $I_{sc}$, $V_{oc}$, and the **maximum power point (MPP)**. Define **fill factor** and state the typical values for silicon and GaAs cells.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A LEO satellite at **800 km** altitude, inclination **82°**, uses silicon cells with **BOL efficiency 16 %**, **annual degradation 2 %/yr**, **mission life 7 years**, **packing factor 0.85**, and **effective Sun angle 30°**. The payload requires **1.5 kW** at EOL. Find the **required array area** at BOL. (Use $G_{sc} = 1367$ W/m², $R_E = 6378$ km.)

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Photovoltaic Effect:**
When a photon of energy $h\nu$ exceeding the semiconductor band-gap $E_g$ is absorbed in the depletion region of a **p-n junction**, it generates an **electron-hole pair**. The junction's built-in electric field sweeps the electron to the n-side and the hole to the p-side, establishing a **photo-voltage** across the terminals. Connecting an external load allows current to flow — this is the photovoltaic effect.

**I-V Characteristic (text-replacement of figure):**
- y-axis: Cell Current $I$ (A)
- x-axis: Cell Voltage $V$ (V)
- Curve starts at $I = I_{sc}$ on the y-axis, drops gradually, and reaches $I = 0$ at $V = V_{oc}$ on the x-axis.
- The **Maximum Power Point (MPP)** is the knee where the inscribed rectangle $P = V \times I$ has maximum area.

**Fill Factor (FF):**

$$
FF = \frac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}
$$

Typical values:
- **Silicon (Si):** FF ≈ 0.70–0.78
- **Gallium Arsenide (GaAs):** FF ≈ 0.80–0.85
- **Multi-junction:** FF ≈ 0.85–0.88

A high FF means a more "square" I-V curve → better cell quality. *(7 marks: 2 for effect, 2 for curve, 1 for FF definition, 2 for typical values)*

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — EOL efficiency after 7 years:**

$$
\eta_{EOL} = 0.16 \times (1 - 0.02)^7
$$

$$
(0.98)^7 = 0.8681
$$

$$
\eta_{EOL} = 0.16 \times 0.8681 = 0.1389
$$

**Step 2 — EOL power per m² of bare cell:**

$$
P_{cell,\,EOL} = 1367 \times 0.1389 = 189.9 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Apply packing factor and cosine loss:**

$$
P_{geom} = 189.9 \times 0.85 \times \cos(30°)
$$

$$
= 189.9 \times 0.85 \times 0.8660
$$

$$
= 189.9 \times 0.7361
$$

$$
= 139.8 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Required area:**

$$
A = \frac{1500}{139.8} = 10.73 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering rounding with 10 % margin:**

$$
A_{final} \approx 11.8 \text{ m}^2 \rightarrow \text{round to } 12 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{BOL} \approx 12 \text{ m}^2}
$$

In practice, this would be split into **two deployable wings** of **6 m² each**, with 3 panels per wing. *(7 marks: 1.5 each step + 1 for engineering margin)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in Power System questions (based on KTU 2019–2024 paper analysis):**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by packing factor** (≈ 0.85–0.9) — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the cosine-of-incidence-angle term** when the array is not Sun-pointed (typical GEO β = 23.5°). −1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up degradation formula:** BOL must always be ≥ EOL. A common error is using $(1-d)^{N}$ in the *wrong* direction.
> 4. **Ignoring DoD in battery sizing:** Sizing battery for **full** capacity instead of dividing by DoD gives a battery that is 5× too small for Li-ion (DoD = 20 %).
> 5. **Forgetting to convert Wh → Ah** at the **bus voltage** (not cell voltage). −1 mark.
> 6. **Stating "solar constant = 1.36 kW/m²" without specifying the 1 AU reference** — partial credit only.
> 7. **Confusing $T_{e}$ for GEO (72 min) with LEO (35–59 min)**. Always show the formula.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Memorize the bold items.**

- ✅ **EPS = Generation + Storage + Conditioning + Distribution**
- ✅ **Solar constant = 1,367 W/m² at 1 AU**
- ✅ **Solar cell equation (ideal):** $I = I_L - I_0(e^{qV/nkT} - 1)$
- ✅ **Three key cell materials:** Si (1st gen), GaAs (2nd gen), Multi-junction (3rd gen)
- ✅ **Maximum eclipse (GEO) = 72 min** during equinox
- ✅ **Maximum eclipse (LEO SSO) ≈ 35–59 min** depending on local time
- ✅ **Power generated:** $P_{gen} = G_{sc} \cdot A \cdot \eta_{cell} \cdot \eta_{pack} \cdot \cos\theta$
- ✅ **Degradation formula:** $\eta_{EOL} = \eta_{BOL} \cdot (1-d)^{N}$ with $d \approx 0.02$–$0.025$
- ✅ **Battery capacity:** $C_{Ah} = \dfrac{P_L \cdot T_e}{V_{bus} \cdot DoD \cdot \eta_{bat}}$
- ✅ **Modern bus voltage:** 50 V (INSAT-4, GSAT) or 100 V (high-power HTS)
- ✅ **Cell count:** Series → for voltage; Parallel → for current/capacity
- ✅ **Shunt regulator** = dominant sunlit regulation technique
- ✅ **BDR (Battery Discharge Regulator)** = dominant eclipse regulation technique
- ✅ **DoD limits:** Ni-H₂ ≤ 70 %, Li-ion ≤ 20 %, Li-Po ≤ 30 %
- ✅ **Fill Factor:** Si ≈ 0.75; GaAs ≈ 0.82; Multi-junction ≈ 0.87
- ✅ **Round-trip battery efficiency:** 80–90 %
- ✅ **GEO eclipse season:** ~45 days around each equinox (March & September)
- ✅ **Why GaAs over Si in space:** Higher η, better radiation hardness, lower temp coefficient
- ✅ **PCU does MPPT** to lock array at maximum power point

---

*End of Module 1 — Power System notes. Continue with Attitude & Orbit Control, Spacecraft TTC, and Thermal subsystems in subsequent KTU-PREMIER outputs.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communication Subsystem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Communication Subsystem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Communication Subsystem** (also called the **Communication Payload** or **Transponder Subsystem**) is the **RF heart of a communication satellite**. It is the on-board assembly of components that **receives, amplifies, frequency-translates, channelizes, and re-transmits** uplink signals back to Earth, forming the *bent-pipe* or *processed* link between the **Earth Station (Uplink)** and the **Earth Station (Downlink)**.

In strict KTU 2024 scheme language, the Communication Subsystem comprises:

| Functional Block | Engineering Role |
|---|---|
| **Receive Antenna** | Captures the weak uplink RF signal from Earth's noise floor |
| **Low Noise Amplifier (LNA)** | First-stage amplification with minimum added noise |
| **Frequency Down-Converter (D/C)** | Translates RF → Intermediate Frequency (IF) for processing |
| **Input De-Multiplexer (IMUX)** | Splits the wideband composite signal into individual channels |
| **High Power Amplifier (HPA)** | Boosts power to a level suitable for downlink transmission |
| **Frequency Up-Converter (U/C)** | Translates IF → Downlink RF |
| **Output Multiplexer (OMUX)** | Combines all amplified channels into one composite downlink |
| **Transmit Antenna** | Beams the downlink to the desired service zone on Earth |

> [!NOTE]
> A single signal path is called a **Transponder** (e.g., 36 MHz, 54 MHz, or 72 MHz bandwidth). Modern Ku-band satellites like INSAT/GSAT/Intelsat carry **12, 16, 24, 32, or even 64 transponders** per spacecraft.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Satellite as a Cosmic Post Office"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine a **central post office on a hill** that handles all the mail between two distant cities.
> - The **incoming mailbox** (receive antenna) collects letters from City A (uplink).
> - The **envelope sorter** (IMUX) sorts letters by pincode (frequency channel).
> - The **photocopier** (TWTA / SSPA) duplicates each letter in large numbers.
> - The **outgoing mail cart** (OMUX + transmit antenna) re-dispatches them to City B (downlink).
> - The **language translator** (down/up-converter) converts from French to English (RF to IF to RF).
> - The **chief postal worker** (TT&C subsystem) monitors the health of the office.
>
> The whole "post office" is your **Communication Subsystem** — it does not create the message, it only **reformats, amplifies, and forwards** it.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Boltzmann's Constant:** $k = 1.38064852 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** (or **−228.6 dBW/K·Hz** in decibel form)
> - **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s**
> - **Free-space path loss at 4 GHz (C-band uplink):** $L_p \approx 196.5$ **dB** (for GEO, 35,786 km)
> - **Free-space path loss at 12 GHz (Ku-band uplink):** $L_p \approx 205.5$ **dB** (for GEO)
> - **GEO orbital altitude:** $h = 35{,}786$ **km** (Geostationary ring)
> - **Common transponder bandwidths:** **36 MHz, 54 MHz, 72 MHz, 120 MHz, 240 MHz**
> - **Standard HPA types:** **TWTA (Travelling Wave Tube Amplifier)** & **SSPA (Solid State Power Amplifier)**

---

## 1.4 Why the Communication Subsystem Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without an efficient Communication Subsystem:
> - The uplink signal (≈ −100 dBm at the satellite) would be **buried in thermal noise** of the cosmos (≈ **2.7 K cosmic background**).
> - The downlink **EIRP** would be insufficient to close the link budget at the receiving Earth Station.
> - **Frequency reuse, polarization diversity, and channelization** — the very foundations of commercial satellite communication — would be impossible.
>
> In short: **The Communication Subsystem defines the satellite's "throughput" in bits/sec and its commercial value in crores of rupees.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Layers of the Communication Subsystem

The communication subsystem is structured in **four logical layers**, each performing a distinct function. KTU 2024 examiners expect students to identify these layers in both descriptive answers and numerical problems.

### Layer 1 — **Antenna Subsystem**
- Consists of a **receive reflector** and a **transmit reflector** (or one shared dual-band reflector).
- Provides **gain** $G = \eta \left( \dfrac{\pi D f}{c} \right)^2$ where $\eta$ is aperture efficiency (typically **0.55–0.70**), $D$ is diameter, $f$ is frequency.
- Often supports **dual polarization** (LHCP + RHCP or H + V) for **frequency reuse** — doubling capacity without new spectrum.

### Layer 2 — **Front-End Electronics (Low-Noise Section)**
- Includes the **LNA**, often followed by a **Low-Noise Converter (LNC)**.
- Cascade **Noise Figure** is the defining figure-of-merit.
- Typical LNA noise figures: **1.0 dB at C-band**, **1.5 dB at Ku-band**, **2.5 dB at Ka-band**.

### Layer 3 — **Channelization & Frequency Translation**
- Splits the wideband uplink into individual **transponder channels** (IMUX).
- Performs **down-conversion** to an Intermediate Frequency (e.g., **4 GHz → 1 GHz IF → 12 GHz** in a C/Ku payload).
- Reassembles channels at downlink (OMUX).

### Layer 4 — **High-Power Amplification**
- **TWTA:** Used for high-power applications (> **50 W RF output**), typical for **Ku/Ka-band**.
- **SSPA:** Used for lower-power, highly linear applications (L/S-band, modern phased arrays).
- Operates near **saturation** for maximum power efficiency, with **Input Back-Off (IBO)** and **Output Back-Off (OBO)** to control intermodulation distortion.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-ready**. Memorize every entry, including the unit column.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Antenna Gain (dB) | $G = 10 \log_{10} \left[ \eta \left( \dfrac{\pi D f}{c} \right)^2 \right]$ | **dBi** | Determines EIRP & G/T |
| 2 | EIRP (satellite downlink) | $EIRP_{DL} = P_{TWTA} + G_{TX} - L_{feed}$ | **dBW** | Downlink power metric |
| 3 | EIRP (satellite uplink) | $EIRP_{UL} = P_{PA} + G_{TX}$ | **dBW** | Uplink power metric |
| 4 | Free-space path loss | $L_p = 32.45 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ | **dB** | GEO link: ~196–206 dB |
| 5 | Receiver G/T | $G/T = G_{RX} - 10 \log_{10}(T_{sys})$ | **dB/K** | Earth-station figure-of-merit |
| 6 | System noise temp | $T_{sys} = T_{ant} + T_{LNA} + T_{misc}$ | **K** | Includes sky, LNA, feed |
| 7 | Cascaded noise figure (Friis) | $F_{tot} = F_1 + \dfrac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \dfrac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots$ | **dimensionless** | Multi-stage receiver |
| 8 | Carrier-to-Noise Density | $\dfrac{C}{N_0} = EIRP - L_p + \dfrac{G}{T} - k$ | **dB·Hz** | Link budget |
| 9 | Saturation Flux Density | $SFD = -10 \log_{10}\left[\dfrac{4 \pi}{\lambda^2} \cdot \dfrac{G_{RX} \cdot k \cdot T_{sys}}{P_{sat}}\right]$ | **dBW/m²** | Uplink power reference |
| 10 | Transponder gain | $G_{trans} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$ (linear) | **dB** | Repeater amplification |
| 11 | Carrier-to-Intermodulation | $\left(\dfrac{C}{I}\right)_{IM} = \left(\dfrac{C}{I}\right)_{0} - 2 (OBO)$ | **dB** | TWTA non-linearity |
| 12 | Number of transponders | $N_T = \dfrac{BW_{usable}}{BW_{per\,ch} \times (1 + \text{guard})}$ | **integer** | Payload sizing |
| 13 | Bandwidth efficiency (QPSK) | $\eta = \dfrac{R_b}{BW} \approx 0.7 \to 0.9$ | **bit/s/Hz** | Spectral efficiency |
| 14 | Boltzmann's constant | $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K | **J/K** | Thermal noise reference |
| 15 | Satellite range (GEO) | $d \approx 35{,}786$ to $41{,}671$ km | **km** | Slant range varies with elevation |

> [!NOTE]
> In all decibel formulas above, **NEVER** use the literal pipe symbol `|A|` inside a markdown table — the renderer breaks. Use `\vert A \vert` or `\mid A \mid` (as shown above) when absolute value is required inside a table cell.

---

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Block

> [!NOTE]
> **Why an LNA first?** Because the **Friis formula** shows the *first stage's noise figure dominates* the cascade. Adding a low-noise pre-amp ($F_1 = 1$ dB) right after the antenna crushes the noise budget.
>
> **Why down-convert to IF?** Because designing stable, sharp filters, demodulators, and gain stages is much easier at a **fixed intermediate frequency** (e.g., 70 MHz, 140 MHz, 1 GHz) than at the variable RF uplink.
>
> **Why TWTA near saturation?** Because **DC-to-RF efficiency** peaks there (≈ 60–70 %), critical when satellite prime power is limited to a few kW from solar arrays.
>
> **Why OMUX?** Because combining amplified channels *before* the antenna (in a single waveguide-run) avoids the **3 dB hybrid-combiner loss** of post-amplifier combining — a major commercial advantage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Friis' Cascaded Noise Figure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most-asked derivation** in KTU Module 1 of OEECT832. Board examiners expect the full algebraic steps.

**Statement:** For a cascade of $n$ amplifier/mixer stages, the overall noise factor $F_{tot}$ is dominated by the first stage.

**Step 1.** Define the available power gain $G_i$ and noise factor $F_i$ of the $i$-th stage.

**Step 2.** The output signal-to-noise ratio of the cascade equals:

$$
\dfrac{S_{out}}{N_{out}} = \dfrac{S_{in} \cdot G_1 G_2 \cdots G_n}{N_{in} F_1 G_1 G_2 \cdots G_n + N_{a2} G_2 G_3 \cdots G_n + N_{a3} G_3 \cdots G_n + \cdots + N_{an}}
$$

**Step 3.** Divide numerator and denominator by the common factor $G_1 G_2 \cdots G_n$:

$$
\dfrac{S_{out}}{N_{out}} = \dfrac{S_{in}}{N_{in} F_1 + \dfrac{N_{a2}}{G_1} + \dfrac{N_{a3}}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots}
$$

**Step 4.** Recall that the *noise factor* of stage $i$ is $F_i = \dfrac{N_{out,i}}{G_i \cdot N_{in}} = 1 + \dfrac{N_{ai}}{G_i \cdot N_{in}}$. Thus $\dfrac{N_{ai}}{G_i} = (F_i - 1) \cdot N_{in}$.

**Step 5.** Substituting back:

$$
F_{tot} = F_1 + \dfrac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \dfrac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots + \dfrac{F_n - 1}{G_1 G_2 \cdots G_{n-1}}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{tot} = F_1 + \sum_{i=2}^{n} \dfrac{F_i - 1}{\prod_{j=1}^{i-1} G_j}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** Multiply the dB form to get $NF_{tot}$ (dB) = $10 \log_{10}(F_{tot})$. The first stage's noise figure $F_1$ sets the floor; every subsequent stage is *divided down* by the gain that precedes it.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Saturation Flux Density (SFD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> SFD defines the *required uplink flux density* at the satellite antenna to drive the TWTA into saturation. It is the **practical handle** the Earth station uses to set its uplink power.

**Step 1.** At saturation, the received RF power at the satellite input equals:

$$
P_{rx,sat} = \dfrac{P_{TWTA,sat}}{G_{transponder}}
$$

**Step 2.** The flux density $\Phi$ that produces $P_{rx,sat}$ through an antenna of effective aperture $A_e$ is:

$$
\Phi_{sat} = \dfrac{P_{rx,sat}}{A_e} = \dfrac{4 \pi P_{rx,sat}}{\lambda^2 \, G_{RX}}
$$

**Step 3.** Substitute $P_{rx,sat} = k \, T_{sys} \cdot SFD_{linear} \cdot BW$ (noise-limited relation; in narrowband, $BW \to 1$ Hz reference is implicit):

$$
SFD \;(\text{dBW/m}^2) = -10 \log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{4 \pi}{\lambda^2 G_{RX}} \cdot \dfrac{P_{TWTA,sat}}{k \, T_{sys} \cdot BW_{ref}}\right]
$$

**Step 4.** Standard form (BW reference = 1 Hz, $BW_{ref} = 0$ dB·Hz):

$$
\boxed{SFD = -10 \log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{4 \pi}{\lambda^2} \cdot \dfrac{G_{RX}}{k T_{sys} / P_{sat}} \right)}
$$

**Step 5.** In a 4 GHz C-band link, typical $SFD \approx -72$ to $-90$ **dBW/m²**. A *higher* SFD value (e.g., −72) means the satellite demands *less* uplink flux — i.e., the transponder is *easier* to drive. Operators lower the gain to raise SFD when uplink EIRP is excessive.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Number of Transponders in a Payload

> [!NOTE]
> A payload engineer allocates the usable bandwidth $BW_{usable}$ into $N_T$ channels of bandwidth $BW_{ch}$ separated by a **guard band** $g$ (typically 5–10 % of $BW_{ch}$).

**Step 1.** Each transponder occupies:

$$
BW_{occupied} = BW_{ch} \, (1 + g)
$$

**Step 2.** The total number of transponders is:

$$
N_T = \left\lfloor \dfrac{BW_{usable}}{BW_{ch}(1+g)} \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 3.** Example: $BW_{usable} = 500$ MHz, $BW_{ch} = 36$ MHz, $g = 0.1$:

$$
N_T = \left\lfloor \dfrac{500}{36 \times 1.1} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 12.626 \right\rfloor = 12 \text{ transponders}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This 12-transponder C-band layout is the **classic design of INSAT-2 / early Intelsat** spacecraft.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — System Noise Temperature from Cascaded Stages

**Step 1.** Convert each stage noise figure to equivalent noise temperature:

$$
T_i = T_0 \, (F_i - 1), \quad T_0 = 290 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2.** Convert each stage gain to a linear (not dB) value:

$$
G_{i,lin} = 10^{G_{i,dB}/10}
$$

**Step 3.** Effective input noise temperature referenced to the antenna terminals:

$$
T_{sys} = T_{ant} + T_{LNA} + \dfrac{T_{mixer}}{G_{LNA}} + \dfrac{T_{IF}}{G_{LNA} \, G_{mixer}} + \cdots
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{sys} = T_{ant} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{T_i}{\prod_{j=1}^{i} G_j}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The antenna sees the *sky temperature* (≈ 25–50 K at C-band pointing at zenith) plus *ground pickup* (≈ 5–15 K) plus *feed losses* (≈ 10–30 K) — this is the irreducible $T_{ant}$. The LNA's $T_{LNA}$ then dominates the cascade.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Transponder Type Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners love this **comparison table** in 14-mark questions. Memorize it cold.

| Parameter | **Bent-Pipe (Single-Conversion)** | **Double-Conversion (Frequency-Translation)** | **Regenerative (On-Board Processing)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Down-conversion stages | 1 (RF → baseband-ish IF) | 2 (RF → IF₁ → IF₂) | Full demod → remod |
| On-board demodulation | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (QPSK/8PSK demod) |
| Signal transparency | Analog + Digital | Analog + Digital | Digital only |
| BER performance | Limited by uplink noise | Better isolation | Best (uplink/downlink errors *don't* add) |
| Complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| Mass & Power | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| EIRP stability | Depends on input level | Better AGC | Best (regenerated) |
| Typical use | TV distribution, FSS | Point-to-point trunk | Mobile, B-ISDN, military |
| Example mission | Early Intelsat, INSAT-2 | Intelsat-VII, Hot-Bird | Inmarsat-4, AEHF, Iridium NEXT |
| Cost | \$ | \$\$ | \$\$\$ |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (For Practice)

**Problem:** A satellite receiver has three cascaded stages: LNA (NF = 1.2 dB, G = 30 dB), Mixer (NF = 8 dB, G = −6 dB), IF amplifier (NF = 4 dB, G = 40 dB). Find the cascaded noise figure.

**Solution:**

**Step 1.** Convert all to linear:

$$
F_1 = 10^{1.2/10} = 1.318, \quad G_1 = 10^{30/10} = 1000
$$

$$
F_2 = 10^{8/10} = 6.310, \quad G_2 = 10^{-6/10} = 0.251
$$

$$
F_3 = 10^{4/10} = 2.512, \quad G_3 = 10^{40/10} = 10000
$$

**Step 2.** Apply Friis:

$$
F_{tot} = 1.318 + \dfrac{6.310 - 1}{1000} + \dfrac{2.512 - 1}{1000 \times 0.251}
$$

$$
F_{tot} = 1.318 + 0.00531 + \dfrac{1.512}{251.2} = 1.318 + 0.00531 + 0.00602
$$

$$
F_{tot} = 1.3293
$$

**Step 3.** Convert to dB:

$$
NF_{tot} = 10 \log_{10}(1.3293) = 1.236 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Despite two noisy later stages (8 dB, 4 dB), the cascade is dominated by the LNA's 1.2 dB. *This is why LNA design is the most critical RF engineering task in any satellite payload.*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams use the **Mermaid block-level functional architecture** style. Node IDs are alphanumeric and labels are plain uppercase text to satisfy the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rendering safeguards.

## 4.1 Overall Communication Subsystem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ANT[ANTENNA SUBSYSTEM]
        RXANT[RX REFLECTOR LNA ASSEMBLY]
        TXANT[TX REFLECTOR]
    end

    subgraph RXFE[RECEIVE FRONT END]
        LNA1[LNA 1 COLD REDUNDANT]
        LNA2[LNA 2 HOT STANDBY]
        DC[DOWN CONVERTER RF TO IF]
        IMUX[INPUT MULTIPLEXER IMUX]
    end

    subgraph CHAN[CHANNELIZATION]
        CH1[TRANSPONDER 1 36 MHZ]
        CH2[TRANSPONDER 2 36 MHZ]
        CH3[TRANSPONDER 3 36 MHZ]
        CH4[TRANSPONDER N 36 MHZ]
    end

    subgraph TXFE[TRANSMIT FRONT END]
        TWTA1[TWTA 1 100 W]
        TWTA2[TWTA 2 100 W]
        OMUX[OUTPUT MULTIPLEXER OMUX]
        UC[UP CONVERTER IF TO RF]
    end

    subgraph TTNC[TT AND C SUBSYSTEM]
        TTC1[TELEMETRY ENCODER]
        TTC2[COMMAND DECODER]
    end

    RXANT --> LNA1
    LNA1 --> DC
    LNA2 --> DC
    DC --> IMUX
    IMUX --> CH1
    IMUX --> CH2
    IMUX --> CH3
    IMUX --> CH4
    CH1 --> TWTA1
    CH2 --> TWTA1
    CH3 --> TWTA2
    CH4 --> TWTA2
    TWTA1 --> OMUX
    TWTA2 --> OMUX
    OMUX --> UC
    UC --> TXANT
    TTC1 -. monitors .- CH1
    TTC2 -. controls .- LNA1
```

---

## 4.2 Single-Conversion "Bent-Pipe" Transponder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RU[UPLINK RF 6 GHZ] --> RA[RECEIVE ANTENNA]
    RA --> LNA3[LNA NF 1 DB GAIN 50 DB]
    LNA3 --> MIX1[MIXER WITH LOCAL OSCILLATOR]
    LO1[LOCAL OSC 5 GHZ] --> MIX1
    MIX1 --> IFAMP[IF AMPLIFIER 4 GHZ]
    IFAMP --> TWTA3[TWTA 60 W]
    TWTA3 --> TXA[TX ANTENNA]
    TXA --> RD[DOWNLINK RF 4 GHZ]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The uplink signal at 6 GHz is amplified, mixed down to a 4 GHz IF, amplified again at IF, and then a single TWTA boosts the IF up to ~60 W for downlink at 4 GHz. **No demodulation** — the signal is *bent through* the satellite like a bent pipe.

---

## 4.3 Double-Conversion Transponder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RU2[UPLINK 14 GHZ] --> RA2[RX ANTENNA]
    RA2 --> LNA4[LNA]
    LNA4 --> M1[MIXER 1]
    LO2[LO1 11.8 GHZ] --> M1
    M1 --> IF1[IF1 2.2 GHZ BPF]
    IF1 --> M2[MIXER 2]
    LO3[LO2 1.8 GHZ] --> M2
    M2 --> IF2[IF2 400 MHZ NARROW BPF]
    IF2 --> TWTA4[TWTA 100 W]
    TWTA4 --> M3[UP MIXER]
    LO4[LO3 11.4 GHZ] --> M3
    M3 --> DL2[DOWNLINK 11.8 GHZ]
    DL2 --> TXA2[TX ANTENNA]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Two down-conversions (RF → IF1 → IF2) provide **better filtering, better AGC, and lower spurious outputs**. Standard in **Intelsat-VII and Hot-Bird** C/Ku payloads.

---

## 4.4 Regenerative (On-Board Processing) Transponder

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    UP3[UPLINK 30 GHZ KA] --> RA3[RX ANT ARRAY]
    RA3 --> LNA5[LNA ARRAY]
    LNA5 --> DC3[DIGITAL DOWN CONVERTER]
    DC3 --> DEMOD[DEMODULATOR QPSK 8PSK]
    DEMOD --> DECOD[CHANNEL DECODER FEC LDPC]
    DECOD --> SWITCH[ONBOARD BASEBAND SWITCH ROUTER]
    SWITCH --> COD[CHANNEL ENCODER]
    COD --> MOD[MODULATOR QPSK 8PSK]
    MOD --> UC3[DIGITAL UP CONVERTER]
    UC3 --> TWTA5[SSPA OR TWTA ARRAY]
    TWTA5 --> TX3[ACTIVE PHASED ARRAY TX]
    TX3 --> DN3[DOWNLINK 20 GHZ KA]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The signal is **fully demodulated and re-modulated on board**. Uplink BER and downlink BER are *independent*, allowing link budgets to be halved. Used in **AEHF, Inmarsat-5 Global Xpress, and Starlink v2-mini satellites**.

---

## 4.5 Functional State Machine — Transponder Redundancy

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Nominal
    Nominal --> Standby : LNA Failure Detected
    Standby --> Nominal : Command Restores
    Nominal --> TWTA_Swap : TWTA Failure
    TWTA_Swap --> Nominal : Spare TWTA Engaged
    Nominal --> Safe_Mode : Multiple Failures
    Safe_Mode --> [*] : End of Mission
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**Define "Communication Subsystem" of a satellite. List its main functional blocks.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> The **Communication Subsystem** (or **payload**) of a satellite is the on-board RF assembly that **receives, amplifies, frequency-translates, channelizes, and re-transmits** signals between Earth stations.

**Main functional blocks (1.5 marks):**
1. **Receive antenna** and **Transmit antenna** (sometimes a common dual-band reflector).
2. **LNA (Low Noise Amplifier)** — first stage gain with minimum noise.
3. **Down-converter** (RF → IF).
4. **Input Multiplexer (IMUX)** — channelization.
5. **High Power Amplifier (TWTA or SSPA)** — power boost.
6. **Up-converter** (IF → RF).
7. **Output Multiplexer (OMUX)** — recombining.

**Block diagram reference (0.5 mark):** Refer to Section 4.1.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]
**Compare TWTA and SSPA. When is each preferred?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | **TWTA** | **SSPA** |
|---|---|---|
| Active device | Travelling-Wave Tube | GaN / GaAs FET |
| Power efficiency at saturation | 60–70 % | 35–50 % |
| RF output power | Up to 250 W | Usually < 50 W |
| Bandwidth | Octave or more | Narrower (multi-octave possible) |
| Linearity | Less linear (back-off needed) | More linear |
| Lifetime | ~15 years (TWT wear) | > 18 years (solid-state) |
| Preferred for | High-power Ku/Ka downlink, DTH TV | L/S-band, mobile, phased-array payloads |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** TWTA is preferred for **high-power bent-pipe transponders**, while SSPA is preferred for **linear, multi-carrier, or active-antenna payloads** (e.g., Starlink, OneWeb, Global Xpress).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Module 1 of OEECT832 has a **choice-based 14-mark question**. The two alternatives given below are mutually exclusive — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ⭐ Question A. [14 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the operation of a **single-conversion bent-pipe transponder**. Discuss the role of LNA, IMUX, TWTA, and OMUX. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Friis formula for cascaded noise figure** for a three-stage receiver. A satellite receiver has LNA (NF = 1.5 dB, G = 40 dB), Mixer (NF = 10 dB, G = −8 dB), IF Amplifier (NF = 5 dB, G = 50 dB). Calculate the overall noise figure in dB. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Operation (7 marks):**

**Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the figure shown in **Section 4.2** above (bent-pipe block diagram).

**Step-by-step explanation (4 marks):**
1. **Receive antenna** captures the uplink signal at e.g. 6 GHz. Power level is around **−100 dBm**.
2. **LNA** amplifies by ~50 dB with NF ≈ 1 dB. Output power is ~−50 dBm.
3. **Down-converting mixer** with a local oscillator at ~5.5 GHz produces a **4 GHz IF**.
4. **IMUX (Input Multiplexer)** splits the wideband composite into individual **36 MHz** transponder channels.
5. Each transponder's **TWTA** amplifies the IF signal to **~50–100 W RF** (i.e., +47 to +50 dBm).
6. **OMUX (Output Multiplexer)** recombines the channels into a single composite downlink.
7. **Transmit antenna** beams the downlink to the Earth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical point:** In bent-pipe, *the modulation format is preserved end-to-end* — the satellite is a "transparent" repeater, oblivious to digital or analog nature of the carrier.

**Part (b) — Friis Derivation & Numerical (7 marks):**

**Derivation (3.5 marks):** Refer to **Section 3.1** for the full derivation. The standard result is:

$$
F_{tot} = F_1 + \dfrac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \dfrac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2}
$$

**Numerical (3.5 marks):**

**Step 1.** Convert all dB values to linear:

$$
F_1 = 10^{1.5/10} = 1.413, \quad G_1 = 10^{40/10} = 10000
$$

$$
F_2 = 10^{10/10} = 10, \quad G_2 = 10^{-8/10} = 0.158
$$

$$
F_3 = 10^{5/10} = 3.162
$$

**Step 2.** Apply Friis' formula:

$$
F_{tot} = 1.413 + \dfrac{10 - 1}{10000} + \dfrac{3.162 - 1}{10000 \times 0.158}
$$

$$
F_{tot} = 1.413 + 0.0009 + \dfrac{2.162}{1585} = 1.413 + 0.0009 + 0.001364
$$

$$
F_{tot} \approx 1.4153
$$

**Step 3.** Convert to dB:

$$
NF_{tot} = 10 \log_{10}(1.4153) \approx 1.508 \text{ dB}
$$

**Answer:** The cascaded noise figure is **≈ 1.51 dB**, dominated by the LNA.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Common mistakes in this question:
> 1. ❌ Forgetting to convert dB → linear *before* applying Friis. Friis works in **linear** domain.
> 2. ❌ Mixing up *gain* with *noise figure* in the second/third terms. The denominator is **gain of *preceding* stages**, not the stage itself.
> 3. ❌ Sign error in the last term: the formula has $(F_i - 1)$, not $(1 - F_i)$.
> 4. ❌ Not converting the final answer back to dB.

---

### ⭐ Question B. [14 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**(a)** Explain the **regenerative transponder** with a neat block diagram. Compare it with the **bent-pipe transponder** in terms of BER performance, complexity, and applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Saturation Flux Density (SFD)** of a satellite transponder. A C-band transponder has $G_{RX} = 30$ dBi, $T_{sys} = 500$ K, $P_{sat} = 20$ W, operating at 4 GHz. Compute the SFD in dBW/m². Take $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Regenerative Transponder (7 marks):**

**Block diagram (3 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.4**.

**Operation (2 marks):** In a regenerative transponder, the uplink signal is:
1. **Demodulated on board** to recover baseband bits.
2. **Re-modulated** on a fresh downlink carrier.
3. Optionally **decoded, switched/routed, re-encoded** (full on-board processing — OBP).

**Comparison table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Bent-Pipe | Regenerative |
|---|---|---|
| BER | Uplink + Downlink errors *add* | Errors *do not* add |
| Complexity | Low | High (modems on board) |
| Power | Low | High (demod/remod power) |
| Flexibility | Limited (transparent) | High (routing, coding gain) |
| Applications | TV, FSS, VSAT | Mobile, broadband (HTS), military |

**Part (b) — SFD Numerical (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Saturation Flux Density is the **uplink flux density at the satellite antenna** that drives the on-board TWTA to its **saturated output power**. It is the operational reference the Earth station uses to set its uplink EIRP.

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$
SFD = -10 \log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{4 \pi}{\lambda^2} \cdot \dfrac{G_{RX} \cdot k \cdot T_{sys}}{P_{sat}} \right)
$$

**Numerical computation (4 marks):**

**Step 1.** Convert all quantities:
- $G_{RX} = 10^{30/10} = 1000$ (linear)
- $T_{sys} = 500$ K
- $P_{sat} = 20$ W
- $f = 4$ GHz $\Rightarrow \lambda = c/f = 3\times10^8 / 4\times10^9 = 0.075$ m

**Step 2.** Compute the inside of the log:

$$
\dfrac{4 \pi}{\lambda^2} = \dfrac{4 \pi}{(0.075)^2} = \dfrac{12.566}{0.005625} = 2234.4 \text{ (m}^{-2}\text{)}
$$

**Step 3.** Compute the gain-temp-power ratio:

$$
\dfrac{G_{RX} \cdot k \cdot T_{sys}}{P_{sat}} = \dfrac{1000 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 500}{20}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{6.9 \times 10^{-18}}{20} = 3.45 \times 10^{-19}
$$

**Step 4.** Multiply:

$$
SFD_{lin} = 2234.4 \times 3.45 \times 10^{-19} = 7.71 \times 10^{-16} \text{ W/m}^2
$$

**Step 5.** Convert to dBW/m²:

$$
SFD = 10 \log_{10}(7.71 \times 10^{-16}) = 10 \times (-15.113) = -151.1 \text{ dBW/m}^2
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** The value **−151 dBW/m²** is the *input-referred* SFD. In practice, the **SFD at the Earth's surface (uplink EIRP equivalent)** adds the satellite G/T and the uplink path loss. Students often confuse the *flux density at the satellite* with the *flux density at the Earth station* — keep the **reference point** straight in the diagram!

**Answer:** SFD ≈ **−151.1 dBW/m²** at the satellite antenna.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this checklist the night before the exam. If you can tick every box, you're ready.

- [ ] **Communication Subsystem = payload = transponder bank + antennas + HPAs**.
- [ ] The **LNA is the most critical component** — Friis formula says first stage dominates noise.
- [ ] **Friis formula:** $F_{tot} = F_1 + \sum (F_i - 1)/\prod G_{prev}$ — always use **linear**, never dB.
- [ ] **Bent-pipe = transparent = single-conversion**; **double-conversion = better filtering**; **regenerative = on-board demod/remod**.
- [ ] **TWTA** is preferred for high-power bent-pipe; **SSPA** is preferred for linear multi-carrier / active antennas.
- [ ] **IMUX** splits uplink spectrum into transponder channels; **OMUX** recombines them after HPA.
- [ ] **SFD** defines the uplink flux needed to saturate the HPA. Operators *raise SFD* (with gain step) when uplink EIRP is too high.
- [ ] **EIRP** = $P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{feed}$ (dBW); **G/T** = $G_{RX} - 10 \log_{10}(T_{sys})$ (dB/K).
- [ ] **Free-space loss** at 4 GHz GEO ≈ **196.5 dB**; at 12 GHz GEO ≈ **205.5 dB**.
- [ ] **GEO altitude** = 35,786 km; slant range varies 35,786 → 41,671 km with elevation.
- [ ] Standard **transponder bandwidths**: 36, 54, 72, 120, 240 MHz.
- [ ] **Boltzmann's constant** = $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K = **−228.6 dBW/K·Hz**.
- [ ] **Number of transponders** = $BW_{usable} / [BW_{ch}(1+g)]$; INSAT-class designs use 12 transponders.
- [ ] **TWTA efficiency** peaks at saturation; **IBO and OBO** control intermodulation distortion.
- [ ] **Cold redundancy** = physically separate spare; **hot redundancy** = spare is on, output switched in.
- [ ] **Frequency reuse** doubles capacity without new spectrum via dual polarization (LHCP/RHCP or H/V).
- [ ] **Noise temperature** of the cascade = $T_{ant} + T_{LNA} + T_{mixer}/G_{LNA} + \cdots$
- [ ] **Polarization isolation** ≥ 30 dB is standard for dual-polarized payloads.
- [ ] A *higher* SFD number means the satellite is *less sensitive* (operator-friendly uplink).
- [ ] **Regenerative transponders** decouple uplink and downlink BER — link budget *halves*.
- [ ] **Block diagram** drawing earns easy marks — never skip it in 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Satellite Antennas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Satellite Antennas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Satellite Antenna:** A satellite antenna is a specialized electromagnetic radiator/receiver mounted on a spacecraft that is responsible for the efficient transmission and reception of radio frequency (RF) signals between the satellite and ground stations (or between satellites in inter-satellite links). For a geostationary communications satellite, the antenna is the single most critical payload element because it determines the **EIRP (Effective Isotropic Radiated Power)**, the **G/T ratio (Gain-to-Noise-Temperature)**, and the **coverage footprint** on Earth.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, satellite antennas are studied under two broad classifications:
1. **Communication Antennas** – used for telemetry, tracking, command (TT&C), and the main communication payload (e.g., global beams, spot beams, shaped beams).
2. **Antennas used in the Satellite Earth Station** – such as the parabolic dish, Cassegrain feed, and phased-array feed systems.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a satellite antenna like a torchlight (flashlight) in the night sky.**

When you point an ordinary bulb in all directions, the light spreads thinly and dimly. But when you place the bulb inside a curved mirror (a reflector), all the rays are concentrated into a tight, powerful beam that travels a long distance. A satellite antenna works on the same principle: a **feed horn** (the "bulb") radiates RF energy toward a **parabolic dish** (the "mirror"), which then collimates the energy into a narrow beam aimed at Earth. The narrower the beam, the higher the **antenna gain** and the further the signal can reach without losing strength. Conversely, a wider beam covers more of the Earth's surface but with less intensity per unit area — this is the classic **gain-versus-coverage trade-off** in satellite antenna design.

### 1.3 Why Satellite Antennas Are Special

Unlike terrestrial antennas, satellite-borne antennas must satisfy extreme engineering constraints:

- **Mass and Volume:** Every kilogram launched costs tens of thousands of dollars; the antenna must be lightweight and stowable (folded during launch, deployed in orbit via deployment mechanisms).
- **Thermal Stability:** The antenna must maintain its electrical shape across temperature swings of **−180 °C to +120 °C** in the vacuum of space.
- **Pointing Accuracy:** A geostationary satellite at **35,786 km** altitude must point its beam within **0.1° to 0.3°** to keep the footprint on the correct country.
- **Multi-beam and Frequency Reuse:** Modern antennas use multiple spot beams to reuse the same frequency band, multiplying the capacity of the satellite.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics (must memorize for KTU exams):**
> - **Free-space wavelength:** $\lambda = c / f$, where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
> - **Operating frequency bands:** L (1–2 GHz), S (2–4 GHz), C (4–8 GHz), Ku (12–18 GHz), Ka (26–40 GHz)
> - **Half-power beamwidth (HPBW):** $\theta_{3dB} \approx 70 \lambda / D$ (in degrees, for a parabolic reflector of diameter $D$)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Antenna Parameters (The "Six Pillars")

For any satellite antenna, the following six parameters completely characterize its performance. The KTU examiner expects students to write clean definitions and unit-aware expressions for each.

1. **Antenna Gain ($G$)** – The ability of the antenna to concentrate power in a given direction compared to an isotropic radiator. Unitless, expressed in dBi.
2. **Half-Power Beamwidth (HPBW, $\theta_{3dB}$)** – The angular width of the main lobe between the two points where the radiated power falls to half (–3 dB) of its maximum.
3. **Effective Aperture ($A_e$)** – The equivalent physical area that "captures" incident RF power. It is always less than the geometric area due to inefficiencies.
4. **Side-Lobe Level (SLL)** – The ratio (in dB) of the peak of the largest side lobe to the peak of the main lobe.
5. **Polarization** – The orientation of the E-field vector (Linear: H/V; Circular: RHCP/LHCP). Satellites typically use **dual-polarization** to double capacity.
6. **Axial Ratio (AR)** – For circular polarization, the ratio of major to minor axis of the polarization ellipse. AR = 1 (0 dB) = perfect circular; AR = $\infty$ = linear.

### 2.2 The Physics of Parabolic Reflector Antennas (Most Common on Satellites)

A parabolic reflector works on the geometric property that **all rays from the focal point reflect off the surface as parallel rays**. This converts a spherical wavefront at the feed into a plane wavefront at the aperture, producing a highly directive beam.

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**

- The **feed horn** is placed at the **focus** of the parabola.
- The feed radiates a spherical wave; each ray travels a different path length to the dish surface.
- Due to the parabola's geometry, every reflected ray leaves the dish **in phase** along a plane perpendicular to the axis.
- The result is a **plane wave** propagating along the axis, equivalent to a uniformly illuminated aperture of diameter $D$.
- The aperture efficiency $\eta_a$ (typically 0.55–0.65) accounts for feed spillover, blockage, surface errors, and illumination taper.

### 2.3 Types of Antennas Used on Satellites

| Type | Typical Application | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Wire dipole / whip** | Early LEO satellites, simple TT&C | Omnidirectional, low gain (~2 dBi) |
| **Horn antenna** | Feed for large reflectors, spot beam feeds | Moderate gain, wide bandwidth |
| **Parabolic reflector (single)** | Most geostationary comms satellites | High gain, narrow beam |
| **Cassegrain dual-reflector** | Large earth-station antennas | Low noise (feed at vertex), long focal length |
| **Phased array** | Multi-beam, beam-hopping (e.g., Starlink) | Electronic steering, no moving parts |
| **Shaped-beam / horn cluster** | Domestic coverage (e.g., INSAT) | Custom contour matching country shape |
| **Helical antenna** | TTC at UHF/S-band | Circular polarization, moderate gain |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following table — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU Part B question on this module.**

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Antenna Gain (from aperture) | $G = \eta_a \left( \dfrac{\pi D}{\lambda} \right)^2$ | dimensionless (dBi) | $\eta_a$ = aperture efficiency (0.55–0.65) |
| Antenna Gain (alternative) | $G = \dfrac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2}$ | dimensionless | Universal relation |
| Half-Power Beamwidth | $\theta_{3dB} \approx 70 \lambda / D$ | degrees | Parabolic reflector, $D \gg \lambda$ |
| First-null Beamwidth | $\theta_{FNBW} \approx 145 \lambda / D$ | degrees | For uniformly illuminated circular aperture |
| Effective Aperture | $A_e = \eta_a A_{phys} = \eta_a \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | m² | $A_{phys}$ = geometric aperture |
| Friis Transmission Equation | $\dfrac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t G_r \left( \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R} \right)^2$ | dimensionless | Free-space, aligned, polarized |
| EIRP | $\text{EIRP} = P_t G_t$ | W (or dBW) | Key uplink/downlink metric |
| G/T Ratio | $G/T = \dfrac{G}{T_{sys}}$ | dB/K | Figure of merit of receiving system |
| Axial Ratio (dB) | $\text{AR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left( \dfrac{E_{maj}}{E_{min}} \right)$ | dB | AR = 0 dB means perfect CP |
| Beamwidth-to-Gain Product | $G \cdot \theta_{3dB}^2 \approx 27000 \, \eta_a$ | (deg)² dimensionless | Useful for quick trade-off checks |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EIRP and G/T together determine the link budget.** Without knowing both, you cannot size the antenna, transmitter power, or receiver noise figure of a satellite link.
- **Gain determines the satellite's "loudness"** in a given direction. Higher gain allows smaller ground dishes.
- **Spot beams** (gain ~ 35–45 dBi) at Ka-band are the foundation of modern High-Throughput Satellites (HTS) such as ViaSat-3, delivering terabits per second.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Antenna Gain from Effective Aperture

**Statement to Prove:** $G = \dfrac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2}$

**Starting Concept:** Consider an isotropic radiator that radiates total power $P_t$ uniformly in all directions. The power flux (Poynting vector magnitude) at a distance $R$ is:

$$
S_{iso} = \frac{P_t}{4\pi R^2}
$$

**Step 1: Power flux of a directive antenna.**

If the same power $P_t$ is fed to a directive antenna of gain $G$ (max direction), the flux at the same distance $R$ in the direction of peak gain becomes:

$$
S_{dir} = G \cdot S_{iso} = \frac{G P_t}{4\pi R^2}
$$

**Step 2: Definition of effective aperture.**

The effective aperture $A_e$ is the equivalent area that intercepts incident power to deliver it to the load. The power captured is:

$$
P_{cap} = S_{dir} \cdot A_e = \frac{G P_t \, A_e}{4\pi R^2}
$$

**Step 3: Equate to a plane wave capture model.**

By definition, the effective aperture must satisfy the reciprocity condition. For any lossless antenna, the gain and effective aperture are linked by:

$$
G = \frac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2}
$$

**Step 4: Rearrangement for the parabolic reflector.**

Substituting $A_e = \eta_a \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G &= \frac{4\pi}{\lambda^2} \cdot \eta_a \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \\[4pt]
  &= \eta_a \cdot \frac{\pi^2 D^2}{\lambda^2} \\[4pt]
  &= \eta_a \left( \frac{\pi D}{\lambda} \right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **standard KTU formula** for parabolic reflector gain. Mark the $\eta_a$ factor — losing it is the most common mistake in the exam.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Half-Power Beamwidth of a Parabolic Dish

**Statement to Prove:** $\theta_{3dB} \approx 70 \lambda / D$ degrees.

**Step 1: Field pattern of a uniformly illuminated circular aperture.**

The far-field radiation pattern of a circular aperture of diameter $D$ in the principal plane is given by the Airy pattern:

$$
E(\theta) = E_0 \cdot \frac{2 J_1(u)}{u}
$$

where $u = \dfrac{\pi D}{\lambda} \sin\theta$ and $J_1(\cdot)$ is the Bessel function of the first kind of order 1.

**Step 2: Power pattern.**

The normalized power pattern is:

$$
P(\theta) = \left[ \frac{2 J_1(u)}{u} \right]^2
$$

**Step 3: Condition for half-power points.**

The 3-dB point occurs where $P(\theta) = 0.5$, which means:

$$
\frac{2 J_1(u_{3dB})}{u_{3dB}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Step 4: Numerical solution of the Bessel equation.**

The first non-trivial root satisfying this condition is $u_{3dB} \approx 1.616$ (radians of the argument of the Bessel function). Therefore:

$$
u_{3dB} = \frac{\pi D}{\lambda} \sin(\theta_{3dB}/2) = 1.616
$$

**Step 5: Approximation for small angles.**

For narrow beams, $\sin(\theta_{3dB}/2) \approx \theta_{3dB}/2$ in radians. So:

$$
\frac{\pi D}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\theta_{3dB}}{2} = 1.616
$$

$$
\theta_{3dB} = \frac{2 \cdot 1.616}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{D} \text{ rad} = 1.029 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{D} \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 6: Convert to degrees.**

$$
\theta_{3dB} = 1.029 \cdot \frac{180}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{D} \approx 58.96 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{D} \text{ degrees}
$$

For a typical parabolic dish with edge taper, the practical empirical formula is:

$$
\boxed{\theta_{3dB} \approx 70 \, \frac{\lambda}{D} \text{ degrees}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Friis Transmission Equation (Satellite Link Foundation)

**Starting relation:** Power received by an antenna with effective aperture $A_{e,r}$ from a transmit antenna of gain $G_t$ at distance $R$:

$$
P_r = S_{t} \cdot A_{e,r} = \frac{G_t P_t}{4\pi R^2} \cdot A_{e,r}
$$

**Substituting** $A_{e,r} = \dfrac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r &= \frac{G_t P_t}{4\pi R^2} \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi} \\[4pt]
    &= P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left( \frac{\lambda}{4\pi R} \right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Friis equation**, the heart of every satellite link budget problem. In dB form:

$$
P_r (dBW) = P_t (dBW) + G_t (dBi) + G_r (dBi) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)
$$

The last term is the **free-space path loss** $L_{fs}$ (a negative number in dB).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Exam Reference

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical KTU Exam Value |
|---|---|---|
| $D$ | Parabolic dish diameter | 0.5 m to 30 m (earth station) |
| $f$ | Operating frequency | 4 GHz (C), 12 GHz (Ku), 20 GHz (Ka) |
| $\lambda$ | Wavelength ($c/f$) | 0.075 m at 4 GHz, 0.025 m at 12 GHz |
| $G$ | Antenna gain | 30 to 65 dBi |
| $\theta_{3dB}$ | Half-power beamwidth | 0.1° to 5° |
| $\eta_a$ | Aperture efficiency | 0.55 to 0.65 |
| SLL | Side-lobe level | −20 to −30 dB (typical design) |
| AR | Axial ratio (CP) | < 3 dB (good), < 1 dB (excellent) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Satellite Antenna Subsystem

This block diagram captures the functional architecture of a typical communications satellite antenna subsystem, from the receiver/transmitter interface to the radiated beam.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Transponder IF Input"] --> nodeB["High Power Amplifier TWTA or SSPA"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Waveguide Runnover and Filter"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Feed Horn Assembly"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Parabolic Main Reflector Diameter D"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Shaped Radiation Pattern Earth Coverage"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Ground Segment User Terminal"]
    nodeH["Telemetery Tracking and Command TTC"] --> nodeI["Omni or Horn Antenna"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["TTC Ground Station"]
    nodeK["Beacon Source"] --> nodeD
    nodeL["Attitude Control Reaction Wheels"] --> nodeE
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Cassegrain Dual-Reflector Geometry (Highly Used in Exam Drawings)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Feed Horn at Vertex of Main Dish"] --> nodeB["Hyperbolic Sub Reflector"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Parabolic Main Reflector Diameter D"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Collimated Plane Wave to Earth"]
    nodeE["Incoming Signal from Earth"] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeA
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Classification of Satellite Antennas

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Satellite Antennas"] --> nodeB["Wire Antennas Dipole Helix"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Aperture Antennas Horn Parabolic"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Reflector Antennas Cassegrain Gregorian"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["Array Antennas Phased Array Planar Array"]
    nodeA --> nodeF["Lens Antennas Bootlace Luneburg"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["Parabolic Single Offset or Symmetric"]
    nodeC --> nodeH["Horn Conical Pyramidal Corrugated"]
    nodeE --> nodeI["Spot Beam Beam Hopping Electronic Steering"]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Antenna Gain and Beamwidth Trade-Off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Increase Dish Diameter D"] --> nodeB["Higher Gain G in dBi"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Narrower Beamwidth in degrees"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Higher EIRP and Smaller Footprint"]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE["Better Pointing Accuracy Required"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Smaller Coverage Area on Earth"]
    nodeG["Increase Frequency Decrease Wavelength"] --> nodeB
    nodeG --> nodeC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Mermaid block diagram and not a physical sketch?** The KTU valuation scheme awards marks for clearly labeled, sequential, and logically connected subsystems. A functional block diagram communicates the signal-flow architecture more precisely than a hand-drawn geometry would in a typed exam answer, and is universally accepted as a substitute for physical drawings.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define the term "effective aperture" of an antenna. How is it related to the antenna gain?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Effective aperture ($A_e$) of an antenna is the equivalent area that intercepts incident electromagnetic wave power and delivers it to the matched load, as if the antenna were a physical area perpendicular to the direction of propagation.

It is related to antenna gain $G$ by the universal relation:

$$
A_e = \frac{G \, \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

For a parabolic reflector of diameter $D$ and aperture efficiency $\eta_a$, $A_e = \eta_a \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition 1 mark • Formula 1 mark • Application to parabolic 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is a "spot beam" antenna? Why is it used in modern communication satellites?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A spot beam is a narrow, high-gain antenna radiation pattern that concentrates RF energy over a small geographical region (typically a few hundred km across), as opposed to a global beam which covers nearly a third of the Earth's surface.

It is used to:
1. Increase the EIRP into a small area, allowing smaller, cheaper user terminals.
2. Allow **frequency reuse** by using the same frequency band in geographically separated beams, thereby multiplying system capacity.
3. Enable **beam hopping** in next-generation HTS (High-Throughput Satellites).

**Mark Distribution:** Definition 1 mark • Two reasons (1 mark each).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) Derive the expression for the gain of a parabolic reflector antenna in terms of its diameter and operating wavelength. (7 marks)**

**(b) A geostationary satellite at 36,000 km uses a parabolic antenna of diameter 2.4 m operating at 12 GHz. Compute the antenna gain, the half-power beamwidth, and the footprint diameter on Earth. Assume aperture efficiency = 0.6. (7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Start from the universal gain-aperture relation:

$$
G = \frac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2}
$$

For a parabolic dish of physical diameter $D$ with aperture efficiency $\eta_a$:

$$
A_e = \eta_a \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\boxed{G = \eta_a \left( \frac{\pi D}{\lambda} \right)^2}
$$

In decibels:

$$
G_{dBi} = 10 \log_{10}(\eta_a) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)
$$

**Mark distribution:** Gain-aperture relation 2 marks • Substitution of $A_e$ 2 marks • Final form 2 marks • Units/dB conversion 1 mark.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Wavelength.

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{12 \times 10^9} = 0.025 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2:** Antenna gain.

$$
\begin{aligned}
G &= 0.6 \times \left( \frac{\pi \times 2.4}{0.025} \right)^2 \\[4pt]
  &= 0.6 \times (301.59)^2 \\[4pt]
  &= 0.6 \times 90957 \\[4pt]
  &\approx 54574
\end{aligned}
$$

In dBi: $G_{dBi} = 10 \log_{10}(54574) \approx 47.37$ dBi.

**Step 3:** Half-power beamwidth.

$$
\theta_{3dB} = 70 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{D} = 70 \cdot \frac{0.025}{2.4} \approx 0.73^\circ
$$

**Step 4:** Footprint diameter on Earth.

For a GEO satellite at altitude $h = 36000$ km, the Earth's surface footprint diameter (approx.) is:

$$
D_{foot} \approx R_E \cdot \theta_{3dB} \text{ (in radians)}
$$

where $R_E = 6371$ km.

$$
\theta_{3dB} \text{ rad} = 0.73 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.01274 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
D_{foot} = 6371 \times 0.01274 \approx 81.2 \text{ km}
$$

**Final Answers:** $G \approx 47.37$ dBi • $\theta_{3dB} \approx 0.73°$ • $D_{foot} \approx 81$ km.

**Mark distribution:** Wavelength 1 mark • Gain 2 marks • Beamwidth 2 marks • Footprint 2 marks.

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternative Choice]**

**(a) Explain the working of a Cassegrain antenna with a neat block diagram. List two advantages over a single parabolic reflector. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the term "axial ratio" for a circularly polarized antenna. A satellite transmits a 4 GHz RHCP signal with EIRP of 50 dBW towards a ground station. The ground antenna has a gain of 40 dBi and the slant range is 38000 km. Calculate the received power assuming no atmospheric or polarization losses. (7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Cassegrain Working (7 marks):**

In a Cassegrain antenna, the feed horn is placed at the **vertex** of the main parabolic dish, not at the focal point. A small **hyperbolic sub-reflector** is placed in front of the main dish at the focal region.

Working:
1. The feed horn radiates a spherical wave that first strikes the hyperbolic sub-reflector.
2. The hyperbolic sub-reflector reflects this wave back to the main parabolic dish, behaving optically as if the wave originated from the main dish's focal point.
3. The main parabolic dish then collimates the wave into a plane wave radiating along the axis.

**Advantages over single parabolic reflector:**
1. **Low noise temperature:** Placing the feed at the vertex allows a low-noise amplifier (LNA) to be mounted directly behind the dish, minimizing waveguide length and hence thermal noise.
2. **Long effective focal length:** Achieved compactly, which improves aperture efficiency and reduces side-lobe level.

**Mark distribution:** Working explanation 4 marks • Diagram credit 1 mark • Two advantages 2 marks.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Wavelength.

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{4 \times 10^9} = 0.075 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2:** Free-space path loss.

$$
L_{fs} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{4\pi R}{\lambda} \right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{4\pi \times 38 \times 10^6}{0.075} \right)
$$

$$
L_{fs} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( 6.366 \times 10^9 \right) \approx 196.08 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3:** Received power using Friis equation in dB.

$$
P_r = \text{EIRP} + G_r - L_{fs} = 50 + 40 - 196.08
$$

$$
\boxed{P_r \approx -106.08 \text{ dBW} \approx 2.47 \text{ nW}}
$$

**Mark distribution:** Wavelength 1 mark • Path loss 3 marks • Friis equation 2 marks • Final answer 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2 to 4 Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting aperture efficiency $\eta_a$** when calculating gain. If the question states $\eta_a = 0.6$, the student who writes $G = (\pi D/\lambda)^2$ will lose 2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Mixing up $\theta_{3dB}$ formulas** — using $58\lambda/D$ instead of the empirical $70\lambda/D$ for a real (tapered) parabolic dish. The $70\lambda/D$ is the one expected unless "uniformly illuminated" is specified.
> 3. **Wrong units in dB conversions** — using natural log instead of log base 10 in the dB formula. The correct relation is: $G_{dBi} = 10 \log_{10}(G)$, NOT $10 \ln(G)$.
> 4. **Neglecting path loss units** in Friis equation — the path loss is in dB and is a *negative* number; do not write it as a positive quantity.
> 5. **Confusing gain with EIRP** — EIRP = $P_t \times G_t$ and includes the transmitter power. Gain is just the antenna's directional performance.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU prep):**
>
> - **Antenna Gain** for a parabolic dish: $G = \eta_a (\pi D / \lambda)^2$ — always include $\eta_a$.
> - **Half-Power Beamwidth** (empirical, for parabolic): $\theta_{3dB} \approx 70 \lambda / D$ degrees.
> - **Friis Equation** in dB: $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{fs}$ where $L_{fs} = 20 \log_{10}(4\pi R / \lambda)$.
> - **Aperture efficiency** $\eta_a$ typically 0.55–0.65 for parabolic reflectors.
> - **Axial Ratio** is a circular-polarization quality metric: 0 dB (perfect), 3 dB (acceptable for satellite), > 6 dB (essentially linear).
> - **GEO satellite altitude** = 35,786 km (sometimes 36,000 km in exam rounding).
> - **Frequency bands:** L, S, C, Ku, Ka — higher frequency = smaller $\lambda$ = higher gain for the same dish, but more rain attenuation.
> - **Cassegrain advantage:** Low noise figure and compact focal geometry.
> - **Phased array advantage:** Electronic beam steering with no moving mechanical parts.
> - **Spot beam purpose:** Frequency reuse and high EIRP into small regions.
> - **Beamwidth-to-Gain product:** $G \cdot \theta_{3dB}^2 \approx 27000 \, \eta_a$ — useful as a sanity check.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Satellite Link Design - Basic Link Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Satellite Link Design - Basic Link Analysis # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Satellite Link Design – Basic Link Analysis** is the quantitative engineering discipline concerned with determining the **received signal power**, **carrier-to-noise ratio (C/N)**, and **figure of merit (G/T)** for the radio-frequency (RF) communication path between an Earth station and a geostationary (GEO) satellite. The link is treated as a *one-dimensional line-of-sight* problem dominated by **free-space propagation**, **antenna directivity**, **amplifier non-ideality**, and **additive thermal noise**. The end goal is to ensure that the **bit-energy-to-noise spectral density ratio (E_b/N_0)** at the demodulator input meets the threshold required by the chosen modulation and coding scheme, while satisfying regulatory constraints of the **ITU** and the **FCC/ISRO spectrum masks**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is a *direct scoring area* in Module 2. Questions typically test the ability to compute **EIRP**, **free-space path loss (FSPL)**, **received carrier power (C)**, and the **uplink/downlink C/N ratio**. Numerical problems of 7 to 14 marks are extremely common. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cosmic Delivery System" Imagine you are sending a fragile, low-weight parcel (your **information signal**) from a high-altitude warehouse (the **satellite**) to a house on the ground (the **Earth station**). | Delivery Component | Satellite-Link Equivalent | Engineering Quantity | |---|---|---| | Powerful launcher (to throw the parcel far) | High-power transmitter + focused antenna | **EIRP (Equivalent Isotropic Radiated Power)** | | Distance the parcel must travel | ~35,786 km for GEO satellite | **Slant range, `d`** | | Air resistance spreading the parcel | Spherical spreading of EM wavefront | **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** | | Rain, dust, fog along the path | Atmospheric & rain attenuation | **L_a** (additional losses) | | The "scoop" at the receiving house | Large dish antenna | **Receive Antenna Gain, `G_r`** | | Background hiss heard by the receiver | Thermal noise from sky + electronics | **System Noise Temperature, `T_s`** | | Quality of the parcel at the end | Usable signal vs. background noise | **C/N and E_b/N_0** | > [!NOTE] > **The "basic link equation" is simply a power-balance statement:** *Power Injected into the Link − All Losses Encountered + All Antenna Gains = Power Successfully Captured at the Receiver.* The link designer balances this equation against the noise floor to guarantee a usable signal. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants (must be memorised) | Quantity | Symbol | Value (SI) | Value in dB units | |---|---|---|---| | Speed of light | `c` | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** | — | | Boltzmann constant | `k` | **1.3806 × 10⁻²³ J/K** | **−228.6 dBW/K/Hz** | | Wavelength–frequency relation | `λ = c/f` | — | — | | Standard reference temperature | `T_0` | **290 K** | **24.6 dBK** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Pillars of a Satellite Link A satellite communication link, whether **uplink** (Earth → Satellite) or **downlink** (Satellite → Earth), is characterised by five sequential engineering operations: 1. **Signal Generation & Amplification** — Information is modulated onto an RF carrier, then boosted by a High-Power Amplifier (HPA) to a level `P_t`. 2. **Directive Radiation** — The transmit antenna concentrates the power in a narrow beam with gain `G_t`, producing an Effective Isotropic Radiated Power of `EIRP = P_t · G_t`. 3. **Free-Space Propagation** — The spherical wavefront expands, diluting power density by the square of distance. This produces the Free-Space Path Loss `L_{fs}`. 4. **Directive Capture** — The receive antenna with effective aperture `A_e` (related to gain `G_r`) intercepts a small fraction of the radiated power. 5. **Reception in Noise** — The weak signal is corrupted by thermal noise from the antenna sky `T_a`, feeder losses, and the Low-Noise Amplifier (LNA) `T_e`. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet — Print This Page! | # | Quantity | Equation (Linear) | Equation (dB form) | Notes / Boundary Condition | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | EIRP | `EIRP = P_t · G_t` | `[EIRP]_{dBW} = [P_t]_{dBW} + [G_t]_{dBi}` | `G_t` is in dBi (isotropic) | | 2 | Free-Space Path Loss | `L_{fs} = (4πd/λ)²` | `[L_{fs}]_{dB} = 32.45 + 20·log₁₀(d_{km}) + 20·log₁₀(f_{MHz})` | Valid only in clear LOS | | 3 | Free-Space Loss (frequency variant) | `L_{fs} = (4π·d·f/c)²` | `[L_{fs}]_{dB} = 92.45 + 20·log₁₀(d_{km}) + 20·log₁₀(f_{GHz})` | Equivalent to row 2 | | 4 | Received Carrier Power | `C = P_t·G_t·G_r / L_{fs}·L_a` | `[C]_{dBW} = [EIRP] + [G_r] - [L_{fs}] - [L_a]` | `L_a` = misc. losses | | 5 | Antenna Gain from Aperture | `G = 4π·A_e / λ²` | `[G]_{dB} = 10·log₁₀(4π·A_e/λ²)` | `A_e` in m² | | 6 | Antenna Noise Temp | `T_a` (K) | dBK | From sky/ground pickup | | 7 | Effective Input Noise Temp (feeder + LNA) | `T_e = T_{e,feed}(L−1) + T_{e,LNA}·L` | — | `L` = feeder ohmic loss ratio | | 8 | System Noise Temperature | `T_s = T_a + T_e` | `[T_s]_{dBK} = 10·log₁₀(T_s)` | Referenced at LNA input | | 9 | Thermal Noise Power | `N = k·T_s·B` | `[N]_{dBW} = −228.6 + [T_s]_{dBK} + 10·log₁₀(B_{Hz})` | — | | 10 | Carrier-to-Noise Ratio | `C/N = EIRP·G_r / (k·T_s·B·L_{fs}·L_a)` | `[C/N]_{dB} = [EIRP] + [G_r] − [k] − [T_s] − [B] − [L_{fs}] − [L_a]` | Core link budget | | 11 | Figure of Merit | `G/T` (1/K) | `[G/T]_{dB/K} = [G_r]_{dBi} − [T_s]_{dBK}` | Earth-station quality index | | 12 | E_b/N_0 (digital link) | `E_b/N_0 = C/N · (B/R_b)` | `[E_b/N_0]_{dB} = [C/N]_{dB} + 10·log₁₀(B/R_b)` | Compare with modulation threshold | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Anti-Trap Rule for Examiners:** In dB arithmetic, *losses are SUBTRACTED and gains are ADDED*. A common student mistake is to add FSPL instead of subtracting it. KTU examiners specifically look for this sign error. ## 2.3 Why These Formulas Matter in Real Engineering - **Satellite Operators (ISRO, Inmarsat, Starlink)** use the **G/T figure of merit** to grade the quality of their Earth stations. A 1 dB/K improvement can reduce dish size by ~20 %. - **Link-budget spreadsheets** in industry are built directly on the C/N equation. The designer performs an *iterative trade-off*: increase HPA power, increase antenna diameter, reduce LNA noise figure, or pick a higher elevation angle — all of which feed back into the same master equation. - **Spectrum regulators** (ITU-R, WPC-India) dictate minimum carrier EIRP and maximum off-axis EIRP density to prevent interference between adjacent satellites — making EIRP both a design and a regulatory quantity. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Free-Space Path Loss (Friis Transmission Equation) ### 3.1.1 Conceptual Setup A transmit antenna radiates isotropically with power `P_t` watts. The power density at distance `d` is `P_t / (4πd²)` W/m² because the energy is distributed over a sphere of area `4πd²`. A receive antenna of effective aperture `A_e` captures `A_e · (P_t / 4πd²)` watts. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Power density at distance } d \quad &\Rightarrow \quad S = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t}{4\pi d^{2}} \quad \text{W/m}^{2} \\ \text{Power captured by receive antenna} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot A_e}{4\pi d^{2}} \\ \text{Substitute } A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^{2}}{4\pi} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi)^{2} d^{2}} \\ \text{Rearranging, define } L_{fs} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r}{L_{fs}} \\ \text{Substitute } \lambda = c/f \quad &\Rightarrow \quad L_{fs} = \left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)^{2} \\ \text{Convert to dB} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \left[L_{fs}\right]_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right) \\ \text{For } d \text{ in km, } f \text{ in MHz} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \left[L_{fs}\right]_{dB} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}\!\left(d_{km}\right) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(f_{MHz}\right) \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.1.3 Numerical Sanity Check (GEO link at 6 GHz) - `d = 36 000 km`, `f = 6 000 MHz` - `20 log₁₀(36 000) = 91.12 dB` - `20 log₁₀(6 000) = 75.56 dB` - **`L_{fs} ≈ 91.12 + 75.56 + 32.45 ≈ 199.13 dB`** - This is the *signature number* for a Ku-band GEO link. If your calculation deviates by more than 2 dB, re-check the unit conversion. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Carrier-to-Noise Ratio in the Uplink ### 3.2.1 Start from Friis $$ \begin{aligned} C = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r}{L_{fs} \cdot L_a} \\ N = k \cdot T_s \cdot B \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2.2 Form the Ratio $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{C}{N} = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r}{L_{fs} \cdot L_a \cdot k \cdot T_s \cdot B} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2.3 Convert Term-by-Term to dB $$ \begin{aligned} \left[\frac{C}{N}\right]_{dB} = \underbrace{[P_t]_{dBW}}_{\text{TX power}} + \underbrace{[G_t]_{dBi}}_{\text{TX ant. gain}} + \underbrace{[G_r]_{dBi}}_{\text{RX ant. gain}} \\ - \underbrace{[L_{fs}]_{dB}}_{\text{path loss}} - \underbrace{[L_a]_{dB}}_{\text{atm./rain/misalign.}} - \underbrace{[k]_{dBW/K}}_{\text{-228.6}} \\ - \underbrace{[T_s]_{dBK}}_{\text{noise temp}} - \underbrace{[B]_{dBHz}}_{\text{bandwidth}} \end{aligned} $$ This is the **canonical uplink C/N equation** memorised by every KTU student. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effective Input Noise Temperature of Feeder + LNA Cascade The receive chain has a *lossy feeder* (attenuation factor `L > 1`, i.e., `L_{dB} > 0`) followed by a **Low-Noise Amplifier** with equivalent noise temperature `T_{LNA}`. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Input noise at LNA input} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad N_{in} = k (T_a + T_{feed}) B \\ \text{Where feeder noise } T_{feed} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad T_{feed} = (L - 1) \cdot T_0 \\ \text{Signal at LNA input} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad S_{in} = \frac{C}{L} \\ \text{Signal at LNA output (gain G)} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad S_{out} = G \cdot \frac{C}{L} \\ \text{Noise at LNA output} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad N_{out} = G \cdot k (T_a + T_{feed}) B + G \cdot k \cdot T_{LNA} \cdot B \\ \text{Refer all noise to LNA input} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad N_{eq} = \frac{N_{out}}{G} = k B \!\left[T_a + (L-1)T_0 + T_{LNA}\right] \\ \therefore \; \text{Total system noise temperature} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\,T_s = T_a + (L-1)T_0 + T_{LNA}\,} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The factor `(L − 1)T_0` is why a high-G/T Earth station places the **LNA *directly at the feed-horn***, eliminating the long lossy waveguide. Even 0.5 dB of feeder loss adds `(10^{0.05} − 1)·290 ≈ 41 K` of noise — devastating to a 50 K system. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — E_b/N_0 from C/N For a digital link, the **bit energy** is `E_b = C / R_b`, where `R_b` is the bit rate. The **noise spectral density** is `N_0 = N / B = k T_s`. $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{E_b}{N_0} &= \frac{C/R_b}{N/B} = \frac{C}{N} \cdot \frac{B}{R_b} \\ \therefore \quad \left[\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right]_{dB} &= \left[\frac{C}{N}\right]_{dB} + 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{B}{R_b}\right) \end{aligned} $$ If the system uses a raised-cosine filter with roll-off `α`, then `B = R_b(1+α)/2` for NRZ signalling. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Uplink vs Downlink Trade-off | Parameter | Uplink (Earth → Sat) | Downlink (Sat → Earth) | Design Implication | |---|---|---|---| | Transmit power source | Ground HPA (kW-class) | Satellite TWTA (tens of W) | **Downlink is power-starved** | | Antenna gain | Large ground dish (high G_t) | Small onboard antenna (low G_t) | Compensates downlink partly | | Receiver noise temp | Satellite LNA (~500 K) | Earth station LNA (~50 K) | Uplink C/N is noise-limited by sat | | Path loss symmetry | Identical (same d, same f if same band) | Identical | — | | Typical bottleneck | EIRP of ground HPA | EIRP of satellite TWTA | **Downlink sets system capacity** | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Basic Satellite Link Architecture (Uplink + Downlink) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Information Source
Voice, Data, Video] --> B[Modulator
BPSK / QPSK] B --> C[Upconverter
to RF carrier] C --> D[High Power Amplifier
HPA - Ground] D --> E[Earth Station Antenna
High Gt, large dish] E -->|Free Space Link
uplink Lfs, La| F[Satellite Antenna
Low Gt, small dish] F --> G[Satellite LNA
Receiver Te1] G --> H[Transponder
Downconverter] H --> I[Satellite TWTA
HPA - Onboard] I --> J[Satellite Antenna
Low Gt, small dish] J -->|Free Space Link
downlink Lfs, La| K[Earth Station Antenna
High Gr, large dish] K --> L[Low Noise Amplifier
LNA, Te2] L --> M[Downconverter
to IF] M --> N[Demodulator] N --> O[Information Sink] subgraph noise1 [Noise Sources Uplink] N1[Sky Ta1] -.-> F N2[Receiver Te1] -.-> G end subgraph noise2 [Noise Sources Downlink] N3[Sky Ta2] -.-> K N4[Receiver Te2] -.-> L end ``` ## 4.2 Functional Power-Budget Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD P0[Pt dBW] --> P1[Add Gt to get EIRP] P1 --> P2[Subtract Lfs Free Space Loss] P2 --> P3[Subtract La Atmospheric Rain Misalignment] P3 --> P4[Add Gr Receive Antenna Gain] P4 --> P5[Result Carrier Power C dBW] P5 --> P6[Subtract kT noise floor] P6 --> P7[Subtract Bandwidth B] P7 --> P8[Result C over N in dB] P8 --> P9[Compare with threshold Eb over N0] P9 --> P10{Link Margin Positive?} P10 -- Yes --> P11[LINK CLOSES - Design Acceptable] P10 -- No --> P12[Increase EIRP, Larger Dish, Better LNA] style P0 fill:#fff7e6,stroke:#d48806 style P11 fill:#d9f7be,stroke:#389e0d style P12 fill:#ffccc7,stroke:#cf1322 style P10 fill:#bae7ff,stroke:#096dd9 ``` ## 4.3 Noise Temperature Cascade (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Antenna Sky Noise Ta ~ 50 K] --> D[Reference Plane LNA Input] B[Feeder Loss L factor Noise L-1 times T0] --> D C[LNA Equivalent Noise Te] --> D D --> E[Total System Noise Temperature Ts] E --> F[Figure of Merit G over T] F --> G[C over N Computation] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents an engineering decision. The yellow "Pt" block is the *knob* the designer turns; the green "LINK CLOSES" block is the *goal*; the red "Increase EIRP…" block is the *fallback* if the link does not close on the first iteration. KTU problems usually fix the goal (e.g., "C/N ≥ 18 dB for QPSK at 10⁻⁵ BER") and ask the student to size the antenna or HPA. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)] **Define Equivalent Isotropic Radiated Power (EIRP). Why is it preferred over transmitter power alone in satellite link design?** **Model Answer:** EIRP is defined as the product of the RF power delivered to the antenna and the transmit antenna gain referred to an isotropic radiator: $$ [EIRP]_{dBW} = [P_t]_{dBW} + [G_t]_{dBi} $$ **Why EIRP is preferred:** The far-field power density depends on *both* the transmitter power and the antenna's ability to focus that power. A 10 W transmitter into a 30 dBi dish delivers the same field strength as a 10 kW transmitter into an omnidirectional whip. EIRP captures the net radiated intensity in a single figure of merit, which is also the quantity regulated by the ITU to prevent inter-satellite interference. --- ### Q2.
