[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)] **State Friis free-space transmission equation and identify each term.** **Model Answer:** $$ P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi)^{2} d^{2}} $$ | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | `P_t` | Power delivered to transmit antenna (W) | | `G_t` | Transmit antenna gain (linear, dimensionless) | | `G_r` | Receive antenna gain (linear, dimensionless) | | `λ` | Wavelength of carrier (m) | | `d` | Slant range between antennas (m) | | `P_r` | Power available at receiver terminals (W) | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — Lossy Feeder:** A common 1-mark error is to write `P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r − L_{fs}` in *linear* units. Always convert each term to dB (or keep all in linear). Mixing units in one equation costs the full 3 marks. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Choice Questions (14 Marks) ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style] **(a)** A geostationary satellite operates at 6 GHz uplink and 4 GHz downlink. The slant range to the Earth station is 37 500 km. For the **uplink**, the Earth station HPA delivers 50 dBW into an antenna of 65 dBi gain. The satellite's receive antenna gain is 20 dBi, and the satellite's system noise temperature referred to the LNA input is 600 K. The transponder bandwidth is 36 MHz. Atmospheric and feeder losses total 2 dB. Compute: 1. The uplink free-space path loss in dB. **(3 marks)** 2. The received carrier power at the satellite. **(3 marks)** 3. The uplink C/N ratio in dB. **(4 marks)** **(b)** A downlink at 4 GHz carries the same signal with satellite EIRP of 40 dBW, Earth station antenna gain of 50 dBi, feeder/atmospheric losses of 1.5 dB, and Earth station system noise temperature of 120 K in a 30 MHz bandwidth. Compute: 1. The downlink C/N ratio. **(3 marks)** 2. The **overall C/N** when both links are cascaded (assuming no interference). **(1 mark)** #### Detailed Model Solution **Part (a)(1) — Uplink FSPL** $$ \begin{aligned} [L_{fs}]_{dB} &= 92.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{GHz}) \\ d_{km} &= 37\,500 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 20\log_{10}(37\,500) = 91.48 \text{ dB} \\ f_{GHz} &= 6 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 20\log_{10}(6) = 15.56 \text{ dB} \\ [L_{fs}]_{dB} &= 92.45 + 91.48 + 15.56 = 199.49 \text{ dB} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (a)(2) — Received Carrier Power** $$ \begin{aligned} [C]_{dBW} &= [P_t]_{dBW} + [G_t]_{dBi} + [G_r]_{dBi} - [L_{fs}]_{dB} - [L_a]_{dB} \\ &= 50 + 65 + 20 - 199.49 - 2 = -66.49 \text{ dBW} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (a)(3) — Uplink C/N** $$ \begin{aligned} [T_s]_{dBK} &= 10\log_{10}(600) = 27.78 \text{ dBK} \\ [N]_{dBW} &= -228.6 + 27.78 + 10\log_{10}(36\times10^{6}) \\ 10\log_{10}(36\times10^{6}) &= 75.56 \text{ dBHz} \\ [N]_{dBW} &= -228.6 + 27.78 + 75.56 = -125.26 \text{ dBW} \\ \left[\frac{C}{N}\right]_{dB} &= [C]_{dBW} - [N]_{dBW} = -66.49 - (-125.26) = 58.77 \text{ dB} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b)(1) — Downlink C/N** $$ \begin{aligned} [L_{fs,down}]_{dB} &= 92.45 + 91.48 + 20\log_{10}(4) \\ 20\log_{10}(4) &= 12.04 \text{ dB} \\ [L_{fs,down}]_{dB} &= 92.45 + 91.48 + 12.04 = 195.97 \text{ dB} \\ [C]_{dBW} &= [EIRP]_{dBW} + [G_r]_{dBi} - [L_{fs}]_{dB} - [L_a]_{dB} \\ &= 40 + 50 - 195.97 - 1.5 = -107.47 \text{ dBW} \\ [T_s]_{dBK} &= 10\log_{10}(120) = 20.79 \text{ dBK} \\ [N]_{dBW} &= -228.6 + 20.79 + 10\log_{10}(30\times10^{6}) \\ 10\log_{10}(30\times10^{6}) &= 74.77 \text{ dBHz} \\ [N]_{dBW} &= -228.6 + 20.79 + 74.77 = -133.04 \text{ dBW} \\ \left[\frac{C}{N}\right]_{down,dB} &= -107.47 - (-133.04) = 25.57 \text{ dB} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b)(2) — Overall C/N in cascade** The combined C/N obeys: $$ \begin{aligned} \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{total}^{-1} &= \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{up}^{-1} + \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{down}^{-1} \\ \text{Convert to linear:} \quad (C/N)_{up} &= 10^{58.77/10} = 7.53 \times 10^{5} \\ (C/N)_{down} &= 10^{25.57/10} = 3.61 \times 10^{2} \\ \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{total}^{-1} &= 1.328 \times 10^{-6} + 2.77 \times 10^{-3} \approx 2.77 \times 10^{-3} \\ \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{total} &= 360.8 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 25.57 \text{ dB} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** The **downlink dominates** the cascade — the high C/N uplink (58.77 dB) contributes only 0.002 dB to the overall loss. The system C/N is essentially the downlink C/N (≈ 25.57 dB). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style] (Internal Choice) **(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, derive the expression for the **figure of merit (G/T)** of an Earth station receiver. Explain why the LNA must be placed close to the antenna feed. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 4 GHz receiver has an antenna noise temperature of 35 K. The LNA has a noise figure of 1.2 dB and is connected to the antenna through a feeder of 0.4 dB loss. Compute: 1. The equivalent noise temperature of the feeder alone. **(2 marks)** 2. The equivalent noise temperature of the LNA. **(2 marks)** 3. The total system noise temperature `T_s`. **(2 marks)** 4. If the receive antenna gain is 52 dBi, find the **G/T** in dB/K. **(1 mark)** #### Detailed Model Solution **Part (a) — Derivation of G/T** **Block diagram (verbal):** Antenna → Feeder (loss `L`) → LNA (gain `G`, noise `T_LNA`) → Downconverter → Demodulator. **Step 1 — Effective noise of feeder:** A passive lossy network at physical temperature `T_0` generates noise `k(L−1)T_0 B` and attenuates the input signal/noise by `L`. Its equivalent input noise temperature is `(L−1)·T_0`. **Step 2 — Effective noise of LNA:** Its self-noise at the input is `T_{LNA}`. **Step 3 — Total system noise (referred to LNA input):** $$ T_s = T_a + (L-1) T_0 + T_{LNA} $$ **Step 4 — Figure of Merit:** $$ \left[\frac{G}{T}\right]_{dB/K} = [G_r]_{dBi} - [T_s]_{dBK} $$ **Why LNA at the feed:** The term `(L−1)T_0` is added *before* the LNA amplifies. Every dB of feeder loss adds roughly `(10^{L_{dB}/10} − 1)·290 K`. Placing the LNA right at the feed eliminates this term, lowering `T_s` and raising G/T. **Part (b)(1) — Feeder Noise Temperature** $$ \begin{aligned} L &= 10^{0.4/10} = 1.0965 \\ T_{feed} &= (L - 1) \cdot 290 = 0.0965 \times 290 = 27.98 \text{ K} \approx 28 \text{ K} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b)(2) — LNA Equivalent Noise Temperature** $$ \begin{aligned} F_{dB} &= 1.2 \text{ dB} \quad\Rightarrow\quad F = 10^{1.2/10} = 1.318 \\ T_{LNA} &= (F - 1) \cdot 290 = 0.318 \times 290 = 92.22 \text{ K} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b)(3) — Total System Noise Temperature** $$ \begin{aligned} T_s &= T_a + T_{feed} + T_{LNA} \\ &= 35 + 28 + 92.22 = 155.22 \text{ K} \\ [T_s]_{dBK} &= 10\log_{10}(155.22) = 21.91 \text{ dBK} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b)(4) — Figure of Merit G/T** $$ \begin{aligned} \left[\frac{G}{T}\right]_{dB/K} &= [G_r]_{dBi} - [T_s]_{dBK} \\ &= 52 - 21.91 = 30.09 \text{ dB/K} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert noise figure from dB** to linear ratio *before* applying `T = (F−1)·290`. The 1.2 dB value is *not* a temperature. > 2. **Sign error in G/T:** G/T is *gain minus noise temperature* in dB. Some students subtract gain from noise, losing 1 mark. > 3. **Mixing Boltzmann constant forms:** Use `k = −228.6 dBW/K/Hz` only when bandwidth is in Hz. If bandwidth is given in MHz, convert first. > 4. **Ignoring antenna noise temperature** `T_a`. Many students compute `T_s = T_{LNA} + T_{feed}` and forget the sky noise contribution. > 5. **Cascading C/N in dB by simple averaging:** Always convert to linear, sum the *reciprocals*, then convert back. KTU explicitly tests this trap. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **EIRP = P_t + G_t** (dBW) — the "effective strength" of the transmitter. - ✅ **FSPL = 32.45 + 20·log(d_km) + 20·log(f_MHz)** dB — the dominant loss in any Earth–space link (~200 dB for GEO). - ✅ **Master link equation:** `C/N = EIRP + G_r − L_{fs} − L_a − k − T_s − B` (all in dB). - ✅ **G/T is the Earth-station quality index:** higher dish gain *and* lower noise temperature both help. - ✅ **System noise temperature** is the *sum* of antenna sky noise `T_a`, feeder contribution `(L−1)T_0`, and LNA noise `T_{LNA}`. - ✅ **LNA must be at the feed** — feeder losses before LNA add noise that LNA cannot remove. - ✅ **k = −228.6 dBW/K/Hz**, `T_0 = 290 K` (24.6 dBK), `c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s` — these three constants are *non-negotiable* in the KTU exam hall. - ✅ **Digital quality measure:** `E_b/N_0 = C/N · (B/R_b)`. Compare with modulation threshold (e.g., QPSK at 10⁻⁵ BER needs ~9.6 dB). - ✅ **Cascading rule:** `(C/N)_total⁻¹ = (C/N)_up⁻¹ + (C/N)_down⁻¹` — convert to linear, sum reciprocals, convert back. - ✅ **Downlink usually dominates** the overall link because the satellite's EIRP and the Earth station's G/T are both finite, while the uplink can be boosted with a high-power ground HPA. - ✅ **KTU trick:** If FSPL alone is asked, plug numbers into the **32.45 / 92.45** formula; if the question is in km–MHz, use the 32.45 form, else use 92.45 + 20·log(d_km) + 20·log(f_GHz).

topics.title: Interference Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Interference Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Interference Analysis** in a satellite communication system refers to the systematic quantitative evaluation of all unwanted co-frequency and overlapping-spectrum signals that co-exist in the operating bandwidth of the desired carrier, and the determination of the resulting degradation in link performance quantified by the **Carrier-to-Noise plus Interference Ratio (CNIR)**.

Mathematically, for a satellite link the operating figure-of-merit is:

$$\left(\frac{C}{N+I}\right) = \frac{1}{\left(\dfrac{1}{C/N}\right) + \left(\dfrac{1}{C/I}\right)}$$

where:
- $C/N$ = carrier-to-noise ratio (thermal)
- $C/I$ = carrier-to-interference ratio
- $C/(N+I)$ = combined ratio

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Tag:** Module 2 mandates the qualitative and quantitative treatment of *inter-system* (satellite vs. terrestrial) and *intra-system* (between satellite links) interference, including co-channel, adjacent-channel, intermodulation, and cross-polarization effects.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive View)

Imagine you are trying to have a private conversation with a friend in a busy railway station. Your friend's voice is the **Carrier (C)**. The general hum of crowd is the **Noise (N)**. But now imagine *two* other friends simultaneously trying to call you from different platforms using the same pitch — that is **Co-Channel Interference**. Someone shouting on a slightly different pitch next to you is **Adjacent-Channel Interference**. If two loud-speakers at the station accidentally couple (a faulty connection) they produce a *new* unwanted tone — that is **Intermodulation (IM) Distortion**. And if your friend stands on the diagonal platform and you mistakenly pick up the *horizontal* part of his voice while listening for the *vertical* part — that is **Cross-Polarization Leakage (XPD)**.

In all cases, the *intelligibility* of the conversation drops. **Interference analysis** is the engineering process of *measuring* how much it drops and *designing* the system to keep intelligibility above a threshold (typically a minimum $C/(N+I)$ for a required $E_b/N_0$ at the demodulator).

> [!NOTE]
> In satellite engineering, interference is **never zero**. The job of the link designer is to ensure that interference contributes only a small, *budgeted* penalty (typically **0.5 to 1.5 dB**) to the overall link margin, not catastrophic link failure.

## 1.3 Sources of Interference — Taxonomy

For KTU, interference in a satellite system is broadly classified into two families:

| Family | Meaning | Typical Sources |
|---|---|---|
| **Intra-System Interference** | Generated *within* the satellite network itself | Adjacent satellites, cross-polarized signals in the same beam, intermodulation in TWTA |
| **Inter-System Interference** | Generated *between* satellite and other radio services | Terrestrial microwave links, radar, other satellite systems (e.g., GEO vs. NGSO) |

Within these families, the *physical* mechanisms are:

1. **Co-Channel Interference (CCI)** — same frequency, same time, different spatial or polarizational source.
2. **Adjacent-Channel Interference (ACI)** — overlapping sidelobes from neighbouring channel filters.
3. **Intermodulation Distortion (IMD)** — generated inside non-linear HPAs / TWTAs.
4. **Cross-Polarization Interference (XPI)** — finite polarization isolation between orthogonal polarizations.
5. **Adjacent Satellite Interference (ASI)** — emission sidelobes of neighbouring satellites picked up by an off-axis earth-station antenna.

The standard KTU reference metric for **measuring interference** is the ratio of wanted carrier power to unwanted power, expressed in **dB**.

> [!NOTE]
> All interference ratios in this module are expressed in **dB** unless stated otherwise. The conversion rule is $\text{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(\text{ratio})$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Co-Channel Interference (CCI)

Co-channel interference arises when two carriers occupy the **same frequency band, the same time slot**, but originate from different spatial directions (e.g., a wanted satellite and an adjacent satellite whose main-lobe is just outside the wanted beam) or from cross-polarized channels re-used on the **same frequency** (frequency-reuse systems).

**Operational logic:**
- The interference power at the victim receiver is the sum of powers from all unwanted co-frequency sources.
- If the victim antenna has a gain $G_v$ and there are $M$ co-channel interferers, each producing spectral power density at the receiver input of $P_{I,k}$, then:

$$I_{\text{total}} = \sum_{k=1}^{M} I_k$$

- The carrier-to-co-channel-interference ratio for the dominant interferer is:

$$\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{\text{CCI}} = \frac{C}{I_{\text{dominant}} + \sum_{k \neq 1} I_k}$$

- For a single dominant interferer, the off-axis discrimination of the earth-station antenna is the key parameter.

> [!NOTE]
> The most effective KTU-level remedy for CCI is **spatial isolation** (narrower antenna beams, careful station siting) and **polarization reuse with high XPD**.

## 2.2 Adjacent-Channel Interference (ACI)

Adjacent-channel interference occurs due to **non-ideal filtering**: the side-lobes of an adjacent channel's spectrum spill into the wanted channel. The interference is characterised by the **Adjacent-Channel Interference Ratio (ACIR)**:

$$\text{ACIR (dB)} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{\text{ACLR}} + \dfrac{1}{\text{ACS}}$$

where:
- $\text{ACLR}$ = Adjacent Channel Leakage Ratio — measures how much of the interferer's power leaks into the victim.
- $\text{ACS}$ = Adjacent Channel Selectivity — measures the victim's ability to reject the interferer.

In satellite practice, ACI is mitigated by:
- Sharp roll-off **BPF** in the transponder.
- Channel spacing chosen ≥ filter **3-dB bandwidth × 1.4**.
- **Guard bands** between carriers.

## 2.3 Intermodulation Distortion (IMD)

When multiple carriers pass through a **non-linear device** (TWTA, SSPA, mixer), they produce spurious products at frequencies $m f_1 \pm n f_2 \pm \dots$ (order = $|m| + |n| + \dots$).

- **3rd-order IM products** ($2f_1 - f_2$ and $2f_2 - f_1$) are the most dangerous because they fall *closest* to the wanted carriers.
- IMD is quantified by the **Carrier-to-Intermodulation ratio (C/IM)** which depends on the **output back-off (OBO)** of the HPA.

A standard empirical relation used in KTU textbooks is:

$$\left(\frac{C}{IM}\right)_{\text{dB}} \approx A - B \cdot (\text{OBO}_{\text{dB}})$$

where typical constants are $A \approx 12$ to $20$ dB and $B \approx 1$ to $2$, varying with HPA type. **Increasing OBO reduces IM but also reduces DC-to-RF efficiency.**

## 2.4 Cross-Polarization Interference (XPI / XPD)

In a **frequency-reuse** system, the *same* band is used twice — once with RHCP (Right-Hand Circular Polarization) and once with LHCP (Left-Hand Circular Polarization). The finite **Cross-Polarization Discrimination (XPD)** of the antenna allows leakage:

$$\text{XPD (dB)} = 10 \log_{10} \frac{P_{\text{co-pol}}}{P_{\text{cross-pol}}}$$

- Typical XPD at boresight: **27 dB** (good design), **30+ dB** (excellent).
- XPD degrades off-axis due to antenna geometry and atmospheric depolarization (rain, ice).
- The interference due to finite XPD is:

$$\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{\text{XPI}} \approx \text{XPD (dB)}$$

## 2.5 Adjacent Satellite Interference (ASI)

The satellite is in a **geostationary orbit** with longitudinal spacing of typically **2°** (Ku-band) or **3°** (C-band) — see **Orbital Separation Standards**.

A victim earth station receiving from a desired satellite at elevation $\theta_d$ will also pick up sidelobe radiation from adjacent satellites separated by $\Delta\lambda$ degrees in longitude. The **off-axis gain** of the victim antenna relative to the interferer is given by the **ITU-R S.465** reference pattern:

$$G(\phi) = \begin{cases} G_{\max} & 0 \le \phi < 1^\circ \\ 32 - 25 \log_{10}\phi \;\text{dBi} & 1^\circ \le \phi < 48^\circ \\ -10 \;\text{dBi} & 48^\circ \le \phi \le 180^\circ \end{cases}$$

where $\phi$ is the off-axis angle in degrees. The resulting C/I from ASI is:

$$\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{\text{ASI, dB}} = G_{\max,\text{dBi}} - G(\phi)_{\text{dBi}} + \text{EIRP}_{\text{interferer}} - \text{EIRP}_{\text{wanted}}$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Combined $C/(N+I)$ | $\dfrac{C}{N+I} = \left[\left(\dfrac{N}{C}\right) + \left(\dfrac{I}{C}\right)\right]^{-1}$ | ratio; convert each term to dB carefully |
| 2 | $C/N$ in dB | $C/N_{\text{dB}} = C_{\text{dBW}} - N_{\text{dBW}}$ | $N = kTB$ (k = Boltzmann const) |
| 3 | Overall link $C/(N+I)$ (uplink + downlink) | $\left(\dfrac{C}{N+I}\right)_{\text{total}}^{-1} = \left(\dfrac{C}{N+I}\right)_u^{-1} + \left(\dfrac{C}{N+I}\right)_d^{-1}$ | inverse-add in linear; dB add with $-10\log_{10}$ |
| 4 | ACIR | $\text{ACIR}^{-1} = \text{ACLR}^{-1} + \text{ACS}^{-1}$ | linear ratio |
| 5 | C/IM (3rd order, empirical) | $C/IM_{\text{dB}} \approx 12 + (\text{OBO}_{\text{dB}})$ for TWTA | values vary with HPA technology |
| 6 | XPD / C/I from XPI | $C/I_{\text{XPI}} \approx \text{XPD}$ | at boresight, atmospheric effects excluded |
| 7 | ITU-R S.465 pattern | $G(\phi) = 32 - 25 \log_{10}\phi$ dBi for $1^\circ \le \phi < 48^\circ$ | $\phi$ in degrees |
| 8 | Boltzmann noise power | $N = kTB$ | $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** |
| 9 | Earth-station system noise temp | $T_s = T_{\text{LNA}} + T_{\text{ant}} \cdot \frac{1}{G_{\text{LNA}}} + \dots$ | K (Kelvin) |
| 10 | Interference margin penalty | $\text{Penalty} = 10 \log_{10}\left(1 + \dfrac{N}{I}\right)$ dB | how much $C/N$ degrades when interference is present |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEY KTU BOARD EXAM TRAP:** When computing the *overall* $C/(N+I)$ of a satellite link, students often **add the dB values of C/N and C/I**. This is **WRONG**. You must first convert each to a *linear* ratio, then add the *inverse* values:

$$\frac{1}{(C/(N+I))_{\text{lin}}} = \frac{1}{(C/N)_{\text{lin}}} + \frac{1}{(C/I)_{\text{lin}}}$$

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

Interference analysis is the **heart of satellite link budget design**. Real-world applications include:
1. **ITU Coordination** — every satellite filing must demonstrate non-interference to existing networks.
2. **Frequency-reuse planning** — determines cluster size $N$ in cellular/satellite beams.
3. **HPA linearization** — drives OBO/IBO trade-off.
4. **Antenna pattern shaping** — informs reflector and feed design.
5. **Atmospheric fading** — combined with XPD, determines rain-margin policy.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Combined $C/(N+I)$ from $C/N$ and $C/I$

We begin with the physical statement that **noise power $N$ and interference power $I$ are uncorrelated** and therefore add in **power** (linear) domain.

**Step 1 — Define the total unwanted power:**

$$P_{\text{unwanted}} = N + I$$

**Step 2 — Form the carrier-to-unwanted ratio:**

$$\left(\frac{C}{N+I}\right) = \frac{C}{N+I}$$

**Step 3 — Manipulate to express in terms of known ratios:**

$$\left(\frac{C}{N+I}\right) = \frac{C}{N+I} \cdot \frac{1/\,C}{1/\,C} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{N}{C} + \dfrac{I}{C}}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the two ratios:**

$$\frac{N}{C} = \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)^{-1}, \qquad \frac{I}{C} = \left(\frac{C}{I}\right)^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{\;\left(\frac{C}{N+I}\right) = \left[ \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)^{-1} + \left(\frac{C}{I}\right)^{-1} \right]^{-1}\;}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to dB (for KTU exam use):**

$$\left(\frac{C}{N+I}\right)_{\text{dB}} = -10 \log_{10}\left(10^{-\,C/N_{\text{dB}}/10} + 10^{-\,C/I_{\text{dB}}/10}\right)$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Combined C/(N+I)

**Problem (KTU-style):** A satellite link has an uplink $C/N = 20$ dB and a downlink $C/I = 14$ dB from a single co-channel interferer. Find the combined $C/(N+I)$ in dB.

**Step 1 — Convert dB → linear:**

$$C/N = 10^{20/10} = 100$$

$$C/I = 10^{14/10} = 25.12$$

**Step 2 — Convert to inverse ratios:**

$$(C/N)^{-1} = 0.01$$

$$(C/I)^{-1} = 0.03981$$

**Step 3 — Sum (linear):**

$$(C/(N+I))^{-1} = 0.01 + 0.03981 = 0.04981$$

**Step 4 — Invert:**

$$C/(N+I) = 1 / 0.04981 = 20.07$$

**Step 5 — Convert back to dB:**

$$C/(N+I)_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(20.07) \approx 13.03 \text{ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical insight:** Even though $C/N$ alone is 20 dB, the *interference* of 14 dB drags the combined ratio down to **13 dB** — a 7 dB loss. This is why interference cannot be ignored.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Overall Uplink + Downlink $C/(N+I)$ for a Bent-Pipe Transponder

A satellite link has *two* segments: uplink (earth → sat) and downlink (sat → earth). The satellite transponder's $C/(N+I)$ at its output depends on both segments.

**Step 1 — At the satellite transponder input (uplink):**

$$\left(\frac{C}{N+I}\right)_u$$

This is the ratio *of the wanted carrier to (uplink noise + uplink interference)* measured at the satellite input.

**Step 2 — The transponder amplifies the carrier along with all noise + interference.** The downlink then adds its own noise and interference. Since these two sets are independent, they combine at the earth-station receiver.

**Step 3 — Independent segments ⇒ inverse addition in linear:**

$$\left(\frac{C}{N+I}\right)_T^{-1} = \left(\frac{C}{N+I}\right)_u^{-1} + \left(\frac{C}{N+I}\right)_d^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Inverting:**

$$\left(\frac{C}{N+I}\right)_T = \left[\left(\frac{C}{N+I}\right)_u^{-1} + \left(\frac{C}{N+I}\right)_d^{-1}\right]^{-1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above equation is the **gateway to the full KTU link-budget calculation**. It is the only correct way to combine two independent segments.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Two-Segment Link

**Problem:** Uplink: $C/(N+I)_u = 23$ dB. Downlink: $C/(N+I)_d = 20$ dB. Find overall $C/(N+I)_T$.

**Step 1 — Linear conversion:**

$$x_u = 10^{23/10} = 199.53$$

$$x_d = 10^{20/10} = 100$$

**Step 2 — Inverse-sum:**

$$x_T^{-1} = (1/199.53) + (1/100) = 0.00501 + 0.01 = 0.01501$$

**Step 3 — Invert:**

$$x_T = 66.62$$

**Step 4 — dB:**

$$C/(N+I)_T = 10 \log_{10}(66.62) \approx 18.24 \text{ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the overall ratio is **dominated by the weaker segment** (the downlink at 20 dB), not the average. The uplink (23 dB) only nudges the result down to 18.24 dB.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Interference Margin Penalty (Excess Loss due to I)

**Step 1 — Without interference**, the link margin is governed by $C/N$.

**Step 2 — With interference**, the effective ratio drops to $C/(N+I)$. The **penalty** $M_I$ is the *extra dB of $C/N$ that would be required to match performance*:

$$M_I = \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{\text{required with I}} - \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{\text{required without I}}$$

**Step 3 — For a given $C/I$:**

$$M_I = 10 \log_{10}\left(1 + \frac{N}{I}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Special cases:**
- If $I \ll N$: $M_I \approx 0$ (interference negligible).
- If $I = N$: $M_I = 10 \log_{10}(2) = 3$ dB.
- If $I \gg N$: $M_I \approx C/I_{\text{dB}}$ (interference dominates).

> [!NOTE]
> KTU designers typically budget $M_I \le 0.5$ to $1.5$ dB, implying $C/I \ge 10$ to $15$ dB *above* the noise floor.

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Adjacent Satellite Interference (ASI) using ITU-R Pattern

**Step 1 — Identify off-axis angle $\phi$** between wanted satellite and interfering satellite as seen from the earth station. For two geostationary satellites at longitudes $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$, $\phi \approx \vert \lambda_2 - \lambda_1 \vert$ for narrow angles.

**Step 2 — Compute interferer antenna gain $G(\phi)$** in the direction of the victim earth station:

$$G(\phi)_{\text{dBi}} = 32 - 25 \log_{10}(\phi), \quad 1^\circ \le \phi < 48^\circ$$

**Step 3 — The interfering flux density at the earth station** is:

$$\text{EIRP}_{\text{interferer}} = P_{T,\text{int}} + G(\phi)$$

**Step 4 — Wanted flux density** uses the on-axis gain $G_{\max}$:

$$\text{EIRP}_{\text{wanted}} = P_{T,w} + G_{\max}$$

**Step 5 — The carrier-to-ASI ratio at victim input is:**

$$\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{\text{ASI}} = \text{EIRP}_{\text{wanted}} - \text{EIRP}_{\text{interferer}} \;(\text{dB})$$

$$= G_{\max} - G(\phi) + (P_{T,w} - P_{T,\text{int}})$$

**Step 6 — Numerical check** (worked): Suppose $G_{\max} = 50$ dBi, $\phi = 2.5^\circ$, equal transmit powers.

$$G(2.5) = 32 - 25 \log_{10}(2.5) = 32 - 25(0.3979) = 32 - 9.95 = 22.05 \text{ dBi}$$

$$C/I_{\text{ASI}} = 50 - 22.05 = 27.95 \text{ dB} \;(\text{≈ 28 dB, excellent isolation})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most-asked ASI numerical in KTU boards**. Memorise: at $2^\circ$ spacing, $G(\phi) = 32 - 25\log_{10}2 \approx 24.5$ dBi; at $3^\circ$, $G(\phi) \approx 20$ dBi.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Interference Injection Points in a Bent-Pipe Satellite Link

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Earth Station A\nWanted Carrier] -->|Uplink C| satA[Satellite Transponder\nNon-Linear HPA]
    satB[Other Satellite\nASI Interferer] -->|Off-axis leak| antA[Victim Earth Antenna]
    terr[Terrestrial Microwave\nInterferer] -->|Co-channel I| antA
    xpol[Cross-Pol Leakage\nfrom Reuse Channel] --> antA
    antA --> lna[Receiver LNA\n+ Down-converter]
    satA -->|Downlink C + N + IM| antA
    lna --> dem[Demodulator\n+ BER Estimator]
    dem --> out[Decoded Bits]
    satA -. IM products .-> lna
    satA -. Amplified N .-> lna
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Interference Classification & Combining

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start[Interference Sources] --> familyA[Intra-System]
    start --> familyB[Inter-System]
    familyA --> a1[Co-Channel from\nadjacent satellite]
    familyA --> a2[Cross-Polarization\nwithin same beam]
    familyA --> a3[Intermodulation\nin TWTA / SSPA]
    familyA --> a4[Adjacent Channel\nfrom neighbour transponder]
    familyB --> b1[Terrestrial Microwave\nlinks in same band]
    familyB --> b2[Other satellite networks\nGEO vs NGSO]
    familyB --> b3[Radar and\nradiolocation services]
    a1 --> combine[Combine via\ninverse-power sum]
    a2 --> combine
    a3 --> combine
    a4 --> combine
    b1 --> combine
    b2 --> combine
    b3 --> combine
    combine --> final[Total C / N+I\ncompared to threshold]
    final --> decision{Satisfies\nBER target?}
    decision -->|Yes| ok[Link Acceptable]
    decision -->|No| fix[Add Margin:\nOBO, Filter, Site, XPD]
```

## 4.3 Signal Flow — Where Each Interference Enters

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Space Segment
        satTx[Satellite HPA\nNon-Linear]
    end
    subgraph Ground Segment
        antRx[Earth Station Antenna\nFinite XPD + Sidelobes]
        lnaRx[Low-Noise Amplifier\nAdds Thermal N]
    end
    int1[ASI Sidelobe] -.-> antRx
    int2[Cross-Pol Leakage] -.-> antRx
    int3[Adjacent Channel] -.-> antRx
    int4[Terrestrial Co-Channel] -.-> antRx
    int5[3rd Order IM] -.-> satTx
    int5 -.-> antRx
    antRx --> lnaRx
    satTx -->|Wanted C + N| antRx
    lnaRx --> demodOut[Demodulated Signal\nwith all I included]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — How the System Deals with Interference

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1{Is C/I > 20 dB?} -->|Yes| regime1[Negligible regime\nPenalty < 0.04 dB]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is C/I > 10 dB?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| regime2[Acceptable\n0.5 dB penalty budgeted]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is C/I > 5 dB?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| regime3[Marginal\n1.0 to 3 dB penalty]
    Q3 -->|No| regime4[Unacceptable\nmust redesign]
    regime1 --> action1[Proceed with\nnominal link budget]
    regime2 --> action2[Add 0.5 dB margin]
    regime3 --> action3[Tighten filtering,\nincrease XPD, add guard band]
    regime4 --> action4[Re-architect:\nnew frequency plan\nor new antenna site]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Most common deduction:** Students add dB values of $C/N$ and $C/I$ directly. The correct method is *inverse-linear sum*, then convert to dB. **Penalty: up to 4 marks.**
> 2. **Second most common:** Forgetting that uplink and downlink are *independent segments* and must be combined with the same inverse-linear rule.
> 3. **Third:** Confusing *XPD* (a property of the antenna) with *C/I_XPI* (a link metric). At boresight, $C/I_{\text{XPI}} = \text{XPD}$ only if the two cross-pol carriers have equal power.
> 4. **ASI trap:** Using the wrong ITU-R reference pattern (S.580 vs S.465 vs S.1428). For KTU exams, **default to S.465**.
> 5. **Unit slip:** Writing $C/N+I$ instead of $C/(N+I)$. Always parenthesise.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define co-channel interference. How is it minimised in a satellite system?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Co-channel interference (CCI) is the unwanted signal power that occupies **the same frequency channel and the same time slot** as the desired carrier, but originates from a *different* source (e.g., another satellite, a cross-polarized reuse channel, or a terrestrial system).

**Minimisation techniques** (any two for 3 marks, all three for full credit):
1. **Spatial isolation** — narrow-beam earth-station and satellite antennas, careful siting outside the main-lobe of interferers.
2. **Polarization reuse with high XPD** — orthogonal LHCP/RHCP with antenna XPD $\ge 27$ dB.
3. **Frequency planning / guard bands** — ITU-coordinated separation between networks.
4. **Adaptive interference cancellation** at the receiver.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** What is intermodulation distortion? Why is the 3rd-order product most harmful in a satellite transponder?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Intermodulation distortion (IMD) is the generation of **spurious spectral products** at frequencies $m f_1 \pm n f_2 \pm \dots$ when multiple carriers pass through a **non-linear** device such as a TWTA or SSPA in the transponder.

The **3rd-order products** ($2f_1 - f_2$ and $2f_2 - f_1$) are most harmful because:
1. They lie **spectrally closest** to the parent carriers $f_1$ and $f_2$ — impossible to filter out.
2. They grow at a rate of **3 dB per 1 dB** of input power increase (rapid growth in high-power regime).
3. They directly fall **inside the transponder passband** and corrupt the wanted signals.

**Remedy:** operate the HPA in *linear* region with adequate **output back-off (OBO)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **KTU Pattern:** *Internal choice* — answer either Q(A) or Q(B).

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the combined carrier-to-noise-plus-interference ratio $C/(N+I)$ in terms of $C/N$ and $C/I$. Explain why the combination is done in linear domain, not dB. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A satellite link has the following measured values:
- Uplink: $C/N_u = 25$ dB, $C/I_u = 18$ dB (from a single co-channel interferer).
- Downlink: $C/N_d = 22$ dB, $C/I_d = 14$ dB (from a single cross-pol interferer).

Compute the overall end-to-end $C/(N+I)_T$ in dB. **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define noise and interference as uncorrelated random processes ⇒ powers add in the *linear* domain:

$$P_{\text{unwanted}} = N + I \quad \text{(linear)}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Form the inverse of the desired ratio:

$$\left(\frac{C}{N+I}\right)^{-1} = \frac{N+I}{C} = \frac{N}{C} + \frac{I}{C}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Express each term as the inverse of known ratios:

$$= \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)^{-1} + \left(\frac{C}{I}\right)^{-1}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Invert to obtain the standard form:

$$\left(\frac{C}{N+I}\right) = \left[\left(\frac{C}{N}\right)^{-1} + \left(\frac{C}{I}\right)^{-1}\right]^{-1}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Explain why **dB addition is wrong.** Logarithms convert multiplication to addition, but here we have *addition of inverse ratios*. The log of a sum is not the sum of logs. Hence the combination must be done in the **linear (power) domain** first, and only then converted to dB:

$$\left(\frac{C}{N+I}\right)_{\text{dB}} = -10 \log_{10}\left(10^{-C/N_{\text{dB}}/10} + 10^{-C/I_{\text{dB}}/10}\right)$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Uplink combined:**

$$(C/(N+I))_u^{-1} = 10^{-25/10} + 10^{-18/10} = 0.003162 + 0.015849 = 0.019011$$

$$(C/(N+I))_u = 52.60 \quad (17.21 \text{ dB})$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Downlink combined:**

$$(C/(N+I))_d^{-1} = 10^{-22/10} + 10^{-14/10} = 0.006310 + 0.039811 = 0.046121$$

$$(C/(N+I))_d = 21.68 \quad (13.36 \text{ dB})$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Total inverse-sum:**

$$(C/(N+I))_T^{-1} = 0.019011 + 0.046121 = 0.065132$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Invert:**

$$(C/(N+I))_T = 15.354$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Convert to dB:**

$$(C/(N+I))_T = 10 \log_{10}(15.354) = 11.86 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Interference penalty analysis:**

- $C/N$ alone (uplink only): 25 dB; $C/(N+I)$ uplink: 17.21 dB → **7.79 dB penalty** from interference.
- $C/N$ alone (downlink only): 22 dB; $C/(N+I)$ downlink: 13.36 dB → **8.64 dB penalty** from interference.
- **Final answer:** $C/(N+I)_T = 11.86$ dB. The overall link is **interference-limited on the downlink** (13.36 dB is the bottleneck segment).

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The interference on the downlink is severe; the designer should improve $C/I_d$ by **increasing cross-polarization discrimination** (better feed design) or **reducing the off-axis pickup** (better antenna siting).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, list the various types of interference encountered in a satellite communication system. Explain *adjacent satellite interference* (ASI) in detail. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** An earth station operates with a satellite at $\lambda_1 = 78°$ E longitude. An adjacent satellite at $\lambda_2 = 80°$ E is the interferer. Both have equal EIRP. The victim earth station antenna has $G_{\max} = 52$ dBi. Assuming the off-axis angle $\phi \approx 2°$ and using the **ITU-R S.465 reference pattern**, calculate the C/I ratio due to ASI. **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 (3 marks) — Block diagram + classification:** Refer to Section 4.1. List the four main types:

1. Co-channel interference (CCI)
2. Adjacent-channel interference (ACI)
3. Intermodulation distortion (IMD)
4. Cross-polarization interference (XPI)

Plus **Adjacent Satellite Interference (ASI)** which is the focus of this question.

**Step 2 (4 marks) — ASI detailed explanation:**

- In the geostationary arc, satellites are spaced by 2° (Ku) or 3° (C) to permit frequency reuse.
- A victim earth station, while tracking the wanted satellite, also receives **sidelobe radiation** from neighbouring satellites.
- The interference power is determined by the **off-axis gain** of the victim antenna in the direction of the interferer and the **on-axis gain** of the wanted satellite.
- For KTU, the **ITU-R S.465 reference radiation pattern** is used:

$$G(\phi)_{\text{dBi}} = 32 - 25 \log_{10}(\phi), \quad 1^\circ \le \phi < 48^\circ$$

- Mitigation: better antenna sidelobe performance, site shielding, careful orbital coordination, larger orbital separation.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given (1 mark):** $G_{\max} = 52$ dBi, $\phi = 2°$, equal EIRP.

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Compute off-axis gain using ITU-R S.465:**

$$G(2) = 32 - 25 \log_{10}(2) = 32 - 25 \times 0.3010 = 32 - 7.525 = 24.475 \text{ dBi}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Compute antenna discrimination:**

$$\Delta G = G_{\max} - G(\phi) = 52 - 24.475 = 27.525 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Apply EIRP equality:**

Since both satellites have **equal EIRP**, the only asymmetry is the antenna gain of the *victim* in the two directions:

$$\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{\text{ASI}} = \Delta G = 27.53 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Convert dB to ratio (optional):**

$$C/I = 10^{27.53/10} = 5.66 \times 10^{2} \approx 566$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Interpretation:**

A $C/I$ of **27.5 dB** is **excellent** — it adds only:

$$M_I = 10 \log_{10}\left(1 + \frac{1}{566}\right) \approx 0.0077 \text{ dB}$$

of penalty to the link. This is well within the typical 0.5 dB interference budget.

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Sanity check:** If the orbital spacing were reduced to $1.5°$, the discrimination would drop sharply:

$$G(1.5) = 32 - 25 \log_{10}(1.5) = 32 - 25 \times 0.1761 = 27.60 \text{ dBi}$$

$$\Delta G = 52 - 27.6 = 24.4 \text{ dB}$$

→ penalty becomes $10\log_{10}(1 + 1/275) \approx 0.016$ dB. Still fine, but trending downward.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Combined $C/(N+I)$** uses *inverse-linear* sum, never direct dB addition.
- [ ] **Combined uplink + downlink $C/(N+I)_T$** uses the **same** inverse-linear rule — segments are independent.
- [ ] **Interference penalty** $M_I = 10 \log_{10}(1 + N/I)$ dB quantifies how much $C/N$ must be increased to compensate.
- [ ] **Co-channel interference** is reduced by *spatial* and *polarization* isolation.
- [ ] **Adjacent-channel interference** is reduced by *filter sharpness* and *guard bands*; uses **ACIR** metric.
- [ ] **Intermodulation** is dominant in non-linear HPAs (TWTAs); controlled by **output back-off (OBO)**; 3rd-order products are the worst.
- [ ] **Cross-polarization interference** depends on **XPD** of the antenna; degraded off-axis and by rain/ice.
- [ ] **Adjacent satellite interference** is computed using the **ITU-R S.465** pattern: $G(\phi) = 32 - 25\log_{10}\phi$ dBi.
- [ ] Standard orbital spacing: **2°** (Ku), **3°** (C) — assumed unless stated otherwise.
- [ ] Boltzmann constant $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K — required for any noise-power calculation.
- [ ] **Interference-limited** link: $I \gg N$, the bottleneck is the *interferer*, not thermal noise.
- [ ] **Noise-limited** link: $N \gg I$, the bottleneck is *thermal noise* (e.g., satellite uplink, downlink to small VSAT).
- [ ] Always parenthesise $C/(N+I)$ — never write $C/N+I$.
- [ ] KTU numerical defaults: $f$ in GHz, $T$ in K, $G$ in dBi, $P$ in dBW, ratios in dB.
- [ ] For frequency-reuse systems, $C/I_{\text{XPI}} = \text{XPD}$ only if the *two* reused carriers have **equal power**.
- [ ] When asked for "**overall**" link performance in a bent-pipe, always combine *up + down* via inverse sum, even if the question gives them separately.
- [ ] Common KTU exam numbers to memorise: $G(2°) \approx 24.5$ dBi; $G(3°) \approx 20$ dBi (S.465 pattern, relative to boresight).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Terrestrial Interference
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Terrestrial Interference ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Terrestrial interference forms the bridge between satellite and terrestrial microwave systems. It is examined both conceptually (types, sources) and quantitatively (interference geometry, coordination contours, PFD limits). ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Terrestrial Interference (TI)** is the unwanted electromagnetic energy entering a satellite earth station receiver (or, conversely, a terrestrial microwave receiver) originating from *earth-based* radio transmitters operating in the same (or adjacent) frequency band. Because the satellite downlink band is shared with line-of-sight (LoS) terrestrial Fixed Service (FS) microwave links, an unprotected satellite earth station antenna pointed toward the satellite also intercepts signals radiated by terrestrial transmitters located anywhere within its main lobe and side-lobe envelope. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Definition (as per ITU-R & KTU textbook):** Terrestrial interference in a satellite communication system is the interference caused at the satellite earth station receiver due to the emissions from terrestrial (ground-based) microwave communication systems operating in overlapping or adjacent frequency bands. Reciprocally, satellite emissions can also interfere with terrestrial receivers — this is termed **reverse terrestrial interference**. **Key Standard Frequency Bands Affected:** | Band | Uplink (Earth → Space) | Downlink (Space → Earth) | Shared with Terrestrial? | |------|------------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------| | **C-Band** | 5.925 – 6.425 GHz | 3.700 – 4.200 GHz | **Yes (heavily)** | | **Ku-Band** | 14.0 – 14.5 GHz | 10.7 – 12.2 GHz | Limited (Region dependent) | | **Ka-Band** | 27.5 – 31.0 GHz | 17.7 – 21.2 GHz | Emerging sharing studies | The fundamental problem in C-Band is that **3.7–4.2 GHz** (satellite downlink) and **3.4–3.7 GHz** (terrestrial WiMAX in some regions) lie adjacent, and the *same band 3.7–4.2 GHz* is also used by terrestrial FS in certain ITU Region-1 and Region-3 allocations. This is the **primary C-band interference scenario**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy (the "Mosquito at a Concert"):** > Imagine you are trying to listen to a faint flute (the satellite signal arriving from 36,000 km away, very weak by the time it reaches your dish). Now imagine hundreds of people in the same room shouting into microphones (terrestrial microwave towers only a few km away, transmitting at very high power). Even though everyone is speaking roughly the same words (same frequency band), the shouting overwhelms the flute. Your ear (the satellite receiver) cannot distinguish the flute. This is terrestrial interference. > > The flute is *legitimately* there because it comes from a designated performer (the satellite) at the back of the hall (geostationary orbit). The shouting is *legally permitted* because those microphones (terrestrial links) have their own license. The problem is that **both share the same acoustic room (the radio spectrum)** without enough separation. ### 1.3 Important Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Speed of light, c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** - **Free-space path loss, FSPL = 92.45 + 20 log₁₀(d_km) + 20 log₁₀(f_GHz) dB** - **Boltzmann constant, k = 1.3806 × 10⁻²³ J/K** - **Effective Isotropic Radiated Power (EIRP)** — units **dBW** - **Power Flux Density (PFD)** — units **dBW/m²** - **Carrier-to-Interference ratio (C/I)** — units **dB**, must exceed a threshold (typically 20–30 dB for digital SATCOM). - **Required antenna discrimination** for line-of-sight to terrestrial transmitter, measured in **dB** relative to main-beam gain. ### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Terrestrial interference is one of the **most frequently tested concepts** in KTU ESE Module 2. Marks are typically awarded for: 1. Identifying the two classes of interference (in-band vs. out-of-band). 2. Drawing the geometry of the interference path. 3. Computing C/I and the resulting link degradation. 4. Discussing coordination procedures (ITU-R, FCC) and mitigation techniques. 5. Drawing the **interference coordination contour** around an earth station. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Two Manifestations of Terrestrial Interference Terrestrial interference is **bidirectional** in nature: **(a) Forward Terrestrial Interference (Most Common, KTU Favourite):** - A terrestrial microwave transmitter radiates energy that is picked up by the **satellite earth station** receiver (which is tuned to the satellite downlink band). - Cause: Inadequate angular discrimination of the earth-station antenna, or insufficient geographic separation between the earth station and terrestrial transmitter. - Effect: Raises the noise floor, degrades the satellite receiver's C/N and C/N+I ratio. **(b) Reverse Terrestrial Interference (Reciprocal):** - The **satellite** (or its uplink earth station) radiates energy that is picked up by a **terrestrial FS receiver**. - Cause: PFD of the satellite downlink at the Earth's surface exceeds the FS receiver's tolerance. - Effect: Terrestrial link quality drops; usually regulated by PFD limits (e.g., −152 dBW/m² in 4 kHz for 4 GHz band, as per ITU-R S.741). > [!NOTE] > In the KTU exam, the *forward* direction (terrestrial → earth station) is the default unless explicitly stated otherwise. ### 2.2 Sources / Classes of Terrestrial Interference Terrestrial interference is broadly classified based on the **frequency relationship** between the interfering and desired signals: 1. **In-Band (Co-channel) Interference:** The terrestrial transmitter operates in the *exact same frequency channel* as the satellite receiver. Hardest to reject; requires spatial/angular separation. 2. **Adjacent-Channel Interference:** Terrestrial transmitter operates in a band immediately next to the satellite band. Filter rejection is the primary tool. 3. **Out-of-Band Interference / Spurious Emissions:** Terrestrial transmitter's harmonics, intermodulation products, or out-of-band noise floor enters the satellite receiver. ### 2.3 Interference Mechanism — "Why" and "How" The coupling between a terrestrial transmitter and a satellite earth station receiver occurs through **three orthogonal mechanisms** (any one of which may dominate, depending on geometry): **Mechanism A — Main Lobe / Side Lobe Pickup:** The earth station antenna, while pointed at the geostationary satellite (typically at an elevation of 30°–50° for Indian latitudes), has side lobes that extend all the way down to the horizon. Any terrestrial transmitter within the **side-lobe envelope** of the earth station antenna radiates energy that the antenna accepts according to its gain pattern G(θ), where θ is the off-axis angle. The first side lobe of a typical parabolic dish is only ~13 dB down from the main beam, and the envelope falls off as roughly **32 − 25 log θ** (ITU-R S.465 envelope for θ in degrees). **Mechanism B — Atmospheric Scatter / Tropospheric Ducting:** Under anomalous propagation conditions (ducting, rain scatter, tropospheric scatter), the terrestrial signal can be refracted or scattered into the earth station's main beam. This becomes significant at frequencies above 10 GHz and over distances of 50–500 km. **Mechanism C — Conducted (Cable/Equipment) Interference:** Local oscillator leakage, insufficient image rejection, or insufficient filtering in the receiver front-end. Mitigated by engineering practice (high-Q filters, image-reject mixers). ### 2.4 Antenna Discrimination — The "Geometric" Defence The earth's rotation, satellite drift, and the broad side-lobe pattern mean that an earth station cannot fully *avoid* terrestrial interference geometrically. However, two geometric techniques help: - **Off-axis Discrimination:** Gain of the earth station antenna in the direction of the terrestrial transmitter (angle θ_TI from antenna boresight) versus the gain toward the satellite. $$\text{Discrimination} = G_{\text{max}} - G(\theta_{TI}) \;\; \text{dB}$$ - **Elevation Discrimination:** For the same transmitter, the off-axis angle is generally large (often 30°–90°) when the earth station antenna is pointed toward a high-elevation satellite. The discrimination grows with off-axis angle. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear almost every semester.** | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---------|---------|-------| | 1 | $C/N = EIRP_{sat} + G/T - FSPL - k - B$ | Standard satellite link CNR (k = Boltzmann) | dB | | 2 | $C/I = EIRP_{TI} + G_{ES}(\theta_{TI}) - FSPL_{TI} - (C_{sat} \text{ terms})$ | Carrier-to-Interference at earth station input | dB | | 3 | $(C/N+I)^{-1} = (C/N)^{-1} + (C/I)^{-1}$ | Total noise + interference (powers add) | linear | | 4 | $FSPL = 32.45 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ | Free-space path loss (d in km, f in MHz) | dB | | 5 | $FSPL = 92.45 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{GHz})$ | FSPL in dB (d in km, f in GHz) | dB | | 6 | $G(\theta) \le 32 - 25 \log \theta$ | ITU-R S.465 side-lobe envelope (1° ≤ θ ≤ 48°) | dBi | | 7 | $G(\theta) \le -10$ dBi (for θ > 48°) | Floor of side-lobe envelope | dBi | | 8 | $PFD = EIRP - FSPL$ (at Earth's surface) | Power flux density from satellite | dBW/m² | | 9 | $PFD_{limit} = -152 \text{ dBW/m}^2 \text{ per 4 kHz}$ | ITU-R limit for 3.7–4.2 GHz downlink | dBW/m²/4kHz | | 10 | $\Delta T_{ant} = P_{int} \cdot \dfrac{1}{k \cdot B}$ | Antenna noise temperature rise due to interference | K | | 11 | $A_{coord} = f(\theta_{sat}, \theta_{TI}, G_{ES}, G_{TI}, PFD_{limit})$ | Coordination contour radius | km | > [!WARNING] > **Do NOT confuse** $C/N$ with $C/N_0$. $C/N_0$ is the carrier-to-noise *spectral density* ratio (dB-Hz); $C/N$ is the carrier-to-noise *bandwidth* ratio (dB, dimensionless). Their relation is $C/N = C/N_0 - 10 \log_{10} B$ where $B$ is the noise bandwidth in Hz. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Terrestrial interference analysis is the **operational backbone** of: - **C-band VSAT hub siting** in India (where the 3.7–4.2 GHz band is shared with heavy terrestrial FS deployment in metro corridors like Mumbai, Delhi, Chennai). - **5G / IMT-2020 spectrum refarming debates** — the C-band auction in many countries has triggered interference studies between 5G base stations (3.4–3.8 GHz) and FSS earth stations (3.7–4.2 GHz). - **Earth-station licensing** by WPC (Wireless Planning & Coordination Wing) in India, which requires an interference study before granting siting permission. - **International coordination** at ITU-R (World Radiocommunication Conferences) where adjacent countries coordinate shared band usage. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Derivation 1 — Carrier-to-Interference Ratio (C/I) at the Earth Station Input** This is the most critical derivation in Module 2 and is essentially guaranteed to appear in the KTU 14-mark question. We derive the C/I delivered at the LNA input of a satellite earth station from a single co-channel terrestrial microwave transmitter. **Step 1 — Identify the interfering signal path:** Consider a terrestrial microwave transmitter with EIRP (in dBW) of $EIRP_{TI}$ located at a distance $d_{TI}$ km from the earth station. The transmitted power density at the earth station antenna aperture is reduced by the free-space path loss $FSPL_{TI}$ (in dB). **Step 2 — Power intercepted by earth station antenna:** The earth station antenna has a gain $G_{ES}(\theta_{TI})$ (in dBi) in the direction of the terrestrial transmitter, where $\theta_{TI}$ is the off-axis angle. The received interference power at the antenna output port is: $$P_{int} = EIRP_{TI} + G_{ES}(\theta_{TI}) - FSPL_{TI} \quad \text{(dBW)}$$ **Step 3 — Free-space path loss for the terrestrial path:** Substituting the standard FSPL formula at the satellite receiver frequency $f_R$ in GHz (since the interference enters the satellite receiver band): $$FSPL_{TI} = 92.45 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{TI}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_R) \quad \text{(dB)}$$ Therefore: $$\begin{aligned} P_{int} = EIRP_{TI} &+ G_{ES}(\theta_{TI}) \\ &- \left[92.45 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{TI}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_R)\right] \quad \text{(dBW)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Carrier power at the same antenna output port:** The desired carrier from the satellite has EIRP $EIRP_{sat}$ and travels a distance $d_{sat} \approx 35{,}786$ km (geostationary). The carrier is received with the antenna's maximum gain $G_{max}$ (since the satellite is on boresight). So: $$C = EIRP_{sat} + G_{max} - FSPL_{sat} \quad \text{(dBW)}$$ where $FSPL_{sat} = 92.45 + 20 \log_{10}(35{,}786) + 20 \log_{10}(f_R) \approx 195.5 + 20 \log_{10}(f_R)$ dB at 4 GHz (≈ 196.5 dB). **Step 5 — Carrier-to-Interference ratio:** Subtracting the interference expression from the carrier expression: $$\begin{aligned} C/I = &(EIRP_{sat} - EIRP_{TI}) \\ &+ (G_{max} - G_{ES}(\theta_{TI})) \\ &+ (FSPL_{TI} - FSPL_{sat}) \quad \text{(dB)} \end{aligned}$$ Since $d_{sat} \gg d_{TI}$, the term $FSPL_{sat}$ is much larger (more negative) than $FSPL_{TI}$, making the third bracket **strongly negative** (i.e., interference is favoured). The defence is therefore entirely in the second bracket — the antenna's *off-axis discrimination*. **Step 6 — Numerical sanity check (typical C-band scenario):** Let $EIRP_{sat} = 35$ dBW, $EIRP_{TI} = 50$ dBW (terrestrial typically higher), $G_{max} = 50$ dBi, $G_{ES}(\theta_{TI}) = 20$ dBi (broadside side-lobe), $d_{sat} = 36{,}000$ km, $d_{TI} = 30$ km, $f_R = 4$ GHz. $$\begin{aligned} FSPL_{sat} &= 92.45 + 20 \log_{10}(36000) + 20 \log_{10}(4) \\ &= 92.45 + 91.10 + 12.04 = 195.59 \text{ dB} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} FSPL_{TI} &= 92.45 + 20 \log_{10}(30) + 20 \log_{10}(4) \\ &= 92.45 + 29.54 + 12.04 = 134.03 \text{ dB} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} C/I &= (35 - 50) + (50 - 20) + (134.03 - 195.59) \\ &= -15 + 30 - 61.56 = -46.56 \text{ dB} \end{aligned}$$ > [!WARNING] > A **negative C/I** in dB means the interference is *stronger* than the carrier. This is the typical C-band scenario with no mitigation. The required C/I for a QPSK link at BER 10⁻⁶ is roughly **17 dB**, so we need **~63 dB of additional discrimination or filtering** to make the link work. This is achieved through: (a) high-gain, low-side-lobe antennas, (b) bandpass filtering, (c) geographic coordination, (d) site shielding. **Derivation 2 — Total C/(N+I):** The thermal noise C/N (in linear, not dB) is: $$(C/N)_{linear} = 10^{(C/N)_{dB}/10}$$ Similarly, $C/I$ in linear. The total is the sum of noise and interference powers (in linear): $$\frac{1}{(C/N+I)_{linear}} = \frac{1}{(C/N)_{linear}} + \frac{1}{(C/I)_{linear}}$$ In dB: $$(C/N+I)_{dB} = -10 \log_{10}\left[10^{-(C/N)_{dB}/10} + 10^{-(C/I)_{dB}/10}\right]$$ The smaller of the two (C/N or C/I) dominates. As a rule of thumb: **if C/I is more than ~10 dB lower than C/N, the link is interference-limited (not noise-limited).** **Derivation 3 — Equivalent Antenna Temperature Rise:** A useful physical interpretation: interference is just "extra noise." We can convert interference power $P_{int}$ to an equivalent antenna temperature rise: $$\Delta T_{ant} = \frac{P_{int}}{k \cdot B} \;\; \text{(Kelvin)}$$ where $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K and $B$ is the receiver noise bandwidth in Hz. The system noise temperature then becomes $T_{sys} = T_{ant} + T_{LNA} + \Delta T_{ant}$. This view lets you reuse the standard $C/N = EIRP \cdot G/(T \cdot k \cdot B \cdot L)$ link-budget framework with the inflated $T_{sys}$. **Analytical Matrix — Dominant Mechanism vs. Geometry:** | Terrestrial Tx distance | Off-axis angle θ_TI | Dominant mechanism | Typical C/I without mitigation | |------------------------|---------------------|---------------------|--------------------------------| | < 5 km | Near 90° (horizon) | Main side-lobe pickup | -20 to -10 dB | | 5 – 50 km | 5° – 45° | Side-lobe pickup | -10 to +10 dB | | 50 – 200 km | 1° – 5° | Near main-lobe side-lobe | +10 to +30 dB | | > 200 km | < 1° | Main-lobe edge / ducting | Variable, ducting dominant | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > **Diagram 1 — Forward Terrestrial Interference Geometry (Block-Level Functional Architecture)** > This shows the *interference coupling paths* from a terrestrial microwave transmitter into a satellite earth station receiver, the regulatory and engineering controls along each path, and the link-budget blocks. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[("Terrestrial Microwave Tx
EIRP_TI 50 dBW")] nodeB["Free Space Path
FSPL_TI 134 dB at 30 km"] nodeC["Antenna Discrimination
G_ES theta_TI
Side Lobe Pickup"] nodeD["Receiver Front End
LNA + BPF"] nodeE["Interference Power
P_int at LNA input"] nodeF["C/I Calculation
in dB"] nodeG[("Satellite GS
EIRP_sat 35 dBW")] nodeH["Geostationary Path
FSPL_sat 196 dB at 36000 km"] nodeI["Main Lobe Gain
G_max 50 dBi"] nodeJ["Carrier Power C
at LNA input"] nodeK["C/N+I

Shielding, Filter, Coordination"] nodeO["ITU-R Coordination
PFD Limits, Contour"] nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE --> nodeF nodeG --> nodeH --> nodeI --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeJ --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeK nodeK --> nodeL nodeL -->|Yes| nodeM nodeL -->|No| nodeN nodeO -.regulates.-> nodeA nodeO -.constrains.-> nodeC ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram 2 — Earth Station Coordination Contour (Plan View)** > The coordination contour defines the geographic boundary around an earth station *inside which* a new terrestrial transmitter cannot be licensed without detailed coordination. The contour radius is a function of azimuth — it is largest in the direction of the satellite (because the antenna is most sensitive there) and smallest at the *anti-satellite* azimuths (where the antenna is most discriminating). ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[("Earth Station
