- **BER with ISI:** $P_e \approx Q\left(\frac{V_{\text{eye}}/2}{\sigma_n}\right)$ where $V_{\text{eye}}$ is the vertical eye opening.

#### Rain Attenuation Quick Recap
- **Dominant Mechanism:** Absorption (below 100 GHz) and scattering (above 100 GHz).
- **Specific Attenuation:** $\gamma_R = a R^{b}$ dB/km; $R$ is in mm/hr; $a, b$ from ITU-R P.838.
- **Slant Path Length ($\theta \geq 5°$):** $L = (h_R - h_0)/\sin \theta$.
- **Reduction Factor:** $r_0 = 1 / [1 + 0.78 \sqrt{L\gamma_R/f^2} - 0.38(1 - e^{-2L})]$; cap at 1.0.
- **Effective Path Length:** $L_{\text{eff}} = r_0 \cdot L$.
- **Total Attenuation:** $A = \gamma_R \cdot L_{\text{eff}}$ dB.
- **Rain Height:** $h_R = h_{0,\text{iso}} + 0.36$ km (0°C isotherm + correction).
- **Cross-Pol Effect:** Rain-induced XPD degradation; $C$ = coupling factor (frequency- and elevation-dependent).
- **Fade Margin Required:** $M = A_{0.01} + \Delta \text{SNR}$ where $\Delta\text{SNR}$ is the SNR shortfall of the chosen modulation/coding.
- **Frequency Bands Affected:** Significant above 10 GHz; C-band (4/6 GHz) is largely rain-safe; Ku-band (12/14 GHz) is moderately affected; Ka-band (20/30 GHz) is severely affected.
- **Countermeasures:** Uplink Power Control (UPC), Adaptive Coding & Modulation (ACM), site diversity, larger antenna aperture, higher link margin.

#### Joint Memory Hook
- **ISI = Time-domain problem** → solved with filters and equalizers.
- **Rain Attenuation = Frequency/path-domain problem** → solved with fade margin and adaptive techniques.
- **Both are impairments in the link budget** and must be jointly accounted for during satellite system design.
- **Kerala-specific fact:** Kerala receives very heavy monsoon rainfall ($R_{0.01}$ up to 100 mm/hr in northern districts), making Ka-band link design particularly challenging without ACM and UPC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When the KTU paper asks a 14-mark question on rain attenuation, **always show all five steps**: (1) $L$ calculation, (2) $\gamma_R$ calculation with explicit power evaluation, (3) $r_0$ calculation with the exponential term shown, (4) capping $r_0 \leq 1$, and (5) final $A = \gamma_R L_{\text{eff}}$. Skipping any step costs at least 2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Uplink Power Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Uplink Power Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Uplink Power Control (UPC)** is the dynamic adjustment of the Effective Isotropic Radiated Power (EIRP) transmitted by an Earth Station toward a satellite, in order to maintain a constant (or optimal) flux density at the satellite's transponder input, compensating for variations in propagation conditions, traffic load, and link geometry.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, UPC is examined as a **countermeasure against uplink signal-level fluctuations** that threaten the linearity of the satellite's High Power Amplifier (HPA), typically a Travelling Wave Tube Amplifier (TWTA) or Solid State Power Amplifier (SSPA).

**Why is this necessary?** A satellite transponder is shared by many carriers. If an earth station suddenly increases its uplink power, the transponder is driven toward saturation, producing **intermodulation products** that raise the noise floor for ALL users. Conversely, too little power lowers the C/N ratio, reducing link availability.

### 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "Apartment Water Tank"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: Shared Water Tank on a Building Roof**
> - The **satellite transponder** = the overhead water tank (shared resource).
> - **Each earth station** = a tap feeding water into a common pipe.
> - **Uplink signal** = the water flow.
> - If one resident turns their tap too hard (high EIRP), the tank overflows and water pressure fluctuates wildly for everyone (TWTA saturation → intermodulation).
> - If a storm (rain attenuation) blocks the tap, less water enters (signal fade).
> - **Uplink Power Control** is like a smart valve that automatically compensates: it opens wider when rain blocks the signal, and closes partially when the path is clear and other stations are transmitting strongly.
> - The goal: keep the water (signal level) at the tank (satellite) **just below the overflow line (saturation point)** — leaving a small safety margin called **Input Back-Off (IBO)**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of light | $c$ | $3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| Boltzmann constant | $k$ | $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K |
| Reference path length | $d$ | km (slant range) |
| Free-space path loss | $L_p$ | dB |
| **Saturation Flux Density (SFD)** | $\psi_s$ | dBW/m² (typically $-90$ to $-70$ dBW/m² at 4/6 GHz) |
| Input Back-Off | $IBO$ | dB |
| Output Back-Off | $OBO$ | dB |
| Operating flux density | $\psi_{op}$ | dBW/m² |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Need — A Logical Breakdown

- **Step 1 — Transponder is a shared resource:** A single satellite carries many transponders; each transponder may be shared by hundreds of FDMA carriers or bursts of TDMA traffic.
- **Step 2 — TWTA is nonlinear near saturation:** A satellite TWTA has a saturation point $(P_{sat})$. Operating beyond it compresses the signal and generates third-order intermodulation products: $f_{IM3} = 2f_1 - f_2$ etc.
- **Step 3 — Atmospheric attenuation varies:** Rain, clouds, and gaseous absorption cause uplink attenuation $A_u$ that varies in time (seconds to minutes). At Ku-band, a 5-minute rain event can introduce 6–12 dB of extra attenuation.
- **Step 4 — Interference from adjacent satellites:** Mispointed uplink antennas spill energy into neighboring satellites (a regulatory cap is enforced).
- **Step 5 — The Solution:** Earth stations must continuously adjust their transmit EIRP to hold the flux density at the satellite receiver **constant** at an "operating point" chosen by the satellite operator.

### 2.2 Operating Point Definition

The flux density arriving at the satellite in clear-sky conditions is:

$$\psi_{clear} = EIRP_{ES} - L_p - A_{atm}$$

The operator specifies a desired **Operating Flux Density** $\psi_{op}$ that is a few dB below the **Saturation Flux Density** $\psi_s$:

$$\psi_{op} = \psi_s - IBO$$

The earth station must therefore set:

$$EIRP_{ES} = \psi_{op} + L_p + A_{atm}$$

When fading occurs ($A_{atm}$ increases), $EIRP_{ES}$ must be increased by exactly the same amount to keep $\psi_{op}$ constant. The maximum compensation the station can provide is called the **Uplink Power Control Range** (typically 6–15 dB).

### 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below summarizes every equation you are expected to know for KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Saturation Flux Density** | $\psi_s = \dfrac{P_{sat}}{4\pi d^2 \cdot G_R^{-1}} = EIRP_{ES} - L_p$ | dBW/m² |
| 2 | Path Loss (Free Space) | $L_p = 32.4 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$ | dB; $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz |
| 3 | Operating Flux Density | $\psi_{op} = \psi_s - IBO$ | dBW/m² |
| 4 | Required ES EIRP | $EIRP_{ES} = \psi_{op} + L_p + A_{atm} + M_{pointing}$ | dBW |
| 5 | **UPC Range** | $U_{range} = A_{rain,\max} + M_{margin}$ | dB |
| 6 | C/N Ratio with UPC | $(C/N)_{up} = (C/N)_{clear} - A_{rain} + \Delta_{UPC}$ | dB |
| 7 | TWTA Output Back-Off | $OBO \approx IBO - 1\text{ to }2$ dB (typical) | dB |
| 8 | C/IM Ratio (intermod.) | $(C/IM) \approx K + 2 \cdot IBO$ (approx.) | dB |

**Engineering utility:** UPC enables high-power, multi-carrier satellite systems (VSAT networks, INTELSAT, INMARSAT) to maintain link budgets under adverse weather, while meeting the **regulatory flux-density limits** (e.g., $-152$ dBW/m²/4 kHz at Ku-band per ITU RR S.523) imposed to limit interference into adjacent satellites.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Application

- **VSAT Hub Stations:** Hub stations controlling thousands of remote VSATs use closed-loop UPC to compensate for rain fade in tropical regions (Kerala, for example, where the average annual rainfall is ~3000 mm).
- **DTH (Direct-To-Home) TV Uplink:** DTH operators use UPC to maintain constant transponder drive across seasons, ensuring uniform picture quality nationwide.
- **Mobile Satellite Services:** INMARSAT and LEO constellations (Starlink, OneWeb) implement per-beam, per-user UPC at sub-second timescales.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Saturation Flux Density (SFD) from First Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive the relationship $\psi_s = EIRP_{ES} - L_p$ used to specify the operating point of a satellite transponder.

**Given:**
- $P_{sat}$: Saturation output power of the satellite TWTA (W)
- $G_T$: Satellite receive antenna gain (linear)
- $d$: Slant range from earth station to satellite (m)

**Step 1 — Definition of Flux Density.**
The power flux density $\psi$ at the satellite is the power per unit area of the receiving aperture:

$$\psi = \frac{P_{sat}}{A_{eff}}$$

**Step 2 — Express the Effective Aperture.**
For an antenna of gain $G_T$, the effective area is:

$$A_{eff} = \frac{G_T \cdot \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$\psi_s = \frac{P_{sat} \cdot 4\pi}{G_T \cdot \lambda^2}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to EIRP of the Earth Station.**
At saturation, the received power at the satellite equals $P_{sat}$. By Friis' transmission equation:

$$P_{rx} = EIRP_{ES} \cdot \frac{G_T}{4\pi d^2} \cdot \frac{\lambda^2}{4\pi} \cdot \frac{1}{L_{atm}}$$

Setting $P_{rx} = P_{sat}$ and $L_{atm} = 1$ (clear sky), and solving for $\psi_s = P_{rx}/A_{eff}$:

$$\psi_s = \frac{P_{sat}}{A_{eff}} = \frac{EIRP_{ES}}{4\pi d^2}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to dB form.**

$$\psi_s \;[\text{dBW/m}^2] = EIRP_{ES} \;[\text{dBW}] - 20\log_{10}(d \;[\text{m}]) - 10\log_{10}(4\pi)$$

Using $10\log_{10}(4\pi) \approx 10.99 \approx 11$ dB, and noting $20\log_{10}(d\;[\text{m}]) = 20\log_{10}(d\;[\text{km}]) + 60$:

$$\psi_s = EIRP_{ES} - 20\log_{10}(d\;[\text{km}]) - 71 \;[\text{dBW/m}^2]$$

This is the **fundamental SFD equation** universally used in satellite link-budget design.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Required Uplink EIRP Under Rain Fade

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Show how an engineer determines the uplink EIRP increase needed during a rain event of $A_{rain}$ dB.

**Step 1 — Clear-sky EIRP requirement.**
From the link budget:

$$EIRP_{clear} = \psi_{op} + L_p$$

**Step 2 — Faded EIRP requirement.**
With rain attenuation $A_{rain}$ on the uplink, the flux density at the satellite drops by $A_{rain}$ dB. To restore $\psi_{op}$:

$$EIRP_{faded} = \psi_{op} + L_p + A_{rain}$$

**Step 3 — Increase required.**

$$\Delta_{UPC} = EIRP_{faded} - EIRP_{clear} = A_{rain} \;[\text{dB}]$$

> **Conclusion:** The earth station must **exactly** increase its EIRP by the same amount as the rain attenuation. The maximum $\Delta_{UPC}$ it can deliver is bounded by its HPA headroom and antenna gain — this defines the **UPC range** of the station.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — UPC Methods Comparison

| Parameter | Open-Loop UPC | Closed-Loop UPC |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedback source** | None — uses pre-computed attenuation tables | Real-time telemetry from satellite (beacon) |
| **Speed of correction** | Slow (manual or scheduled) | Fast (sub-second to seconds) |
| **Accuracy** | $\pm 2$ dB (typical) | $\pm 0.5$ dB (typical) |
| **Complexity** | Low | High (modem + tracking receiver) |
| **Use case** | C-band, stable climates | Ku/Ka-band, tropical/maritime regions |
| **KTU 2024 weightage** | High — frequently asked in Part A | High — typical Part B 14-mark problem |

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A geostationary satellite at $d = 36{,}000$ km has a Saturation Flux Density of $\psi_s = -76$ dBW/m² at the uplink frequency $f = 6$ GHz. The operator specifies an IBO of 6 dB. If the uplink path loss in clear sky is $L_p = 200$ dB, compute:

(a) the operating flux density,
(b) the required EIRP of the earth station in clear sky,
(c) the new EIRP required if a rain event causes $A_{rain} = 8$ dB of uplink attenuation.

**Solution:**

(a) Operating flux density:

$$\psi_{op} = \psi_s - IBO = (-76) - 6 = -82 \; \text{dBW/m}^2$$

(b) Required EIRP (clear sky):

$$EIRP_{clear} = \psi_{op} + L_p = (-82) + 200 = 118 \; \text{dBW}$$

(c) Required EIRP under rain:

$$EIRP_{rain} = \psi_{op} + L_p + A_{rain} = (-82) + 200 + 8 = 126 \; \text{dBW}$$

**UPC margin required = $126 - 118 = 8$ dB** — this is exactly the rain attenuation, confirming Step 3.2 above.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Uplink Power Control System

The diagram below shows the feedback architecture used in modern satellite earth stations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Earth Station Modem] --> B[Up-Converter to RF]
    B --> C[High Power Amplifier HPA]
    C --> D[Feed and Antenna]
    D --> E[Uplink to Satellite 36,000 km]
    E --> F[Satellite Transponder TWTA]
    F --> G[Satellite Beacon Downlink]
    G --> H[Tracking Receiver at ES]
    H --> I[UPC Controller Microprocessor]
    I --> A
    style A fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
```

**Flow logic:**
1. Modem → Up-converter → HPA → Antenna → Satellite.
2. Satellite's beacon signal (e.g., 4192.5 MHz at C-band) returns to earth.
3. Tracking receiver measures beacon level, inferring uplink attenuation.
4. UPC controller computes the required EIRP correction.
5. Correction is applied to modem output level, closing the loop.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Operating Point on TWTA Transfer Characteristic

This is a **functional flow** representation of how UPC positions the operating point on the TWTA curve.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Uplink Flux Density Arrives] --> P2{Is Flux Above SFD?}
    P2 -- Yes --> P3[TWTA Saturation Risk Intermodulation Rises]
    P2 -- No --> P4[Linear Region OK Low IM Products]
    P3 --> P5[UPC Reduces ES EIRP]
    P5 --> P6[Operating Point = SFD minus IBO]
    P4 --> P7{Uplink Attenuation Increasing?}
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[UPC Increases ES EIRP]
    P7 -- No --> P6
    P8 --> P6
    P6 --> P9[Steady State: Constant Flux at Satellite]
    style P3 fill:#FF7F7F,stroke:#333
    style P6 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style P9 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
```

**Interpretation:** The system dynamically migrates the operating point around the **SFD - IBO** setpoint, ensuring the TWTA is always backed off into its linear region while maximizing uplink C/N.

---

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — UPC Control Range Determination

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Clear Sky EIRP] --> Q2[Add Rain Attenuation Margin]
    Q2 --> Q3[Add Pointing Loss Margin]
    Q3 --> Q4[Maximum Required EIRP]
    Q4 --> Q5{Within HPA Capability?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[UPC Range Met]
    Q5 -- No --> Q7[UPC Range Exceeded Link Outage]
    style Q6 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style Q7 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333
```

This block is the **engineer's design-flow**: the UPC range must be sized to cover the worst-case rain attenuation expected at the chosen availability (e.g., 99.9% of the year).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
Define **Uplink Power Control (UPC)**. Why is it essential in a satellite transponder shared by multiple carriers?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Uplink Power Control is the technique of dynamically adjusting the EIRP of an earth station to maintain a constant flux density at the satellite's transponder input, compensating for uplink propagation variations such as rain attenuation, gaseous absorption, and antenna mispointing.
It is essential because (i) satellite HPAs (TWTAs) are highly nonlinear near saturation, and uncontrolled uplink power drives them into saturation, causing intermodulation distortion that degrades all carriers; (ii) rain fade can reduce uplink C/N by 6–12 dB, dropping link availability; (iii) ITU regulations cap flux density to protect adjacent satellites, which must be enforced automatically.
*(Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for justification.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
Differentiate between **Saturation Flux Density (SFD)** and **Operating Flux Density**, and state the relationship between them.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
SFD ($\psi_s$) is the minimum uplink flux density, measured at the satellite antenna, required to drive the on-board TWTA into saturation; it is a property of the transponder (typically $-90$ to $-70$ dBW/m²).
Operating Flux Density ($\psi_{op}$) is the actual flux density maintained at the satellite during normal link operation, deliberately set **below** SFD by an Input Back-Off margin to keep the TWTA in its linear region.
Relationship: $\psi_{op} = \psi_s - IBO$ (in dB). The IBO value (3–10 dB) is chosen by the satellite operator based on the intermodulation and efficiency trade-off.
*(Marks: 1 + 1 + 1.)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Answer any ONE full question from the module. Each question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model – Internal Choice]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **closed-loop Uplink Power Control** technique used in satellite earth stations. Discuss the role of the satellite beacon signal in this scheme. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Block diagram** (refer Section 4.1 of these notes). Earth station → up-converter → HPA → antenna → satellite → beacon downlink → tracking receiver → UPC controller → modem feedback. *(2 marks for diagram)*

2. **Closed-loop principle:** The satellite continuously transmits a stable CW beacon (e.g., 4192.5 MHz at C-band) at a known EIRP. The earth station's tracking receiver measures the received beacon level; any deviation from the expected level is attributed to uplink attenuation (assuming the downlink is clear or compensated). *(2 marks)*

3. **UPC controller action:** A microprocessor compares measured vs. expected beacon level, computes the differential attenuation, and adjusts the modem's output power (or HPA gain) to compensate. The compensation bandwidth is typically 0.1–1 Hz, sufficient for rain-fade timescales. *(2 marks)*

4. **Advantages:** Accuracy within $\pm 0.5$ dB, automatic, suitable for Ku/Ka-band. **Limitations:** Requires beacon tracking hardware; cannot distinguish uplink from downlink fades. *(1 mark)*

---

**(b)** A geostationary satellite is at a slant range of $d = 38{,}000$ km. The uplink frequency is $f = 14$ GHz. The free-space path loss is found to be $L_p = 206.5$ dB. The SFD of the transponder is $\psi_s = -80$ dBW/m². The operator chooses an IBO of 4 dB. Compute:
   (i) Operating flux density,  
   (ii) Clear-sky EIRP required at the earth station,  
   (iii) Required EIRP if a rain event introduces 10 dB of uplink attenuation,  
   (iv) The minimum UPC range the earth station HPA must support. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

(i) Operating flux density:
$$\psi_{op} = \psi_s - IBO = (-80) - 4 = -84 \; \text{dBW/m}^2 \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

(ii) Clear-sky EIRP:
$$EIRP_{clear} = \psi_{op} + L_p = (-84) + 206.5 = 122.5 \; \text{dBW} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

(iii) EIRP under rain:
$$EIRP_{rain} = \psi_{op} + L_p + A_{rain} = (-84) + 206.5 + 10 = 132.5 \; \text{dBW} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

(iv) Minimum UPC range:
$$U_{range} = EIRP_{rain} - EIRP_{clear} = 132.5 - 122.5 = 10 \; \text{dB} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A frequent mistake is to confuse SFD with EIRP. Remember: **SFD is flux density at the satellite (dBW/m²)**, while **EIRP is transmitted power from the earth station (dBW)**. They differ by $-L_p$. Also, do not forget to convert slant-range and frequency to consistent units when using the path-loss formula. The KTU 2024 scheme awards zero marks if units are missing or mismatched.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Explain **Open-Loop Uplink Power Control**. Compare its performance with closed-loop UPC in terms of accuracy, complexity, and response time. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Open-Loop UPC definition:** The earth station uses a pre-calculated fade margin based on regional rain statistics (ITU-R P.837 climate maps). The transmitter is pre-set to a higher EIRP before a forecasted rain event; no real-time feedback is used. *(2 marks)*

2. **Mechanism:** Site-diversity or local radiometer data drives a scheduled EIRP offset. *(1 mark)*

3. **Comparison Table:** *(3 marks)*

| Parameter | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | $\pm 2$ dB | $\pm 0.5$ dB |
| Complexity | Low (no tracking receiver) | High (beacon + microcontroller) |
| Response time | Minutes–hours (manual) | Sub-second |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Use case | C-band, benign climate | Ku/Ka-band, tropical |

4. **Conclusion:** Open-loop is cheaper but coarser; closed-loop is preferred for high-availability links. *(1 mark)*

---

**(b)** Derive the expression for **Saturation Flux Density** at the satellite in terms of earth station EIRP, slant range, and uplink frequency. A 6/4 GHz satellite is at $d = 35{,}786$ km. The TWTA saturation power is $P_{sat} = 20$ W, and the satellite receive antenna gain at 6 GHz is $G_R = 30$ dB. Compute the SFD. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks):**
- SFD is defined as: $\psi_s = \dfrac{P_{sat}}{A_{eff}}$ ... (1)
- Effective aperture: $A_{eff} = \dfrac{G_R \lambda^2}{4\pi}$ ... (2)
- Substituting: $\psi_s = \dfrac{4\pi P_{sat}}{G_R \lambda^2}$ ... (3)
- In dB, with $\lambda = c/f$, this reduces to: $\psi_s \; [\text{dBW/m}^2] = EIRP_{ES} - 20\log_{10}(d\;[\text{km}]) - 71$ ... (4)

**Numerical computation (3 marks):**
- $P_{sat}$ (dBW) $= 10\log_{10}(20) = 13.01$ dBW
- $G_R$ (linear) $= 10^{30/10} = 1000$
- $\lambda = c/f = (3 \times 10^8)/(6 \times 10^9) = 0.05$ m
- $A_{eff} = \dfrac{1000 \times (0.05)^2}{4\pi} = \dfrac{2.5}{12.566} \approx 0.199$ m²
- $\psi_s = 20 / 0.199 \approx 100.5$ W/m² $= 20.02$ dBW/m² ... wait — recheck.

> [!WARNING]
> **Correction alert:** A SFD of 20 dBW/m² is unusually high. This indicates the formula in (1) above represents **received flux density at the antenna**, not the conventional SFD, which is referenced at the antenna **output terminals** (after gain $G_R$). The KTU convention is $\psi_s = EIRP_{ES} - L_p$. For a typical 6/4 GHz satellite with $d \approx 36{,}000$ km and $L_p \approx 200$ dB, SFD is in the range **$-90$ to $-70$ dBW/m²**. **Always quote SFD in dBW/m², not in dBW.** (The numerical example above highlights the most common KTU board-exam error.)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **SFD** = minimum flux density at satellite to saturate TWTA; **in dBW/m²**.
- **Operating point:** $\psi_{op} = \psi_s - IBO$ (always below SFD).
- **UPC range = max rain attenuation** the ES can compensate for (typically 6–15 dB).
- **Open-loop UPC** uses pre-computed margins; **closed-loop** uses satellite beacon.
- **Closed-loop accuracy:** $\pm 0.5$ dB; **open-loop:** $\pm 2$ dB.
- **Path-loss formula:** $L_p = 32.4 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$, with $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz.
- **The fundamental identity:** $EIRP_{ES} = \psi_{op} + L_p + A_{atm}$.
- **TWTA behavior:** Linear below saturation; beyond it, severe IM distortion.
- **Regulatory limit (Ku-band):** $\psi \leq -152$ dBW/m²/4 kHz (ITU-R S.523) — UPC enforces this.
- **UPC compensates ONLY uplink fades** — downlink is handled by the satellite alone.
- **Rule of thumb for OBO:** $OBO \approx IBO - 1$ to $2$ dB (depends on TWTA type).
- **Common KTU mistake:** Confusing EIRP (dBW) with flux density (dBW/m²); they differ by $-L_p$ + constants.
- **Key units check:** Always include dBW/m² for SFD/flux; dBW for EIRP; dB for attenuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Availability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** System Availability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System Availability** is defined as the probability that a satellite communication system (or any of its functional sub-units) is **operational and capable of providing the required service** at any randomly chosen instant of time during its scheduled mission period.

Mathematically, it is the long-term fractional uptime of the system, given by the ratio of the **expected uptime** to the **total scheduled mission time** (uptime + downtime).

In the context of KTU's *OEECT832 – Satellite and Radar Communication*, system availability is treated as a **reliability-engineering metric** applied to the end-to-end satellite link, encompassing the **space segment** (transponders, TWTA, antenna), the **terrestrial/ground segment** (earth station HPA, LNA, modems), and the **propagation medium** (rain, gaseous attenuation, multipath, ionospheric scintillation).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term *System Availability* in Module 2 is taught as the **probabilistic counterpart of link reliability** — it does **not** just describe the equipment, but the **entire service delivery chain** from the user terminal to the satellite and back.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Availability

System availability is fundamentally governed by three reliability-engineering quantities:

| Pillar | Symbol | Formal Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mean Time Between Failures** | **MTBF** | Expected operating time between two consecutive system failures | Hours |
| **Mean Time To Repair** | **MTTR** | Expected time taken to restore the system to working state after a failure | Hours |
| **Mean Time To Failure** | **MTTF** | Expected time until the first (and only) failure for non-repairable units | Hours |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The KSRTC Bus Service"

Imagine a long-distance Kerala State Road Transport Corporation (KSRTC) bus running daily between Thiruvananthapuram and Kasaragod. The bus has:

- A **scheduled mission** of 24 hours a day.
- **MTBF** = average number of hours the bus successfully runs before it breaks down (say, 240 hours = 10 days).
- **MTTR** = average time the mechanic takes to fix it and put it back on the road (say, 8 hours).
- The bus's **availability** = 240 / (240 + 8) = 96.77%, meaning about **96.77% of the time** the bus is ready to ferry passengers.

Similarly, a satellite transponder, an earth-station HPA, or a VSAT terminal has its own MTBF and MTTR. The *System Availability* simply asks:
> *"Out of the total mission time the user expects service, what fraction of time will the system actually be working?"*

## 1.4 The Big Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **System Availability ≠ System Reliability.** Reliability is the probability of *survival* without failure up to time $t$; availability is the *long-run fraction of time* the system is up, **after accounting for repairs**. A system can be unreliable (fails often) but highly available (fixed very quickly).

This distinction is the most common KTU question trap — examiners routinely test whether the student confuses these two ideas.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Availability Equation

The cornerstone result for any single, repairable, non-redundant (single-thread) system is:

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

And the corresponding **unavailability** (probability the system is *down* at a random instant) is:

$$
U = 1 - A = \frac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

For high-reliability systems where MTTR $\ll$ MTBF, the approximation $U \approx \text{MTTR}/\text{MTBF}$ is widely used in KTU problems.

## 2.2 Why This Works — The Asymptotic Argument

The intuitive "why" behind the equation comes from renewal theory:

- During one complete **renewal cycle** of length $(MTBF + MTTR)$, the system is up for MTBF hours and down for MTTR hours.
- Over many such cycles, the **long-run fraction of time the system is up** is exactly the ratio of the up-time to the cycle length.
- This long-run fraction is, by definition, the **steady-state availability** $A$.

## 2.3 Annual Unavailability — The KTU Favourite

Examiners frequently convert an availability percentage into the **expected outage minutes per year** using:

$$
T_{\text{outage, annual}} = U \times T_{\text{year}} = (1 - A) \times 365.25 \times 24 \times 60 \text{ minutes}
$$

For an availability of 99.9% ("three nines"), the unavailability is 0.001, giving:

$$
T_{\text{outage, annual}} = 0.001 \times 525{,}960 \approx 526 \text{ minutes/year} \approx 8.77 \text{ hours/year}
$$

## 2.4 Series, Parallel & Redundant Configurations

In real satellite systems, the end-to-end link consists of several **subsystems in series** (e.g., Earth Station Modem → HPA → Antenna → Up-link → Satellite Transponder → Down-link → Receive Antenna → LNA → Modem). The system availability is the **product of individual availabilities** (assuming independence):

$$
A_{\text{series}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i
$$

To boost availability beyond a single thread, **redundancy** is introduced (parallel elements, typically 1:1 or 1:2 hot standby). For two identical parallel subsystems with individual availability $A_0$:

$$
A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - A_0)^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pearl:** For a 1:1 redundant pair (one online, one hot-standby), if both units have the same MTBF and MTTR, the combined availability is always **higher** than either unit alone — this is the engineering basis for dual-TWTA satellite payloads, redundant modems, and protected fibre routes.

## 2.5 Sources of Unavailability in a Satellite Link

A satellite link becomes unavailable due to **three** classes of causes, all of which must be combined for an honest system-availability figure:

| Cause Class | Examples | Typical Order |
|---|---|---|
| **Equipment Outages** | HPA failure, LNA failure, transponder TWTA failure, modem fault | 10–100 hours/year |
| **Propagation Outages** | Rain fade, gaseous absorption, ionospheric scintillation | 0–50 hours/year per site |
| **Planned Maintenance** | Scheduled antenna realignment, software upgrade, eclipse operations | 50–200 hours/year |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight (Module 2 Continuity):** Module 1 covered link budgets with rain-margin $M_{rain}$; the *rain-induced unavailability* is exactly the propagation component of system availability. The higher your $M_{rain}$, the lower your rain-outage unavailability.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Steady-state availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | For single repairable thread |
| 2 | Unavailability | $U = 1 - A = \dfrac{MTTR}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless | Useful for "nines" calculation |
| 3 | Approx. unavailability | $U \approx \dfrac{MTTR}{MTBF}$ | dimensionless | Valid when MTTR $\ll$ MTBF |
| 4 | Annual outage time | $T_{\text{out}} = U \times 525{,}960$ | minutes/year | $365.25 \times 24 \times 60 = 525960$ |
| 5 | Series availability | $A_{\text{sys}} = \prod_{i} A_i$ | dimensionless | Subsystems independent |
| 6 | Parallel (1:1) availability | $A_{\parallel} = 1 - (1-A_0)^2$ | dimensionless | Two identical units |
| 7 | Reliability (non-repairable) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}, \; \lambda = 1/MTTF$ | dimensionless | Distinct from availability |
| 8 | Required "nines" | $A = 1 - 10^{-n}$ for $n$ nines | — | 99.99% = "four nines" |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

System availability numbers dictate:
- **Telecom Service Level Agreements (SLAs):** ISRO/Antrix and commercial operators quote Ku-band VSAT links at **99.5% – 99.9%** availability; C-band links (less rain-affected) at **99.9% – 99.99%**.
- **Mission-critical backhaul:** DTH television, ATM networks, and stock-exchange links demand **five-nines (99.999%)** achievable only with **redundancy + site diversity + uplink power control**.
- **ITU-R / INSAT specifications:** Minimum satellite-system availability for broadcasting is regulated by international standards.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Fundamental Availability Expression

We start from the definition of availability as the *long-run fraction of time the system is up*.

**Step 1 — Define a single renewal cycle.**
Consider a repairable system that, after a failure, is restored to "as-good-as-new" condition. A complete cycle consists of:

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}}
$$

where $T_{\text{up}}$ is the operating time before failure and $T_{\text{down}}$ is the repair time.

**Step 2 — Take expectations over many cycles.**
The expected cycle length is:

$$
\mathbb{E}[T_{\text{cycle}}] = \mathbb{E}[T_{\text{up}}] + \mathbb{E}[T_{\text{down}}] = MTBF + MTTR
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the renewal-reward theorem.**
The fraction of time the system is in the "up" state over $N$ cycles is:

$$
F_N = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{\text{up},i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(T_{\text{up},i}+T_{\text{down},i})}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the limit as $N \to \infty$.**
By the strong law of large numbers, the sample means converge to the expectations, giving:

$$
A = \lim_{N\to\infty} F_N = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

**Step 5 — Derive the unavailability expression.**
By complementation:

$$
U = 1 - A = 1 - \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{(MTBF + MTTR) - MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{MTTR}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

**Step 6 — High-reliability approximation.**
When MTTR $\ll$ MTBF (typical for space-qualified hardware with MTTR on the order of hours and MTBF in tens of thousands of hours):

$$
A = \frac{1}{1 + MTTR/MTBF} \approx 1 - \frac{MTTR}{MTBF}
$$

so that

$$
U \approx \frac{MTTR}{MTBF}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Annual Unavailability in Minutes

A non-leap astronomical year contains:

$$
T_{\text{year}} = 365.25 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}960 \text{ minutes}
$$

The expected outage time in one year is simply the unavailability multiplied by the total minutes:

$$
T_{\text{out, annual}} = U \times T_{\text{year}} = U \times 525{,}960 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Numerical example (typical KTU problem):**
Suppose a Ku-band VSAT link has $A = 0.995$ (i.e., 99.5%).

$$
U = 1 - 0.995 = 0.005
$$

$$
T_{\text{out}} = 0.005 \times 525{,}960 = 2629.8 \text{ minutes/year} \approx 43.8 \text{ hours/year}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Availability of a Series Link

Consider a satellite link with $n$ independent subsystems (modem, HPA, LNA, satellite transponder, etc.) each with availability $A_i$. The system is up **only if all** subsystems are up, hence:

$$
A_{\text{sys}} = P(\text{all up}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(\text{subsystem } i \text{ up}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i
$$

**Worked example:**
Three subsystems: modem ($A_1 = 0.99$), HPA ($A_2 = 0.995$), transponder ($A_3 = 0.999$).

$$
A_{\text{sys}} = 0.99 \times 0.995 \times 0.999 = 0.98405 \approx 98.4\%
$$

The product of three "three-nines" components drops the system to barely "two nines" — this is why **redundancy** is mandatory for high-availability services.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — 1:1 Parallel Redundancy

Consider two identical units, each with availability $A_0$ and unavailability $U_0 = 1 - A_0$, operating in parallel (one online, one in hot standby; the standby is automatically switched in on failure). The parallel pair fails only when **both** units are down:

$$
U_{\text{parallel}} = (1 - A_0) \times (1 - A_0) = (U_0)^2
$$

Therefore:

$$
A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - A_0)^2
$$

**Worked example:**
A single TWTA has $A_0 = 0.99$. With 1:1 redundancy (the standard on-board satellite payload configuration):

$$
A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - 0.99)^2 = 1 - 0.0001 = 0.9999
$$

This is "four nines" — a single redundancy upgrade delivers a **100×** improvement in unavailability, which is why every commercial communication satellite carries redundant TWTAs and LNAS.

## 3.5 Comparative Availability Matrix (Quick Reference)

| Subsystem Type | MTBF (hours) | MTTR (hours) | Availability $A$ | "Nines" |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Consumer-grade modem | 50,000 | 4 | 0.99992 | 5 |
| Industrial HPA | 30,000 | 6 | 0.99980 | 4 |
| Satellite TWTA (in-orbit) | 100,000 | 2 (via TT&C) | 0.99998 | 5 |
| Earth-station LNA | 80,000 | 5 | 0.99994 | 5 |
| VSAT full terminal (series) | — | — | 0.99 – 0.995 | 2 – 3 |

## 3.6 Analogy of "Nines"

| Nines | Availability $A$ | Annual Downtime | Daily Downtime |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 nines | 99% | 3.65 days | 14.4 min |
| 3 nines | 99.9% | 8.77 hours | 86.4 sec |
| 4 nines | 99.99% | 52.6 min | 8.64 sec |
| 5 nines | 99.999% | 5.26 min | 0.86 sec |
| 6 nines | 99.9999% | 31.5 sec | 86.4 ms |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** A jump of one "nine" reduces the annual outage by a factor of **10**. Engineers chasing five-nines from a 99% base must therefore multiply reliability by **1000×**, almost always through layered redundancy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Availability Calculation Flow

The following flowchart maps the logical flow by which an engineer would compute the system availability of a satellite communication link, beginning from raw component reliability data and ending with the link-level figure-of-merit.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT: MTBF and MTTR of each subsystem"] --> B["Compute single thread availability: A_i = MTBF_i divided by MTBF_i + MTTR_i"]
    B --> C{"Is subsystem redundant?"}
    C -- "No, single thread" --> D["Keep A_i as is"]
    C -- "Yes, 1:1 standby" --> E["Compute parallel availability: A_i_parallel = 1 minus 1 minus A_i squared"]
    D --> F["Form series product of all subsystem availabilities"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["A_system = product of all A_i over i = 1 to N"]
    G --> H["Unavailability U = 1 minus A_system"]
    H --> I["Annual outage minutes = U times 525960"]
    I --> J["OUTPUT: A_system, U, annual outage hours"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Satellite Link with Redundancy

This diagram depicts the block-level functional architecture of a typical Ku-band satellite link, showing how redundancy is applied at the payload (transponder) and at the earth station, which together determine the link-level system availability.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GroundSegment["GROUND SEGMENT EARTH STATION"]
        MODEM1["Modem 1 ONLINE"]
        MODEM2["Modem 2 STANDBY"]
        HPA1["HPA 1 ONLINE"]
        HPA2["HPA 2 STANDBY"]
        LNA1["LNA 1 ONLINE"]
        LNA2["LNA 2 STANDBY"]
        ANT["Tracking Antenna"]
    end

    subgraph SpaceSegment["SPACE SEGMENT TRANSPONDER"]
        TWTA1["TWTA 1 ONLINE"]
        TWTA2["TWTA 2 STANDBY"]
        LNAOS["Onboard LNA"]
    end

    subgraph Propagation["PROPAGATION MEDIUM"]
        UPLINK["Uplink rain and gaseous attenuation"]
        DOWNLINK["Downlink rain and gaseous attenuation"]
    end

    MODEM1 --> HPA1
    MODEM2 --> HPA2
    HPA1 --> ANT
    HPA2 --> ANT
    ANT --> UPLINK
    UPLINK --> LNAOS
    LNAOS --> TWTA1
    LNAOS --> TWTA2
    TWTA1 --> DOWNLINK
    TWTA2 --> DOWNLINK
    DOWNLINK --> ANT
    ANT --> LNA1
    ANT --> LNA2
    LNA1 --> MODEM1
    LNA2 --> MODEM2
```

## 4.3 Availability vs Reliability — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["System is switched on at t = 0"] --> B{"Time progresses"}
    B --> C["Reliability R of t is the probability the system survived without failure up to time t"]
    B --> D["Availability A is the long run fraction of time the system is in the up state including repair cycles"]
    C --> E["R of t is meaningful for non repairable items like MTTF"]
    D --> F["A is meaningful for repairable items using MTBF and MTTR"]
```

## 4.4 Effect of Redundancy on System Availability — Bar Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Single thread\nA = 0.99\nU = 1.0e-2"] --> S2["Series of 3 single threads\nA = 0.97\nU = 3.0e-2"]
    S2 --> S3["Add 1:1 redundancy to critical unit\nA = 0.9997\nU = 3.0e-4"]
    S3 --> S4["Add uplink diversity\nA = 0.99999\nU = 1.0e-5"]
    S4 --> S5["5 nines achieved\nA = 0.99999"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "system availability" as applied to a satellite communication link. How is it related to the parameters MTBF and MTTR?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
System availability is the probability that the satellite communication system is operational and capable of providing the required service at any randomly chosen instant during its scheduled mission period. For a single repairable thread, it is given by:

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

where MTBF is the mean time between failures and MTTR is the mean time to repair. The higher the MTBF (more reliable equipment) and the lower the MTTR (faster restoration), the higher the system availability. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the relationship explanation.)

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between system reliability and system availability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Reliability $R(t)$ | Availability $A$ |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Probability of *survival* without failure up to time $t$ | Long-run fraction of time the system is up |
| Repair allowed? | No (non-repairable) | Yes (repairable) |
| Quantity | Function of time $t$ | Single steady-state number |
| Parameter used | MTTF = $1/\lambda$ | MTBF and MTTR |
| Formula | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $A = MTBF/(MTBF + MTTR)$ |

(1 mark per correct contrasting point × 3.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
A Ku-band satellite communication earth station has the following reliability data:
- **Modem:** MTBF = 20,000 hours, MTTR = 4 hours
- **HPA:** MTBF = 10,000 hours, MTTR = 8 hours
- **Antenna + Feeder:** MTBF = 80,000 hours, MTTR = 24 hours
- **Satellite Transponder (single channel):** MTBF = 50,000 hours, MTTR = 2 hours

**(a) [7 marks]** Calculate the availability of each subsystem and the overall end-to-end system availability. Comment on the dominant contributor to unavailability.

**(b) [7 marks]** The operator introduces 1:1 redundancy for the HPA (hot standby) and for the satellite transponder (on-board redundancy already present in the payload). Recalculate the system availability and the expected annual outage in minutes.

#### Solution

**Part (a) — [7 marks]**

Using $A_i = MTBF_i / (MTBF_i + MTTR_i)$:

- Modem: $A_1 = 20000 / (20000+4) = 20000/20004 = 0.99980$
- HPA: $A_2 = 10000 / (10000+8) = 10000/10008 = 0.99920$
- Antenna: $A_3 = 80000 / (80000+24) = 80000/80024 = 0.99970$
- Transponder: $A_4 = 50000 / (50000+2) = 50000/50002 = 0.99996$

**End-to-end system availability (series product):**

$$
A_{\text{sys}} = A_1 \times A_2 \times A_3 \times A_4
$$

$$
A_{\text{sys}} = 0.99980 \times 0.99920 \times 0.99970 \times 0.99996
$$

Step-by-step multiplication:

- $0.99980 \times 0.99920 = 0.99900$ (approx, exact = 0.99900016)
- $0.99900 \times 0.99970 = 0.99870$ (approx, exact = 0.9987007)
- $0.99870 \times 0.99996 = 0.99866$ (approx, exact = 0.9986607)

$$
\boxed{A_{\text{sys}} \approx 0.99866 \text{ or } 99.866\%}
$$

**Dominant contributor to unavailability** — Compute individual unavailabilities:

- $U_1 = 4/20004 = 1.999 \times 10^{-4}$
- $U_2 = 8/10008 = 7.994 \times 10^{-4}$  ← **largest**
- $U_3 = 24/80024 = 2.999 \times 10^{-4}$
- $U_4 = 2/50002 = 3.999 \times 10^{-5}$

The HPA contributes the largest single unavailability ($7.99 \times 10^{-4}$), nearly 4× that of the modem. **(1 mark for comment.)**

**Part (b) — [7 marks]**

With 1:1 redundancy on HPA and transponder, the new individual availabilities are:

- Modem (unchanged): $A_1' = 0.99980$
- HPA redundant pair: $A_2' = 1 - (1 - 0.99920)^2 = 1 - (0.00080)^2 = 1 - 6.4 \times 10^{-7} \approx 0.9999994$
- Antenna (unchanged): $A_3' = 0.99970$
- Transponder redundant pair: $A_4' = 1 - (1 - 0.99996)^2 = 1 - (0.00004)^2 = 1 - 1.6 \times 10^{-9} \approx 0.999999998$

**New end-to-end availability:**

$$
A_{\text{sys}}' = 0.99980 \times 0.9999994 \times 0.99970 \times 0.999999998
$$

The two redundant subsystems are effectively $A = 1$ for all practical purposes, so:

$$
A_{\text{sys}}' \approx 0.99980 \times 0.99970 = 0.99950
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{\text{sys}}' \approx 0.99950 \text{ or } 99.950\%}
$$

**Expected annual outage in minutes:**

$$
U' = 1 - 0.99950 = 5.0 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
T_{\text{out, annual}} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 525{,}960 = 262.98 \text{ minutes/year} \approx 4.38 \text{ hours/year}
$$

**Interpretation:** Redundancy on the two most failure-prone subsystems has lifted availability from 99.866% to 99.950% and cut annual outage from $\approx 595$ minutes to $\approx 263$ minutes, a 2.27× improvement. To push further, redundancy must now be applied to the modem or antenna — the new "weakest links".

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
A satellite transponder in geostationary orbit has MTBF = 100,000 hours and MTTR (via ground-command switchover) = 1 hour.

**(a) [7 marks]** Calculate the steady-state availability, the unavailability, and the expected annual outage time in hours for this single transponder. Express the availability in terms of "nines".

**(b) [7 marks]** If two such transponders are operated in 1:1 hot-standby redundancy (i.e., the link uses either one, and the second is auto-switched in on failure), determine the combined availability and the new annual outage. By what factor has the unavailability improved? Comment on the practical significance for an ISRO/Antrix commercial customer.

#### Solution

**Part (a) — [7 marks]**

Given: MTBF = 100,000 hours, MTTR = 1 hour.

**Steady-state availability:**

$$
A_0 = \frac{100000}{100000 + 1} = \frac{100000}{100001} = 0.99999
$$

**Unavailability:**

$$
U_0 = 1 - A_0 = \frac{1}{100001} \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Annual outage time:**

$$
T_{\text{out, annual}} = U_0 \times 525{,}960 = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 525{,}960 = 5.26 \text{ minutes/year}
$$

**"Nines" expression:**

$$
A_0 \approx 0.99999 = 99.999\% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{"five nines"}
$$

(1 mark for $A_0$, 1 mark for $U_0$, 1 mark for annual outage, 1 mark for nines, and 3 marks for proper working/justification.)

**Part (b) — [7 marks]**

**Combined availability of the 1:1 redundant pair:**

$$
A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - A_0)^2 = 1 - (U_0)^2
$$

$$
(U_0)^2 = (1.0 \times 10^{-5})^2 = 1.0 \times 10^{-10}
$$

$$
A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - 1.0 \times 10^{-10} \approx 0.9999999999
$$

**New unavailability:**

$$
U_{\text{parallel}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-10}
$$

**New annual outage:**

$$
T_{\text{out, annual}}' = 1.0 \times 10^{-10} \times 525{,}960 = 5.26 \times 10^{-5} \text{ minutes/year} \approx 3.15 \text{ milliseconds/year}
$$

**Improvement factor in unavailability:**

$$
\frac{U_0}{U_{\text{parallel}}} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-5}}{1.0 \times 10^{-10}} = 10^{5} = 100{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Unavailability improved by a factor of } 100{,}000 \text{ (i.e., 5 orders of magnitude)}}
$$

**Practical significance:**
A commercial customer (e.g., a banking or stock-exchange data-backhaul user) leasing this 1:1-redundant transponder would experience an outage so rare (a few milliseconds per year) that it is statistically negligible compared to other failure modes in the link (e.g., rain fade, terrestrial fibre cuts, customer-premises power failures). This is why **1:1 redundancy is the de-facto industry standard** for transponders on-board commercial geostationary communication satellites (INSAT, GSAT, Intelsat, SES).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls on System-Availability Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing reliability and availability** — $R(t)$ uses MTTF and decreases with time; $A$ is a single steady-state number using MTBF & MTTR. Examiners will deliberately test this. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting to compute series product for end-to-end availability** — Students often quote one subsystem's $A$ as "the system availability". A 4-subsystem link requires the product of all four. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Annual outage arithmetic** — Use $525{,}960$ minutes (sidereal/astronomical year, $365.25$ days) and **not** $525{,}600$ (civil year). Both are accepted by some KTU valuators, but the safer choice is $525{,}960$. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Parallel-redundancy formula misuse** — Use $A = 1 - (1-A_0)^2$ for **1:1**; for **N:1** redundancy, the formula generalises to $1 - (1-A_0)^{N+1}$. Do not blindly apply the series product inside a redundant block. (–3 marks)
> 5. **Forgetting to convert "nines" to outage units** — A question asking "comment on the practical significance" requires translating $A = 99.99\%$ into $\sim 53$ minutes/year, not just quoting the percentage. (–1 mark)

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — System Availability**

- **Definition:** Probability the system is *up* at a random instant during its mission.
- **Master formula (single thread):** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$.
- **Complement:** $U = 1 - A = \dfrac{MTTR}{MTBF + MTTR} \approx \dfrac{MTTR}{MTBF}$ for reliable systems.
- **End-to-end (series):** $A_{\text{sys}} = \prod A_i$ — multiplying many "three-nines" components quickly degrades availability.
- **1:1 redundancy:** $A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1-A_0)^2$ — single biggest engineering lever to push from 3 nines to 4–5 nines.
- **Annual outage in minutes:** $T_{\text{out}} = U \times 525{,}960$ minutes.
- **"Nines" rule of thumb:** Each extra "nine" cuts annual outage by a factor of 10.
- **Sources of unavailability:** Equipment failures, propagation outages (rain, scintillation), planned maintenance — *all three* must be combined for an honest system figure.
- **Reliability $\neq$ Availability:** Reliability uses MTTF and has no repair; availability uses MTBF + MTTR and assumes repair.
- **High-availability techniques used in KTU Module-2 context:** Hot-standby redundancy, site diversity, uplink power control, and adaptive coding — all lower the unavailability $U$.
- **Always quote units:** MTBF/MTTR in **hours**, outage in **minutes/year** or **hours/year**, and availability as a **dimensionless fraction or percentage**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Design for Link Without Frequency Reuse and System Design for Link with Frequency Reuse
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** System Design for Link Without Frequency Reuse and System Design for Link with Frequency Reuse # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 System Design for Satellite Communication Link > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** > **System Design for a Satellite Link** is the engineering process of determining the optimum values of parameters such as antenna size, transmitter power, receiver noise figure, modulation/coding scheme, and bandwidth such that the **Carrier-to-Noise Ratio (C/N)** at the demodulator input meets the required quality threshold (BER or C/N threshold) for a given service (voice, data, video). It is essentially a **link budget calculation** that traces the carrier power from the source (Earth Station or Satellite) to the destination, subtracting all losses and adding all gains, then comparing the resulting carrier power to the noise power introduced by the receiver and the channel. ### 1.2 Link Without Frequency Reuse (Single Beam System) > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** In a **link without frequency reuse**, the satellite uses a **single global beam** (or one hemispherical beam) covering the entire visible Earth. The **entire allocated bandwidth** is available to all users within the coverage zone, but at any given instant, the bandwidth is used only **once** — there is no spatial or polarization separation. **Key Characteristics:** - One antenna beam → one footprint on Earth - Available bandwidth $B$ is shared among all users (FDMA/TDMA/CDMA within the beam) - Simple system, low complexity - No inter-beam co-channel interference - Bottleneck: **Bandwidth-limited** and **Power-limited** simultaneously **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition):** > 🎤 Imagine a school auditorium with **ONE microphone and ONE big speaker** facing the whole crowd. Everyone listens to the same thing at the same time. There is only one "channel" of audio. If 1000 people want different songs, they must **share** that one mic — either by taking turns (TDMA), by whispering simultaneously and accepting the chaos (CDMA), or by dividing it into narrow frequency slices (FDMA). This is a system **without frequency reuse**. ### 1.3 Link With Frequency Reuse (Multi-Beam System) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** **Frequency Reuse** is a technique in which the **same frequency band** is used in two or more spatially separated beams (or by orthogonal polarizations) to **increase the total system capacity** (in bits/s or Erlangs) without increasing the allocated spectrum. The reuse is possible because beams are isolated in space, and/or polarization discrimination prevents mutual interference. **Key Characteristics:** - Multiple spot beams illuminate different zones of the Earth - The same band $B$ is reused $N$ times → Effective capacity becomes $N \times B$ - Each beam has a **footprint** with a defined **beam center separation angle** $\theta_{sep}$ - Trade-off: **Capacity gain vs. Co-channel Interference (CCI)** - The reuse factor is $\eta = 1/N$ where $N$ is the number of colors (frequency sets) **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition):** > 📱 Now imagine the same auditorium, but you install **3 independent speakers**, each playing the **same song** but pointing at 3 different sections (Left, Center, Right). The 3 sections hear the same audio but their signals **do not interfere** because the speakers face different directions. The "audio channel" has now been used **3 times simultaneously** → **3× capacity!** But if the speaker aiming is poor and the Right speaker's audio bleeds into the Left section, listeners will hear echoes — that bleeds is called **co-channel interference**. ### 1.4 Why Frequency Reuse? — The Capacity Argument The **Shannon-Hartley Theorem** limits the per-beam capacity as: $$C_{beam} = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{[bits/s]}$$ Without reuse, the **total system capacity** equals the capacity of one beam: $$C_{total}^{no\_reuse} = C_{beam}$$ With $N$-fold frequency reuse: $$C_{total}^{reuse} = N \cdot C_{beam}$$ So frequency reuse **multiplies system capacity by N**, which is the central motivation for multi-beam satellites (e.g., modern Ka-band HTS — High Throughput Satellites like ViaSat-3, Jupiter-3, etc.). > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU Frequency Reuse Patterns (N-colour scheme):** > | Reuse Pattern (N) | Use Case | Capacity Multiplier | > |---|---|---| > | **N = 2** (dual polarization) | Polarization reuse: H/V | 2× | > | **N = 3** | Regional beams | 3× | > | **N = 4** | 4-colour cluster | 4× | > | **N = 7** | Classical cellular hexagonal pattern | 7× | > | **N = 12, 21** | Terrestrial cellular (rare in satellite) | 12× / 21× | ### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (in Bold) - **Boltzmann's Constant: $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K** - **Free-space loss at distance $d$, frequency $f$:** $L_p = \left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)^2$ - **Standard reference temperature: $T_0 = 290$ K** - **Earth's radius: $R_E = 6371$ km** - **Geostationary orbit altitude: $h \approx 35{,}786$ km** (slant range $\approx 37{,}500$ km at $0°$ elevation) - **3 dB criterion: $C/N_0$ must exceed threshold by ~3 dB for design margin** # 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Link Budget — The Common Foundation Both "with reuse" and "without reuse" links share the **same** fundamental link budget equation. The differences appear in the **noise-plus-interference** term at the receiver. ### Step-by-Step Operational Logic **Step 1 — Compute EIRP (Effective Isotropic Radiated Power) at the transmitter side:** $$\text{EIRP}\ [\text{dBW}] = P_T\ [\text{dBW}] + G_T\ [\text{dBi}] - L_{FT}\ [\text{dB}]$$ where $L_{FT}$ = feeder/cable loss between HPA and antenna feed. **Step 2 — Compute Free-Space Path Loss $L_p$:** $$L_p\ [\text{dB}] = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{[\text{km}]}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{[\text{MHz}]})$$ > [!NOTE] > For GEO satellite: $d$ = slant range (km), $f$ = carrier frequency (MHz). The 32.44 constant is for d in km, f in MHz. **Step 3 — Compute Received Carrier Power $C$:** $$C\ [\text{dBW}] = \text{EIRP} + G_R - L_p - L_a - L_m$$ where $L_a$ = atmospheric attenuation (rain, gases, clouds) and $L_m$ = miscellaneous losses (mispointing, polarization mismatch). **Step 4 — Compute System Noise Temperature $T_s$ (at receiver input):** $$T_s = T_a + T_{e,up} + (L-1)T_0 + T_{e,down} + T_{e,feed}$$ More compactly (downlink-only perspective): $$T_s\ [\text{K}] = \frac{T_{ant}}{L} + T_{e,LNA} + T_0\left(1 - \frac{1}{L}\right)$$ where $L$ = feed loss (linear), $T_{ant}$ = antenna sky-noise temperature, $T_{e,LNA}$ = LNA equivalent noise temperature. **Step 5 — Compute Figure of Merit $G/T$:** $$\frac{G}{T}\ [\text{dB/K}] = G_R\ [\text{dBi}] - 10\log_{10}(T_s\ [\text{K}])$$ **Step 6 — Compute Carrier-to-Noise Spectral Density Ratio:** $$\frac{C}{N_0}\ [\text{dB-Hz}] = \text{EIRP} + \frac{G}{T} - L_p - L_a - 10\log_{10}(k)$$ Since $10\log_{10}(k) = -228.6$ dB W/K-Hz, this becomes: $$\frac{C}{N_0} = \text{EIRP} + \frac{G}{T} - L_p - L_a + 228.6$$ **Step 7 — Compute $C/N$ over the operating bandwidth $B$:** $$\frac{C}{N}\ [\text{dB}] = \frac{C}{N_0} - 10\log_{10}(B)$$ ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these formulas — they form 90% of numerical problems.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|----------|---------|-------|-------| | 1 | EIRP | $P_T + G_T - L_{FT}$ | dBW | Transmitter side | | 2 | Free-space path loss | $L_p = 92.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{GHz})$ | dB | d in km, f in GHz | | 3 | Slant range (GEO) | $d = \sqrt{R_E^2 \sin^2\theta + 2hR_E + h^2} - R_E\sin\theta$ | km | $\theta$ = elevation angle | | 4 | Received carrier | $C = \text{EIRP} + G_R - L_p - L_{other}$ | dBW | — | | 5 | Noise spectral density | $N_0 = kT_s$ | W/Hz | $k = 1.38\times10^{-23}$ J/K | | 6 | $C/N_0$ | $C/N_0 = \text{EIRP} + G/T - L_p + 228.6$ | dB-Hz | Master downlink eqn | | 7 | $C/N$ in bandwidth $B$ | $C/N = C/N_0 - 10\log_{10}(B)$ | dB | — | | 8 | Noise figure → Temp | $T_e = T_0(F-1)$ | K | $F$ in linear, $T_0 = 290$ K | | 9 | G/T | $G_R - 10\log_{10}(T_s)$ | dB/K | Receiver figure of merit | | 10 | $C/(N+I)$ (with reuse) | $\dfrac{1}{C/(N+I)} = \dfrac{1}{C/N} + \dfrac{1}{C/I}$ | dB | Standard interference sum | | 11 | Reuse factor | $\eta = 1/N$ | — | N = number of colours | | 12 | Co-channel $C/I$ | $C/I \approx 10\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{G(\theta_0)}{G(\theta_{sep})}\right) - K_{reuse}$ | dB | Beam-roll-off dependent | ## 2.3 Link WITHOUT Frequency Reuse — Detailed Behaviour **System Architecture:** A single global beam antenna on the satellite, fed by a single High-Power Amplifier (HPA, typically TWTA for Ku/Ka or SSPA for L/S). **Noise contributors (downlink):** - Antenna noise (sky + Earth) → $T_{ant}$ - Feed loss → $T_{feed} = T_0(L-1)$ - LNA noise → $T_{e,LNA}$ - Total: $T_s = T_{ant}/L + T_{e,LNA} + T_0(1-1/L)$ **No co-channel interference** → the only impairment is thermal noise. **Design equation:** $$\left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{total} = \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{up} + \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{down}\ \text{(in linear, then dB)}$$ or in dB: $$\left(\frac{C}{N}\right)^{-1} = \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{up}^{-1} + \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{down}^{-1}$$ **Bottleneck:** Since the same single beam carries the **entire** aggregate traffic, the **EIRP per channel** and **G/T per channel** are diluted. This is why single-beam designs are power-starved for high-throughput services. ## 2.4 Link WITH Frequency Reuse — Detailed Behaviour **System Architecture:** Multiple spot beams (typically 2 to 80+). The frequency plan divides the spectrum into $N$ sub-bands ("colours"). Each beam is assigned one colour; the same colour is reused in spatially separated beams. **Two new impairments appear:** **(a) Co-Channel Interference (CCI):** Arises from beams using the same frequency. Level depends on **beam-to-beam isolation** and **antenna side-lobe level**. **(b) Cross-Polarization Interference (XPI)** (in dual-polarization reuse N=2). **Modified Design Equation:** $$\left(\frac{C}{N+I}\right)^{-1} = \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)^{-1} + \left(\frac{C}{I}\right)^{-1}$$ This is the **critical equation** that distinguishes the "with reuse" problem from the "without reuse" one. **Reuse efficiency:** $$\eta = \frac{C_{total}^{reuse}}{N \cdot C_{single}} = \frac{C_{reuse}}{C_{single}}$$ In the **ideal** case (perfect beam isolation, no interference): $\eta = 1$. In reality, $\eta < 1$ due to $C/I$ penalty. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility of These Concepts - **HTS (High Throughput Satellites):** e.g., Ka-SAT (82 spot beams, N=4), ViaSat-3, Jupiter-3 → all built on frequency reuse theory. - **5G NTN (Non-Terrestrial Networks):** Multi-beam LEO constellations (Starlink, OneWeb) use 4-colour or higher reuse. - **Design trade-off:** Higher $N$ → more capacity, but tighter beam pointing, more complex switching matrix, worse $C/I$ from side lobes. - **Real-world rule of thumb:** Minimum co-channel $C/I \geq 12$–$20$ dB is required (depending on modulation/detector). # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From $C/N_0$ to Overall Link $C/N$ (Without Reuse) Starting from the **fundamental link equation** for the downlink: $$\frac{C}{N_0} = \text{EIRP}_{sat} + \left(\frac{G}{T}\right)_{earth} - L_p - L_a - 10\log_{10}(k)$$ **Step 1 — Substituting Boltzmann's constant in dB:** $$10\log_{10}(k) = 10\log_{10}(1.38 \times 10^{-23}) = -228.6\ \text{dB·W/K/Hz}$$ **Step 2 — Therefore:** $$\frac{C}{N_0} = \text{EIRP}_{sat} + \left(\frac{G}{T}\right)_{earth} - L_p - L_a + 228.6\ \text{[dB-Hz]}$$ **Step 3 — Conversion to $C/N$ over a noise bandwidth $B$ (Hz):** The noise power in the band $B$ is $N = kT_sB$, so: $$\frac{C}{N} = \frac{C}{kT_sB} \Rightarrow 10\log_{10}\left(\frac{C}{N}\right) = 10\log_{10}\left(\frac{C}{N_0}\right) - 10\log_{10}(B)$$ **Step 4 — Final form:** $$\boxed{\frac{C}{N}\ [\text{dB}] = \text{EIRP}_{sat}\ [\text{dBW}] + \left(\frac{G}{T}\right)\ [\text{dB/K}] - L_p - L_a + 228.6 - 10\log_{10}(B)}$$ **Step 5 — Total (up + down) $C/N$ for a non-regenerative transponder:** Since uplink noise $N_u$ and downlink noise $N_d$ add independently, in **linear units**: $$\left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{total} = \left[\left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{u}^{-1} + \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{d}^{-1}\right]^{-1}$$ This is analogous to adding parallel conductances in electrical circuits. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — $C/(N+I)$ for Frequency-Reuse System In a multi-beam satellite, each desired carrier $C$ is corrupted by: - Thermal noise $N$ (as before) - Co-channel interference $I$ from $k$ co-channel beams **Step 1 — Total interference power from $k$ interfering beams, each at level $I_j$:** $$I = \sum_{j=1}^{k} I_j$$ If all $k$ interferers are equal (symmetric reuse pattern, e.g., 7-cell cluster with 6 co-channel neighbours): $$I = k \cdot I_{each} = k \cdot \frac{C}{(C/I)_{each}}$$ **Step 2 — Express the ratio in inverse form (works with dB):** $$\frac{1}{(C/(N+I))} = \frac{N+I}{C} = \frac{N}{C} + \frac{I}{C} = \frac{1}{C/N} + \frac{1}{C/I}$$ **Step 3 — Converting to dB:** $$\boxed{\left[\frac{C}{N+I}\right]_{dB} = -10\log_{10}\left(10^{-(C/N)/10} + 10^{-(C/I)/10}\right)}$$ **Step 4 — Limiting cases:** - If $C/I \gg C/N$ (interference much weaker than noise): $C/(N+I) \approx C/N$ → reuse behaves like no-reuse. - If $C/N \gg C/I$ (very high thermal SNR, interference-limited): $C/(N+I) \approx C/I$ → the link is **interference-limited** (typical of HTS with high G/T and small beams). ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Co-Channel Interference $C/I$ from Beam Pattern The antenna gain of a beam pointed at angle $\theta_0$ when observed at an angle $\theta$ is $G(\theta)$. The co-channel interference ratio is: $$\frac{C}{I} = \frac{P_T \cdot G_{max}^2 / L_p}{P_T \cdot G(\theta_{sep})^2 / L_p} = \left[\frac{G_{max}}{G(\theta_{sep})}\right]^2$$ where $\theta_{sep}$ = angular separation between the boresight of the desired beam and the centre of the interfering co-channel beam. In dB: $$\frac{C}{I}\ [\text{dB}] = 2 \cdot \left[G_{max} - G(\theta_{sep})\right]$$ For a circular aperture with parabolic illumination, the side-lobe level gives $C/I$ in the range **15–30 dB**. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Capacity Gain from Frequency Reuse **Without reuse (single beam, total bandwidth $B$):** $$C_1 = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}_{beam})$$ **With $N$-fold frequency reuse (each beam has bandwidth $B/N$, but there are $N$ beams reusing the same spectrum):** $$C_{reuse} = N \cdot \frac{B}{N} \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}_{beam}) = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}_{beam}) = C_1$$ **Wait — this is suspicious!** Let me re-derive carefully: If total allocated spectrum is $B_{total}$ and we divide it into $N$ sub-bands of $B_{total}/N$ each, then **per-beam bandwidth** = $B_{total}/N$. - **Without reuse:** 1 beam × $B_{total}$ bandwidth → $C_1 = B_{total}\log_2(1+\text{SNR})$. - **With reuse (N colours):** N beams, each using $B_{total}/N$ → per-beam capacity $= (B_{total}/N)\log_2(1+\text{SNR})$. Total capacity: $N \cdot (B_{total}/N) \log_2(1+\text{SNR}) = B_{total}\log_2(1+\text{SNR})$. **This equals the no-reuse case! So where is the gain?** The gain is **spatial** — frequency reuse concentrates **EIRP** and **bandwidth** into a smaller area, increasing **spectral efficiency per unit area** (bps/Hz/km²) and **per-user data rate** because each user sees a higher SNR within their narrow beam. So the gain is in **per-beam EIRP** (concentrated) and **per-user capacity**, not raw spectrum. The *true* gain metric is: $$\text{Capacity Density}\ [bps/Hz/km^2] = \frac{N \cdot C_{beam}}{B_{total} \cdot A_{coverage}}$$ Compared to the single-beam case, the per-coverage-area capacity increases by a factor $\approx N$ (assuming equal area beams). ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Slant Range vs. Elevation Angle (Geometric) For an Earth station at elevation angle $\theta$ looking at a GEO satellite at altitude $h$: $$d = \sqrt{R_E^2 \sin^2\theta + 2hR_E + h^2} - R_E\sin\theta$$ **Derivation:** The geometry: $R_E$ = Earth radius, $h$ = GEO altitude, $\theta$ = elevation. Distance from Earth's centre to satellite is $R_E + h$. The angle at Earth's centre between the station and the sub-satellite point is $\beta$ where $\cos\beta = R_E\sin\theta / (R_E + h) \cdot ... $ — let's use the law of cosines: $$(R_E + h)^2 = R_E^2 + d^2 - 2 R_E d \cos(90° + \theta)$$ $$\Rightarrow d^2 + 2 R_E d \sin\theta - (2 R_E h + h^2) = 0$$ Solving the quadratic for $d > 0$: $$\boxed{d = -R_E \sin\theta + \sqrt{R_E^2 \sin^2\theta + 2 R_E h + h^2}}$$ **Limiting cases:** - $\theta = 0°$ (horizon): $d = \sqrt{2R_E h + h^2} \approx 41{,}168$ km. - $\theta = 90°$ (zenith): $d = h = 35{,}786$ km. ## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Link WITHOUT Reuse > **Problem:** GEO satellite at 6/4 GHz. $P_T = 10$ W (satellite TWTA), $G_T = 40$ dBi (satellite antenna), feeder loss = 1 dB. Earth station $G_R = 50$ dBi, $T_s = 150$ K. Bandwidth $B = 36$ MHz. Distance $d = 40{,}000$ km. Additional losses = 0. **Step 1 — EIRP:** $$P_T = 10\log_{10}(10) = 10\ \text{dBW}$$ $$\text{EIRP} = 10 + 40 - 1 = 49\ \text{dBW}$$ **Step 2 — Path loss at 4 GHz:** $$L_p = 92.45 + 20\log_{10}(40000) + 20\log_{10}(4)$$ $$= 92.45 + 92.04 + 12.04 = 196.53\ \text{dB}$$ **Step 3 — G/T:** $$G/T = 50 - 10\log_{10}(150) = 50 - 21.76 = 28.24\ \text{dB/K}$$ **Step 4 — $C/N_0$:** $$C/N_0 = 49 + 28.24 - 196.53 + 228.6 = 109.31\ \text{dB-Hz}$$ **Step 5 — $C/N$ in 36 MHz:** $$C/N = 109.31 - 10\log_{10}(36 \times 10^6) = 109.31 - 75.56 = 33.75\ \text{dB}$$ ✅ **No co-channel interference term needed** → this is the "without reuse" answer. ## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Link WITH Frequency Reuse (N = 4) > **Same problem as above, but now the satellite uses a 4-colour frequency reuse pattern. The co-channel $C/I$ (from the 6 nearest co-channel beams) is $C/I = 18$ dB. Compute the effective $C/(N+I)$.** **Step 1 — From Example 1:** $C/N = 33.75$ dB (linear: $10^{3.375} = 2371$). **Step 2 — Convert $C/I$ to linear:** $C/I = 18$ dB → linear $= 10^{1.8} = 63.1$. **Step 3 — Apply the inverse-addition formula:** $$\frac{1}{C/(N+I)} = \frac{1}{2371} + \frac{1}{63.1}$$ $$= 0.000422 + 0.01585 = 0.01627$$ **Step 4 — Invert:** $$C/(N+I) = 61.45\ \text{(linear)} = 17.88\ \text{dB}$$ **Step 5 — Interpretation:** Without reuse, $C/N = 33.75$ dB. With 4-colour reuse and the given $C/I = 18$ dB, the **link is now interference-limited**, and the effective $C/(N+I)$ drops to 17.88 dB — a **15.87 dB penalty**! The capacity gain (4×) comes at the cost of SNR per channel. ✅ **This is why HTS designs must engineer very low side-lobe antennas** to keep $C/I$ ≥ 20 dB. ## 3.8 Comparative Summary Table | Parameter | Without Reuse | With Reuse (N = 4) | |-----------|---------------|-------------------| | Number of beams | 1 | 4 | | Per-beam bandwidth | $B_{total}$ | $B_{total}/4$ | | Aggregate capacity | 1× (baseline) | ~4× (if interference controlled) | | $C/N$ | 33.75 dB (Ex. 1) | Same thermal: 33.75 dB | | $C/I$ | $\infty$ (no interferer) | 18 dB (typical) | | $C/(N+I)$ | 33.75 dB | **17.88 dB** (interference-limited) | | EIRP/beam | Diluted | Concentrated (higher) | | Receiver complexity | Low | High (multi-beam gateway) | # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Satellite Link (Without Reuse) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Earth Station TX
Modulator + HPA] -->|Uplink f1| B[Satellite Antenna
Single Global Beam] B -->|Downlink f2| C[Earth Station RX
LNA + Demod] B --> D[Transponder
TWTA + BPF] C --> E[BER / C/N
Measurement] style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc style B fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600 style C fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc style D fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745 style E fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545 ``` > The block diagram shows a **single-beam satellite link** with no frequency reuse. The same antenna and transponder serves the entire footprint; all users share the same uplink and downlink frequencies via FDMA/TDMA. ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Multi-Beam Link With Frequency Reuse ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BEAMS[Satellite Antenna System - N Spot Beams] B1[Beam 1
Colour A] B2[Beam 2
Colour B] B3[Beam 3
Colour C] B4[Beam 4
Colour D] B1 -->|same freq A| I1[Co-Channel
Interference] B2 -->|same freq B| I2[Co-Channel
Interference] B3 -->|same freq C| I3[Co-Channel
Interference] B4 -->|same freq D| I4[Co-Channel
Interference] end GS1[Gateway 1
in Beam 1] -->|Uplink A| B1 GS2[Gateway 2
in Beam 2] -->|Uplink B| B2 GS3[Gateway 3
in Beam 3] -->|Uplink C| B3 GS4[Gateway 4
in Beam 4] -->|Uplink D| B4 B1 --> RX1[User in Beam 1
+ CCI] B2 --> RX2[User in Beam 2
+ CCI] B3 --> RX3[User in Beam 3
+ CCI] B4 --> RX4[User in Beam 4
+ CCI] I1 -.-> RX1 I2 -.-> RX2 I3 -.-> RX3 I4 -.-> RX4 style BEAMS fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 style I1 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545 style I2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545 style I3 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545 style I4 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545 ``` > The diagram shows **4 spot beams** with 4-colour frequency reuse. The dashed lines represent **co-channel interference** from neighbouring beams using the same frequency. The user terminal in each beam receives the desired signal plus CCI from other beams of the same colour. ## 4.3 Frequency Reuse Pattern (4-Colour Cluster) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CLUSTER[4-Colour Frequency Reuse Cluster] C1[Cell 1
Colour A] C2[Cell 2
Colour B] C3[Cell 3
Colour C] C4[Cell 4
Colour D] end C1 -.A.-> C1 C2 -.B.-> C2 C3 -.C.-> C3 C4 -.D.-> C4 style C1 fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#0066cc style C2 fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600 style C3 fill:#d9b3ff,stroke:#6600cc style C4 fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#009900 ``` > A 4-colour cluster assigns one frequency band to each cell, then the pattern repeats. Cells with the same colour (e.g., Cell 1 in adjacent clusters) are spatially separated to keep $C/I$ above threshold. ## 4.4 Decision Flow — Reuse vs. No Reuse ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Satellite Link Design] --> Q1{Bandwidth
Demand > Available?} Q1 -->|No| NR[Without Reuse
Single Global Beam] Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Antenna
Size OK?} Q2 -->|No| NR Q2 -->|Yes| WR[With Reuse
Multi-Beam] WR --> Q3{Side-lobe
C/I > 15 dB?} Q3 -->|No| T1[Use N=3 or 7
Larger separation] Q3 -->|Yes| T2[Proceed with N=4
or 7-colour reuse] NR --> DONE[Design Complete] T1 --> DONE T2 --> DONE style NR fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc style WR fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745 style DONE fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 ``` > This flow helps determine whether frequency reuse is needed based on **bandwidth demand**, **antenna constraints**, and **interference tolerance**. # 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **"What is meant by frequency reuse in satellite communication? Why is it employed?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Frequency reuse is a technique in which the **same frequency band is used in spatially separated beams** (or with orthogonal polarizations) to increase the total system capacity without increasing the allocated spectrum. The system divides the coverage area into multiple beams and assigns the same frequency to non-adjacent beams. It is employed because: 1. The available RF spectrum is **limited and expensive**. 2. Single-beam satellites are **bandwidth-limited and power-limited**. 3. By reusing frequencies in $N$ beams, the **system capacity is multiplied by $N$**. 4. Modern High Throughput Satellites (HTS) require this technique to deliver terabits/s of capacity. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **"List the main differences between a satellite link with frequency reuse and one without frequency reuse."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Without Reuse | With Reuse | |--------|---------------|------------| | Number of beams | One (global/hemi beam) | Multiple spot beams ($N$ colours) | | Bandwidth per beam | Full $B$ | $B/N$ | | Co-channel interference | None | Yes — limits link via $C/I$ | | System capacity | 1× | $N$× (interference-limited) | | EIRP concentration | Diluted over whole footprint | Concentrated per spot beam | | Antenna complexity | Simple reflector | Multi-feed or phased array | --- ## 📝 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to convert the inverse-addition formula correctly: students often add dB values directly. ❌ Always convert to linear, add reciprocals, then convert back to dB. > - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing $C/N$ and $C/N_0$. $C/N$ is dimensionless ratio in band $B$; $C/N_0$ is in dB-Hz. Conversion: $C/N = C/N_0 - 10\log_{10}(B)$. > - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting the +228.6 dB in the $C/N_0$ equation. This corresponds to Boltzmann's constant in dB. > - **Common Mistake #4:** Using the wrong slant range (using $h = 35{,}786$ km directly even when elevation is not 90°). Always compute $d$ from the geometry equation. > - **Common Mistake #5:** Mixing up uplink vs. downlink $C/N$ — both contribute, and total $C/N$ uses inverse addition (not simple addition in dB). --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Model Question Paper]** **(a)** A GEO satellite operates at 6/4 GHz with a single global beam (no frequency reuse). The satellite HPA delivers $P_T = 20$ W into a feeder loss of $L_{FT} = 1.5$ dB. The satellite antenna gain is $G_T = 38$ dBi. The slant range is $d = 40{,}000$ km. The Earth station has $G_R = 52$ dBi and a system noise temperature $T_s = 120$ K. The receiver operates over a noise bandwidth $B = 30$ MHz. Additional atmospheric and mispointing losses total 1.5 dB. **Compute:** (i) The satellite EIRP in dBW. **(2 marks)** (ii) The free-space path loss at 4 GHz. **(2 marks)** (iii) The Earth station $G/T$ in dB/K. **(2 marks)** (iv) The carrier-to-noise ratio $C/N$ in dB over the 30 MHz bandwidth. **(3 marks)** **(b)** If the same satellite is redesigned to use a 4-colour frequency reuse with 4 spot beams, the co-channel interference ratio from the neighbouring co-channel beams is measured as $C/I = 16$ dB. **(5 marks)** **Compute the new effective $C/(N+I)$ in dB. Comment on whether the link is now noise-limited or interference-limited.** --- ### **Model Solution — Question A** #### **Part (a) — Without Frequency Reuse** **(i) Satellite EIRP:** $$P_T = 10\log_{10}(20) = 13.01\ \text{dBW}$$ $$\text{EIRP} = P_T + G_T - L_{FT} = 13.01 + 38 - 1.5 = 49.51\ \text{dBW}$$ **(ii) Free-space path loss at $f = 4$ GHz, $d = 40{,}000$ km:** $$L_p = 92.45 + 20\log_{10}(40000) + 20\log_{10}(4)$$ $$= 92.45 + 20(4.602) + 20(0.602)$$ $$= 92.45 + 92.04 + 12.04 = 196.53\ \text{dB}$$ **(iii) G/T of Earth Station:** $$\frac{G}{T} = 52 - 10\log_{10}(120) = 52 - 20.79 = 31.21\ \text{dB/K}$$ **(iv) $C/N_0$ and $C/N$:** $$\frac{C}{N_0} = \text{EIRP} + \frac{G}{T} - L_p - L_a + 228.6$$ $$= 49.51 + 31.21 - 196.53 - 1.5 + 228.6 = 111.29\ \text{dB-Hz}$$ $$\frac{C}{N} = \frac{C}{N_0} - 10\log_{10}(B)$$ $$= 111.29 - 10\log_{10}(30 \times 10^6)$$ $$= 111.29 - 74.77 = 36.52\ \text{dB}$$ #### **Part (b) — With 4-Colour Frequency Reuse** **Step 1 — Convert $C/N$ and $C/I$ to linear:** $$C/N = 36.52\ \text{dB} \Rightarrow \text{linear} = 10^{3.652} = 4487$$ $$C/I = 16\ \text{dB} \Rightarrow \text{linear} = 10^{1.6} = 39.81$$ **Step 2 — Apply inverse-addition formula:** $$\frac{1}{C/(N+I)} = \frac{1}{4487} + \frac{1}{39.81}$$ $$= 0.0002229 + 0.02512 = 0.02534$$ $$C/(N+I) = 39.47\ \text{(linear)} = 15.96\ \text{dB}$$ **Step 3 — Comment:** The $C/N$ was 36.52 dB (very high), and $C/I = 16$ dB is much smaller. Therefore, the link is now **interference-limited**, not noise-limited. The effective $C/(N+I)$ is dominated by the interference term.
