The capacity gain (4×) from frequency reuse has been achieved at the cost of a 20.56 dB drop in effective SNR per channel. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Model Question Paper]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **system design of a satellite communication link without frequency reuse**. Derive the expression for the carrier-to-noise ratio $C/N$ at the receiver in terms of EIRP, $G/T$, path loss, and bandwidth. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **concept of frequency reuse** in multi-beam satellites. Derive the expression for $C/(N+I)$ when co-channel interference is present. A satellite link has $C/N = 30$ dB and $C/I = 20$ dB. Compute the effective $C/(N+I)$ and determine whether the link is noise-limited or interference-limited. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution — Question B** #### **Part (a) — Link Design Without Frequency Reuse** **Block Diagram (textual description):** The system consists of: 1. **Earth Station Transmitter:** Modulator → Up-converter → HPA → Earth Station Antenna (uplink $f_1$). 2. **Satellite Transponder:** Receiving antenna → LNA → Down-converter → TWTA → Transmitting antenna (downlink $f_2$). 3. **Earth Station Receiver:** Receiving antenna → LNA → Down-converter → Demodulator → BER measurement. **Derivation of $C/N$:** The carrier power at the receiver input is: $$C = \frac{\text{EIRP}_{sat} \cdot G_R}{L_p \cdot L_a}$$ In dB: $$C\ [\text{dBW}] = \text{EIRP}_{sat} + G_R - L_p - L_a$$ The noise power in bandwidth $B$ is: $$N = kT_sB$$ Therefore: $$\frac{C}{N} = \frac{\text{EIRP}_{sat} \cdot G_R}{kT_sB \cdot L_p \cdot L_a}$$ In dB: $$\boxed{\frac{C}{N} = \text{EIRP}_{sat} + \left(\frac{G}{T}\right) - L_p - L_a - 10\log_{10}(k) - 10\log_{10}(B)}$$ Substituting $10\log_{10}(k) = -228.6$: $$\frac{C}{N}\ [\text{dB}] = \text{EIRP} + \frac{G}{T} - L_p - L_a + 228.6 - 10\log_{10}(B)$$ #### **Part (b) — Frequency Reuse and Effective $C/(N+I)$** **Concept of Frequency Reuse:** In multi-beam satellite systems, the coverage area is divided into multiple spot beams. The available frequency band is divided into $N$ sub-bands ("colours"). Each beam is assigned one sub-band, and the same sub-band is reused in spatially separated beams. This multiplies the system capacity by $N$ but introduces co-channel interference from beams using the same frequency. **Derivation of $C/(N+I)$:** The total interference from $k$ co-channel beams: $$I = \sum_{j=1}^{k} I_j$$ The total noise-plus-interference power: $$N + I$$ The inverse of the effective ratio: $$\frac{1}{C/(N+I)} = \frac{N+I}{C} = \frac{1}{C/N} + \frac{1}{C/I}$$ In dB: $$\left[\frac{C}{N+I}\right]_{dB} = -10\log_{10}\left(10^{-(C/N)/10} + 10^{-(C/I)/10}\right)$$ **Numerical Computation:** Given: $C/N = 30$ dB, $C/I = 20$ dB. **Step 1 — Convert to linear:** $$C/N = 10^3 = 1000$$ $$C/I = 10^2 = 100$$ **Step 2 — Apply inverse addition:** $$\frac{1}{C/(N+I)} = \frac{1}{1000} + \frac{1}{100} = 0.001 + 0.01 = 0.011$$ $$C/(N+I) = \frac{1}{0.011} = 90.91\ \text{(linear)} = 19.59\ \text{dB}$$ **Step 3 — Determine Limiting Factor:** - $C/N = 30$ dB (high) - $C/I = 20$ dB (lower) - $C/(N+I) = 19.59$ dB ≈ $C/I - 0.4$ dB (close to $C/I$) Since $C/(N+I) \approx C/I$ (within 0.4 dB), the link is **interference-limited**. The thermal noise is negligible compared to the co-channel interference. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** - ✅ **Link without reuse** = single global beam, no CCI, simpler design, capacity-limited. - ✅ **Link with reuse** = multi-beam, $N$-colour frequency plan, capacity multiplied by $N$, but introduces CCI. - ✅ **Master equation:** $C/N_0 = \text{EIRP} + G/T - L_p + 228.6$ (dB-Hz) — applies to both cases. - ✅ **$C/N = C/N_0 - 10\log_{10}(B)$** — convert spectral density to in-band ratio. - ✅ **With reuse, replace $C/N$ by $C/(N+I)$** using inverse addition in linear units. - ✅ **Inverse addition rule:** $1/(C/(N+I)) = 1/(C/N) + 1/(C/I)$ — **never add dB values directly**. - ✅ **$C/I$** depends on antenna side-lobe level and beam separation angle; typical values 15–25 dB. - ✅ **Reuse factor** $\eta = 1/N$; capacity density $\propto N$ (per unit area). - ✅ **Path loss formula:** $L_p = 92.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{GHz})$ for $d$ in km, $f$ in GHz. - ✅ **Slant range:** $d = -R_E\sin\theta + \sqrt{R_E^2\sin^2\theta + 2R_Eh + h^2}$. - ✅ **$G/T$ is the receiver figure of merit** — higher is better. - ✅ **Boltzmann's constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, or $-228.6$ dB W/K/Hz. - ✅ **GEO altitude:** $h = 35{,}786$ km; typical slant range 36,000–41,000 km. - ✅ **HTS examples:** Ka-SAT (N=4, 82 beams), ViaSat-3, Jupiter-3 — all use frequency reuse. - ✅ **Limiting case check:** If $C/I \gg C/N$ → noise-limited; if $C/N \gg C/I$ → interference-limited. - ✅ **Total link $C/N$ (up + down):** Inverse addition of uplink and downlink $C/N$ values.

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of RADAR

**RADAR** is an acronym for **RAdio Detection And Ranging**. It is an electromagnetic system designed for the detection, location, velocity measurement, and recognition of distant objects (targets) by transmitting electromagnetic (EM) energy into space and analyzing the echo (reflected/scattered) signal returned from the target.

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**  
> *Radar is a device that uses electromagnetic waves (typically in the microwave frequency band) to detect the presence, position, velocity, and nature of remote objects by illuminating them with a transmitted signal and receiving/processing the reflected echo.*

The basic operating principle relies on three measurable properties of the reflected signal:
1. **Time Delay (τ)** → gives the **Range (R)** of the target
2. **Frequency Shift (f_d)** → gives the **Radial Velocity (v_r)** via Doppler effect
3. **Amplitude & Phase** → gives the **Target Signature / Radar Cross Section (σ)**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bat & the Lighthouse"

Imagine you are standing in a dark, foggy field holding a flashlight (transmitter) and a mirror (receiver). You flash the light (**transmit a pulse**), wait for the echo, and listen for it to bounce back. The time it takes to hear the echo tells you **how far** the obstacle is. This is *exactly* how a bat navigates — it screams in ultrasonic frequencies and listens to the returning echoes to hunt insects in total darkness.

A radar does this same job, but instead of light or sound, it uses **invisible microwave radio waves** (typically **1 GHz to 100 GHz**) that travel at the speed of light.

> [!NOTE] 
> **Key Engineering Insight:**  
> Because radio waves travel at $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, radar can measure distances with extreme precision. A round-trip time of just **1 microsecond (1 μs)** corresponds to a target range of **150 meters** — enabling centimeter-level accuracy in modern pulsed radars.

## 1.3 Historical Milestones

| Year | Milestone | Significance |
|------|----------|------------|
| 1904 | Hülsmeyer — Telemobiloskop | First device to detect ships using radio waves |
| 1935 | Sir Robert Watson-Watt | Demonstrated first practical radar in UK (Daventry Experiment) |
| 1940 | MIT Radiation Laboratory | Mass production of cavity magnetron → microwave radar |
| 1970s | Phased Array Radars | Electronic beam steering introduced |
| 1990s–Present | AESA / SAR Radars | Active Electronically Scanned Arrays & Synthetic Aperture Radar |

## 1.4 Classification of Radar Systems (High-Level Overview)

Radar systems are broadly classified based on operational parameters:

- **Based on Waveform:**
  - **Pulsed Radar** (most common — transmits short pulses, listens in between)
  - **Continuous Wave (CW) Radar** (transmits continuously, uses Doppler for velocity)

- **Based on Function:**
  - **Primary Radar** (passive reflection from target — like aircraft detection)
  - **Secondary Radar** (target carries a transponder, e.g., ATC mode-S)

- **Based on Deployment:**
  - **Ground-Based Radar** (Surveillance, Tracking)
  - **Airborne Radar** (Weather, SAR)
  - **Spaceborne Radar** (Satellite remote sensing)
  - **Shipborne / Naval Radar**

- **Based on Application:**
  - Military (Weapon control, Missile guidance, Surveillance)
  - Civilian (Air Traffic Control, Weather, Speed enforcement)
  - Scientific (Astronomy, Remote Sensing, Planetary mapping)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Basic Principle of Operation — The "Why" and the "How"

A radar's operation can be broken into a **five-step logical chain**:

1. **Signal Generation & Transmission** — A high-power, highly stable RF pulse is generated in the transmitter (e.g., via a magnetron or klystron) and routed through a **Duplexer** to the antenna.
2. **Illumination of Target** — The directional antenna focuses the EM energy into a narrow beam aimed at the target region.
3. **Scattering / Reflection** — The target intercepts a small fraction of the incident energy and re-radiates (scatters) it in various directions, including back toward the radar.
4. **Echo Reception** — The same (or a separate) antenna captures the extremely weak returned echo; the Duplexer switches the antenna to the receiver.
5. **Signal Processing & Display** — The receiver amplifies, filters, and processes the echo to extract range, velocity, and angular information, displaying it on a PPI (Plan Position Indicator) or modern digital scope.

> [!NOTE] 
> **Why a Duplexer?**  
> The transmit pulse may be **megawatts** in power, while the received echo may be **picowatts** — a ratio of $10^{18}$! A duplexer (usually a gas-filled TR cell or ferrite circulator) protects the sensitive receiver and routes the signals correctly.

## 2.2 The Simple Radar Range Equation — Conceptual Derivation

The **radar range equation** is the foundational equation of radar engineering. It relates the **received echo power (P_r)** to the **transmitted power (P_t)**, antenna parameters, target distance, and target properties.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

- **Step 1 — Power Density at Target:**  
  If a transmitter radiates $P_t$ watts isotropically (uniformly in all directions), the power density at distance $R$ is:
  $$\text{PD}_{\text{iso}} = \dfrac{P_t}{4\pi R^2}$$

  *Why $4\pi R^2$?* Because power spreads uniformly over the surface of a sphere of radius $R$.

- **Step 2 — Actual Power Density (with Directional Antenna):**  
  A real radar antenna has gain $G$ and concentrates the energy into a narrow beam. The power density becomes:
  $$\text{PD}_{\text{at target}} = \dfrac{P_t \, G}{4\pi R^2}$$

- **Step 3 — Power Intercepted by the Target:**  
  The target "captures" a fraction of this power proportional to its **Radar Cross Section (σ)**, measured in **square meters (m²)**:
  $$P_{\text{captured}} = \text{PD}_{\text{at target}} \times \sigma = \dfrac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{4\pi R^2}$$

- **Step 4 — Scattered Power Density Back at the Radar Antenna:**  
  The target re-radiates the captured power isotropically (worst-case assumption). At the radar's location, this re-radiated power is spread over a sphere of radius $R$:
  $$\text{PD}_{\text{at radar}} = \dfrac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4\pi R^2)(4\pi R^2)}$$

- **Step 5 — Power Captured by the Receiving Antenna:**  
  The receiving antenna has an effective aperture $A_e$, so the received power is:
  $$P_r = \dfrac{P_t \, G \, \sigma \, A_e}{(4\pi)^2 \, R^4}$$

This is the **monostatic, simple radar range equation**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Speed of light** | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | EM wave propagation |
| 2 | **Range from time delay** | $R = \dfrac{c \, \tau}{2}$ | m (factor of 2 for round trip) |
| 3 | **Range resolution** | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2B}$ | m, where $B$ = bandwidth (Hz) |
| 4 | **Doppler frequency shift** | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r f_0}{c}$ | Hz, $v_r$ = radial velocity (m/s) |
| 5 | **Antenna effective aperture** | $A_e = \dfrac{\lambda^2 \, G}{4\pi}$ | m², $G$ = antenna gain (linear) |
| 6 | **Beamwidth (approx.)** | $\theta \approx \dfrac{70 \lambda}{D}$ | degrees, $D$ = antenna diameter (m) |
| 7 | **Maximum unambiguous range** | $R_{\text{unamb}} = \dfrac{c \, T_p}{2}$ | m, $T_p$ = PRI (s) |
| 8 | **Simple Radar Range Eq.** | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$ | W (monostatic, $G = G_t = G_r$) |
| 9 | **Pulse Repetition Frequency** | $\text{PRF} = \dfrac{1}{T_p}$ | Hz |
| 10 | **Duty cycle** | $d = \tau \, \times \, \text{PRF}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 11 | **Average transmitted power** | $P_{\text{av}} = P_t \times d$ | W |
| 12 | **Noise power (thermal)** | $N = k T_0 B \, F$ | W, $k$ = Boltzmann's const. = $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K |

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **$R^4$ Dependency — The "Fourth Power Law":**  
> The received power drops as the **fourth power of range**. This is the single most important fact in radar engineering. To double the detection range, you must increase the transmitted power by a factor of **16** (assuming all other parameters are constant). This drives the use of high-power transmitters and high-gain antennas in long-range radar systems.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The radar principle is the **backbone** of:
- **Air Traffic Control (ATC)** — primary surveillance radar at airports
- **Defense & Missile Systems** — phased array fire-control radars
- **Weather Forecasting** — Doppler weather radar (e.g., India's DWR network)
- **Automotive Safety** — adaptive cruise control, collision avoidance
- **Remote Sensing** — Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) on satellites (RISAT, Sentinel-1)
- **Speed Enforcement** — police Doppler radars
- **Space Debris Tracking** — Space Fence systems
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Radar Range Equation

We will now derive the **simple (free-space) radar range equation** rigorously, step by step. This is a **KTU-favorite 14-mark derivation**.

### Starting Assumptions:
- Monostatic radar (transmitter and receiver share the same antenna)
- Point target with RCS = $\sigma$
- Free-space propagation (no atmospheric attenuation, clutter, or noise effects — added later)
- Antenna gains: $G_t = G_r = G$ (same antenna used for Tx and Rx)

---

### **Derivation — Step 1: Power Transmitted**

Let the radar transmit a peak power $P_t$ (in watts) through an antenna of gain $G$ (linear, dimensionless). 

The radiated power is concentrated in a beam. At a distance $R$ from the antenna, this power is spread over the surface of a sphere of area $4\pi R^2$.

Therefore, the **power density** (W/m²) at the target's location is:

$$\text{PD}_{\text{target}} = \dfrac{P_t \, G}{4\pi R^{2}} \quad \text{... (Eq. 1)}$$

*Reasoning:* The factor $4\pi R^2$ represents the spherical surface area over which the transmitted power is distributed. The gain $G$ amplifies the density in the target's direction compared to an isotropic radiator.

---

### **Derivation — Step 2: Power Intercepted by the Target**

A target is characterized by its **radar cross section (RCS), $\sigma$**, defined as the equivalent area that captures and re-radiates the incident power. The product of power density and RCS gives the power **intercepted and scattered** by the target:

$$P_{\text{scattered}} = \text{PD}_{\text{target}} \times \sigma = \dfrac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{4\pi R^{2}} \quad \text{... (Eq. 2)}$$

*Reasoning:* RCS $\sigma$ has units of m² and represents the "size" of the target as seen by the radar. A fighter aircraft may have $\sigma \approx 1 \text{ m}^2$, a large ship $\sigma \approx 10^4 \text{ m}^2$, and a small drone $\sigma \approx 0.01 \text{ m}^2$.

---

### **Derivation — Step 3: Power Density of Echo Back at the Radar**

The scattered power is re-radiated (assumed isotropically for worst-case analysis). At the radar, located at distance $R$, this re-radiated power is again spread over a sphere of area $4\pi R^2$:

$$\text{PD}_{\text{radar}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{scattered}}}{4\pi R^{2}} = \dfrac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4\pi R^{2})(4\pi R^{2})} \quad \text{... (Eq. 3)}$$

*Reasoning:* The $4\pi R^2$ in the denominator represents the **second** spreading loss on the return path. Hence the appearance of $R^4$ in the final expression.

---

### **Derivation — Step 4: Power Captured by the Receiving Antenna**

The radar antenna, with effective aperture $A_e$ (in m²), captures a portion of the echo:

$$P_r = \text{PD}_{\text{radar}} \times A_e = \dfrac{P_t \, G \, \sigma \, A_e}{(4\pi)^{2} \, R^{4}} \quad \text{... (Eq. 4)}$$

This is the **general form of the radar range equation** for monostatic radar.

---

### **Derivation — Step 5: Substitution Using Antenna Aperture Relation**

The effective aperture $A_e$ of an antenna is related to its gain $G$ and wavelength $\lambda$ by:

$$A_e = \dfrac{\lambda^{2} \, G}{4\pi} \quad \text{... (Eq. 5)}$$

*Reasoning:* A high-gain antenna has a large physical aperture; a small wavelength (high frequency) also increases gain for a given physical size.

Substituting Eq. (5) into Eq. (4):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r &= \dfrac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{2} \, R^{4}} \times \dfrac{\lambda^{2} \, G}{4\pi} \\
    &= \dfrac{P_t \, G^{2} \, \lambda^{2} \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \, R^{4}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\,P_r = \dfrac{P_t \, G^{2} \, \lambda^{2} \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \, R^{4}}\,}$$

This is the **famous Simple Radar Range Equation**.

---

### **Derivation — Step 6: Solving for Maximum Range R_max**

If the minimum detectable signal (MDS) at the receiver input is $S_{i,\min}$, then the **maximum detection range** is found by setting $P_r = S_{i,\min}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{i,\min} &= \dfrac{P_t \, G^{2} \, \lambda^{2} \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \, R_{\max}^{4}} \\
R_{\max}^{4} &= \dfrac{P_t \, G^{2} \, \lambda^{2} \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \, S_{i,\min}} \\
R_{\max} &= \left[ \dfrac{P_t \, G^{2} \, \lambda^{2} \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \, S_{i,\min}} \right]^{1/4}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\,R_{\max} = \left[ \dfrac{P_t \, G^{2} \, \lambda^{2} \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \, S_{i,\min}} \right]^{1/4}\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A pulsed radar operates at $f_0 = 10$ GHz with peak power $P_t = 1$ MW, antenna gain $G = 30$ dB, target RCS $\sigma = 1$ m², and minimum detectable signal $S_{i,\min} = 10^{-13}$ W. Find the maximum detection range $R_{\max}$.

### Solution:

**Step A — Convert antenna gain from dB to linear:**
$$G_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(G) \Rightarrow G = 10^{30/10} = 1000$$

**Step B — Compute wavelength:**
$$\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f_0} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8}}{10 \times 10^{9}} = 0.03 \text{ m}$$

**Step C — Substitute into the range equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\max}^{4} &= \dfrac{(10^{6}) \times (1000)^{2} \times (0.03)^{2} \times (1)}{(4\pi)^{3} \times (10^{-13})} \\
&= \dfrac{10^{6} \times 10^{6} \times 9 \times 10^{-4} \times 1}{1984.4 \times 10^{-13}} \\
&= \dfrac{9 \times 10^{8}}{1.9844 \times 10^{-10}} \\
&= 4.535 \times 10^{18} \text{ m}^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step D — Take the fourth root:**
$$R_{\max} = (4.535 \times 10^{18})^{1/4} \approx 8.22 \times 10^{4} \text{ m} \approx 82.2 \text{ km}$$

### Analytical Observations:

| Observation | Implication |
|------------|------------|
| **$R^4$ dependence** | Range is highly sensitive to all parameters |
| **Doubling $P_t$** | Increases $R_{\max}$ by a factor of $2^{1/4} \approx 1.19$ (~19%) |
| **Doubling $G$** | Same effect (factor of 1.19) |
| **Doubling $\sigma$** | Same effect (factor of 1.19) |
| **Reducing $S_{i,\min}$ by 16×** | Doubles $R_{\max}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Basic Pulsed Radar Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **complete signal flow** in a typical monostatic pulsed radar system. Each block is annotated with its primary engineering function.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Timing and Trigger Generator] --> A2[Modulator and Pulse Shaper]
    A2 --> A3[High Power RF Transmitter Magnetron or Klystron]
    A3 --> A4[Duplexer TR Cell and ATR]
    A4 --> A5[Antenna Parabolic Dish with Feed]
    A5 --> A6[Target in Free Space]
    A6 --> A5
    A5 --> A4
    A4 --> A7[Low Noise RF Amplifier LNA]
    A7 --> A8[Mixer and Local Oscillator]
    A8 --> A9[IF Amplifier and Matched Filter]
    A9 --> A10[Signal Processor and Detector]
    A10 --> A11[Display PPI or Digital Scope]
    A10 --> A12[Threshold Detector and Alarm]
    A1 --> A10
    A1 --> A11
    A1 --> A9
```

### Block-Level Functional Description:

| Block | Engineering Function |
|-------|---------------------|
| **Timing & Trigger Generator** | Master clock; sets PRF, range gate timing, sweep synchronization |
| **Modulator** | Generates high-voltage pulse to drive the transmitter (e.g., line-type pulser) |
| **High-Power RF Transmitter** | Produces the microwave pulse (e.g., magnetron, TWT, klystron) |
| **Duplexer** | Routes Tx pulse to antenna; protects Rx from Tx leakage (TR/ATR cells) |
| **Antenna** | Focuses Tx energy into narrow beam; collects weak echo |
| **LNA** | Low-noise pre-amplification of the weak echo (critical for sensitivity) |
| **Mixer + LO** | Down-converts RF echo to Intermediate Frequency (IF) for processing |
| **IF Amplifier + Matched Filter** | Maximizes SNR; sets bandwidth for range resolution |
| **Signal Processor** | Extracts range, velocity, and angle; applies MTI / Doppler processing |
| **Display** | Visualizes detections on a PPI scope (range vs azimuth) |

---

## 4.2 Radar System Architecture — Subsystem Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph X1[Transmitter Subsystem]
        B1[Master Oscillator] --> B2[Pulse Modulator] --> B3[Power Amplifier]
    end
    subgraph X2[Antenna Subsystem]
        B4[Waveguide Feed] --> B5[Reflector Dish]
    end
    subgraph X3[Receiver Subsystem]
        B6[RF Front End] --> B7[IF Section] --> B8[Detector and Video]
    end
    subgraph X4[Signal Processing and Display]
        B9[Digital Signal Processor DSP] --> B10[Tracker and Plot Extractor] --> B11[PPI Display]
    end
    B3 --> B4
    B5 --> B6
    B8 --> B9
```

---

## 4.3 Target Echo Geometry (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C1[Radar Antenna] -- Transmitted Wave Path R outward --> C2[Target Object]
    C2 -- Returned Echo Path R inward --> C1
    C2 -. RCS sigma captures and scatters energy .-> C2
```

> [!NOTE] 
> **Visualization Note:** The two-way path (outward + return) is what produces the **$R^4$ dependence** in the radar equation. Each leg of the journey spreads the wave over a $4\pi R^2$ spherical area, so total spreading loss is proportional to $(4\pi R^2)^2 \propto R^4$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define RADAR. Mention its full form and any two applications.**  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**RADAR** stands for **RAdio Detection And Ranging**. It is an electromagnetic system used to detect the presence, range, velocity, and angular position of distant objects by transmitting microwave signals and analyzing the reflected echo.

**Two Applications:**
1. **Air Traffic Control (ATC)** — Detection and tracking of aircraft at airports.
2. **Weather Monitoring** — Doppler weather radar for precipitation and storm tracking.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for full form, 1 for applications)*

---

### **Q2. What is Radar Cross Section (RCS)? State its unit.**  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Radar Cross Section (RCS), denoted by $\sigma$**, is a measure of how detectable an object is by radar. It is defined as the equivalent area of a perfectly conducting sphere that would scatter the same amount of power back to the radar as the actual target.

**Unit:** Square meters (**m²**).

**Typical Values:** Small drone $\sigma \approx 0.01$ m²; Fighter jet $\sigma \approx 1$ m²; Large ship $\sigma \approx 10^4$ m²; Stealth aircraft $\sigma \approx 0.001$ m².

*(3 marks: 2 for definition, 1 for unit + example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a basic pulsed radar system. List the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Derive the simple radar range equation and explain the $R^4$ dependence. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (a) — Block Diagram of Pulsed Radar (7 marks)**

The basic pulsed radar consists of the following subsystems (refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the detailed diagram):

1. **Timing & Trigger Generator (1 mark):** Provides the master timing pulses; sets the Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF).
2. **Modulator (1 mark):** Generates high-voltage pulse to switch the transmitter ON for a short duration $\tau$ (pulse width).
3. **High-Power RF Transmitter (1 mark):** Produces a microwave pulse of peak power $P_t$ (typically kW to MW) at the carrier frequency.
4. **Duplexer (1 mark):** Switches the antenna between transmitter and receiver. Protects the sensitive receiver during transmission.
5. **Antenna (1 mark):** A directional parabolic dish focuses the Tx energy into a narrow beam and collects the weak echo.
6. **Receiver (LNA + Mixer + IF) (1 mark):** Amplifies the weak echo and down-converts it to IF for further processing.
7. **Display (PPI) (1 mark):** Shows the detected targets as bright blips on a range-azimuth display.

**Operation:** The transmitter sends a short pulse, then the radar "listens" for echoes. The round-trip time $\tau$ gives the target range via $R = c\tau/2$.

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (b) — Derivation of Radar Range Equation (7 marks)**

*(See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation.)*

**Summary of derivation steps (1 mark each, plus 2 marks for $R^4$ explanation):**

1. Power density at target: $\text{PD} = \dfrac{P_t G}{4\pi R^2}$
2. Power intercepted by target: $P_{\text{cap}} = \text{PD} \times \sigma$
3. Power density of echo at radar: $\dfrac{P_t G \sigma}{(4\pi R^2)^2}$
4. Power captured by Rx antenna: $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G \sigma A_e}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}$
5. Substitute $A_e = \dfrac{\lambda^2 G}{4\pi}$ and simplify:
$$P_r = \dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$$

**$R^4$ Dependence Explanation (2 marks):**  
The $R^4$ factor arises because the EM wave suffers **spreading loss twice** — once on the outward path to the target and once on the return path back to the radar. Each leg spreads power over $4\pi R^2$. This is why radar performance is extremely sensitive to range, and why long-range radars need very high power and large antennas.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the various applications of radar in military and civilian domains. List the major frequency bands used in radar. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A radar operates at 5 GHz with $P_t = 500$ kW, $G = 40$ dB, and $\sigma = 5$ m². If the minimum detectable signal is $S_{i,\min} = 10^{-12}$ W, calculate the maximum range. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (a) — Applications and Frequency Bands (7 marks)**

**Military Applications (3.5 marks):**
- **Surveillance radars** — Early warning of incoming aircraft/missiles
- **Fire-control radars** — Directing guns/missiles at targets
- **Tracking radars** — Continuous tracking of moving targets
- **Missile guidance** — Semi-active radar homing
- **Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR)** — Battlefield reconnaissance

**Civilian Applications (3.5 marks):**
- **Air Traffic Control (ATC)** — Aircraft detection at airports
- **Weather radar** — Detecting rain, storms, tornadoes
- **Marine radar** — Ship navigation and collision avoidance
- **Automotive radar** — Adaptive cruise control, blind-spot detection
- **Speed enforcement** — Police Doppler guns
- **Space debris tracking & planetary radar astronomy**

**Standard Radar Frequency Bands:**

| Band | Frequency Range | Typical Use |
|------|----------------|-------------|
| VHF | 30–300 MHz | Long-range surveillance |
| UHF | 300–1000 MHz | Early warning, ATC |
| L-band | 1–2 GHz | ATC, long-range tracking |
| S-band | 2–4 GHz | Weather radar, ATC |
| C-band | 4–8 GHz | Satellite communication, weather |
| X-band | 8–12 GHz | Military fire-control, marine radar |
| Ku-band | 12–18 GHz | Police radar, SAR |
| Ka-band | 26–40 GHz | Missile seekers, automotive |

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $f_0 = 5$ GHz $= 5 \times 10^9$ Hz
- $P_t = 500$ kW $= 5 \times 10^5$ W
- $G = 40$ dB $\Rightarrow G = 10^{40/10} = 10^4$
- $\sigma = 5$ m²
- $S_{i,\min} = 10^{-12}$ W

**Step 1 — Wavelength (1 mark):**
$$\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f_0} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8}}{5 \times 10^{9}} = 0.06 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Numerator of the range equation (2 marks):**
$$P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma = (5 \times 10^{5}) \times (10^{4})^{2} \times (0.06)^{2} \times 5$$
$$= 5 \times 10^{5} \times 10^{8} \times 3.6 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 = 9 \times 10^{11}$$

**Step 3 — Denominator (1 mark):**
$$(4\pi)^3 \times S_{i,\min} = 1984.4 \times 10^{-12} \approx 1.9844 \times 10^{-9}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $R_{\max}^4$ (1 mark):**
$$R_{\max}^4 = \dfrac{9 \times 10^{11}}{1.9844 \times 10^{-9}} = 4.535 \times 10^{20} \text{ m}^{4}$$

**Step 5 — Take the fourth root (2 marks):**
$$R_{\max} = (4.535 \times 10^{20})^{1/4}$$
$$= (45.35 \times 10^{19})^{1/4} = (4.535 \times 10^{20})^{0.25}$$
$$\approx 2.06 \times 10^{5} \text{ m} \approx 206 \text{ km}$$

$$\boxed{R_{\max} \approx 206 \text{ km}}$$

---

> [!WARNING] 
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $R = c\tau/2$ (round-trip vs. one-way). This costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Not converting dB to linear** when applying the radar equation. Always: $G_{\text{lin}} = 10^{G_{\text{dB}}/10}$.
> 3. **Mixing up monostatic vs. bistatic** — In monostatic, $G^2$ appears (single antenna for Tx and Rx). In bistatic, it's $G_t \times G_r$.
> 4. **Confusing range with range resolution** — $R = c\tau/2$ is the range of a target; $\Delta R = c/(2B)$ is the resolution (ability to separate two close targets).
> 5. **Using isotropic assumption incorrectly** — Most derivations assume the antenna has gain $G$, not $G = 1$. Setting $G = 1$ is a common error.
> 6. **Units mismatch in numerical problems** — Always convert MHz to Hz, kW to W, etc., before substituting.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] 
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Introduction):**

- ✅ **RADAR** = **RAdio Detection And Ranging** — uses microwave EM waves.
- ✅ Basic principle: **Transmit → Propagate → Reflect → Receive → Process → Display**.
- ✅ Range from time delay: $\mathbf{R = c\tau / 2}$
- ✅ Range resolution: $\mathbf{\Delta R = c / (2B)}$
- ✅ Doppler shift: $\mathbf{f_d = 2 v_r f_0 / c}$ (gives velocity)
- ✅ Antenna effective aperture: $\mathbf{A_e = \lambda^2 G / (4\pi)}$
- ✅ **Simple Radar Range Equation:**
$$\mathbf{P_r = \dfrac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R^4}}$$
- ✅ **Maximum range:**
$$\mathbf{R_{\max} = \left[\dfrac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, S_{i,\min}}\right]^{1/4}}$$
- ✅ **$R^4$ dependence** — the defining characteristic of radar; double the range requires **16×** the transmitted power.
- ✅ **Radar Cross Section (RCS), $\sigma$** — unit **m²**; typical: drone ~0.01, jet ~1, ship ~10⁴.
- ✅ **Duplexer** is essential to protect the receiver from high-power Tx leakage.
- ✅ Master Oscillator → Modulator → Transmitter → Duplexer → Antenna → Target → Antenna → LNA → Mixer → IF → Signal Processor → Display (PPI).
- ✅ **Pulsed Radar** measures **range**; **CW Doppler Radar** measures **velocity**.
- ✅ Common radar frequency bands: **L, S, C, X, Ku, Ka** (mnemonic: "**L**ittle **S**ausage **C**omes **X**tra **K**rispy").
- ✅ Applications: **ATC, military surveillance, weather, marine, automotive, SAR, space-debris tracking**.
- ✅ Boltzmann's constant: $\mathbf{k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}}$ J/K (used in noise calculations).
- ✅ Speed of light: $\mathbf{c = 3 \times 10^8}$ m/s.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Radar Range

In the context of primary surveillance **Radar (Radio Detection And Ranging)** systems, **Range** is defined as the **one-way slant distance between the radar antenna and the target** being illuminated by the transmitted electromagnetic wave. It is the most fundamental observable parameter extracted from a radar system, derived directly by measuring the **round-trip propagation delay** $\tau$ between the instant of pulse transmission and the instant of echo reception.

Mathematically, if a target is located at a slant distance $R$ from the radar, the transmitted electromagnetic wave must travel a total path length of $2R$ (outward journey to the target, plus the return journey back to the receiver). Since electromagnetic waves in free space propagate at the speed of light, denoted by the constant $c \approx \mathbf{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}$, the round-trip time $\tau$ is related to $R$ by:

$$R = \frac{c \tau}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The factor of **2** in the denominator is non-negotiable in all radar range derivations. It accounts for the **two-way (duplex) propagation** of the wave. Satellite links are generally one-way, but Radar links are intrinsically two-way. Always remember — *range is half the distance the wave actually travels.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mountain Echo"

Imagine you are standing at the bottom of a deep mountain valley and you shout loudly toward the opposite cliff face.

- You shout at exactly **12:00:00 noon**.
- After roughly **2 seconds**, you hear the echo return.
- Knowing that sound travels at roughly **340 m/s**, you instinctively compute the distance to the cliff as $(340 \times 2) / 2 = 340 \text{ meters}$.

A radar system performs exactly the same operation, but using **electromagnetic waves instead of sound**, and at the speed of light rather than the speed of sound. The radar transmits a short, high-power burst of RF energy, and a precise timer (called the **range clock** or **range gate**) measures the time elapsed until the tiny reflected echo returns from the target.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Radar vs. Satellite Range:**
> In a **Satellite Communication** link, the slant range is determined by orbital geometry (Kepler's laws) and the propagation delay is purely **one-way**. In a **Radar** system, the delay is **two-way**, because the target is a passive reflector. This is the central physical difference that produces the factor of 2.

## 1.3 The Three Sub-Categories of Radar Range

The single word "Range" in radar engineering actually comprises **three distinct quantities**, each governed by different design parameters:

| Sub-Term | Engineering Question It Answers |
|:---|:---|
| **Maximum Unambiguous Range ($R_{un}$)** | "What is the farthest target I can detect without confusing its echo with a later pulse?" |
| **Minimum Range ($R_{min}$)** | "What is the closest target I can detect, given my receiver recovery time?" |
| **Range Resolution ($\Delta R$)** | "Can my radar distinguish between two targets that are very close in distance to each other?" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 Focus:** The KTU syllabus groups all three of these sub-concepts under the umbrella heading "Range." A complete answer in the exam requires you to discuss **all three** with their governing equations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Maximum Unambiguous Range ($R_{un}$)

### 2.1.1 The Core Idea

A pulsed radar transmits pulses periodically at a rate called the **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF)**, denoted $f_r$ (Hz), with the corresponding **Pulse Repetition Interval (PRI)** given by:

$$T = \frac{1}{f_r}$$

If the radar transmits pulse #1, and then a second pulse (#2) is transmitted before the echo from pulse #1 has returned, the receiver will mistakenly associate the late-arriving echo of pulse #1 with the most recently transmitted pulse #2. This creates a **range ambiguity**.

The **maximum unambiguous range** is therefore defined as the longest range at which a target echo can return *before* the next pulse is transmitted:

$$R_{un} = \frac{c \, T}{2} = \frac{c}{2 \, f_r}$$

### 2.1.2 Why This Matters

- **Low PRF radars** (search radars, long-range surveillance): Large $R_{un}$, but suffer from **Doppler ambiguities**.
- **High PRF radars** (fire-control, missile tracking): Small $R_{un}$, but excellent **Doppler resolution**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** PRF selection is one of the most fundamental design choices in any pulse radar. There is no "perfect" PRF — every choice is a compromise between unambiguous range and unambiguous velocity.

## 2.2 Minimum Range ($R_{min}$)

### 2.2.1 The Core Idea

When the radar transmitter fires a pulse, the **high-power transmit energy** leaks into the sensitive receiver through the duplexer/circulator. The receiver requires a finite time to recover from this saturation. Furthermore, the transmitted pulse has a finite width, $\tau$ (pulse width in seconds).

During the pulse duration $\tau$, the receiver is effectively **blind** — it cannot detect any returning echo because it is overwhelmed by the transmitted energy bleeding through. Therefore, the closest target that can be detected is one whose echo arrives *just after* the pulse ends:

$$R_{min} = \frac{c \, \tau}{2}$$

### 2.2.2 Practical Considerations

- A **shorter pulse width** $\tau$ allows detection of closer targets.
- Modern radars use **intrapulse modulation** (e.g., linear FM chirp) combined with **pulse compression** to simultaneously achieve short $\tau$ (for low $R_{min}$) AND large time-bandwidth product (for high energy and fine resolution).

## 2.3 Range Resolution ($\Delta R$)

### 2.3.1 The Core Idea

Range resolution is the radar's ability to **distinguish two targets separated by a small radial distance**. Consider two targets at ranges $R_1$ and $R_2$ such that $R_2 > R_1$. The echoes from these two targets are separated in time by:

$$\Delta \tau = \frac{2(R_2 - R_1)}{c}$$

For the radar to detect them as **two separate echoes** rather than one merged blob, the time separation must exceed the **pulse width $\tau$**:

$$\Delta \tau \geq \tau$$

Substituting and rearranging:

$$\Delta R = R_2 - R_{1, \text{min sep}} = \frac{c \, \tau}{2}$$

For a rectangular pulse of width $\tau$, the **matched-filter output resolution** is improved to:

$$\Delta R = \frac{c}{2 \, B}$$

where $B$ is the receiver **bandwidth** (Hz). For a simple rectangular pulse, $B \approx 1/\tau$, so both forms are consistent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Range resolution depends **only on the pulse width**, NOT on the carrier frequency, antenna size, or transmitted power. Doubling the range resolution (halving $\Delta R$) requires halving the pulse width, which in turn requires doubling the receiver bandwidth.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — "Range"

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbol Meanings | Typical Unit |
|:--:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Basic Range | $R = \dfrac{c \tau}{2}$ | $c$ = speed of light, $\tau$ = round-trip delay | meters |
| 2 | Maximum Unambiguous Range | $R_{un} = \dfrac{c}{2 \, f_r}$ | $f_r$ = PRF in Hz | km |
| 3 | Minimum Range | $R_{min} = \dfrac{c \, \tau}{2}$ | $\tau$ = pulse width in seconds | meters |
| 4 | Range Resolution (Rect. Pulse) | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c \, \tau}{2}$ | $\tau$ = pulse width | meters |
| 5 | Range Resolution (Matched Filter) | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2 \, B}$ | $B$ = receiver bandwidth | meters |
| 6 | Pulse Repetition Interval | $T = \dfrac{1}{f_r}$ | $f_r$ = PRF | seconds |
| 7 | Staggered PRF Ratio | $\dfrac{f_{r1}}{f_{r2}} = \dfrac{p}{q}$, $\gcd(p,q) = 1$ | $p, q$ = coprime integers | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Range parameters dictate nearly every commercial and military radar specification — from airport surveillance radars (ASR) with $R_{un} \approx 60 \text{ nm}$, to police speed guns with $\Delta R \approx 1 \text{ m}$, to weather radars needing fine resolution to distinguish rain from snow.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — The Fundamental Range Equation $R = c\tau/2$

**Given:**
- Radar transmits an electromagnetic pulse at time $t = 0$.
- Pulse travels outward at speed $c$.
- Pulse strikes a target at range $R$, and a portion of energy is reflected back.
- Reflected echo arrives back at the radar antenna at time $t = \tau$.

**Step 1 — Total distance travelled by the wave:**

The wave must travel **from the radar to the target** (distance $R$), and then **from the target back to the radar** (distance $R$ again, since the target is stationary or assumed stationary for the first-order range measurement). The total one-dimensional path length is therefore $2R$.

$$\text{Total Path} = R + R = 2R$$

**Step 2 — Apply the basic kinematic relation $d = v \times t$:**

Using $v = c$ (speed of light in free space) and $t = \tau$ (round-trip delay):

$$2R = c \cdot \tau$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $R$:**

$$R = \frac{c \tau}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting the numerical value of $c$:**

$$R = \frac{(3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}) \cdot \tau}{2} = (1.5 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}) \cdot \tau$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Useful Rule of Thumb:** A range delay of **1 microsecond** corresponds to a target range of exactly **150 meters**. A delay of **1 millisecond** corresponds to **150 km**. Memorize this for quick mental calculations in the exam hall.

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Maximum Unambiguous Range

**Given:**
- Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF) = $f_r$ pulses per second.
- Pulse Repetition Interval (PRI) = $T$ seconds.
- Speed of light = $c$.

**Step 1 — Express the PRI:**

The PRI is the reciprocal of the PRF, because PRI is the time between two consecutive transmitted pulses:

$$T = \frac{1}{f_r}$$

**Step 2 — Condition for unambiguous reception:**

For a target at the **maximum unambiguous range** $R_{un}$, the round-trip time of its echo must be *exactly equal to* the PRI. If the round-trip time exceeds $T$, the echo will arrive *after* the next pulse has already been transmitted, and the radar will mistakenly interpret the echo as belonging to the *next* pulse.

$$\tau_{\max} = T = \frac{1}{f_r}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the basic range equation with $\tau = \tau_{\max}$:**

$$R_{un} = \frac{c \cdot \tau_{\max}}{2} = \frac{c \cdot T}{2} = \frac{c}{2 \, f_r}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s:**

$$R_{un} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \, f_r} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^8}{f_r} \text{ meters}$$

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Range Resolution

**Given:**
- Two point targets at ranges $R_1$ and $R_2$, where $R_2 > R_1$.
- Pulse width = $\tau$.
- Speed of light = $c$.

**Step 1 — Time delay of echo from target 1:**

$$\tau_1 = \frac{2 R_1}{c}$$

**Step 2 — Time delay of echo from target 2:**

$$\tau_2 = \frac{2 R_2}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Time separation between the two echoes:**

$$\Delta \tau = \tau_2 - \tau_1 = \frac{2 R_2}{c} - \frac{2 R_1}{c} = \frac{2 (R_2 - R_1)}{c}$$

**Step 4 — Rayleigh criterion for distinguishability:**

The two echoes can be resolved as separate only if their time separation is **at least equal to the pulse width**. Setting $\Delta \tau = \tau$:

$$\tau = \frac{2 (R_2 - R_1)}{c}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the minimum separable range difference $\Delta R$:**

$$\Delta R = R_2 - R_1 = \frac{c \, \tau}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why high-resolution radars (e.g., synthetic aperture radar for terrain mapping) use **extremely short pulses** on the order of nanoseconds, achieving $\Delta R$ on the order of centimeters to meters.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Style)

**Problem:** A pulsed surveillance radar has a PRF of **1 kHz** and a pulse width of **2 microseconds**. Compute:
- (i) The maximum unambiguous range.
- (ii) The minimum detectable range.
- (iii) The range resolution.

**Solution:**

**Part (i):** Given $f_r = 1000$ Hz.

$$R_{un} = \frac{c}{2 \, f_r} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 1000} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2000} = 1.5 \times 10^5 \text{ m} = 150 \text{ km}$$

**Part (ii):** Given $\tau_{pw} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ s.

$$R_{min} = \frac{c \, \tau_{pw}}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 2 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{600}{2} = 300 \text{ m}$$

**Part (iii):** Using the same pulse width:

$$\Delta R = \frac{c \, \tau_{pw}}{2} = 300 \text{ m}$$

**Interpretation:** This radar can detect targets anywhere between **300 m and 150 km**, and can resolve two targets only if they are at least **300 m apart** in slant range.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Radar Range Measurement Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transmitter fires pulse at t = 0] --> B[Pulse propagates outward at speed c]
    B --> C[TargeT illuminates and reflects echo]
    C --> D[Echo propagates back at speed c]
    D --> E[Receiver detects echo at t = tau]
    E --> F[Range Computer divides tau by 2c]
    F --> G[Output: Target Range R displayed]
    H[PRF Clock triggers next pulse at t = T] --> A
    I[Condition: tau less than T] --> J[Unambiguous Range]
    K[Condition: tau greater than T] --> L[Range Ambiguity Error]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Pulsed Radar Range Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PRF[PRF Generator] --> TX[Pulse Modulator]
    TX --> ANT[Antenna Duplexer]
    ANT -. Transmitted Wave .-> TGT[Target at Range R]
    TGT -. Reflected Echo .-> ANT
    ANT --> LNA[Low Noise Amplifier]
    LNA --> MIX[Mixer and IF Stage]
    MIX --> DET[Envelope Detector]
    DET --> RNG[Range Gate and Clock]
    RNG --> DSP[Signal Processor]
    DSP --> DISP[Range Display in km]
    CLK[Master Timing Clock] --> PRF
    CLK --> RNG
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The blue dashed arrows represent *electromagnetic wave propagation through free space*, while the solid arrows represent *electrical signal flow inside the radar hardware*. The common master clock synchronizes both transmission and reception to maintain timing coherence.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Range Ambiguity Condition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Pulse 1 transmitted at t = 0] --> W1[Wave travels outward]
    W1 --> T1[Target echo 1 returns at t = tau1]
    P2[Pulse 2 transmitted at t = T] --> W2[Wave travels outward]
    W2 --> T2[Target echo 2 returns at t = T + tau2]
    COND1{Is tau1 less than T?}
    COND1 -- YES --> OK1[Echo 1 correctly assigned to Pulse 1]
    COND1 -- NO --> ERR1[Echo 1 arrives AFTER Pulse 2 - AMBIGUITY]
    COND2{Is tau2 less than T?}
    COND2 -- YES --> OK2[Echo 2 correctly assigned to Pulse 2]
    COND2 -- NO --> ERR2[Echo 2 arrives AFTER Pulse 3 - AMBIGUITY]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Define maximum unambiguous range of a radar and write its expression in terms of PRF.

**Model Answer:**
The maximum unambiguous range of a radar is the largest slant range from which a target echo can return and still be unambiguously associated with the most recently transmitted pulse. If the round-trip delay exceeds the Pulse Repetition Interval (PRI), the echo will be mistakenly assigned to the next pulse, producing range ambiguity.

Mathematically:

$$R_{un} = \frac{c \, T}{2} = \frac{c}{2 \, f_r}$$

where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s is the speed of light, $T = 1/f_r$ is the PRI, and $f_r$ is the PRF in Hz.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for equation + 1 for explanation of ambiguity condition.)*

---

### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** What is meant by range resolution of a radar? Why is it important?

**Model Answer:**
Range resolution $\Delta R$ is the minimum radial separation between two targets at the same bearing such that the radar can display them as two distinct echoes rather than as one merged return.

$$\Delta R = \frac{c \, \tau}{2}$$

where $\tau$ is the transmitted pulse width.

**Importance:** Fine range resolution allows the radar to discriminate closely spaced targets — for example, separating an aircraft from a chaff decoy, distinguishing two aircraft flying in formation, or mapping small terrain features in synthetic aperture radar (SAR).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for equation + 1 for importance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the maximum unambiguous range of a pulsed radar. Explain the term *range ambiguity* and how it arises. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pulsed radar operating at a PRF of **2 kHz** with a pulse width of **1 $\mu$s** is used to detect an aircraft. Calculate:
- (i) Maximum unambiguous range.
- (ii) Minimum range.
- (iii) Range resolution.
- (iv) If a target is actually at 100 km, what apparent range will the radar display? Justify with reasoning. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation of Maximum Unambiguous Range:**

The pulse repetition interval (PRI) is:

$$T = \frac{1}{f_r}$$

For unambiguous reception, the round-trip time $\tau$ of the echo must satisfy $\tau \leq T$. The maximum permissible delay is:

$$\tau_{\max} = T = \frac{1}{f_r}$$

Using the basic radar range equation $R = c\tau/2$, the maximum unambiguous range is:

$$\boxed{R_{un} = \frac{c \, T}{2} = \frac{c}{2 \, f_r}}$$

**Range Ambiguity:** If a target lies beyond $R_{un}$, its echo arrives *after* the next pulse has been transmitted. The receiver cannot know whether the echo originated from the original pulse or the next pulse, so it displays the target at a *shorter* (apparent) range than the true range. This erroneous display is called range ambiguity.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- 2 marks — PRI expression.
- 3 marks — Correct derivation of $R_{un}$.
- 2 marks — Clear explanation of ambiguity phenomenon.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

Given: $f_r = 2000$ Hz, $\tau = 1 \, \mu$s = $1 \times 10^{-6}$ s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**(i) Maximum unambiguous range:**

$$R_{un} = \frac{c}{2 \, f_r} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 2000} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{4000} = 7.5 \times 10^4 \text{ m} = 75 \text{ km}$$

**(ii) Minimum range:**

$$R_{min} = \frac{c \, \tau}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{300}{2} = 150 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Range resolution:**

$$\Delta R = \frac{c \, \tau}{2} = 150 \text{ m}$$

**(iv) Apparent range for actual target at 100 km:**

The true round-trip delay is:

$$\tau_{\text{true}} = \frac{2 \times 100 \times 10^3}{3 \times 10^8} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 666.7 \, \mu\text{s}$$

The PRI is:

$$T = \frac{1}{2000} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 500 \, \mu\text{s}$$

Since $\tau_{\text{true}} = 666.7 \, \mu\text{s} > T = 500 \, \mu\text{s}$, the echo arrives *after* the next pulse has been transmitted. The number of PRIs that have elapsed before the echo returns:

$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{\tau_{\text{true}}}{T} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{666.7}{500} \right\rfloor = 1$$

The *residual* delay (which the radar interprets as the actual range delay) is:

$$\tau_{\text{apparent}} = \tau_{\text{true}} - 1 \times T = 666.7 - 500 = 166.7 \, \mu\text{s}$$

The apparent range is:

$$R_{\text{apparent}} = \frac{c \, \tau_{\text{apparent}}}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 166.7 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{5 \times 10^4}{2} = 25 \text{ km}$$

**Conclusion:** The radar will display the target at **25 km**, whereas it is actually at 100 km — this is the *second-time-around echo* phenomenon, a classic range ambiguity.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- 1 mark for each of (i), (ii), (iii) computation.
- 4 marks for (iv): 1 mark for $\tau_{\text{true}}$ calculation, 1 mark for identifying ambiguity, 1 mark for residual delay, 1 mark for final answer.

---

### **Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** *(Alternative to Question A)*

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a pulsed radar receiver used for range measurement. Clearly show how the range gate and PRF clock cooperate to extract target range. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **range resolution** of a radar in terms of pulse width and bandwidth. A radar uses a pulse width of **0.5 $\mu$s**. Find:
- (i) The range resolution.
- (ii) The required receiver bandwidth (assuming matched filter reception).
- (iii) Two targets are at 50 km and 50.1 km. Will the radar resolve them? Show your computation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Pulsed Radar Range Subsystem:**

*(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in SECTION 4.2.)*

The functional architecture is as follows:

1. **PRF Generator** produces a periodic trigger at frequency $f_r$.
2. **Pulse Modulator** generates a short, high-power RF pulse of width $\tau$ at each trigger.
3. The pulse passes through the **Antenna Duplexer** and radiates into free space.
4. The target at range $R$ reflects a small portion of the energy back.
5. The returning echo enters the duplexer and is routed to the **Low Noise Amplifier (LNA)**.
6. The amplified signal is down-converted in the **Mixer/IF Stage**.
7. The **Envelope Detector** extracts the pulse envelope.
8. A **Range Gate** (a time-controlled window) opens at the expected echo time, blocking noise at all other times.
9. The **Range Clock** measures the elapsed time between the transmitted pulse and the range gate echo.
10. The **Signal Processor** computes $R = c\tau/2$ and displays it.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- 2 marks for block diagram (or equivalent textual description).
- 3 marks for functional description of each block.
- 2 marks for explaining the role of the PRF clock and range gate.

---

**Part (b) — Range Resolution Derivation and Computation:**

**Derivation:** Two targets separated by $\Delta R$ produce echoes separated in time by:

$$\Delta \tau = \frac{2 \, \Delta R}{c}$$

For matched-filter resolution, $\Delta \tau \geq 1/B$, where $B$ is the receiver bandwidth. Substituting:

$$\Delta R = \frac{c}{2 \, B}$$

For a simple rectangular pulse of width $\tau$, $B \approx 1/\tau$, giving:

$$\Delta R = \frac{c \, \tau}{2}$$

**Computation:** Given $\tau = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ s = $5 \times 10^{-7}$ s.

**(i) Range resolution:**

$$\Delta R = \frac{c \, \tau}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 5 \times 10^{-7}}{2} = \frac{150}{2} = 75 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Required receiver bandwidth:**

$$B = \frac{1}{\tau} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-7}} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 2 \text{ MHz}$$

**(iii) Resolution check for targets at 50 km and 50.1 km:**

The actual separation is:

$$\Delta R_{\text{actual}} = 50.1 - 50.0 = 0.1 \text{ km} = 100 \text{ m}$$

Comparing with the radar's resolution:

$$\Delta R_{\text{actual}} = 100 \text{ m} > \Delta R_{\text{radar}} = 75 \text{ m}$$

**Conclusion:** Since the actual separation (100 m) **exceeds** the radar's resolution limit (75 m), the radar **CAN resolve** the two targets.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- 2 marks for derivation.
- 1.5 marks each for (i) and (ii).
- 2 marks for (iii) — including comparison logic.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2.** The most common error is writing $R = c \tau$ instead of $R = c \tau / 2$. Always justify the factor of 2 as the *two-way* propagation.
> 2. **Confusing PRF with PRI.** PRF is in Hz (pulses/sec); PRI is in seconds (time between pulses). They are reciprocals of each other.
> 3. **Not identifying range ambiguity in numericals.** When asked for "apparent range," students often compute only the true delay and forget to subtract multiples of PRI.
> 4. **Mixing up minimum range and range resolution.** Both have the formula $c \tau/2$, but they refer to different physical quantities — $R_{min}$ is governed by receiver recovery, while $\Delta R$ is governed by Rayleigh criterion. **Always state the context.**
> 5. **Units.** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, but if pulse width is given in microseconds, you must convert to seconds before substitution. Showing units in every step earns partial credit even if the final answer is wrong.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

✅ **Radar Range** = one-way slant distance from radar to target, extracted from round-trip delay.

✅ **Basic equation:** $R = c \tau / 2$ — the factor **2** is mandatory.

✅ **Maximum unambiguous range:** $R_{un} = c / (2 f_r)$ — depends on **PRF**, not pulse width.

✅ **Minimum range:** $R_{min} = c \tau / 2$ — depends on **pulse width**, set by receiver recovery time.

✅ **Range resolution:** $\Delta R = c \tau / 2 = c / (2B)$ — depends on **pulse width and bandwidth** only.

✅ **Range ambiguity** arises when $\tau_{\text{echo}} > T = 1/f_r$ — the echo is mistakenly assigned to a later pulse.

✅ **Second-time-around echo** is the most common manifestation of range ambiguity, producing a target display at a fraction of its true range.

✅ **Engineering trade-off:** Lower PRF → larger $R_{un}$ but worse Doppler; higher PRF → smaller $R_{un}$ but better Doppler.

✅ **Pulse compression** is used in modern radars to achieve short pulse (low $\Delta R$) AND high energy (long detection range) simultaneously.

✅ **Memory aid:** "**Range depends on Time. Velocity depends on Frequency.**" — in pulse-Doppler radar, these two observables are inextricably linked through PRF.

✅ **For numerical answers**, always show: (a) given data, (b) formula, (c) substitution with units, (d) final answer with units, (e) interpretation sentence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radar Waveforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Radar Waveforms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Radar Waveforms

A **Radar Waveform** is a precisely engineered time-varying electromagnetic (EM) signal — designed, shaped, and modulated at the transmitter — that is radiated into free space, interacts with a target, and is reflected back to the radar receiver for detection, ranging, velocity measurement, and target classification. The waveform is the *signature* of the radar system: it dictates the radar's range resolution, Doppler resolution, maximum unambiguous range, maximum unambiguous velocity, and ability to extract target information in the presence of noise and clutter.

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, radar waveforms are classified as **Low-Pass Equivalent (Baseband)** or **Band-Pass (RF) signals** and are mathematically represented as:

$$
s(t) = \text{Re}\left\{u(t)\,e^{j2\pi f_c t}\right\}
$$

where:
- $u(t)$ = **Complex Envelope (Modulation Function)** — contains all the radar's intelligence (pulse shape, phase coding, frequency modulation).
- $f_c$ = **Carrier Frequency** (typically **3 GHz to 94 GHz** in operational radars).
- $\text{Re}\{\cdot\}$ = Real part operator (the physical, real-valued radiated signal).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must master the representations of low-pass and band-pass radar signals, the analysis of **CW (Continuous Wave)**, **Pulsed**, and **Pulse-Compressed (LFM) waveforms**, the concept of the **Matched Filter**, the **Ambiguity Function**, and the relationship between waveform design and **Range/Doppler ambiguities**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bat & The Bullhorn

Imagine you are standing in a pitch-dark cave, wearing a blindfold. To "see" the cave walls:

- **Analogy 1 — A Single Hand-Clap (Pulsed Waveform):** You clap once loudly, then *listen* to the echo. The delay tells you how far the wall is, and the echo's loudness tells you how big it is. A short, sharp clap (short pulse) lets you measure close walls accurately — but a long, drawn-out clap blurs close echoes.
- **Analogy 2 — A Continuous Humming Tone (CW Waveform):** You continuously hum a constant pitch. A stationary wall gives no pitch change in the echo, but a *moving* bat (Doppler shift) makes the echo a slightly higher/lower pitch. Pure CW can detect *motion* but cannot measure *distance*.
- **Analogy 3 — A Bat's Chirp (LFM/Linear FM):** A real bat sweeps its voice from high to low pitch over a millisecond. The "instant" frequency of the returning echo reveals not just delay, but the *exact moment* within the chirp it reflected from. This grants both range AND fine resolution — a technique called **Pulse Compression**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Engineering Intuition:** The radar engineer chooses the waveform *exactly* like the bat chooses its chirp — balancing **Energy on Target** (loudness/duration), **Range Resolution** (sharpness of the pulse), **Doppler Sensitivity** (motion detection), and **Resistance to Jamming/Clutter** (uniqueness of the signature).

### 1.3 Why Radar Waveforms Matter in Engineering

| Radar Mission | Waveform Property Required | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Long-range surveillance** | High average power (long pulse or high PRF) | Air Traffic Control (ATC) radar |
| **Fine range imaging** | Wide bandwidth → narrow pulse | Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) |
| **Missile tracking** | High PRF, Doppler-tolerant | Fire-control radar (X-band) |
| **Stealth detection** | Low probability of intercept (LPI) | Modern AESA fighter radars |
| **Weather monitoring** | Pulse-to-pulse phase stability | Doppler weather radar (Doppler) |

### 1.4 The Four Canonical Radar Waveform Families

1. **Continuous Wave (CW)** — Unmodulated sinusoid; pure Doppler radar.
2. **Pulsed CW (PCW)** — Rectangular pulses at Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF); classical surveillance radar.
3. **Linear Frequency Modulated (LFM) Chirp** — Pulse whose frequency sweeps linearly; enables pulse compression.
4. **Phase-Coded (Barker, Frank, P-codes)** — Binary phase modulation inside a long pulse; spreads energy while maintaining resolution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** Every waveform in the syllabus can be reduced to a choice along **two fundamental axes** — *bandwidth* (sets range resolution) and *time-duration* (sets energy and Doppler resolution). The art of radar waveform design is the art of optimizing this *time-bandwidth product*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Mathematical Taxonomy of Radar Signals

Every radar signal can be written as one of two canonical forms:

**Form 1 — Band-Pass Signal (Physical Radiated Wave):**

$$
s(t) = a(t)\,\cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + \theta(t)\right]
$$

**Form 2 — Equivalent Low-Pass / Complex Envelope:**

$$
\tilde{s}(t) = a(t)\,e^{j\theta(t)} \quad \text{such that} \quad s(t) = \text{Re}\left\{\tilde{s}(t)\,e^{j2\pi f_c t}\right\}
$$

The complex envelope $\tilde{s}(t)$ is what the radar designer actually manipulates. Its magnitude $a(t)$ is the *amplitude modulation* (pulse shape) and $\theta(t)$ is the *phase/frequency modulation* (LFM, BPSK codes).

### 2.2 Continuous Wave (CW) Radar Signal — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Definition:** A pure, unmodulated sinusoid radiated continuously.

$$
s_{CW}(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 2 — Why it works for Doppler:** When reflected from a moving target with radial velocity $v_r$, the echo is frequency-shifted by the **Doppler frequency**:

$$
f_d = \frac{2 v_r f_c}{c}
$$

**Step 3 — The trade-off:** A pure CW radar has *infinite* time duration but *zero* bandwidth. Infinite duration gives infinite energy (good SNR), but zero bandwidth means:
- **No range resolution** (cannot distinguish two targets at different ranges).
- **No isolation between transmit and receive** (must use separate antennas or a circulator with leakage issues).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** CW = "See Speed, Not Distance." The waveform is blind to range but uniquely sensitive to velocity.

### 2.3 Pulsed Radar Signal — The Workhorse of Surveillance

**Step 1 — Definition:** Rectangular pulses of width $\tau$ repeated at the Pulse Repetition Interval (PRI), $T$.

$$
s_p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t - nT}{\tau}\right) \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

where:

$$
\text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right) = \begin{cases} 1 & \vert t \vert \le \tau/2 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Key Parameters:**
- **Pulse Width $\tau$** — Sets range resolution: $\Delta R = \dfrac{c\,\tau}{2}$.
- **PRI $T$** — Sets max unambiguous range: $R_{unamb} = \dfrac{cT}{2}$.
- **PRF $f_r = 1/T$** — Sets max unambiguous Doppler: $v_{max} = \dfrac{\lambda f_r}{4}$.
- **Duty Cycle** — $d = \tau/T$; sets average power: $P_{avg} = P_{peak}\cdot d$.

**Step 3 — The Range/Doppler Dilemma (KTU Classic):**
- **High PRF** → unambiguous velocity but range-ambiguous.
- **Low PRF** → unambiguous range but Doppler-ambiguous (blind speeds).
- **Medium PRF** → a balanced compromise used in modern airborne radars.

### 2.4 Linear Frequency Modulated (LFM) Chirp — Pulse Compression

**Step 1 — Definition:** A rectangular pulse whose *instantaneous frequency* sweeps linearly from $f_c - \Delta f/2$ to $f_c + \Delta f/2$ over the pulse width $\tau$.

$$
s_{LFM}(t) = A\,\text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right)\cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + \pi k t^2\right]
$$

where the **chirp rate** $k = \Delta f / \tau$ (Hz/s).

**Step 2 — Phase Form:** The instantaneous phase is $\phi(t) = \pi k t^2$. The instantaneous frequency is:

$$
f_i(t) = f_c + \frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{d\phi}{dt} = f_c + k\,t
$$

**Step 3 — The Magic of LFM:** A long pulse of width $\tau$ contains a *sweep bandwidth* $\Delta f$. After matched filtering, the effective pulse width collapses to $\approx 1/\Delta f$, giving a **Time-Bandwidth Product** of:

$$
\text{TBP} = \tau \cdot \Delta f
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pulse Compression Gain** = Time-Bandwidth Product. A 100 µs LFM pulse sweeping 10 MHz compresses to a 0.1 µs effective pulse — yielding a **30 dB processing gain** while maintaining high energy on target.

### 2.5 The Matched Filter — The Optimal Linear Receiver

**Step 1 — Concept:** For a known transmitted signal $s(t)$ corrupted by additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN), the *linear filter* that maximizes the output **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** at the sampling instant is the **Matched Filter**.

**Step 2 — Impulse Response:**

$$
h(t) = s^*(T - t)
$$

i.e., the time-reversed complex conjugate of the signal.

**Step 3 — Transfer Function:**

$$
H(f) = K\,S^*(f)\,e^{-j2\pi f T}
$$

**Step 4 — Output (Convolution):**

$$
y(t) = s(t) \ast h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} s(\lambda)\,h(t-\lambda)\,d\lambda
$$

**Step 5 — Peak SNR at the Matched Filter Output:**

$$
\text{SNR}_{out} = \frac{2 E}{N_0}
$$

where $E$ = signal energy, $N_0$ = noise PSD.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The matched filter is the *gold standard* against which every practical radar receiver is benchmarked. In a real radar, the matched filter is implemented digitally using FFT-based *cross-correlation* or specialized SAW (Surface Acoustic Wave) analog devices for chirp compression.

### 2.6 The Ambiguity Function — The Waveform's DNA

**Definition:** The **Ambiguity Function** $\chi(\tau, f_d)$ characterizes the response of a matched filter to a target at range offset $\tau$ and Doppler shift $f_d$:

$$
\chi(\tau, f_d) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} s(t)\,s^*(t - \tau)\,e^{j2\pi f_d t}\,dt
$$

**Properties (must memorize for KTU):**
1. **Maximum at origin:** $\vert \chi(0,0) \vert = 1$ (or $2E$ depending on normalization).
2. **Volume Invariance:** $\iint \vert \chi(\tau, f_d) \vert^2\,d\tau\,df_d = 1$ (constant).
3. **Symmetry:** $\chi(-\tau, -f_d) = \chi^*(\tau, f_d)$.
4. **Zero-Doppler Cut:** $\chi(\tau, 0) = $ autocorrelation of $s(t)$.
5. **Zero-Range Cut:** $\chi(0, f_d) = $ Fourier transform of $\vert s(t) \vert^2$.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MANDATORY: NO `|` inside tables. All absolute-value bars rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` for LaTeX/Markdown safety.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Band-Pass Radar Signal | $s(t) = \text{Re}\{\tilde{s}(t)\,e^{j2\pi f_c t}\}$ | V/m | Real radiated wave |
| 2 | Doppler Frequency | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r f_c}{c} = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$ | Hz | Velocity → frequency shift |
| 3 | Range Resolution (Rectangular pulse) | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c\,\tau}{2}$ | m | Minimum separable target spacing |
| 4 | Unambiguous Range | $R_{ua} = \dfrac{c\,T}{2} = \dfrac{c}{2 f_r}$ | m | Max range before aliasing |
| 5 | Unambiguous Velocity | $v_{ua} = \dfrac{\lambda\,f_r}{4}$ | m/s | Max velocity before aliasing |
| 6 | Duty Cycle | $d = \tau/T = \tau f_r$ | dimensionless | Fraction of time transmitting |
| 7 | Average Power | $P_{avg} = P_{peak}\,d$ | W | Long-term power budget |
| 8 | LFM Chirp Phase | $\phi(t) = \pi k t^2$, $k = \Delta f/\tau$ | rad | Quadratic phase → linear FM |
| 9 | Compressed Pulse Width | $\tau_c = 1/\Delta f$ | s | Post-matched-filter width |
| 10 | Time-Bandwidth Product | $\text{TBP} = \tau\,\Delta f$ | dimensionless | Pulse compression gain |
| 11 | Matched Filter Response | $h(t) = s^*(T - t)$ | V/s | Optimal linear filter |
| 12 | Matched Filter Output SNR | $\text{SNR}_{out} = 2E/N_0$ | dimensionless | Best achievable SNR |
| 13 | Ambiguity Function | $\chi(\tau, f_d) = \int s(t)s^*(t - \tau)e^{j2\pi f_d t}\,dt$ | complex | Waveform's range-Doppler map |
| 14 | Radar Range Equation | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$ | W | Received echo power |
| 15 | Phase-Coded (Barker-13) | $\text{Sidelobe level} = -22.3$ dB | dB | Lowest known binary code |

### 2.8 Real-World Utility of Waveform Engineering

- **LFM Chirp** — Used in SAR (Synthetic Aperture Radar) for high-resolution Earth imaging from satellites (e.g., Sentinel-1).
- **Phase Coding (Barker, Gold codes)** — Used in GPS and CDMA cellular for *separation* of multiple signals.
- **CW Doppler** — Used in police radar guns, weather radar wind profilers, and military continuous-wave illuminators.
- **Matched Filtering** — Universal in digital communications (CDMA), sonar, medical ultrasound, and seismology.

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Matched Filter Frequency Response

**Statement to Prove:** For a known signal $s(t)$ with energy $E$ in AWGN of PSD $N_0/2$, the filter $H(f)$ that maximizes output SNR at $t = T$ satisfies $H(f) = K\,S^*(f)\,e^{-j2\pi f T}$.

**Step 1 — Set up the output at sampling instant $t_0$:**

$$
y(t_0) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} H(f)\,S(f)\,e^{j2\pi f t_0}\,df
$$

**Step 2 — Define output signal power:**

$$
P_{signal} = \vert y(t_0) \vert^2
$$

**Step 3 — Define output noise power.** AWGN with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$ entering filter $H(f)$ produces output noise PSD $(N_0/2)\,\vert H(f) \vert^2$. Total noise variance:

$$
P_{noise} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{N_0}{2}\,\vert H(f) \vert^2\,df
$$

**Step 4 — Form the SNR functional to maximize:**

$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{\vert y(t_0) \vert^2}{P_{noise}} = \frac{\left\vert \int H(f)\,S(f)\,e^{j2\pi f t_0}\,df \right\vert^2}{\int (N_0/2)\,\vert H(f) \vert^2\,df}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality:**

$$
\left\vert \int H(f)\,S(f)\,e^{j2\pi f t_0}\,df \right\vert^2 \le \int \vert H(f) \vert^2\,df \cdot \int \vert S(f)\,e^{j2\pi f t_0} \vert^2\,df
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the right side. The second integral is:**

$$
\int \vert S(f) \vert^2\,df = \int \vert s(t) \vert^2\,dt = E
$$

(using Parseval's theorem).

**Step 7 — Combine. The maximum SNR is achieved when equality in Cauchy-Schwarz holds, i.e., when:**

$$
H(f) = K\,S^*(f)\,e^{-j2\pi f t_0}
$$

Substituting $t_0 = T$:

$$
H(f) = K\,S^*(f)\,e^{-j2\pi f T}
$$

**Step 8 — The resulting peak SNR is:**

$$
\text{SNR}_{max} = \frac{2E}{N_0}
$$

**Conclusion:** The matched filter is the unique linear filter achieving this SNR. It is *optimal* in AWGN. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Range Resolution of a Rectangular Pulse

**Statement:** A radar transmitting a rectangular pulse of width $\tau$ can resolve two targets separated by at least $\Delta R = c\tau/2$.

**Step 1 — Two targets at ranges $R_1$ and $R_2$.** The echoes arrive at times:

$$
t_1 = \frac{2 R_1}{c}, \quad t_2 = \frac{2 R_2}{c}
$$

**Step 2 — Time separation of echoes:**

$$
\Delta t = t_2 - t_1 = \frac{2(R_2 - R_1)}{c} = \frac{2 \Delta R}{c}
$$

**Step 3 — Rayleigh criterion for resolution:** Two rectangular pulses of width $\tau$ are just resolved when their separation equals the pulse width (one pulse's trailing edge aligns with the next pulse's leading edge):

$$
\Delta t = \tau
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the minimum resolvable range difference:**

$$
\frac{2 \Delta R}{c} = \tau \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \Delta R = \frac{c\,\tau}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (KTU favorite):** For $\tau = 1\ \mu s$:

$$
\Delta R = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 150 \text{ m}
$$

**Conclusion:** To achieve 15 m range resolution, the pulse width must be $\le 100$ ns, OR one must use **pulse compression** to synthesize a 100 ns effective pulse from a longer, higher-energy LFM pulse. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: LFM Chirp — Instantaneous Frequency and Pulse Compression Ratio

**Step 1 — Define the LFM complex envelope:**

$$
\tilde{u}(t) = \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right) e^{j\pi k t^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Instantaneous frequency is the time derivative of phase:**

$$
f_i(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{d}{dt}\!\left[\pi k t^2\right] = k t
$$

So the frequency sweeps linearly through the pulse, with slope $k = \Delta f / \tau$ (Hz/s).

**Step 3 — Matched filter impulse response** (using the matched filter result $h(t) = \tilde{u}^*(-t)$ for baseband):

$$
h(t) = \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right) e^{-j\pi k t^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Output of the matched filter** (convolution):

$$
y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \tilde{u}(\lambda)\,h(t - \lambda)\,d\lambda = \int_{-\tau/2}^{\tau/2} e^{j\pi k \lambda^2}\,e^{-j\pi k (t - \lambda)^2}\,d\lambda
$$

**Step 5 — Expand the second exponent:**

$$
(t - \lambda)^2 = t^2 - 2 t \lambda + \lambda^2
$$

So the integrand becomes:

$$
e^{j\pi k \lambda^2} \cdot e^{-j\pi k (t^2 - 2 t \lambda + \lambda^2)} = e^{-j\pi k t^2} \cdot e^{j 2\pi k t \lambda}
$$

**Step 6 — Factor out the constant phase term:**

$$
y(t) = e^{-j\pi k t^2} \int_{-\tau/2}^{\tau/2} e^{j 2\pi k t \lambda}\,d\lambda
$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the integral. The limits clamp it to $\tau$:**

$$
\int_{-\tau/2}^{\tau/2} e^{j 2\pi k t \lambda}\,d\lambda = \frac{\sin(\pi k t \tau)}{\pi k t}
$$

**Step 8 — Use $k = \Delta f / \tau$. Define compressed time $\tau_c = 1/\Delta f$:**

$$
y(t) = e^{-j\pi k t^2} \cdot \tau \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t}{\tau_c}\right)
$$

where $\text{sinc}(x) = \sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)$.

**Step 9 — The magnitude of the output:**

$$
\vert y(t) \vert = \tau \left\vert \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t}{\tau_c}\right) \right\vert
$$

This is a sinc-function pulse of width $\approx \tau_c = 1/\Delta f$, centered at $t = 0$.

**Step 10 — The pulse compression ratio (PCR):**

$$
\text{PCR} = \frac{\tau}{\tau_c} = \tau \cdot \Delta f = \text{TBP}
$$

**Conclusion:** An LFM chirp of width $\tau$ and swept bandwidth $\Delta f$ compresses to a pulse of width $1/\Delta f$, achieving a compression ratio exactly equal to the time-bandwidth product. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Maximum Unambiguous Range and Blind Speed

**Step 4a — Maximum Unambiguous Range:**

A pulse transmitted at $t = 0$ and reflected from a target at range $R$ returns at $t = 2R/c$. The next pulse is transmitted at $t = T$. If the echo from the first pulse arrives *after* the second pulse is sent, the radar misinterprets the echo as belonging to a *later* pulse.

**Constraint for unambiguity:**

$$
\frac{2 R}{c} \le T \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R \le R_{ua} = \frac{cT}{2}
$$

**Step 4b — Blind Speed:**

A target moving with radial velocity $v_r$ produces a Doppler shift $f_d = 2v_r/\lambda$. If the PRF is $f_r$, Doppler shifts exceeding $f_r/2$ alias. The maximum unambiguous Doppler is $f_r/2$, giving:

$$
v_{ua} = \frac{\lambda f_r}{4}
$$

A *blind speed* occurs when the Doppler shift equals an integer multiple of $f_r$:

$$
v_{blind} = \frac{n\,\lambda\,f_r}{2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots
$$

At these velocities, the moving target is indistinguishable from stationary clutter, and the radar "cannot see" it. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Ambiguity Function of a Rectangular Pulse

**Step 1 — Rectangular pulse:** $s(t) = \text{rect}(t/\tau)$.

**Step 2 — Compute the zero-Doppler cut** (autocorrelation):

$$
\chi(\tau, 0) = \int s(t)\,s^*(t - \tau)\,dt
$$

**Step 3 — For two overlapping rectangles, the area of overlap is $\tau - \vert \tau \vert$ for $\vert \tau \vert \le \tau$:**

$$
\chi(\tau, 0) = \begin{cases} 1 - \dfrac{\vert \tau \vert}{\tau_{pulse}} & \vert \tau \vert \le \tau_{pulse} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the zero-range cut** (Fourier transform of $|s(t)|^2$):

$$
\chi(0, f_d) = \int \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right) e^{j2\pi f_d t}\,dt = \tau \cdot \text{sinc}(f_d \tau)
$$

**Step 5 — Full ambiguity function** (combining via the time-shift property):

$$
\chi(\tau, f_d) = \left(1 - \frac{\vert \tau \vert}{\tau_{pulse}}\right) \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left[f_d \left(\tau_{pulse} - \vert \tau \vert\right)\right] \cdot e^{j\pi f_d \tau}, \quad \vert \tau \vert \le \tau_{pulse}
$$

**Conclusion:** The ambiguity function of a rectangular pulse is a **thumbtack shape** (a tall narrow spike along the $\tau$-axis with a sinc-shaped skirt along $f_d$). The width of the central spike in $\tau$ is $\tau$, and in $f_d$ is $1/\tau$, reflecting the inverse time-bandwidth uncertainty principle. $\blacksquare$

## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Functional Classification of Radar Waveforms

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[RADAR WAVEFORMS] --> B[Continuous Wave CW]

    D --> D4[Phase Coded Frank]

    D1 --> D1A[Up Chirp]

    D3 --> D3A[Barker 13 Code]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
