
**Interpretation:** The diagram classifies the three principal waveform families and their sub-variants. CW is Doppler-only, Pulsed is range-measuring, and Pulse-Compressed (LFM, NLFM, Phase-Coded) combines high energy with high resolution.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Matched Filter Receiver (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    RF[Received Echo RF Signal r t] --> LNA[Low Noise Amplifier]
    LNA --> MIX[Mixer with Local Oscillator f_c]
    MIX --> IF[Intermediate Frequency IF Stage]
    IF --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> MF[Matched Filter h t = s* T - t]
    MF --> DET[Envelope Detector]
    DET --> TH[Threshold Detector]
    TH --> OUT[Target Detection and Range Output]

    REF[Reference Signal Generator s t] --> REF_STORE[Stored Replica Memory]
    REF_STORE --> MF

    style RF fill:#FFB6C1
    style MF fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style OUT fill:#FFD700
```

**Interpretation:** The received RF echo is amplified, down-converted to IF, digitized, and then passed through a matched filter implemented digitally. The matched filter cross-correlates the received signal with a stored replica of the transmitted waveform. The output is envelope-detected and thresholded to declare a detection. The matched filter maximizes SNR at the sampling instant, ensuring optimal detection in AWGN.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: LFM Pulse Compression Radar Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Waveform Generator LFM Chirp] --> B[Up Converter to RF f_c]
    B --> C[High Power Amplifier HPA]
    C --> D[Circulator / Duplexer]
    D --> E[Antenna Radiate]

    E --> F[Target Echo Return]
    F --> D
    D --> G[Low Noise Amplifier LNA]
    G --> H[Down Converter to Baseband]
    H --> I[Matched Filter Pulse Compressor]
    I --> J[Compressed Pulse Output narrow sinc]
    J --> K[Range and Doppler Processor]

    style A fill:#87CEEB
    style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#FFD700
```

**Interpretation:** The chirp generator creates a long LFM pulse, which is up-converted, amplified, and transmitted. The echo is received, down-converted, and passed through the matched filter (compressor) which collapses the long chirp into a short, high-peak pulse. The compressed pulse output enables high range resolution while preserving the energy of the long transmitted pulse.

---

### 4.4 Ambiguity Function Surface (Conceptual Block Layout)

```mermaid
graph TD
    AF[AMBIGUITY FUNCTION X tau f_d] --> P[Properties]
    P --> P1[Thumbtack Shape for Rectangular Pulse]
    P --> P2[Sheared Ridge for LFM Chirp]
    P --> P3[Pin Bed for Phase Coded]
    P --> P4[Volume Invariance Constraint]

    P1 --> P1A[Wide tau narrow f_d]
    P2 --> P2A[Coupled range Doppler: tilt]
    P3 --> P3A[Many low sidelobes: Barker -22 dB]

    P4 --> P4A[Integral of modulus squared is constant]
    P4 --> P4B[Trade off resolution in range vs Doppler]

    style AF fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P2 fill:#FFA07A
```

**Interpretation:** The ambiguity function plots matched-filter response versus target range offset ($\tau$) and Doppler shift ($f_d$). Different waveforms produce different topologies: a thumbtack (rectangular), a slanted ridge (LFM), or a bed of pins (phase-coded). The volume invariance theorem states that redistributing ambiguity is free, but the total integrated energy is fixed — you cannot simultaneously have infinite resolution in both dimensions.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam Pattern:** Short-answer type. Each requires a precise 1-paragraph answer with the relevant formula.

---

**Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**What is a matched filter? Write its impulse response and the maximum achievable output SNR.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **matched filter** is the optimal linear time-invariant filter that maximizes the output signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) for a known signal corrupted by additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN).

Its impulse response is:

$$
h(t) = s^*(T - t)
$$

where $s(t)$ is the known transmitted signal, $T$ is the observation interval, and $*$ denotes complex conjugation. In the frequency domain:

$$
H(f) = K\,S^*(f)\,e^{-j2\pi f T}
$$

The maximum achievable output SNR at the sampling instant is:

$$
\text{SNR}_{out} = \frac{2E}{N_0}
$$

where $E = \int \vert s(t) \vert^2\,dt$ is the signal energy and $N_0/2$ is the two-sided noise power spectral density. **(3 marks)**

---

**Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]`

**Define the ambiguity function of a radar signal. List any two of its important properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **ambiguity function** of a radar signal $s(t)$ is a two-dimensional function of time delay $\tau$ and Doppler frequency $f_d$ that characterizes the output of the matched filter when the received echo is delayed by $\tau$ and Doppler-shifted by $f_d$ relative to the transmitted signal:

$$
\chi(\tau, f_d) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} s(t)\,s^*(t - \tau)\,e^{j2\pi f_d t}\,dt
$$

**Two important properties:**

1. **Peak Value Property:** The ambiguity function attains its maximum magnitude at the origin: $\vert \chi(0, 0) \vert = 1$ (or $2E$, depending on normalization).

2. **Volume Invariance:** The total volume under $\vert \chi(\tau, f_d) \vert^2$ is constant, independent of the waveform:

$$
\iint \vert \chi(\tau, f_d) \vert^2\,d\tau\,df_d = (2E)^2
$$

This implies that improving resolution in one domain (range) degrades it in the other (Doppler). **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. **TWO alternatives are provided**; the student answers ONE.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Pulsed & CW Radar Analysis**

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]`

A pulsed radar operating at **10 GHz** has a pulse width of **2 µs**, a PRF of **1 kHz**, and a peak transmit power of **1 MW**.

Compute:
1. The maximum unambiguous range.
2. The range resolution.
3. The duty cycle and average power.
4. The first blind speed.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $f_c = 10\ \text{GHz} = 10^{10}\ \text{Hz}$, $\tau = 2\ \mu s = 2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s}$, $f_r = 1\ \text{kHz} = 10^3\ \text{Hz}$, $P_{peak} = 10^6\ \text{W}$, $c = 3 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$, $\lambda = c/f_c = 0.03\ \text{m}$.

**1. Maximum Unambiguous Range:**

$$
R_{ua} = \frac{c}{2 f_r} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^3} = 1.5 \times 10^5\ \text{m} = 150\ \text{km}
$$

**2. Range Resolution:**

$$
\Delta R = \frac{c \tau}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 2 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 300\ \text{m}
$$

**3. Duty Cycle & Average Power:**

$$
d = \tau f_r = 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^3 = 2 \times 10^{-3} = 0.002
$$

$$
P_{avg} = P_{peak} \cdot d = 10^6 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} = 2000\ \text{W} = 2\ \text{kW}
$$

**4. First Blind Speed:**

$$
v_{blind} = \frac{\lambda f_r}{2} = \frac{0.03 \times 10^3}{2} = 15\ \text{m/s} \quad (54\ \text{km/h})
$$

**Part (a) answer summary:** $R_{ua} = 150$ km, $\Delta R = 300$ m, $d = 0.2\%$, $P_{avg} = 2$ kW, $v_{blind} = 15$ m/s. **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]`

Compare **CW radar** and **Pulsed radar** under the following heads: (i) range measurement capability, (ii) velocity measurement capability, (iii) transmit/receive isolation, (iv) typical application.

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | CW Radar | Pulsed Radar |
|-----------|----------|--------------|
| **(i) Range Measurement** | **Not possible** (continuous transmission, no time reference) | **Possible** (echo time delay → range) |
| **(ii) Velocity Measurement** | **Excellent** (Doppler shift directly obtained) | **Possible but ambiguous** (blind speeds at $v = n\lambda f_r/2$) |
| **(iii) T/R Isolation** | **Difficult** (simultaneous transmit & receive, needs separate antennas) | **Easier** (transmit during pulse, receive during listen period) |
| **(iv) Typical Application** | Police radar guns, doppler weather radar, motion sensors | Air traffic control, surveillance radar, fire-control radar, SAR |

**Discussion:** A CW radar is essentially a *Doppler-only* instrument — it can detect motion with extreme sensitivity (a fraction of a m/s) but cannot tell you how far the target is. Pulsed radars trade some Doppler clarity for unambiguous range information, with the PRF dictating the trade-off. Modern radars often use **hybrid CW/pulsed** or **FMCW (Frequency-Modulated CW)** waveforms to combine the advantages of both.

**Part (b) answer summary:** Tabular comparison with brief engineering rationale. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — LFM Chirp & Matched Filter Analysis**

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

An LFM chirp radar transmits pulses of width **τ = 20 µs** with a swept bandwidth **Δf = 10 MHz** at carrier frequency **f_c = 5.6 GHz**. Compute:

1. The chirp rate $k$.
2. The time-bandwidth product.
3. The compressed pulse width after matched filtering.
4. The pulse compression gain in dB.
5. The range resolution improvement factor over an equivalent unmodulated rectangular pulse of the same width.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\tau = 20 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s}$, $\Delta f = 10 \times 10^6\ \text{Hz}$, $f_c = 5.6 \times 10^9\ \text{Hz}$.

**1. Chirp Rate:**

$$
k = \frac{\Delta f}{\tau} = \frac{10 \times 10^6}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = 5 \times 10^{11}\ \text{Hz/s}
$$

**2. Time-Bandwidth Product:**

$$
\text{TBP} = \tau \cdot \Delta f = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 10 \times 10^6 = 200
$$

**3. Compressed Pulse Width:**

$$
\tau_c = \frac{1}{\Delta f} = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^6} = 0.1\ \mu s = 100\ \text{ns}
$$

**4. Pulse Compression Gain in dB:**

$$
G_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(\text{TBP}) = 10 \log_{10}(200) = 10 \times 2.301 = 23.01\ \text{dB}
$$

**5. Range Resolution Improvement:**

Unmodulated pulse: $\Delta R_1 = c\tau/2 = 3 \times 10^8 \times 20 \times 10^{-6} / 2 = 3000$ m = 3 km.

Compressed pulse: $\Delta R_2 = c\tau_c/2 = 3 \times 10^8 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} / 2 = 15$ m.

Improvement factor = $\Delta R_1 / \Delta R_2 = 3000/15 = 200$ (consistent with TBP).

**Part (a) answer summary:** $k = 5 \times 10^{11}$ Hz/s, TBP = 200, $\tau_c = 100$ ns, gain = 23 dB, range resolution improves from 3 km to 15 m. **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]`

Derive the **matched filter impulse response** for a known transmitted signal $s(t)$ embedded in AWGN. State and justify the **Cauchy-Schwarz inequality** step in the proof.

**Model Solution:**

**Statement:** A linear filter $H(f)$ maximizing output SNR at $t_0$ for known signal $s(t)$ in AWGN of two-sided PSD $N_0/2$ has impulse response $h(t) = s^*(t_0 - t)$.

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Output signal at $t_0$:

$$
y(t_0) = \int H(f) S(f) e^{j2\pi f t_0}\,df
$$

**Step 2:** Output noise variance:

$$
\sigma_n^2 = \int \frac{N_0}{2} \vert H(f) \vert^2\,df
$$

**Step 3:** SNR functional:

$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{\vert y(t_0) \vert^2}{\sigma_n^2} = \frac{\left\vert \int H(f) S(f) e^{j2\pi f t_0}\,df \right\vert^2}{\int (N_0/2) \vert H(f) \vert^2\,df}
$$

**Step 4 — Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality:**

For any two complex functions $A(f)$ and $B(f)$:

$$
\left\vert \int A(f) B(f)\,df \right\vert^2 \le \int \vert A(f) \vert^2\,df \cdot \int \vert B(f) \vert^2\,df
$$

with equality if and only if $A(f) = K\,B^*(f)$ for some constant $K$.

**Step 5 — Apply with $A(f) = H(f)$ and $B(f) = S(f) e^{j2\pi f t_0}$:**

$$
\left\vert \int H(f) S(f) e^{j2\pi f t_0}\,df \right\vert^2 \le \int \vert H(f) \vert^2\,df \cdot \int \vert S(f) \vert^2\,df
$$

By Parseval's theorem, $\int \vert S(f) \vert^2 df = \int \vert s(t) \vert^2 dt = E$.

**Step 6 — Bound the SNR:**

$$
\text{SNR} \le \frac{\int \vert H(f) \vert^2\,df \cdot E}{\int (N_0/2) \vert H(f) \vert^2\,df} = \frac{2E}{N_0}
$$

**Step 7 — Equality condition yields the optimal filter:**

$$
H(f) = K\,S^*(f)\,e^{-j2\pi f t_0}
$$

Taking the inverse Fourier transform:

$$
h(t) = K\,s^*(t_0 - t)
$$

Setting $t_0 = T$ and $K = 1$ (normalization): $h(t) = s^*(T - t)$. $\blacksquare$

**Justification of Cauchy-Schwarz step:** The inequality is the cornerstone of the proof. It bounds the inner product of two functions by the product of their $L^2$-norms. Equality holds when the two functions are linearly dependent (i.e., one is a constant multiple of the conjugate of the other). This is precisely the matched filter condition — $H(f)$ is "matched" (i.e., conjugate) to the *delayed* signal spectrum.

**Part (b) answer summary:** 7-step derivation with Cauchy-Schwarz as the bounding principle. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Radar Waveform Questions (KTU Examiner Observations):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in range equations.** A radar signal travels *out and back*, so the round-trip time is $2R/c$. Writing $R = ct$ instead of $R = ct/2$ is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
>
> 2. **Confusing PRF and pulse repetition interval (PRI).** They are reciprocals: $f_r = 1/T$. Many students interchange them in formulas.
>
> 3. **Mixing up the Doppler formula variants.** Remember: $f_d = 2v_r/\lambda = 2v_r f_c/c$. Use whichever is convenient, but be consistent with units.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the time-bandwidth product is dimensionless.** Pulse compression gain is in dB: $10 \log_{10}(\tau \Delta f)$, not in linear units.
>
> 5. **In matched filter derivations, students often skip the Cauchy-Schwarz step.** The KTU examiner specifically allocates 1–2 marks for explicitly *stating and applying* the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality. Never omit it.
>
> 6. **Confusing ambiguity function with cross-correlation.** The ambiguity function includes the Doppler term $e^{j2\pi f_d t}$; cross-correlation does not. The AF is a 2-D function; cross-correlation is 1-D.
>
> 7. **In LFM pulse compression, forgetting the sinc envelope.** The compressed pulse is $|\text{sinc}(t/\tau_c)|$, not a perfect rectangle. The first sidelobe is at $-13.2$ dB; use windowing (Hamming, Taylor) to suppress if needed.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Radar Waveforms" (Module 3, KTU 2024)**

- [x] Every radar signal = **complex envelope $\tilde{s}(t)$ × carrier** $e^{j2\pi f_c t}$. Know the math.
- [x] **CW radar** = Doppler only (no range). Use separate Tx/Rx antennas.
- [x] **Pulsed radar** = range via $\Delta t = 2R/c$. Range resolution $\Delta R = c\tau/2$.
- [x] **Unambiguous range** $R_{ua} = cT/2 = c/(2f_r)$. **Unambiguous velocity** $v_{ua} = \lambda f_r/4$.
- [x] **Blind speed** = $n \lambda f_r/2$, $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$
- [x] **High PRF** → unambiguous velocity, ambiguous range. **Low PRF** → vice versa.
- [x] **LFM Chirp** has quadratic phase $\pi k t^2$, linear instantaneous frequency $f_i(t) = f_c + kt$.
- [x] **Time-Bandwidth Product** = $\tau \Delta f$ = **pulse compression ratio** = **processing gain**.
- [x] **Matched filter** $h(t) = s^*(T - t)$ → $H(f) = K S^*(f) e^{-j2\pi fT}$. Optimal in AWGN.
- [x] **Maximum SNR** at matched-filter output: $\text{SNR}_{out} = 2E/N_0$.
- [x] **Cauchy-Schwarz inequality** is the *key* step in the matched filter proof.
- [x] **Ambiguity function** $\chi(\tau, f_d)$ = 2-D map of range-Doppler response.
- [x] **Volume invariance:** $\iint |\chi(\tau, f_d)|^2 d\tau\,df_d$ = constant. Cannot beat physics.
- [x] **Phase-coded waveforms** (Barker, Gold, Frank) — Barker-13 gives -22.3 dB sidelobes.
- [x] **FMCW** combines CW + frequency sweep → simultaneous range + velocity.
- [x] **Sinc envelope** is the compressed LFM pulse shape; first sidelobe at -13.2 dB.
- [x] **Radar range equation:** $P_r = P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma / [(4\pi)^3 R^4]$.
- [x] **Always double the time** when computing radar range from a one-way delay.
- [x] **Carrier wavelength** $\lambda = c/f_c$. Use $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s in KTU numericals.

> **End of Topic Notes — Radar Waveforms (Module 3, OEECT832, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple Form of Radar Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Simple Form of Radar Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **Simple Form of the Radar Equation** is the fundamental mathematical model that relates the **maximum detection range** of a monostatic radar system to the transmitted power, antenna gain, operating wavelength, target radar cross-section (RCS), and the minimum detectable signal at the receiver. It quantifies the two-way power propagation process: the forward path (transmitter → target) and the return path (target → receiver).

In its canonical form, the simple radar equation is expressed as:

$$R_{max} = \left[\frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, S_{min}}\right]^{1/4}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The simple form assumes a **monostatic radar** (single antenna for transmit/receive), an **isotropic point scatterer** target, and a noise-free environment. It is the foundational equation from which all advanced radar equations (search radar, tracking radar, MTI, SAR) are derived.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a large canyon. You **shout loudly** (transmit power) towards the opposite cliff. The sound wave spreads out as it travels. Some of it hits the cliff face (the **target**), and a tiny echo bounces back to you. The loudness of the echo you hear depends on:

- **How loud you shouted** → Transmitted Power ($P_t$)
- **How well you focused your voice using a megaphone** → Antenna Gain ($G$)
- **How far the cliff is** → Range ($R$)
- **How big and reflective the cliff is** → Radar Cross-Section ($\sigma$)
- **How quiet the surroundings are** → Minimum Detectable Signal ($S_{min}$)

If the echo is quieter than the ambient noise, you cannot detect the cliff. The radar equation mathematically describes exactly this principle for electromagnetic waves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Remember:**
> - $4\pi$ : Solid angle of a full sphere (steradians) = **12.566 sr**
> - $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s : Speed of light
> - $f$ (Hz) and $\lambda$ (m) related by: $c = f\lambda$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic (The "Why" and "How")

The derivation of the simple radar equation follows a clean four-stage logic flow. Each stage captures a unique physical phenomenon:

1. **Power Density at the Target (Spreading Loss — Forward Path):**
   - *Why?* EM energy from a directional antenna spreads spherically as it propagates.
   - *How?* The product of transmit power and antenna gain, divided by the surface area of a sphere of radius $R$, gives the power density at the target.

2. **Power Intercepted and Re-radiated by the Target:**
   - *Why?* Only a portion of the incident wave bounces back, governed by the target's geometric and reflective properties.
   - *How?* Multiplying the incident power density by the target's Radar Cross-Section ($\sigma$) gives the equivalent re-radiated power (treating the target as an isotropic radiator).

3. **Power Density of the Echo at the Radar (Spreading Loss — Return Path):**
   - *Why?* The re-radiated echo again spreads spherically over the return path.
   - *How?* Divide the re-radiated power by $4\pi R^2$.

4. **Power Captured by the Receiving Antenna:**
   - *Why?* The receiving antenna can only collect energy falling within its effective aperture.
   - *How?* Multiply the return power density by the antenna's effective area $A_e = G\lambda^2/4\pi$.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** This equation is the **design cornerstone** for all radar systems — military surveillance, air traffic control, weather radar, automotive collision avoidance, and space-debris tracking. It lets engineers predict performance BEFORE building expensive hardware.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Symbol | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power density at target | $S_1 = \frac{P_t G}{4\pi R^2}$ | W/m² | Forward-path power per unit area |
| 2 | Power re-radiated by target | $P_2 = S_1 \cdot \sigma$ | W | Echo power from RCS |
| 3 | Echo power density at radar | $S_2 = \frac{P_2}{4\pi R^2}$ | W/m² | Return-path power per unit area |
| 4 | Received echo power | $P_r = S_2 \cdot A_e$ | W | Power collected by Rx antenna |
| 5 | Effective aperture | $A_e = \frac{G \lambda^2}{4\pi}$ | m² | Physical collection area equivalent |
| 6 | Monostatic radar equation (received power) | $P_r = \frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$ | W | Simple-form radar equation |
| 7 | Maximum range | $R_{max} = \left[\frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 S_{min}}\right]^{1/4}$ | m | Detection range limit |
| 8 | Bistatic radar form | $P_r = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R_t^2 R_r^2}$ | W | Separate Tx/Rx antennas |
| 9 | One-way path loss | $L_{1W} = \left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)^2$ | unitless | Ratio of transmitted to received isotropic power |
| 10 | Two-way path loss | $L_{2W} = \left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)^4$ | unitless | Radar's two-way spreading loss |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Units Convention:** If $\lambda$ is in **metres**, $R$ comes out in **metres**. For km, divide by 1000. KTU examiners frequently penalise incorrect unit conversions. Always state units explicitly in numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Simple Radar Equation

**Given:**
- Transmitted power: $P_t$ (W)
- Transmitting antenna gain: $G_t$
- Receiving antenna gain: $G_r$ (for monostatic, $G_t = G_r = G$)
- Operating wavelength: $\lambda$ (m)
- Target range: $R$ (m)
- Target radar cross-section: $\sigma$ (m²)
- Minimum detectable signal: $S_{min}$ (W)

---

**Step 1: Power density at the target location**

The transmit antenna concentrates power through its gain. At distance $R$, the power is spread uniformly over the surface of a sphere of area $4\pi R^2$.

$$
S_1 = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi R^2}
$$

*Logic:* Power density = (Effective Isotropic Radiated Power) / (Surface area of sphere).

---

**Step 2: Power captured and re-radiated by the target**

The target intercepts a portion of the incident wave determined by its Radar Cross-Section $\sigma$ (in m²). For the simple-form equation, the target is modelled as an **isotropic scatterer** (re-radiates equally in all directions).

$$
P_2 = S_1 \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, \sigma}{4\pi R^2}
$$

*Logic:* RCS has units of area because it represents the equivalent projected area of an isotropic scatterer that would return the same echo power.

---

**Step 3: Echo power density returned to the radar**

The re-radiated power $P_2$ spreads again over a sphere of radius $R$ on its return path.

$$
S_2 = \frac{P_2}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, \sigma}{(4\pi R^2)(4\pi R^2)} = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 \, R^4}
$$

*Logic:* This is the second spreading loss, causing the characteristic $1/R^4$ dependence of radar.

---

**Step 4: Power captured by the receiving antenna**

The receiving antenna has an effective aperture $A_e$ that physically collects a portion of the returning wave.

$$
P_r = S_2 \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, \sigma \, A_e}{(4\pi)^2 \, R^4}
$$

---

**Step 5: Substitute the effective aperture formula**

The relationship between antenna gain $G_r$ and effective aperture $A_e$ for any antenna is:

$$
A_e = \frac{G_r \, \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

Substituting this into the expression for $P_r$:

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 \, R^4} \cdot \frac{G_r \, \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R^4}
$$

---

**Step 6: Apply monostatic condition**

For a monostatic radar, the same antenna is used for both transmission and reception. Thus $G_t = G_r = G$.

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R^4}
$$

This is the **Simple Form of the Radar Equation**.

---

**Step 7: Solve for the maximum detection range**

For reliable detection, the received echo power $P_r$ must equal or exceed the receiver's minimum detectable signal $S_{min}$ (also called the receiver sensitivity threshold). Setting $P_r = S_{min}$ and solving for $R$:

$$
S_{min} = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R_{max}^4}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
R_{max}^4 = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, S_{min}}
$$

Taking the fourth root:

$$
R_{max} = \left[\frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, S_{min}}\right]^{1/4}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Famous $1/4$ Power Law:** Doubling the transmitted power increases range by only $2^{1/4} \approx 1.19$ (a 19% increase). To double the range, you must increase $P_t$ by a factor of **16**. This non-intuitive behaviour is the central insight of the radar equation.

### 3.2 Analytical Dependency Matrix

| Parameter Change | Effect on $R_{max}$ | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Double $P_t$ | $R \times 1.19$ | Poor return on power investment |
| Double $G$ | $R \times 1.41$ | Larger antennas are more effective |
| Double $\lambda$ | $R \times 1.41$ | Longer wavelengths = longer range |
| Double $\sigma$ | $R \times 1.19$ | Hard to control target properties |
| Halve $S_{min}$ | $R \times 1.41$ | Better receiver sensitivity is valuable |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Radar Equation Signal Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete signal propagation chain used in deriving the radar equation, from the transmitter to the receiver threshold decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Tx Power Source P_t] --> B[Transmit Antenna Gain G]
    B --> C[Forward Path Loss: 1 over 4 pi R squared]
    C --> D[Target Radar Cross Section sigma]
    D --> E[Return Path Loss: 1 over 4 pi R squared]
    E --> F[Receive Antenna Effective Aperture A_e]
    F --> G[Received Echo Power P_r]
    G --> H{Is P_r greater than S_min?}
    H -- Yes --> I[TARGET DETECTED]
    H -- No --> J[TARGET NOT DETECTED]

    subgraph Forward_Link
        A
        B
        C
    end

    subgraph Target_Interaction
        D
    end

    subgraph Return_Link
        E
        F
        G
    end

    subgraph Detection_Decision
        H
        I
        J
    end
```

### 4.2 Concept Hierarchy — Equation Component Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Simple Radar Equation] --> F1[Transmitted Power P_t]
    R --> F2[Antenna Gain G]
    R --> F3[Wavelength lambda]
    R --> F4[Target RCS sigma]
    R --> F5[Min Detectable Signal S_min]
    R --> F6[Geometry factor 4 pi cubed]

    F2 --> F2a[Transmit Gain G_t]
    F2 --> F2b[Receive Gain G_r]
    F2a --> F2c[Monostatic: G_t equals G_r]
    F2b --> F2c
```

### 4.3 The Two-Way Propagation Path (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Radar Antenna Position] -->|Forward Path: P_t G / 4 pi R squared| T1[Target at Range R]
    T1 -->|Echo back: sigma isotropic scatter| R1

    R1 -.->|Receives P_r| R2[Receiver Threshold S_min]
    R2 -.-> D{Detect Decision}
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualising the $R^4$ Law:** Notice in the diagram that the signal makes a round trip — it propagates outward ($R^2$ loss) and then propagates back ($R^2$ loss). The product of these two losses gives the famous $R^4$ two-way dependence unique to radar (versus the $R^2$ one-way dependence of communications links).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
State the simple form of the radar equation and explain the significance of the $1/R^4$ dependence of received echo power on range.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The simple form of the radar equation is:

$$P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R^4}$$

**Significance of $1/R^4$ dependence:** The echo power is inversely proportional to the **fourth power of the range**. This is because the EM wave experiences **two-way propagation loss**:
- Forward path: Power density falls as $1/R^2$ (spherical spreading).
- Return path: Echo power density falls as another $1/R^2$.
- Combined two-way loss: $1/R^2 \times 1/R^2 = 1/R^4$.

This makes radar a **range-critical system**: small increases in range demand disproportionately large increases in transmitted power or antenna gain. *(1 mark for equation, 1 mark for forward path, 1 mark for return path explanation)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
Define Radar Cross-Section (RCS). What are its units, and how does it appear in the simple radar equation?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** The Radar Cross-Section ($\sigma$) of a target is a measure of its ability to reflect electromagnetic energy back to the radar. It is defined as the equivalent projected area of an **isotropic scatterer** that would return the same echo power as the actual target.

**Units:** Square metres (m²) — *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for units)*

**Role in the equation:** $\sigma$ appears in the **numerator** of the simple radar equation:

$$P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R^4}$$

A larger RCS produces a stronger echo, increasing $P_r$ and consequently increasing $R_{max}$ as $\sigma^{1/4}$. *(1 mark for equation role)*

Typical RCS values: small fighter aircraft ~ 1 m², large airliner ~ 100 m², ship ~ 10,000 m².

---

### PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks** sub-parts. You must attempt **ONE full question** (either Q.A or Q.B) — internal choice is provided.

---

#### ❖ QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Starting from first principles, derive the **simple form of the radar equation** for a monostatic radar. Clearly state all assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A monostatic radar operating at **10 GHz** has a peak transmitted power of **1 MW**, an antenna gain of **40 dB**, and a minimum detectable signal of **$10^{-13}$ W**. If the target has an RCS of **20 m²**, calculate the **maximum detection range**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

Refer to the exhaustive derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Expected answer structure:

1. Assumption 1: Monostatic radar ($G_t = G_r = G$). *(0.5 mark)*
2. Assumption 2: Target modelled as isotropic point scatterer with RCS $\sigma$. *(0.5 mark)*
3. Assumption 3: Free-space propagation, no atmospheric attenuation. *(0.5 mark)*
4. Step 1: Power density at target: $S_1 = P_t G / 4\pi R^2$ *(1 mark)*
5. Step 2: Power re-radiated: $P_2 = S_1 \sigma$ *(1 mark)*
6. Step 3: Echo power density at radar: $S_2 = P_2 / 4\pi R^2$ *(1 mark)*
7. Step 4: Received power using $A_e = G\lambda^2/4\pi$, substitute $G_t = G_r = G$ *(1.5 marks)*
8. Final boxed equation + $R_{max}$ expression *(1 mark)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- $f = 10$ GHz $\Rightarrow \lambda = c/f = (3 \times 10^8) / (10 \times 10^9) = 0.03$ m
- $P_t = 1$ MW $= 10^6$ W
- $G = 40$ dB $\Rightarrow$ linear gain $G = 10^{40/10} = 10^4$
- $S_{min} = 10^{-13}$ W
- $\sigma = 20$ m²

**Formula:**

$$
R_{max} = \left[\frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, S_{min}}\right]^{1/4}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute numerator:**

$$
P_t \cdot G^2 \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma = (10^6) \cdot (10^4)^2 \cdot (0.03)^2 \cdot 20
$$

$$
= 10^6 \cdot 10^8 \cdot 9 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 20
$$

$$
= 10^6 \cdot 10^8 \cdot 1.8 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
= 1.8 \times 10^{13}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute denominator:**

$$
(4\pi)^3 \cdot S_{min} = (12.566)^3 \cdot 10^{-13}
$$

$$
= 1984.4 \cdot 10^{-13} \approx 1.9844 \times 10^{-10}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio:**

$$
\frac{1.8 \times 10^{13}}{1.9844 \times 10^{-10}} = 9.07 \times 10^{22}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the fourth root:**

$$
R_{max} = (9.07 \times 10^{22})^{1/4} = (9.07)^{0.25} \times (10^{22})^{0.25}
$$

$$
(9.07)^{0.25} \approx 1.737, \quad (10^{22})^{0.25} = 10^{5.5} \approx 3.162 \times 10^5
$$

$$
R_{max} \approx 1.737 \times 3.162 \times 10^5 \approx 5.49 \times 10^5 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{max} \approx 549 \text{ km}}
$$

---

#### ❖ QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the **two-way propagation path** in a radar system. Derive the expression for **two-way path loss** and state its significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A radar receiver has a minimum detectable signal of **$10^{-12}$ W**. If the transmitted power is **500 kW**, antenna gain is **35 dB**, target RCS is **10 m²**, and operating frequency is **6 GHz**, determine the **maximum range** of detection. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Two-way propagation:** The EM wave from the radar travels to the target (forward path) and the reflected echo travels back to the radar (return path). Both paths suffer spherical spreading loss. *(2 marks)*

**Derivation of two-way path loss:**

Power density at target: $S_1 = P_t G_t / 4\pi R^2$ *(1.5 marks)*

Power intercepted by target: $P_2 = S_1 \cdot \sigma = P_t G_t \sigma / 4\pi R^2$ *(1 mark)*

Echo power density at radar: $S_2 = P_2 / 4\pi R^2 = P_t G_t \sigma / (4\pi)^2 R^4$ *(1.5 marks)*

**Two-way path loss** (ratio of transmitted to received isotropic power with $\sigma = 1$):

$$
L_{2W} = \frac{P_t}{P_r}\bigg|_{\text{isotropic, }\sigma=1} = (4\pi)^2 R^4 \cdot \frac{(4\pi)}{G\lambda^2} = \left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)^4
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{2W} = \left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)^4}
$$

**Significance:** The two-way loss grows as $R^4$, making radar extremely sensitive to range. Doubling $R$ increases path loss by a factor of **16**. This is the fundamental reason radar designers focus on high antenna gain and sensitive receivers. *(1 mark)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- $P_t = 500 \text{ kW} = 5 \times 10^5$ W
- $G = 35$ dB $\Rightarrow$ linear $G = 10^{35/10} = 10^{3.5} \approx 3162.3$
- $\sigma = 10$ m²
- $S_{min} = 10^{-12}$ W
- $f = 6$ GHz $\Rightarrow \lambda = c/f = (3 \times 10^8) / (6 \times 10^9) = 0.05$ m

**Step 1 — Numerator:**

$$
P_t \cdot G^2 \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma = (5 \times 10^5) \cdot (3162.3)^2 \cdot (0.05)^2 \cdot 10
$$

$$
G^2 = (3162.3)^2 \approx 10^7
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 5 \times 10^5 \cdot 10^7 \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 10 = 1.25 \times 10^{11}
$$

**Step 2 — Denominator:**

$$
(4\pi)^3 \cdot S_{min} = 1984.4 \cdot 10^{-12} \approx 1.9844 \times 10^{-9}
$$

**Step 3 — Ratio:**

$$
\frac{1.25 \times 10^{11}}{1.9844 \times 10^{-9}} = 6.30 \times 10^{19}
$$

**Step 4 — Fourth root:**

$$
R_{max} = (6.30 \times 10^{19})^{1/4} = (6.30)^{0.25} \times (10^{19})^{0.25}
$$

$$
(6.30)^{0.25} \approx 1.582, \quad (10^{19})^{0.25} = 10^{4.75} \approx 5.623 \times 10^4
$$

$$
R_{max} \approx 1.582 \times 5.623 \times 10^4 \approx 8.90 \times 10^4 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{max} \approx 89 \text{ km}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **dB vs Linear confusion (–2 marks):** $G = 40$ dB means $G = 10^4$ linearly, NOT 40. Always convert: $G_{\text{linear}} = 10^{G_{dB}/10}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the square on G (–2 marks):** In the monostatic equation, $G$ is **squared** because it appears once for Tx and once for Rx.
> 3. **Wrong wavelength calculation (–1 mark):** $\lambda = c/f$ where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s and $f$ must be in Hz.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions (–1 mark):** KTU evaluators deduct for missing "monostatic, isotropic scatterer, free-space" statements.
> 5. **Numerical rounding errors (–1 mark):** Carry at least 3 significant figures until the final step.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Equation:** $R_{max} = \left[\frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 S_{min}}\right]^{1/4}$
- ✅ **The $R^4$ law:** Two-way propagation creates a fourth-power dependence on range — unique to radar.
- ✅ **Assumptions:** Monostatic, isotropic scatterer, noise-free, free-space propagation.
- ✅ **dB-to-linear gain conversion:** $G_{\text{linear}} = 10^{G_{dB}/10}$ (power ratio).
- ✅ **Wavelength relation:** $\lambda = c/f$ with $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- ✅ **RCS units:** Always in m² (square metres), not dBsm unless explicitly asked.
- ✅ **RCS examples:** Stealth aircraft ~ 0.01 m²; small drone ~ 0.1 m²; car ~ 100 m²; ship ~ 10,000 m².
- ✅ **Doubling the range** requires a **16×** increase in $P_t$ (or $G$, or $\lambda$, or $\sigma$, or $1/S_{min}$).
- ✅ **Bistatic variant:** $P_r = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R_t^2 R_r^2}$ — used when Tx and Rx are at different locations.
- ✅ **Order of derivation to memorise:** (1) Power density at target → (2) RCS power capture → (3) Return power density → (4) Aperture capture → (5) Substitute $A_e$ → (6) Monostatic simplification.
- ✅ **Minimum Detectable Signal ($S_{min}$):** The weakest echo power the receiver can reliably detect above noise — typically set by receiver noise figure and bandwidth.
- ✅ **Effective Aperture:** $A_e = G\lambda^2/4\pi$ links the antenna's electrical (gain) and physical (collection area) properties.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radar Block Diagram and Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Radar Block Diagram and Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RADAR (Radio Detection And Ranging)** is an electromagnetic system for the detection and localization of reflecting objects (targets) such as aircraft, ships, vehicles, people, or natural terrain, by transmitting an RF signal and analyzing the **echo (back-scattered signal)** returned from the target.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, the radar system operates on the principle of **primary radar (active radar)**, where the system itself illuminates the target with RF energy and then listens for the reflected echo. The two fundamental measurements extracted are:

1. **Range (R)** — determined by the round-trip time delay $\tau$ of the echo.
2. **Direction (Angular Position)** — determined by the antenna's beam pointing.

The general radar functional chain consists of a **synchronizer (timer)**, a **transmitter**, a **duplexer (TR switch)**, a **common transmit/receive antenna**, a **sensitive receiver**, and a **display/indicator**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a deep valley and you shout loudly. The sound travels outward, hits the opposite mountain wall, and the **echo** returns to you. By measuring how long the echo takes to come back (and knowing the speed of sound), you can estimate **how far** the mountain is. If you rotate your head before shouting again, you can also figure out **which direction** the mountain lies in.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bats & SONAR Analogy** — A bat does the same thing but with sound (SONAR). A radar does the same thing but with **radio waves** travelling at the speed of light ($c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s). Because radio waves travel nearly a million times faster than sound, the timing circuits must operate with extreme precision — usually at the **microsecond** or **nanosecond** scale.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s** (the speed of the radar wave).
- **Radar frequency bands** (IEEE standard): L-band (**1–2 GHz**), S-band (**2–4 GHz**), C-band (**4–8 GHz**), X-band (**8–12 GHz**), Ku-band (**12–18 GHz**).
- **Pulse repetition frequency (PRF):** typically **100 Hz to 300 kHz**.
- **Pulse width ($\tau$):** typically **0.1 µs to 10 µs**.
- **Peak transmitted power:** ranges from **kilowatts (kW)** to **megawatts (MW)** for long-range surveillance radars.

## 1.4 Historical Engineering Footprint

The foundational principle of radar was first demonstrated by **Sir Robert Watson-Watt** in 1935 (UK). It became a decisive technology during World War II for early warning of enemy aircraft and ships. Today, civil and military applications include air traffic control, weather forecasting (Doppler weather radar), automotive adaptive cruise control, ground-penetrating radar (GPR), and space surveillance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Sequence of a Pulse Radar

The radar operation is a **time-synchronized, cyclic, transmit–receive process**. The block-by-block logic is as follows:

- **Step 1 — Synchronization (Timing):** The *synchronizer* (or timer) generates the master clock pulse that triggers the entire system. It defines the **PRF (Pulse Repetition Frequency)** and supplies a reference sweep to the display.
- **Step 2 — RF Pulse Generation:** A high-power RF oscillator (e.g., a magnetron) is gated ON for a very short duration ($\tau$) by a *pulse modulator*. This produces a short, high-peak-power RF burst (the *transmitted pulse*).
- **Step 3 — Duplexing (TR Switch):** The *duplexer* is a fast-acting switch that connects the antenna to the **transmitter** during transmission and to the **receiver** during reception. It protects the sensitive receiver from the high-power transmit pulse (which can be millions of times stronger than the echo).
- **Step 4 — Radiation:** The antenna, which is **highly directional** (high gain $G$), focuses the RF energy into a narrow beam aimed at the target. A small leakage sample of the transmitted pulse is also routed to the receiver to serve as the **local oscillator (coho/stalo)** reference.
- **Step 5 — Echo Reception:** The same antenna (in monostatic radar) intercepts the weak back-scattered echo from the target. The duplexer routes it to the receiver.
- **Step 6 — Receiver Processing:** The receiver is a **superheterodyne** chain: a *low-noise RF amplifier* (LNA) → *mixer* → *IF amplifier* → *envelope detector* → *video amplifier*. Its job is to amplify the extremely weak echo (often in picowatts) into a displayable signal.
- **Step 7 — Display:** The detected video is sent to a display (historically a PPI — Plan Position Indicator). The time delay between the transmitted pulse and the received echo gives the target's **slant range**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is range = $c\tau/2$?** — The pulse travels *out* to the target and *back*. So the total path is $2R$. Equating to $c \cdot t$ gives $R = c \cdot \tau / 2$. The factor **1/2 is mandatory** in every KTU range calculation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------------------|----------------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Target slant range | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$ | metres (m) | $\tau$ is round-trip echo delay |
| 2 | Pulse repetition period | $T = \dfrac{1}{f_r}$ where $f_r$ = PRF | seconds (s) | Sets the unambiguous range |
| 3 | Unambiguous range | $R_{u} = \dfrac{c}{2 f_r}$ | metres (m) | Maximum range without range ambiguity |
| 4 | Duty cycle | $d = \tau \cdot f_r$ | dimensionless | Fraction of time transmitter is ON |
| 5 | Average transmitted power | $P_{av} = P_t \cdot d = P_t \cdot \tau \cdot f_r$ | Watts (W) | Often limited by the modulator |
| 6 | Antenna gain (aperture) | $G = \dfrac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2}$ | dimensionless | $A_e$ = effective aperture |
| 7 | Free-space path loss (one way) | $L_{fs} = \left(\dfrac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)^2$ | dimensionless | Used in radar equation |
| 8 | Monostatic radar range equation (received echo power) | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$ | Watts (W) | $\sigma$ = target radar cross-section (m²) |
| 9 | Maximum detection range | $R_{max} = \left[\dfrac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 S_{min}}\right]^{1/4}$ | metres (m) | $S_{min}$ = minimum detectable signal |
| 10 | Doppler frequency shift (moving target) | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$ | Hertz (Hz) | $v_r$ = radial velocity of target |

> [!NOTE]
> In the monostatic radar range equation, $G_t = G_r = G$ because a single antenna is used for both transmit and receive. This is why $G$ appears **squared** in the numerator.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

The radar equation is the **single most important design tool** in radar engineering. A radar designer uses it to answer questions like: "What transmitter power is needed to detect a 1 m² target at 100 km?" or "What antenna diameter is required to achieve a 0.5° beamwidth?" It also reveals the **4th-power law of nature**: doubling the range requires a **16× increase** in transmitted power (or antenna gain, or target size).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Monostatic Radar Range Equation

We derive the equation by tracking the **power budget** of the radar signal from the transmitter to the receiver, assuming isotropic radiation and a point target.

**Step 1 — Power density at the target (using a directive antenna).**

If the transmitter radiates a peak power $P_t$ through an antenna of gain $G$, the power density incident on the target located at range $R$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_t &= \frac{P_t \, G}{4\pi R^2} \quad \text{(W/m}^2\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** A lossless isotropic antenna would spread the power over the surface of a sphere of radius $R$, i.e., $4\pi R^2$. A directive antenna of gain $G$ concentrates the power $G$ times more in the target's direction.

**Step 2 — Power intercepted and re-radiated by the target.**

The target intercepts an effective area equal to its **radar cross-section (RCS), $\sigma$** (measured in m²), and re-radiates it isotropically (worst-case assumption). The power re-radiated by the target is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{re-radiated}} &= S_t \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{4\pi R^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Power density of the echo back at the radar antenna.**

This re-radiated power spreads over a sphere of radius $R$ when it returns. So the echo power density at the receiving antenna is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_r &= \frac{P_{\text{re-radiated}}}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4\pi R^2)(4\pi R^2)} = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Echo power captured by the receiving antenna.**

The receiving antenna has an effective aperture $A_e$, which captures a portion of the echo equal to the power density times the area. Recall the antenna relation $A_e = G \lambda^2 / (4\pi)$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r &= S_r \cdot A_e = S_r \cdot \frac{G \lambda^2}{4\pi} = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 R^4} \cdot \frac{G \lambda^2}{4\pi}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form of the radar range equation.**

Combining the constants:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r &= \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R^4}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verification of units (dimensional check):**
> $[P_r] = \text{W} \cdot \text{dim}^{2} \cdot \text{m}^2 \cdot \text{m}^2 \,/\, \text{m}^4 = \text{W}$ ✔

**Step 6 — Solving for the maximum detectable range $R_{max}$.**

A target is declared "detected" only if the received echo power $P_r$ exceeds the receiver's **minimum detectable signal** $S_{min}$. Setting $P_r = S_{min}$ and solving for $R$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{min} &= \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R_{max}^4} \\[4pt]
R_{max}^4 &= \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, S_{min}} \\[4pt]
R_{max} &= \left[ \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, S_{min}} \right]^{1/4}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **The 4th-root rule:** $R_{max}$ is a very *gentle* function of $P_t$. To double the detection range, you must increase $P_t$ by a factor of $2^4 = 16$. This is why long-range radars use **enormous** peak power and **massive** antennas.

## 3.2 Derivation of Range from Time Delay

For a target at range $R$, the signal travels a total round-trip distance of $2R$ at the speed of light $c$. If the measured round-trip delay is $\tau$ seconds:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{One-way distance} &= c \cdot \tau = 2R \\[4pt]
\therefore R &= \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Numerical sanity check:** If a target is 150 km away, the round-trip delay is $\tau = 2R/c = 2(150 \times 10^3)/(3 \times 10^8) = 1 \text{ ms}$. So one-way time is 0.5 ms — perfectly measurable with modern electronics.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Pulse Radar vs CW Radar

| Feature | Pulse Radar | CW (Continuous Wave) Radar |
|---------|-------------|----------------------------|
| Transmission | Gated RF bursts | Continuous sinusoidal wave |
| Range measurement | Via time delay $\tau = 2R/c$ | Cannot directly measure range (no time mark) |
| Velocity measurement | Requires MTI / Doppler processing | Inherent (Doppler shift) |
| Peak power | Very high (kW–MW) | Low (W) |
| Typical use | Surveillance, ATC, military | Speed guns, missile seekers |
| Duplexer | Mandatory | Not required (separate Tx/Rx antennas) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Monostatic Pulse Radar

The figure below is a faithful Mermaid representation of the **block-level functional architecture** of a monostatic pulse radar, showing the signal flow from the synchronizer through the transmitter, duplexer, antenna, target, and back through the receiver to the display.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph timing["TIMING AND CONTROL"]
        syncNode["SYNCHRONIZER / TIMER"]
    end

    subgraph tx["TRANSMITTER CHAIN"]
        modNode["PULSE MODULATOR"]
        oscNode["RF POWER OSCILLATOR (MAGNETRON)"]
    end

    subgraph rfpath["ANTENNA AND DUPLEXER"]
        duplexNode["DUPLEXER (TR SWITCH)"]
        antennaNode["DIRECTIVE ANTENNA"]
    end

    subgraph target["TARGET SCENE"]
        tgtNode["TARGET AT RANGE R"]
    end

    subgraph rx["RECEIVER CHAIN"]
        lnaNode["LOW NOISE RF AMPLIFIER"]
        mixNode["MIXER WITH STALO AND COHO"]
        ifNode["IF AMPLIFIER AND MATCHED FILTER"]
        detNode["ENVELOPE DETECTOR"]
        vidNode["VIDEO AMPLIFIER"]
    end

    subgraph display["OUTPUT"]
        dispNode["DISPLAY / INDICATOR (PPI OR A SCOPE)"]
    end

    syncNode -- "TRIGGER PULSE" --> modNode
    syncNode -- "REFERENCE SWEEP" --> dispNode
    modNode -- "MODULATING PULSE" --> oscNode
    oscNode -- "HIGH POWER RF BURST" --> duplexNode
    duplexNode -- "RF OUT TO ANTENNA" --> antennaNode
    antennaNode -- "TRANSMITTED BEAM" --> tgtNode
    tgtNode -- "BACK SCATTERED ECHO" --> antennaNode
    antennaNode -- "WEAK ECHO" --> duplexNode
    duplexNode -- "ROUTED ECHO" --> lnaNode
    lnaNode -- "AMPLIFIED RF" --> mixNode
    mixNode -- "INTERMEDIATE FREQUENCY" --> ifNode
    ifNode -- "FILTERED IF" --> detNode
    detNode -- "VIDEO PULSE" --> vidNode
    vidNode -- "DISPLAY SIGNAL" --> dispNode
    oscNode -. "COHO REFERENCE LEAK" .-> mixNode
```

## 4.2 Functional Role of Each Block

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Synchronizer:** The "heartbeat" of the radar. Without it, range measurement is impossible.
> - **Pulse Modulator + RF Oscillator:** Together they form the high-power transmitter. The modulator is essentially a high-current switch (e.g., a thyratron or solid-state switch) that gates the DC power supply to the oscillator.
> - **Duplexer (TR Switch):** A **gas-filled discharge tube** or **ferrite circulator** that becomes a short circuit during the transmit pulse and a pass-through during reception. It protects the LNA, which would otherwise be destroyed by the multi-kilowatt transmit pulse.
> - **Antenna:** Usually a **parabolic reflector** or **phased array**. It must produce a narrow beam for good angular resolution.
> - **Receiver:** Almost universally a **superheterodyne** receiver. The *STALO* (Stable Local Oscillator) and *COHO* (Coherent Oscillator) together with the mixer perform the frequency down-conversion. Matched filtering maximizes the output SNR for a pulse of known shape.
> - **Display:** Historically a **CRT** (PPI for 2-D map, A-scope for amplitude vs range). Modern systems use digital raster displays or extract data into a tracker computer.

## 4.3 Signal Flow — A Closed-Loop View

The diagram above visually emphasizes the **closed-loop nature** of radar: the same antenna is reused for transmission and reception, gated by the duplexer. The synchronizer is the only element that "knows" when transmission occurred, and that knowledge is what allows the receiver to translate echo delay into target range.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in range:** Range = $c \tau / 2$, NOT $c \tau$. This is the **#1 mistake** in KTU scripts. Expect to lose **2 marks** outright.
> 2. **Confusing $G$ and $A_e$:** Many students write $P_r \propto G$ instead of $P_r \propto G^2$. Remember, monostatic radar uses the **same antenna** for both transmit and receive, so gain appears squared.
> 3. **Units of $\sigma$:** The radar cross-section $\sigma$ is in **m²**, not in dBsm unless the problem explicitly asks for the decibel form.
> 4. **Range equation exponent:** $R_{max}$ is the **4th root** of the right-hand side, not the square root. This is a classic KTU trap.
> 5. **Duplexer omission:** In a block diagram question, students frequently omit the duplexer or draw it incorrectly. Marks are reserved for its correct placement and labeling.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Radar. Briefly explain the basic principle of operation of a primary radar.**

**Model Answer:**

RADAR stands for **RAdio Detection And Ranging**. It is an electromagnetic system used to detect the presence of a target (such as an aircraft, ship, vehicle, or natural object), determine its **range**, **angular position**, and sometimes its **velocity**.

**Principle of operation:** A pulse of RF energy is generated by the radar transmitter and radiated into space by a highly directive antenna. When this pulse strikes a target, a small fraction of the incident energy is scattered back toward the radar. The radar antenna intercepts this **echo**, and the receiver amplifies and processes it. The round-trip time delay $\tau$ between transmission and reception is measured. The **range** of the target is then calculated as:

$$
R = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2}
$$

The **direction** of the target is inferred from the angular pointing of the antenna beam at the moment the echo is received. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List the main functional blocks of a pulse radar and state the function of the duplexer.**

**Model Answer:**

The main functional blocks of a pulse radar are:
1. **Synchronizer (Timer)**
2. **Transmitter** (Pulse Modulator + RF Oscillator)
3. **Duplexer (TR Switch)**
4. **Antenna** (common Tx/Rx)
5. **Receiver** (LNA + Mixer + IF Amplifier + Detector)
6. **Display / Indicator**

**Function of the Duplexer:** The duplexer is a fast-acting switch that connects the common antenna to the transmitter during the transmit pulse and to the receiver during the receive interval. It (a) protects the sensitive receiver from the high-power transmit pulse that would otherwise destroy it, and (b) routes the weak returning echo into the receiver with minimum loss. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **monostatic pulse radar**. Label all blocks clearly and describe the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **radar range equation** for a monostatic radar. State clearly the meaning of every symbol used. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

A **monostatic pulse radar** uses a single antenna for both transmission and reception, switched by a duplexer. The block diagram and signal flow are:

1. **Synchronizer:** Generates the master timing pulse, sets the PRF, and provides a reference sweep to the display.
2. **Transmitter:** Consists of a *Pulse Modulator* and an *RF Power Oscillator* (e.g., magnetron). When triggered by the synchronizer, it emits a short, high-power RF burst (peak power $P_t$, pulse width $\tau$).
3. **Duplexer (TR Switch):** Routes the high-power RF pulse to the antenna during transmission, and routes the weak incoming echo to the receiver during reception.
4. **Antenna:** A high-gain, narrow-beam antenna (parabolic dish or phased array) that radiates the RF pulse toward the target and collects the echo.
5. **Receiver:** A superheterodyne chain — *LNA → Mixer (with STALO & COHO) → IF Amplifier → Matched Filter → Envelope Detector → Video Amplifier.* It amplifies the picowatt-level echo to a usable level and extracts the video pulse.
6. **Display (A-scope or PPI):** Presents the video pulse at a position proportional to the round-trip time delay $\tau$, so the operator can read the target's range directly. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

We have already derived this in **Section 3.1**. The concise examiner-style derivation is:

**Step 1 — Power density at target (range $R$, antenna gain $G$):**

$$
S_t = \frac{P_t \, G}{4\pi R^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Power intercepted by target of radar cross-section $\sigma$:**

$$
P_{\text{re}} = S_t \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{4\pi R^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Echo power density back at radar (re-radiated isotropically):**

$$
S_r = \frac{P_{\text{re}}}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}
$$

**Step 4 — Power captured by receiving antenna of effective aperture $A_e = G\lambda^2 / (4\pi)$:**

$$
P_r = S_r \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}
$$

This is the **monostatic radar range equation**. Symbols: $P_t$ = peak transmitted power, $G$ = antenna gain (same Tx/Rx), $\lambda$ = wavelength, $\sigma$ = target radar cross-section, $R$ = target range. **(7 marks)**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a)** Explain in detail the function of each block of a pulse radar. Why is the duplexer considered a critical component? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pulse radar operates at **10 GHz** with a peak power of **1 MW**, an antenna gain of **40 dB**, and a minimum detectable signal of **$10^{-13}$ W**. The target radar cross-section is **20 m²**. Calculate the **maximum detection range** $R_{max}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

The detailed functions are as follows (one mark each, plus the duplexer question):

- **Synchronizer:** Acts as the master clock; defines the PRF, supplies gating pulses to the transmitter, and the range-sweep trigger to the display.
- **Pulse Modulator:** A high-voltage switch that shapes the DC supply into a rectangular pulse of width $\tau$.
- **RF Oscillator (Magnetron):** Generates a high-power microwave burst at the carrier frequency during the modulator pulse.
- **Duplexer (TR Switch):** A waveguide / gas-discharge / ferrite device that connects the antenna to **either** the transmitter or the receiver. **Why critical?** It must switch in **microseconds** and provide **>60 dB isolation** to protect the LNA. Without it, the LNA would burn out within the first transmitted pulse. The radar would be useless.
- **Antenna:** Provides high directive gain $G$ and a narrow beam for angular resolution. The same antenna is used in monostatic radar to save cost and aperture.
- **Receiver (Superheterodyne):** Amplifies the weak echo. Uses STALO + COHO to preserve phase coherence (important for MTI and Doppler processing).
- **Display:** Converts the time delay into a visible range mark. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Numerical Problem

**Given data:**
- $f = 10 \text{ GHz} \Rightarrow \lambda = c / f = (3 \times 10^8) / (10 \times 10^9) = 0.03 \text{ m}$
- $P_t = 1 \text{ MW} = 10^6 \text{ W}$
- $G = 40 \text{ dB} = 10^{40/10} = 10^4$ (dimensionless)
- $S_{min} = 10^{-13} \text{ W}$
- $\sigma = 20 \text{ m}^2$

**Formula:**

$$
R_{max} = \left[ \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, S_{min}} \right]^{1/4}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the numerator:**

$$
P_t \cdot G^2 \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma = (10^6)(10^4)^2 (0.03)^2 (20)
$$

$$
= 10^6 \cdot 10^8 \cdot 9 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 20 = 10^6 \cdot 10^8 \cdot 0.018 = 1.8 \times 10^{11}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator $(4\pi)^3$:**

$$
(4\pi)^3 = 64 \pi^3 \approx 64 \cdot 31.006 = 1984.4
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio inside the bracket:**

$$
\frac{1.8 \times 10^{11}}{1984.4 \times 10^{-13}} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{11}}{1.9844 \times 10^{-10}} = 9.07 \times 10^{20}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the 4th root:**

$$
R_{max} = (9.07 \times 10^{20})^{1/4} = (9.07)^{1/4} \cdot (10^{20})^{1/4} \approx 1.735 \cdot 10^5
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{max} \approx 1.735 \times 10^5 \text{ m} \approx 173.5 \text{ km}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer check (intuitive sense):** A 1 MW, X-band, 40 dB-gain surveillance radar should comfortably detect a large target (σ = 20 m²) at ranges of order 100–200 km. Our result of 173.5 km lies exactly in that range, validating the calculation. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Radar Block Diagram and Operation"**

- **RADAR** = RAdio Detection And Ranging — *active* system that illuminates the target.
- **Range formula:** $R = c\tau / 2$ — the **factor of 2 is non-negotiable**.
- **Unambiguous range:** $R_u = c / (2 f_r)$ — increase PRF ⇒ decrease $R_u$.
- **Duplexer (TR switch):** Protects the receiver; routes Tx and Rx paths on a single antenna.
- **Block chain:** Synchronizer → Modulator → RF Oscillator → Duplexer → Antenna → Target → Antenna → Duplexer → LNA → Mixer → IF → Detector → Video → Display.
- **Transmitter peak power $P_t$ is huge (MW)** but average power is much smaller due to the small duty cycle.
- **Radar range equation (monostatic):** $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$.
- **$R_{max}$ depends on the 4th root** of the power-budget product, hence the famous "4th-power law" — to double range, you need **16×** the power.
- **RCS $\sigma$** depends on target shape, material, orientation, and frequency; typical values: small aircraft ≈ 1 m², large aircraft ≈ 100 m², ship ≈ 10⁴–10⁶ m².
- **Doppler shift:** $f_d = 2 v_r / \lambda$ — used in MTI and CW radars to extract radial velocity.
- **Match the receiver bandwidth** to the pulse width (matched filter) to maximize SNR: $B \cdot \tau \approx 1$.
- **KTU trap words:** "monostatic" ⇒ $G$ appears **squared**; "bistatic" ⇒ $G_t$ and $G_r$ may differ; "CW" ⇒ no duplexer, no time-based ranging.
- **Display types:** A-scope (amplitude vs range), PPI (plan position indicator — 2-D map).
- **Typical KTU mark weightage:** Block-diagram-with-explanation ≈ 7 marks, derivation of radar equation ≈ 7 marks, numerical on $R_{max}$ ≈ 7 marks, 2-mark short notes on duplexer/PRF/MTI are common in Part A.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radar Frequencies and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Radar Frequencies and Applications # Radar Frequencies and Applications — Foundational Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the discipline of **Radio Detection and Ranging (RADAR)**, the operating frequency of an electromagnetic (EM) wave is the single most critical design parameter governing range, resolution, target detectability, and propagation viability. The **radar frequency spectrum** refers to the continuum of EM frequencies — extending from High Frequency (HF) at **3 MHz** through the millimeter-wave (mm-wave) region near **300 GHz** — that are allocated for radar transmission by international regulatory bodies such as the **ITU (International Telecommunication Union)**, **IEEE Standard 521-1984**, and the **Electronic Countermeasures (ECM)** nomenclature system adopted by NATO. The **selection of radar frequency** is dictated by a multi-objective optimization among atmospheric attenuation, target radar cross-section (RCS), antenna beamwidth, hardware maturity, Doppler resolution, and spectrum regulation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The learner must internalize the IEEE radar letter-band nomenclature (L, S, C, X, Ku, K, Ka, V, W), the corresponding frequency and wavelength ranges, atmospheric propagation losses (rain, oxygen, water-vapor), and the dominant engineering applications associated with each band. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Whistle vs. Bat Echolocation" Picture Imagine standing at one end of a foggy field trying to determine how far away a wall is. - If you **shout in a low voice (low frequency, long wavelength)**, the sound wraps around obstacles, penetrates fog easily, and travels far — but the **resolution is poor** (you only sense "something large is there"). This mimics **HF / VHF / UHF radar**, used for over-the-horizon surveillance of ballistic missiles or aircraft at extreme ranges. - If you **clap your hands (mid frequency)** or **whistle sharply (high frequency, short wavelength)**, the sound reflects cleanly off the wall, giving a crisp echo — but the sound does not travel as far in fog. This mimics **X-band or Ka-band radar**, used by fire-control systems and weather radar to pinpoint precipitation droplets. Thus, the **higher the radar frequency**, the **sharper the spatial resolution** but the **poorer the all-weather penetration**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Constants Used in This Module:** > - Speed of light in vacuum: $c = 3 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}$ > - Speed of EM wave in atmosphere: $v \approx c$ (refractive index $n \approx 1.0003$) > - Wavelength–frequency relation: $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ > - Standard temperature reference: $T_0 = 290 \ \text{K}$ ## 1.3 Why Radar Frequency Choice is Foundational A radar's *purpose* (search, track, image, weather, missile guidance) is inseparable from its *frequency*. A 2-D search radar for an air-traffic-control tower operating at **L-band** cannot achieve the angular accuracy of an **X-band fire-control radar**, just as a 77 GHz automotive radar cannot penetrate a heavy rain cell the way an **S-band surveillance radar** can. The remainder of this section organizes these facts into frequency bands, formulas, derivations, diagrams, and KTU exam-style questions. # Deep Theoretical Analysis — Radar Frequency Bands and Selection Logic ## 2.1 The IEEE / NATO Standard Radar Letter Bands The internationally accepted **IEEE Std 521-1984** nomenclature assigns letter designations to contiguous microwave sub-bands. KTU expects students to memorize the following table verbatim. | Letter Band | Frequency Range (GHz) | Wavelength Range (cm) | Typical Wavelength Reference | |:-----------:|:---------------------:|:---------------------:|:----------------------------:| | HF (not lettered) | 0.003 – 0.03 | 10000 – 1000 | Decametric | | VHF | 0.03 – 0.30 | 1000 – 100 | Metric | | UHF | 0.30 – 1.00 | 100 – 30 | Decimetric | | **L** | 1.00 – 2.00 | 30 – 15 | 22 cm | | **S** | 2.00 – 4.00 | 15 – 7.5 | 10 cm | | **C** | 4.00 – 8.00 | 7.5 – 3.75 | 5 cm | | **X** | 8.00 – 12.00 | 3.75 – 2.50 | 3 cm | | **Ku** | 12.00 – 18.00 | 2.50 – 1.67 | 2 cm | | **K** | 18.00 – 27.00 | 1.67 – 1.11 | 1.25 cm | | **Ka** | 27.00 – 40.00 | 1.11 – 0.75 | 0.8 cm | | **V** | 40.00 – 75.00 | 0.75 – 0.40 | 0.4 cm | | **W** | 75.00 – 110.00 | 0.40 – 0.27 | 0.3 cm | | mm-wave | 110 – 300 | 0.27 – 0.10 | sub-mm | > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic to remember the lower microwave bands:** *"**L**ong **S**low **C**ar **X**tra **K**ool"* — L → S → C → X → Ku → K (frequencies increase, wavelengths decrease). ## 2.2 Physics of Why Frequency Matters The **"Why"** behind each design trade-off is captured below in structured form: 1. **Antenna Beamwidth & Gain.** - Beamwidth $\theta \approx \dfrac{70 \lambda}{D}$ (in degrees), where $D$ is antenna aperture in metres. - For a *fixed physical antenna size*, **higher frequency (smaller $\lambda$)** yields **narrower beam** and **higher gain**, enabling sharper angular resolution. 2. **Target RCS Behaviour.** - In the **Rayleigh region** ($\lambda \gg \sqrt[2]{\text{target size}}$), RCS $\propto \lambda^{-4}$ — low-frequency radar sees small targets poorly. - In the **optical/resonance region** ($\lambda \approx \text{target size}$), RCS oscillates with frequency. - In the **optical region** ($\lambda \ll \text{target size}$), RCS is roughly constant — preferred for tracking. 3. **Atmospheric Attenuation.** - Rain attenuation scales roughly as $\alpha_{\text{rain}} \propto f^{2}$ to $f^{4}$. - Gaseous absorption peaks at **22.235 GHz** ($H_2O$ resonance), **60 GHz** ($O_2$ complex), and **118.75 GHz** ($O_2$ line). 4. **Doppler Resolution.** - Velocity resolution $\Delta v = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta f}{2 f_0}$, so higher $f_0$ improves Doppler resolution for fixed $\Delta f$. 5. **Hardware Maturity.** - Lower microwave bands (L, S, C) have mature magnetrons, klystrons, TWTs; mm-wave requires GaN, InP, or SiGe solid-state MMICs. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units | |:-:|:-------:|:-------------:|:-----:| | 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength–frequency conversion | m, Hz | | 2 | $\theta_B = \dfrac{70 \lambda}{D}$ | Approximate 3-dB antenna beamwidth | degrees | | 3 | $G = \dfrac{4 \pi A_e}{\lambda^2}$ | Antenna gain from effective aperture | dimensionless | | 4 | $\text{FSPL} = \left(\dfrac{4 \pi R}{\lambda}\right)^2$ | Free-space path loss (one-way, ratio) | dimensionless | | 5 | $\text{FSPL (dB)} = 32.45 + 20 \log_{10} f_{\text{MHz}} + 20 \log_{10} R_{\text{km}}$ | One-way FSPL in decibels | dB | | 6 | $\alpha_{\text{rain}} \approx k R^{\alpha}$ | Specific rain attenuation (ITU-R P.838) | dB/km | | 7 | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4 k T_0 B F \cdot L}$ | Monostatic radar equation (range form) | dimensionless | | 8 | $R_{\max} = \left(\dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 k T_0 B F \cdot L \cdot \text{SNR}_{\min}}\right)^{1/4}$ | Maximum radar range | m | | 9 | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c \tau}{2} = \dfrac{c}{2 B}$ | Range resolution (pulse) | m | | 10 | $\Delta f_D = \dfrac{2 v_r f_0}{c}$ | Doppler frequency shift | Hz | | 11 | $\delta_v = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 N_{\text{FFT}}}$ | Velocity resolution (MTI/MTD) | m/s | | 12 | $N = (n - 1) \times 10^6$ | Atmospheric refractivity from index $n$ | N-units | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Substitution Identity (Used Repeatedly):** $\dfrac{\lambda^2}{R^4} = \dfrac{c^2}{f^2 R^4}$, showing that *all else equal*, a radar operating at higher frequency has *less maximum range* but *better angular/Doppler resolution*. ## 2.4 Atmospheric Propagation — Loss Mechanisms by Frequency The earth's atmosphere is **not** a lossless medium. Three dominant mechanisms degrade radar signals: 1. **Gaseous Absorption** — Resonant absorption by $O_2$ and $H_2O$ molecules. - $H_2O$ resonance at **22.235 GHz**, **183.31 GHz**. - $O_2$ resonance complex at **50–70 GHz**, peak near **60 GHz**. - Generally low attenuation (< 1 dB/km) below 10 GHz in clear air. 2. **Rain Attenuation** — Scattering and absorption by water droplets. - Specific attenuation $\gamma_R \ (\text{dB/km}) = k R^{\alpha}$, where $R$ is rain rate in mm/hr and $k, \alpha$ are ITU-R P.838 coefficients. 3. **Cloud, Fog, and Snow Attenuation** — Significant above ~30 GHz. 4. **Ducting / Anomalous Propagation** — Temperature inversions and humidity gradients create refractive layers, bending radar rays and causing *anomalous propagation* (ducting, super-refraction). ## 2.5 Real-World Utility — Why This Matters in Industry - **Air Traffic Control (ATC) radars** use **S-band (2.7 – 2.9 GHz)** for long-range en-route surveillance and **C-band (5.6 – 5.9 GHz)** for shorter-range terminal approach. - **Airborne Weather Radar (AWR)** operates at **X-band (9.3 – 9.5 GHz)** to map precipitation in front of the aircraft. - **Automotive ACC radar** operates at **W-band (76 – 77 GHz)** — mm-wave for compact beam forming. - **Ground-Penetrating Radar (GPR)** uses **UHF / L-band** to penetrate soil/dirt. - **Space Debris Tracking** uses **Ka-band** (35 GHz) for high-accuracy orbit determination. # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Free-Space Path Loss for a Radar The free-space path loss (FSPL) defines the loss in signal strength as an EM wave travels through vacuum. For a radar, both the *outbound* and *return* paths are subject to this loss, leading to the $(4\pi)^3$ factor in the radar equation. ### Derivation 3.1.1 — One-Way FSPL Consider an isotropic radiator of power $P_t$. The power flux density (poynting) on a sphere of radius $R$ is: $$ S = \frac{P_t}{4 \pi R^2} $$ A receiving antenna of effective aperture $A_e$ captures: $$ P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \, A_e}{4 \pi R^2} $$ For an isotropic receiving antenna, $A_e = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{4\pi}$. Substituting: $$ P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot \lambda^2}{(4 \pi)^2 R^2} $$ Therefore, the one-way power ratio loss is: $$ \text{FSPL}_{\text{one-way}} = \frac{P_t}{P_r} = \left(\frac{4 \pi R}{\lambda}\right)^2 $$ In decibels: $$ \boxed{\text{FSPL (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4 \pi R}{\lambda}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(4 \pi) + 20 \log_{10} R - 20 \log_{10}\lambda} $$ Using $\lambda = c / f$, and substituting numerical constants, this collapses to the well-known **engineering approximation** valid for $f$ in MHz and $R$ in km: $$ \boxed{\text{FSPL (dB)} \approx 32.45 + 20 \log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 20 \log_{10}(R_{\text{km}})} $$ ### Derivation 3.1.2 — Two-Way Radar FSPL In a monostatic radar, the transmitted wave traverses $R$ to the target (loss $L_1$), reflects from the target (effective gain $\sigma / 4\pi R^2$ into the return path), and traverses another $R$ back (loss $L_2$ identical to $L_1$). The total one-way FSPL squared, applied at both ends, gives the **two-way loss**: $$ L_{\text{two-way}} = \left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)^{4} $$ This fourth-power dependence on $R$ is the reason that radar range is so sensitive to system losses — and is the dominant factor in the famous **radar range equation** introduced next. ### Derivation 3.1.3 — The Monostatic Radar Equation Combining transmit power $P_t$, antenna gain $G$, target RCS $\sigma$, two-way FSPL, and receiver noise floor $k T_0 B F$, the received SNR is: $$ \text{SNR} = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R^4 \, k T_0 B F \, L_{\text{sys}}} $$ Each symbol represents: - $G$ — antenna gain (same Tx/Rx for monostatic) - $\sigma$ — target radar cross section (m²) - $B$ — receiver noise bandwidth (Hz) - $F$ — receiver noise figure (linear) - $L_{\text{sys}}$ — total system losses (cables, radome, atmospheric) Setting $\text{SNR} = \text{SNR}_{\min}$ and solving for $R$: $$ \boxed{R_{\max} = \left[\frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, k T_0 B F \, L_{\text{sys}} \, \text{SNR}_{\min}}\right]^{1/4}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for frequency selection:** $R_{\max} \propto \lambda^{1/2}$ for fixed aperture (because $G \propto D^2 / \lambda^2$ cancels the $\lambda$ in numerator partially). So *halving the wavelength roughly halves the maximum range* if aperture is held constant. This is the trade-off behind every band selection. ## 3.2 Derivation of Atmospheric Refractivity & Ducting The atmosphere's refractive index $n$ deviates from unity by about 300 parts per million at sea level. Engineers use the **refractivity** $N$: $$ N = (n - 1) \times 10^6 $$ The **Standard Atmosphere** defines a vertical refractivity gradient that bends EM rays slightly toward the earth. For a standard atmosphere: $$ N(h) = 315 \, e^{-0.136 \, h_{\text{km}}} $$ The corresponding **effective earth radius** that straightens the ray path is: $$ \boxed{R_e = \dfrac{R_0}{1 + \dfrac{R_0}{2}\dfrac{dN}{dh} \times 10^{-6}} \approx \dfrac{4}{3} R_0} $$ This famous "**four-thirds earth model**" is used in virtually every radar coverage diagram. When $\frac{dN}{dh}$ is unusually negative (a *duct*), rays can be trapped in a layer — causing **anomalous propagation** that produces clutter and *over-the-horizon* detection. ### Derivation 3.2.1 — Numerical Example Suppose a surface-based duct exists with $\dfrac{dN}{dh} = -200 \ \text{N/km}$ between 0 and 100 m altitude. Step 1: Compute the modified earth radius factor: $$ k = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{R_0}{2} \frac{dN}{dh} 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{6371 \times 10^3}{2}(-200)\times 10^{-6}} $$ $$ k = \frac{1}{1 - 0.6371} = \frac{1}{0.3629} \approx 2.755 $$ Step 2: The effective earth radius becomes: $$ R_e = k \cdot R_0 = 2.755 \times 6371 \approx 17552 \ \text{km} $$ Interpretation: Radar rays now curve downward 2.755 times more strongly than the earth curves — easily trapping signals in the duct and producing extreme-range propagation, or — conversely — causing surface targets to be missed. ## 3.3 Rain Attenuation Estimation (ITU-R Simplified) The ITU-R P.838 model gives specific attenuation: $$ \gamma_R = k \cdot R^{\alpha} \quad [\text{dB/km}] $$ For a slant path of length $L_{\text{eff}}$ km, total rain attenuation is: $$ A_{\text{rain}} = \gamma_R \cdot L_{\text{eff}} \quad [\text{dB}] $$ ### Worked Numerical Example — Attenuation at X-band vs Ka-band Given: Rain rate $R = 4 \ \text{mm/hr}$, slant path length $L = 10 \ \text{km}$, horizontal polarization. Step 1 — Read ITU-R coefficients for X-band (10 GHz): $k = 0.00887$, $\alpha = 1.264$. Step 2 — Compute specific attenuation: $$ \gamma_{R,X} = 0.00887 \times (4)^{1.264} $$ Compute $4^{1.264}$: $$ 4^{1.264} = e^{1.264 \ln 4} = e^{1.264 \times 1.3863} = e^{1.7523} \approx 5.768 $$ Therefore: $$ \gamma_{R,X} = 0.00887 \times 5.768 \approx 0.0512 \ \text{dB/km} $$ Total attenuation over 10 km: $$ A_{R,X} = 0.0512 \times 10 \approx 0.512 \ \text{dB} $$ Step 3 — Read coefficients for Ka-band (30 GHz): $k = 0.187$, $\alpha = 1.021$. Step 4 — Specific attenuation: $$ \gamma_{R,Ka} = 0.187 \times (4)^{1.021} = 0.187 \times 4.072 \approx 0.7615 \ \text{dB/km} $$ Total attenuation: $$ A_{R,Ka} = 0.7615 \times 10 \approx 7.615 \ \text{dB} $$ Step 5 — Comparison and physical insight: $$ \boxed{A_{R,Ka} / A_{R,X} \approx 14.87} $$ **Insight:** For the same rainfall, the Ka-band link suffers nearly **15×** the attenuation of the X-band link.
