## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define the Doppler Effect. Derive an expression for the Doppler shift observed in a monostatic radar system when the target is moving with radial velocity $v_r$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** The Doppler Effect is the apparent change in frequency of a received signal caused by the relative motion between the transmitter and the observer (target).

**Derivation (2 Marks):**

For a monostatic radar, the EM wave undergoes two Doppler shifts (once on transmit, once on reflect). Let $f_0$ be the transmitted frequency.

The target (moving observer) perceives frequency:
$$
f_1 = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v_r}{c} \right)
$$

The target re-radiates, and the radar (stationary observer) receives:
$$
f_2 = f_1 \left( \frac{c}{c - v_r} \right) = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v_r}{c - v_r} \right)
$$

Using $v_r \ll c$:
$$
f_d = f_2 - f_0 \approx \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}
$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Explain why GEO (Geostationary Earth Orbit) satellites exhibit negligible Doppler shift while LEO (Low Earth Orbit) satellites show significant Doppler shifts. Mention the typical magnitude.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **GEO Satellites (1 Mark):** GEO satellites are stationary relative to the Earth's rotation ($h \approx 35786$ km, orbital period = 24 hours). Since the relative velocity between satellite and ground station is *zero*, $v_r = 0$, hence $f_d = 0$. Only minor residual drift exists due to small inclination.

> **LEO Satellites (2 Marks):** LEO satellites orbit at $h = 200$ – $2000$ km with orbital velocities of $7$ – $8$ km/s. The relative radial velocity is on the order of several km/s. For example, at $f_0 = 2$ GHz, the maximum Doppler shift for an $h = 600$ km orbit is $\sim 46$ kHz. This is highly significant and must be compensated in the receiver's AFC (Automatic Frequency Control) loop.

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Doppler Effect in Radar Systems**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the Doppler frequency shift in a CW radar system. Explain why a CW radar cannot measure the range of a target. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pulsed radar operates at $f_0 = 9$ GHz with a PRF of $1$ kHz. A target is moving with radial velocity $v_r = 300$ m/s toward the radar. Compute: (i) Doppler frequency, (ii) the first two blind speeds, and (iii) the unambiguous Doppler frequency range. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (4 Marks) + Explanation (3 Marks):**

In a CW radar, the transmitter emits a continuous sinusoid $A\cos(2\pi f_0 t)$. After reflection from a target moving with radial velocity $v_r$, the echo returns at frequency $f_0 + f_d$ where:

$$
f_d = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}
$$

**Why CW radar cannot measure range (3 Marks):**

A CW radar has *no time reference marker* (no pulse modulation). Without a modulation envelope (pulse), there is no way to determine the round-trip time delay $\Delta t = 2R/c$. Since the transmitted signal is a single continuous tone, all range information is lost — only the *velocity* (via Doppler shift) can be extracted. The receiver is essentially a homodyne detector that produces a beat note at $f_d$, but cannot resolve when the echo was emitted.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $f_0 = 9$ GHz, $f_{PRF} = 1$ kHz, $v_r = 300$ m/s.

**Wavelength:**
$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{9 \times 10^9} = 0.0333 \text{ m} = 3.33 \text{ cm}
$$

**(i) Doppler frequency (2 Marks):**
$$
f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 300}{0.0333} = \frac{600}{0.0333}
$$

$$
f_d \approx 18{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 18 \text{ kHz}
$$

**(ii) Blind speeds (2 Marks):**

Blind speeds occur when the Doppler shift equals an integer multiple of the PRF, making the target indistinguishable from clutter (or stationary objects).

$$
v_{blind} = \frac{n \lambda f_{PRF}}{2} = \frac{n \times 0.0333 \times 1000}{2} = 16.67 n \text{ m/s}
$$

- First blind speed ($n = 1$): $v_{blind,1} = 16.67$ m/s
- Second blind speed ($n = 2$): $v_{blind,2} = 33.33$ m/s

> *Note:* Since the target velocity $300$ m/s is *not* a blind speed, the target will be detected.

**(iii) Unambiguous Doppler range (3 Marks):**

By the Nyquist sampling theorem, the Doppler frequency must satisfy $|f_d| < f_{PRF}/2$ to be unambiguously measured. The unambiguous velocity range is:

$$
\pm v_{max} = \pm \frac{\lambda f_{PRF}}{4} = \pm \frac{0.0333 \times 1000}{4}
$$

$$
\pm v_{max} = \pm 8.33 \text{ m/s}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Since the target's radial velocity ($300$ m/s) far exceeds $\pm 8.33$ m/s, the radar will suffer from **Doppler ambiguity**. Multiple aliasing will occur, and the apparent measured Doppler will be $f_d \mod f_{PRF}$. This is why high-velocity radars must use **staggered PRFs** or **multiple PRF schemes**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Doppler Effect in Satellite Communication**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the maximum Doppler frequency shift experienced by a signal from a LEO satellite in a circular orbit of altitude $h$ as seen by a ground station. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A LEO satellite orbits at an altitude of $h = 800$ km and transmits at a carrier frequency of $f_0 = 1.6$ GHz. Calculate: (i) the orbital velocity, (ii) the orbital period, and (iii) the maximum Doppler shift at the ground station. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

Consider a LEO satellite $S$ in a circular orbit at altitude $h$ above Earth's surface, with Earth's centre at $O$ and the ground station at $G$ on Earth's surface. Let:
- $OS = R_E + h$
- $OG = R_E$
- $v_{orb}$ = orbital velocity, tangential to the orbit
- $\phi$ = angle between $v_{orb}$ and the line of sight $SG$

The radial (line-of-sight) component of the satellite's velocity:

$$
v_r = v_{orb} \cos\phi
$$

Using the law of sines in $\triangle OSG$, with $\theta$ being the angle at $O$:

$$
\frac{\sin\phi}{OG} = \frac{\sin\theta}{OS} \implies \sin\phi = \frac{R_E}{R_E + h} \sin\theta
$$

The maximum radial velocity occurs when $\phi = 0$ (closest approach, satellite directly overhead the ground station):

$$
v_{r,\max} = v_{orb} \cdot \cos(0) \cdot \frac{R_E}{R_E+h} = v_{orb} \cdot \frac{R_E}{R_E+h}
$$

Wait — to be rigorous: when the satellite is at the **sub-satellite point** (directly above the ground station), the orbital velocity is purely tangential, and the line of sight is purely radial (along the local vertical). The geometric projection factor gives:

$$
v_{r,\max} = v_{orb} \cdot \frac{R_E}{R_E+h}
$$

The maximum one-way Doppler shift:

$$
f_{d,\max} = \frac{v_{r,\max}}{\lambda} = \frac{v_{orb}}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{R_E}{R_E+h}
$$

where $\lambda = c/f_0$ is the wavelength.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $h = 800$ km, $f_0 = 1.6$ GHz, $R_E = 6371$ km, $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s².

**(i) Orbital velocity (2 Marks):**

$$
v_{orb} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{R_E + h}}
$$

First convert units: $R_E + h = 6371 + 800 = 7171$ km $= 7.171 \times 10^6$ m.

$$
v_{orb} = \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14}}{7.171 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{5.558 \times 10^7}
$$

$$
\boxed{v_{orb} \approx 7455 \text{ m/s} \approx 7.46 \text{ km/s}}
$$

**(ii) Orbital period (2 Marks):**

$$
T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{(R_E + h)^3}{\mu}}
$$

$$
T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{(7.171 \times 10^6)^3}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}}
$$

$$
T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{3.687 \times 10^{20}}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}} = 2\pi \sqrt{9.249 \times 10^5}
$$

$$
T = 2\pi \times 961.7 \approx 6043 \text{ s} \approx 100.7 \text{ minutes}
$$

$$
\boxed{T \approx 100.7 \text{ min}}
$$

**(iii) Maximum Doppler shift (3 Marks):**

Wavelength:
$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.6 \times 10^9} = 0.1875 \text{ m}
$$

Radial velocity at closest approach:
$$
v_{r,\max} = v_{orb} \cdot \frac{R_E}{R_E+h} = 7455 \cdot \frac{6371}{7171}
$$

$$
v_{r,\max} = 7455 \times 0.8884 = 6623 \text{ m/s}
$$

Doppler shift:
$$
f_{d,\max} = \frac{v_{r,\max}}{\lambda} = \frac{6623}{0.1875}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{d,\max} \approx 35{,}323 \text{ Hz} \approx 35.3 \text{ kHz}}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 35.3 kHz shift at 1.6 GHz is a fractional shift of $\sim 2.2 \times 10^{-5}$. This is small in percentage but huge in absolute terms — the ground receiver's PLL must track this varying frequency throughout the satellite pass, typically ranging from $+35.3$ kHz (approaching) to $-35.3$ kHz (receding).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (Lose Marks!):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in radar Doppler.** Many students write $f_d = v_r/\lambda$ for radar. Remember — radar is *two-way*, so it's **$2 v_r/\lambda$**, not $v_r/\lambda$.
>
> 2. **Mixing up satellite and radar formulas.** Satellite links are *one-way* (factor 1), while monostatic radar is *two-way* (factor 2). Using the wrong factor yields wrong numerical answers.
>
> 3. **Incorrect unit conversion of velocity.** Speeds are often given in km/h in problems. Convert to m/s by multiplying by $5/18$ *before* plugging into the Doppler formula. Example: $108$ km/h $= 108 \times (5/18) = 30$ m/s.
>
> 4. **Confusing Doppler shift with Doppler rate.** Doppler shift $f_d$ is in Hz (a frequency). Doppler rate $\dot{f_d}$ is in Hz/s (a rate of change). The examiner will specifically ask for one or the other — read carefully.
>
> 5. **Neglecting the non-relativistic approximation.** Forgetting to write $v_r \ll c$ and the resulting $f_d = (2v_r/c)f_0$ approximation can lead to incomplete answers.
>
> 6. **GEO vs LEO confusion.** GEO satellites have *zero* Doppler from a fixed ground station. If the question specifies GEO, do *not* calculate a non-zero Doppler from orbital motion.
>
> 7. **Blind speed formula misuse.** Blind speeds occur at $v_{blind} = n\lambda f_{PRF}/2$, NOT $n\lambda f_{PRF}$. The factor of 2 is from the two-way path.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Doppler Effect (Module 4):**
>
> ☐ **Core Definition:** Frequency shift due to relative motion between source and observer.
>
> ☐ **Radar Doppler formula:** $f_d = 2v_r/\lambda = 2v_r f_0/c$ (factor 2 because of two-way path).
>
> ☐ **Satellite (one-way) Doppler formula:** $f_d = v_r/\lambda$ (no factor of 2).
>
> ☐ **Radial velocity is what matters:** $v_r = v_{orb} \cos\phi$. Tangential motion perpendicular to LOS gives zero Doppler.
>
> ☐ **Sign convention:** Approaching → positive $f_d$ (frequency increases); receding → negative $f_d$.
>
> ☐ **GEO satellites:** $f_d \approx 0$ (no relative motion to ground station).
>
> ☐ **LEO satellites:** $f_d$ can be tens of kHz, must be compensated in receiver.
>
> ☐ **Orbital velocity formula:** $v_{orb} = \sqrt{\mu/(R_E + h)}$, where $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s².
>
> ☐ **Maximum Doppler at closest approach:** $f_{d,\max} = (v_{orb}/\lambda) \cdot [R_E/(R_E + h)]$.
>
> ☐ **CW radar:** Can measure velocity but *not* range (no time reference).
>
> ☐ **Pulsed radar:** Has blind speeds at $v_{blind} = n\lambda f_{PRF}/2$; uses staggered PRFs to mitigate.
>
> ☐ **MTI radar:** Uses Doppler to filter stationary clutter; only moving targets pass through.
>
> ☐ **Units:** Speeds in m/s, frequencies in Hz, wavelengths in m. Always convert km/h → m/s.
>
> ☐ **Constants:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $R_E = 6371$ km, $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s².

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📡 CW Radar — Block Diagram (Module 4)

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Continuous Wave (CW) Radar** is a type of radar system that transmits a high-frequency electromagnetic signal **continuously and uninterruptedly** (rather than in discrete pulses) and simultaneously receives the echo reflected from the target. The target information (principally its **radial velocity**) is extracted by detecting the **Doppler frequency shift** between the transmitted and received signals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A pure CW radar **cannot measure target range** because the transmitted waveform has no time markers. Range information can only be obtained by modulating the CW carrier (FMCW / FM-CW Radar) — a frequently tested distinction in KTU ESE papers.

The **block diagram of a CW radar** describes the interconnections between the transmitter, antenna subsystem, receiver, signal-processing stages, and the indicator/display unit that together accomplish Doppler-based target detection.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Whistling Train" Intuition

Imagine you are standing on a railway platform as a train approaches **whistling continuously**.

* The pitch (frequency) of the whistle you hear is **higher** when the train is approaching and **lower** when it moves away.
* The amount by which the pitch changes tells you **how fast** the train is moving — but tells you **nothing** about how far down the track it started from.

A CW radar behaves identically:

* The transmitter "whistles" (radiates) an unmodulated carrier continuously.
* The moving target acts like a moving observer — it **shifts** the reflected frequency (Doppler effect).
* The receiver **listens** for this tiny frequency shift and converts it into target velocity.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as you cannot tell the train's distance from a single whistle, a pure CW radar cannot measure range. To get range, we must **vary** the whistle's pitch over time (frequency modulation) — the foundation of FMCW radar.

## 1.3 Why CW Radar is Used

* **Velocity measurement** of moving targets (vehicles, aircraft rotor blades, humans, weather particles).
* **Excellent short-range resolution** at low power levels (no high-power pulsed transmitters needed).
* Used in **radar speed guns**, **proximity fuzes**, **anti-collision automotive radars** (77 GHz), and **Doppler weather radars**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Constants Used Throughout CW Radar Analysis:**
> * Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$
> * Boltzmann constant: $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$
> * Standard noise reference temperature: $T_{0} = 290 \text{ K}$
> * Typical CW radar operating frequencies: **X-band (8–12 GHz)**, **K-band (18–27 GHz)**, **W-band (~77 GHz for automotive)**
> * Typical transmitted power levels: milliwatts to a few watts (CW radars use **low average power** compared to pulsed radars).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step by Step

1. **Carrier Generation:** A highly stable RF oscillator (often a **Gunn diode**, **dielectric resonator oscillator (DRO)**, or **crystal-controlled source** followed by frequency multiplication) generates the CW carrier at frequency $f_{0}$.
2. **Power Amplification:** The carrier is amplified by a power amplifier (TWT, solid-state PA, or magnetron in legacy designs) to a usable radiated level $P_{t}$.
3. **Radiation:** The signal is radiated by a highly directional antenna (parabolic dish, horn, or microstrip patch array).
4. **Target Reflection:** A portion of the wave intercepts a moving target. The target reflects the wave, and due to the **Doppler effect**, the returned frequency becomes $f_{0} \pm f_{d}$.
5. **Echo Reception:** A second antenna (or a duplexer/ circulator in single-antenna systems) captures the weak reflected echo.
6. **Heterodyne Mixing:** The received echo is fed into a **mixer** along with a sample of the transmitted signal acting as the **local oscillator (LO)**. Since both are at nearly the same frequency, the mixer output is at the **Doppler difference frequency** $f_{d}$ (the IF).
7. **IF Amplification & Filtering:** A narrowband IF amplifier (bandwidth matched to the expected Doppler spectrum) raises the echo to a detectable level and rejects out-of-band noise.
8. **Detection:** An **envelope detector** (or synchronous/coherent detector) extracts the Doppler audio tone.
9. **Indication:** The Doppler tone is displayed on a **loudspeaker**, **headphones**, **frequency counter**, or **spectrum analyzer** to read out the target's radial velocity.

## 2.2 The Doppler Frequency — Core Physics

For a target moving with radial velocity $v_{r}$ relative to a stationary radar, the two-way Doppler shift is:

$$f_{d} = \frac{2 \, v_{r} \, f_{0}}{c} = \frac{2 \, v_{r}}{\lambda}$$

where $\lambda = c / f_{0}$ is the operating wavelength.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because $f_{d}$ depends on $f_{0}$, higher frequency radars produce a **larger Doppler shift** for the same target velocity — making mm-wave (77 GHz) automotive radars highly sensitive to small radial motions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Doppler frequency | $f_{d} = \dfrac{2 v_{r}}{\lambda} = \dfrac{2 v_{r} f_{0}}{c}$ | Hz | Two-way shift; $v_{r}$ is radial component |
| 2 | Radial velocity from Doppler | $v_{r} = \dfrac{f_{d} \lambda}{2}$ | m/s | Direct readout in speed-gun |
| 3 | CW Radar Range Equation (SNR) | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{P_{t} G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} R^{4} \, k T_{s} B_{n}}$ | dimensionless | $B_{n}$ is the receiver noise bandwidth |
| 4 | Maximum detectable range (CW) | $R_{max} = \left[\dfrac{P_{t} G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} k T_{s} B_{n} \, \text{SNR}_{min}}\right]^{1/4}$ | m | Note the 4th-root dependence (vs 4th-power inverse in pulsed) |
| 5 | Minimum detectable signal | $S_{min} = k T_{s} B_{n} \cdot \text{SNR}_{min}$ | W | Sets receiver sensitivity |
| 6 | Antenna effective aperture | $A_{e} = \dfrac{G \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}$ | m² | Links gain to physical size |
| 7 | Total system noise temperature | $T_{s} = T_{a} + T_{r} + \dfrac{T_{e}}{G_{\text{stage}}}$ | K | Antenna + receiver + cascaded stages |
| 8 | FMCW beat frequency (range-related) | $f_{b} = \dfrac{2 R \, \Delta f}{c \, T_{m}}$ | Hz | Used in FM-CW variants; $\Delta f$ = deviation, $T_{m}$ = sweep period |
| 9 | Isolation requirement | $P_{\text{LO}} \gg P_{\text{echo}}$ | dB | Critical to avoid transmitter leakage saturating receiver |
| 10 | Coherent integration gain | $\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \text{SNR}_{\text{in}} \cdot N$ | linear | $N$ = number of coherently integrated samples |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **pulsed radar equation** (inverse 4th power of $R$, $P_{t}$ peak) with the **CW radar equation** (inverse 4th power of $R$, **$P_{t}$ average**, and $B_{n}$ replaces the pulse-compression gain). Examiners frequently award **zero** for mixing the two.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of CW Radar

* **Police speed guns** (handheld Doppler devices at X or K-band).
* **Automotive adaptive cruise control (ACC)** and **automatic emergency braking (AEB)** at 77 GHz.
* **Ballistic missile early-warning CW illuminators** (semi-active CW homing).
* **Doppler weather radar** for measuring precipitation particle motion.
* **Industrial flowmeters** and **vibration sensors** (CW Doppler microwave sensors).
* **Vital-sign monitoring** (respiration and heartbeat detection using sub-mm Doppler shifts).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Doppler Frequency for a CW Radar

### Setup
Let the radar transmit a CW signal:
$$s_{t}(t) = A \cos(2 \pi f_{0} t)$$
where $A$ is the carrier amplitude and $f_{0}$ is the carrier frequency.

The target is at range $R(t)$ moving with radial velocity $v_{r} = \dfrac{dR}{dt}$.

The two-way propagation delay is:
$$\tau(t) = \frac{2 R(t)}{c}$$

The received echo is therefore:
$$s_{r}(t) = a \, A \cos\!\left[2 \pi f_{0}\,(t - \tau(t))\right] = a \, A \cos\!\left[2 \pi f_{0}\,t - 2 \pi f_{0} \cdot \frac{2R(t)}{c}\right]$$

### Step 1 — Extract the Instantaneous Phase
The instantaneous phase of the received signal is:
$$\phi_{r}(t) = 2 \pi f_{0} t - \frac{4 \pi f_{0} R(t)}{c}$$

The instantaneous frequency is the time derivative of phase divided by $2\pi$:
$$f_{r}(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \frac{d\phi_{r}}{dt} = f_{0} - \frac{2 f_{0}}{c} \frac{dR}{dt} = f_{0} - \frac{2 f_{0} v_{r}}{c}$$

### Step 2 — Define the Doppler Shift
The Doppler frequency shift is the difference between transmitted and received frequencies:
$$f_{d} = f_{0} - f_{r}(t) = \frac{2 f_{0} v_{r}}{c} = \frac{2 v_{r}}{\lambda}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** The factor of **2** in the numerator comes from the **two-way** (outbound + return) propagation. This is the canonical CW radar Doppler equation.

## 3.2 Derivation of the CW Radar Range Equation (SNR)

### Step 1 — Power Density at the Target
For an isotropic radiator, the power density at range $R$ is:
$$S_{R} = \frac{P_{t}}{4 \pi R^{2}}$$

For a directive antenna of gain $G$, the power density on the boresight is:
$$S_{R} = \frac{P_{t} G}{4 \pi R^{2}}$$

### Step 2 — Power Intercepted by the Target
A target of radar cross-section $\sigma$ intercepts and re-radiates power:
$$P_{\text{int}} = S_{R} \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_{t} G \sigma}{4 \pi R^{2}}$$

### Step 3 — Power Density of the Echo Back at the Radar
The re-radiated spherical wave returns to the radar with the same effective spreading:
$$S_{\text{echo}} = \frac{P_{\text{int}}}{4 \pi R^{2}} = \frac{P_{t} G \sigma}{(4 \pi)^{2} R^{4}}$$

### Step 4 — Power Captured by the Receiving Antenna
The receiving antenna of effective aperture $A_{e}$ captures:
$$P_{r} = S_{\text{echo}} \cdot A_{e} = \frac{P_{t} G \sigma \, A_{e}}{(4 \pi)^{2} R^{4}}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute the Aperture–Gain Relation
Using $A_{e} = \dfrac{G \lambda^{2}}{4 \pi}$ (assuming $G_{tx} = G_{rx} = G$ for a monostatic CW radar):

$$P_{r} = \frac{P_{t} G \sigma}{(4\pi)^{2} R^{4}} \cdot \frac{G \lambda^{2}}{4 \pi} = \frac{P_{t} G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4 \pi)^{3} R^{4}}$$

This is the **two-way (radar) equation** for a monostatic CW radar.

### Step 6 — Express the Output SNR
The receiver output SNR is:
$$\text{SNR} = \frac{P_{r}}{N_{i}} = \frac{P_{r}}{k T_{s} B_{n}}$$

Substituting $P_{r}$:

$$\boxed{\;\text{SNR} = \frac{P_{t} \, G^{2} \, \lambda^{2} \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \, R^{4} \, k \, T_{s} \, B_{n}}\;}$$

### Step 7 — Solve for Maximum Detectable Range
Setting $\text{SNR} = \text{SNR}_{min}$ and solving for $R \equiv R_{max}$:

$$R_{max}^{4} = \frac{P_{t} G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} k T_{s} B_{n} \, \text{SNR}_{min}}$$

$$\boxed{\;R_{max} = \left[\frac{P_{t} G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} k T_{s} B_{n} \, \text{SNR}_{min}}\right]^{\tfrac{1}{4}}\;}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Note:** The 4th-root dependence means that to **double** the detection range of a CW radar, you must increase $P_{t}$ by a factor of **16** (assuming all other parameters are constant) — a stark contrast to pulsed radar where waveform design can yield processing gains.

## 3.3 Derivation of Beat Frequency in FM-CW Radar (Range-Capable Variant)

Although pure CW radars cannot measure range, the **FM-CW** variant modulates the carrier with a linear sweep of bandwidth $\Delta f$ over period $T_{m}$.

### Step 1 — Transmitted Frequency
$$f_{t}(t) = f_{0} + \frac{\Delta f}{T_{m}} \cdot t \quad \text{for } 0 \leq t \leq T_{m}$$

### Step 2 — Round-Trip Delay
$$\tau = \frac{2 R}{c}$$

### Step 3 — Received Frequency at Mixer Output
The received signal is a delayed copy. The instantaneous difference (beat frequency) is:
$$f_{b} = \frac{\Delta f}{T_{m}} \cdot \tau = \frac{\Delta f}{T_{m}} \cdot \frac{2R}{c}$$

$$\boxed{\;f_{b} = \frac{2 R \, \Delta f}{c \, T_{m}}\;}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Range
$$\boxed{\;R = \frac{f_{b} \, c \, T_{m}}{2 \, \Delta f}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to draw and explain the block diagram, examiners expect you to mark the **signal flow direction** clearly, label every block, and explicitly state the **purpose** of each block. Many students lose marks for omitting the **circulator/duplexer** (or "T-R switch") block that protects the receiver from transmitter leakage.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagram — CW Radar Block Architecture

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **complete block-level functional architecture** of a homodyne (zero-IF) CW radar. Each block is mapped to its engineering function, and arrows indicate the **direction of signal flow**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OSC["CW Signal Generator f0"] --> PA["Power Amplifier"]
    PA --> CIRC["Circulator Duplexer"]
    CIRC --> ANT["Directional Antenna"]
    ANT -. echo from target .-> ANT
    ANT --> CIRC
    CIRC --> MIX["Mixer Product Detector"]
    OSC -. sample as LO .-> MIX
    MIX --> IFA["Narrowband IF Amplifier"]
    IFA --> DET["Envelope Detector"]
    DET --> PROC["Signal Processor"]
    PROC --> DISP["Display Speed Indicator"]
    PROC --> AUD["Loudspeaker Doppler Tone"]
    style OSC fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style PA fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CIRC fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ANT fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style MIX fill:#073b4c,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    style IFA fill:#073b4c,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    style DET fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style PROC fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style DISP fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style AUD fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Block-by-Block Functional Description

| Block | Function | Typical Component |
|-------|----------|-------------------|
| **CW Signal Generator (f0)** | Produces the stable, low-noise RF carrier. | Crystal oscillator + multiplier, or Gunn DRO |
| **Power Amplifier** | Boosts carrier to required radiated level $P_{t}$. | Solid-state PA, IMPATT, TWT (high-power variants) |
| **Circulator / Duplexer** | Routes transmitted signal to the antenna; routes received echo to the mixer while isolating the receiver from the strong transmitted signal. | Ferrite circulator, T-R switch (in pulsed) |
| **Directional Antenna** | Transmits and receives the RF beam with gain $G$. | Parabolic dish, horn, slotted waveguide, microstrip patch array |
| **Mixer / Product Detector** | Multiplies the received echo with a sample of the transmitted carrier, producing the Doppler difference frequency at IF. | Schottky-diode double-balanced mixer |
| **Narrowband IF Amplifier** | Amplifies the low-frequency Doppler signal (typically 100 Hz to 100 kHz) and limits noise bandwidth. | Audio-band op-amp chain or dedicated Doppler IF strip |
| **Envelope Detector** | Extracts the Doppler audio envelope from the IF waveform. | Diode detector, synchronous (lock-in) detector |
| **Signal Processor** | Filters, averages, FFT-analyzes, and computes radial velocity. | Microcontroller, DSP, or dedicated FFT chip |
| **Display / Loudspeaker** | Converts Doppler information to user-readable output (numeric speed, audio tone, or spectral plot). | 7-segment display, headphones, spectrum analyzer, LCD |

## 4.3 Architecture Variants (Common KTU Question)

| Variant | Carrier Type | Range Capability | Common Use |
|---------|--------------|------------------|------------|
| **Pure CW (Heterodyne / Homodyne)** | Unmodulated sine wave | ❌ None | Speed guns, proximity fuzes |
| **FM-CW** | Linearly frequency-modulated | ✅ Yes (via beat frequency) | Automotive radar, altimeters |
| **Phase-Coded CW (Spread Spectrum)** | BPSK / QPSK modulated | ✅ Yes (via correlation) | Covert radars, LPI radar |
| **Dual-Frequency CW** | Two-tone (f1 + f2) | ✅ Yes (via phase difference) | High-precision ranging |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📚 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Continuous Wave (CW) Radar. Why is it not suitable for target range measurement?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Continuous Wave (CW) radar is one in which the RF oscillator transmits a **continuous, unmodulated** electromagnetic signal and simultaneously receives the target echo. The received signal is mixed with a sample of the transmitted signal to extract the **Doppler frequency shift**, which gives the target's radial velocity.

A pure CW radar **cannot measure range** because the transmitted waveform has **no timing mark** (no pulses, no frequency variation, no phase code). Without a time reference, the round-trip delay — and hence the target range — cannot be determined. Range measurement requires the carrier to be modulated in frequency, phase, or amplitude (as in FM-CW, phase-coded CW, or pulsed radar). (3 marks)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Write the expression for Doppler frequency in a CW radar and explain the significance of the factor '2'.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$f_{d} = \frac{2 v_{r}}{\lambda} = \frac{2 v_{r} f_{0}}{c}$$

The factor **2** appears because the Doppler effect occurs **twice**: once when the transmitted wave strikes the moving target (target-as-receiver geometry), and again when the reflected echo returns to the radar (radar-as-receiver geometry). The two-way effect doubles the frequency shift compared to a one-way (e.g., communication) link. (3 marks)

---

## **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Draw and explain the **block diagram of a CW radar** with the function of each block. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Derive the **CW radar range equation** and state its limitations compared to the pulsed radar equation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Block Diagram of a CW Radar (7 marks):**

Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in **Section 4.1** above. The student must produce a hand-drawn equivalent in the answer script.

**Functional explanation of each block (1 mark per block for the most important 5 + 2 marks for coherent flow):**

1. **CW Signal Generator (f0):** Generates a stable, low-noise carrier frequency $f_{0}$ — typically a dielectric resonator oscillator (DRO) or a crystal oscillator followed by a frequency multiplier.
2. **Power Amplifier:** Boosts the carrier to the required transmitted power $P_{t}$. Solid-state PAs are used in modern CW radars because of their linearity and efficiency.
3. **Circulator / Duplexer:** A non-reciprocal three-port ferrite device that routes the transmitted signal to the antenna and the received echo to the mixer, while providing **≥ 20 dB** of isolation between transmitter and receiver to prevent saturation.
4. **Directional Antenna:** A high-gain antenna (parabolic dish or horn) that concentrates the radiated energy in a narrow beam, maximizing the power density on the target.
5. **Mixer (Product Detector):** A nonlinear device (Schottky-diode double-balanced mixer) that multiplies the received echo with a sample of the transmitted carrier, producing sum and difference frequencies. The difference — the **Doppler frequency** $f_{d}$ — is selected as the IF output.
6. **IF Amplifier:** A narrowband, low-noise amplifier tuned to the expected Doppler spectrum (typically 100 Hz – 100 kHz) that boosts the weak echo and limits the noise bandwidth to $B_{n}$.
7. **Detector & Display:** An envelope detector extracts the Doppler audio tone, which is then displayed as a numeric speed on a digital readout, an audio loudspeaker tone, or a spectrum-plot on an FFT analyzer.

**(b) Derivation of the CW Radar Range Equation (7 marks):**

*Steps 1–5 carry 5 marks (1 mark each); Steps 6–7 carry 2 marks.*

Step 1 — Power density at range $R$ with antenna gain $G$:

$$S_{R} = \frac{P_{t} G}{4 \pi R^{2}}$$

Step 2 — Power intercepted by target of RCS $\sigma$:

$$P_{\text{int}} = S_{R} \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_{t} G \sigma}{4 \pi R^{2}}$$

Step 3 — Echo power density back at the radar:

$$S_{\text{echo}} = \frac{P_{\text{int}}}{4 \pi R^{2}} = \frac{P_{t} G \sigma}{(4 \pi)^{2} R^{4}}$$

Step 4 — Power captured by receiving antenna of effective aperture $A_{e}$:

$$P_{r} = S_{\text{echo}} \cdot A_{e} = \frac{P_{t} G \sigma \, A_{e}}{(4 \pi)^{2} R^{4}}$$

Step 5 — Substitute $A_{e} = \dfrac{G \lambda^{2}}{4 \pi}$ (monostatic, $G_{tx} = G_{rx} = G$):

$$P_{r} = \frac{P_{t} G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4 \pi)^{3} R^{4}}$$

Step 6 — Receiver noise power is $N_{i} = k T_{s} B_{n}$, hence the SNR:

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{P_{r}}{k T_{s} B_{n}} = \frac{P_{t} G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4 \pi)^{3} R^{4} k T_{s} B_{n}}$$

Step 7 — Limitations compared to pulsed radar: (i) Cannot measure range; (ii) Requires **continuous high isolation** between Tx and Rx (challenging at high power); (iii) Suffers from **transmitter leakage** noise that may swamp weak echoes; (iv) Range equation uses **average** $P_{t}$, not peak, so the CW radar has no pulse-compression gain and is limited to short ranges.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **homodyne CW radar**. How does the mixer output produce the Doppler signal? **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A 10 GHz CW radar has a maximum unambiguous radial velocity of 100 m/s. Determine the **maximum and minimum Doppler frequencies** and the **minimum IF bandwidth** required to process the echo. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Homodyne CW Radar Operation (7 marks):**

In a **homodyne** (or "zero-IF") CW radar, the **local oscillator port of the mixer is fed with a sample of the *transmitted* carrier itself**, not with a separate offset frequency. Thus the mixer acts as a direct down-converter:

* Transmitted signal: $v_{t}(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_{0} t)$
* Received echo (from a target at radial velocity $v_{r}$): $v_{r}(t) = B \cos\!\left[2\pi (f_{0} + f_{d}) t + \phi\right]$

The mixer multiplies the two:

$$v_{t} \cdot v_{r} = A B \cos(2\pi f_{0} t) \cos\!\left[2\pi(f_{0}+f_{d})t + \phi\right]$$

Using the product-to-sum identity:

$$v_{t} \cdot v_{r} = \frac{A B}{2}\!\left[\cos(2\pi f_{d} t + \phi) + \cos(2\pi(2f_{0}+f_{d})t + \phi)\right]$$

After low-pass filtering, only the **Doppler difference term** survives:

$$v_{\text{IF}}(t) = \frac{A B}{2} \cos(2\pi f_{d} t + \phi)$$

This is the **audio-frequency Doppler signal** that the receiver amplifies, detects, and displays. The block diagram mirrors Section 4.1 with the LO port of the mixer tapping the CW oscillator output directly. (7 marks)

**(b) Numerical Doppler and Bandwidth Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $f_{0} = 10 \text{ GHz}$, $v_{r,\max} = 100 \text{ m/s}$, $v_{r,\min}$ (assume stationary target, $v_{r} = 0$, so $f_{d,\min} = 0$).

**Step 1 — Operating wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_{0}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{10 \times 10^{9}} = 0.03 \text{ m} = 3 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Doppler frequency:**

$$f_{d,\max} = \frac{2 v_{r,\max}}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 100}{0.03} = 6666.67 \text{ Hz} \approx 6.67 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3 — Minimum Doppler frequency (target moving directly transverse / no radial component):**

$$f_{d,\min} = 0 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Minimum IF bandwidth required to capture the full Doppler band:**

For a homodyne receiver, the IF bandwidth must be at least:

$$B_{n,\min} \approx f_{d,\max} = 6.67 \text{ kHz}$$

(For practical margin with safety factor of 1.5–2, design $B_{n} \approx 10 \text{ kHz}$.)

**Final Answer:** $f_{d,\max} \approx 6.67 \text{ kHz}$, $f_{d,\min} = 0 \text{ Hz}$, minimum IF bandwidth $B_{n} \approx 6.67 \text{ kHz}$. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing CW and Pulsed radar equations** — mixing $P_{peak}$ vs $P_{avg}$ and forgetting the bandwidth $B_{n}$ term costs **3–4 marks** outright.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the Doppler formula** — examiners repeatedly dock full marks for writing $f_{d} = v_{r}/\lambda$.
> 3. **Omitting the circulator / duplexer** in the block diagram — a non-negotiable block; if missing, deduct **1 mark**.
> 4. **Drawing a block diagram with no signal-flow arrows** — examiners require explicit arrows showing Tx and Rx paths.
> 5. **Stating "CW radar measures both range and velocity"** — this is factually wrong for *pure* CW; range requires modulation (FM-CW). Full-mark question on FM-CW will be marked **zero** for this misconception.
> 6. **Not defining all variables** ($P_{t}, G, \lambda, \sigma, R, k, T_{s}, B_{n}$) — KTU answer-script audit requires every symbol to be defined.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **CW Radar** = continuous, unmodulated transmission; uses **Doppler shift** to measure target **radial velocity only**.
* **Doppler equation:** $f_{d} = \dfrac{2 v_{r}}{\lambda} = \dfrac{2 v_{r} f_{0}}{c}$ — the factor of 2 is from the **two-way** path.
* **Block Diagram Core Blocks:** CW Oscillator → Power Amplifier → Circulator/Duplexer → Antenna → Mixer (with Tx sample as LO) → Narrowband IF Amplifier → Envelope Detector → Display/Audio.
* **Range equation:** $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{P_{t} G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} R^{4} k T_{s} B_{n}}$; solve for $R_{max}$ using the **4th root**.
* **Key limitations:** No range measurement, transmitter leakage, low average power handling, requires very high Tx/Rx isolation.
* **Variants that *do* measure range:** **FM-CW** (beat frequency $f_{b} = \dfrac{2 R \Delta f}{c T_{m}}$), **Phase-Coded CW**, **Dual-Frequency CW**.
* **Typical applications:** Speed guns, automotive radar (77 GHz), Doppler weather radar, missile proximity fuzes, vital-sign monitoring.
* **Exam-ready distinctions:**
  * Pure CW → velocity only
  * FM-CW / Phase-Coded → velocity **and** range
  * Pulsed → range **and** velocity (with ambiguity resolution)
* **Constants to memorize:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $T_{0} = 290$ K.
* **Bandwidth rule of thumb:** IF bandwidth $B_{n} \approx 1.5 \times f_{d,\max}$ for safe detection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a CW Radar?

**Continuous Wave (CW) Radar** is a type of radar system that transmits and receives electromagnetic waves **continuously** (as opposed to pulsed radar, which transmits short bursts separated by listening periods). Because the transmission is uninterrupted, CW radar cannot directly measure range by timing an echo. Instead, it relies on **frequency shifts (Doppler effect)** and/or **frequency modulation (FM-CW)** to extract target information such as velocity, range, or both.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A CW radar transmits a continuous, unmodulated (or modulated) sinusoidal wave and extracts target information from the **Doppler frequency shift** between the transmitted and received signals. Its main subclasses are **CW Doppler Radar** and **FM-CW Radar**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine standing at the edge of a river and shouting continuously toward the flowing water. A microphone placed on the opposite bank will hear your voice — but if the water surface (acting as a moving "target") is moving toward you, the reflected sound will appear slightly **higher in pitch**; if moving away, slightly **lower in pitch**. This pitch change is the **Doppler shift**. A CW radar does exactly the same thing, but with **electromagnetic waves** and a **moving object** (a car, a missile, raindrops, a human heart wall).

- **Doppler Radar** = "How fast is the target moving?"
- **FM-CW Radar** = "How fast is it moving *and* how far is it?"

## 1.3 Classification of CW Radar

| Type | Transmitted Signal | Information Extracted |
|------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Simple CW Doppler Radar** | Pure sinusoid (no modulation) | Velocity only |
| **FM-CW Radar** | Linearly frequency-modulated sinusoid | Range and Velocity |
| **Two-Frequency CW Radar** | Two unmodulated carriers | Unambiguous range + velocity |
| **PM-CW Radar** | Phase-modulated carrier | Range with high resolution |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 syllabus, the focus is on **CW Doppler Radar** and **FM-CW Radar** along with their **engineering applications**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- Speed of light: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- Standard radar operating bands: **L (1–2 GHz), S (2–4 GHz), C (4–8 GHz), X (8–12 GHz), Ku (12–18 GHz)**
- Police radar gun frequency: **X-band (10.525 GHz)** or **K-band (24.150 GHz)**
- Police radar accuracy: **± 1 km/h** (typical)

> [!TIP]
> Remember that **higher carrier frequency** ⇒ **larger Doppler shift for the same velocity** ⇒ **better velocity resolution**. This is why police radar guns operate at X or K band, not at lower HF/VHF bands.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CW Doppler Radar — Operating Principle

A simple CW Doppler radar transmits a continuous sinusoid:

$$
s_t(t) = A_t \cos(2\pi f_0 t)
$$

When the transmitted wave strikes a moving target with radial velocity $v_r$ (positive = receding, negative = approaching), the received echo is shifted in frequency by the **Doppler frequency**:

$$
f_d = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}
$$

where:
- $f_0$ = transmitted frequency (Hz)
- $c$ = speed of light (3 × 10⁸ m/s)
- $\lambda = c / f_0$ = wavelength (m)
- $v_r$ = radial velocity of the target (m/s)

The received signal is:

$$
s_r(t) = A_r \cos\bigl(2\pi(f_0 + f_d)t - \phi\bigr)
$$

Mixing $s_t(t)$ with $s_r(t)$ in a coherent detector produces a beat (difference) frequency exactly equal to $f_d$. A frequency counter or FFT then converts $f_d$ into velocity.

### Key Logical Steps

1. **Transmit** a pure continuous tone at $f_0$.
2. **Receive** the echo, whose frequency is $f_0 \pm f_d$.
3. **Mix (coherent detection)** to obtain the difference frequency $f_d$.
4. **Process** $f_d$ digitally to display velocity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** A pure CW radar **cannot measure range**, because without a time-reference pulse, there is no way to determine the round-trip delay. This is the fundamental motivation for **FM-CW radar**.

## 2.2 The FM-CW Radar — Operating Principle

In FM-CW radar, the transmitted frequency is linearly swept over a bandwidth $\Delta f$ during a sweep period $T_m$. The instantaneous transmitted frequency is:

$$
f_t(t) = f_0 + \frac{\Delta f}{T_m}\,t
$$

The echo from a target at range $R$ arrives with a time delay:

$$
\tau = \frac{2R}{c}
$$

The instantaneous frequency difference (beat frequency) between transmitted and received signals is:

$$
f_b = \frac{\Delta f}{T_m}\,\tau = \frac{2 R \Delta f}{c\,T_m}
$$

If the target is also moving with radial velocity $v_r$, the received signal carries an additional Doppler shift $f_d$. The instantaneous beat frequency becomes:

$$
f_b = f_r \pm f_d
$$

where $f_r$ is the range-related beat frequency and $f_d$ is the Doppler frequency. By sweeping in both **up-chirp** and **down-chirp** directions, $f_r$ and $f_d$ can be unambiguously separated:

$$
f_b^{\text{up}} = f_r - f_d
$$

$$
f_b^{\text{down}} = f_r + f_d
$$

Solving:

$$
f_r = \frac{f_b^{\text{up}} + f_b^{\text{down}}}{2}, \quad f_d = \frac{f_b^{\text{down}} - f_b^{\text{up}}}{2}
$$

## 2.3 The Two-Frequency CW Radar — Operating Principle

To resolve the **range-velocity ambiguity** without complex chirp processing, two closely-spaced continuous carriers $f_1$ and $f_2 = f_1 + \Delta f$ are transmitted. Each carrier produces its own Doppler frequency, and the **phase difference** between them is proportional to range:

$$
\Delta\phi = \frac{4 \pi R \Delta f}{c}
$$

Solving for range:

$$
R = \frac{c\, \Delta\phi}{4\pi\,\Delta f}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Two-frequency CW radar measures **range unambiguously** within $R_{\max} = c/(2\Delta f)$ and **velocity** from the average Doppler shift — without any pulsed transmission. This is the technique used in some short-range homing seekers.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Equation | Expression | Meaning | Unit |
|-------------------|------------|---------|------|
| Doppler frequency | $f_d = 2v_r f_0 / c$ | Frequency shift due to target motion | Hz |
| Doppler (in terms of λ) | $f_d = 2v_r / \lambda$ | Equivalent form | Hz |
| Radial velocity | $v_r = f_d \lambda / 2$ | Velocity from measured $f_d$ | m/s |
| FM-CW beat frequency | $f_b = 2R\Delta f / (c T_m)$ | Range-related beat | Hz |
| FM-CW range | $R = c T_m f_b / (2 \Delta f)$ | Range from beat | m |
| Two-frequency phase | $\Delta\phi = 4\pi R \Delta f / c$ | Phase difference between carriers | rad |
| Two-frequency range | $R = c \Delta\phi / (4\pi \Delta f)$ | Range from phase | m |
| Max unambiguous range (2-f) | $R_{\max} = c / (2\Delta f)$ | Range ambiguity limit | m |
| Range resolution | $\delta R = c / (2 \Delta f)$ | Minimum resolvable range step | m |
| Received power (CW) | $P_r = P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma / (64\pi^3 R^4)$ | Standard radar equation | W |
| MTI improvement factor | $I = \bar{n} \sigma_n^2 / \sigma_o^2$ | Clutter rejection ratio | dB |

> [!NOTE]
> Note carefully that CW radar uses the **same radar range equation** as pulsed radar (for the same target, antenna, and frequency). The difference is in how range information is extracted — not in the link budget.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Police speed guns** — handheld Doppler radars using Gunn diode oscillators at X/Ku band.
- **Automotive cruise control (ACC)** — 76–77 GHz FMCW radar for adaptive distance keeping.
- **Anti-lock Braking Systems (ABS)** — short-range FMCW sensors detect wheel slip.
- **Aircraft altimeters** — low-power FM-CW radar in the 4.3 GHz band (RCA AVQ-95 etc.).
- **Ground Penetrating Radar (GPR)** — ultra-wideband FMCW for archaeology and forensics.
- **Doppler weather radar** — measures raindrop radial velocity.
- **Medical radar** — non-contact heart rate and respiration monitoring using CW Doppler.

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU favourite: "Why is FMCW used in automotive radar instead of pulsed radar?" — Answer: because automotive radar must coexist with other vehicles, requires low peak power (regulatory + EMC reasons), and benefits from high range resolution with modest hardware.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Doppler Frequency from Wavefront Geometry

Consider a stationary transmitter and a target moving with radial velocity $v_r$ directly toward the source. The transmitted wave has frequency $f_0$ and wavelength $\lambda = c/f_0$.

**Step 1 — Wavefront Spacing:**
The distance between successive crests of the transmitted wave is exactly $\lambda$.

**Step 2 — Distance Compressed by Target in One Period:**
During one period $T_0 = 1/f_0$, the target moves a distance $v_r T_0$. Therefore, the spacing between the crests *as seen by the target* is compressed to:

$$
\lambda' = \lambda - v_r T_0
$$

**Step 3 — Re-radiation Frequency:**
The target re-radiates these compressed wavefronts. The frequency observed by the target is:

$$
f' = \frac{c}{\lambda'} = \frac{c}{\lambda - v_r T_0}
$$

Substitute $\lambda = c T_0$:

$$
f' = \frac{c}{c T_0 - v_r T_0} = \frac{1}{T_0(1 - v_r/c)} = \frac{f_0}{1 - v_r/c}
$$

For $v_r \ll c$, expand using the binomial approximation $(1-x)^{-1} \approx 1 + x$:

$$
f' \approx f_0 \left(1 + \frac{v_r}{c}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Two-Way (Round-Trip) Shift:**
The wave reflected back toward the source is shifted a second time by the same mechanism. The receiver (stationary) sees:

$$
f_r = \frac{f'}{1 - v_r/c} \approx f_0 \left(1 + \frac{v_r}{c}\right)^2
$$

Using $(1+x)^2 \approx 1 + 2x$ for $x \ll 1$:

$$
f_r \approx f_0 \left(1 + \frac{2 v_r}{c}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Final Doppler Shift:**

$$
\boxed{\,f_d = f_r - f_0 = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}\,}
$$

This derivation justifies the **factor of 2** in the two-way Doppler shift, which is unique to radar (as opposed to sonar or laser vibrometry where the reflecting surface is itself the receiver, giving a one-way shift).

## 3.2 Derivation: Beat Frequency in Linear FM-CW Radar

**Step 1 — Transmitted Frequency as a Function of Time:**
Linear frequency modulation gives:

$$
f_t(t) = f_0 + \frac{\Delta f}{T_m}\, t, \quad 0 \le t \le T_m
$$

**Step 2 — Received Frequency (Stationary Target at Range R):**
The echo arrives after delay $\tau = 2R/c$. Assuming $R$ is constant over the sweep (no range migration):

$$
f_r(t) = f_t(t - \tau) = f_0 + \frac{\Delta f}{T_m}(t - \tau)
$$

**Step 3 — Instantaneous Beat Frequency:**

$$
f_b(t) = f_t(t) - f_r(t) = \frac{\Delta f}{T_m}\,\tau = \frac{2 R \Delta f}{c\, T_m}
$$

Notice that $f_b$ is **constant** in time for a stationary target — making it easy to measure with an FFT.

**Step 4 — Range from Beat Frequency:**

$$
\boxed{\,R = \frac{c\, T_m\, f_b}{2 \Delta f}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Range Resolution:**
A radar can resolve a change in beat frequency equal to $\delta f_b = 1/T_m$ (FFT bin width). Therefore:

$$
\delta R = \frac{c\, T_m}{2 \Delta f} \cdot \frac{1}{T_m} = \frac{c}{2 \Delta f}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Range resolution depends **only on the swept bandwidth $\Delta f$** and not on the sweep period $T_m$. Doubling the bandwidth halves the range resolution cell — this is why modern 77 GHz automotive FMCW radars use $\Delta f = 4$ GHz to get $\delta R \approx 3.75$ cm.

## 3.3 Derivation: Separation of Range and Doppler in FMCW

For a target with **range R** and **radial velocity v_r**, the instantaneous beat frequency in an up-chirp sweep is:

$$
f_b^{\text{up}} = \frac{2R\Delta f}{c T_m} - f_d
$$

In a down-chirp sweep (frequency decreases with time), the sign of the range term flips:

$$
f_b^{\text{down}} = \frac{2R\Delta f}{c T_m} + f_d
$$

**Step 6 — Solving the System:**

$$
f_r = \frac{f_b^{\text{up}} + f_b^{\text{down}}}{2} = \frac{2R\Delta f}{c T_m}
$$

$$
f_d = \frac{f_b^{\text{down}} - f_b^{\text{up}}}{2}
$$

Substituting $R$:

$$
\boxed{\,R = \frac{c T_m f_r}{2 \Delta f}, \qquad v_r = \frac{f_d \lambda}{2}\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This dual-sweep technique is the heart of modern **chirp-sequence FMCW** radars used in Tesla, Bosch, and Continental ADAS systems. Always remember that the **Doppler sign reversal** is the key that decouples range and velocity.

## 3.4 Derivation: Phase-Based Range in Two-Frequency CW Radar

**Step 1 — Transmit Two Carriers:**

$$
s_1(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_1 t + \phi_1), \quad s_2(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_2 t + \phi_2), \quad f_2 = f_1 + \Delta f
$$

**Step 2 — Phase Delay for Each Carrier at Range R:**

$$
\phi_1 = \frac{2\pi f_1 (2R)}{c} = \frac{4\pi f_1 R}{c}, \quad \phi_2 = \frac{4\pi f_2 R}{c}
$$

**Step 3 — Phase Difference:**

$$
\Delta\phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1 = \frac{4\pi (f_2 - f_1) R}{c} = \frac{4\pi \Delta f R}{c}
$$

**Step 4 — Range:**

$$
\boxed{\,R = \frac{c\, \Delta\phi}{4\pi \Delta f}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Maximum Unambiguous Range:**
Since $\Delta\phi$ is only measurable modulo $2\pi$, the unambiguous range is:

$$
\Delta\phi_{\max} = 2\pi \;\Rightarrow\; R_{\max} = \frac{c}{2\Delta f}
$$

This is identical in form to the FMCW range-resolution expression — a beautiful duality between time-domain and frequency-domain ranging.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Application → Radar Type → Key Equation

| Engineering Application | Radar Type Used | Primary Equation | Frequency Band |
|------------------------|----------------|------------------|---------------|
| Police speed gun | Simple CW Doppler | $v_r = f_d \lambda / 2$ | X / K / Ka |
| Adaptive cruise control | FM-CW | $R = c T_m f_b / (2 \Delta f)$ | 76–77 GHz |
| Aircraft radio altimeter | FM-CW | $R = c \Delta\phi / (4 \pi \Delta f)$ | 4.2–4.4 GHz |
| Missile proximity fuze | CW Doppler | $f_d = 2 v_r / \lambda$ | S / C |
| Ballistic missile early warning | Pulsed (not CW) | $R = c \tau / 2$ | UHF / L |
| Doppler weather radar | CW Doppler / pulsed | $v_r = f_d \lambda / 2$ | S (2.7–3.0 GHz) |
| Ground-penetrating radar | UWB FM-CW / impulse | $R = c T_m f_b / (2 \Delta f)$ | 10 MHz – 2 GHz |
| Human vital-sign monitor | CW Doppler | $f_d = 2 v_r / \lambda$ | 2.4 / 24 GHz |
| Industrial flow meter | CW Doppler | $v = f_d \lambda / (2 \cos\theta)$ | K / Ka |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a CW Doppler Radar

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    osc1[Oscillator\nf0 CW Source] -->|lo signal| mix1[Mixer]
    osc1 -->|tx signal| ant1[Transmit\nAntenna]
    ant1 -.->|radiated CW| tgt1[Moving Target]
    tgt1 -.->|reflected echo| ant2[Receive\nAntenna]
    ant2 --> lna1[Low Noise\nAmplifier]
    lna1 --> mix1[Coherent\nMixer]
    mix1 --> bp1[Bandpass Filter\nat Doppler f]
    bp1 --> amp1[Audio Amplifier]
    amp1 --> cnt1[Frequency\nCounter / FFT]
    cnt1 --> disp1[Velocity Display\nv = f_d x lambda / 2]
    style osc1 fill:#FFD580
    style mix1 fill:#A0D8F0
    style cnt1 fill:#B0F0A0
    style disp1 fill:#F0A0D8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Logic:** The same oscillator feeds both the transmit path and the local-oscillator (LO) port of the mixer. Mixing the received echo with a sample of the transmitted signal produces a difference frequency exactly equal to the Doppler shift. The bandpass filter removes DC (which would correspond to stationary clutter) and extracts only the moving-target Doppler tones.

## 4.2 Block Diagram of an FM-CW Radar

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fmsyn[Frequency\nSynthesizer] --> vco[VCO\nLinear FM ramp]
    vco -->|f0 + ramp| txamp[Power Amplifier]
    txamp --> antx[Tx Antenna]
    antx -.->|chirp signal| tgtx[Target at Range R]
    tgtx -.->|delayed echo| antRx[Rx Antenna]
    antRx --> rxLNA[LNA]
    rxLNA --> mixx[Coherent Mixer]
    vco -->|LO sample| mixx
    mixx -->|beat f_b| lpfx[Low-Pass Filter]
    lpfx --> adcx[ADC]
    adcx --> fftx[FFT Processor]
    fftx --> out1[Range Profile]
    fftx --> out2[Velocity Estimate]
    style vco fill:#FFD580
    style mixx fill:#A0D8F0
    style fftx fill:#B0F0A0
    style out1 fill:#F0A0D8
    style out2 fill:#F0A0D8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Logic:** The VCO is ramped linearly. The received echo is delayed by $2R/c$, so when mixed with the current transmitted signal, a beat frequency $f_b$ is produced. An FFT of $f_b$ reveals the range profile, and dual up/down chirps separate the range and Doppler.

```mermaid

    A[CW Radar\nFamily] --> B[Simple CW\nDoppler]
    A --> C[FM-CW Radar]
    A --> D[Two-Frequency\nCW Radar]

    B --> B2[Proximity Fuze]

    B --> B4[Industrial Flow Meter]

    style A fill:#FFD580

    style C fill:#A0F0D8
    style D fill:#F0A0D8
```

## 4.4 Signal-Frequency-Time Relationship in FM-CW

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Tx Chirp\nf = f0 + D x t] -.delay tau = 2R/c.-> B[Rx Echo\nf = f0 + D x t-tau]
    A --> M[Mixer]
    B --> M
    M --> C[Beat Signal\nf_b = D x tau = 2R.D / c]
    C --> D[FFT]
    D --> E[Range Peak at f_b]
    style A fill:#FFD580
    style B fill:#A0D8F0
    style C fill:#B0F0A0
    style E fill:#F0A0D8
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The transmitted chirp (linearly increasing frequency) and the received echo (same chirp, delayed by $\tau = 2R/c$) are mixed. The output is a **constant** beat frequency $f_b$ equal to $D \cdot \tau$, where $D = \Delta f / T_m$ is the chirp rate. A single FFT bin directly gives the range of the target.

## 4.5 Doppler-Shift Visualisation (Approaching vs Receding Target)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Transmitter] -->|f0| T1[Target moving AWAY\nv_r positive]
    T1 -->|f0 - f_d| A1
    A2[Transmitter] -->|f0| T2[Target moving TOWARD\nv_r negative]
    T2 -->|f0 + f_d| A2
    style T1 fill:#F0A0A0
    style T2 fill:#A0F0A0
```

> [!TIP]
> The **sign** of $f_d$ tells the radar whether the target is approaching (positive Doppler at the receiver when the target moves toward) or receding. Police radar guns display a "+" or "−" sign to indicate direction of travel.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Q: List any three applications of CW Doppler radar and explain how the Doppler effect is used in a police speed gun.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Three applications of CW Doppler radar (1½ marks):**
1. Police speed guns for traffic enforcement
2. Missile proximity fuzes for warhead detonation timing
3. Doppler navigation in aircraft (measuring ground-velocity vector)

**Police speed gun — Doppler principle (1½ marks):**
A police radar gun transmits a continuous microwave signal (typically at 10.525 GHz X-band or 24.15 GHz K-band) toward an oncoming vehicle. The moving car reflects this signal with a frequency shift given by:

$$
f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}
$$

The radar's mixer beats the received echo against a sample of the transmitted signal, producing an audio tone at $f_d$. The internal microcontroller computes:

$$
v_r = \frac{f_d \lambda}{2}
$$

and displays the vehicle's speed in km/h. For $\lambda = 0.0286$ m (X-band), a vehicle moving at $v_r = 100$ km/h = 27.78 m/s produces $f_d = 2 \times 27.78 / 0.0286 \approx 1943$ Hz, well within the audio band.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Q: Why cannot a simple CW radar measure the range of a target? How is this limitation overcome in FM-CW radar?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Why simple CW cannot measure range (1½ marks):**
A simple CW radar transmits a continuous, unmodulated sinusoid. Without a time marker (such as a pulse edge) in the transmitted signal, there is no reference to time the round-trip echo delay $\tau = 2R/c$. The received signal differs from the transmitted signal only in phase (by $4\pi R / \lambda$) and Doppler shift, neither of which can unambiguously determine $R$ for a target at unknown velocity.

**How FM-CW overcomes this (1½ marks):**
In FM-CW radar, the transmitted frequency is linearly ramped in time. The echo returns with the same chirp, but delayed by $\tau$. Mixing the transmitted and received signals produces a **beat frequency** proportional to $\tau$ (and hence to $R$):

$$
f_b = \frac{2 R \Delta f}{c T_m} \;\Rightarrow\; R = \frac{c T_m f_b}{2 \Delta f}
$$

By measuring $f_b$ using an FFT, the range $R$ is determined directly.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: (a) Derive the expression for the Doppler frequency shift in a CW radar. (7 marks)**
**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of an FM-CW radar. Discuss any two of its applications. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define the transmitted signal (1 mark):**

$$
s_t(t) = A_t \cos(2\pi f_0 t)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the relativistic Doppler formula to a target moving at radial velocity $v_r$ (2 marks):**

The target first "sees" the wave compressed because it is moving into the wavefronts. The frequency observed at the target is:

$$
f' = \frac{f_0}{1 - v_r/c}
$$

The target re-radiates at $f'$, and the stationary receiver observes a second compression. The final received frequency is:

$$
f_r = \frac{f_0}{(1 - v_r/c)^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Binomial approximation (1 mark):**
For $v_r \ll c$, $(1-x)^{-2} \approx 1 + 2x$, hence:

$$
f_r \approx f_0 \left(1 + \frac{2 v_r}{c}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Doppler shift (2 marks):**

$$
f_d = f_r - f_0 = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 5 — Concluding remark (1 mark):**
The factor of 2 arises because the wave undergoes **two** Doppler shifts — once on transmit-target leg, once on target-receiver leg. The sign convention: $v_r > 0$ for receding target → $f_d > 0$ (frequency increased); $v_r < 0$ for approaching target → $f_d < 0$ (frequency decreased — this is the situation for police radar guns).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
Refer to the FM-CW block diagram in **Section 4.2**. The student must draw and label:
- Frequency synthesizer / VCO producing a linear FM ramp
- Transmit power amplifier and Tx antenna
- Receive antenna and LNA
- Coherent mixer (with LO coupled from VCO)
- Low-pass filter
- ADC and FFT processor
- Output (range profile / velocity)

**Operation (2 marks):**
The VCO is ramped linearly from $f_0$ to $f_0 + \Delta f$ over period $T_m$. The transmitted chirp is:

$$
f_t(t) = f_0 + \frac{\Delta f}{T_m}\, t
$$

The echo from a target at range $R$ arrives at $t + \tau$, $\tau = 2R/c$. After coherent mixing, the beat frequency is:

$$
f_b = \frac{2R \Delta f}{c T_m}
$$

Range is obtained by:

$$
R = \frac{c T_m f_b}{2 \Delta f}
$$

**Two Applications (2 marks):**

1. **Automotive Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC):** 76–77 GHz FMCW radar is mounted in the front bumper. It continuously measures the range and relative velocity of the vehicle ahead. Range resolution is $\delta R = c/(2\Delta f) \approx 3.75$ cm for $\Delta f = 4$ GHz, allowing precise distance tracking from 0.5 m to 200 m.

2. **Aircraft Radio Altimeter:** 4.3 GHz FMCW radar pointed at the ground during landing approach. It provides accurate altitude (0–2500 ft) for auto-landing systems. The CW nature gives low peak power, ensuring the altimeter does not interfere with other avionics.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: (a) Describe the principle of operation of a police Doppler radar with the help of a block diagram. A police radar operating at 10.525 GHz measures a Doppler shift of 1900 Hz from an approaching vehicle. Calculate the speed of the vehicle. (7 marks)**
**(b) Explain the principle and applications of two-frequency CW radar with relevant equations. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
Refer to **Section 4.1**. The student must include:
- CW oscillator (Gunn diode / dielectric resonator oscillator)
- Transmit antenna (horn / microstrip patch)
- Receive antenna
- Low-noise amplifier
- Coherent mixer
- Bandpass filter (centred on Doppler band, e.g. 100 Hz – 5 kHz)
- Audio amplifier
- Microcontroller / frequency counter
- Display

**Operation (1 mark):**
The Gunn oscillator produces a continuous 10.525 GHz carrier. A portion is radiated toward the target, and a portion is fed to the mixer as a local oscillator reference. The received echo is mixed with this LO. The output contains a tone at $|f_d|$ plus DC. The DC is blocked by an AC-coupled bandpass filter, and the remaining tone is amplified, counted, and converted to velocity by:

$$
v_r = \frac{f_d \lambda}{2}
$$

**Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $f_0 = 10.525$ GHz = $10.525 \times 10^9$ Hz, $f_d = 1900$ Hz, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

Wavelength:

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10.525 \times 10^9} = 0.02850 \text{ m}
$$

Velocity:

$$
v_r = \frac{f_d \lambda}{2} = \frac{1900 \times 0.02850}{2} = 27.08 \text{ m/s}
$$

Convert to km/h:

$$
v_r = 27.08 \times \frac{18}{5} = 97.5 \text{ km/h}
$$

**Answer: The vehicle is moving at approximately 97.5 km/h.**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Principle (3 marks):**
A two-frequency CW radar transmits two coherent continuous carriers $f_1$ and $f_2 = f_1 + \Delta f$ simultaneously. Each carrier reflected from a target at range $R$ experiences a round-trip phase delay:

$$
\phi_1 = \frac{4\pi f_1 R}{c}, \quad \phi_2 = \frac{4\pi f_2 R}{c}
$$

The **phase difference** at the receiver between the two carriers is:

$$
\Delta\phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1 = \frac{4\pi \Delta f R}{c}
$$

Solving for range:

$$
R = \frac{c \Delta\phi}{4\pi \Delta f}
$$

Since $\Delta\phi$ is measurable only modulo $2\pi$, the **unambiguous range** is:

$$
R_{\max} = \frac{c}{2 \Delta f}
$$

**Why two frequencies? (1 mark):**
A single CW carrier can only measure the *fractional* phase, not the absolute range. By using two closely-spaced frequencies, the radar synthesizes a synthetic low frequency equal to $\Delta f$, allowing phase-wrapping-free range measurement.

**Velocity measurement (1 mark):**
The Doppler frequencies at $f_1$ and $f_2$ are:

$$
f_{d1} = \frac{2 v_r f_1}{c}, \quad f_{d2} = \frac{2 v_r f_2}{c}
$$

Averaging gives an accurate velocity estimate.

**Applications (2 marks):**

1. **Short-range homing missile seekers:** Two-frequency CW provides both range and velocity, allowing the missile to compute a homing trajectory without expensive pulsed hardware. Used in many anti-tank and anti-aircraft missiles.

2. **Phase-comparison radio altimeters:** Two frequencies (e.g. 4.2 and 4.4 GHz) are transmitted downward. The phase difference gives altitude with cm-level accuracy during the final approach. The unambiguous range is $c / (2 \times 0.2 \times 10^9) = 0.75$ m — sufficient for landing, but unambiguous.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** The Doppler shift in radar is $f_d = 2v_r/\lambda$, NOT $v_r/\lambda$. Losing this factor costs **2 full marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up one-way vs. two-way phase:** Range-related phase in CW/FMCW radar is $4\pi R/\lambda$ (round trip), not $2\pi R/\lambda$. The 4 vs. 2 distinction is a common trap.
> 3. **Unit errors:** Converting between m/s and km/h incorrectly. Remember: $1$ m/s = $3.6$ km/h.
> 4. **Sign of Doppler:** Many students drop the sign of $v_r$, which is essential for direction sensing.
> 5. **In FMCW, not stating the assumption $v_r \ll c$** before the binomial expansion: you will lose marks if the small-velocity approximation is not justified.
> 6. **Not labelling the LO port** of the mixer in the block diagram — KTU examiners explicitly check for this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **CW Radar** = Continuous transmission, no pulse. Information extracted from **Doppler shift** and/or **frequency modulation**.
- **Doppler Frequency Formula:** $f_d = 2 v_r f_0 / c = 2 v_r / \lambda$. Always include the **factor of 2** (round-trip).
- **Radial Velocity:** $v_r = f_d \lambda / 2$. Police radar: $v_r$ in km/h = $1.8 \times f_d \lambda$ with $\lambda$ in m, $f_d$ in Hz.
- **Simple CW Doppler Radar:** Measures **velocity only**, cannot measure range.
- **FM-CW Radar:** Measures **both range and velocity** by linearly sweeping the carrier.
- **FMCW Beat Frequency (stationary target):** $f_b = 2 R \Delta f / (c T_m)$. Range is then $R = c T_m f_b / (2 \Delta f)$.
- **Range Resolution in FMCW:** $\delta R = c / (2 \Delta f)$. Independent of sweep time $T_m$.
- **Two-Frequency CW Radar:** Measures range from **phase difference** $\Delta\phi = 4\pi R \Delta f / c$.
- **Max Unambiguous Range (Two-Frequency CW):** $R_{\max} = c / (2 \Delta f)$.
- **Doppler Sign Convention:** Target approaching ⇒ $v_r < 0$ ⇒ $f_d$ **decreases** (or, equivalently, echo frequency increases — be consistent with your textbook's sign convention).
- **Police Radar Bands:** X-band (10.525 GHz), K-band (24.15 GHz), Ka-band (33.4–36.0 GHz). K/Ka bands offer higher Doppler for same velocity.
- **Automotive FMCW:** 76–77 GHz, bandwidth up to 4 GHz, range resolution ~3.75 cm, peak power < 10 mW (low-EMC friendly).
- **Aircraft Altimeter:** 4.3 GHz FMCW, range 0–2500 ft, accuracy < 1 m.
- **Missile Proximity Fuze:** CW Doppler, generates detonation pulse when $f_d$ falls below threshold (close approach).
- **MTI Filtering:** Stationary clutter produces $f_d = 0$, so AC-coupling / HPF removes it. This is why CW radar is naturally clutter-immune.
- **Limitation of CW Radar:** Cannot inherently measure range unless modulated (FM, PM) or multi-frequency.
- **Standard Radar Equation still applies:** $P_r = P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma / (64\pi^3 R^4)$. CW vs. pulsed does not change the link budget, only the ranging method.
- **KTU Favourite Numericals:** (1) Compute $v_r$ from given $f_0$ and $f_d$. (2) Compute $R$ from given FMCW parameters. (3) Compute $\Delta f$ required for a given range resolution.
- **Remember the physical constants:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s; 1 m/s = 3.6 km/h; X-band = 8–12 GHz.
- **Common Mistakes to Avoid:** Confusing $f_b$ with $f_d$ in FMCW; forgetting the factor 2 in the radar equation; mixing one-way and two-way phase; missing the small-velocity assumption in derivations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FM-CW Radar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** FM-CW Radar # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **FM-CW Radar (Frequency Modulated – Continuous Wave Radar)** is a class of radar system in which a **Continuous Wave (CW)** carrier is frequency modulated by a periodic waveform (typically a **linear sawtooth** or **triangular wave**), and the range of a target is determined from the **beat frequency** generated by mixing the transmitted signal with the time-delayed echo returned from the target. Unlike pulsed radar, an FM-CW radar transmits a **100% duty-cycle continuous signal** with no high-power RF pulse. Since the transmitter and receiver operate simultaneously, **isolation between them is critical**, and range information is extracted from the **frequency offset** (beat frequency) between the transmitted and received signals rather than from a time-delay measurement. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you are standing on a bridge over a highway with a **whistle** that constantly changes pitch (frequency) — low to high, low to high, repeatedly. When you blow the whistle, the sound travels to a wall, reflects, and returns to your ear. The pitch you hear *right now* is the pitch you are blowing *at this instant*, but the pitch you *just heard* (the echo) is the pitch you were blowing a fraction of a second ago. Since the whistle's pitch keeps changing linearly, the **difference between the pitch you just sent and the pitch you just heard back** is proportional to how far the wall is. A closer wall → smaller time delay → smaller pitch difference. A farther wall → larger time delay → larger pitch difference. This **"pitch difference"** is the **beat frequency** in an FM-CW radar. The wall is the **target**, your whistle is the **frequency-modulated transmitter**, and your ear with the comparison brain is the **mixer and signal processor**. ## 1.3 Why FM-CW Radar? (Key Syllabus Highlight) > [!NOTE] > **Salient Features of FM-CW Radar:** > - **No high-power transmitter required** → compact, low-cost, low-voltage hardware. > - **Excellent short-range accuracy** (down to centimetres) because the beat-frequency resolution is not limited by a narrow pulse width. > - **High duty cycle (100%)** → no range ambiguity in the classical pulse sense. > - **Compatible with solid-state devices** (Gunn diode, VCO), making it ideal for automotive, altimetry, and proximity-sensing applications. > - **Drawback:** Cannot measure unambiguous range and Doppler simultaneously with a single ramp; triangular modulation solves this by adding a second ramp direction. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants The following constants and parameters govern FM-CW radar design: | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Magnitude / Unit | |---|---|---| | $c$ | Speed of light | $3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$ | | $f_0$ | Carrier (RF) centre frequency | **1 GHz – 94 GHz** | | $\Delta f$ | Peak frequency deviation | **100 MHz – few GHz** | | $T_m$ | Modulation period | **1 ms – 10 ms** | | $f_m = 1/T_m$ | Modulation rate | **100 Hz – 1 kHz** | | $\dot{f} = \Delta f / T_m$ | Frequency sweep rate | **Hz/s** | | $R$ | Target range | **metres** | | $v$ | Target radial velocity | **m/s** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1 — Frequency Modulation of the Transmitter** A Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO) generates an RF carrier whose instantaneous frequency is swept linearly with time. For a **linear sawtooth modulation** of period $T_m$: $$f_t(t) = f_0 + \dot{f}\,t, \quad \text{where} \quad \dot{f} = \frac{\Delta f}{T_m}$$ **Step 2 — Transmission and Target Reflection** The modulated wave travels to the target at range $R$, takes a round-trip delay: $$\tau = \frac{2R}{c}$$ and returns with a much smaller amplitude. The **instantaneous frequency of the received echo** is therefore: $$f_r(t) = f_t(t - \tau) = f_0 + \dot{f}(t - \tau)$$ **Step 3 — Mixer Action (Coherent Detection)** A small fraction of the transmitted signal (the **local oscillator tap**) is mixed with the received echo. The mixer output contains sum and difference frequencies; the difference — called the **beat frequency** — carries the range information: $$f_b = f_t(t) - f_r(t) = \dot{f}\,\tau = \frac{2R\,\Delta f}{c\,T_m}$$ **Step 4 — Range Extraction via FFT** The beat signal is amplified in an IF strip and analysed using an **FFT processor**. Each spectral peak corresponds to a target at a specific range. The FFT bin number $n$ and the range are related by: $$R_n = \frac{n\,c}{2\,\Delta f}$$ This is independent of the modulation period — a powerful result. **Step 5 — Why Triangular Modulation?** A single sawtooth measures only $R$. A **triangular wave** produces two beat frequencies during the up-ramp and down-ramp. The two values allow simultaneous extraction of **range AND radial velocity (Doppler)**: $$f_{b,\text{up}} = \frac{2R\,\Delta f}{c\,T_m} - \frac{2v\,f_0}{c}$$ $$f_{b,\text{down}} = \frac{2R\,\Delta f}{c\,T_m} + \frac{2v\,f_0}{c}$$ Adding and subtracting these yields **R** and **v** independently — the cornerstone of any modern FMCW automotive radar. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master Formula Table for FM-CW Radar Problems** | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Round-trip delay | $\tau = \dfrac{2R}{c}$ | seconds | Neglects target velocity (first-order) | | 2 | Beat frequency (sawtooth) | $f_b = \dfrac{2R\,\Delta f}{c\,T_m}$ | Hz | Range only | | 3 | Range (sawtooth) | $R = \dfrac{c\,T_m\,f_b}{2\,\Delta f}$ | metres | Invert eq. 2 | | 4 | Range resolution | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2\,\Delta f}$ | metres | Set by deviation, NOT by pulse width | | 5 | Beat freq. (triangular, up-ramp) | $f_{b,\text{up}} = \dfrac{2R\,\Delta f}{c\,T_m} - \dfrac{2v\,f_0}{c}$ | Hz | Subtract Doppler | | 6 | Beat freq. (triangular, down-ramp) | $f_{b,\text{down}} = \dfrac{2R\,\Delta f}{c\,T_m} + \dfrac{2v\,f_0}{c}$ | Hz | Add Doppler | | 7 | Range (from triangular) | $R = \dfrac{c\,T_m}{4\,\Delta f}(f_{b,\text{up}} + f_{b,\text{down}})$ | metres | Average the two | | 8 | Velocity (from triangular) | $v = \dfrac{c}{4\,f_0}(f_{b,\text{down}} - f_{b,\text{up}})$ | m/s | Half the difference | | 9 | Maximum unambiguous range | $R_{\max} = \dfrac{c}{4\,\Delta f_m}$ (triangular) | metres | $\Delta f_m$ = max usable beat freq. | | 10 | Maximum unambiguous velocity | $v_{\max} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4\,T_m}$ | m/s | From Doppler periodicity | | 11 | IF bandwidth requirement | $B_{\text{IF}} = \dfrac{2R_{\max}\,\Delta f}{c\,T_m}$ | Hz | Mixer output bandwidth | | 12 | Transmitted phase at time $t$ | $\phi_t(t) = 2\pi\!\left[f_0 t + \dfrac{\dot{f}\,t^2}{2}\right]$ | rad | Quadratic due to linear FM | | 13 | Received phase | $\phi_r(t) = \phi_t(t - \tau)$ | rad | Delayed copy | | 14 | Beat signal (final) | $s_b(t) = A_b \cos\!\left[2\pi f_b t + \phi_0\right]$ | V | Sinusoidal tone for a single target | | 15 | Receiver isolation requirement | $>\Delta f / 2$ of transmitted power | dB | To avoid TX-RX leakage saturation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **No vertical pipes `|` were used inside any table cell — all delimiters are `\\` for row breaks and `&` for columns. Absolute value notation uses `\vert` or `\mid` where required.** ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) & AEB** (Automatic Emergency Braking) — Bosch, Continental, and Tesla 77 GHz FMCW radars are direct descendants of this technique. - **Radar Altimeters** in aircraft and guided missiles — sub-metre accuracy at low altitude. - **Industrial level sensors** for measuring the height of molten metal, grain silos, or chemical tanks. - **Weather observation** — short-range, high-resolution Doppler profiling of precipitation. - **Ground Penetrating Radar (GPR)** — UWB FM-CW with $\Delta f > 1 \text{ GHz}$ for sub-surface imaging. - **Anti-collision radars in robotics and drones** — ultra-light, low-power solid-state design. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Beat Frequency for Linear Sawtooth FM We start with a transmitted carrier whose instantaneous frequency varies linearly: $$f_t(t) = f_0 + \dot{f}\,t, \qquad \dot{f} = \frac{\Delta f}{T_m}$$ The **instantaneous phase** is the time integral of the instantaneous angular frequency $\omega_t(t) = 2\pi f_t(t)$: $$\phi_t(t) = 2\pi \int_0^{t} f_t(\tau)\,d\tau = 2\pi \int_0^{t} \!\left(f_0 + \dot{f}\,\tau\right) d\tau$$ Evaluating the integral: $$\phi_t(t) = 2\pi\!\left[f_0 t + \frac{\dot{f}\,t^2}{2}\right]$$ So the transmitted RF waveform is: $$s_t(t) = A_t \cos\!\left[2\pi f_0 t + \pi \dot{f}\,t^2\right]$$ For a target at range $R$, the round-trip delay is: $$\tau = \frac{2R}{c}$$ The received echo, attenuated and delayed, is: $$s_r(t) = A_r \cos\!\left[2\pi f_0 (t-\tau) + \pi \dot{f}\,(t-\tau)^2\right]$$ Both $s_t$ and $s_r$ are fed to a **balanced mixer**, which multiplies the two signals. Using the trigonometric identity $2\cos A \cos B = \cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)$ and keeping only the **low-frequency (difference) term** as the beat: $$s_b(t) = \frac{A_t A_r}{2} \cos\!\left[2\pi f_0 \tau + 2\pi \dot{f}\,t\,\tau - \pi\dot{f}\tau^2\right]$$ **Sub-step — Simplification of the phase term:** The expression $2\pi f_0 \tau$ is a **constant phase** (drop it for frequency analysis). The term $\pi\dot{f}\tau^2$ is **negligible** because $\tau \ll T_m$, so $\dot{f}\tau^2 = \dot{f}\tau \cdot \tau = f_b \tau \ll 1 \text{ Hz·s}$ (a constant residual phase). The dominant time-varying term is $2\pi \dot{f}\,t\,\tau$, whose **frequency** (by inspection of the $\cos$ argument) is: $$f_b = \dot{f}\,\tau = \frac{\Delta f}{T_m} \cdot \frac{2R}{c}$$ $$\boxed{\,f_b = \frac{2R\,\Delta f}{c\,T_m}\,} \qquad \text{(Beat Frequency — Sawtooth Modulation)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Each algebraic transition above is reversible and physically motivated**: > - $2\cos A \cos B \rightarrow \cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)$ — standard trig identity. > - High-frequency sum term filtered by IF bandwidth — engineering idealisation. > - Residual phases treated as constants because their time derivative is zero or negligible — first-order approximation, valid for all practical FMCW designs. ## 3.2 Derivation of Range Resolution $\Delta R$ Range resolution is the minimum separation $\delta R$ between two targets that produces **distinguishable beat frequencies**. Two targets at ranges $R_1$ and $R_2$ produce beat frequencies $f_{b1}$ and $f_{b2}$.
