The difference is: $$\Delta f_b = f_{b2} - f_{b1} = \frac{2\,\Delta f}{c\,T_m}(R_2 - R_1) = \frac{2\,\Delta f}{c\,T_m}\,\delta R$$ For two tones to be resolved by an FFT of duration $T_m$, their frequency separation must satisfy: $$\Delta f_b \ge \frac{1}{T_m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2\,\Delta f}{c\,T_m}\,\delta R \ge \frac{1}{T_m}$$ Cancelling $T_m$ and rearranging: $$\delta R \ge \frac{c}{2\,\Delta f}$$ $$\boxed{\,\Delta R = \frac{c}{2\,\Delta f}\,} \qquad \text{(Range Resolution)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Notice the elegance:** range resolution depends **only** on the total frequency deviation $\Delta f$, **not** on the modulation period or the carrier frequency. This is fundamentally different from pulsed radar, where $\Delta R = c\,\tau_p / 2$ depends on the (usually very short) pulse width. ## 3.3 Derivation of Range and Velocity from Triangular Modulation A **triangular modulating waveform** has period $T_m$ and a linear rise from $f_0$ to $f_0 + \Delta f$ during the first half-period ($0 \le t \le T_m/2$), and a linear fall from $f_0 + \Delta f$ to $f_0$ during the second half ($T_m/2 \le t \le T_m$). For a moving target, the **received signal is delayed and Doppler-shifted**. The instantaneous received frequency is: $$f_r(t) = f_0\!\left(1 - \frac{2v}{c}\right) + \dot{f}\,(t - \tau)$$ where the factor $(1 - 2v/c)$ arises from the Doppler shift $f_D = -2v f_0/c$ (negative for an approaching target if up-ramp convention is used). **During the up-ramp** ($\dot{f} > 0$): $$f_{b,\text{up}} = f_t - f_r = \frac{2R\,\Delta f}{c\,T_m} - \frac{2v\,f_0}{c}$$ **During the down-ramp** ($\dot{f} < 0$, magnitude $|\dot{f}| = \Delta f/T_m$): $$f_{b,\text{down}} = |\dot{f}|\,\tau + \frac{2v\,f_0}{c} = \frac{2R\,\Delta f}{c\,T_m} + \frac{2v\,f_0}{c}$$ **Solving the simultaneous pair:** $$\text{Add: } f_{b,\text{up}} + f_{b,\text{down}} = \frac{4R\,\Delta f}{c\,T_m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = \frac{c\,T_m}{4\,\Delta f}\left(f_{b,\text{up}} + f_{b,\text{down}}\right)$$ $$\text{Subtract: } f_{b,\text{down}} - f_{b,\text{up}} = \frac{4v\,f_0}{c} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = \frac{c}{4\,f_0}\left(f_{b,\text{down}} - f_{b,\text{up}}\right)$$ $$\boxed{\,R = \frac{c\,T_m\,(f_{b,\text{up}} + f_{b,\text{down}})}{4\,\Delta f}\,} \qquad \boxed{\,v = \frac{c\,(f_{b,\text{down}} - f_{b,\text{up}})}{4\,f_0}\,}$$ ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Model Problem) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** An FM-CW radar uses a linear sawtooth modulation. Given $f_0 = 10 \text{ GHz}$, $\Delta f = 200 \text{ MHz}$, $T_m = 1 \text{ ms}$. Calculate (a) the beat frequency for a target at $R = 150 \text{ m}$, and (b) the range resolution. **Solution:** (a) Substitute into the beat frequency formula: $$f_b = \frac{2R\,\Delta f}{c\,T_m} = \frac{2 \times 150 \times 200 \times 10^{6}}{3 \times 10^{8} \times 1 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$f_b = \frac{6 \times 10^{10}}{3 \times 10^{5}} = 2 \times 10^{5} \text{ Hz} = 200 \text{ kHz}$$ (b) Range resolution: $$\Delta R = \frac{c}{2\,\Delta f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 200 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{4 \times 10^{8}} = 0.75 \text{ m}$$ **Result:** A target at 150 m produces a beat tone at 200 kHz, and the system can resolve two targets separated by at least 0.75 m. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Single-Antenna FM-CW Radar The figure below shows the **complete signal flow** of a typical FM-CW radar. Because TX and RX operate simultaneously, a **directional coupler / circulator** provides the necessary 20–30 dB of isolation, and a single antenna is shared between transmit and receive paths. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Modulation Generator
Sawtooth or Triangular Wave] --> B[VCO Voltage Controlled Oscillator
FM Carrier f0 plus delta f] B --> C[Power Amplifier PA] C --> D[Directional Coupler or Circulator] D --> E[Antenna TX and RX shared] E --> F[Target at range R] F --> E D --> G[Mixer Local Oscillator Port] E --> H[LNA Low Noise Amplifier] H --> G G --> I[IF Amplifier centred at f b] I --> J[Bandpass Filter to limit noise] J --> K[Envelope or FFT Processor] K --> L[Display Range or Range plus Velocity] M[Coupling Tap from PA output] --> G ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture of the Beat-Frequency Processing The following diagram traces **how a single beat tone is generated, filtered, digitised, and converted to a range value**, and highlights where range and velocity are decoupled for triangular modulation. ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: VCO sweeps from f0 to f0 plus delta f linearly] S2[Step 2: Echo from target at range R arrives delayed by tau = 2R over c] S3[Step 3: Mixer multiplies TX and RX, output contains sum and difference] S4[Step 4: IF filter selects the difference term f b dot f tau] S5{Modulation Type} S6[Step 5a: For sawtooth, one f b per target, range = c T f b over 2 delta f] S7[Step 5b: For triangular, two beat frequencies f b up and f b down] S8[Step 6a: Sum the two to obtain range R] S9[Step 6b: Subtract the two to obtain velocity v] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 S5 -->|Sawtooth| S6 S5 -->|Triangular| S7 --> S8 S5 -->|Triangular| S7 --> S9 ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Functional Flow — Sawtooth vs Triangular Modulation ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SAW [Sawtooth Modulation] S1[Single f b per target] --> S2[Range R only] --> S3[Velocity v not measurable] end subgraph TRI [Triangular Modulation] T1[Up ramp f b up] --> T3[Sum operation] T2[Down ramp f b down] --> T3 T1 --> T4[Difference operation] T2 --> T4 T3 --> T5[Range R and Velocity v simultaneously] T4 --> T5 end ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]` **Compare pulsed radar and FM-CW radar in terms of (i) transmitter power, (ii) range measurement technique, and (iii) range resolution formula.** **Model Answer (3 marks — full):** - **(i) Transmitter power:** Pulsed radar requires **very high peak power** (kW–MW) to achieve long range with a short duty cycle. FM-CW radar uses **low continuous power** (mW–W) because the energy is integrated over a long observation window. - **(ii) Range measurement technique:** Pulsed radar measures range by timing the echo return — $R = c\tau/2$. FM-CW radar measures range from the **beat frequency** — $R = cT_m f_b / (2\Delta f)$. - **(iii) Range resolution:** Pulsed radar $\Delta R = c\tau_p/2$ (limited by pulse width). FM-CW radar $\Delta R = c/(2\Delta f)$ (limited by frequency deviation). **1 mark each sub-part.** --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]` **Why is triangular modulation preferred over sawtooth modulation in FM-CW radars used for automotive applications?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. Sawtooth modulation yields only the **range beat frequency** — radial velocity appears as a small perturbation and cannot be isolated unambiguously. **(1 mark)** 2. Triangular modulation provides **two beat frequencies** (up-ramp and down-ramp). The range and Doppler components add in one and subtract in the other. **(1 mark)** 3. Hence triangular modulation enables **simultaneous, unambiguous measurement of both range AND radial velocity** of vehicles — essential for adaptive cruise control and collision warning. **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Do NOT mix up** the beat frequency formula with the Doppler frequency formula. They look similar but use **different physical parameters** — beat frequency uses $\Delta f$ and $T_m$, while Doppler uses $f_0$ and $v$. > - **For triangular modulation**, always show **both** $f_{b,\text{up}}$ and $f_{b,\text{down}}$ equations explicitly. Students who write only one lose 2–3 marks. > - **Units:** Always include units in the final answer. A number without units is awarded **zero** for the result step in strict valuation. > - **Algebraic slips:** Range resolution is $c/(2\Delta f)$, NOT $c/\Delta f$. The factor of 2 is a common error. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of an FM-CW radar using **linear sawtooth modulation**. Derive the expression for the beat frequency in terms of range $R$ and the radar parameters. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** An FM-CW radar operating at a centre frequency of $f_0 = 9.4 \text{ GHz}$ uses triangular frequency modulation with a deviation $\Delta f = 150 \text{ MHz}$ and a modulation period $T_m = 2 \text{ ms}$. For a target at range $R = 300 \text{ m}$ moving with a radial velocity $v = 30 \text{ m/s}$ (approaching), calculate the up-ramp and down-ramp beat frequencies. **\[7 marks\]** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]** **Block diagram (2 marks):** Draw (or describe) the standard FM-CW radar block diagram: Modulating waveform generator → VCO → Power amplifier → Directional coupler → Antenna → Target → Antenna (same) → LNA → Mixer (with LO tap from PA) → IF amplifier → Beat frequency detector/FFT. **Derivation (5 marks):** Instantaneous transmitted frequency: $$f_t(t) = f_0 + \dot{f}\,t, \quad \dot{f} = \frac{\Delta f}{T_m}$$ Round-trip time delay: $$\tau = \frac{2R}{c}$$ Instantaneous received frequency: $$f_r(t) = f_t(t - \tau) = f_0 + \dot{f}(t - \tau)$$ Beat frequency (mixer difference output): $$f_b = f_t(t) - f_r(t) = \dot{f}\,\tau$$ Substituting: $$\boxed{\,f_b = \frac{2R\,\Delta f}{c\,T_m}\,} \quad \text{(final expression — 1 mark for boxed result)}$$ **Physical interpretation (1 mark):** The beat frequency is directly proportional to target range, allowing an FFT to be calibrated as a range scale. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]** **Step 1 — Compute the frequency-sweep rate:** $$\dot{f} = \frac{\Delta f}{T_m} = \frac{150 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ Hz/s}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the range beat component:** $$f_{\text{range}} = \dot{f}\,\tau = \dot{f} \cdot \frac{2R}{c} = 7.5 \times 10^{10} \times \frac{2 \times 300}{3 \times 10^8}$$ $$f_{\text{range}} = 7.5 \times 10^{10} \times 2 \times 10^{-6} = 1.5 \times 10^{5} \text{ Hz} = 150 \text{ kHz}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the Doppler shift:** $$f_D = \frac{2v f_0}{c} = \frac{2 \times 30 \times 9.4 \times 10^{9}}{3 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{5.64 \times 10^{11}}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 1.88 \times 10^{3} \text{ Hz} = 1.88 \text{ kHz}$$ **Step 4 — Apply triangular-modulation beat equations:** For an **approaching** target, the up-ramp shows a **reduced** beat and the down-ramp shows an **increased** beat: $$f_{b,\text{up}} = f_{\text{range}} - f_D = 150 \text{ kHz} - 1.88 \text{ kHz} = 148.12 \text{ kHz}$$ $$f_{b,\text{down}} = f_{\text{range}} + f_D = 150 \text{ kHz} + 1.88 \text{ kHz} = 151.88 \text{ kHz}$$ **Final Answer:** - $f_{b,\text{up}} \approx 148.12 \text{ kHz}$ **(1 mark)** - $f_{b,\text{down}} \approx 151.88 \text{ kHz}$ **(1 mark)** - Intermediate working $\dot{f}$, $\tau$, $f_D$ each earns marks as shown. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]` **(a)** Explain the principle of operation of a **triangularly modulated FM-CW radar**. Derive the expressions for simultaneous measurement of range $R$ and radial velocity $v$ of a moving target. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** An FM-CW radar uses a sawtooth modulation with $\Delta f = 250 \text{ MHz}$ and $T_m = 4 \text{ ms}$. (i) Calculate the maximum unambiguous range. (ii) If two targets are separated by 1.5 m, will the radar resolve them? Comment on the result. Take $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$. **\[7 marks\]** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]** **Principle (2 marks):** A triangular wave modulates the VCO such that the carrier frequency linearly rises from $f_0$ to $f_0 + \Delta f$ during the first half of $T_m$ and linearly falls from $f_0 + \Delta f$ to $f_0$ during the second half. For a moving target, the round-trip delay $\tau$ AND the Doppler shift $f_D$ together produce two distinct beat frequencies during the up-ramp and down-ramp. **Derivation of up-ramp beat (2 marks):** $$f_{b,\text{up}} = \frac{2R\,\Delta f}{c\,T_m} - \frac{2v\,f_0}{c}$$ **Derivation of down-ramp beat (2 marks):** $$f_{b,\text{down}} = \frac{2R\,\Delta f}{c\,T_m} + \frac{2v\,f_0}{c}$$ **Solving for R and v (1 mark):** $$R = \frac{c\,T_m}{4\,\Delta f}\left(f_{b,\text{up}} + f_{b,\text{down}}\right), \quad v = \frac{c}{4f_0}\left(f_{b,\text{down}} - f_{b,\text{up}}\right)$$ --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]** **(i) Maximum unambiguous range** — for sawtooth modulation, the maximum is set by the maximum beat frequency that the IF/FFT can handle. If we assume the system processes beat frequencies up to the Nyquist limit, $f_{b,\max} \approx 1/T_m$: $$R_{\max} = \frac{c\,T_m^2\,f_{b,\max}}{2\,\Delta f\,T_m} = \frac{c\,T_m}{2\,\Delta f} \cdot \frac{1}{1} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 4 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 250 \times 10^6}$$ Wait — using the simpler unambiguous-range form tied to the **modulation period** itself (so that the return arrives before the next ramp starts): $$R_{\max} = \frac{c\,T_m}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 4 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 6 \times 10^5 \text{ m} = 600 \text{ km}$$ **Note:** The other textbook form $R_{\max} = c/(2\Delta f_m)$ depends on the maximum beat frequency the system can resolve. For our parameters, if $f_{b,\max} = 1/T_m = 250 \text{ Hz}$: $$R_{\max} = \frac{c\,f_{b,\max}}{2\,\Delta f/T_m} = \frac{3\times 10^8 \times 250}{2 \times 250 \times 10^6 / 4 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{10}}{1.25 \times 10^{11}} = 0.6 \text{ m}$$ This is incorrect dimensionally; the conventional $R_{\max} = cT_m/2 = 600$ km is the correct interpretation for a single target case. **(2 marks for the correct $R_{\max}$ calculation and explanation.)** **(ii) Range resolution check:** $$\Delta R = \frac{c}{2\,\Delta f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 250 \times 10^6} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{5 \times 10^8} = 0.6 \text{ m}$$ **Comparison:** Two targets separated by 1.5 m > $\Delta R = 0.6$ m ⇒ **YES, the radar will resolve them clearly.** **(2 marks for the numerical comparison and conclusion.)** **Comment (1 mark):** The 1.5 m separation is **2.5 times** the resolution cell width, ensuring robust detection with a comfortable FFT bin margin. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — FM-CW Radar** - ✅ **FM-CW** = **F**requency **M**odulated **C**ontinuous **W**ave. Carrier is frequency-modulated by a periodic (sawtooth / triangular / sine) waveform. **100% duty cycle.** - ✅ **Beat frequency** $f_b$ is the difference between the instantaneous transmitted and received frequencies — generated by the mixer. - ✅ **Master equation (sawtooth):** $f_b = \dfrac{2R\,\Delta f}{c\,T_m}$. **Range** = $\dfrac{c\,T_m\,f_b}{2\,\Delta f}$. - ✅ **Range resolution:** $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2\,\Delta f}$ — depends **only on frequency deviation**. - ✅ **Triangular modulation** decouples range and velocity: - $R = \dfrac{c\,T_m}{4\,\Delta f}\bigl(f_{b,\text{up}} + f_{b,\text{down}}\bigr)$ - $v = \dfrac{c}{4\,f_0}\bigl(f_{b,\text{down}} - f_{b,\text{up}}\bigr)$ - ✅ **Doppler shift:** $f_D = \dfrac{2v\,f_0}{c}$ — independent of $\Delta f$ and $T_m$. - ✅ **IF bandwidth requirement:** $B_{\text{IF}} \ge \dfrac{2R_{\max}\,\Delta f}{c\,T_m}$. - ✅ **Advantages:** low power, high short-range accuracy, solid-state compatibility, no range ambiguity for single ramp, simultaneous R/v with triangular. - ✅ **Disadvantages:** requires excellent TX/RX isolation, leakage from circulator can mask nearby targets, narrow instantaneous bandwidth limits long-range performance. - ✅ **Applications:** automotive ACC/AEB (77 GHz), radar altimeters, industrial tank level gauges, ground-penetrating radar, drone obstacle avoidance. - ✅ **Common pitfall:** confusing beat frequency with Doppler — they are **different quantities** governed by **different formulas**. Beat uses $\Delta f$ and $T_m$; Doppler uses $f_0$ and $v$. - ✅ **KTU marker keywords** to memorise for 2-mark sub-questions: "beat frequency", "linear FM modulation", "triangular modulation", "TX/RX isolation", "range resolution", "Doppler shift".

topics.title: Range and Doppler Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Range and Doppler Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Range Measurement** in radar refers to the determination of the distance between the radar and a target by measuring the round-trip time delay (τ) of an electromagnetic wave reflected from the target. **Doppler Measurement** refers to the determination of the radial velocity of a target by measuring the frequency shift between the transmitted and received signal caused by the relative motion between the radar and the target.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle (KTU High-Yield):**
> The fundamental parameters of a target — **Range (R)** and **Radial Velocity (v_r)** — are extracted from the received echo by exploiting two distinct physical effects:
> 1. **Time Delay** (used for Range): $R = \dfrac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$
> 2. **Frequency Shift** (used for Velocity): $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$

where **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** (speed of light) and **λ** is the operating wavelength.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Echo Clapping Experiment**
>
> Imagine standing in a large empty hall and shouting *"Hello!"* toward a far wall. If the wall is **100 m away**, the echo returns after roughly **0.6 seconds** ($100 \times 2 / 330 \approx 0.6$ s, since sound travels at ~330 m/s). By measuring the delay between your shout and the echo, you estimate the distance to the wall.
>
> A **radar does the same thing**, but with **electromagnetic waves traveling at the speed of light (3 × 10⁸ m/s)** — that is why radar can measure distances of hundreds of kilometers in milliseconds.
>
> Now, suppose a **train is moving toward you** while blowing its whistle. Even though the train engineer blows the whistle at a **fixed pitch**, you hear it at a **higher pitch** as the train approaches and a **lower pitch** as it recedes. This is the **Doppler effect**. A radar measures the tiny Doppler frequency shift in the returned microwave echo to compute the **target's radial velocity**.

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of light | $c$ | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** | Conversion between time delay and range |
| Operating frequency (typical) | $f_0$ | **10 GHz** (X-band) | Defines wavelength $\lambda$ |
| Wavelength (X-band) | $\lambda$ | **0.03 m** | Doppler frequency computation |
| Pulse duration (typical) | $\tau_p$ | **1 µs** | Defines minimum range |
| PRF (typical) | $f_r$ | **1 kHz** | Defines max unambiguous range |
| Antenna rotation (typical) | $\omega$ | **6 rpm** | Defines time on target |

---

### 1.4 Taxonomy of Radar Measurement Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Classification — Must Memorize:**
>
> 1. **Pulsed Radar** — Measures **range via time delay**; range-only or range + Doppler (pulsed Doppler).
> 2. **CW (Continuous Wave) Radar** — Measures **Doppler shift** only (no range info, since there is no timing reference).
> 3. **FMCW (Frequency Modulated CW) Radar** — Measures **both range and Doppler** by sweeping the carrier frequency.
> 4. **MTI / Pulsed Doppler Radar** — Measures **Doppler** using a coherent pulsed waveform (coherence = stable phase reference).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Pulsed Radar Range Measurement

A pulsed radar transmits a narrow burst of RF energy of duration $\tau_p$ (pulse width) and waits for the echo. The receiver is gated OFF during transmission to avoid saturation.

**Step 1 — Transmission:** At $t = 0$, the transmitter emits an RF pulse.

**Step 2 — Propagation to Target:** The pulse travels at the speed of light $c$ and strikes a target at distance $R$.

**Step 3 — Reflection:** The target re-radiates a small portion of the incident energy back toward the radar.

**Step 4 — Echo Reception:** The echo returns after a round-trip time:
$$\tau = \frac{2R}{c}$$

**Step 5 — Range Extraction:** The receiver measures $\tau$, and the range is computed as:
$$R = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for the factor of 2:** The electromagnetic wave travels the distance $R$ **twice** — once to the target and once back. Hence the round-trip time is $2R/c$, and we divide by 2 to get a one-way range.

---

### 2.2 Maximum Unambiguous Range ($R_{max}$)

The radar transmits pulses at a Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF) $f_r$. The received echo from a target must arrive **before** the next pulse is transmitted; otherwise, the radar cannot tell whether the echo belongs to the current pulse or a previous one — this is called **range ambiguity**.

The maximum unambiguous round-trip time equals the Pulse Repetition Interval (PRI), $T_r = 1/f_r$:

$$R_{unambiguous} = \frac{c \cdot T_r}{2} = \frac{c}{2 f_r}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Trade-off (KTU Favourite Question):**
> - **Low PRF** → large $R_{unambiguous}$ → good for long-range surveillance
> - **High PRF** → small $R_{unambiguous}$ → but **good for Doppler measurement** (no Doppler ambiguity)
> - This is the **fundamental dilemma** of pulsed radar design.

---

### 2.3 Minimum Range ($R_{min}$)

The receiver cannot detect a target whose echo returns while the transmitter is still active. Therefore:
$$R_{min} = \frac{c \cdot \tau_p}{2}$$

where $\tau_p$ is the **pulse width**.

---

### 2.4 Range Resolution ($\Delta R$)

Two targets can be resolved in range only if their echoes do not overlap. The minimum separable range difference is:
$$\Delta R = \frac{c}{2B}$$

where $B$ is the **bandwidth** of the transmitted pulse. For a simple rectangular pulse, $B \approx 1/\tau_p$, so:
$$\Delta R = \frac{c \cdot \tau_p}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off:** Shorter pulses → better range resolution, but **lower average transmitted power**, hence weaker echo. This trade-off is solved by **pulse compression** (linear FM chirp, phase coding).

---

### 2.5 Doppler Effect — Mathematical Origin

When a target moves with radial velocity $v_r$ (positive = approaching), the round-trip geometry causes two Doppler shifts in series: once on the way to the target, and once on the return. Each shift is $f_d = v_r/\lambda$, so the total shift is:
$$f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **factor of 2** is critical for KTU problems. Students often forget it.

---

### 2.6 Blind Speeds

In a coherent pulsed radar, a target moving with velocity $v$ produces a phase change of $2\pi f_d \cdot T_r$ between successive pulses. When this phase shift is an integer multiple of $2\pi$, the target appears **stationary** to the MTI filter — this is a **blind speed**:
$$v_{blind} = \frac{n \cdot \lambda}{2 T_r} = \frac{n \cdot \lambda \cdot f_r}{2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering solution:** Use **staggered PRF** (alternating between two PRFs) to push the first blind speed out of the practical velocity range.

---

### 2.7 CW Radar (Continuous Wave) — Doppler Only

A pure CW radar transmits a continuous sinusoid. It cannot measure range (no time reference), but it can measure Doppler shift via a mixer that produces $f_d$ at the baseband (after the carrier is cancelled). It is used in:
- Police speed guns
- Missile proximity fuzes
- Traffic monitoring

---

### 2.8 FMCW Radar — Range + Doppler

The transmitted frequency is linearly swept with slope $\mu$ (Hz/s). The echo is delayed by $\tau = 2R/c$. The receiver mixes a sample of the transmitted signal with the echo to obtain an **intermediate frequency (IF) beat**:
$$f_b = \mu \cdot \tau = \frac{2 R \mu}{c}$$

Range is extracted from the beat frequency. If the target is also moving with $v_r$, an additional Doppler term appears, which can be separated by using **up-chirp** and **down-chirp** segments.

---

### 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these formulas appear in 90% of KTU 2024 range/Doppler problems.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Range from time delay | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$ | m | $\tau$ is round-trip delay |
| 2 | Doppler frequency | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \dfrac{2 v_r f_0}{c}$ | Hz | Factor of 2 — round trip |
| 3 | Max unambiguous range | $R_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 f_r}$ | m | $f_r$ = PRF in Hz |
| 4 | Min detectable range | $R_{min} = \dfrac{c \cdot \tau_p}{2}$ | m | $\tau_p$ = pulse width |
| 5 | Range resolution | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2B}$ | m | $B$ = signal bandwidth |
| 6 | Blind speed | $v_{blind} = \dfrac{n \cdot \lambda \cdot f_r}{2}$ | m/s | $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ |
| 7 | Doppler ambiguity | $v_{max} = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot f_r}{2}$ | m/s | Maximum unambiguous Doppler |
| 8 | FMCW beat frequency | $f_b = \dfrac{2 R \mu}{c}$ | Hz | $\mu$ = chirp slope in Hz/s |
| 9 | Pulse compression gain | $G_{pc} = B \cdot \tau_p$ | dimensionless | Time-bandwidth product |
| 10 | Duty cycle | $d = f_r \cdot \tau_p$ | dimensionless | $\le 1$ |

---

### 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where this knowledge is applied (Industry Context):**
> - **Air Traffic Control (ATC)** radars: En-route radar (long range, low PRF) and terminal approach radar (short range, higher PRF).
> - **Weather radar (Doppler weather radar)**: Measures precipitation radial velocity to detect tornadoes and wind shear.
> - **Automotive radar** (77 GHz FMCW): Adaptive cruise control, blind-spot detection — relies on FMCW for simultaneous range and velocity.
> - **Military phased-array radars** (e.g., AN/TPY-2): Use pulsed Doppler with staggered PRF to track ballistic missiles.
> - **Police LIDAR / RADAR speed guns**: CW Doppler at 24 GHz / 35 GHz.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Range from Round-Trip Delay

**Given:** Electromagnetic wave traveling at $c$ strikes a target at one-way range $R$ and reflects back to the radar.

**Find:** Expression for $R$ in terms of measured round-trip delay $\tau$.

**Step 1:** Write the one-way propagation time from radar to target.

The wave covers the distance $R$ at the speed $c$:

$$t_{one-way} = \frac{R}{c}$$

**Step 2:** The echo travels the same distance back. The round-trip time is therefore **twice** the one-way time:

$$t_{round-trip} = 2 \cdot \frac{R}{c} = \frac{2R}{c}$$

**Step 3:** The radar receiver observes this round-trip delay and measures it as $\tau$:

$$\tau = \frac{2R}{c}$$

**Step 4:** Solve algebraically for $R$ (multiply both sides by $c$ and divide by 2):

$$R = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$$

**Step 5 (unit check):** $[R] = \dfrac{\text{(m/s)}}{\text{1}} \cdot \text{s} = \text{m}$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical sanity check:** For a target at $R = 100$ km, $\tau = (2 \times 100 \times 10^3) / (3 \times 10^8) = 6.67 \times 10^{-4}$ s $\approx$ **667 µs** — a comfortably measurable electronic delay.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Doppler Frequency for a Moving Target

**Given:** Radar operating at frequency $f_0$ (or wavelength $\lambda = c/f_0$) observes a target moving with radial velocity $v_r$ (positive approaching).

**Find:** The Doppler frequency shift $f_d$ observed at the receiver.

**Step 1 — Doppler on the way IN:** As the wave propagates from the radar to the moving target, the target "sees" a frequency shifted by the classical Doppler formula. The received frequency at the target is:

$$f' = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v_r}{c} \right)$$

(We take $v_r > 0$ for an approaching target — the apparent frequency is higher.)

**Step 2 — Doppler on the way OUT:** The target re-radiates at frequency $f'$, but now the target is the "source" moving toward the radar (which acts as the observer). The radar receives:

$$f_r = f' \left( \frac{c + v_r}{c} \right) = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v_r}{c} \right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Simplification (Non-Relativistic):** For $v_r \ll c$, expand using the binomial approximation $(1+x)^2 \approx 1 + 2x$ when $x \ll 1$:

$$f_r \approx f_0 \left( 1 + \frac{2 v_r}{c} \right) = f_0 + \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Doppler shift:** The Doppler frequency is the difference between received and transmitted frequencies:

$$f_d = f_r - f_0 = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$$

**Step 5 (unit check):** $[f_d] = \dfrac{\text{m/s}}{\text{m}} = \text{Hz}$ ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the factor of 2?** The target acts as a *moving receiver* on the way in AND as a *moving source* on the way out. Both effects add up, giving twice the single-path Doppler shift.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Unambiguous Range from PRF

**Given:** A pulsed radar with PRF $f_r$ (pulses per second).

**Find:** The maximum range at which a target can be detected without ambiguity.

**Step 1:** The time between consecutive transmitted pulses is the Pulse Repetition Interval (PRI):

$$T_r = \frac{1}{f_r}$$

**Step 2:** If an echo from a target arrives **after** the next pulse has been transmitted, the radar cannot tell whether the echo belongs to the most recent pulse or the previous one. The latest unambiguous echo must arrive within one PRI:

$$\tau_{max} = T_r = \frac{1}{f_r}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the basic range equation with $\tau = \tau_{max}$:

$$R_{max} = \frac{c \cdot \tau_{max}}{2} = \frac{c}{2 f_r}$$

**Step 4 (numerical example):** For a typical surveillance radar with $f_r = 1$ kHz:

$$R_{max} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^3} = 1.5 \times 10^5 \text{ m} = 150 \text{ km}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Blind Speed Derivation

**Given:** A coherent pulsed radar with PRF $f_r$ and wavelength $\lambda$. Two consecutive echoes are received from a moving target.

**Find:** The target radial velocities at which the radar cannot detect motion (blind speeds).

**Step 1:** The target moves a distance $\Delta R = v_r T_r$ between successive pulses. This corresponds to a round-trip phase change of:

$$\Delta \phi = \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot 2 \Delta R = \frac{4 \pi v_r T_r}{\lambda}$$

**Step 2:** The MTI (Moving Target Indication) filter cancels the signal from stationary clutter and detects moving targets. It works by **subtracting** successive echoes. For a stationary target, $\Delta \phi = 0$, so the echo is cancelled perfectly. For the MTI filter to also cancel a *moving* target, the phase change must also be an integer multiple of $2\pi$:

$$\Delta \phi = 2 \pi n, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the expression for $\Delta \phi$ and solve for $v_r$:

$$\frac{4 \pi v_r T_r}{\lambda} = 2 \pi n \implies v_r = \frac{n \lambda}{2 T_r} = \frac{n \lambda f_r}{2}$$

**Step 4 — These are the BLIND SPEEDS:**

$$\boxed{v_{blind}(n) = \frac{n \lambda f_r}{2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots}$$

**Step 5 (numerical example):** For a 10 GHz radar ($\lambda = 0.03$ m) with $f_r = 1$ kHz:

$$v_{blind,1} = \frac{1 \times 0.03 \times 1000}{2} = 15 \text{ m/s} \approx 54 \text{ km/h}$$

A car moving at exactly 54 km/h would appear stationary — this is the blind speed problem.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: FMCW Beat Frequency

**Given:** A linear FM chirp with frequency sweep rate $\mu = \Delta f / T_s$ (Hz/s), where $\Delta f$ is the total frequency deviation and $T_s$ is the sweep duration.

**Find:** The IF beat frequency that encodes the target range.

**Step 1:** The instantaneous frequency of the transmitted chirp is:

$$f_{tx}(t) = f_0 + \mu t, \quad 0 \le t \le T_s$$

**Step 2:** The echo arrives after a delay $\tau = 2R/c$, so the received signal at time $t$ corresponds to the transmitted signal at time $t - \tau$:

$$f_{rx}(t) = f_0 + \mu (t - \tau) = f_0 + \mu t - \mu \tau$$

**Step 3:** The mixer multiplies the transmitted and received signals and low-pass filters, producing a beat frequency equal to the difference:

$$f_b = f_{tx}(t) - f_{rx}(t) = \mu \tau = \frac{2 \mu R}{c}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for range:**

$$R = \frac{c \cdot f_b}{2 \mu}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** A target at greater range produces a larger delay $\tau$, which translates to a larger instantaneous frequency difference between the transmitted (current) and received (earlier) chirps. The beat frequency is therefore a direct linear measure of range.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Typical KTU-Style Numerical Setup

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practice Workflow (Use this matrix for every range/Doppler problem):**

1. **Identify waveform type:** Pulsed, CW, or FMCW?
2. **Extract given quantities:** $f_0$, $\lambda$, $\tau_p$, $f_r$, $\mu$, $B$.
3. **Select the appropriate formula** from the KTU cheat sheet.
4. **Substitute numerically** — keep units consistent (Hz vs kHz, m vs km).
5. **Verify by order-of-magnitude check.**
6. **Express the final answer in the units requested.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Pulsed Radar Range and Doppler Measurement

This is the canonical block diagram a KTU examiner expects on a "draw and explain the block schematic of a pulsed radar" question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Master Timer PRF Generator] --> B[Modulator]
    B --> C[Power Amplifier Transmitter]
    C --> D[ circulator duplexer ]
    D --> E[ parabolic antenna ]
    E --> F[ target at range R ]
    F --> E
    D --> G[Low Noise Amplifier LNA]
    G --> H[Mixer with Coherent Oscillator STALO]
    H --> I[IF Amplifier]
    I --> J[Matched Filter Range Gate]
    J --> K[Range Display A scan]
    H --> L[Phase Detector Coherent Detector]
    L --> M[Doppler Filter Bank MTI]
    M --> N[Doppler Display Velocity Output]
    A -.coherence reference.-> H
    A -.coherence reference.-> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - The **upper path** (red logic flow) implements **range measurement** via the matched filter and range gate.
> - The **lower path** (green logic flow) implements **Doppler measurement** via the phase detector and Doppler filter bank (MTI / pulse-Doppler processing).
> - The **dashed arrows** from the master timer carry the **coherence reference** (stable local oscillator / STALO) to both the receiver mixer and the phase detector. This coherence is what allows Doppler (phase) measurement to be possible.

---

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — FMCW Radar

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Waveform Generator Linear Chirp] --> P2[Power Amplifier]
    P2 --> P3[Duplexer Circulator]
    P3 --> P4[Antenna]
    P4 -.->|Echo Delay tau equals 2R over c| P4
    P3 --> P5[Low Noise Amplifier]
    P5 --> P6[Mixer beat product]
    P1 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Low Pass Filter Beat Frequency]
    P7 --> P8[FFT Processor Range Doppler Map]
    P8 --> P9[Display and Tracking]
```

---

### 4.3 Conceptual Map — Range and Doppler Ambiguity Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Operating Mode Selection] --> B{Low PRF}
    A --> C{High PRF}
    A --> D{Medium PRF}
    B --> B1[Long Unambiguous Range]
    B --> B2[Doppler Ambiguous]
    C --> C1[Short Unambiguous Range]
    C --> C2[Doppler Unambiguous]
    D --> D1[Both Ambiguous resolved by multiple PRFs]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A medium-PRF radar is Doppler-ambiguous AND range-ambiguous, but resolves both by using **multiple PRFs** in a sequence. This is the operating mode of most military airborne radars (e.g., APG-68, APG-81).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define the term **Doppler Effect** as applied to radar. Derive the expression for the Doppler frequency shift in terms of the target radial velocity and the radar wavelength.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Doppler Effect:** It is the apparent change in the frequency of the received echo caused by the relative radial motion between the radar and the target.
>
> For a target moving with radial velocity $v_r$ (positive when approaching the radar), the round-trip geometry produces a frequency shift given by:
>
> $$f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c}$$
>
> where $\lambda$ is the operating wavelength, $f_0$ is the transmitted frequency, and $c$ is the speed of light. The factor of 2 arises because the Doppler shift occurs **twice** — once on the path to the target and once on the return path.
>
> A positive $f_d$ indicates an **approaching** target; a negative $f_d$ indicates a **receding** target.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Differentiate between **CW radar** and **FMCW radar** in terms of measurable parameters and typical applications.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> | Feature | CW Radar | FMCW Radar |
> |---|---|---|
> | Transmitted signal | Pure sinusoid of constant frequency | Sinusoid whose frequency is linearly swept |
> | Range measurement | **Not possible** (no timing reference) | **Possible** (beat frequency encodes range) |
> | Doppler measurement | **Possible** (direct $f_d$ measurement) | **Possible** (extracted from beat during up- and down-chirps) |
> | Maximum range | Limited only by transmit power and isolation | Limited by sweep linearity and FFT resolution |
> | Typical applications | Police speed guns, proximity fuzes, short-range motion sensors | Automotive radar (77 GHz), altimeters, weather radar |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with an internal choice of **two 7-mark sub-parts (a) and (b)**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the operating principle of a **pulsed radar** for measurement of target range. Derive the expression for **maximum unambiguous range** in terms of PRF. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pulsed radar operates at 10 GHz with a PRF of 2 kHz and a pulse width of 1 µs. Calculate:
  (i) Maximum unambiguous range
  (ii) Minimum detectable range
  (iii) First blind speed
  (iv) Doppler frequency for a target moving with radial velocity 30 m/s approaching the radar. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution A(a) — Pulsed Radar Block Diagram & Max Unambiguous Range

**Step 1: Block Diagram (3 marks)**

Refer to the block diagram in **Section 4.1** above. The key functional blocks are:
- **Master Timer / PRF Generator:** Sets the pulse repetition rate.
- **Modulator + Power Amplifier:** Forms the high-power RF pulse.
- **Duplexer (Circulator / TR Switch):** Routes the transmitted pulse to the antenna and protects the receiver during transmission.
- **Antenna:** Sends/receives the RF energy.
- **LNA + Mixer + IF Amplifier:** Receiver front-end.
- **Matched Filter / Range Gate:** Slices the received echo at a specific time delay corresponding to a specific range.
- **Range Display (A-scan):** Plots received echo amplitude vs. range.

**Step 2: Operating Principle (2 marks)**

The radar transmits a short RF pulse. The pulse propagates outward, hits the target, and a small portion is reflected back. The receiver measures the round-trip delay $\tau$ between transmission and echo reception. Range is then computed using:

$$R = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$$

**Step 3: Derivation of Maximum Unambiguous Range (2 marks)**

The Pulse Repetition Interval (PRI) is $T_r = 1/f_r$. An echo from a target at range $R$ arrives after time $\tau = 2R/c$. For unambiguous measurement, this delay must be less than one PRI:

$$\frac{2R}{c} \le T_r \implies R \le \frac{c \, T_r}{2} = \frac{c}{2 f_r}$$

$$\boxed{R_{max} = \frac{c}{2 f_r}}$$

---

#### Solution A(b) — Numerical Computations

**Given:** $f_0 = 10$ GHz $= 10^{10}$ Hz, $f_r = 2$ kHz $= 2 \times 10^3$ Hz, $\tau_p = 1$ µs $= 10^{-6}$ s, $v_r = 30$ m/s (approaching).

**Wavelength first:**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10^{10}} = 0.03 \text{ m}$$

**(i) Maximum Unambiguous Range:**

$$R_{max} = \frac{c}{2 f_r} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 2 \times 10^3} = 7.5 \times 10^4 \text{ m} = 75 \text{ km}$$

**(ii) Minimum Detectable Range:**

$$R_{min} = \frac{c \cdot \tau_p}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 150 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) First Blind Speed ($n = 1$):**

$$v_{blind} = \frac{1 \cdot \lambda \cdot f_r}{2} = \frac{0.03 \times 2000}{2} = 30 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iv) Doppler Frequency for $v_r = 30$ m/s:**

$$f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 30}{0.03} = 2000 \text{ Hz} = 2 \text{ kHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Observation:** Notice that $f_d = 2$ kHz **equals** the PRF $f_r = 2$ kHz. This is **not a coincidence** — at the first blind speed, the Doppler frequency equals the PRF, so the target produces zero net phase change between pulses.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of **Doppler measurement** in a CW radar. Why is a pure CW radar unable to measure target range? How is this limitation overcome in an **FMCW radar**? Derive the FMCW beat-frequency expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An FMCW radar sweeps from 76 GHz to 77 GHz in 1 ms. Calculate:
  (i) The chirp slope $\mu$.
  (ii) The beat frequency for a target at 150 m.
  (iii) The range resolution if the sweep bandwidth is 1 GHz.
  (iv) The Doppler frequency for a target moving at 20 m/s. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution B(a) — CW vs FMCW Principle

**Step 1: CW Radar Doppler Measurement (2 marks)**

In a CW radar, a continuous sinusoid of frequency $f_0$ is transmitted. A sample of the transmitted signal is mixed with the received echo in a product detector. The mixer output is low-pass filtered to give the **Doppler audio frequency**:

$$f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$$

This audio tone is heard on a loudspeaker or its frequency is measured electronically. The CW radar is highly sensitive for slow-moving targets because the integration time is unlimited (no blind range).

**Step 2: Why Range Cannot Be Measured (1 mark)**

Since the radar transmits **continuously**, there is no time-mark or pulse edge to start a stopwatch against. The received echo is an unbroken sinusoid that the receiver cannot distinguish from the transmitted signal except by frequency. Range information is **erased**.

**Step 3: FMCW Solution and Beat Frequency Derivation (4 marks)**

In FMCW, the transmitted frequency is linearly swept as $f_{tx}(t) = f_0 + \mu t$. The echo arrives after $\tau = 2R/c$, with instantaneous frequency $f_{rx}(t) = f_0 + \mu(t - \tau)$. Mixing and low-pass filtering yield a **beat frequency**:

$$f_b = f_{tx}(t) - f_{rx}(t) = \mu \tau = \frac{2 \mu R}{c}$$

Range is recovered by measuring the beat frequency:

$$R = \frac{c \, f_b}{2 \mu}$$

---

#### Solution B(b) — FMCW Numerical Computations

**Given:** $f_0 = 76$ GHz, $f_{end} = 77$ GHz, $T_s = 1$ ms $= 10^{-3}$ s, $R = 150$ m, $\Delta f = 1$ GHz, $v_r = 20$ m/s.

**(i) Chirp slope:**

$$\mu = \frac{\Delta f_{sweep}}{T_s} = \frac{77 - 76}{10^{-3}} = 1 \times 10^{12} \text{ Hz/s} = 1 \text{ THz/s}$$

(Or using $\Delta f_{sweep} = 1$ GHz, $\mu = 10^9 / 10^{-3} = 10^{12}$ Hz/s.)

**(ii) Beat frequency for R = 150 m:**

$$f_b = \frac{2 \mu R}{c} = \frac{2 \times 10^{12} \times 150}{3 \times 10^8} = \frac{3 \times 10^{14}}{3 \times 10^8} = 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ MHz}$$

**(iii) Range resolution:**

$$\Delta R = \frac{c}{2B} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^9} = 0.15 \text{ m} = 15 \text{ cm}$$

**(iv) Doppler frequency for $v_r = 20$ m/s:**

First compute $\lambda$ at the operating frequency (use $f_0 = 76.5$ GHz as the mid-band value):

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{76.5 \times 10^9} \approx 3.92 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 20}{3.92 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 10.2 \text{ kHz}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Doppler formula** — causes the answer to be off by exactly 2×. Examiners actively look for this. (Loss: 2–3 marks)
> 2. **Confusing range resolution $\Delta R = c/2B$ with minimum range $R_{min} = c \tau_p / 2$** — these are NOT the same. The first depends on **bandwidth** (signal-processing limit), the second on **pulse width** (hardware gating limit). For a rectangular pulse, they numerically coincide, but conceptually they are different. (Loss: 2 marks)
> 3. **Mixing up $f_r$ and $\tau_p$** — students sometimes compute $R_{max}$ using $\tau_p$ instead of $1/f_r$. Always double-check: **range depends on PRF; resolution/gating depends on pulse width.**
> 4. **Using PRF in kHz without unit conversion** — if $f_r = 2$ kHz, you must write $2 \times 10^3$ Hz before substituting. (Loss: 1 mark per instance)
> 5. **Skipping units in the final answer** — KTU valuation always deducts at least **0.5–1 mark** if the final numerical answer has no unit.
> 6. **Neglecting the "factor of 2" in the range equation** — same caution as for Doppler. The round trip is $2R$, not $R$. (Loss: 2 marks)
> 7. **Writing the blind speed formula with $n$ in the denominator** — $n$ is in the **numerator** ($v_{blind} = n \lambda f_r / 2$). Easy slip when copying from the board.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this on your wall before the exam):**
>
> **Core Equations (Memorize in this exact form):**
> - $R = c \tau / 2$
> - $f_d = 2 v_r / \lambda = 2 v_r f_0 / c$
> - $R_{max} = c / (2 f_r)$
> - $R_{min} = c \tau_p / 2$
> - $\Delta R = c / (2 B)$
> - $v_{blind} = n \lambda f_r / 2$
> - $f_b^{FMCW} = 2 \mu R / c$
>
> **Key Trade-offs:**
> - **Pulse width $\tau_p$** ↔ Range resolution (smaller is better, but lower energy)
> - **PRF $f_r$** ↔ Unambiguous range (lower is better) AND Doppler ambiguity (higher is better)
> - **Bandwidth $B$** ↔ Range resolution (larger is better) AND pulse compression gain
> - **Wavelength $\lambda$** ↔ Doppler sensitivity (smaller $\lambda$ = larger $f_d$ = more sensitive velocity measurement)
>
> **Three Radar Flavours:**
> - **Pulsed radar:** Range via time delay; Doppler via phase coherence between pulses.
> - **CW radar:** Doppler only; no range.
> - **FMCW radar:** Range AND Doppler; used in 77 GHz automotive radar.
>
> **Memorable Numerical Anchors (for fast sanity check):**
> - $R_{max} = 75$ km for $f_r = 2$ kHz.
> - $R_{min} = 150$ m for $\tau_p = 1$ µs.
> - $f_d = 2$ kHz for a target at 30 m/s with a 10 GHz radar.
>
> **The "Factor of 2" Mantra:**
> - Doppler: round-trip → factor of 2.
> - Range: round-trip → factor of 2 in the denominator.
> - **Always remember: 2 goes in the Doppler numerator, 2 goes in the range denominator.**
>
> **Blind-Speed Concept (Popular KTU question):**
> - Occurs in MTI filters when target phase change per PRI is a multiple of $2\pi$.
> - Eliminated by **staggered PRF** (alternating between two or more PRFs) or by using **multiple PRFs sequentially** (medium-PRF mode).
>
> **FMCW vs Pulsed — When to use which?**
> - FMCW: short range (< 1 km), high resolution, low peak power (e.g., 77 GHz automotive radar).
> - Pulsed: long range (> 10 km), high peak power, surveillance (e.g., ATC, military).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Diagram and Characteristics (Approaching/Receding Targets)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Block Diagram and Characteristics (Approaching/Receding Targets) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition In a **Continuous Wave (CW) Doppler Radar**, the radar transmits an unmodulated sinusoidal wave of frequency $f_t$ and receives an echo from the target. If the target is in **radial motion** relative to the radar, the received echo undergoes a **Doppler frequency shift** ($f_d$) proportional to the radial velocity of the target. The block diagram of the receiver processes this echo by mixing it with a sample of the transmitted signal, extracting the **Doppler (difference) frequency** as the baseband output. The **sign of the Doppler shift** characterizes whether the target is **approaching** (positive $f_d$) or **receding** (negative $f_d$) from the radar. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)**: The Doppler effect is the foundation of CW radar and MTI (Moving Target Indication) systems. Students must master the sign convention for approaching vs. receding targets — this is a frequently tested 3-mark concept. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ambulance Siren" Imagine standing on a road as an ambulance with a siren approaches you: | Situation | Siren Pitch (Frequency) | Doppler Shift | |---|---|---| | Ambulance approaching you | **Higher pitch** (waves compressed) | $f_d > 0$ | | Ambulance passing you | Sudden **pitch drop** | $f_d$ changes sign | | Ambulance receding from you | **Lower pitch** (waves stretched) | $f_d < 0$ | A CW Doppler radar works on **exactly the same principle** with electromagnetic waves instead of sound: - **Approaching target** → reflected waves are **squeezed** → received frequency $f_r > f_t$ - **Receding target** → reflected waves are **stretched** → received frequency $f_r < f_t$ The "pitch drop" of the ambulance is, in essence, what a Doppler radar detects — but with radio waves and at the speed of light. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metrics & Constants Used in This Module:** > - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s > - Radar operating frequency: typically **1 GHz to 100 GHz** > - Doppler frequency $f_d$ for vehicular targets: typically in the **audio range (100 Hz to 30 kHz)** > - Sign convention: **Radial velocity $v_r$ is positive if target approaches the radar** ## 1.3 Key Terms Introduced - **CW Transmitter**: Generates an unmodulated, pure sinusoidal RF signal. - **Circulator / Directional Coupler**: Separates the transmitted and received signals using a single antenna. - **Mixer (Coherent Detector)**: Multiplies the received echo with a sample of the transmitted signal; outputs sum and difference frequency components. - **Doppler Amplifier (IF/AF Amplifier)**: Amplifies the difference frequency $f_d$, which lies in the audio range. - **Display / Loudspeaker**: The Doppler tone is heard as audio — its frequency $\propto$ target radial velocity, its **polarity** $\propto$ direction of motion. > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** A *simple* CW radar **cannot measure range** (because $f_t$ is unmodulated) and **cannot distinguish** between an approaching and receding target producing the *same* radial speed magnitude. To extract direction, a coherent (I/Q) receiver is required — this is a common KTU question. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step The CW Doppler radar processes echoes through the following sequence: 1. **Signal Generation** — The CW oscillator (often a klystron, Gunn oscillator, or solid-state source) generates a stable RF signal at frequency $f_t$. This is routed simultaneously to: - The transmitting antenna (via a circulator), and - The local oscillator (LO) port of the mixer. 2. **Wave Propagation** — The transmitted electromagnetic wave travels at the speed of light $c$ through free space. If a target exists within the radar's line-of-sight, it intercepts a portion of the incident energy. 3. **Reflection from Target** — The target acts as a secondary radiator. If the target has a **radial velocity component** $v_r$ (component along the radar-target line), the reflected wave undergoes a **Doppler frequency shift** $f_d$ proportional to $v_r$. 4. **Reception** — A portion of the reflected wave is captured by the radar's receiving antenna (shared with the transmitter via a circulator/duplexer). 5. **Coherent Mixing** — The received echo (at frequency $f_r = f_t \pm f_d$) is fed to the RF port of the mixer, where it is multiplied with the LO sample of the transmitted signal. The mixer output contains: - A **sum component** at $2f_t$ (high frequency, easily filtered out) - A **difference component** at $f_d$ (the Doppler frequency, in the audio range) 6. **Doppler Amplification** — The low-pass filter and AF amplifier isolate and amplify the Doppler tone $f_d$. 7. **Output Stage** — The Doppler signal is presented to the operator (loudspeaker, headphones) or processed further for velocity measurement. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step | Step | Why it matters | |---|---| | CW transmission | Simpler, no range modulation needed; power efficient | | Coherent mixing | Extracts only the *velocity* information, rejects the carrier | | Difference frequency | Audio-frequency output — easy to amplify, process, and display | | Filtering the sum | The sum term $2f_t$ carries no information about target motion | ## 2.3 Characteristics — Approaching vs. Receding Targets > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favourite:** The comparison of approaching vs. receding target characteristics is a frequently asked **7-mark** sub-question. | Characteristic | Approaching Target | Receding Target | |---|---|---| | Radial velocity $v_r$ | **Positive** ($v_r > 0$) | **Negative** ($v_r < 0$) | | Doppler shift $f_d$ | **Positive** ($f_d > 0$) | **Negative** ($f_d < 0$) | | Received frequency $f_r$ | $f_r = f_t + f_d$ (i.e., $f_r > f_t$) | $f_r = f_t + f_d$ (i.e., $f_r < f_t$) | | Wavelength of echo | **Compressed** ($\lambda_r < \lambda_t$) | **Stretched** ($\lambda_r > \lambda_t$) | | Phase of received signal | **Leads** the transmitted signal | **Lags** the transmitted signal | | Sign of mixer output | Positive audio frequency | Negative audio frequency (same magnitude) | | Wavefront spacing | Decreasing | Increasing | | Spectral content | Shifted up in frequency | Shifted down in frequency | | Practical detection | Higher pitched tone | Lower pitched tone | > [!NOTE] > **Subtle Point:** A *non-coherent* CW radar mixer cannot distinguish the sign of $f_d$ — it can only detect the *magnitude* $\vert f_d \vert$. To detect **direction**, the receiver must be **coherent (I/Q)**, retaining both in-phase and quadrature phase information. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > All KTU exam questions on this topic reduce to applying these 3-4 core equations. Memorize the **sign convention**: positive $v_r$ for approaching targets. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Transmitted wavelength | $\lambda_t = \dfrac{c}{f_t}$ | metres (m) | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | | 2 | Doppler frequency (general) | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r f_t}{c} = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda_t}$ | Hertz (Hz) | $v_r$ is radial velocity, **positive for approach** | | 3 | Received frequency (general) | $f_r = f_t + f_d$ | Hertz (Hz) | Sign of $f_d$ decides approach/recede | | 4 | Approaching target: $f_r$ | $f_r = f_t + \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda_t}$ | Hz | $f_r > f_t$ | | 5 | Receding target: $f_r$ | $f_r = f_t - \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda_t}$ | Hz | Here $v_r$ is the receding speed; $f_r < f_t$ | | 6 | Mixer output (sum term) | $f_{sum} = f_t + f_r \approx 2 f_t$ | Hz | Filtered out | | 7 | Mixer output (difference term) | $f_{diff} = \vert f_r - f_t \vert = \vert f_d \vert$ | Hz | This is the **Doppler tone** | > **Table formatting note:** All absolute values use `\vert` (LaTeX) to remain compatible with markdown tables. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Traffic Speed Guns** — Handheld police radars operate as X-band (~10.5 GHz) CW Doppler radars; they measure $f_d$ and convert directly to speed. - **Ballistic Missile Early Warning** — Continuous-wave surveillance radars track re-entry vehicles whose Doppler signature is unmistakable. - **Sports & Drones** — Baseball pitch-speed radars, drone speed enforcement, and gait analysis. - **Automotive ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control)** — Modern cars use 76-77 GHz FMCW Doppler radar for collision avoidance; the Doppler shift enables closing-rate estimation. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Doppler Frequency from First Principles **Setup:** A stationary radar transmits a sinusoidal wave of frequency $f_t$ towards a target moving with radial velocity $v_r$ relative to the radar. ### Step 1 — Define the Transmitted Signal The transmitted signal can be written as: $$ s_t(t) = A_t \cos(2 \pi f_t t) $$ This wave travels outward at the speed of light $c$ with wavelength $\lambda_t = c / f_t$. ### Step 2 — Frequency Observed at the Target The target is a moving observer. By the classical Doppler effect, a moving observer perceives a frequency: $$ f_{obs} = f_t \left( \frac{c + v_r}{c} \right) $$ where $v_r$ is the radial velocity of the observer (target) relative to the source (radar). The "+" sign is used because the target is moving **towards** the source when $v_r$ is positive (convention adopted: $v_r > 0$ for approaching). ### Step 3 — Frequency Reflected Back by the Target The target now **re-radiates** (reflects) the wave back toward the radar, but now it acts as a **moving source** with the observed frequency $f_{obs}$. The stationary radar receives: $$ f_r = f_{obs} \left( \frac{c}{c - v_r} \right) $$ The "$-v_r$" in the denominator accounts for the fact that the target is moving *toward* the radar (chasing the wavefront, so the wavefronts are emitted more frequently). ### Step 4 — Combine the Two Effects Substituting the expression for $f_{obs}$: $$ f_r = f_t \left( \frac{c + v_r}{c} \right) \left( \frac{c}{c - v_r} \right) = f_t \left( \frac{c + v_r}{c - v_r} \right) $$ This is the **relativistic-accurate two-way (radar) Doppler equation**. ### Step 5 — Apply the Non-Relativistic Approximation For radar targets (aircraft, vehicles, ships), $v_r \ll c$ (typically $v_r < 10^3$ m/s). Using the binomial approximation $\dfrac{1}{1 - x} \approx 1 + x$ for small $x$: $$ \frac{c + v_r}{c - v_r} = \frac{1 + v_r/c}{1 - v_r/c} \approx (1 + v_r/c)(1 + v_r/c) \approx 1 + \frac{2 v_r}{c} $$ Therefore: $$ f_r \approx f_t \left( 1 + \frac{2 v_r}{c} \right) = f_t + \frac{2 v_r f_t}{c} $$ ### Step 6 — Extract the Doppler Shift The **Doppler frequency** is defined as the difference between received and transmitted frequencies: $$ f_d = f_r - f_t = \frac{2 v_r f_t}{c} $$ Substituting $\lambda_t = c / f_t$: $$ \boxed{\,f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda_t}\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sign Convention (MANDATORY for KTU):** > - $v_r > 0$ (target **approaching**) $\Rightarrow$ $f_d > 0$ (positive Doppler) > - $v_r < 0$ (target **receding**) $\Rightarrow$ $f_d < 0$ (negative Doppler) ### Step 7 — Mixer Output (Detector Output) The mixer multiplies $s_t(t)$ with the received signal $s_r(t) = A_r \cos(2\pi f_r t + \phi)$: $$ s_t(t) \cdot s_r(t) = A_t A_r \cos(2\pi f_t t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_r t + \phi) $$ Using the trigonometric identity $\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$: $$ s_{mixer}(t) = \frac{A_t A_r}{2} \left[ \cos\bigl(2\pi (f_t - f_r) t - \phi\bigr) + \cos\bigl(2\pi (f_t + f_r) t + \phi\bigr) \right] $$ The two components are: - **Difference frequency:** $f_t - f_r = -f_d$ (the Doppler tone, audio range) - **Sum frequency:** $f_t + f_r \approx 2 f_t$ (very high frequency, filtered out) After low-pass filtering, the output is the Doppler tone: $$ s_{out}(t) = \frac{A_t A_r}{2} \cos(2\pi f_d t - \phi) $$ > [!NOTE] > The output is an audio-frequency sinusoid whose **frequency** is the magnitude of the Doppler shift and whose **phase** $\phi$ encodes the direction of motion (approach vs. recede). --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** A CW Doppler radar operates at 10 GHz. A car is approaching the radar at 108 km/hr. Calculate (a) the Doppler frequency, (b) the received frequency, and (c) the wavelength of the echo. Repeat for a receding target at the same speed. ### Given: - $f_t = 10$ GHz $= 10 \times 10^9$ Hz - $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s - $v_r = 108$ km/hr (positive for approach, negative for receding) ### Step (i) — Convert velocity $$ v_r = 108 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 30 \text{ m/s} $$ ### Step (ii) — Transmitted wavelength $$ \lambda_t = \frac{c}{f_t} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10 \times 10^9} = 0.03 \text{ m} = 3 \text{ cm} $$ ### Step (iii) — Doppler frequency (Approaching, $v_r = +30$ m/s) $$ f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda_t} = \frac{2 \times 30}{0.03} = 2000 \text{ Hz} = 2 \text{ kHz} $$ Since $f_d > 0$, the target is **approaching** ✓ ### Step (iv) — Received frequency (Approaching) $$ f_r = f_t + f_d = 10 \times 10^9 + 2000 = 10{,}000{,}002{,}000 \text{ Hz} $$ The received frequency is **2 kHz higher** than transmitted. ### Step (v) — Wavelength of the Echo (Approaching) $$ \lambda_r = \frac{c}{f_r} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10{,}000{,}002{,}000} \approx 0.029999994 \text{ m} $$ The wavelength is **shorter** (compressed) compared to $\lambda_t = 0.03$ m. ### Step (vi) — Receding Case ($v_r = -30$ m/s) $$ f_d = \frac{2 \times (-30)}{0.03} = -2000 \text{ Hz} $$ $$ f_r = 10 \times 10^9 - 2000 = 9{,}999{,}998{,}000 \text{ Hz} $$ $$ \lambda_r \approx 0.030000006 \text{ m} $$ The wavelength is **longer** (stretched) — the wave is "pulled" by the receding target. > [!TIP] > **Pattern Recognition:** Approaching and receding produce Doppler tones of **identical magnitude** (2 kHz) but **opposite sign** in $f_d$. The mixer output is the same audio pitch; only phase/direction is different. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Motion Direction | Parameter | Approaching Target ($v_r > 0$) | Stationary Target ($v_r = 0$) | Receding Target ($v_r < 0$) | |---|---|---|---| | $v_r$ (radial velocity) | $+v_0$ | $0$ | $-v_0$ | | $f_d$ | $+\dfrac{2 v_0}{\lambda_t}$ | $0$ | $-\dfrac{2 v_0}{\lambda_t}$ | | $f_r$ | $f_t + f_d$ | $f_t$ | $f_t - \vert f_d \vert$ | | $\lambda_r$ | Slightly less than $\lambda_t$ | Equal to $\lambda_t$ | Slightly greater than $\lambda_t$ | | Mixer output frequency | $\vert f_d \vert$ (audio) | $0$ (DC, no detection) | $\vert f_d \vert$ (audio) | | Mixer output phase | $0$ (in-phase) | — | $\pi$ (reversed) | | Detection by loudspeaker | Audible tone | Silence | Audible tone (same pitch, opposite phase) | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of CW Doppler Radar The following Mermaid block diagram represents the canonical architecture of a single-antenna CW Doppler radar. The arrows indicate the **direction of signal flow**, and labels identify the function of each stage. ```mermaid graph LR A["CW OSCILLATOR
Transmitter
Frequency ft"] --> B["POWER
DIVIDER"] B --> C["CIRCULATOR
Tx-Rx
Separator"] C --> D["TRANSMITTING
ANTENNA
Radiates ft"] D --> E["TARGET
Moving with
Radial Velocity vr"] E --> F["RECEIVING
ANTENNA
Captures Echo"] F --> G["MIXER
Coherent
Detector"] B --> G G --> H["IF AMPLIFIER
Audio Frequency
Amplifier fd"] H --> I["DOPPLER
DISPLAY
Loudspeaker
or Counter"] ``` ### Explanation of the Block Diagram | Block | Function | |---|---| | **CW Oscillator** | Generates stable RF carrier at $f_t$ (e.g., Gunn / Klystron source) | | **Power Divider** | Splits the CW signal: one path to the antenna, one to the mixer LO port | | **Circulator** | Routes transmitted signal to antenna and received echo to mixer (3-port ferrite device) | | **Transmitting Antenna** | Radiates $f_t$ toward the target | | **Target** | Reflects signal with a Doppler shift proportional to its radial velocity $v_r$ | | **Receiving Antenna** | Captures the Doppler-shifted echo at $f_r = f_t + f_d$ | | **Mixer** | Multiplies $f_t$ (LO) with $f_r$ (RF) → produces $f_d$ and $2f_t$ components | | **IF Amplifier** | Low-pass filter + audio amplifier; amplifies the Doppler tone $f_d$ | | **Display** | Loudspeaker, headphones, frequency counter, or spectrum analyzer | > [!NOTE] > **Two-antenna variant:** Many CW radars use **separate transmit and receive antennas** instead of a circulator. This eliminates the duplexer loss and improves isolation — important for sensitive Doppler detection. ## 4.2 Signal Flow Sequence (Numbered) ```mermaid graph TD S1["Step 1: CW Oscillator generates ft"] --> S2["Step 2: Power Divider splits signal"] S2 --> S3["Step 3: ft sent to antenna via circulator"] S2 --> S4["Step 3b: ft routed to mixer as LO"] S3 --> S5["Step 4: Wave propagates to target"] S5 --> S6["Step 5: Target reflects Doppler-shifted echo fr = ft + fd"] S6 --> S7["Step 6: Antenna receives echo"] S7 --> S8["Step 7: Echo sent to mixer RF port"] S8 --> S9["Step 8: Mixer multiplies ft and fr"] S4 --> S9 S9 --> S10["Step 9: Output components: 2ft (sum) and fd (difference)"] S10 --> S11["Step 10: Low-pass filter removes 2ft"] S11 --> S12["Step 11: Doppler tone fd amplified and displayed"] ``` ## 4.3 Frequency Spectrum — Approaching vs. Receding ```mermaid graph LR A["Transmitted
Spectrum
Peak at ft"] --> B["Mixer Output
Spectrum"] C["Received Echo
Peak at ft + fd
(approaching)"] --> B D["Received Echo
Peak at ft - fd
(receding)"] --> B B --> E["Low-Pass
Filter"] E --> F["Doppler Tone
at fd audio"] ``` ### Visual Summary of Approach vs. Recede ```mermaid graph LR P1["Target Moving
TOWARDS Radar
vr greater than 0"] --> Q1["fd is POSITIVE
fr is GREATER than ft
Wavelength COMPRESSED
Phase LEADS"] P2["Target Moving
AWAY FROM Radar
vr less than 0"] --> Q2["fd is NEGATIVE
fr is LESS than ft
Wavelength STRETCHED
Phase LAGS"] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** Define the Doppler effect as applied to radar. Derive the expression for the Doppler frequency shift. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]` **Model Answer:** The **Doppler effect** in radar is the apparent change in frequency of the received echo caused by the relative motion between the radar and the target. If the target has a radial velocity $v_r$ (component along the radar-target line), the received frequency $f_r$ differs from the transmitted frequency $f_t$ by the Doppler shift $f_d$. **Derivation (brief):** Treating the target as a moving observer and a moving source successively, and applying the non-relativistic limit ($v_r \ll c$): $$ f_d = f_r - f_t = \frac{2 v_r f_t}{c} = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda_t} $$ **Sign convention:** $v_r$ is positive for an approaching target (giving positive $f_d$) and negative for a receding target (giving negative $f_d$). --- ### **Q2.** Distinguish between the characteristics of approaching and receding targets in a CW Doppler radar. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]` **Model Answer:** | Characteristic | Approaching Target | Receding Target | |---|---|---| | Radial velocity $v_r$ | Positive | Negative | | Doppler frequency $f_d$ | Positive | Negative | | Received frequency $f_r$ | Higher than $f_t$ | Lower than $f_t$ | | Echo wavelength | Compressed | Stretched | | Phase of received signal | Leads transmitted | Lags transmitted | | Mixer output | Audio tone (positive) | Audio tone (same magnitude, opposite phase) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write that the Doppler frequency for a receding target is *zero* or *positive*. The correct sign is **negative** for receding motion. Marks are deducted for sign-convention errors. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]` **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a CW Doppler radar. Discuss the role of each block. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** A CW Doppler radar is the simplest form of radar that can detect the **radial velocity** of a target. Its block diagram (refer to Section 4) consists of: 1. **CW Oscillator (Transmitter):** Generates a stable, unmodulated RF signal at frequency $f_t$. The output is split by a power divider. 2. **Power Divider:** Sends part of the transmitted signal to the antenna and the other part to the mixer's local oscillator (LO) port — necessary for coherent detection. 3. **Circulator (or Duplexer):** A non-reciprocal 3-port ferrite device that routes the transmitted signal to the antenna and the received echo to the mixer, allowing a single antenna for both transmit and receive. 4. **Antenna:** Radiates $f_t$ and captures the reflected echo at $f_r = f_t + f_d$. 5. **Mixer (Coherent Detector):** Multiplies the received signal with the LO sample. The output contains the sum component ($2f_t$, filtered) and the difference component ($f_d$, the Doppler tone). 6. **Doppler Amplifier:** A low-noise audio-frequency amplifier that boosts $f_d$ for display. 7. **Display:** A loudspeaker/headphone (the Doppler tone is heard as an audio "beep") or a frequency counter (for precise velocity measurement). The system cannot determine **range** (no time delay can be measured because the signal is unmodulated), but it can determine the **radial velocity** of the target from $v_r = f_d \lambda_t / 2$. **(b)** A CW radar operates at 9 GHz. Calculate the Doppler frequency returned by an aircraft moving with a radial velocity of (i) 300 m/s approaching the radar, and (ii) 200 m/s receding from the radar. Also find the corresponding received frequencies. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $f_t = 9$ GHz $= 9 \times 10^9$ Hz - $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s - (i) $v_r = +300$ m/s (approaching), (ii) $v_r = -200$ m/s (receding) **Step 1 — Wavelength of transmitted signal** $$ \lambda_t = \frac{c}{f_t} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{9 \times 10^9} = 0.0333 \text{ m} = 3.33 \text{ cm} $$ **Case (i): Approaching Target, $v_r = +300$ m/s** $$ f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda_t} = \frac{2 \times 300}{0.0333} = 18{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 18 \text{ kHz} $$ $$ f_r = f_t + f_d = 9 \times 10^9 + 18{,}000 = 9{,}000{,}018{,}000 \text{ Hz} $$ **Case (ii): Receding Target, $v_r = -200$ m/s** $$ f_d = \frac{2 \times (-200)}{0.0333} = -12{,}000 \text{ Hz} = -12 \text{ kHz} $$ $$ f_r = f_t + f_d = 9 \times 10^9 - 12{,}000 = 8{,}999{,}988{,}000 \text{ Hz} $$ **Result Summary:** | Case | $v_r$ (m/s) | $f_d$ (kHz) | $f_r$ (Hz) | |---|---|---|---| | Approaching | +300 | +18 | 9,000,018,000 | | Receding | -200 | -12 | 8,999,988,000 | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** In part (b), many students forget to attach the **sign** to the receding Doppler frequency. Always carry $v_r$ with its sign — never use the magnitude without the sign convention. --- ### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4, Alternative Choice]` **(a)** Explain the characteristics of approaching and receding targets in a CW Doppler radar with supporting equations and a block diagram. Discuss how the received frequency, Doppler shift, and phase of the received signal differ in the two cases. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Equations Governing the System:** $$ f_d = \frac{2 v_r f_t}{c} = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda_t} $$ $$ f_r = f_t + f_d $$ **Approaching Target ($v_r > 0$):** - The radar-target distance **decreases** with time. - Each successive wavecrest is reflected from a *closer* position, compressing the wavelength. - $f_d > 0$, $f_r > f_t$, $\lambda_r < \lambda_t$. - The received echo's **phase leads** the transmitted signal. - Mixer output is a positive-frequency audio tone. **Receding Target ($v_r < 0$):** - The radar-target distance **increases** with time. - Each successive wavecrest is reflected from a *farther* position, stretching the wavelength. - $f_d < 0$, $f_r < f_t$, $\lambda_r > \lambda_t$. - The received echo's **phase lags** the transmitted signal. - Mixer output is a negative-frequency audio tone (same magnitude as the approaching case, opposite sign). **Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4 of these notes. The signal flow is: CW Oscillator → Power Divider → (Antenna via Circulator + Mixer LO) → Mixer → Doppler Amplifier → Display **Distinguishing Approaching from Receding:** A *simple* mixer output gives only $\vert f_d \vert$. To detect the sign (direction), the radar must use a **coherent I/Q receiver** with two mixers fed by LO signals 90° apart in phase. **(b)** A police radar gun operates at 10.525 GHz. It detects a car whose Doppler frequency is measured as 1.5 kHz. Determine: (i) whether the car is approaching or receding, (ii) the radial velocity of the car, and (iii) the received frequency. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $f_t = 10.525$ GHz $= 10.525 \times 10^9$ Hz - $f_d = 1.5$ kHz $= 1500$ Hz (positive value measured) **Step 1 — Wavelength** $$ \lambda_t = \frac{c}{f_t} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10.525 \times 10^9} = 0.02850 \text{ m} = 2.85 \text{ cm} $$ **Step 2 — Determine Direction** Since the police radar gun *typically* detects only $\vert f_d \vert$, the **magnitude** is positive. However, in KTU convention, a **positive $f_d$** is associated with an **approaching** target (as the gun is usually pointed at oncoming traffic). > [!NOTE] > **Assumption:** The gun measures a positive $f_d$ → target is **approaching**. **Step 3 — Radial Velocity** $$ v_r = \frac{f_d \lambda_t}{2} = \frac{1500 \times 0.02850}{2} = \frac{42.75}{2} = 21.375 \text{ m/s} $$ Converting to km/hr: $$ v_r = 21.375 \times \frac{3600}{1000} = 76.95 \text{ km/hr} \approx 77 \text{ km/hr} $$ **Step 4 — Received Frequency** $$ f_r = f_t + f_d = 10{,}525{,}000{,}000 + 1500 = 10{,}525{,}001{,}500 \text{ Hz} $$ **Final Answer:** | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Direction | **Approaching** (since $f_d > 0$) | | Radial velocity | **21.375 m/s** (≈ 77 km/hr) | | Received frequency | **10,525,001,500 Hz** | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** In part (b), students often forget to convert m/s to km/hr (which is the more intuitive answer for a "speed gun" question). Always state the final answer in **both** units if the question does not specify. Also, do not write $f_r = f_t - f_d$ for an approaching target — the sign convention demands $f_r = f_t + f_d$ with $f_d$ carrying its own sign. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision:** Memorize these 8 points before stepping into the exam hall. - ✅ **Doppler Equation (Master Formula):** $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda_t} = \dfrac{2 v_r f_t}{c}$ — the **single most important equation** for this module. - ✅ **Sign Convention:** $v_r > 0$ → **approaching**; $v_r < 0$ → **receding**. The sign of $f_d$ follows the sign of $v_r$. - ✅ **Mixer Output:** The mixer produces sum ($2f_t$, filtered) and difference ($f_d$, audible) frequencies. The Doppler tone is in the **audio range** (Hz to kHz). - ✅ **CW Radar Limitation:** Cannot measure **range** (unmodulated signal); can only measure **radial velocity** (magnitude of $f_d$). - ✅ **Received Frequency:** $f_r = f_t + f_d$ — for approach, $f_r > f_t$; for recede, $f_r < f_t$. - ✅ **Direction Detection:** Requires a **coherent I/Q receiver** (two mixers, 90° phase offset on LO). - ✅ **Block Diagram Order:** CW Oscillator → Power Divider → Circulator → Antenna → (target) → Antenna → Mixer → Doppler Amplifier → Display. - ✅ **Wavelength Behaviour:** Approaching target → **compressed** $\lambda_r$; Receding target → **stretched** $\lambda_r$. > **End of Module 4 Notes — Block Diagram and Characteristics (Approaching/Receding Targets).** ---

topics.title: FM-CW Altimeter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** FM-CW Altimeter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

An **FM-CW Altimeter** is a short-range, continuous-wave radar system that determines the altitude of an aircraft (or the height of a platform above a reflecting surface) by transmitting a **Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave (FM-CW)** signal and measuring the **beat frequency** generated by coherently mixing the transmitted wave with the time-delayed echo returned from the terrain.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the FM-CW altimeter belongs to the family of **coherent CW radars** in which the carrier frequency is deliberately swept (modulated) over a known bandwidth $\Delta f$ in a controlled periodic pattern — typically a **sawtooth** or **triangular waveform** — so that range information is encoded onto the frequency domain as a low-frequency beat tone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEECT832 – Module 4):**
> The FM-CW altimeter is a direct application of the FM-CW radar principle studied earlier in the module. It is treated as a classic illustrative case because altitude measurement is the simplest practical deployment (single, stationary target: the ground).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you stand on a cliff and shout your name into a canyon. The echo returns a few seconds later. You know how fast sound travels, so the time gap tells you the depth of the canyon.

Now imagine you replace the shout with a **whistle whose pitch you continuously slide up and down** (a "siren whistle"). The echo comes back with the *same* sliding pitch, but **shifted in time**. At any given instant, the pitch you are *currently* blowing and the pitch coming back from the canyon are **slightly different** — and the *size* of that pitch difference depends on **how far away the canyon wall is** (because a longer delay means a bigger pitch mismatch).

The FM-CW altimeter does exactly this with **radio waves** and a precise electronic pitch (frequency) sweep:

- The aircraft's transmitter **sweeps** its transmit frequency up and down linearly.
- The **echo from the ground** returns this sweep with a tiny time delay.
- A **mixer** subtracts the two and produces a low-pitched tone (the **beat frequency**).
- The **pitch of that beat tone** is directly proportional to **altitude**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use a pulsed radar like primary radar?**
> For very short ranges (a few metres to a few kilometres) — exactly the regime of an aircraft altimeter — pulsed radars would need nanosecond-scale pulses and high-speed sampling. FM-CW systems achieve the same range resolution cheaply by exploiting *frequency* rather than *time* gating.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics & Constants

| Quantity | Standard Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of light | $c$ | $\mathbf{3 \times 10^8}$ | m/s |
| Modulation period (sawtooth/triangular) | $T_m$ | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$ | s |
| Frequency deviation (sweep bandwidth) | $\Delta f$ | $10^6$ to $10^8$ | Hz |
| Centre (carrier) frequency | $f_0$ | $\mathbf{4.3\ \text{GHz}}$ (typical radar altimeter band) | Hz |
| Beat frequency | $f_b$ | $10^3$ to $10^5$ | Hz |
| Maximum unambiguous altitude | $R_{max}$ | a few km | m |

> [!TIP]
> **Typical operational RF band:** Civil aviation radar altimeters operate around **4.2 – 4.4 GHz** (C-band). The choice is a balance between antenna size, ground-reflection characteristics and atmospheric attenuation.

---

## 1.4 Block-Level Architecture (Quick Look)

The complete FM-CW altimeter chain consists of:

1. **Waveform Generator** — produces a low-frequency linear ramp (sawtooth or triangular) at frequency $f_m = 1/T_m$.
2. **Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO) / FM Source** — translates the ramp into a frequency-swept RF carrier centred at $f_0$ with deviation $\Delta f$.
3. **Power Divider (Directional Coupler)** — splits the swept RF into two paths: (a) the transmit path and (b) the local-oscillator (LO) reference path.
4. **Transmit Antenna** — radiates the swept CW towards the ground.
5. **Receive Antenna (or shared duplexed antenna)** — collects the time-delayed echo.
6. **RF Mixer** — multiplies the echo with the *current* LO reference, producing the beat tone.
