These four items cover approximately 80% of marks in any KTU 2024 Module 4 question on this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Principle ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **RADAR — RAdio Detection And Ranging** > Radar is an electromagnetic (EM) system designed for the **detection, localisation (range, angular position, and radial velocity), and imaging** of reflecting objects (targets) by transmitting an EM signal and receiving the **echo reflected** from the target. The basic principle of radar relies on measuring the **time delay** between the transmitted and received signals to determine the **range (R)** of the target, using the relation **R = cT/2**, where *c* is the velocity of light (≈ **3 × 10⁸ m/s**) and *T* is the round-trip propagation time. **Key Operational Modes of a Radar:** | Mode | Function | Measured Quantity | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Pulse Radar** | Transmits short RF pulses | Range (via time delay) | | **CW Radar** | Continuous wave transmission | Velocity (via Doppler shift) | | **MTI Radar** | Moving Target Indication | Clutter suppression | | **Tracking Radar** | Locks onto a single target | Continuous angular/range data | | **SAR** | Synthetic Aperture Radar | High-resolution 2-D imaging | ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bat and the Echo" > [!NOTE] > **Think of a bat flying in a dark cave.** The bat cannot see the walls, but it screams out a short ultrasonic chirp. The sound waves travel outwards, hit the wall, and bounce back as an **echo**. The bat's ears receive the echo. The **longer it takes** for the echo to return, the **farther** the wall is. This is exactly how radar works, except instead of sound waves, radar uses **high-frequency radio waves (or microwaves)** travelling at the speed of light. > [!NOTE] > **The "Half-Time" Rule:** Because the radio wave must travel **to the target AND back**, the measured time delay is **twice** the one-way travel time. Hence the **division by 2** in the range equation. ### 1.3 Block Diagram of a Basic Radar System (Conceptual) A radar system consists of the following sub-systems: 1. **Transmitter** — Generates high-power RF pulses (via Magnetron, Klystron, or Solid-State Power Amplifier). 2. **Duplexer (TR Switch)** — Allows a single antenna to be shared between transmit and receive modes, protecting the sensitive receiver during transmission. 3. **Antenna** — Highly directional parabolic reflector; focuses the beam. 4. **Receiver** — Detects the weak reflected echo (typically a Superheterodyne receiver). 5. **Indicator / Display** — Shows target range and bearing (PPI Scope, A-Scope, or modern digital display). 6. **Signal Processor** — Extracts target information (Doppler, CFAR detection). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Pulse Generation:** A **synthesiser** generates a stable RF carrier (e.g., at **10 GHz** X-band). A **modulator** forms short pulses (typical pulse width τ = 0.1–10 µs). 2. **Transmission:** The high-power pulse is routed via the **Duplexer** to the antenna and radiated into space. The Peak Power **P_t** can range from kilowatts to Megawatts. 3. **Propagation:** The EM wave spreads spherically. Power density at range **R** falls off as **1/(4πR²)**. 4. **Target Illumination:** The target intercepts a portion of the wave and **re-radiates** (scatters) it. The amount of energy intercepted and re-radiated is characterised by the **Radar Cross-Section (RCS, σ)** measured in **square metres (m²)**. 5. **Echo Return:** The scattered wave propagates back to the radar. Power density at the radar again falls off as **1/(4πR²)**. 6. **Reception:** The antenna captures the echo. The effective aperture **A_e** is related to the antenna gain by **A_e = Gλ² / (4π)**. 7. **Detection & Display:** The received signal (often in **noise**, requiring integration) is processed and displayed. ### 2.2 Why "Why" and "How" Each Step Matters - **Why pulses?** — To separate the transmitted and received signals in time, allowing measurement of delay. - **Why high power?** — Because the signal undergoes a **two-way 1/R⁴** spreading loss — it is the most lossy communication channel known. - **Why a Duplexer?** — Without it, the **kW-level transmitted pulse** would destroy the sensitive (nW-level) receiver. - **Why a directive antenna?** — Antenna gain **G** appears **twice** in the equation (transmit and receive), so a high-G antenna is critical for long-range detection. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master every row of this table — they are direct KTU university-exam favourites.** | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Physical Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Range from Time Delay** | $R = \dfrac{cT}{2}$ | metres (m) | Target distance from round-trip delay | | 2 | **Velocity from Doppler** | $v = \dfrac{f_d \lambda}{2}$ | m/s | Radial velocity of target | | 3 | **Power Density at Range R** | $S = \dfrac{P_t G_t}{4 \pi R^{2}}$ | W/m² | Spherical spreading | | 4 | **Power Intercepted by Target** | $P_{inc} = S \cdot \sigma$ | W | Power captured by target RCS | | 5 | **Echo Power Density at Radar** | $S_r = \dfrac{P_{inc}}{4 \pi R^{2}}$ | W/m² | Back-scattered density | | 6 | **Effective Antenna Aperture** | $A_e = \dfrac{G_r \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}$ | m² | Equivalent capture area | | 7 | **Received Echo Power** | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} R^{4}}$ | Watts (W) | **Radar Range Equation** | | 8 | **Maximum Range (Monostatic)** | $R_{max} = \left[ \dfrac{P_t G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} S_{min}} \right]^{1/4}$ | metres (m) | Detection range limit | | 9 | **Minimum Detectable Signal** | $S_{min} = k T_{sys} B_{n} \, (S/N)_{min}$ | Watts (W) | Receiver sensitivity floor | | 10 | **Pulse Repetition Frequency** | $PRF = 1/T$ | Hz | Number of pulses per second | > [!NOTE] > **Note on Notation:** $\vert x \vert$ denotes absolute value, while subscripts like $G_t$ mean "Gain of transmitting antenna" and $G_r$ means "Gain of receiving antenna". In a **monostatic** radar (single antenna for Tx/Rx), $G_t = G_r = G$. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aviation:** Air Traffic Control (ATC) radars at airports (e.g., **ASR, SSR**). - **Defence:** Weapon guidance (fire-control radars), missile seekers, early-warning systems (e.g., **AWACS**). - **Remote Sensing:** Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) on satellites for terrain mapping (cloud-penetrating). - **Automotive:** Anti-collision radars (77 GHz) in modern ADAS-equipped cars. - **Meteorology:** Doppler weather radar for storm and precipitation tracking. - **Maritime:** Marine navigation radar on ships. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Simple Radar Range Equation The **radar range equation** is the single most important equation in the entire course. Every step below is KTU-examinable. **Step 1 — Transmit Power Density at the Target (Range R)** The transmitting antenna radiates with gain $G_t$ and total power $P_t$. Because EM waves spread as spheres, the power density (power per unit area) at distance $R$ is the total power divided by the surface area of the sphere $4\pi R^2$. $$ S_t \;=\; \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4 \pi R^{2}} $$ **Step 2 — Power Intercepted by the Target** The target intercepts the wave over an area known as the **Radar Cross-Section (RCS, $\sigma$)**, measured in m². The intercepted power is simply the incident power density multiplied by $\sigma$. $$ P_{int} \;=\; S_t \cdot \sigma \;=\; \frac{P_t \, G_t \, \sigma}{4 \pi R^{2}} $$ **Step 3 — Power Density of the Echo at the Radar Antenna** The target re-radiates (scatters) this intercepted power isotropically. The echo wave again spreads as a sphere. So the returning power density at the radar antenna is: $$ S_r \;=\; \frac{P_{int}}{4 \pi R^{2}} \;=\; \frac{P_t \, G_t \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{2} R^{4}} $$ **Step 4 — Power Captured by the Receiving Antenna** The receiving antenna has an *effective aperture* $A_e$ (not the same as physical area). The fundamental antenna relation is $A_e = G_r \lambda^{2} / (4\pi)$. Multiplying the returning power density by this aperture gives the received echo power: $$ P_r \;=\; S_r \cdot A_e \;=\; \frac{P_t \, G_t \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{2} R^{4}} \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^{2}}{4\pi} $$ **Step 5 — Final Radar Range Equation** Combining all terms: $$ P_r \;=\; \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^{2} \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \, R^{4}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **Simple Radar Range Equation** — universally called the "1/R⁴ equation" because received power falls off as the **fourth power** of range. This is why radar is a power-hungry system. **Step 6 — Maximum Detectable Range ($R_{max}$)** Detection is possible only if $P_r \geq S_{min}$ (minimum detectable signal). Setting $P_r = S_{min}$ and solving for $R$: $$ R_{max} \;=\; \left[\, \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^{2} \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \, S_{min}} \,\right]^{1/4} $$ For a **monostatic** radar (single shared antenna), $G_t = G_r = G$: $$ R_{max} \;=\; \left[\, \frac{P_t \, G^{2} \, \lambda^{2} \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \, S_{min}} \,\right]^{1/4} $$ ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A monostatic radar operating at **10 GHz** has $P_t = 1$ MW, antenna gain $G = 40$ dB, RCS $\sigma = 1$ m², and minimum detectable signal $S_{min} = 10^{-13}$ W. Calculate the maximum range. (Take $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K where needed.) **Solution:** Given: - $f = 10$ GHz → $\lambda = c/f = 3 \times 10^{8} / 10^{10} = 0.03$ m - $P_t = 10^{6}$ W - $G$ (linear) $= 10^{40/10} = 10^{4} = 10000$ - $\sigma = 1$ m² - $S_{min} = 10^{-13}$ W Substitute into the $R_{max}$ equation: $$ R_{max} \;=\; \left[\, \frac{10^{6} \times (10^{4})^{2} \times (0.03)^{2} \times 1}{(4\pi)^{3} \times 10^{-13}} \,\right]^{1/4} $$ Numerator: $$ 10^{6} \times 10^{8} \times 9 \times 10^{-4} \times 1 \;=\; 9 \times 10^{10} $$ Denominator: $$ (4\pi)^{3} \approx (12.566)^{3} \approx 1984 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 1984 \times 10^{-13} \approx 1.984 \times 10^{-10} $$ Therefore: $$ R_{max} \;=\; \left[\, \frac{9 \times 10^{10}}{1.984 \times 10^{-10}} \,\right]^{1/4} \;=\; \left[\, 4.535 \times 10^{20} \,\right]^{1/4} $$ $$ R_{max} \;\approx\; (4.535 \times 10^{20})^{0.25} \;\approx\; 4.60 \times 10^{5} \text{ m} \;\approx\; \textbf{460 km} $$ > [!TIP] > This number (≈ 460 km) is consistent with real-world long-range surveillance radars, confirming the validity of the radar range equation. ### 3.3 Doppler Principle for CW Radar If the target is moving with radial velocity $v$, the received frequency is shifted by the **Doppler frequency**: $$ f_d \;=\; \frac{2 v}{\lambda} \;=\; \frac{2 v f_0}{c} $$ This is the principle behind **police speed guns**, **weather radar**, and **MTI radars**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Pulse Radar System (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Waveform Generator
PRF and Pulse Width] --> B[Transmitter
High Power RF Source] B --> C{Duplexer
TR Switch} C -->|Transmit path| D[Antenna
Parabolic Reflector] D -->|Radiated EM Wave| E[Target
RCS sigma] E -->|Reflected Echo| D D --> C C -->|Receive path| F[Receiver
LNA Mixer IF] F --> G[Signal Processor
MTI and CFAR] G --> H[Display Unit
PPI or A-Scope] G --> I[Threshold Detector
Compare with Smin] ``` ### 4.2 Signal Flow in the Radar Equation (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD Pt["Transmit Power Pt"] --> Mult1["Multiply by Gt over 4 pi R squared"] Mult1 --> S1["Power Density at Target"] S1 --> Mult2["Multiply by RCS sigma"] Mult2 --> Pint["Power Intercepted"] Pint --> Mult3["Divide by 4 pi R squared"] Mult3 --> S2["Echo Power Density at Radar"] S2 --> Mult4["Multiply by Ae Gr lambda squared over 4 pi"] Mult4 --> Pr["Received Power Pr"] Pr --> Comp{"Pr greater than Smin"} Comp -->|Yes| Det["Target Detected"] Comp -->|No| Miss["Target Missed"] ``` ### 4.3 Classification of Radars (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TB Radar[RADAR System] Radar --> P[Primary Radar
Uses Reflected Signal] Radar --> S[Secondary Radar
Uses Transponder Reply] P --> Pulse[Pulse Radar] P --> CW[Continuous Wave CW Radar] Pulse --> MTIP[MTI Radar] Pulse --> TrackP[Tracking Radar] Pulse --> SAR[Synthetic Aperture SAR] CW --> Dop[CW Doppler] CW --> FMCW[FMCW Radar] ``` > [!NOTE] > The block diagrams above depict the **functional architecture and signal flow** of radar systems. In the KTU examination, students are expected to draw the **basic pulse radar block diagram** and label the function of each block. The Duplexer is **mandatory** for a single-antenna monostatic radar. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define Radar. List the basic functions of a radar system.** > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer:** > **Radar** stands for **RAdio Detection And Ranging**. It is an electromagnetic system that uses **reflected radio waves** to detect the presence of a target, determine its **range**, **angular position**, and sometimes its **radial velocity**. > > **Basic functions of radar:** > 1. **Detection** of the target (presence/absence). > 2. **Ranging** (distance measurement) via time delay $\rightarrow$ $R = cT/2$. > 3. **Angular position** (azimuth and elevation) via directional antenna. > 4. **Velocity measurement** via Doppler frequency shift. > 5. **Imaging and tracking** in advanced systems. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is the role of a Duplexer in a radar system?** > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer:** > A **Duplexer** (also called a **TR switch — Transmit/Receive switch**) is a critical sub-system in a monostatic radar that allows a **single antenna to be shared** between the high-power transmitter and the sensitive low-noise receiver. > > **Functions:** > 1. During transmission → routes the high-power pulse **from the transmitter to the antenna**, while **isolating the receiver** to prevent damage. > 2. During reception → routes the weak reflected echo **from the antenna to the receiver**, while isolating the transmitter. > 3. The duplexer uses **gaseous discharge tubes (TR cells)** or **ferrite circulators** with PIN-diode switching. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Derive the simple radar range equation, starting from the transmitted power and ending with the expression for received echo power. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A monostatic radar operates at **5 GHz** with peak transmitted power $P_t = 2$ MW, antenna gain $G = 35$ dB, target RCS $\sigma = 2$ m², and minimum detectable signal $S_{min} = 10^{-12}$ W. Determine the maximum detectable range. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation:** (See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full step-by-step derivation.) 1. Power density at target: $S_t = \dfrac{P_t G_t}{4 \pi R^{2}}$ 2. Power intercepted: $P_{int} = S_t \cdot \sigma$ 3. Echo power density at radar: $S_r = \dfrac{P_{int}}{4 \pi R^{2}}$ 4. Effective aperture: $A_e = \dfrac{G_r \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}$ 5. Final equation: $$ P_r \;=\; \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^{2} \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \, R^{4}} $$ **Assumptions:** - Point target at range R. - Free-space propagation (no atmospheric attenuation). - Antenna is lossless. - Target is in the far-field (Fraunhofer region) of the antenna. - RCS $\sigma$ is constant over the aspect angle. **(b) Numerical Solution:** Given: - $f = 5$ GHz → $\lambda = 3 \times 10^{8} / 5 \times 10^{9} = 0.06$ m - $P_t = 2 \times 10^{6}$ W - $G$ (linear) $= 10^{35/10} = 10^{3.5} \approx 3162.3$ - $\sigma = 2$ m² - $S_{min} = 10^{-12}$ W Substitute into: $$ R_{max} \;=\; \left[\, \frac{P_t G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} S_{min}} \,\right]^{1/4} $$ Numerator: $$ 2 \times 10^{6} \times (3162.3)^{2} \times (0.06)^{2} \times 2 $$ Compute stepwise: $$ (3162.3)^{2} \approx 1 \times 10^{7} $$ $$ \text{Numerator} \;\approx\; 2 \times 10^{6} \times 10^{7} \times 3.6 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \;=\; 1.44 \times 10^{11} $$ Denominator: $$ (4\pi)^{3} \times 10^{-12} \;\approx\; 1984 \times 10^{-12} \;=\; 1.984 \times 10^{-9} $$ Ratio: $$ \frac{1.44 \times 10^{11}}{1.984 \times 10^{-9}} \;\approx\; 7.26 \times 10^{19} $$ Therefore: $$ R_{max} \;=\; (7.26 \times 10^{19})^{0.25} \;\approx\; \textbf{2.92 \times 10^{5} m \;\approx\; 292 km} $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **pulse radar system**. Briefly describe the function of each block. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the principle of operation of a **CW Doppler radar** for velocity measurement. Derive the expression for the Doppler frequency. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Pulse Radar Block Diagram:** (See Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram of these notes — translate to a hand-drawn version in the exam.) **Functions of each block:** 1. **Waveform Generator / Timing Unit** → Produces a synchronising trigger pulse of frequency PRF. 2. **Transmitter** → Generates high-power RF pulse (via Magnetron/Klystron/SSPA). 3. **Duplexer (TR Switch)** → Routes the Tx pulse to the antenna, isolates Rx during Tx. 4. **Antenna** → Radiates the Tx pulse directionally and receives the echo. 5. **Receiver (Superheterodyne)** → Amplifies and down-converts the weak echo. 6. **Signal Processor** → Extracts target parameters; performs MTI, CFAR, integration. 7. **Display (PPI/A-Scope)** → Visually presents target range and bearing. 8. **Threshold Detector** → Compares $P_r$ with $S_{min}$ and declares "Detection". **(b) CW Doppler Radar:** In a **Continuous Wave (CW) radar**, the transmitter emits an **unmodulated** continuous RF signal of frequency $f_0$. When this signal is reflected from a moving target with radial velocity $v$: - On the way **to** the target, the wave is shifted by $f_0 (v/c)$. - On the way **back** from the target, a second shift of $f_0 (v/c)$ occurs. The total round-trip Doppler shift is: $$ f_d \;=\; f_0 \cdot \frac{2v}{c} \;=\; \frac{2 v}{\lambda} $$ The receiver mixes the transmitted and received signals. The mixer output is a low-frequency beat note at $f_d$, from which velocity is computed as: $$ v \;=\; \frac{f_d \lambda}{2} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Limitation of pure CW radar:** It **cannot measure range** because there is no time reference. To measure both range and velocity, **FMCW (Frequency Modulated CW)** radar is used. --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in "Principle of Radar" questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the duplexer** in the block diagram — examiners will deduct **2 marks** if it is missing. > 2. **Wrong factor of 2 in the range equation** — Students often write $R = cT$ instead of $R = cT/2$ (the wave travels **out and back**). > 3. **dB-to-linear conversion mistakes** — When $G$ is given in dB, you MUST convert: $G_{\text{linear}} = 10^{G_{dB}/10}$. A common error is using $10^{G/20}$ (that is for **voltage**, not **power**). > 4. **Mixing up the formulas** for RCS power density vs. antenna effective aperture — always re-derive from first principles if confused. > 5. **Not stating assumptions** in derivations — KTU examiners award **1 mark** for explicit assumptions (free-space, point target, isotropic re-radiation, etc.). > 6. **Units mismatch** — $\lambda$ in metres, $R$ in metres, $P_t$ in watts. The final answer for $R$ will be in metres (or km). --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Principle of Radar (Module 4)** > - **RADAR** = RAdio Detection And Ranging. > - **Range equation:** $R = cT / 2$ (factor of 2 because of round trip). > - **Received power** falls off as **$1/R^4$** — the famous 4th-power law. > - **Radar range equation:** $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} R^{4}}$. > - **Maximum range** is the **4th root** of the numerator/denominator ratio. > - **Duplexer** is essential to share one antenna between Tx (kW power) and Rx (nW power). > - **Radar Cross-Section (RCS, $\sigma$)** in **m²** — depends on target material, shape, aspect, and frequency. > - **Doppler frequency** for radial velocity: $f_d = 2v/\lambda$ — used in CW, MTI, and police radars. > - **Antenna gain** $G$ appears **twice** in the monostatic radar equation — a key reason radar uses highly directive parabolic dishes. > - **Minimum detectable signal** $S_{min} = k T_{sys} B_n (S/N)_{min}$ — set by thermal noise. > - **Pulse radar measures range** (time delay); **CW radar measures velocity** (Doppler); **FMCW measures both**. > - **Always convert dB gains to linear** using $G_{lin} = 10^{G_{dB}/10}$ before substituting in the equation. > - **Free-space velocity of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s. **Boltzmann constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.

topics.title: MTI versus Pulse Doppler Radar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** MTI versus Pulse Doppler Radar

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is MTI Radar?

**MTI (Moving Target Indication) Radar** is a pulse radar system that uses the **Doppler effect** to distinguish moving targets (such as aircraft, vehicles) from stationary clutter (buildings, terrain, trees). It operates by transmitting a coherent train of pulses and applying a **delay-line canceller** (high-pass filter in the Doppler domain) that suppresses echoes with zero or near-zero Doppler shift (clutter) while preserving echoes with non-zero Doppler shift (moving targets).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** MTI radar is a coherent radar that uses the principle of phase comparison between successive pulses to extract the Doppler frequency of moving targets and reject returns from stationary objects.

## 1.2 What is Pulse Doppler Radar?

**Pulse Doppler Radar** is a coherent pulse radar that uses a **high Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF)** to obtain a high unambiguous Doppler measurement, and processes the received echo through a **bank of narrowband Doppler filters (typically implemented via FFT)** to extract both range and velocity information. It is the natural evolution of MTI, designed to overcome the **blind-speed** problem.

> [!NOTE]
> Pulse Doppler radar is the workhorse of modern airborne fire-control systems (e.g., AWACS, fighter radars) because it can resolve targets within strong ground-clutter environments at high platform speeds.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Stethoscope & Tuner" View

Imagine standing at a busy railway crossing listening to trains with two different tools:

- An **MTI Radar** is like a **basic stethoscope**: it simply filters out the constant, unchanging "hum" of the stationary environment (clutter) and lets the *rhythmic pulsations* of an approaching train pass through. However, if a train horn happens to oscillate at exactly the cancellation frequency, the train becomes **invisible** — this is the **blind speed** problem.

- A **Pulse Doppler Radar** is like a **musical tuner with a spectrum analyzer**: it listens to *every frequency component* of the echo, separates them into multiple frequency bins, and can spot a faint note (slow target) hiding in the broad noise spectrum. It uses **high PRF** to "over-sample" the Doppler spectrum, eliminating blind speeds entirely.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (KTU 2024)

| Metric | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Operating Frequency Band | **L (1–2 GHz), S (2–4 GHz), X (8–12 GHz)** |
| Blind Speed (MTI) | **$v_{blind} = n\lambda f_{PRF}/2$** |
| PRF Categories | **Low, Medium, High PRF** |
| Clutter Attenuation | **CA = 50 to 60 dB** (typical) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Both radars are *coherent* radars. The key conceptual difference lies in **PRF selection** and the **filtering philosophy**: MTI uses time-domain cancellers; Pulse Doppler uses frequency-domain filter banks.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Philosophy — The Core "Why"

### 2.1.1 MTI Radar Operating Principle
1. Transmit a **coherent** train of pulses at PRF $f_r$ (or $f_{PRF}$). Coherence means a fixed phase reference is maintained from pulse to pulse (often via a **COHO** — Coherent Oscillator and **STALO** — Stable Local Oscillator).
2. Receive echoes from both clutter (stationary) and targets (moving).
3. The received signal from a moving target at range $R$ and radial velocity $v_r$ contains a Doppler phase shift:
$$\phi_n = 2\pi f_d n T_r = \frac{4\pi v_r}{\lambda} n T_r$$
4. A **single-delay-line canceller** computes the difference between consecutive pulse returns:
$$y(n) = x(n) - x(n - 1)$$
5. Since clutter returns have $f_d \approx 0$, they cancel out. Moving targets with $f_d \neq 0$ produce a residual that passes through.
6. The canceller transfer function:
$$H(f) = 2j \sin(\pi f T_r)$$

### 2.1.2 Pulse Doppler Radar Operating Principle
1. Transmit a **high-PRF** coherent pulse train.
2. Digitize the received I/Q samples at each range bin.
3. Apply a **Doppler filter bank** (implemented via **FFT** — usually 64, 128, 512, or 1024 points).
4. Each filter bin corresponds to a narrow Doppler band $\Delta f_d = 1/(N T_r)$.
5. Detection is performed by setting a CFAR threshold in each Doppler bin.
6. The unambiguous Doppler window:
$$f_{d, unamb} = \pm f_{PRF}/2$$

## 2.2 Why Both Systems? — The "Blind Speed" Trade-Off

The fundamental dilemma: **Range and velocity cannot be measured unambiguously simultaneously** with a single-PRF radar.

- **Unambiguous Range:** $R_u = c/(2 f_r)$
- **Unambiguous Velocity:** $v_u = \lambda f_r / 4$

To increase $R_u$ → decrease $f_r$ (MTI philosophy: Low PRF).
To increase $v_u$ → increase $f_r$ (Pulse Doppler philosophy: High PRF).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MTI radar trades velocity ambiguity for range clarity.**
> **Pulse Doppler radar trades range ambiguity for velocity clarity.**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_d = 2 v_r / \lambda$ | Doppler frequency shift | Hz |
| 2 | $v_r = f_d \lambda / 2$ | Radial velocity extraction | m/s |
| 3 | $R_u = c / (2 f_r)$ | Maximum unambiguous range | m |
| 4 | $v_{blind} = n \lambda f_r / 2$ | Blind speed (n = 1, 2, 3...) | m/s |
| 5 | $f_{d, unamb} = \pm f_r / 2$ | Unambiguous Doppler window | Hz |
| 6 | $H(f) = 2j\sin(\pi f T_r)$ | Single canceller response | dimensionless |
| 7 | $I = \bar{G} \cdot (S_o/\sigma_c) \cdot (\sigma_{c,in}/S_{i,in})$ | Improvement Factor | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{CA} = I_f = \frac{1}{2\sigma_n^2 \int G(f) \, df}$ | Clutter Attenuation (MTI) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $P_c = \exp(-R/\lambda_c)$ | Clutter decorrelation coefficient | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\Delta f_d = 1/(N T_r)$ | Doppler resolution of FFT | Hz |
| 11 | $f_{r,\text{airborne}} = 2 v_p / \lambda$ | PRF needed to offset platform motion | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical bar in $\vert 2\sigma_n^2 \int G(f)\,df \vert$ notation is rendered here using `\vert` for LaTeX purity and has been replaced with parentheses in formula 8 to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **MTI Radar:** Civil aviation Air Traffic Control (ATC) radars, weather radars, ground-based surveillance — wherever the **clutter rejection of slow targets** is paramount and targets are within the **unambiguous range**.
- **Pulse Doppler Radar:** Airborne Intercept (AI) radars, Missile Guidance, Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) preprocessing, Doppler weather radar — wherever **high-speed target detection in severe clutter** is required.
- The **MTI vs. Pulse Doppler** comparison is foundational to choosing a radar architecture in any modern RF system design.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Single-Delay-Line Canceller Response

**Given:** The input signal is a sampled version of the received echo $x(t)$ with period $T_r$.

**Step 1:** Let the received echo from a single point scatterer (target) be:
$$x(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi(t))$$

**Step 2:** For a moving target with Doppler $f_d$, the phase variation between consecutive pulses is:
$$\Delta \phi = 2\pi f_d T_r$$

**Step 3:** The sampled sequence at $t = n T_r$ becomes:
$$x(n) = A \cos(2\pi f_c n T_r + 2\pi f_d n T_r + \phi_0)$$

**Step 4:** The single-delay-line canceller computes:
$$y(n) = x(n) - x(n - 1)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute and expand using the identity $\cos\alpha - \cos\beta = -2\sin\!\left(\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{\alpha-\beta}{2}\right)$:

$$y(n) = -2A \sin\!\left(2\pi f_c n T_r + 2\pi f_d n T_r + \phi_0 - \pi f_d T_r\right) \cdot \sin(\pi f_d T_r)$$

**Step 6:** The **envelope** of the canceller output (ignoring carrier) is:
$$\vert y(n) \vert = 2A \left\vert \sin(\pi f_d T_r) \right\vert$$

**Step 7:** The transfer function (frequency response) is therefore:
$$H(f) = 2j\sin(\pi f T_r)$$

**Step 8:** The **magnitude response** is:
$$\vert H(f) \vert = 2 \left\vert \sin(\pi f T_r) \right\vert$$

**Step 9:** Find the blind speeds by setting $H(f_d) = 0$:
$$\sin(\pi f_d T_r) = 0 \implies \pi f_d T_r = n\pi \implies f_d = n f_r$$

**Step 10:** Convert to radial velocity using $v_r = f_d \lambda / 2$:
$$\boxed{\,v_{blind} = \frac{n \lambda f_r}{2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Blind speed occurs when the target moves *exactly one half-wavelength* between successive pulses, making the two received pulses identical in phase — and thus perfectly cancelled by the MTI filter.

## 3.2 Derivation: MTI Improvement Factor (I)

**Definition:** The Improvement Factor $I$ is the *signal-to-clutter ratio at the output* divided by the *signal-to-clutter ratio at the input*:

$$I = \frac{(S_o / C_o)}{(S_i / C_i)}$$

**Step 1:** For a single-delay-line canceller, the average power gain for the signal (S) and the clutter (C) can be expressed via the integrated power spectrum.

**Step 2:** Assume clutter has a Gaussian-shaped power spectral density centered at $f = 0$ with variance $\sigma_c^2$:
$$G_c(f) = \frac{P_c}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma_c} \exp\!\left(-\frac{f^2}{2\sigma_c^2}\right)$$

**Step 3:** The clutter power at the output of the canceller is:
$$C_o = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert H(f) \vert^2 G_c(f)\, df = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} 4 \sin^2(\pi f T_r) G_c(f)\, df$$

**Step 4:** For narrow clutter spectrum ($\sigma_c T_r \ll 1$), use the approximation $\sin^2(\pi f T_r) \approx (\pi f T_r)^2$:
$$C_o \approx 4 (\pi T_r)^2 \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f^2 G_c(f)\, df = 4 (\pi T_r)^2 \sigma_c^2 P_c$$

**Step 5:** The signal output power for a target with Doppler $f_d$ exactly mid-bin (maximum response):
$$S_o = S_i \cdot 4 \sin^2(\pi f_d T_r) = 4 S_i \quad \text{(at } f_d = f_r/2 \text{)}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute into the Improvement Factor:
$$I = \frac{S_o / C_o}{S_i / C_i} = \frac{4 S_i \cdot C_i}{4 (\pi T_r)^2 \sigma_c^2 P_c \cdot S_i} = \frac{C_i}{(\pi T_r)^2 \sigma_c^2 P_c}$$

**Step 7:** Since $C_i = P_c$ (total clutter power input), and noting that $C_i = \sigma_c$ in normalized notation:

$$\boxed{\,I = \frac{1}{(\pi f_r T_r)^2 \cdot 2\sigma_c^2} = \frac{1}{2\sigma_c^2 \pi^2}\, \text{ (for unit PRF)}\,}$$

**Step 8:** In generalized form (for an M-pulse canceller), Barton's formula:

$$\boxed{\,I_{MTI} \approx \frac{1}{2 \sigma_c^2 \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} a_k^2 / \left( \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} a_k \right)^2}\,}$$

where $a_k$ are the canceller weights and $\sigma_c$ is the normalized clutter standard deviation (in units of $f_r$).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — MTI vs. Pulse Doppler

| S.No. | Parameter | MTI Radar | Pulse Doppler Radar |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **PRF Selection** | Low PRF (clear range) | High PRF (clear velocity) |
| 2 | **Unambiguous Range** | Large ($R_u = c/2f_r$) | Small (highly ambiguous) |
| 3 | **Unambiguous Velocity** | Limited (blind speed issues) | Large (no blind speeds) |
| 4 | **Clutter Rejection** | Moderate (canceller) | Excellent (FFT filter bank) |
| 5 | **Filter Architecture** | Time-domain delay-line canceller | Frequency-domain FFT filter bank |
| 6 | **Number of Filters** | 1 (or 2 for double canceller) | N (64, 128, 512...) |
| 7 | **Clutter Attenuation (CA)** | 20–30 dB (single canceller) | 50–80 dB (with MTI preprocessing) |
| 8 | **Blind Speeds** | Present at $n \lambda f_r/2$ | Eliminated by high PRF |
| 9 | **Range Ambiguity** | None | Severe (multiple time-around echoes) |
| 10 | **Best For** | Slow targets in ground clutter | Fast targets in airborne clutter |
| 11 | **Typical Application** | ATC, ground surveillance | Fighter radar, AWACS, missile guidance |
| 12 | **Hardware Complexity** | Low–Moderate | High (DSP, A/D converters) |
| 13 | **Doppler Resolution** | Poor (single notch) | Excellent ($\Delta f_d = 1/NT_r$) |
| 14 | **Coherency Required** | Yes (pulse-to-pulse) | Yes (pulse-to-pulse + I/Q) |
| 15 | **Data Processing** | Analog or simple digital | Fully digital, real-time FFT |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern radars use a **hybrid approach**: MTI pre-processing (single or double canceller) followed by Pulse Doppler FFT filter bank — combining the clutter rejection of MTI with the velocity resolution of Pulse Doppler.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU Style):** An MTI radar operates at $f_c = 10$ GHz with PRF $f_r = 1$ kHz. Calculate the first three blind speeds.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Wavelength:
$$\lambda = c/f_c = 3 \times 10^8 / 10 \times 10^9 = 0.03 \text{ m} = 3 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2:** First blind speed (n = 1):
$$v_{blind,1} = \frac{1 \times 0.03 \times 1000}{2} = 15 \text{ m/s} = 54 \text{ km/h}$$

**Step 3:** Second blind speed (n = 2):
$$v_{blind,2} = 30 \text{ m/s} = 108 \text{ km/h}$$

**Step 4:** Third blind speed (n = 3):
$$v_{blind,3} = 45 \text{ m/s} = 162 \text{ km/h}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students forget the factor of 2 in the blind speed formula (it comes from the round-trip Doppler shift). Always use $v_{blind} = n\lambda f_r/2$, not $n\lambda f_r$.

**Functionality Mapping:**

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define MTI radar. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> MTI (Moving Target Indication) radar is a type of **coherent pulse radar** that uses the **Doppler effect** to detect moving targets in the presence of strong stationary clutter. It works on the principle of **phase comparison between successive received pulses**. The phase of the echo from a stationary target remains constant from pulse to pulse, while that of a moving target varies due to Doppler shift. A **delay-line canceller** subtracts the previous pulse return from the current one, thereby cancelling clutter (constant phase) and passing only moving targets (varying phase). The canceller is essentially a **high-pass filter in the Doppler domain**, with a transfer function $H(f) = 2j\sin(\pi f T_r)$, that produces nulls at $f_d = n f_r$ (the *blind speeds*). **(3 marks — full-coverage answer)**

### Question 2: What is a Pulse Doppler radar? How does it differ from MTI radar? [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Pulse Doppler radar** is a coherent pulse radar that uses a **high Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF)** combined with a **Doppler filter bank** (typically implemented via FFT) to provide **unambiguous velocity measurement** of moving targets. **(1 mark)**
>
> **Key Differences from MTI radar: (2 marks)**
>
> | Aspect | MTI Radar | Pulse Doppler Radar |
> |---|---|---|
> | PRF | Low PRF | High PRF |
> | Filtering | Delay-line canceller (1 filter) | FFT filter bank (N filters) |
> | Blind speeds | Present at $v = n\lambda f_r / 2$ | Eliminated |
> | Range ambiguity | None | Present (multiple time-around) |
> | Application | Ground surveillance | Airborne / fire-control |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Question A(a):** With the help of a block diagram, explain the operation of an MTI radar. Discuss the function of the delay-line canceller and derive the expression for blind speed. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Block Diagram and Operation (3 marks):**

The MTI radar consists of a **STALO** (Stable Local Oscillator), **COHO** (Coherent Oscillator), transmitter, duplexer, antenna, receiver, and a **delay-line canceller** (subtractor with one-period delay). The COHO provides the phase reference for pulse-to-pulse coherence.

- The transmitted pulse train has constant initial phase.
- Echoes from clutter have **constant phase** from pulse to pulse.
- Echoes from a moving target have a **progressively varying phase** $\phi_n = 4\pi v_r n T_r / \lambda$.
- The canceller output $y(n) = x(n) - x(n-1)$ suppresses clutter and passes moving targets.

**2. Delay-Line Canceller Transfer Function (2 marks):**

The frequency response of the single-delay-line canceller is:
$$H(f) = 2j\sin(\pi f T_r)$$

**3. Blind Speed Derivation (2 marks):**

The canceller output is zero when $\sin(\pi f_d T_r) = 0$, i.e., $f_d = n f_r$. Substituting $f_d = 2 v_r / \lambda$:

$$v_{blind} = \frac{n \lambda f_r}{2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

> **Conclusion:** Targets moving at blind speeds produce zero output and are invisible to MTI radar.

---

**Question A(b):** Compare MTI radar and Pulse Doppler radar in detail. Mention the role of PRF in both systems. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition and Architecture (2 marks):**

- **MTI Radar:** Coherent pulse radar using low PRF and a single delay-line canceller. Suitable for clear-range surveillance.
- **Pulse Doppler Radar:** Coherent pulse radar using high PRF and an FFT-based Doppler filter bank. Suitable for clear-velocity airborne surveillance.

**2. Role of PRF (3 marks):**

PRF is the central design parameter. The trade-off is governed by:

- Unambiguous range: $R_u = c/(2f_r)$ — decreases with PRF.
- Unambiguous velocity: $v_u = \lambda f_r / 4$ — increases with PRF.

**MTI uses Low PRF** to make $R_u$ large (often hundreds of km). The cost is small $v_u$ and the presence of **blind speeds**.

**Pulse Doppler uses High PRF** to make $v_u$ large (eliminates blind speeds). The cost is severe **range ambiguity** — returns from multiple pulses overlap at the receiver, requiring **range-gating and PRF staggering** to resolve.

**3. Detailed Comparison (2 marks):**

| Parameter | MTI | Pulse Doppler |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Doppler filters | 1 | $N$ (e.g., 64, 128) |
| Doppler resolution | Very poor | $\Delta f_d = 1/(N T_r)$ |
| Clutter attenuation | 20–30 dB | 50–80 dB |
| Blind speeds | Present | Eliminated |
| Range ambiguity | None | Severe (handled via stagger) |
| Hardware | Simple | Complex DSP |
| Application | ATC, ground radar | AWACS, fire control |

> **Conclusion:** MTI sacrifices velocity clarity for range clarity; Pulse Doppler does the opposite. Modern systems use **hybrid MTI + Pulse Doppler** for the best of both worlds.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Question B(a):** Explain the working of a Pulse Doppler radar with a neat block diagram. Discuss how it overcomes the blind-speed problem of MTI radar. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Block Diagram (2 marks):**

The Pulse Doppler radar consists of a master timing generator, coherent transmitter, antenna (often phased array), LNA, I/Q quadrature mixer, A/D converters, range-gated buffer memory, **FFT Doppler filter bank**, CFAR detector, and display.

**2. Working Principle (2 marks):**

- The I/Q mixer produces in-phase (I) and quadrature (Q) samples at each range bin.
- For each range bin, a **block of N successive pulses** is collected.
- An **N-point FFT** is applied across the slow-time (pulse) dimension, producing N Doppler bins.
- Each bin has a narrow bandwidth $\Delta f_d = 1/(N T_r)$.
- Target echoes produce a peak at the bin corresponding to their Doppler frequency.

**3. Overcoming Blind Speeds (3 marks):**

In MTI, the canceller has discrete nulls at $f_d = n f_r$. In Pulse Doppler:

- The PRF is **high enough** that the first blind speed $v_{blind,1} = \lambda f_r / 2$ exceeds the maximum expected target velocity. Hence **no blind speed lies within the target velocity range**.
- Alternatively, the FFT filter bank has multiple filters covering $\pm f_r/2$, and the filters are designed with low sidelobes (windowing) so there is no complete null in the Doppler spectrum.
- Multiple PRFs can be **staggered** to further resolve residual ambiguities.

$$\boxed{\,v_{blind,1,PD} = \frac{\lambda f_r}{2} \gg v_{target,max} \implies \text{No blind speed problem}\,}$$

---

**Question B(b):** Derive the expression for the MTI Improvement Factor. An MTI radar operates at 10 GHz with a PRF of 1 kHz. The clutter has a Gaussian spectrum with $\sigma_c = 20$ Hz. Calculate the improvement factor. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Derivation of Improvement Factor (4 marks):**

Improvement Factor $I$ is defined as:
$$I = \frac{(S_o / C_o)}{(S_i / C_i)}$$

For a single-delay-line canceller with clutter having Gaussian spectrum:

$$I = \frac{1}{2 (\pi \sigma_c T_r)^2}$$

where $\sigma_c$ is the **clutter spectral spread in Hz**, and $T_r = 1/f_r$ is the PRI.

**Derivation Steps:**

- Clutter power at output: $C_o = \int 4 \sin^2(\pi f T_r) \cdot G_c(f)\, df$
- For narrow spectrum: $\sin^2(\pi f T_r) \approx (\pi f T_r)^2$
- So: $C_o \approx 4 (\pi T_r)^2 \sigma_c^2 P_c$
- Signal at output (max): $S_o = 4 S_i$
- Therefore: $I = S_o \cdot C_i / (C_o \cdot S_i) = P_c / [(\pi T_r)^2 \sigma_c^2 P_c]$
- Hence: $I = 1 / [(\pi \sigma_c T_r)^2] = 1 / [2(\pi \sigma_c T_r)^2 / 2]$, giving the standard form $I = 1 / [2(\pi \sigma_c T_r)^2]$ (numerical factor varies by definition).

**2. Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

**Given:** $f_c = 10$ GHz, $f_r = 1$ kHz, $\sigma_c = 20$ Hz.

**Step 1:** PRI $T_r = 1/f_r = 1/1000 = 10^{-3}$ s.

**Step 2:** Compute $\sigma_c T_r = 20 \times 10^{-3} = 0.02$.

**Step 3:** Compute $\pi \sigma_c T_r = \pi \times 0.02 = 0.0628$.

**Step 4:** Square it: $(\pi \sigma_c T_r)^2 = 3.95 \times 10^{-3}$.

**Step 5:** Apply formula:
$$I = \frac{1}{2 \times 3.95 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{7.90 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 126.6$$

**Step 6:** In dB:
$$I_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(126.6) \approx 21.02 \text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{\,I \approx 126.6 \;\; (\text{or } 21 \text{ dB})\,}$$

> **Interpretation:** The MTI radar improves the signal-to-clutter ratio by a factor of ~127 (or 21 dB) — sufficient for detecting moving targets in moderate clutter.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in MTI vs. Pulse Doppler Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Doppler formula** — Use $f_d = 2v_r/\lambda$ (round-trip), not $f_d = v_r/\lambda$. This is the **#1 mistake** in KTU exams.
> 2. **Confusing PRF and PRI** — $f_r = 1/T_r$. Numerical errors are common.
> 3. **Writing the canceller as "high-pass filter" without specifying "in the Doppler domain"** — Always mention *Doppler* explicitly.
> 4. **Omitting the COHO/STALO in MTI block diagram** — Examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for "coherent oscillator." Without it, full marks are not awarded.
> 5. **Stating "Pulse Doppler has no clutter"** — Wrong. It has *excellent* clutter rejection (via FFT filters), but clutter still exists; it is attenuated.
> 6. **Writing $v_{blind} = n\lambda f_r$ instead of $n\lambda f_r/2$** — Factor of 2 error costs 2 marks.
> 7. **Not drawing the I/Q channels in Pulse Doppler block diagram** — Quadrature sampling is *mandatory* for Pulse Doppler to resolve positive and negative Doppler.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **MTI** = Moving Target Indication → uses **delay-line canceller** to suppress stationary clutter.
- [x] **Pulse Doppler** = high PRF coherent radar with **FFT filter bank** for fine Doppler resolution.
- [x] **Doppler frequency:** $f_d = 2v_r/\lambda$ (round-trip factor of 2).
- [x] **Blind speed:** $v_{blind} = n\lambda f_r/2$ — only present in MTI.
- [x] **Unambiguous Range:** $R_u = c/(2f_r)$ — large when PRF is low.
- [x] **Unambiguous Velocity:** $v_u = \lambda f_r/4$ — large when PRF is high.
- [x] **MTI canceller response:** $H(f) = 2j\sin(\pi f T_r)$ — single delay line.
- [x] **Double canceller response:** $H(f) = [2\sin(\pi f T_r)]^2$ — better clutter rejection.
- [x] **Pulse Doppler uses High PRF** → eliminates blind speeds but creates range ambiguity.
- [x] **PRF Staggering:** used to resolve range ambiguities in Pulse Doppler.
- [x] **Improvement Factor (I):** output S/C divided by input S/C; in dB typically 20–30 dB.
- [x] **Clutter Attenuation (CA):** ratio of clutter input power to clutter output power.
- [x] **COHO (Coherent Oscillator)** maintains pulse-to-pulse phase reference — essential for both.
- [x] **I/Q sampling** is mandatory in Pulse Doppler to preserve Doppler sign.
- [x] **Modern radars** use hybrid MTI + Pulse Doppler architecture.
- [x] **MTI is best for** slow targets in ground clutter (ATC).
- [x] **Pulse Doppler is best for** fast targets in airborne clutter (AWACS, fighter radar).
- [x] **Doppler resolution** of Pulse Doppler: $\Delta f_d = 1/(N T_r)$ — improves with more pulses.
- [x] **Key trade-off:** Range clarity (MTI) vs. Velocity clarity (Pulse Doppler) — governed by PRF.
- [x] **MTI canceller is essentially a high-pass filter in Doppler** — remember to state this in exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tracking Radar: Various Techniques of Tracking with Radar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT832 - SATELLITE AND RADAR COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tracking Radar: Various Techniques of Tracking with Radar

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Tracking Radar

A **Tracking Radar** is a specialized radar system designed to continuously determine and follow one or more specific targets in space by measuring their **range, angular position (azimuth and elevation), and radial velocity** with high precision over extended time intervals. Unlike surveillance radars that scan a broad volume, a tracking radar concentrates its antenna beam and processing resources on a single target (or a small number of high-priority targets), updating its pointing angle and range gate at a high refresh rate (typically **10–100 Hz** for mechanical tracking, and up to **kHz** for phased-array systems).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** A tracking radar is a closed-loop servo system in which the antenna and the range/velocity gate are continuously steered so that the antenna boresight and the target coincide in angle, range, and Doppler.

The key output parameters of a tracking radar are:
- **Range (R):** Line-of-sight distance to the target
- **Azimuth Angle (θ):** Horizontal angle measured clockwise from True North
- **Elevation Angle (φ):** Vertical angle above the local horizon
- **Radial Velocity (Ṙ):** Rate of change of range (from Doppler shift)

These four parameters, tracked as functions of time, are used to **predict the future position of the target**—a function critical in fire-control, missile guidance, ballistic missile defense, and rendezvous operations.

## 1.2 Why Is Tracking Needed?

Most radar missions fall into two categories:

| Mission Type | Radar Function | Example |
|--------------|----------------|---------|
| **Search / Surveillance** | Detect new targets across a wide volume | Air-traffic-control primary radar |
| **Track / Fire Control** | Maintain a precise, continuous trajectory on a known target | Anti-aircraft gun-laying, missile guidance |

> [!NOTE]
> In a real KTU 2024 Scheme problem, a tracking radar is often described as a **double closed-loop system**: an inner "angle-tracking" loop that drives the antenna gimbals, and an outer "range-tracking" loop that drives the time-delay of the range gate.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Spotlight Follower

Imagine you are standing on a stage in a large dark stadium holding a torchlight (a narrow beam of light). A friend is walking around the field. Your job is to keep the torchlight centered on your friend at all times.

- **The torchlight = the radar antenna beam (narrow pencil beam)**
- **Your eyes = the radar receiver, sensing the strength of the returned signal**
- **Your arms rotating the torch = the antenna pedestal servomotors**
- **Your brain = the tracking computer, computing the angle error**

If the torch is pointed slightly to the left of your friend, you see a darker patch on the right side of the beam and a brighter patch on the left. Your brain instantly says "rotate clockwise by 2°"—this is exactly what the **error-detector / servo loop** in a tracking radar does. The same logic holds whether you use one beam scanned around (conical scan), two beams switched alternately (lobe switching), or three beams measured simultaneously (monopulse).

## 1.4 Classification of Tracking Techniques

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups tracking techniques along two principal axes:

**A. By beam-steering method:**
1. **Mechanical Tracking** – antenna physically rotated on pedestal (slow but accurate)
2. **Electronic Tracking (Phased Array)** – beam steered electronically (fast, agile, no inertia)

**B. By error-sensing method (the focus of this module):**
1. **Sequential Lobing (Lobe Switching)** – earliest technique
2. **Conical Scan** – widely used in fire-control radars
3. **Monopulse Tracking** – modern, single-pulse, high-accuracy method
4. **Track-While-Scan (TWS)** – software-based tracking using a surveillance radar

We will now study each of these techniques in depth.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sequential Lobing (Lobe Switching)

### 2.1.1 Concept
Two (or four) **overlapping, squinted beams** are generated by displacing the feed horn slightly off the focus of a parabolic reflector. The beams point at small equal angles $\pm\theta_q$ from the antenna boresight. The receiver is switched between the two beams at a rate faster than the target motion but slower than the PRF.

### 2.1.2 Error Detection Logic
Let $S_1$ and $S_2$ be the echo amplitudes received in the two beams. For a target at angular offset $\theta$ from the boresight:

$$
S_1 = G(\theta_q - \theta) \cdot \sigma \cdot \frac{1}{R^4}
$$

$$
S_2 = G(\theta_q + \theta) \cdot \sigma \cdot \frac{1}{R^4}
$$

The angular **error signal** is the normalized difference:

$$
e_\theta = \frac{S_1 - S_2}{S_1 + S_2}
$$

If $\theta = 0$ (target on boresight), $S_1 = S_2$ and $e_\theta = 0 \Rightarrow$ no corrective command. If $\theta \neq 0$, a non-zero error drives the servo to rotate the antenna toward the target.

### 2.1.3 Disadvantages
- **Glint and amplitude fluctuations** between the two switching intervals introduce angular noise.
- Switching time creates an **inherent lag**.
- Susceptible to **inverse-gain jamming**.

## 2.2 Conical Scan Tracking

### 2.2.1 Concept
A single **squinted beam** is rotated about the antenna boresight at a constant angular rate $\Omega_s$ (typically **20–100 Hz**). The squint angle $\theta_q$ is small (≈ **0.1–0.5 beamwidths**). The boresight itself is the tracking reference.

### 2.2.2 Error-Signal Generation
If the target lies on the boresight, the received echo amplitude is constant at a level $A_0$ (a DC value). If the target is offset by a small angle $\theta$ at an azimuthal phase $\phi$, the echo becomes **amplitude modulated** at the scan frequency $\Omega_s$:

$$
A(t) = A_0 \left[ 1 + m \cos(\Omega_s t + \psi) \right]
$$

where:
- $m$ = **modulation depth**, proportional to the angular error magnitude $\theta$
- $\psi$ = **modulation phase**, indicating the direction of the error

The modulation depth and phase are extracted by synchronous (coherent) detection at the scan frequency, producing two orthogonal error signals:

$$
e_A = m \cos\psi \quad \text{(azimuth error component)}
$$

$$
e_E = m \sin\psi \quad \text{(elevation error component)}
$$

These drive the two orthogonal servo motors (azimuth and elevation pedestals).

### 2.2.3 Error Slope
The slope of the error signal versus angular offset is the **error-slope coefficient** $k_s$:

$$
k_s = \frac{1.39 \cdot \theta_q}{\theta_{3\text{dB}}^2} \quad \text{(volts per radian)}
$$

This determines the **angular accuracy** and the **loop gain** of the tracking servo.

### 2.2.4 Disadvantages
- **Scan modulation is itself observable** to the target—an enemy jammer can detect the scan frequency and inject a false modulation (inverse-gain or range-gate pull-off jamming).
- Requires a **scan reference signal** with a stable, well-known phase.

## 2.3 Monopulse Tracking (Single-Pulse Tracking)

### 2.3.1 Concept
Monopulse tracking extracts the **full angular error information from a single transmitted pulse**, eliminating the time-varying modulation problem of conical scan. Three (or four) simultaneous beams are formed; the echo is processed through a **monopulse comparator** to produce Sum ($\Sigma$) and Difference ($\Delta$) patterns.

### 2.3.2 Sum and Difference Patterns
The sum pattern $\Sigma(\theta)$ points along the boresight with high gain:

$$
\Sigma(\theta) = F_1(\theta) + F_2(\theta)
$$

The difference pattern $\Delta(\theta)$ has a null on the boresight and odd symmetry:

$$
\Delta(\theta) = F_1(\theta) - F_2(\theta)
$$

### 2.3.3 Monopulse Ratio (The Core Equation)
The angular error is proportional to the real part of the **complex monopulse ratio**:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta}{\Sigma}
$$

For small target offsets within the linear region of the difference pattern:

$$
\varepsilon = k_m \cdot \theta
$$

where $k_m$ is the **monopulse slope** (a property of the antenna design).

The error signal in the receiver is therefore:

$$
e_\theta = \text{Re}\left\{\frac{\Delta}{\Sigma}\right\} = k_m \cdot \theta
$$

### 2.3.4 Amplitude-Comparison vs Phase-Comparison Monopulse

| Feature | Amplitude Comparison | Phase Comparison |
|---------|----------------------|------------------|
| Beam formation | Two squinted beams | Two overlapping beams from separated apertures |
| Error sensing | Difference in amplitude | Difference in phase of returns |
| Comparator | Magic-T / hybrid junction | Phase detector / correlator |
| Typical use | Reflector antennas | Phased arrays and interferometers |

### 2.3.5 Advantages
- **Single-pulse accuracy**—immune to pulse-to-amplitude fluctuation.
- **No scan frequency to detect**—jam-resistant.
- **High accuracy** (~ **0.1 to 0.05 beamwidths**).

## 2.4 Track-While-Scan (TWS)

### 2.4.1 Concept
A **surveillance radar** is operated in a way that the same beam scans the volume periodically. On each scan, **detected plots** (range, angle, amplitude) are associated with previous plots using **Kalman filtering** or **alpha-beta tracking**, and a smoothed trajectory is maintained.

### 2.4.2 Key Parameters
- **Scan period $T_s$:** Time between successive illuminations of the target.
- **Track update rate:** $1/T_s$.
- **Track accuracy** improves with the square root of the number of integrated plots: $\sigma_\theta \propto 1/\sqrt{N}$.

### 2.4.3 Tracking Equations (α-β Filter)
The simplest TWS smoother uses two gains $\alpha$ and $\beta$:

$$
x_{n+1} = x_n + \alpha \cdot (z_n - x_n)
$$

$$
\dot{x}_{n+1} = \dot{x}_n + \frac{\beta}{T_s} \cdot (z_n - x_n)
$$

where $z_n$ is the measured plot at scan $n$, and $x_n$ is the smoothed estimate.

## 2.5 Range Tracking (Split-Gate / Early-Late Gate)

A tracking radar must also maintain a **range gate** that moves with the target. The classical implementation is the **early-late gate** or **split range gate**:

- The matched filter output is sampled at three times: $t_0 - \tau/2$, $t_0$, and $t_0 + \tau/2$ (where $\tau$ is the pulse width).
- Let $E$ = early-gate output, $L$ = late-gate output.
- The **range error signal** is:

$$
e_R = E - L
$$

This drives a **voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO)** that closes the range-tracking loop.

## 2.6 Velocity Tracking (Doppler Tracking)

The radial velocity $\dot{R}$ is obtained from the **Doppler shift** $f_d$:

$$
f_d = \frac{2 \dot{R}}{\lambda}
$$

$$
\dot{R} = \frac{\lambda f_d}{2}
$$

A narrow-band **Doppler filter bank** (or a phase-locked loop) tracks $f_d$, and the corresponding velocity is fed to the fire-control computer.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize the formulas below. They appear in nearly every KTU 2024 ESE Part-B question on tracking radar.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | $\varepsilon = \Delta / \Sigma$ | Monopulse ratio (complex) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $e_\theta = k_m \cdot \theta$ | Monopulse error signal (linear region) | V |
| 3 | $k_s = 1.39 \,\theta_q / \theta_{3\text{dB}}^2$ | Conical-scan error slope | V/rad |
| 4 | $A(t) = A_0 [1 + m \cos(\Omega_s t + \psi)]$ | Conical-scan echo envelope | V |
| 5 | $S_1 - S_2$ / $S_1 + S_2$ | Sequential-lobing error signal | dimensionless |
| 6 | $f_d = 2 \dot{R} / \lambda$ | Doppler shift of echo | Hz |
| 7 | $\dot{R} = \lambda f_d / 2$ | Radial velocity | m/s |
| 8 | $e_R = E - L$ | Range error (early-late gate) | V |
| 9 | $\Delta R = c / (2B)$ | Range resolution | m |
| 10 | $\sigma_\theta = \theta_{3\text{dB}} / (k_s \sqrt{2 \text{SNR}})$ | Angular tracking accuracy | rad |

**Physical Constants (memorize):**
- Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- Boltzman constant: $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- Standard temperature: $T_0 = 290$ K
- Noise power density at room temperature: $-174$ dBm/Hz

## 2.8 Engineering Applications

1. **Fire-control radars** (e.g., anti-aircraft gun-laying) → use conical scan or monopulse.
2. **Missile-seeker radars** → use monopulse on a small antenna mounted in the radome.
3. **Ballistic Missile Defense (BMD)** → phased-array monopulse for simultaneous multi-target tracking.
4. **Air-traffic-control beacons** → TWS for hundreds of aircraft.
5. **Weather radars** → TWS for storm cell motion and nowcasting.
6. **Space surveillance** → TWS for satellite tracking, conjunction analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Common Mistake:** Do not confuse "tracking" (closing the loop on a single target) with "scanning" (sweeping a beam across a volume). TWS uses scanning but produces tracking outputs through software, while conical scan and monopulse use hardware loops.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sequential-Lobing Error Signal for Symmetric Beams

**Given:** Two identical squinted beams with antenna gain $G(\theta)$ approximated as a Gaussian:

$$
G(\theta) = G_0 \, e^{-2.77 (\theta / \theta_{3\text{dB}})^2}
$$

The two beams point at $+\theta_q$ and $-\theta_q$ from the boresight. The target is at angular offset $\theta_T$ from the boresight.

**Step 1:** Write the received echo power in each beam. Using the radar range equation:

$$
S_1 = \frac{P_t G_1(\theta_T) \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4 L}
$$

$$
S_2 = \frac{P_t G_2(\theta_T) \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4 L}
$$

The common range-dependent factor cancels when we form the ratio. So we can write:

$$
S_1 \propto G_0 \, e^{-2.77 \left( \frac{\theta_T - \theta_q}{\theta_{3\text{dB}}} \right)^2}
$$

$$
S_2 \propto G_0 \, e^{-2.77 \left( \frac{\theta_T + \theta_q}{\theta_{3\text{dB}}} \right)^2}
$$

**Step 2:** Form the error signal $e_\theta = (S_1 - S_2) / (S_1 + S_2)$:

$$
e_\theta = \frac{e^{-2.77 \left( \frac{\theta_T - \theta_q}{\theta_{3\text{dB}}} \right)^2} - e^{-2.77 \left( \frac{\theta_T + \theta_q}{\theta_{3\text{dB}}} \right)^2}}{e^{-2.77 \left( \frac{\theta_T - \theta_q}{\theta_{3\text{dB}}} \right)^2} + e^{-2.77 \left( \frac{\theta_T + \theta_q}{\theta_{3\text{dB}}} \right)^2}
$$

**Step 3:** Use the identity $(e^u - e^{-u}) / (e^u + e^{-u}) = \tanh(u)$ to simplify. With $u = 2.77 \cdot 2 \theta_T \theta_q / \theta_{3\text{dB}}^2$:

$$
e_\theta = \tanh\!\left( \frac{5.54 \, \theta_T \theta_q}{\theta_{3\text{dB}}^2} \right)
$$

**Step 4:** For small target offsets ($\theta_T \ll \theta_{3\text{dB}}^2 / \theta_q$), $\tanh(x) \approx x$, so:

$$
e_\theta \approx \frac{5.54 \, \theta_T \theta_q}{\theta_{3\text{dB}}^2} = k_s \, \theta_T
$$

This is the **linear discriminator characteristic** of the sequential-lobing tracker. The slope $k_s = 5.54 \theta_q / \theta_{3\text{dB}}^2$ determines the loop gain and the **angular accuracy** of the system.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Monopulse Ratio from Sum and Difference Patterns

**Given:** Two identical beams $F_1(\theta)$ and $F_2(\theta)$ squinted by $\pm \theta_q/2$ from the boresight. For small squint angles we expand the pattern around the boresight ($\theta = 0$):

**Step 1:** Taylor-series expansion of $F_1$ and $F_2$:

$$
F_1(\theta) \approx F(0) + F'(0)(\theta - \theta_q/2)
$$

$$
F_2(\theta) \approx F(0) + F'(0)(\theta + \theta_q/2)
$$

**Step 2:** Form sum and difference:

$$
\Sigma(\theta) = F_1 + F_2 = 2 F(0) + 2 F'(0) \, \theta
$$

$$
\Delta(\theta) = F_1 - F_2 = -F'(0) \, \theta_q
$$

**Step 3:** Notice that $\Delta(\theta)$ is **independent of $\theta$ to first order** (the difference pattern is essentially constant within the linear region of the sum pattern). The ratio is:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta(\theta)}{\Sigma(\theta)} = \frac{-F'(0) \, \theta_q}{2 F(0) + 2 F'(0) \, \theta}
$$

**Step 4:** For $\theta$ small enough that $F'(0) \theta \ll F(0)$ (the linear region):

$$
\varepsilon \approx \frac{-F'(0) \, \theta_q}{2 F(0)} = k_m \cdot \theta
$$

**Step 5:** The monopulse slope $k_m$ is a constant of the antenna:

$$
k_m = -\frac{F'(0) \, \theta_q}{2 F(0)}
$$

The **target angular offset** can be recovered directly:

$$
\theta = \frac{\varepsilon}{k_m} = \frac{\Delta}{\Sigma \cdot k_m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why monopulse is so powerful: the error is computed from a single pulse and is independent of the absolute echo amplitude. Even if the target RCS varies from pulse to pulse, the **ratio** is unchanged.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Conical-Scan Modulation Depth

**Given:** Beam with peak gain $G_0$ rotated about the boresight at angular rate $\Omega_s$ with squint angle $\theta_q$. Target at angular offset $\theta$ at azimuthal phase $\phi$ measured from the beam's instantaneous pointing direction.

**Step 1:** The instantaneous angle between the beam axis and the target line-of-sight is:

$$
\alpha(t) = \sqrt{\theta^2 + \theta_q^2 - 2 \theta \theta_q \cos(\Omega_s t - \phi)}
$$

**Step 2:** For small $\theta$ and $\theta_q$ (linear region), expand the cosine and use the small-angle approximation:

$$
\alpha(t) \approx \theta_q \left[ 1 - \frac{\theta}{\theta_q} \cos(\Omega_s t - \phi) \right]
$$

**Step 3:** The echo amplitude is proportional to the antenna gain at the angle $\alpha(t)$. With a Gaussian pattern:

$$
G(\alpha) = G_0 e^{-2.77 \alpha^2 / \theta_{3\text{dB}}^2}
$$

**Step 4:** Expand the exponential for small $\alpha$ deviation:

$$
G(t) \approx G_0 \left[ 1 + \frac{5.54 \theta \theta_q}{\theta_{3\text{dB}}^2} \cos(\Omega_s t - \phi) \right]
$$

**Step 5:** Compare with the canonical form $A(t) = A_0[1 + m \cos(\Omega_s t + \psi)]$:

$$
m = \frac{5.54 \theta \theta_q}{\theta_{3\text{dB}}^2}
$$

$$
\psi = -\phi
$$

Thus the **modulation depth** $m$ is proportional to the target's angular offset $\theta$, and the **modulation phase** $\psi$ indicates the direction of the error. Two orthogonal synchronous detectors (driven by $\cos\Omega_s t$ and $\sin\Omega_s t$) produce the azimuth and elevation error voltages:

$$
e_A = k_d \, m \cos\psi \quad , \quad e_E = k_d \, m \sin\psi
$$

where $k_d$ is the detector gain. These two voltages close the two-axis (azimuth, elevation) tracking servo loop.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Range-Tracking Error from Early-Late Gate

**Given:** Matched-filter output for a rectangular pulse of width $\tau$ centered at $t_0$:

$$
y(t) = A \cdot \text{tri}\!\left( \frac{t - t_0}{\tau} \right)
$$

where $\text{tri}(x) = 1 - \vert x \vert$ for $\vert x \vert \le 1$ and $0$ otherwise.

**Step 1:** Sample at $t = t_0 - \tau/2$ and $t = t_0 + \tau/2$ (the early and late gate samples):

$$
E = A \cdot \text{tri}(-1/2) = 0.5 \, A \quad , \quad L = A \cdot \text{tri}(+1/2) = 0.5 \, A
$$

The sum is $A$, and the difference is **zero** when the gate is centered. Good.

**Step 2:** Now suppose the gate center is in error by $\delta t$ (the true target time is $t_0 + \delta t$):

$$
E = A \cdot \left( 1 - \left\vert \frac{\delta t}{\tau} + \frac{1}{2} \right\vert \right)
$$

$$
L = A \cdot \left( 1 - \left\vert \frac{\delta t}{\tau} - \frac{1}{2} \right\vert \right)
$$

**Step 3:** For $\vert \delta t \vert < \tau/2$:

$$
E = A \left( \frac{1}{2} - \frac{\delta t}{\tau} \right) \quad , \quad L = A \left( \frac{1}{2} + \frac{\delta t}{\tau} \right)
$$

**Step 4:** Range error signal:

$$
e_R = E - L = -\frac{2 A \delta t}{\tau}
$$

The range error is **proportional to the time error** $\delta t$, which corresponds to a range error of $\delta R = c \delta t / 2$:

$$
e_R = -\frac{A \, c \, \delta R}{\tau}
$$

This signal drives a VCO that adjusts the gate delay to keep $\delta R \to 0$.

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix (For KTU 2-Mark Comparison Questions)

| Property | Sequential Lobing | Conical Scan | Monopulse | TWS |
|----------|-------------------|--------------|-----------|-----|
| Pulses needed for one error | $\ge 2$ | 1 cycle ($\sim$ 4–10 pulses) | **1** | Many (per scan) |
| Scan frequency observable to jammer | Yes | Yes | **No** | No |
| Sensitivity to amplitude fluctuations | High | High | **Low** | Medium |
| Angular accuracy (beamwidths) | 0.1 | 0.05–0.1 | **0.03–0.05** | 0.2–0.5 |
| Hardware complexity | Low | Medium | **High** | Software |
| Data rate | Medium | Medium | **High** | Low |
| Typical application | Early fire control | Anti-aircraft guns | Missile guidance | ATC |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid node IDs are pure alphanumeric prefixed with a letter. All node labels are **raw uppercase alphanumeric text** with no markdown bold, italics, or HTML tags.

## 4.2 Diagram 1: High-Level Block Architecture of a Tracking Radar

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TRACKING RADAR SYSTEM] --> B[ANTENNA PEDESTAL MECHANICAL 2 AXIS]
    A --> C[TRANSMITTER HIGH POWER RF]
    A --> D[RECEIVER LOW NOISE]
    A --> E[SIGNAL PROCESSOR AND ERROR DETECTOR]
    A --> F[TRACK COMPUTER AND DISPLAY]

    B --> B1[AZIMUTH SERVO]
```

## 4.7 Functional Description (Substitute for Physical Drawing)

Because Mermaid cannot accurately draw the **antenna radiation pattern lobes**, the following functional description captures the essence of the patterns used in tracking-radar textbooks.

**Sequential Lobing:** Two identical pencil beams point at $+\theta_q$ and $-\theta_q$ from the boresight. They overlap in the boresight direction. At the half-power overlap point the two patterns are equal. The target's true angular position is recovered by switching between the beams and comparing echo amplitudes.

**Conical Scan:** A single beam is offset from the reflector axis by squint angle $\theta_q$. The feed is rotated mechanically (or a sub-reflector is wobbled) at rate $\Omega_s$. The beam tip traces a circle of radius $\theta_q$ in the far field. The boresight is the geometric center of this circle. A target on the boresight receives a constant illumination; an off-boresight target receives an amplitude-modulated echo at the scan frequency.

**Monopulse:** Two (or four) feeds illuminate the same reflector. A hybrid network simultaneously forms Sum (boresight-pointing) and Difference (null-on-boresight) patterns. The monopulse ratio $\Delta/\Sigma$ gives the angular error in a single pulse.

**Track-While-Scan:** No physical beam-steering; the antenna scans the full volume and a digital computer associates plots over successive scans to maintain tracks.
