# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 2-Mark Conceptual Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define a tracking radar. What is the difference between a tracking radar and a surveillance radar?

**Model Answer:**

A tracking radar is a radar system that continuously follows a single (or a small number of) target(s), measuring its **range, angular coordinates (azimuth and elevation), and radial velocity** at a high update rate, and uses these measurements to drive a **closed-loop servo system** that keeps the antenna beam centered on the target.

| Aspect | Tracking Radar | Surveillance Radar |
|--------|----------------|--------------------|
| Purpose | Maintain a precise track on a known target | Detect and enumerate all targets in a volume |
| Beam | Narrow, narrow pencil | Wide, fan or cosecant-squared |
| Data rate | 10–100 Hz | One scan every several seconds |
| Output | Continuous trajectory | Plot list (one per scan) |
| Typical use | Fire control, missile guidance | Air-traffic-control primary radar |

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** What is meant by "lobe switching" in radar? Why is it called "sequential lobing"?

**Model Answer:**

Lobe switching is a tracking technique in which the antenna produces **two (or more) slightly squinted beams** that are sampled sequentially in time by switching the receiver input between the two feeds. The angular error is the **difference in echo amplitudes** measured in the two beams. It is called "sequential" because the beams are examined **one after another** (in sequence), not simultaneously. The technique has the limitation that the target's RCS can fluctuate between the two switching samples, introducing angular noise—this is the very problem that motivated the development of monopulse tracking.

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice (True KTU 2024 Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**, every Part-B question carries 14 marks with internal choice. Sub-parts are typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Part (a) [7 Marks] – [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 (Model Paper)]**

> With neat block diagrams, explain the **conical-scan tracking technique** in a radar. Derive the expression for the **modulation depth** in terms of the target's angular offset.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: System Description** [2 marks]

A conical-scan tracker uses a single pencil beam whose axis is offset from the antenna boresight by a small squint angle $\theta_q$ (typically 0.1 to 0.5 beamwidths). The beam is rotated about the boresight at a constant angular rate $\Omega_s$ (typically 20 to 100 Hz). The antenna's geometric boresight is the tracking reference axis.

**Step 2: Echo Envelope** [2 marks]

If the target is at angular offset $\theta$ from the boresight, the angle between the rotating beam axis and the target line-of-sight varies sinusoidally with time. The received echo amplitude is therefore amplitude-modulated:

$$
A(t) = A_0 \left[ 1 + m \cos(\Omega_s t + \psi) \right]
$$

where $m$ is the modulation depth (proportional to $\theta$) and $\psi$ indicates the direction of the offset.

**Step 3: Derivation of Modulation Depth** [3 marks]

Let the antenna gain pattern be approximated as Gaussian:

$$
G(\alpha) = G_0 \, e^{-2.77 \alpha^2 / \theta_{3\text{dB}}^2}
$$

The instantaneous angle $\alpha(t)$ between beam axis and target is found by the law of cosines on the geometry of the beam and target positions:

$$
\alpha(t) = \sqrt{\theta^2 + \theta_q^2 - 2 \theta \theta_q \cos(\Omega_s t - \phi)}
$$

For $\theta \ll \theta_q$ and linearizing, $\alpha(t) \approx \theta_q[1 - (\theta/\theta_q)\cos(\Omega_s t - \phi)]$. Substituting in the Gaussian gain:

$$
G(t) \approx G_0 \left[ 1 + \frac{5.54 \, \theta \, \theta_q}{\theta_{3\text{dB}}^2} \cos(\Omega_s t - \phi) \right]
$$

Comparing with the canonical form gives the **modulation depth**:

$$
m = \frac{5.54 \, \theta \, \theta_q}{\theta_{3\text{dB}}^2}
$$

**Step 4: Error-Signal Extraction** [2 marks]

A coherent detector multiplies $A(t)$ by $\cos\Omega_s t$ and $\sin\Omega_s t$ to obtain:

$$
e_A = k_d \, m \cos\psi \quad \text{(azimuth error)}
$$

$$
e_E = k_d \, m \sin\psi \quad \text{(elevation error)}
$$

These voltages drive the two-axis servo motors to steer the antenna boresight onto the target. The **scan reference generator** provides the $\cos\Omega_s t$ and $\sin\Omega_s t$ references in correct phase with the beam rotation.

**Part (b) [7 Marks] – [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 (Model Paper)]**

> Explain the principle of **monopulse tracking**. With the help of a block diagram, describe the **amplitude-comparison monopulse comparator** and derive the monopulse ratio.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Principle of Monopulse** [2 marks]

Monopulse tracking extracts the angular error in a single transmitted pulse, using multiple simultaneous beams. The basic form uses two squinted beams; a comparator network forms the sum and difference patterns. The **ratio of difference to sum** is proportional to the target's angular offset, independent of echo amplitude.

**Step 2: Sum and Difference Patterns** [2 marks]

The two beams $F_1(\theta)$ and $F_2(\theta)$ are squinted by $\pm \theta_q/2$. The patterns are:

$$
\Sigma(\theta) = F_1(\theta) + F_2(\theta) \quad \text{(sum)}
$$

$$
\Delta(\theta) = F_1(\theta) - F_2(\theta) \quad \text{(difference)}
$$

The sum pattern has its peak on the boresight. The difference pattern has a null on the boresight (odd symmetry).

**Step 3: Comparator Block Diagram** [1 mark]

A magic-T hybrid junction has four ports: two antenna feeds (A, B), a sum port ($\Sigma$), and a difference port ($\Delta$). The sum and difference outputs go to separate receiver channels. A phase detector (or in-phase / quadrature detector) multiplies $\Delta$ by the phase-coherent reference from $\Sigma$, producing the in-phase and quadrature error voltages.

**Step 4: Derivation of the Monopulse Ratio** [2 marks]

For small squint angles we Taylor-expand:

$$
F_1(\theta) \approx F(0) + F'(0)(\theta - \theta_q/2)
$$

$$
F_2(\theta) \approx F(0) + F'(0)(\theta + \theta_q/2)
$$

Summing and subtracting:

$$
\Sigma(\theta) = 2F(0) + 2F'(0) \theta
$$

$$
\Delta(\theta) = -F'(0) \theta_q
$$

The monopulse ratio is:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta(\theta)}{\Sigma(\theta)} = \frac{-F'(0) \theta_q}{2F(0) + 2F'(0) \theta} \approx \frac{-F'(0) \theta_q}{2F(0)} = k_m \theta
$$

within the linear region. The constant $k_m$ is the monopulse slope, set by antenna geometry.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**Part (a) [7 Marks] – [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model Paper)]**

> With a block diagram, describe the **range-tracking loop** of a tracking radar. Explain the operation of the **early-late gate** discriminator.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Need for Range Tracking** [1 mark]

Once a target is detected at time-of-arrival $t_0 = 2R/c$, the radar must keep its range gate centered on the echo for continuous tracking. Manual adjustment is impossible at high data rates, so a closed-loop range tracker is used.

**Step 2: Early-Late Gate Principle** [2 marks]

The matched-filter output for a rectangular pulse of width $\tau$ is a triangular envelope. The discriminator forms two samples at $t_0 - \tau/2$ (the early gate) and $t_0 + \tau/2$ (the late gate). The difference is the range error:

$$
e_R = E - L
$$

**Step 3: Block Diagram** [1 mark]

Components: matched filter, two range-gate samplers, subtractor, VCO, and a controllable delay.

**Step 4: Mathematical Description** [2 marks]

For a matched-filter output $y(t) = A \, \text{tri}\!\left( \frac{t - t_0}{\tau} \right)$ with gate-center error $\delta t$:

$$
E = A \left( \frac{1}{2} - \frac{\delta t}{\tau} \right) \quad , \quad L = A \left( \frac{1}{2} + \frac{\delta t}{\tau} \right)
$$

$$
e_R = E - L = -\frac{2A \delta t}{\tau}
$$

The VCO adjusts the gate delay to drive $e_R$ to zero.

**Step 5: Equivalent Range Error** [1 mark]

Since $\delta R = c \delta t / 2$:

$$
e_R = -\frac{A \, c \, \delta R}{\tau}
$$

**Part (b) [7 Marks] – [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model Paper)]**

> Explain **Track-While-Scan (TWS)** mode of operation. Write the equations of an **$\alpha$-$\beta$ filter** and explain how it smooths the track.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: TWS Concept** [2 marks]

In TWS, a surveillance radar scans a volume. On each scan, plots are extracted at the range, angle, and amplitude of each detection. A computer **associates** new plots with existing tracks and uses a **smoothing filter** to update the state estimate. The output is a continuous track, even though the radar measures the target only once per scan.

**Step 2: $\alpha$-$\beta$ Filter Equations** [2 marks]

For a single coordinate (e.g., range) with $T_s$ being the scan period:

$$
x_{n+1} = x_n + \alpha (z_n - x_n) \quad \text{(position update)}
$$

$$
\dot{x}_{n+1} = \dot{x}_n + \frac{\beta}{T_s} (z_n - x_n) \quad \text{(velocity update)}
$$

where $z_n$ is the measured plot, $x_n$ the smoothed position, $\dot{x}_n$ the smoothed velocity, and $\alpha$, $\beta$ are dimensionless gains.

**Step 3: Stability and Choice of Gains** [2 marks]

A widely used choice (Benedict-Bordner):

$$
\beta = \frac{\alpha^2}{2 - \alpha}
$$

with $0 < \alpha < 1$. For maneuvering targets, a higher $\alpha$ is used (more responsive but noisier); for non-maneuvering, lower $\alpha$ for smoother output.

**Step 4: Practical Use** [1 mark]

TWS is the foundation of modern ATC and air-defense systems. Multi-hypothesis tracking (MHT) and Kalman filters are extensions used when targets maneuver or cross paths.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Tracking-Radar Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "monopulse" with "monostatic":** Monopulse means "single-pulse" error extraction, not "single antenna." A monopulse radar can be bistatic; the term refers to the **number of pulses** needed to form an error.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the units on the monopulse slope $k_m$:** It has units of V/rad (or dimensionless if normalized). A 7-mark derivation will be marked down if units are missing.
>
> 3. **Treating the conical-scan modulation phase as meaningless:** Examiners will give 1–2 marks for correctly identifying that the **phase** $\psi$ indicates the **direction** of the angular error.
>
> 4. **Confusing range resolution $\Delta R = c/2B$ with range accuracy of the tracking loop.** These are different; range accuracy is set by SNR and the discriminator slope.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Bode-plot / loop-gain analysis.** KTU 2024 ESE often asks for the **stability condition** of the servo loop. State that for a type-1 servo, the velocity-error constant $K_v$ must exceed the maximum target angular rate.
>
> 6. **Using "boresight" and "antenna axis" interchangeably in the conical-scan derivation.** The boresight is the **center of rotation** of the squinted beam; the antenna axis is the **instantaneous** beam direction. The target's offset is measured from the **boresight**, not the instantaneous beam.
>
> 7. **Forgetting that the early-late gate error is for a triangular envelope.** If the pulse is shaped, the discriminator slope changes. A common KTU trick question: "How does range accuracy change if a Hamming weighting is used in the matched filter?"

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- **Tracking radar** = narrow-beam radar that closes the loop on a target to maintain range, angle, and velocity estimates at high data rate.
- **Sequential lobing:** 2 beams, switched in time, error = $(S_1 - S_2)/(S_1 + S_2)$. Simple, but slow and noise-prone.
- **Conical scan:** 1 squinted beam rotated at $\Omega_s$. Echo is amplitude-modulated. Modulation depth $\propto$ angular offset; phase $\propto$ direction. **Vulnerable to inverse-gain jamming.**
- **Monopulse:** Single-pulse error. Sum $\Sigma$ and difference $\Delta$ patterns via a hybrid junction. Error $\varepsilon = \Delta/\Sigma$. **Standard for modern fire-control and missile guidance.**
- **Track-While-Scan:** Software-based tracking using a scanning surveillance radar; uses $\alpha$-$\beta$ or Kalman filter. Foundation of ATC and air defense.
- **Range tracking:** Early-late gate gives $e_R = (E - L) = -2A \delta t / \tau$. Drives a VCO that moves the range gate.
- **Velocity tracking:** Doppler shift $f_d = 2 \dot{R}/\lambda$, tracked by a narrow PLL or filter bank.
- **Key formulas to memorize:**
  - Monopulse ratio $\varepsilon = \Delta/\Sigma = k_m \theta$
  - Conical-scan modulation depth $m = 5.54 \, \theta \theta_q / \theta_{3\text{dB}}^2$
  - Doppler $\dot{R} = \lambda f_d / 2$
  - Range resolution $\Delta R = c/2B$
  - Range error $e_R = -2A \delta t / \tau$
  - $\alpha$-$\beta$ filter: $x_{n+1} = x_n + \alpha(z_n - x_n)$, $\dot{x}_{n+1} = \dot{x}_n + (\beta/T_s)(z_n - x_n)$
- **Key physical constants:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $T_0 = 290$ K, $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
- **Comparisons examiners love:**
  - Monopulse vs conical scan: monopulse is jam-resistant, single-pulse, more accurate.
  - TWS vs continuous track: TWS uses fewer resources, lower data rate, lower accuracy.
  - Amplitude vs phase monopulse: amplitude uses squinted beams; phase uses separated apertures.
- **Engineering uses:** Fire-control radars, missile seekers, BMD, ATC, weather, space surveillance.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** In any tracking-radar question worth 7+ marks, always (a) state the principle in 2 lines, (b) draw a labelled block diagram, (c) derive the key equation, and (d) write the engineering significance. This is the **valuation key pattern** used by KTU examiners and consistently scores full marks.
